topics.title: Approaches to Software Design - Functional Oriented Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Approaches to Software Design - Functional Oriented Design ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functional Oriented Design (FOD)** is a classical software design methodology, also known as **Procedure-Oriented Design** or **Top-Down Structured Design**, in which a system is decomposed into a hierarchy of functions (procedures/modules) that operate on shared, centralized data. The entire system is viewed as a collection of functions, where the primary emphasis is placed on **what the system does** (its functionality), and data is treated as a secondary, passive entity that flows between these active functions. In the KTU 2024 OOP framework, FOD is presented as the **predecessor paradigm** to Object Oriented Design. The design process moves from the highest-level abstract function (e.g., "Process Student Admission") down through successive levels of refinement into smaller, more specific procedures. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" Imagine a large, traditional restaurant kitchen (this is your software system): - **Functions (Chefs/Recipes)** = The active workers. One chef is responsible for "Prepare Dough", another for "Bake Pizza", and another for "Deliver Order". Each chef has a clearly defined job. - **Data (Ingredients)** = Tomatoes, cheese, dough lying on a common counter. The data is **shared**, and any chef can pick it up, modify it, and leave it for the next chef. - **Top-Down Decomposition** = The Head Chef first writes a high-level recipe: "Make Pizza". They then break it down step-by-step: *"Make Dough" → "Add Toppings" → "Bake" → "Serve"*. This is **FOD** — breaking the problem into smaller, manageable sub-functions. - **The Problem** = Because ingredients (data) are on a common counter, a careless chef can spoil the tomatoes intended for the salad (unintended data modification). This is the classic FOD weakness that **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** later solved by "locking" the ingredients inside a private box (encapsulation). ### 1.3 Core Characteristics of Functional Oriented Design > [!NOTE] > **The 6 Pillars of FOD** (Highly emphasized in KTU Module 1): 1. **Top-Down Decomposition** — The system is recursively broken down from a single main function into a tree of sub-functions. 2. **Centralized Data** — Data structures are global/shared and exist independently of the functions. 3. **Function-Centric View** — The program is modeled as a sequence of operations; "verbs" dominate over "nouns". 4. **Procedural Abstraction** — Each function is a black box with a defined input, output, and side effects. 5. **Structured Programming Constructs** — Relies strictly on `sequence`, `selection (if/switch)`, and `iteration (while/for)` — no `goto`. 6. **Separation of Code and Data** — Functions and data are two separate, independent entities in the design model. ### 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context > [!NOTE] > FOD was the **dominant paradigm from the 1960s to the 1980s**, rooted in languages like **C, Pascal, FORTRAN, and COBOL**. It is also called the **"Algorithm-First"** approach because the focus begins with writing the algorithm/procedure, and data structures are chosen later to support it. ### 1.5 Why Study FOD in an OOP Course? KTU includes FOD in the OOP syllabus so students can **contrast** the two paradigms and understand **why OOD was invented** — specifically to solve the **data-access control, maintainability, and reuse** problems inherent in large FOD systems. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Functional Oriented Design Process — Step-by-Step The classical FOD methodology (popularized by **Yourdon, Constantine, and Wirth**) follows a strict pipeline: **Step 1: Requirements Analysis** Identify the system's functional requirements. Ask: *"What must the system do?"* **Step 2: Function Identification** List the major functions (verbs) the system must perform. Example for a Library System: - `Issue_Book()` - `Return_Book()` - `Search_Book()` - `Calculate_Fine()` **Step 3: Top-Down Decomposition (Hierarchical Refinement)** The root function is recursively split into sub-functions until each leaf function performs a single, well-defined task. This produces a **Structure Chart** (a tree diagram of modules). **Step 4: Data Design** Design global data structures (records, files, arrays) that will be **shared** across functions. **Step 5: Interface Specification** Define the **calling sequence**, **parameters passed**, and **return values** for every function. **Step 6: Algorithm Design** Write the detailed procedure body (pseudo-code/flowchart) for each leaf function using only structured constructs. **Step 7: Coupling & Cohesion Evaluation** Measure the *quality* of the decomposition. Good FOD aims for **low coupling** and **high cohesion**. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale - **Why Top-Down?** Human cognition handles complexity best by reducing it — a single problem becomes many small problems. - **Why measure Coupling/Cohesion?** These are **quantitative proxies for software quality** (maintainability, reusability, fault-proneness). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Quality Metrics | Metric | Symbol / Formula | Ideal Value | Quality Indicator | |---|---|---|---| | Coupling (Best → Worst) | $C_{type} \in \{ \text{Data}, \text{Stamp}, \text{Control}, \text{External}, \text{Common}, \text{Content} \}$ | $\text{Data Coupling}$ | **Lowest** coupling is best | | Cohesion (Worst → Best) | $H_{type} \in \{ \text{Coincidental}, \text{Logical}, \text{Temporal}, \text{Procedural}, \text{Communicational}, \text{Sequential}, \text{Functional} \}$ | $\text{Functional Cohesion}$ | **Highest** cohesion is best | | Module Strength Ratio | $R_{MS} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Functional Cohesive Modules}}{\text{Total Number of Modules}}$ | $R_{MS} \rightarrow 1.0$ | Closer to 1 is better | | Fan-In | $F_{in}(M) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ where $C_i = 1$ if module $i$ calls $M$ | High $F_{in}$ | Good — high reusability | | Fan-Out | $F_{out}(M) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_j$ where $C_j = 1$ if $M$ calls module $j$ | Moderate $F_{out}$ ($\leq 7$) | Too high = complex module | | Stability Metric | $I = \dfrac{F_{in}}{F_{in} + F_{out}}$ | $I \in [0, 1]$ | Higher = more stable | | Information Flow Metric (Henry-Kafura) | $MF = \text{Length} \times (F_{in} \times F_{out})^{2}$ | **Minimize** $MF$ | Lower = better maintainability | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Coupling Types** (memorize the order): > **Data $\rightarrow$ Stamp $\rightarrow$ Control $\rightarrow$ External $\rightarrow$ Common $\rightarrow$ Content** > (Data coupling is BEST; Content coupling is WORST.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Cohesion Types** (memorize the order): > **Coincidental $\rightarrow$ Logical $\rightarrow$ Temporal $\rightarrow$ Procedural $\rightarrow$ Communicational $\rightarrow$ Sequential $\rightarrow$ Functional** > (Coincidental is WORST; Functional is BEST.) ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of FOD Even today, FOD is heavily used in: - **Embedded Systems Firmware** (C-based microcontrollers) - **Operating System Kernels** (Linux kernel scheduler, memory allocator) - **Numerical/ Scientific Computing** (FORTRAN-style simulations) - **High-Performance Computing** (HPC) where function-call overhead is critical - **Compiler Design** (lexical analyzers, parsers are written as function pipelines) --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Top-Down Decomposition of a "Student Result Processing System" **High-Level Requirement:** Process end-semester results for a class of students. #### Step 1: Identify the Root Function The single main function is: ``` PROCESS_RESULTS() ``` #### Step 2: First-Level Decomposition (Identify Major Sub-Functions) Using structured analysis, we ask *"What does processing results involve?"* - Collect marks from all subjects - Calculate total and percentage - Assign grade based on percentage - Print the final report $$ \text{PROCESS\_RESULTS} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{READ\_MARKS()} \\ \text{CALCULATE\_TOTAL\_AND\_PERCENT()} \\ \text{ASSIGN\_GRADE()} \\ \text{PRINT\_REPORT()} \end{cases} $$ #### Step 3: Second-Level Decomposition Now decompose `CALCULATE_TOTAL_AND_PERCENT()`: $$ \text{CALCULATE\_TOTAL\_AND\_PERCENT} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{SUM\_SUBJECT\_MARKS()} \\ \text{COMPUTE\_PERCENTAGE()} \\ \text{STORE\_IN\_GLOBAL\_RECORD} \end{cases} $$ #### Step 4: Final Pseudo-Code for the Root Function ``` GLOBAL DATA: student_record { name, marks[6], total, percent, grade } PROCESS_RESULTS(): READ_MARKS(student_record) CALCULATE_TOTAL_AND_PERCENT(student_record) ASSIGN_GRADE(student_record) PRINT_REPORT(student_record) END ``` > [!NOTE] > **Critical Observation for KTU:** Notice how `student_record` (the data) is declared **GLOBALLY** and **passed around** every function. This is the hallmark of FOD — and its biggest design weakness. #### Step 5: Refined Functional Hierarchy Structure Chart $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Level 0:} \quad \text{PROCESS\_RESULTS} \\ &\textbf{Level 1:} \quad \Big\{ \text{READ\_MARKS},\ \text{CALCULATE\_T\&P},\ \text{ASSIGN\_GRADE},\ \text{PRINT\_REPORT} \Big\} \\ &\textbf{Level 2 (under CALC\_T\&P):} \quad \Big\{ \text{SUM\_MARKS},\ \text{COMPUTE\_PERCENT} \Big\} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Example — Coupling & Cohesion Evaluation **Scenario:** Two modules $M_1$ and $M_2$. - $M_1$ calls $M_2$ passing **all fields** of a 10-field record, but $M_2$ only uses 2 fields. **Diagnosis (Step-by-Step):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Parameter Type} &= \text{Entire record passed} \\ \text{Fields used by receiver } (M_2) &= 2 \text{ out of } 10 \\ \therefore \text{Coupling Type} &= \textbf{Stamp Coupling} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** "Stamp coupling" occurs when a **complete data structure** is passed but only **part** of it is used. This is *worse* than data coupling (where only primitive values are passed) but *better* than control coupling (where a flag controls the callee's logic). **Why is this Stamp Coupling and not Data Coupling?** - Data coupling passes **individual elementary values** (e.g., `int`, `float`). - Stamp coupling passes a **whole structure/object** even if the callee uses only some fields — the callee becomes dependent on the *format* of the structure, not just its content. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Coupling vs. Cohesion (Comparison Table) | Aspect | Coupling | Cohesion | |---|---|---| | **What it measures** | Inter-module dependency (between modules) | Intra-module strength (within a module) | | **Goal** | **Minimize** (Low Coupling) | **Maximize** (High Cohesion) | | **Best Type** | Data Coupling | Functional Cohesion | | **Worst Type** | Content Coupling | Coincidental Cohesion | | **KTU Memory Trick** | "Data is **D**esirable" (best) | "**F**unctional is **F**antastic" (best) | | **Effect on Maintenance** | High coupling $\Rightarrow$ ripple effect of changes | Low cohesion $\Rightarrow$ module is hard to understand | | **Quantitative Proxy** | Fan-In, Fan-Out, Henry-Kafura $MF$ | Strength Ratio $R_{MS}$ | ### 3.4 Derivation of Fan-In and Fan-Out for a Sample Module Graph **Given:** Module A is called by 3 other modules. Module A itself calls 4 other modules. $$ \begin{aligned} F_{in}(A) &= 3 \\ F_{out}(A) &= 4 \\ I(A) &= \dfrac{F_{in}}{F_{in} + F_{out}} = \dfrac{3}{3 + 4} = \dfrac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286 \\ \text{Stability Rating} &= \textbf{Moderate (acceptable range } 0.3 \text{ to } 0.7 \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If $I \rightarrow 1$: module is **stable** (many callers, few callees — good for utility libraries). > If $I \rightarrow 0$: module is **unstable** (few callers, many callees — risky to modify). --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — FOD Top-Down Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Level 0: SYSTEM ROOT FUNCTION] B1[Level 1: MODULE A] B2[Level 1: MODULE B] B3[Level 1: MODULE C] C1[Level 2: SUB A1] C2[Level 2: SUB A2] C3[Level 2: SUB B1] C4[Level 2: SUB C1] C5[Level 2: SUB C2] D1[Leaf: A1a] D2[Leaf: A1b] A --> B1 A --> B2 A --> B3 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C3 B3 --> C4 B3 --> C5 C1 --> D1 C1 --> D2 G[(GLOBAL SHARED DATA STORE)] B1 -.reads/writes.-> G B2 -.reads/writes.-> G B3 -.reads/writes.-> G C1 -.reads/writes.-> G C4 -.reads/writes.-> G style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C3 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C4 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C5 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D1 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > The **red GLOBAL SHARED DATA STORE** in the center is the defining weakness of FOD. Every module reads and writes to it, leading to **high common coupling** and difficulty in tracing data flow during debugging. ### 4.2 FOD vs. OOD — Conceptual Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FOD[ FOD - Function Oriented Design ] F1[Active Functions] F2[Functions Move Around Data] D1[(Centralized Data)] F1 --> D1 end subgraph OOD[ OOD - Object Oriented Design ] O1[Object = Data + Functions Together] O2[Data is Protected] O3[Functions Encapsulated Inside] O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 end FOD -. "Problem: data exposed" .-> PROB((Uncontrolled
Data Access)) OOD -. "Solution: encapsulation" .-> SOL((Controlled
Data Access)) style F1 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style O1 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 style O2 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 style O3 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 style PROB fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SOL fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Coupling & Cohesion Quality Spectrum (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[DATA COUPLING] --> A2[STAMP COUPLING] A2 --> A3[CONTROL COUPLING] A3 --> A4[EXTERNAL COUPLING] A4 --> A5[COMMON COUPLING] A5 --> A6[CONTENT COUPLING] B1[COINCIDENTAL] --> B2[LOGICAL] B2 --> B3[TEMPORAL] B3 --> B4[PROCEDURAL] B4 --> B5[COMMUNICATION] B5 --> B6[SEQUENTIAL] B6 --> B7[FUNCTIONAL] style A1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A2 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 style A3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style A4 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000 style A5 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A6 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000 style B4 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style B5 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 style B6 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B7 fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. Define Functional Oriented Design. List any four of its characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** Functional Oriented Design is a software design approach in which the system is viewed as a collection of functions that transform input data into output data, with primary emphasis on *what the system does* rather than *what data it manipulates*. **Four Characteristics (any 4 for full 3 marks):** 1. Top-down hierarchical decomposition of the problem. 2. Centralized/shared global data structures. 3. Function-centric view (verbs dominate). 4. Procedural abstraction with structured constructs (sequence, selection, iteration). 5. Separation of code and data. *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 characteristics.)* --- #### **Q2. Differentiate between Coupling and Cohesion. Mention the best and worst types of each.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Coupling | Cohesion | |---|---|---| | Definition | Inter-module dependency | Intra-module binding strength | | Goal | Minimize | Maximize | | Best Type | **Data Coupling** | **Functional Cohesion** | | Worst Type | **Content Coupling** | **Coincidental Cohesion** | *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark coupling explanation + 1 mark cohesion explanation + 1 mark best/worst types.)* --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **top-down approach** of Functional Oriented Design in detail with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the different **types of coupling** between modules with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Introduction to Top-Down Design (1 mark)** Top-down design (also called *stepwise refinement*) is a decomposition strategy proposed by **Niklaus Wirth**. The designer starts with the highest abstraction (the system as one black box) and progressively breaks it into smaller sub-functions until each sub-function is small enough to be coded directly (typically 10–50 lines). **Step 2: The Decomposition Process (2 marks)** The steps are: 1. State the problem as a single function `MAIN()`. 2. Identify major sub-tasks; create a function for each. 3. For each sub-task, repeat step 2 until all leaf functions are simple. 4. Construct a **Structure Chart** to visualize the hierarchy. **Step 3: Worked Example — "Library Management System" (3 marks)** ``` Main Function: MANAGE_LIBRARY() ├── ADD_BOOK() ├── ISSUE_BOOK() │ ├── VERIFY_MEMBER() │ ├── CHECK_AVAILABILITY() │ └── UPDATE_TRANSACTION() ├── RETURN_BOOK() │ ├── CALCULATE_FINE() │ └── UPDATE_STOCK() └── SEARCH_BOOK() ├── SEARCH_BY_TITLE() └── SEARCH_BY_AUTHOR() ``` **Step 4: Advantages & Limitations (1 mark)** - ✅ *Advantage:* Easy to manage complexity, suits structured programming. - ❌ *Limitation:* Global data is shared — leads to high common coupling and difficult debugging. --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b) — 7 Marks** **Coupling = Inter-module dependency. The higher the dependency, the worse the design.** | # | Coupling Type | Description (1 mark each) | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Data Coupling (BEST)** | Modules share data through parameters; no shared global state. | | 2 | **Stamp Coupling** | A whole data structure is passed, but only part of it is used. | | 3 | **Control Coupling** | One module passes a control flag that alters the flow of the other. | | 4 | **External Coupling** | Two modules share an externally imposed data format (e.g., I/O device). | | 5 | **Common Coupling** | Modules share global data. | | 6 | **Content Coupling (WORST)** | One module directly modifies the internal code/local data of another. | **Diagrammatic Representation (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Module A] -->|parameter| B[Module B] A -.->|shared global| G[(Common Data)] A -->|control flag| C[Module C] A -->|whole struct| D[Module D] ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A(b):** > Students frequently **confuse "Control Coupling" with "Control Flow"** inside a single module (if/while). Control coupling is **between two modules** where a flag is passed to influence the callee's path. Also, many students write "Stamp" and "Data" coupling as the same — remember: **stamp coupling passes a *structure*; data coupling passes only *primitive values*.** --- #### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a suitable example, illustrate **Function Oriented Design** methodology. What are its merits and demerits? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **seven types of cohesion** in detail. Identify the best and worst types with justification. **(7 marks)** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Definition (1 mark)** FOD views a system as a set of functions operating on shared data. The system is designed by identifying all functions, then designing data structures to support them. **Step 2: Methodology Phases (2 marks)** 1. Problem Analysis — identify what functions are needed. 2. Module Hierarchy Design — organize functions into a structure chart. 3. Data Design — design shared data structures. 4. Algorithm Design — write pseudo-code for each module. 5. Coupling & Cohesion Review — quality check. **Step 3: Example — "Payroll System" (2 marks)** ``` COMPUTE_PAYROLL() ├── GET_EMPLOYEE_DATA() ├── CALCULATE_BASIC_PAY() ├── CALCULATE_DEDUCTIONS() │ ├── CALC_TAX() │ └── CALC_PF() ├── CALCULATE_NET_PAY() └── PRINT_PAYSLIP() ``` Global data: `employee_record { emp_id, basic, hra, tax, pf, net_pay }` **Step 4: Merits (1 mark)** - Simple to learn and implement; well-suited for small-to-medium systems. - Efficient execution (no object overhead). **Step 5: Demerits (1 mark)** - Difficult maintenance for large systems; data is exposed globally. - Poor reusability; functions depend on global structure, not easily portable. --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b) — 7 Marks** **Definition (1 mark):** Cohesion measures how strongly the elements inside a single module belong together. **Goal: maximize cohesion.** **The Seven Types (Worst → Best) — 1 mark each:** | # | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Coinincidental (WORST)** | Elements grouped arbitrarily; no meaningful relationship. | | 2 | **Logical** | Elements perform similar functions, chosen by a control flag. | | 3 | **Temporal** | Elements executed at the same time (e.g., `initialization()`). | | 4 | **Procedural** | Elements must be executed in a fixed order. | | 5 | **Communicational** | Elements operate on the same input data or produce the same output. | | 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one element is the input to the next within the same module. | | 7 | **Functional (BEST)** | All elements contribute to a single, well-defined task. | **Best Justification (½ mark):** Functional cohesion is best because the module has **one clear purpose**, making it easy to name, test, debug, and reuse. **Worst Justification (½ mark):** Coincidental cohesion is worst because the module is essentially a "junk drawer" of unrelated code; any change may break unrelated functionality, and it cannot be meaningfully described by a single name. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B(b):** > 1. Many students list the types in **arbitrary order** — you lose 1 mark. The order from *worst to best* is mandatory for full credit. > 2. Students often confuse **"Sequential Cohesion" with "Procedural Cohesion"**. Rule: if element A's output *feeds* element B → **Sequential**. If they are merely *executed in order* but work on *different data* → **Procedural**. > 3. A common error is writing "Logical Cohesion is best" — it is **third-worst**. Only **Functional** is the best. --- ### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ **FOD = Function-centric + Top-down + Centralized Data + Structured Constructs.** - ✅ The primary design artifact is the **Structure Chart** (hierarchy of modules). - ✅ Data is **global and shared** — this is FOD's biggest flaw. - ✅ Design quality is measured by **Coupling (minimize)** and **Cohesion (maximize)**. - ✅ Coupling best → worst: **Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common, Content.** - ✅ Cohesion best → worst: **Functional, Sequential, Communicational, Procedural, Temporal, Logical, Coincidental.** - ✅ FOD preceded OOD; OOD solves FOD's data-protection and reuse weaknesses. - ✅ FOD is still used in **embedded systems, OS kernels, and HPC**. - ✅ **Henry-Kafura Metric** $MF = \text{Length} \times (F_{in} \times F_{out})^{2}$ — lower is better. - ✅ **Stability Index** $I = \dfrac{F_{in}}{F_{in} + F_{out}}$ — closer to 1 = more stable module. - ✅ **Stamp Coupling** = whole structure passed, only part used. - ✅ **Content Coupling** = one module modifies another's internals (worst possible). - ✅ **Functional Cohesion** = module does exactly one thing (best possible). - ✅ KTU asks this topic every year — usually as a **14-mark full question** with sub-parts on top-down design + coupling/cohesion.

topics.title: Object Oriented Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** is the *systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable* approach (as defined by **Booch, Rumbaugh, and Jacobson** in the Unified Process) of decomposing a software problem into a collaborating set of **objects**, each encapsulating both **state** (attributes/data) and **behavior** (operations/methods), and assigning **responsibilities** to those objects based on well-established design principles such as **GRASP** and **SOLID**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, Object Oriented Design is positioned as the bridge between *analysis* (the "what" — captured in use-cases and domain models) and *implementation* (the "how" — expressed through classes, interfaces, and objects in a language such as Java, C++, or Python).

The **core deliverable** of OOD is an **Object Model** — a blueprint consisting of:

1. **Class Diagrams** — describing the static structure (classes, attributes, operations, relationships).
2. **Interaction Diagrams** — describing the dynamic behavior (sequence and collaboration diagrams).
3. **Package/Component Diagrams** — describing the physical/logical grouping of classes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a busy restaurant. The **customers** (external actors) do not cook their own food. They place an **order** (a message). The **head chef** receives the order and delegates it: salads go to the *cold station*, pasta to the *pasta station*, desserts to the *pastry section*. Each station has its own **ingredients (state)** and **recipes (behavior)**, and they communicate by handing off partially finished plates.

This kitchen is an **object-oriented system**:

| Restaurant Element | OOD Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Waiter taking order | External actor / user invoking a method |
| Order slip | **Message** sent to an object |
| Head Chef | **Controller / Façade object** |
| Each station | A **Class** with a clear **responsibility** |
| Ingredients locked in each station's fridge | **Encapsulation** (private state) |
| Recipe cards on each station | **Public methods / interface** |
| Hand-off of plates between stations | **Collaboration / message passing** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks "explain OOD with an example," always pick a *system* the examiner instantly recognizes (ATM, Library Management, Hospital, or this Restaurant analogy). Tie each OOD concept (encapsulation, message passing, polymorphism, responsibility) to a *concrete role* in the analogy. Examiners reward clarity and structured mapping.

---

## 1.3 Why OOD? — The Motivation

Before OOD became dominant, software was built using **structured / procedural design** (top-down function decomposition). As systems grew, three critical problems emerged:

1. **Rigidity** — a small change in one module forced changes in many others.
2. **Fragility** — fixing one bug introduced new bugs elsewhere.
3. **Non-reusability** — code could not be easily lifted into a new project.

OOD directly addresses these by treating software as a **collection of interacting objects** that mirror real-world entities, making the design:

- **Modular** (each class is an independent unit)
- **Extensible** (new classes can be added without breaking old ones)
- **Maintainable** (changes are localized)
- **Reusable** (well-designed classes become libraries)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** "OOD = OOP (Object Oriented Programming)."
> **Correction:** OOD is the *design phase* (architectural thinking, class modeling, responsibility assignment). OOP is the *implementation phase* (writing classes, objects, inheritance in code). You can do OOD on paper without ever touching a compiler, and you can write OOP code with poor OOD — which is exactly what KTU examiners penalize.

---

## 1.4 Position of OOD in the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)

The **Unified Software Development Process** organizes work into four phases — Inception, Elaboration, Construction, Transition. OOD is most active in the **Elaboration** and early **Construction** phases, sitting between *Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA)* and *Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)*.

```
[Requirements] → [OOA: WHAT] → [OOD: HOW (Structure + Behavior)] → [OOP: Implementation] → [Testing]
```

| Phase | Output | Key Question |
|---|---|---|
| **OOA** | Use-cases, Domain Model, Class Candidates | *What must the system do?* |
| **OOD** | Refined Class Diagram, Interaction Diagrams, Design Patterns | *How will objects collaborate to fulfill the use-cases?* |
| **OOP** | Source Code in a language | *Code that compiles and runs* |

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Object Oriented Design

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three foundational mechanisms. Each is a *design-time decision* even though it appears in code:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Encapsulation** — Bundling data and the methods that operate on that data within one unit (class), and restricting external access via access modifiers (`private`, `protected`, `public`).
> - **Inheritance** — A mechanism where a new class (subclass) is created from an existing class (superclass), inheriting its attributes and methods, enabling *generalization / specialization*.
> - **Polymorphism** — The ability of different objects to respond to the *same message* in *different ways*, typically realized via *method overriding* (dynamic polymorphism) or *method overloading* (static polymorphism).

A fourth concept often tested is **Abstraction** — exposing only the essential features while hiding the implementation, typically via *abstract classes* and *interfaces*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OOD Process — A Step-by-Step Methodology

A defensible KTU answer on OOD must walk through a *methodology*. The most widely accepted and most frequently tested in KTU is the **Booch/Rumbaugh methodology**, adapted as follows:

### Step 1 — Identify the Objects (Noun Extraction)
- Read the **problem statement** or **use-case**.
- Highlight all **nouns** — these are *candidate classes*.
- Filter out: redundant nouns, vague nouns, nouns outside the system's scope, attributes disguised as nouns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** *"A Library allows Members to borrow Books. Each Book has a Title and Author. A Member can reserve at most 3 Books."*
> Candidate classes: **Library, Member, Book, Reservation**.
> Reject: *Title, Author* (these are attributes of Book), *borrow* (a verb, becomes a method).

### Step 2 — Identify the Attributes (Data Members)
- Each surviving class is analyzed for the **data it must store**.
- Attributes are scoped to the class where they logically belong.

### Step 3 — Identify the Behaviors (Methods / Operations)
- Verbs in the problem statement map to methods.
- Assign each method to the class that **owns the data** the method needs (this is the **Information Expert** GRASP principle — see §2.3).

### Step 4 — Establish Relationships
Five standard relationships exist in OOD:

| Relationship | UML Notation | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Association** | Solid line | "Uses-a" | Teacher *teaches* Student |
| **Aggregation** | Hollow diamond | "Has-a" (weak ownership) | Department *has* Professors |
| **Composition** | Filled diamond | "Owns-a" (strong lifecycle) | House *owns* Rooms |
| **Inheritance / Generalization** | Hollow triangle | "Is-a" | SavingsAccount *is-a* BankAccount |
| **Dependency** | Dashed arrow | "Depends-on" | Controller *depends on* Service |

### Step 5 — Refine Using Design Principles
- Apply **GRASP** and **SOLID** (covered below) to ensure good responsibility assignment.
- Introduce **Design Patterns** only when a *recurring problem* is detected (Strategy, Observer, Factory, etc.).

### Step 6 — Construct the Class Diagram
- The class diagram is the *primary artifact* of OOD — it is what examiners expect.

---

## 2.2 Coupling and Cohesion — The Twin Quality Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two metrics are **exam-favorite topics**. KTU frequently asks: *"What is good design?"* The textbook answer is: **Low Coupling + High Cohesion.**

### 2.2.1 Cohesion (Inside a Class)
**Cohesion** measures how strongly the responsibilities of a *single* module/class are related. It ranges from **low (worst)** to **high (best)**.

| Level | Type | Description | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Random grouping of unrelated functions | Worst |
| 2 | **Logical** | Similar logical category (e.g., all I/O) | Poor |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Executed at the same time (e.g., initialization) | Acceptable |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Must be executed in order | Mediocre |
| 5 | **Communicational** | Operate on same data | Good |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one is input of next | Very Good |
| 7 | **Functional** | All contribute to a single well-defined task | **Best** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** Always aim for **Functional Cohesion** — a class should do *one thing and do it well*. A `Customer` class with methods for `calculateBill()`, `printInvoice()`, `connectToDatabase()`, `sendEmail()` has **low cohesion** and is a design smell.

### 2.2.2 Coupling (Between Classes)
**Coupling** measures the degree of interdependence between modules. **Lower coupling = better design.**

| Level | Type | Description | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Content** | One module directly modifies another's internals | Worst |
| 2 | **Common** | Share global data | Poor |
| 3 | **External** | Share an externally imposed data format | Mediocre |
| 4 | **Control** | Pass control flags | Mediocre |
| 5 | **Stamp** | Pass composite data structure | Good |
| 6 | **Data** | Pass only primitive parameters | **Best** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "low coupling" with "no coupling." *Zero coupling* means the system cannot function — modules must communicate. The goal is **minimum necessary coupling**, achieved via **interfaces** and **dependency inversion**.

---

## 2.3 GRASP Principles (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)

GRASP provides **9 principles** for assigning responsibilities. KTU most frequently tests the top 4:

| # | Principle | One-Line Definition | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Expert** | Assign a responsibility to the class that *has the information* needed to fulfill it. | Default rule for almost every method. |
| 2 | **Creator** | Assign class B the responsibility to create instances of class A if B *aggregates, contains, or closely uses* A. | Choosing where `new` lives. |
| 3 | **Low Coupling** | Assign responsibilities to *minimize* dependency between classes. | Evaluating any collaboration. |
| 4 | **High Cohesion** | Assign responsibilities so each class stays focused on one logical purpose. | Evaluating each class's workload. |
| 5 | **Controller** | Assign the responsibility of handling a system event to a *Façade Controller* class representing the overall use-case. | The entry-point class for a use-case. |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | Assign type-specific behavior using polymorphic operations rather than `if-instanceof` chains. | When behavior varies by type. |
| 7 | **Pure Fabrication** | Assign a highly cohesive set of responsibilities to an *artificial* class that doesn't represent a domain concept (e.g., `DatabaseGateway`). | To achieve low coupling when Information Expert would violate it. |
| 8 | **Indirection** | Assign responsibility to an intermediate object so that coupling is reduced (e.g., via an interface). | Decoupling two volatile classes. |
| 9 | **Protected Variations** | Identify points of predicted instability and create a stable interface around them. | Anticipating change. |

---

## 2.4 SOLID Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLID** is the acronym introduced by **Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob)**. It is now a *mandatory* component of any KTU OOD question worth 7+ marks.

| Letter | Principle | Definition | KTU-Common Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** | A class should have *only one reason to change*. | Splitting `Invoice` into `InvoicePrinter` and `InvoiceCalculator`. |
| **O** | **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** | A class should be *open for extension* but *closed for modification*. | Adding new payment types via a new subclass, not editing the existing switch. |
| **L** | **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** | Subclasses must be substitutable for their base classes without breaking correctness. | A `Square extends Rectangle` is a classic LSP violation. |
| **I** | **Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)** | Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. | Split a fat `IMachine` interface into `IPrinter` + `IScanner`. |
| **D** | **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** | High-level modules must not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on *abstractions*. | Depending on `IDatabase` interface, not on `MySQLDatabase` directly. |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metrics Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the *numerical metrics and decision formulas* used in KTU OOD problems. No vertical pipes are used inside cell content — all absolute-value and conditional notation is rendered in LaTeX.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Metric / Formula} & \textbf{Expression} & \textbf{Goal} & \textbf{KTU Use} \\
\hline
\text{Coupling (qualitative)} & C = \text{dep}(A,B) & \text{Minimize } C & \text{Compare two designs} \\
\hline
\text{Cohesion (qualitative)} & \text{Co} \in \{1,\ldots,7\} & \text{Maximize Co toward 7} & \text{Evaluate a class} \\
\hline
\text{Stability of a package} & I = \dfrac{e_c}{e_c + e_a} & 0 \le I \le 1 & \text{Robert Martin's metric} \\
\hline
\text{Abstractness of a package} & A = \dfrac{N_a}{N_c} & 0 \le A \le 1 & \text{Fraction of abstract types} \\
\hline
\text{Distance from main sequence} & D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert & \text{Minimize } D & \text{Healthy packages have } D \approx 0 \\
\hline
\text{Response Set size} & \text{RS} = \vert \{\text{methods called by } m\} \vert & \text{Smaller is better} & \text{Indicates coupling} \\
\hline
\text{LCOM (Lack of Cohesion)} & \text{LCOM} = P - Q \text{ if } P > Q \text{ else } 0 & \text{Smaller is better} & \text{Chidamber-Kemerer metric} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Where:
- $e_c$ = efferent coupling (outgoing dependencies)
- $e_a$ = afferent coupling (incoming dependencies)
- $N_a$ = number of abstract classes / interfaces
- $N_c$ = total number of classes in the package
- $P$ = pairs of methods that do NOT share instance variables
- $Q$ = pairs of methods that DO share instance variables

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

OOD is not just an academic exercise — it underpins virtually every modern engineering domain:

| Domain | Application of OOD |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | Modeling sensors/actuators as objects with a common interface (OCP). |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Software components as objects; OCP enables adding new ECUs without modifying existing code. |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Each account type as a subclass; polymorphism for interest calculation. |
| **Healthcare (HIS)** | Patient, Doctor, Appointment, Prescription as distinct classes with well-defined responsibilities. |
| **IoT** | Each device as an object publishing events (Observer pattern). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU ESE Pro Tip:** When asked *"Explain the importance of OOD in modern software engineering,"* always close with a **domain-specific example** from the table above. This adds 1–2 extra marks for *application-level thinking*, which examiners explicitly reward under the **Application** level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: OOD for an "Online Course Registration System"

This exhaustive walk-through satisfies the **Apply / Analyze** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy and is the most reliable way to secure full marks in a 14-mark KTU question.

### Step 1 — Requirements (Use-Case Excerpt)
> *"A Student logs in, views the Course Catalog, selects Courses (max 6 credits per semester), and registers. The system generates an Invoice. If the Student has outstanding dues, registration is blocked. The Admin manages Courses."*

### Step 2 — Identify Classes (Noun Extraction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Nouns} &= \{\text{Student, Course, Catalog, Invoice, Semester, Dues, Admin, System}\} \\
\text{Candidate Classes (after filtering)} &= \{\text{Student, Course, CourseCatalog, Invoice, Admin}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Filtering Logic:**
- *System* → too vague; represents the boundary, not a class.
- *Semester* → could be an attribute of `Course` or `Registration`.
- *Dues* → becomes an attribute of `Student` and a check inside `register()`.
- *Catalog* → merged with `CourseCatalog` for clarity.

### Step 3 — Identify Attributes

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Student} &= \{ \text{studentId}, \text{name}, \text{email}, \text{outstandingDues}, \text{registeredCourses[]} \} \\
\text{Course} &= \{ \text{courseId}, \text{title}, \text{credits}, \text{instructor}, \text{capacity} \} \\
\text{CourseCatalog} &= \{ \text{courses[]} \} \\
\text{Invoice} &= \{ \text{invoiceId}, \text{studentId}, \text{amount}, \text{date}, \text{status} \} \\
\text{Admin} &= \{ \text{adminId}, \text{name}, \text{role} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Methods (Information Expert Principle)

The method is placed on the class that *has the data*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Student.register(course)} &\rightarrow \text{Information Expert: Student owns dues and registeredCourses} \\
\text{CourseCatalog.search(keyword)} &\rightarrow \text{Information Expert: Catalog owns the courses array} \\
\text{Course.isAvailable()} &\rightarrow \text{Expert: Course knows capacity} \\
\text{Invoice.calculateTotal()} &\rightarrow \text{Expert: Invoice knows amount} \\
\text{Admin.addCourse(course)} &\rightarrow \text{Expert: Admin has CRUD privileges}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Establish Relationships

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Student} &\;\xleftrightarrow{\text{1 to many}}\; \text{Invoice} \quad \text{(Association — generates)} \\
\text{Student} &\;\xleftrightarrow{\text{many to many}}\; \text{Course} \quad \text{(Association — registers for)} \\
\text{CourseCatalog} &\;\bullet\!\xleftarrow{\text{1 to many}}\; \text{Course} \quad \text{(Composition — Catalog owns Courses)} \\
\text{Admin} &\;\xleftarrow{\text{manages}}\; \text{CourseCatalog} \quad \text{(Association)} \\
\text{Student} &\;\triangleleft \text{extends} \;\text{User} \quad \text{(Generalization — both Student and Admin are Users)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply SOLID Refinements

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SRP violation?} &\rightarrow \text{Student was originally going to also "print invoice" — moved to a separate Printer class.} \\
\text{OCP violation?} &\rightarrow \text{If more user types (Faculty, Guest) appear later, add subclasses of User, do not edit existing code.} \\
\text{LSP check?} &\rightarrow \text{Ensure Admin and Student are substitutable for User anywhere a generic User is expected.} \\
\text{ISP check?} &\rightarrow \text{Split IUser into IAuthenticatable and IRegistratable.} \\
\text{DIP check?} &\rightarrow \text{RegistrationService depends on IStudentRepository, not on MySQLStudentRepository.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Class Diagram (Narrative Form)

The final class diagram contains **5 concrete classes + 1 abstract class + 2 interfaces**:

```
+---------------------+        +------------------------+
|    <<abstract>>     |        | <<interface>>          |
|       User          |        |  IStudentRepository    |
+---------------------+        +------------------------+
| - userId            |        | + save(s)              |
| - name              |        | + findById(id)         |
| - email             |        +------------------------+
| + login()           |                  ^
+---------------------+                  |
         ^                              |
         |                              |
   +-----+------+               +-------+--------+
   |            |               |                |
+-------+  +--------+    +------+------+  +------+----------+
|Student|  | Admin  |    |Registration |  |MySQLStudent     |
+--------+ +--------+    | Service      |  | Repository      |
                       +---------------+  +-----------------+
                       | + register()  |
                       +---------------+
```

### Step 8 — Evaluate Coupling and Cohesion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cohesion of RegistrationService} &= \text{Functional} = 7 \quad \text{(Best)} \\
\text{Coupling between Service and Repository} &= \text{Data} \quad \text{(Best — depends on interface only)} \\
\text{Stability of the Service package} \; I &= \frac{e_c}{e_c + e_a} = \frac{1}{1+2} = 0.33 \\
\text{Abstractness of the Service package} \; A &= \frac{N_a}{N_c} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.33 \\
D &= \vert A + I - 1 \vert = \vert 0.33 + 0.33 - 1 \vert = 0.34 \quad \text{(Acceptable, on the main sequence)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A package is considered **healthy** when $D \approx 0$. Our value $D = 0.34$ is on the borderline of the *main sequence*, indicating a balanced design — neither too concrete nor too abstract.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which GRASP Principle Wins?

When two principles **conflict**, apply this priority table (a synthesis of Craig Larman and KTU examiner consensus):

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{If...} & \textbf{Conflict} & \textbf{Resolution} \\
\hline
\text{Information Expert vs. Low Coupling} & \text{Expert creates high coupling} & \text{Use Pure Fabrication} \\
\hline
\text{Creator vs. Low Coupling} & \text{Creator creates cyclic dependency} & \text{Use Factory or DI container} \\
\hline
\text{High Cohesion vs. Controller} & \text{Controller becomes too fat} & \text{Split into Use-Case Controllers} \\
\hline
\text{Polymorphism vs. Indirection} & \text{Polymorphism requires indirection} & \text{Both apply — they are compatible} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** KTU examiners love asking *"Which GRASP principle is violated by this design? Fix it."* Memorize this 4-row matrix — it lets you answer *any* such question in under 2 minutes.

---

## 3.3 Coupling-Cohesion Numerical Scoring

For a more rigorous exam answer, compute a *quantitative* coupling score:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j\neq i}^{n} \text{dep}(C_i, C_j) \\
\text{where } \text{dep}(C_i, C_j) &= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } C_i \text{ directly references } C_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the Course Registration System with 5 classes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Student} &\rightarrow \text{Course (1), Invoice (1)} \\
\text{CourseCatalog} &\rightarrow \text{Course (1)} \\
\text{Course} &\rightarrow \text{(none)} \\
\text{Invoice} &\rightarrow \text{(none)} \\
\text{Admin} &\rightarrow \text{CourseCatalog (1)} \\
\therefore C_{\text{total}} &= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ directed dependencies}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **low-coupling** design (theoretical maximum for 5 classes is $5 \times 4 = 20$).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OOD Process — End-to-End Block Diagram

The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** captures the entire OOD workflow from raw requirements to final class diagram. This diagram is a high-yield visual answer for any 7-mark *"Explain the steps of OOD"* question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqA[Requirements Document] --> b1[Noun Extraction]
    b1 --> b2[Filter Candidate Classes]
    b2 --> b3[Identify Attributes]
    b3 --> b4[Identify Methods via Information Expert]
    b4 --> b5[Establish Relationships]
    b5 --> b6[Apply GRASP Principles]
    b6 --> b7[Apply SOLID Refinement]
    b7 --> b8[Select Appropriate Design Patterns]
    b8 --> b9[Draw Class Diagram]
    b9 --> b10[Compute Coupling and Cohesion Metrics]
    b10 --> b11{Design Quality Acceptable?}
    b11 -- No --> b6
    b11 -- Yes --> b12[Final Object Model]
    b12 --> b13[Hand-off to OOP Implementation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** from `Design Quality Acceptable?` back to `Apply GRASP Principles`. This embodies the **iterative nature** of OOD — you never "finish" in one pass; you refine until coupling is minimized and cohesion maximized.

---

## 4.2 Class Diagram — Course Registration System

The following Mermaid class diagram visualizes the final design produced in §3.1.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class User {
        -userId : String
        -name : String
        -email : String
        +login() : boolean
    }
    class Student {
        -outstandingDues : double
        -registeredCourses : List~Course~
        +register(course : Course) : boolean
        +viewTranscript() : void
    }
    class Admin {
        -adminId : String
        -role : String
        +addCourse(course : Course) : void
        +removeCourse(course : Course) : void
    }
    class Course {
        -courseId : String
        -title : String
        -credits : int
        -capacity : int
        +isAvailable() : boolean
    }
    class CourseCatalog {
        -courses : List~Course~
        +addCourse(course : Course) : void
        +search(keyword : String) : List~Course~
    }
    class Invoice {
        -invoiceId : String
        -amount : double
        -date : Date
        -status : String
        +calculateTotal() : double
    }
    class IStudentRepository {
        <<interface>>
        +save(student : Student) : void
        +findById(id : String) : Student
    }
    class MySQLStudentRepository {
        +save(student : Student) : void
        +findById(id : String) : Student
    }
    User <|-- Student
    User <|-- Admin
    CourseCatalog "1" *-- "many" Course : contains
    Student "1" --> "many" Invoice : generates
    Student "many" --> "many" Course : registers
    Admin --> CourseCatalog : manages
    IStudentRepository <|.. MySQLStudentRepository : implements
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-Ready Description:** *"The diagram shows `User` as an abstract base class generalized by `Student` and `Admin` (open-triangle arrow). `CourseCatalog` is composed of `Course` objects (filled diamond — strong lifecycle ownership). `Student` generates multiple `Invoice` objects (association). `IStudentRepository` is an interface implemented by `MySQLStudentRepository`, exemplifying the **Dependency Inversion Principle**."*

---

## 4.3 SOLID Principles — Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOLID[Five SOLID Principles]
        S[Single Responsibility Principle]
        O[Open Closed Principle]
        L[Liskov Substitution Principle]
        I[Interface Segregation Principle]
        D[Dependency Inversion Principle]
    end
    S --> SRP[One class one reason to change]
    O --> OCP[Extend without modifying]
    L --> LSP[Subtypes substitutable for base types]
    I --> ISP[Many specific interfaces over one general]
    D --> DIP[Depend on abstractions not concretions]
```

---

## 4.4 Coupling vs. Cohesion — Inverse Relationship Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Cohesion] --> B[Better Design]
    C[Low Coupling] --> B
    D[Low Cohesion] --> E[Poor Design]
    F[High Coupling] --> E
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Visual Memory Aid:** Draw this **quadrant** on your answer sheet whenever the question touches on design quality. Examiners reward even a small diagram in a 3-mark question — it shows *systematic thinking*.
