# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> **Define Object Oriented Design. Differentiate between OOD and OOP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | OOD | OOP |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Design phase where the system is decomposed into interacting objects with well-defined responsibilities. | Implementation phase where the design is realized using a programming language (Java, C++). |
| **Focus** | *How* the system will fulfill use-cases (structure + behavior). | *Writing* classes, inheritance, polymorphism in code. |
| **Output** | Class diagram, interaction diagrams, design patterns. | Executable, tested source code. |
| **Tools** | UML, design principles (GRASP, SOLID). | IDEs, compilers, debuggers. |
| **When** | After OOA, before coding. | After OOD is approved. |

OOD is a **conceptual, language-independent activity**, whereas OOP is **language-specific and concrete**. (Full 3 marks awarded for the contrast table + the conceptual sentence.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **List the three pillars of OOD and give one-line definitions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Encapsulation** — Bundling data and methods that operate on that data inside one unit, with controlled access via access modifiers.
2. **Inheritance** — Mechanism by which a child class acquires attributes and methods of a parent class, supporting generalization and specialization.
3. **Polymorphism** — The ability of different objects to respond to the same message in different ways, enabling flexible behavior substitution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
> **(a)** Explain the **GRASP principles** used in Object Oriented Design. Discuss any **five** principles with examples. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is **coupling and cohesion**? Explain the different types with examples. State the relationship between good design, low coupling, and high cohesion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)** is a set of nine guidelines introduced by **Craig Larman** to systematically assign responsibilities to classes and objects during OOD. Five key principles are:

1. **Information Expert**
   - *Definition:* Assign a responsibility to the class that has the information necessary to fulfill it.
   - *Example:* In a billing system, the method `calculateTotal()` belongs to the `Order` class because `Order` holds the line items and quantities.
2. **Creator**
   - *Definition:* Assign class B the responsibility to create instances of class A if B contains or aggregates A.
   - *Example:* `ShoppingCart` creates `Order` objects because a cart aggregates orders.
3. **Controller**
   - *Definition:* Use a dedicated controller class to handle system events from the UI layer.
   - *Example:* `CheckoutController` handles the "Place Order" use-case, instead of putting logic inside the UI button.
4. **Low Coupling**
   - *Definition:* Minimize dependencies between classes so changes in one class do not ripple through others.
   - *Example:* A `PaymentService` interface decouples `OrderProcessor` from specific gateways (Stripe, PayPal).
5. **High Cohesion**
   - *Definition:* Keep each class focused on a single, well-defined purpose.
   - *Example:* A `UserAuth` class handles only authentication; user-profile editing is in a separate class.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Coupling** is the measure of *interdependence* between modules. **Cohesion** is the measure of how closely related the responsibilities of a *single* module are.

**Types of Cohesion (worst → best):**
Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional.

**Types of Coupling (worst → best):**
Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data.

**Relationship with Good Design:**
$$
\text{Good Design} = (\text{Low Coupling}) \wedge (\text{High Cohesion})
$$

Good design aims to **minimize coupling** so that modules can be modified independently, and **maximize cohesion** so each module has a clear, single purpose. These two qualities reinforce each other — a class with high cohesion tends to need fewer external collaborators (low coupling), and loosely coupled classes can be re-tested in isolation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
> **(a)** Explain the **SOLID principles** with examples. How do they improve software design? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Design an **Object Oriented model** (class diagram) for a **Hospital Management System** with classes such as `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Appointment`, `Ward`, and `Bill`. Show relationships clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**SOLID** is an acronym coined by **Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob)** for five design principles that make software more understandable, flexible, and maintainable.

1. **S — Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**
   - *Meaning:* A class should have exactly one reason to change.
   - *Example:* Instead of a `Patient` class that both stores data and prints reports, split into `Patient` and `PatientReportPrinter`.

2. **O — Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**
   - *Meaning:* A class should be open for extension but closed for modification.
   - *Example:* To add a new payment method, create a new subclass of `PaymentMethod`; do not edit the existing `PaymentProcessor`.

3. **L — Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)**
   - *Meaning:* Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering correctness.
   - *Example:* If `Bird` has `fly()`, then `Penguin extends Bird` violates LSP because a penguin cannot fly.

4. **I — Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)**
   - *Meaning:* Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface.
   - *Example:* Split `IHospitalStaff` into `IDoctor` and `INurse` so a nurse class does not implement doctor-specific methods.

5. **D — Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)**
   - *Meaning:* High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions.
   - *Example:* `WardAllocationService` depends on `IInventoryRepository` interface, not on `MySQLInventoryRepository` directly.

**How they improve design:**
They reduce coupling, raise cohesion, make the code easier to test (via mock interfaces), and ease future change. They align with the **Open-Closed** philosophy: code should be added, not edited.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
        -personId : String
        -name : String
        -contact : String
    }
    class Patient {
        -patientId : String
        -dateOfBirth : Date
        -medicalHistory : String
        +bookAppointment(d : Doctor, dt : Date)
    }
    class Doctor {
        -doctorId : String
        -specialization : String
        +prescribe(p : Patient)
    }
    class Nurse {
        -nurseId : String
        -shift : String
    }
    class Appointment {
        -appointmentId : String
        -date : Date
        -status : String
        +cancel()
    }
    class Ward {
        -wardId : String
        -type : String
        -capacity : int
    }
    class Bill {
        -billId : String
        -amount : double
        +generate()
    }
    Person <|-- Patient
    Person <|-- Doctor
    Person <|-- Nurse
    Patient "1" --> "many" Appointment : books
    Doctor "1" --> "many" Appointment : handles
    Ward "1" o-- "many" Patient : admits
    Patient "1" --> "many" Bill : receives
```

**Relationships Explanation:**
- `Person` is an abstract base — generalization with `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Nurse`.
- `Patient` *books* `Appointment`; `Doctor` *handles* `Appointment` — both associations.
- `Ward` *admits* `Patient` — aggregation (hollow diamond; patients exist outside the ward too).
- `Patient` *receives* `Bill` — one-to-many association.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on OOD questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing code instead of design** — OOD questions require *diagrams and principles*, not Java/C++ snippets. Writing code costs you at least **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing OOD with OOP** — If the question asks for design and you write only class definitions without relationships and principles, you lose marks for missing the *design* aspect.
> 3. **Skipping the UML notation** — Always state the *type* of relationship (association, aggregation, composition, generalization) explicitly. Examiners award marks for correct terminology.
> 4. **Naming without explaining** — Saying *"SRP = Single Responsibility"* without an example gets only 1 of 3 marks. You must *apply* it.
> 5. **No metrics, no proof** — In 14-mark questions, including *coupling/cohesion analysis* or *GRASP conflict resolution* differentiates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OOD is a design-time activity; OOP is an implementation-time activity.**
- The three pillars: **Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism** — know one-line definitions and Java-style examples.
- **Abstraction** (often the unspoken 4th pillar) is achieved via abstract classes and interfaces.
- **Good Design = Low Coupling + High Cohesion.**
- Coupling spectrum: Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data (best).
- Cohesion spectrum: Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional (best).
- **GRASP** = 9 principles; KTU focuses on **Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Pure Fabrication, Indirection, Protected Variations**.
- **SOLID** = SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP — each requires a real-world example in the answer.
- **UML Relationships**: Association (solid line), Aggregation (hollow diamond), Composition (filled diamond), Generalization (hollow triangle), Dependency (dashed arrow), Realization (dashed triangle).
- **Design Pattern triggers**: Use Strategy when behavior varies by type; use Observer when state changes need to be broadcast; use Factory when creation logic is complex; use Façade when a unified interface to a subsystem is needed.
- **Stability formula**: $I = e_c / (e_c + e_a)$ — keep $I$ moderate for balanced packages.
- **Main Sequence Distance**: $D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert$ — aim for $D \approx 0$ in healthy packages.
- **Common exam formats**: *"Explain GRASP principles with examples"* (7 marks); *"Draw class diagram for ___ system"* (7 marks); *"Discuss SOLID with examples"* (7 marks); full 14-mark combined questions with internal choice.
- **Always end** a 14-mark answer with a **metrics evaluation** or a **principle-based justification** of your design — this secures the last 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study of Automated Fire Alarm System. Object Modeling Using Unified Modeling Language (UML) - Basic Object-Oriented Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study of Automated Fire Alarm System. Object Modeling Using Unified Modeling Language (UML) - Basic Object-Oriented Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Automated Fire Alarm System — The Real World Object

An **Automated Fire Alarm System** is a safety-critical, real-time embedded system designed to detect the early signatures of fire (smoke, heat, gas, flame) and automatically initiate alarm and suppression actions (audible sirens, visual strobes, sprinkler activation, emergency notifications) without human intervention.

In the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** paradigm, we do not treat the system as a monolithic block of code. Instead, we decompose it into **objects** that interact with each other. Each physical entity in the real world (sensor, alarm bell, control panel) becomes a software object with **attributes** (state) and **methods** (behavior).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The case study methodology expects students to (i) identify objects from the problem statement, (ii) classify them, (iii) determine attributes & operations, and (iv) establish relationships — this sequence is the standard KTU valuation scheme for OOP descriptive questions.

## 1.2 What is UML?

**Unified Modeling Language (UML)** is a standardized, graphical, general-purpose visual modeling language standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It is used to **specify, visualize, construct, and document** the artifacts of a software-intensive system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU board standard):** "UML is a standardized general-purpose modeling language in the field of object-oriented software engineering. It provides a standard way to visualize the design of a system."

### 1.3 Basic Object-Oriented Concepts — The Seven Pillars

| # | Concept | One-Line KTU Definition |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Object** | A real-world entity with state and behavior. |
| 2 | **Class** | A blueprint/template that defines the structure of objects. |
| 3 | **Encapsulation** | Wrapping data and methods into a single unit and hiding internal details. |
| 4 | **Abstraction** | Hiding complex implementation and exposing only essential features. |
| 5 | **Inheritance** | Mechanism by which one class acquires properties of another. |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | Ability of a message to take different forms based on the receiver. |
| 7 | **Modularity** | Decomposition of a system into smaller, manageable modules. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** **"O-C-E-A-I-P-M"** → Objects, Classes, Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Modularity.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Fire Alarm System as a Smart Building

Imagine a **modern hospital building**. Inside it:

- Each **smoke detector** is an *object*. It has a state ("Normal" or "Alarm"), and a behavior ("sense smoke" and "send signal").
- All smoke detectors are built from the **same blueprint** — that blueprint is the **Class** `SmokeSensor`.
- The detector's internal circuitry is hidden behind a cover (you press a "test" button, but cannot see the circuit) — this is **Encapsulation**.
- The detector shows only a green/red LED to the outside world, hiding how it actually works — this is **Abstraction**.
- A specialized "Heat + Smoke" detector is a kind of detector → it **inherits** from both `HeatSensor` and `SmokeSensor`.
- The "Test" button on a detector, the "Reset" button on the control panel, and the "Silence" button on the alarm all *initiate an action* — when the same command "activate" is given, each device responds differently — this is **Polymorphism**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU answers, always begin a case study solution with a sentence: *"After analyzing the problem statement, the following objects/classes are identified..."* — this guarantees the examiner sees your analytical methodology and rewards full marks.

## 1.5 Object-Orientation vs Procedure-Orientation

| Feature | Procedure-Oriented | Object-Oriented |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Primary unit | Function | Object |
| Data treatment | Passed between functions | Encapsulated within objects |
| Reusability | Low (function libraries) | High (inheritance & polymorphism) |
| Real-world mapping | Weak | Direct (objects ↔ entities) |
| Security | Data is exposed | Data is hidden (private/protected) |
| Example languages | C, Pascal, Fortran | Java, C++, Python, C# |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the Fire Alarm System — Object Identification

The first step in any OOP case study is **object identification** (also called *noun identification* in structured analysis). Reading the problem statement, we extract all **nouns** and filter them to find candidate objects.

### Nouns in the Fire Alarm Problem:
- *Building, Smoke, Heat, Sensor, Detector, Control Unit, Alarm, Siren, Sprinkler, Display, Power Supply, Operator, Fire Brigade, Door, Vent, Battery*

### Filtering — Apply the "**RBC Criteria**":
1. **R**etain (relevant to system scope)
2. **B**oundary (must have clear boundary)
3. **C**andidate (must have behavior)

After filtering, the **candidate classes** for the Fire Alarm System are:

| Candidate Class | Justification |
|-----------------|---------------|
| `Sensor` (abstract) | Has state (reading value) and behavior (sense, calibrate) |
| `SmokeSensor` | Specialized type of Sensor |
| `HeatSensor` | Specialized type of Sensor |
| `ControlUnit` | Central decision-maker — has methods like `processSignal()`, `triggerAlarm()` |
| `Alarm` | Has state (ON/OFF) and behavior (sound, stop) |
| `Sprinkler` | Has state and behavior (activate, deactivate) |
| `Display` | Shows system status to operator |
| `Battery` | Power source — has state (charge level) |
| `Building` / `Zone` | Spatial container for sensors |

## 2.2 Three Core OOP Concepts — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Encapsulation — The "Data Hiding Vault"

Encapsulation has **two tightly coupled aspects**:

1. **Packaging:** Grouping data (attributes) and the functions (methods) that operate on that data into a single unit — the **class**.
2. **Hiding:** Restricting direct external access to some of the object's components, using access specifiers.

**Access Specifiers in C++ / Java:**

| Specifier | Visibility | KTU Notation |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| `private` | Accessible only within the class | `-` |
| `protected` | Accessible within class & derived classes | `#` |
| `public` | Accessible from anywhere | `+` |

### 2.2.2 Abstraction — The "User's Black Box"

Abstraction is achieved through two mechanisms:
- **Abstract Classes** (cannot be instantiated, may have pure virtual functions)
- **Interfaces** (Java) / **Pure Abstract Classes** (C++)

In the Fire Alarm system, `Sensor` could be an **abstract class** with a pure virtual method `readValue()` — every concrete sensor (`SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`) must override this.

### 2.2.3 Inheritance — The "Family Tree"

Inheritance models the **"is-a"** relationship. There are **five types**:

| Type | Description | Fire Alarm Example |
|------|-------------|--------------------|
| Single | One parent, one child | `SmokeSensor` extends `Sensor` |
| Multilevel | Chain of inheritance | `Sensor` → `SmokeSensor` → `AddressableSmokeSensor` |
| Hierarchical | One parent, many children | `Sensor` → {`SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `GasSensor`} |
| Multiple | Many parents, one child | `Multisensor` extends `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor` |
| Hybrid | Combination of above | Combination of hierarchical + multiple |

## 2.3 UML Diagram Types — The KTU-Critical Subset

UML defines **14 types of diagrams** divided into two categories:

| Category | Diagram Type | KTU Relevance |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Structural** | Class Diagram | ⭐⭐⭐ (Most asked) |
| **Structural** | Object Diagram | ⭐⭐ |
| **Structural** | Component Diagram | ⭐ |
| **Structural** | Deployment Diagram | ⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Use-Case Diagram | ⭐⭐⭐ (Most asked) |
| **Behavioral** | Activity Diagram | ⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | State-Chart Diagram | ⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Sequence Diagram | ⭐⭐⭐ (Most asked) |
| **Behavioral** | Collaboration Diagram | ⭐ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — UML Notation Cheat Sheet

| Notation | Symbol | Meaning | Example in Fire Alarm |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------------|
| Class | `+ ClassName` | Rectangle with 3 compartments | `+ SmokeSensor` |
| Visibility | `+ # - ~` | Public / Protected / Private / Package | `- smokeLevel: float` |
| Attribute | `name: type` | Instance variable | `- threshold: int` |
| Operation | `name(args): return` | Method | `+ readValue(): float` |
| Inheritance | `▷` (hollow triangle) | "is-a" | `SmokeSensor ▷ Sensor` |
| Realization | `▷` (dashed) | Implements interface | `SmokeSensor -▷ ISensor` |
| Association | `——` | Uses / knows | `ControlUnit —— Sensor` |
| Aggregation | `◇——` (hollow diamond) | Weak "has-a" | `Building ◇—— Sensor` |
| Composition | `◆——` (filled diamond) | Strong "has-a" | `ControlUnit ◆—— Alarm` |
| Multiplicity | `1, *, 1..*, 0..1` | Cardinality | `Building 1 —— * Sensor` |
| Dependency | `- - ▷` (dashed) | "uses" (temporary) | `Display - - ▷ ControlUnit` |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Architecture:** UML is the **lingua franca** for communicating software design across teams in industry.
- **Code Generation:** Tools like *Rational Rose, Enterprise Architect, StarUML, Visual Paradigm* can auto-generate code from UML class diagrams.
- **Reverse Engineering:** UML can be reconstructed from existing code for documentation.
- **Safety-Critical Domains:** In fire alarm, avionics, and medical systems, UML is mandated by standards like **ISO 26262, DO-178C, IEC 61508** because it forces rigorous specification before code is written.
- **Reverse Tracing:** OOP's encapsulation makes bug localization easier in large industrial systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The 7-Step OOP Case Study Methodology (KTU Standard)

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step methodology** examiners expect when a case study is asked in the exam. Every step is explicitly broken down.

### **STEP 1 — Problem Statement Analysis**

**Input:** The narrative of the Fire Alarm System.
**Action:** Read carefully, identify the **scope** and **actors**.

> *Example problem statement:* "A building has multiple smoke and heat sensors distributed across floors. When smoke density crosses a threshold, the sensor sends a signal to a central control unit. The control unit triggers a loud siren, activates sprinklers in the affected zone, and displays the zone number on an LCD. An operator can acknowledge and reset the system."

**Actors identified:**
- `Sensor` (system entity)
- `ControlUnit` (system entity)
- `Operator` (human actor)
- `Building` (environment)

### **STEP 2 — Object Identification (Noun Extraction)**

Extract all nouns → filter using RBC criteria.

$$
\text{Candidate Objects} = \text{All Nouns} - \text{Overlapping Nouns} - \text{Outside Scope Nouns}
$$

**Candidates retained:** `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `ControlUnit`, `Alarm`, `Sprinkler`, `Display`, `Operator`, `Building`, `Zone`, `Battery`.

### **STEP 3 — Class Identification**

Group similar candidate objects into classes. Decide on:
- **Concrete class** (can be instantiated) — e.g., `SmokeSensor`
- **Abstract class** (cannot be instantiated) — e.g., `Sensor`

**Final class list:**

$$
\text{Classes} = \{\text{Sensor, SmokeSensor, HeatSensor, ControlUnit, Alarm, Sprinkler, Display, Battery, Building, Zone, Operator}\}
$$

### **STEP 4 — Attribute Identification (Data Members)**

For each class, determine the **state variables**.

| Class | Attributes (with Visibility) |
|-------|------------------------------|
| `Sensor` (abstract) | `- sensorID: String`, `- location: String`, `- status: String`, `- threshold: float` |
| `SmokeSensor` | `- smokeLevel: float`, `- calibrationFactor: float` |
| `HeatSensor` | `- temperature: float`, `- rateOfRise: float` |
| `ControlUnit` | `- activeAlarms: List`, `- batteryLevel: float`, `- mode: String` |
| `Alarm` | `- volume: int`, `- frequency: int`, `- isActive: boolean` |
| `Sprinkler` | `- waterPressure: float`, `- zoneID: int` |
| `Display` | `- screenSize: int`, `- currentMessage: String` |
| `Building` | `- address: String`, `- numFloors: int` |
| `Zone` | `- zoneNumber: int`, `- floorNumber: int` |
| `Operator` | `- operatorID: String`, `- name: String` |
| `Battery` | `- voltage: float`, `- chargePercent: int` |

### **STEP 5 — Method Identification (Operations)**

For each class, determine the **behaviors**.

| Class | Methods |
|-------|---------|
| `Sensor` | `+ readValue(): float` *(abstract)*, `+ calibrate()`, `+ sendSignal()` |
| `SmokeSensor` | `+ readValue(): float` *(override)*, `+ detectSmoke(): boolean` |
| `HeatSensor` | `+ readValue(): float` *(override)*, `+ detectHeat(): boolean` |
| `ControlUnit` | `+ processSignal(s: Sensor)`, `+ triggerAlarm()`, `+ activateSprinkler(zone)`, `+ resetSystem()`, `+ notifyOperator()` |
| `Alarm` | `+ sound()`, `+ silence()` |
| `Sprinkler` | `+ activate()`, `+ deactivate()` |
| `Display` | `+ showMessage(msg: String)`, `+ clearScreen()` |
| `Building` | `+ getZone(zoneID): Zone` |
| `Battery` | `+ getCharge(): int`, `+ isLow(): boolean` |

### **STEP 6 — Relationship Identification**

Apply the three relationship tests:

$$
\text{Test 1: Is-A?} \Rightarrow \text{Use Inheritance}
$$
$$
\text{Test 2: Has-A (strong/whole-part)?} \Rightarrow \text{Use Composition}
$$
$$
\text{Test 3: Has-A (weak/association)?} \Rightarrow \text{Use Aggregation/Association}
$$

**Relationships derived for Fire Alarm System:**

| Source Class | Target Class | Relationship | Multiplicity | Reasoning |
|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|-----------|
| `SmokeSensor` | `Sensor` | Inheritance (is-a) | — | A smoke sensor *is a* sensor |
| `HeatSensor` | `Sensor` | Inheritance (is-a) | — | A heat sensor *is a* sensor |
| `ControlUnit` | `Sensor` | Composition | 1 to * | Control unit *owns* sensors |
| `ControlUnit` | `Alarm` | Composition | 1 to 1 | Control unit *has-a* alarm |
| `ControlUnit` | `Sprinkler` | Aggregation | 1 to * | Sprinklers exist independently |
| `ControlUnit` | `Display` | Composition | 1 to 1 | Display is integral to control unit |
| `ControlUnit` | `Battery` | Aggregation | 1 to 1 | Battery can be replaced |
| `Building` | `Zone` | Composition | 1 to * | Building is composed of zones |
| `Zone` | `Sensor` | Aggregation | 1 to * | Zone contains sensors |
| `Operator` | `ControlUnit` | Association | * to 1 | Operator uses control unit |

### **STEP 7 — UML Diagram Construction**

Construct the **Class Diagram**, **Use-Case Diagram**, and **Sequence Diagram** using the data assembled in steps 1–6. (See Section 4 for the rendered diagrams.)

## 3.2 Mathematical Representation of OOP Cardinality

For aggregation/composition, multiplicity is a **range constraint**:

$$
\text{Multiplicity} : \mathbb{N}_0 \rightarrow \mathbb{N}_0 \cup \{*\}, \quad * \equiv \infty
$$

**Common notations in UML:**

| Notation | Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| `1` | Exactly one | $\vert x \vert = 1$ |
| `0..1` | Zero or one | $0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1$ |
| `*` | Zero or more | $0 \le \vert x \vert \le \infty$ |
| `1..*` | One or more | $1 \le \vert x \vert \le \infty$ |
| `n..m` | Range | $n \le \vert x \vert \le m$ |

## 3.3 Java Implementation Skeleton (For Reference)

The following skeleton maps the design directly to Java code — useful for KTU lab exams.

```java
// Abstract base class
abstract class Sensor {
    protected String sensorID;
    protected String location;
    protected float threshold;

    public Sensor(String id, String loc, float th) {
        this.sensorID = id;
        this.location = loc;
        this.threshold = th;
    }

    public abstract float readValue();
    public abstract boolean detect();

    public void sendSignal(ControlUnit cu) {
        cu.processSignal(this);
    }
}

// Concrete subclass
class SmokeSensor extends Sensor {
    private float smokeLevel;

    public SmokeSensor(String id, String loc, float th) {
        super(id, loc, th);
    }

    @Override
    public float readValue() {
        return smokeLevel;
    }

    @Override
    public boolean detect() {
        return smokeLevel >= threshold;
    }
}

// Control unit
class ControlUnit {
    private List<Sensor> sensors = new ArrayList<>();
    private Alarm alarm = new Alarm();
    private Display display = new Display();

    public void processSignal(Sensor s) {
        if (s.detect()) {
            alarm.sound();
            display.showMessage("ALARM: " + s.location);
        }
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The above 7-step methodology is the *exact* order in which examiners award marks. Skipping any step costs you 1–2 marks. The steps themselves form a **5-mark sub-question** ("Identify classes and draw class diagram") and a **7-mark sub-question** ("Full OOP analysis with diagrams").

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use-Case Diagram — Fire Alarm System

The use-case diagram captures **actor interactions** with the system. In a fire alarm system, the actors are: *Operator, Sensor, Fire Brigade, Maintenance Staff, Control Unit (system internal actor)*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    operator1([Operator])
    brigade1([Fire Brigade])
    maint1([Maintenance Staff])
    sensor1([Smoke Sensor])
    heat1([Heat Sensor])

    usecaseA([Detect Fire])
    usecaseB([Trigger Alarm])
    usecaseC([Activate Sprinkler])
    usecaseD([Display Zone Info])
    usecaseE([Acknowledge Alarm])
    usecaseF([Reset System])
    usecaseG([Self Test])
    usecaseH([Replace Battery])
    usecaseI([Notify Fire Brigade])

    operator1 --- usecaseE
    operator1 --- usecaseF
    operator1 --- usecaseG
    maint1 --- usecaseH
    brigade1 --- usecaseI

    sensor1 --- usecaseA
    heat1 --- usecaseA
    usecaseA --- usecaseB
    usecaseB --- usecaseC
    usecaseB --- usecaseD
    usecaseB --- usecaseE
    usecaseB --- usecaseI
    usecaseE --- usecaseF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- *Operator* interacts with **Acknowledge Alarm** and **Reset System**.
- *Sensors* and *Heat Sensors* participate in **Detect Fire**.
- *Fire Brigade* receives the **Notify Fire Brigade** use case.

## 4.2 Class Diagram — Fire Alarm System (Most Important for KTU)

The class diagram below uses **Mermaid `classDiagram`** syntax to render the OOP design.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Sensor {
        <<abstract>>
        - sensorID : String
        - location : String
        - threshold : float
        - status : String
        + readValue() float
        + detect() boolean
        + calibrate() void
        + sendSignal(cu: ControlUnit) void
    }

    class SmokeSensor {
        - smokeLevel : float
        - calibrationFactor : float
        + readValue() float
        + detect() boolean
    }

    class HeatSensor {
        - temperature : float
        - rateOfRise : float
        + readValue() float
        + detect() boolean
    }

    class ControlUnit {
        - unitID : String
        - activeAlarms : List~String~
        - mode : String
        - batteryLevel : float
        + processSignal(s: Sensor) void
        + triggerAlarm() void
        + activateSprinkler(zone: Zone) void
        + resetSystem() void
        + notifyOperator() void
    }

    class Alarm {
        - volume : int
        - frequency : int
        - isActive : boolean
        + sound() void
        + silence() void
    }

    class Sprinkler {
        - waterPressure : float
        - zoneID : int
        - isActive : boolean
        + activate() void
        + deactivate() void
    }

    class Display {
        - screenSize : int
        - currentMessage : String
        + showMessage(msg: String) void
        + clearScreen() void
    }

    class Battery {
        - voltage : float
        - chargePercent : int
        + getCharge() int
        + isLow() boolean
    }

    class Building {
        - address : String
        - numFloors : int
        + getZone(zoneID: int) Zone
    }

    class Zone {
        - zoneNumber : int
        - floorNumber : int
        + getSensors() List~Sensor~
    }

    class Operator {
        - operatorID : String
        - name : String
        + acknowledge() void
        + reset() void
    }

    Sensor <|-- SmokeSensor : is-a
    Sensor <|-- HeatSensor : is-a
    ControlUnit "1" *-- "1" Alarm : owns
    ControlUnit "1" *-- "1" Display : owns
    ControlUnit "1" o-- "1" Battery : uses
    ControlUnit "1" o-- "*" Sprinkler : controls
    ControlUnit "1" o-- "*" Sensor : manages
    Building "1" *-- "*" Zone : contains
    Zone "1" o-- "*" Sensor : groups
    Operator "*" --> "1" ControlUnit : operates
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU mark-winning explanations):**

- `Sensor <|-- SmokeSensor` ⇒ **SmokeSensor inherits from Sensor** (is-a relationship)
- `ControlUnit "1" *-- "1" Alarm` ⇒ **Composition** — Control unit owns exactly 1 Alarm; if control unit is destroyed, the alarm is destroyed
- `ControlUnit "1" o-- "1" Battery` ⇒ **Aggregation** — Control unit has a Battery, but the battery can exist independently (replaceable)
- `Building "1" *-- "*" Zone` ⇒ A building contains 1..* zones (whole-part composition)
- `Zone "1" o-- "*" Sensor` ⇒ A zone groups many sensors (aggregation)

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Fire Detection Workflow

The sequence diagram captures the **time-ordered message exchange** between objects when a fire is detected.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SmokeSensor1 as SmokeSensor
    participant HeatSensor1 as HeatSensor
    participant ControlUnit1 as ControlUnit
    participant Alarm1 as Alarm
    participant Sprinkler1 as Sprinkler
    participant Display1 as Display
    participant Operator1 as Operator

    SmokeSensor1->>ControlUnit1: processSignal(smoke detected)
    HeatSensor1->>ControlUnit1: processSignal(heat detected)
    ControlUnit1->>ControlUnit1: validate threshold
    ControlUnit1->>Alarm1: sound()
    Alarm1-->>ControlUnit1: alarm ON
    ControlUnit1->>Sprinkler1: activate(zone 3)
    Sprinkler1-->>ControlUnit1: sprinkler ON
    ControlUnit1->>Display1: showMessage(zone 3 fire)
    Display1-->>ControlUnit1: message displayed
    ControlUnit1->>Operator1: notify(alarm triggered)
    Operator1->>ControlUnit1: acknowledge()
    Operator1->>ControlUnit1: reset()
    ControlUnit1->>Alarm1: silence()
    ControlUnit1->>Sprinkler1: deactivate()
    ControlUnit1->>Display1: clearScreen()
```

## 4.4 State-Chart Diagram — Lifecycle of a Sensor

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Detecting : start monitoring
    Detecting --> Idle : value normal
    Detecting --> Alarm : value exceeds threshold
    Alarm --> Detecting : reset by operator
    Detecting --> Fault : sensor malfunction
    Alarm --> Fault : hardware failure
    Fault --> Maintenance : service requested
    Maintenance --> Idle : repaired
    Maintenance --> [*] : decommissioned
```

**Reading the diagram:** A sensor cycles through **Idle → Detecting → Alarm** during normal operation. On any internal hardware failure, it transitions to **Fault**, requiring **Maintenance** before returning to **Idle**.

## 4.5 Activity Diagram — Operator's Reset Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Alarm Triggered]) --> ack1[Operator Acknowledges Alarm]
    ack1 --> decision1{Threat Cleared?}
    decision1 -- No --> brigade1[Call Fire Brigade]
    brigade1 --> wait1[Wait for Resolution]
    wait1 --> decision1
    decision1 -- Yes --> reset1[Press Reset Button]
    reset1 --> silence1[Silence Alarm]
    silence1 --> deactivate1[Deactivate Sprinkler]
    deactivate1 --> clear1[Clear Display]
    clear1 --> log1[Log Event in Database]
    log1 --> end1([System Returns to Normal])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the following object-oriented concepts: (i) Object (ii) Class (iii) Encapsulation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Object:** An object is a real-world entity that has **state** (data/attributes) and **behavior** (functions/methods). In a Fire Alarm System, an example of an object is a `SmokeSensor` with state `smokeLevel` and behavior `readValue()`.

**(ii) Class:** A class is a **blueprint or template** that defines the structure (attributes) and behavior (methods) common to a group of similar objects. Example: `SmokeSensor` class is the blueprint for all smoke-sensor objects.

**(iii) Encapsulation:** Encapsulation is the mechanism of **binding data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class)** and **restricting external access** to some components through access specifiers (`private`, `protected`, `public`). Example: The internal `smokeLevel` of a `SmokeSensor` is marked `private` and can only be modified through the public `calibrate()` method.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Aggregation and Composition with an example from a Fire Alarm System.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Aggregation (Weak "Has-A") | Composition (Strong "Has-A") |
|---------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| UML symbol | Hollow diamond `◇` | Filled diamond `◆` |
| Lifetime | Child can exist without parent | Child cannot exist without parent |
| Example | `ControlUnit ◇— Battery` | `ControlUnit ◆— Alarm` |
| Reasoning | Battery can be replaced | Alarm is destroyed with control unit |

**In a Fire Alarm System:**
- **Composition Example:** A `ControlUnit` *owns* an `Alarm`. If the control unit is destroyed, the alarm cannot function independently — this is composition.
- **Aggregation Example:** A `Building` *has* `Zones`. Zones are physical areas that can be repurposed even if the building's logical control structure changes — this is aggregation.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** typically. Solve the question of your choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Identify the classes, attributes, and methods for an Automated Fire Alarm System. Draw the class diagram. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Class Identification (2 marks):**

Based on the problem statement, the key classes are:
- `Sensor` (abstract superclass)
- `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor` (concrete subclasses)
- `ControlUnit` (central coordinator)
- `Alarm`, `Sprinkler`, `Display` (output devices)
- `Battery`, `Zone`, `Building`, `Operator` (supporting classes)

**Step 2 — Attributes (2 marks):**

| Class | Attributes |
|-------|------------|
| `SmokeSensor` | `smokeLevel`, `threshold`, `location`, `sensorID` |
| `HeatSensor` | `temperature`, `rateOfRise`, `threshold` |
| `ControlUnit` | `activeAlarms`, `mode`, `batteryLevel` |
| `Alarm` | `volume`, `isActive` |
| `Sprinkler` | `waterPressure`, `zoneID`, `isActive` |

**Step 3 — Methods (1 mark):**

| Class | Methods |
|-------|---------|
| `SmokeSensor` | `readValue()`, `detect()`, `calibrate()` |
| `ControlUnit` | `processSignal()`, `triggerAlarm()`, `resetSystem()` |
| `Alarm` | `sound()`, `silence()` |
| `Sprinkler` | `activate()`, `deactivate()` |

**Step 4 — Class Diagram (2 marks):**

(See the Mermaid class diagram in Section 4.2 above for the full diagram. Key points to draw:)

```
+--------------------+        +---------------------+
|      Sensor        |<|-----+|   SmokeSensor       |
|   (abstract)       |        |  - smokeLevel       |
| - sensorID         |        | + readValue()       |
| - threshold        |        | + detect()          |
| + readValue()*     |        +---------------------+
| + detect()*        |        +---------------------+
+--------------------+        |   HeatSensor        |
                              |  - temperature      |
                              | + readValue()       |
                              | + detect()          |
                              +---------------------+
```

#### **(b) Explain the concepts of inheritance and polymorphism with suitable examples from the Fire Alarm System. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Inheritance (3.5 marks):**

Inheritance is the OOP mechanism that allows a new class (**subclass/derived class**) to acquire the properties (attributes) and behaviors (methods) of an existing class (**superclass/base class**). It models the **"is-a"** relationship and promotes **code reuse**.

**Types demonstrated in the Fire Alarm System:**

1. **Single Inheritance:** `SmokeSensor` inherits from `Sensor` (one parent, one child).
2. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** Both `SmokeSensor` and `HeatSensor` inherit from the common parent `Sensor` (one parent, multiple children).
3. **Multilevel Inheritance:** `Sensor` → `SmokeSensor` → `AddressableSmokeSensor` (a specialized sensor with an addressable ID for multi-drop communication).

**Java Code Example:**

```java
class Sensor {
    String location;
    float threshold;

    void calibrate() {
        System.out.println("Calibrating sensor at " + location);
    }
}

class SmokeSensor extends Sensor {
    float smokeLevel;

    @Override
    void calibrate() {
        super.calibrate();
        System.out.println("Smoke-specific calibration applied");
    }
}
```

**Polymorphism (3.5 marks):**

Polymorphism (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) allows the **same interface to invoke different behaviors** depending on the actual object type. It exists in two forms:

1. **Compile-time (Static) Polymorphism** — achieved by **method overloading**:
   - `ControlUnit.processSignal(Sensor s)` and `ControlUnit.processSignal(List<Sensor> sensors)` are two methods with the same name but different signatures.
2. **Run-time (Dynamic) Polymorphism** — achieved by **method overriding**:
   - The abstract method `readValue()` in `Sensor` is overridden differently in `SmokeSensor` (returns smoke density) and `HeatSensor` (returns temperature).
   - When the control unit calls `s.readValue()` polymorphically, the correct overridden version is executed at runtime.

**Fire Alarm Example:**

```java
Sensor s1 = new SmokeSensor(...);
Sensor s2 = new HeatSensor(...);
s1.readValue();  // Calls SmokeSensor.readValue()
s2.readValue();  // Calls HeatSensor.readValue()
```

The same call `readValue()` produces **two different behaviors** — this is polymorphism.

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

#### **(a) What is UML? List and explain any three UML diagrams with reference to the Fire Alarm System. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** UML (Unified Modeling Language) is a standardized, general-purpose visual modeling language developed by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It is used to specify, visualize, construct, and document software systems. UML is **not a programming language** but a way to model software graphically.

**Three Important UML Diagrams:**

**1. Class Diagram (2 marks):**
- A **static structural diagram** that shows the classes, their attributes, methods, and the relationships among them.
- For the Fire Alarm System, the class diagram shows `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `ControlUnit`, `Alarm`, `Sprinkler` with their attributes, methods, and inheritance/association arrows.
- (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2.)

**2. Use-Case Diagram (1.5 marks):**
- A **behavioral diagram** that captures the functional requirements of a system from the actor's perspective.
- For the Fire Alarm System, actors include *Operator, Fire Brigade, Maintenance Staff*. Use cases include *Detect Fire, Trigger Alarm, Acknowledge Alarm, Reset System*.
- (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1.)

**3. Sequence Diagram (1.5 marks):**
- A **behavioral diagram** that emphasizes the **time-ordering of messages** between objects.
- For the Fire Alarm System, it shows the sequence: `SmokeSensor → ControlUnit → Alarm → Sprinkler → Display → Operator`.
- (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3.)

#### **(b) Differentiate between the following with examples: (i) Abstraction vs Encapsulation (ii) Association vs Dependency. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Abstraction vs Encapsulation (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Abstraction | Encapsulation |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Purpose | Hide **implementation complexity**; show only **essential features** | Hide **data**; restrict direct access to internal state |
| Focus | **Design-level** (what to expose) | **Implementation-level** (how to protect) |
| Achieved by | Abstract classes, interfaces | Access specifiers (`private`, `protected`) |
| Example | `Sensor` abstract class with `readValue()` abstract method | `smokeLevel` marked `private` in `SmokeSensor` |

**Fire Alarm Example:**
- **Abstraction:** A `SmokeSensor` exposes only `readValue()` and `detect()` methods. The internal smoke-detection algorithm (ionization chamber, photodetector circuit) is hidden.
- **Encapsulation:** The `smokeLevel` field is `private`; it can be changed only via the public `calibrate()` method.

**(ii) Association vs Dependency (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Association | Dependency |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Strength | Strong, structural relationship | Weak, temporary relationship |
| UML symbol | Solid line `——` | Dashed line `- - ▷` |
| Lifetime | Long-term | Short-term (method parameter) |
| Example | `Operator —— ControlUnit` | `Display - - ▷ ControlUnit` |

**Fire Alarm Example:**
- **Association:** An `Operator` is *associated* with a `ControlUnit` permanently; the operator can interact with it anytime.
- **Dependency:** A `Display` *depends on* `ControlUnit` because the display's `showMessage(msg)` method receives the `ControlUnit` as a parameter temporarily — the display does not hold a long-term reference to it.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — How Marks Are Lost on This Topic:**
> 1. **Skipping the methodology:** Writing only a class diagram without first listing identified classes/attributes/methods loses 2–3 marks. Always show the **analysis** *before* the diagram.
> 2. **Wrong UML symbols:** Students often draw a *filled* diamond for aggregation or use a *solid* line for dependency. Memorize: `◇` = aggregation, `◆` = composition, `- - ▷` = dependency, `——` = association, `▷` = inheritance.
> 3. **Missing multiplicity:** Omitting `1`, `*`, `0..1` notation on the class diagram loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing abstraction with encapsulation:** They are related but distinct. *Abstraction = design (what to show), Encapsulation = implementation (how to protect).*
> 5. **Forgetting `<<abstract>>` stereotype:** When `Sensor` is abstract, it must be shown with `<<abstract>>` notation, or the inheritance hierarchy loses marks.
> 6. **No access specifiers:** Every attribute and method in the class diagram MUST have `+`, `-`, or `#` prefix.
> 7. **Hand-drawn diagram quality:** Diagrams should be drawn with a ruler and pen, with clear compartment separation. Messy diagrams lose 1–2 marks even if logically correct.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **OOP is a paradigm** that models real-world entities as **objects** containing **data (attributes)** and **behavior (methods)**.
- ✅ **Class** = Blueprint; **Object** = Instance.
- ✅ **Encapsulation** = Data hiding using `private`/`protected`/`public` access specifiers; bind data and methods together.
- ✅ **Abstraction** = Hiding implementation complexity using abstract classes or interfaces; show only essential features.
- ✅ **Inheritance** = "is-a" relationship; 5 types: single, multilevel, hierarchical, multiple, hybrid.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** = "many forms"; compile-time via overloading, run-time via overriding.
- ✅ **Modularity** = Decomposing system into smaller independent modules for maintainability.
- ✅ **UML** = Unified Modeling Language; standardized by OMG; **14 diagram types** split into structural and behavioral.
- ✅ **Class diagram** is the most important UML diagram for KTU — must include visibility, attributes, methods, relationships, and multiplicity.
- ✅ **Three critical UML relationships** — Inheritance (▷), Composition (◆), Aggregation (◇); Memorize their symbols.
- ✅ **Fire Alarm Case Study classes:** `Sensor, SmokeSensor, HeatSensor, ControlUnit, Alarm, Sprinkler, Display, Battery, Zone, Building, Operator`.
- ✅ **Methodology for case study answers:** (1) Identify actors → (2) Identify objects → (3) Identify classes → (4) Identify attributes → (5) Identify methods → (6) Identify relationships → (7) Draw diagrams.
- ✅ **Multiplicity notations:** `1, *, 0..1, 1..*, n..m`.
- ✅ **Exam must-includes:** Class diagram, use-case diagram, and at least one behavioral diagram (sequence/activity).
- ✅ **Code reuse via inheritance** is the primary OOP advantage over procedure-oriented programming.
- ✅ **KTU mark scheme reminder:** 14-mark question = 7 + 7. Always answer both sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UML Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** UML Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is UML?

**UML (Unified Modeling Language)** is a standardized, general-purpose visual modeling language used in software engineering to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artifacts of a software system. It was developed by **Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson** (the "Three Amigos") at Rational Software in the **1990s**, and is now maintained by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** UML is NOT a programming language. It is a **visual blueprinting language** used to model object-oriented systems *before*, *during*, or *after* coding. Think of it as the "architect's drawing" of software.

### 1.2 The 13 Official UML 2.5 Diagrams — Classification

UML diagrams are broadly classified into two categories based on their purpose:

| Category | Purpose | Diagrams Covered |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure Diagrams** (Static View) | Show the *static structure* of the system — its classes, objects, components, and deployment. | Class, Object, Component, Deployment, Composite Structure, Package, Profile |
| **Behavior Diagrams** (Dynamic View) | Show the *dynamic behavior* — how objects interact, change state, and the flow of activities. | Use Case, Activity, State Machine, Sequence, Communication, Interaction Overview, Timing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Priority Diagrams (Module 1 Focus):**
> 1. **Class Diagram** (Most important)
> 2. **Object Diagram**
> 3. **Use Case Diagram**
> 4. **Sequence Diagram**
> 5. **Activity Diagram**
> 6. **State Chart Diagram**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"

Imagine you are building a house:

- **Class Diagram** → The **architect's blueprint** showing rooms, doors, windows, and how they connect (static structure).
- **Object Diagram** → A **snapshot** of the house on a particular day, with the actual furniture placed inside.
- **Use Case Diagram** → The **list of things a homeowner can do** (lock door, open window, turn on light) and who does what.
- **Sequence Diagram** → A **time-ordered script** of what happens when a guest rings the doorbell (1. Bell rings → 2. Owner hears → 3. Owner opens).
- **Activity Diagram** → A **flowchart of business processes** like "How does the kitchen prepare a meal?"
- **State Chart Diagram** → Shows the **lifecycle states** of the door: *Closed → Opening → Open → Closing → Closed*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **Modeling**: A simplification of reality used to understand a system.
> - **Notation**: The set of graphical symbols (rectangles, arrows, stick figures).
> - **Meta-model**: The underlying rules that define what UML elements mean and how they relate.
> - **Tool Standard**: UML is the ISO/IEC 19505 standard.

---

## 1.4 The Building Blocks of UML — "Things, Relationships, Diagrams"

Any UML model is built from **three essential ingredients**:

1. **Things** (the vocabulary): Structural, Behavioral, Grouping, Annotational.
2. **Relationships** (the glue): Association, Dependency, Generalization, Realization.
3. **Diagrams** (the sentences): Views that group related things and relationships.

> [!TIP]
> **Why UML for OOP?** Since OOP revolves around **classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism**, UML provides the perfect visual vocabulary to represent these concepts *before* writing a single line of Java/C++/Python code. It bridges the gap between **requirements** and **implementation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & UML Notation Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Types of UML "Things"

### 2.1.1 Structural Things (Nouns of the model)
These are the static parts of a system — they represent concepts or physical elements.

- **Class**: A rectangle divided into 3 compartments (name, attributes, operations).
- **Interface**: A circle (lollipop) or rectangle with `<<interface>>` stereotype.
- **Collaboration**: Dashed ellipse representing a set of cooperating roles.
- **Use Case**: Ellipse describing a sequence of actions.
- **Component**: Rectangle with two small rectangles attached on the side.
- **Node**: A 3D box representing physical hardware.
- **Actor**: Stick figure (outside the system boundary).

### 2.1.2 Behavioral Things (Verbs of the model)
These are the dynamic parts — they represent behavior over time.

- **Interaction**: A message exchanged between objects.
- **State Machine**: A sequence of states an object goes through.

### 2.1.3 Grouping Things
- **Package**: A folder-like rectangle used to organize model elements.

### 2.1.4 Annotational Things
- **Note**: A dog-eared rectangle for comments.

## 2.2 The Four Types of UML Relationships

This is a **high-yield KTU area** — examiners frequently test the difference between these arrows.

| Relationship | Symbol | Meaning | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Association** | Solid line | "uses-a" / structural link between peers | Structural |
| **Directed Association** | Solid line with arrow | "knows-a" (one-way navigation) | Structural |
| **Aggregation** | Empty (hollow) diamond | "has-a" — weak ownership, parts can exist independently | Weak "whole–part" |
| **Composition** | Filled (solid) diamond | "contains-a" — strong ownership, parts die with whole | Strong "whole–part" |
| **Dependency** | Dashed line with arrow | "depends-on" — change in one affects the other | Weakest link |
| **Generalization** | Solid line with **hollow triangle** arrow | "is-a" (inheritance) | Strong |
| **Realization** | Dashed line with **hollow triangle** arrow | "implements" (class realizes interface) | Contractual |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregation vs. Composition — The Classic KTU Question:**
> - **Aggregation (empty diamond ◇):** A *Team* has *Players*. If the team is dissolved, players still exist and may join another team. (Shared, weak ownership.)
> - **Composition (filled diamond ◆):** A *House* has *Rooms*. If the house is destroyed, the rooms are destroyed too. (Exclusive, strong ownership, lifetime dependency.)

### 2.3 UML Notation Cheat Sheet (KTU High-Yield)

| Element | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Class | Rectangle (3 compartments) | Top: Name, Middle: Attributes, Bottom: Operations |
| Abstract Class | Name in *italics* | Cannot be instantiated |
| Interface | Lollipop `○` or `<<interface>>` | Pure abstract contract |
| Visibility `+` | Public | Accessible from anywhere |
| Visibility `-` | Private | Accessible only within the class |
| Visibility `#` | Protected | Accessible in subclasses |
| Visibility `~` | Package | Accessible within the same package |
| Static (underlined) | `_underline_` | Class-level member |
| Multiplicity | `1`, `*`, `1..*`, `0..1` | Number of instances involved |
| Object | Rectangle with **underlined name** | `objectName : ClassName` |
| Actor | Stick figure | External entity (user/system) |
| System Boundary | Large rectangle | Encapsulates the use cases |
| Message (Sync) | Solid arrow ▲ | Caller waits for reply |
| Message (Async) | Open arrow ▶ | Caller does not wait |
| Message (Return) | Dashed arrow | Optional return value |
| Initial State | Filled black circle ● | Start of state machine |
| Final State | Bull's-eye (○ with dot) | End of state machine |
| Decision | Diamond ◇ | Branching in activity flow |
| Fork/Join | Thick horizontal/vertical bar | Parallel execution |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of UML

- **Software Architecture:** Designing the blueprint for large distributed systems.
- **Database Design:** Reverse-engineering ER diagrams from class diagrams.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Tools like *Rational Rose*, *StarUML*, *Visual Paradigm*, *Enterprise Architect* generate UML from code.
- **Requirements Engineering:** Communicating with stakeholders via use case diagrams.
- **Agile & DevOps:** Used in CI/CD pipelines for documentation (e.g., PlantUML in Markdown).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis of Each Major UML Diagram

## 3.1 CLASS DIAGRAM (The Most Important Diagram)

A **Class Diagram** represents the static structure of a system by showing the system's classes, their attributes, operations, and the relationships among objects.

### 3.1.1 Structure of a Class

A class is drawn as a rectangle with **three compartments**:

```
+----------------------+
|      ClassName       |   ← Name compartment
+----------------------+
| - attribute1 : Type  |   ← Attribute compartment
| + attribute2 : Type  |
+----------------------+
| + operation1() : Type|   ← Operation compartment
| - operation2() : void|
+----------------------+
```

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — "University Management System"

**Step 1: Identify the classes.** *Student, Professor, Course, Department*.

**Step 2: Identify attributes and methods.**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| Student | `- name : String`, `- rollNo : int`, `- marks : float` | `+ register() : void`, `+ payFee() : void` |
| Professor | `- name : String`, `- empId : int`, `- salary : double` | `+ teach() : void`, `+ evaluate() : void` |
| Course | `- courseCode : String`, `- title : String`, `- credits : int` | `+ addStudent() : void` |

**Step 3: Identify relationships.**
- `Professor` *teaches* `Course` → Association with multiplicity `1 .. *` to `1 .. *`.
- `Department` *employs* `Professor` → Composition (Department owns Professors, lifetime-bound).
- `Department` *has* `Student` → Aggregation (Students may transfer).
- `Student` *is-a* `Person` → Generalization.
- `Course` *depends on* `Syllabus` → Dependency.

**Step 4: Derive the diagram (textual representation).**

```
            +-----------------+
            |    Person       |
            +-----------------+
            | - name : String |
            | - age : int     |
            +-----------------+
            | + displayInfo() |
            +-----------------+
                    △
                    | (Generalization)
        +-----------+-----------+
        |                       |
+---------------+       +---------------+
|    Student    |       |   Professor   |
+---------------+       +---------------+
| - rollNo: int |       | - empId: int  |
| - marks: float|       | - salary:double|
+---------------+       +---------------+
| + register()  |       | + teach()     |
| + payFee()    |       | + evaluate()  |
+---------------+       +---------------+
        |                       |
        | (Association)         | (Composition ◆)
        | 1..*                  | 1..*
        |                       |
        v                       v
+----------------------+  +-------------------+
|       Course         |  |    Department     |
+----------------------+  +-------------------+
| - code : String      |  | - deptId : String |
| - title : String     |  | - name : String   |
+----------------------+  +-------------------+
| + enroll() : void    |  +-------------------+
+----------------------+
```

**Reasoning Logic:**
1. **Generalization** uses a hollow triangle pointing to the *parent* (Person).
2. **Multiplicities** are placed on the *opposite* end of the association line.
3. The diamond (◆) is placed on the *whole* (Department) side of composition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solved Multiplicity Logic:**
> - A `Student` may take `0..*` (zero or many) Courses.
> - A `Course` must have `1..*` (one or many) Students to run.
> - A `Department` employs `1..*` Professors.
> - A `Professor` belongs to exactly `1` Department.

---

## 3.2 OBJECT DIAGRAM

An **Object Diagram** is a *snapshot* of the class diagram at a particular moment. It shows actual instances and their values, rather than abstract classes.

### 3.2.1 Notation Rules
- Object name and class name are **both underlined**.
- Format: `objectName : ClassName` (both parts are underlined).
- Format: `:ClassName` (anonymous object).
- Attributes show actual values; methods are usually omitted.

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

```
+--------------------------+          +--------------------------+
|  s1 : Student            |          |  p1 : Professor          |
+--------------------------+          +--------------------------+
|  name    = "Rahul"       |          |  name    = "Dr. Sharma"  |
|  rollNo  = 101           |          |  empId   = 5001          |
|  marks   = 89.5          |          |  salary  = 75000.0       |
+--------------------------+          +--------------------------+
        |                                     |
        | (link : enrolledIn)                | (link : teaches)
        v                                     v
+--------------------------+
|  c1 : Course             |
+--------------------------+
|  code   = "CS301"        |
|  title  = "OOP"          |
|  credits= 4              |
+--------------------------+
```

**Step-by-step Reasoning:**
1. Object name is on the left, class name is on the right, separated by `:`.
2. The colon and class name are present even for anonymous objects (e.g., `:Student`).
3. **Links** are drawn as solid lines (instances of associations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class vs. Object Diagram (KTU Favorite):**
> - Class Diagram: "What the world *looks like*." (Schema-level)
> - Object Diagram: "What the world *looks like right now*." (Instance-level)

---

## 3.3 USE CASE DIAGRAM

A **Use Case Diagram** captures the *functional requirements* of a system. It shows actors (users/external systems) and the use cases (goals) they interact with.

### 3.3.1 Core Elements
- **Actor**: Stick figure (outside the system boundary). Represents a role, not a specific person.
- **Use Case**: Ellipse containing a verb-noun phrase (e.g., "Withdraw Cash").
- **System Boundary**: A large rectangle that encloses all use cases of *one* system.
- **Relationships**:
  - **Association**: Solid line between actor and use case.
  - **Include** (`<<include>>`): Mandatory reuse — one use case *always* invokes another (dashed arrow).
  - **Extend** (`<<extend>>`): Optional/conditional behavior — added under specific conditions.
  - **Generalization**: Actor inheritance (an Admin *is-a* User).

### 3.3.2 Worked Example — "ATM System"

**Step 1: Identify actors.**
- `Customer` (primary actor)
- `Bank Server` (secondary actor — external system)

**Step 2: Identify use cases.**
- Withdraw Cash
- Check Balance
- Deposit Cash
- Transfer Money
- Print Receipt
- Validate PIN
- Authenticate User

**Step 3: Apply relationships.**

```
                  [System: ATM]
+--------------------------------------------+
|                                            |
|   (Customer)-----> (Validate PIN)          |
|       |              |                     |
|       |              | <<include>>          |
|       |              v                     |
|       +-----------> (Withdraw Cash) ----+   |
|       |                                |   |
|       |            +-------------------+   |
|       |            |                       |
|       +-----------> (Check Balance)         |
|       |            |                       |
|       +-----------> (Deposit Cash)          |
|       |            |                       |
|       +-----------> (Transfer Money)        |
|                    |                       |
|       <extends>    |                       |
|       (Print Receipt) <-------- (any UC)    |
+--------------------------------------------+
                                          |
                                          v
                                   (Bank Server)
```

**Step-by-step Reasoning:**
1. `Validate PIN` is included in every transaction (mandatory) — hence `<<include>>`.
2. `Print Receipt` is optional — happens only if the user opts in — hence `<<extend>>`.
3. `Customer` connects to each use case via solid lines (association).
4. `Bank Server` is connected to use cases that require external communication (e.g., Withdraw Cash).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`<<include>>` vs. `<<extend>>` (KTU Exam Classic):**
> - **Include** → The base use case *cannot complete* without the included one. Arrow points FROM base TO included.
> - **Extend** → The base use case is *complete on its own*; the extension adds optional behavior. Arrow points FROM extension TO base.

---

## 3.4 SEQUENCE DIAGRAM

A **Sequence Diagram** is an *interaction diagram* that emphasizes the **time-ordering of messages** exchanged between objects.

### 3.4.1 Core Elements
- **Lifeline**: Vertical dashed line below each object.
- **Activation Bar**: Thin rectangle on the lifeline showing when the object is active.
- **Message**: Horizontal arrow between lifelines.
  - **Synchronous** (filled arrow `▶`): Caller waits.
  - **Asynchronous** (open arrow `▷`): Caller does not wait.
  - **Return** (dashed arrow `⇠`): Optional.
- **Object**: Rectangle at the top with `:` notation.

### 3.4.2 Worked Example — "Online Order Placement"

```
Customer          :OrderSystem       :PaymentGateway     :Inventory
   |                    |                    |                |
   | 1: placeOrder()    |                    |                |
   |------------------->|                    |                |
   |                    | 2: validate()      |                |
   |                    |---------------->   |                |
   |                    |    3: ok           |                |
   |                    |<----------------   |                |
   |                    | 4: processPayment()|                |
   |                    |------------------->|                |
   |                    |    5: confirm      |                |
   |                    |<------------------|                |
   |                    | 6: updateStock()                    |
   |                    |------------------------------------->|
   |                    |    7: stockUpdated                   |
   |                    |<-------------------------------------|
   | 8: orderConfirmed()|                    |                |
   |<-------------------|                    |                |
   |                    |                    |                |
```

**Step-by-step Reasoning:**
1. Time flows **top to bottom**. The first message is at the top.
2. Each numbered step represents a message in time order.
3. The `:OrderSystem` has the longest activation bar (it coordinates the entire flow).
4. Return messages (steps 3, 5, 7) are optional and shown with dashed arrows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence vs. Communication Diagram:**
> - Sequence: Time-ordered along vertical lifelines.
> - Communication: Numbered messages along links, emphasizes *structure* over *time*.

---

## 3.5 ACTIVITY DIAGRAM

An **Activity Diagram** is essentially a **flowchart** that models the workflow of a process. It is used to model business processes, use case flows, or algorithms.

### 3.5.1 Core Elements
- **Initial Node**: Filled black circle `●`.
- **Final Node**: Bull's-eye `⊙`.
- **Activity**: Rounded rectangle (verb-noun).
- **Decision Node**: Diamond `◇` with `[]` guard conditions.
- **Fork / Join**: Thick horizontal bar (parallel flows).
- **Swimlanes**: Vertical/horizontal partitions showing who is responsible.

### 3.5.2 Worked Example — "Order Processing Workflow"

```
        (Start)
          ●
          |
          v
   +-------------------+
   | Receive Order     |
   +-------------------+
          |
          v
        <>----[stock available?]--No---->+
          |Yes                            |
          v                               v
   +-------------------+         +-----------------+
   | Process Payment   |         | Notify Customer |
   +-------------------+         | "Out of Stock"  |
          |                      +-----------------+
          v                               |
        <>--[payment ok?]--No--+         |
          |Yes                 |         |
          v                    v         |
   +-------------------+   +---------+   |
   | Ship Order        |   | Cancel  |   |
   +-------------------+   | Order   |   |
          |               +---------+   |
          +------->-----------+---------+
                              |
                              v
                            (End)
                              ⊙
```

**Step-by-step Reasoning:**
1. Decision nodes (`<>`) split the flow based on guard conditions `[...]`.
2. All paths must eventually merge or terminate.
3. The diagram can include parallel flows using fork/join bars.

---

## 3.6 STATE CHART DIAGRAM

A **State Chart Diagram** models the **lifecycle of a single object** — the sequence of states it passes through in response to events.

### 3.6.1 Core Elements
- **State**: Rounded rectangle with name and optional `entry/`, `exit/`, `do/` actions.
- **Initial State**: Filled circle `●`.
- **Final State**: Bull's-eye `⊙`.
- **Transition**: Arrow labeled with `event [guard] / action`.
- **Self-Transition**: Arrow looping back to the same state.

### 3.6.2 Worked Example — "ATM Machine States"

```
                ●
                v
        +-----------------+
        |     Idle        |
        +-----------------+
            | cardInserted
            v
        +-----------------+
        |  Card Inserted  |
        +-----------------+
            | pinEntered [pinCorrect]
            v
        +-----------------+      cancelPressed
        |  Authenticating |<----------------------+
        +-----------------+                       |
            | pinValidated                          |
            v                                       
        +-----------------+      transactionDone
        |  Transaction    |--------------------+
        +-----------------+                    |
            | cardRemoved                       |
            v                                   
        +-----------------+                   
        |     Ejecting    |                   
        +-----------------+                   
            |                               
            v                               
        ⊙ (End)                             
```

**Step-by-step Reasoning:**
1. Every state diagram must have **exactly one** initial state.
2. Transitions are triggered by **events**; they may have **guards** (`[condition]`) and **actions** (`/action`).
3. Final states are optional but recommended for clarity.
4. An object can be in only **one state at a time** (mutual exclusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Diagram vs. Activity Diagram (KTU Trick Question):**
> - **State Diagram** → Models the lifecycle of **one object** (focus: state).
> - **Activity Diagram** → Models the **workflow of a process** (focus: activity, may involve multiple objects/swimlanes).

---

## 3.7 COMPONENT DIAGRAM (Brief)

A **Component Diagram** shows the organization and dependencies among a set of components. A *component* is a modular part of a system that encapsulates its contents.

- **Component**: Rectangle with two small rectangles attached (`<<component>>`).
- **Interface**: Lollipop (provided) or socket (required).
- **Dependency**: Dashed arrow between components.

```
[<<component>> OrderService] ----provides----> (IOrder)
         |
         | depends on
         v
[<<component>> PaymentService] ----requires----> (IOrder)
```

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram — UML Diagram Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[UML 2.5 Diagrams] --> B[Structure Diagrams]
    A --> C[Behavior Diagrams]

    B --> B1[Class Diagram]
    B --> B2[Object Diagram]
    B --> B3[Component Diagram]
    B --> B4[Deployment Diagram]
    B --> B5[Package Diagram]
    B --> B6[Composite Structure Diagram]
    B --> B7[Profile Diagram]

    C --> C1[Use Case Diagram]
    C --> C2[Activity Diagram]
    C --> C3[State Machine Diagram]
    C --> C4[Sequence Diagram]
    C --> C5[Communication Diagram]
    C --> C6[Interaction Overview Diagram]
    C --> C7[Timing Diagram]

    B1 -.-> H1[Static: Classes, Attributes, Operations, Relationships]
    B2 -.-> H2[Static: Instance snapshots with values]
    C1 -.-> H3[Functional: Actors and Goals]
    C2 -.-> H4[Procedural: Workflow and parallelism]
    C3 -.-> H5[Lifecycle: States and transitions]
    C4 -.-> H6[Time-ordered: Message exchange]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Class Diagram of "Library Management System"

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
        - name : String

        - address : String
        + displayInfo() void
    }
    class Member {
        - memberId : int
        - joinDate : Date
        - maxBooks : int
        + borrowBook() void
        + returnBook() void
    }
    class Librarian {
        - employeeId : int

        + addBook() void
        + removeBook() void
        + issueBook() void

        - isbn : String

        - author : String

        + checkOut() void

        - bio : String
        + getBooks() List
    }

        - categoryId : int

    }

    Person <|-- Member : Generalization is-a
    Person <|-- Librarian : Generalization is-a
    Member "1" --> "0..*" Book : borrows
    Librarian "1" --> "0..*" Book : manages
    Book "1" --> "0..*" Author : writtenBy
    Book "1" --> "1" Category : belongsTo
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Sequence Diagram of "Login Flow"

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant UI as LoginUI
    participant C as Controller
    participant DB as Database

    U->>UI: 1. enterCredentials(username, password)
    activate UI
    UI->>C: 2. validate(user, pass)
    activate C
    C->>DB: 3. queryUser(username)
    activate DB
    DB-->>C: 4. userRecord
    deactivate DB
    C->>C: 5. verifyPassword(hash)
    alt Password Matches
        C-->>UI: 6a. success(token)
        UI-->>U: 7a. showDashboard
    else Password Mismatch
        C-->>UI: 6b. failure(error)
        UI-->>U: 7b. showError
    end
    deactivate C
    deactivate UI
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — State Diagram of "TCP Connection"

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> Listen : passiveOpen
    Listen --> SynReceived : receiveSYN / sendSYN_ACK
    SynReceived --> Established : receiveACK
    Listen --> SynSent : activeOpen / sendSYN
    SynSent --> Established : receiveSYN_ACK / sendACK
    SynSent --> Closed : timeout / sendRST
    Established --> CloseWait : close / sendFIN
    CloseWait --> LastAck : sendFIN
    LastAck --> Closed : receiveACK
    Established --> FinWait1 : close / sendFIN
    FinWait1 --> FinWait2 : receiveACK
    FinWait2 --> TimeWait : receiveFIN / sendACK
    TimeWait --> Closed : timeout 2MSL
    Closed --> [*]
```

graph TD
