# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three structural UML diagrams with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Class Diagram** — Depicts the static structure of a system by showing its classes, attributes, operations, and relationships.
2. **Component Diagram** — Shows the organization and dependencies among software components in a system.
3. **Deployment Diagram** — Illustrates the physical deployment of software artifacts on hardware nodes.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between aggregation and composition with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Aggregation** is a weak "whole–part" relationship (represented by a hollow diamond ◇) where the part can exist independently of the whole. *Example:* A `Team` aggregates `Player` objects — if the team is dissolved, players continue to exist and may join other teams.
- **Composition** is a strong "whole–part" relationship (represented by a filled diamond ◆) where the part's lifetime is bound to the whole. *Example:* A `House` is composed of `Room` objects — if the house is demolished, the rooms are destroyed with it.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Draw a **Use Case Diagram** for an **Online Banking System**. The system allows customers to view account balance, transfer funds, pay bills, and request a cheque book. The bank administrator manages customer accounts. Identify at least 3 use cases and 2 actors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the difference between **`<<include>>`** and **`<<extend>>`** relationships in a use case diagram with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Use Case Diagram: Online Banking System

**Actors identified:**
- `Customer` (primary actor)
- `Bank Administrator` (primary actor)
- `External Payment Gateway` (secondary/supporting actor)

**Use cases identified:**
- `Login` (common to all)
- `View Account Balance`
- `Transfer Funds`
- `Pay Bills`
- `Request Cheque Book`
- `Manage Customer Accounts`
- `Authenticate User` (reused)

**Mermaid Representation:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    Customer([Customer]):::actor
    Admin([Bank Administrator]):::actor
    PG([Payment Gateway]):::actor

    subgraph BankSystem [Online Banking System]
        UC1((Login))
        UC2((View Account Balance))
        UC3((Transfer Funds))
        UC4((Pay Bills))
        UC5((Request Cheque Book))
        UC6((Manage Customer Accounts))
        UC7((Authenticate User))
        UC8((Process Payment))
    end

    Customer --- UC1
    Customer --- UC2
    Customer --- UC3
    Customer --- UC4
    Customer --- UC5
    Admin --- UC6
    PG --- UC8

    UC1 -.->|include| UC7
    UC3 -.->|include| UC7
    UC4 -.->|include| UC7
    UC4 -.->|include| UC8
    UC5 -.->|extend| UC2

    classDef actor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Explanation:**
- `Customer` is connected to 5 use cases (Login, View Balance, Transfer Funds, Pay Bills, Request Cheque Book).
- `Bank Administrator` is connected to `Manage Customer Accounts`.
- `Payment Gateway` is an external actor connected to `Process Payment`.
- `Login`, `Transfer Funds`, and `Pay Bills` all `<<include>>` `Authenticate User` for security.
- `Request Cheque Book` `<<extends>>` `View Account Balance` (optional, only if cheque book is requested while viewing balance).

#### Part (b) — `<<include>>` vs `<<extend>>`

| Aspect | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Mandatory reuse of behavior | Optional, conditional addition |
| **Direction of arrow** | From base → included | From extension → base |
| **Independence** | Base cannot complete without included UC | Base is complete on its own |
| **Trigger** | Always executed when base runs | Executes only if extension point is reached |
| **Example** | "Place Order" always includes "Validate Payment" | "Generate Invoice" extends "Place Order" (only if customer wants a printed invoice) |

**Example Explanation:**
- In the **Online Banking System**, `Login`, `Transfer Funds`, and `Pay Bills` all `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`. This is because **no transaction can occur without authentication** — it is mandatory.
- Conversely, `Request Cheque Book` `<<extends>>` `View Account Balance`. The user is not forced to request a cheque book every time they view their balance; it is an **optional, conditional flow**.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Draw a **Class Diagram** for a **Hospital Management System** with at least 4 classes (e.g., `Doctor`, `Patient`, `Appointment`, `Department`). Show attributes, methods, and at least one each of: inheritance, association, aggregation, and composition. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Draw a **State Chart Diagram** for an **Order** object in an e-commerce system. The order goes through states: *Placed → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered*, with possible transitions to *Cancelled* from any state before shipping. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Class Diagram: Hospital Management System

**Classes and Members:**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `Person` (Abstract) | `- name : String`, `- age : int`, `- gender : String` | `+ displayInfo() : void` |
| `Doctor` | `- doctorId : int`, `- specialization : String`, `- salary : double` | `+ diagnose() : void`, `+ prescribe() : void` |
| `Patient` | `- patientId : int`, `- disease : String`, `- admissionDate : Date` | `+ getTreatment() : void`, `+ payBill() : void` |
| `Department` | `- deptId : int`, `- deptName : String`, `- location : String` | `+ addDoctor() : void` |
| `Appointment` | `- appointmentId : int`, `- date : Date`, `- time : Time` | `+ schedule() : void`, `+ cancel() : void` |

**Mermaid Representation:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
        - name : String
        - age : int
        - gender : String
        + displayInfo() void
    }
    class Doctor {
        - doctorId : int
        - specialization : String
        - salary : double
        + diagnose() void
        + prescribe() void
    }
    class Patient {
        - patientId : int
        - disease : String
        - admissionDate : Date
        + getTreatment() void
        + payBill() void
    }
    class Department {
        - deptId : int
        - deptName : String
        - location : String
        + addDoctor() void
    }
    class Appointment {
        - appointmentId : int
        - date : Date
        - time : Time
        + schedule() void
        + cancel() void
    }

    Person <|-- Doctor : is-a
    Person <|-- Patient : is-a
    Department "1" o-- "0..*" Doctor : aggregates
    Department "1" *-- "1" Ward : composes
    Doctor "1" --> "0..*" Appointment : schedules
    Patient "1" --> "0..*" Appointment : books
```

**Explanation of Relationships:**
- **Inheritance (Generalization)**: `Doctor` and `Patient` both inherit from the abstract class `Person` (the `<|--` arrow points to the parent).
- **Aggregation (◇)**: `Department` aggregates `Doctor` — if a department is closed, doctors may transfer to other departments. The empty diamond is on the `Department` side.
- **Composition (◆)**: `Department` is composed of `Ward` — if the department ceases to exist, the ward is also dissolved. The filled diamond is on the `Department` side.
- **Association**: `Doctor` schedules `Appointment`, and `Patient` books `Appointment`. These are simple "uses-a" relationships.

#### Part (b) — State Chart Diagram: Order Lifecycle

**Mermaid Representation:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Placed
    Placed --> Confirmed : paymentReceived / validateOrder
    Placed --> Cancelled : cancelRequest [beforeConfirmation]
    Confirmed --> Shipped : dispatchOrder / updateInventory
    Confirmed --> Cancelled : cancelRequest [beforeShipping]
    Shipped --> Delivered : deliveryConfirmed
    Delivered --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

**Explanation:**
1. The order is created (`[*] → Placed`) when the user clicks "Place Order".
2. Once payment is received, it transitions to `Confirmed` (with a guard action to validate the order).
3. The warehouse dispatches the order → transitions to `Shipped` (with an action to update inventory).
4. The courier confirms delivery → transitions to `Delivered` (final state, marked with `[(*)]`).
5. **Cancellation** is possible from both `Placed` and `Confirmed` states, but **not from `Shipped`** (because the order is already in transit).
6. The guards `[beforeConfirmation]` and `[beforeShipping]` make the cancellation conditional.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Aggregation vs. Composition Symbols:** Students frequently use filled diamond for aggregation or vice-versa. *Remember: hollow ◇ = aggregation (weak), filled ◆ = composition (strong).*
> 2. **Inheritance Arrow Direction:** The hollow triangle should point to the **parent** class, not the child. *Example:* `Doctor <|— Person` means Doctor inherits from Person.
> 3. **`<<include>>` vs. `<<extend>>` Arrow Direction:** Include arrow points **from base to included**. Extend arrow points **from extension to base**. Reversing these is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Sequence Diagram Time Flow:** Time flows top-to-bottom. Reversing the order or omitting activation bars loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Object Diagram Underlining:** In object diagrams, both `objectName` and `:ClassName` **must be underlined**. Forgetting the underline loses 1 mark.
> 6. **State Diagram — Missing Initial/Final States:** Every state diagram must have **one initial state** and at least one **final state** for completeness. Omission leads to 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Multiplicity Placement:** Multiplicity numbers are placed on the **far end** of the association line, not the source. *Example:* `Department "1" — "1..*" Doctor` means one Department has many Doctors.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for UML Diagrams:**

### 1. Foundational Concepts
- UML is a **visual modeling language**, not a programming language. It is **ISO/IEC 19505** standard.
- Developed by **Booch, Rumbaugh, Jacobson**; maintained by **OMG**.
- Two main categories: **Structure Diagrams** (static) and **Behavior Diagrams** (dynamic).

### 2. Class Diagram — Most Critical
- Three compartments: **Name | Attributes | Operations**.
- Visibility symbols: `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- Abstract classes have *italicized* names.
- Key relationships (must memorize symbols and meanings):
  - **Association** → solid line
  - **Directed Association** → solid line with arrow
  - **Aggregation** → hollow diamond (◇) — "has-a" weak
  - **Composition** → filled diamond (◆) — "owns-a" strong
  - **Generalization** → solid line + hollow triangle — "is-a" (inheritance)
  - **Realization** → dashed line + hollow triangle — "implements" (interface)
  - **Dependency** → dashed line with arrow — "uses" (weakest)

### 3. Object Diagram
- Shows **instances** with actual values.
- Notation: `objectName : ClassName` (both parts **underlined**).
- Links are instances of associations.

### 4. Use Case Diagram
- **Actor** = stick figure (role, not person).
- **Use case** = ellipse with verb-noun name.
- **System boundary** = large rectangle.
- **`<<include>>`** = mandatory, arrow base → included.
- **`<<extend>>`** = optional, arrow extension → base.

### 5. Sequence Diagram
- **Time flows top-to-bottom**.
- Lifeline = vertical dashed line; activation = thin rectangle.
- Messages: synchronous (filled arrow), asynchronous (open arrow), return (dashed arrow).
- Used for **time-ordered** interactions.

### 6. Activity Diagram
- A **flowchart** of a process with possible parallelism.
- Initial node = filled circle `●`, Final node = bull's-eye `⊙`.
- Decisions use diamonds with guard conditions.
- Forks/Joins = thick bars for parallel execution.

### 7. State Chart Diagram
- Models **lifecycle of one object**.
- States = rounded rectangles; transitions = labeled arrows.
- Notation: `event [guard] / action`.
- One initial state (mandatory); final state (recommended).

### 8. Component & Deployment (Brief)
- **Component Diagram**: shows software modules and interfaces.
- **Deployment Diagram**: shows physical hardware (nodes) and software artifacts.

### 9. Exam Strategy Tips
- Always **label all multiplicities** in class diagrams (e.g., `1`, `*`, `1..*`).
- **Underline** object names and class names in object diagrams.
- **Show direction** in directed associations and dependencies.
- **Mention actor roles** explicitly (e.g., "Customer," "Admin") in use case diagrams.
- For sequence diagrams, **number the messages** in time order.

### 10. Common Mistake Matrix
| Mistake | Correction |
|---|---|
| Using filled diamond for aggregation | Use **hollow** diamond for aggregation |
| Reversing generalization arrow | Triangle points to **parent** |
| Forgetting initial state in state diagram | Always add **filled circle** at start |
| Including arrows in reverse direction | Include: base → included; Extend: extension → base |
| Missing multiplicity labels | Always show **1, *, 1..*** on association ends |
| Mixing up state and activity diagrams | State = object lifecycle; Activity = process workflow |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Case Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use Case Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Use Case Diagram** is a **behavioral diagram** in the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** that graphically depicts a set of **actors** (external entities), their corresponding **use cases** (system functions), and the **relationships** between them, all enclosed within a defined **system boundary**. It captures the *functional requirements* of a system from an outside-in perspective, answering the question: *"What should the system do for whom?"*

It is the most fundamental artifact in **Requirement Modeling** and forms the first step in UML-based object-oriented analysis as prescribed by the KTU OOP Module 1 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Use Case Diagrams belong to the **UML 2.5 Behavioral Diagram** family and are mandatory for documenting functional requirements before any structural (Class/Object) diagrams are constructed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you walk into a **restaurant** for the very first time:
- **You (the customer)** are an *actor*.
- The actions you want to do — *view menu, place order, request bill, make payment* — are *use cases*.
- The *kitchen, the waiter, the billing counter* are internal components of the system that you don't see inside, but the service boundaries are clear.
- A *use case diagram* is essentially the **"menu card of the software"** — it tells every external user what services the system offers them, without revealing *how* the system does it.

Similarly, for an **ATM**:
- The *Customer* and *Bank Server* are actors.
- *Withdraw Cash, Check Balance, Deposit, Transfer* are use cases.
- The actor cannot see the database, the cash dispenser mechanics, or the encryption modules — only the *services*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A Use Case Diagram describes **WHAT**, never **HOW**. Implementation details (algorithms, classes, data structures) belong to later diagrams like **Class Diagrams** and **Sequence Diagrams**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Actor** | An external entity (human, hardware device, or another system) that interacts with the system. |
| **Use Case** | A specific function or service the system provides to an actor to achieve a tangible goal. |
| **System Boundary** | A rectangular box defining the scope of the system — what is *inside* vs *outside*. |
| **Subject** | The system under consideration, labelled at the top of the boundary box. |
| **Association** | A solid line connecting an actor to a use case showing participation. |

All measurements and notations follow **UML 2.5 Specification** standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Use Case Diagram

A use case diagram is built from exactly **four categories** of elements. Mastering these guarantees marks in any KTU descriptive question.

#### 2.1.1 Actors
- Represented by a **stick figure** (for human actors) or a **rectangle with the «actor» stereotype** (for non-human system actors).
- Placed **outside** the system boundary.
- Classified into:
  - **Primary Actors** — actively initiate use cases (e.g., Customer, Admin).
  - **Secondary Actors** — provide services to the system (e.g., Bank Server, Payment Gateway).
  - **Concrete Actors** — physically exist in the real world.
  - **Abstract Actors** — represent roles (e.g., "Authenticated User"), not specific people.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always name actors with a **singular noun** in **Title Case** — e.g., `Customer`, not `customers` or `The Customer`.

#### 2.1.2 Use Cases
- Represented by a **horizontal ellipse** (oval).
- Always placed **inside** the system boundary.
- Naming convention: **Verb + Noun** in title case — e.g., `Place Order`, `Withdraw Cash`, `Generate Report`.
- Each use case must represent an **observable result of value** to a specific actor.

#### 2.1.3 System Boundary
- A **large rectangle** drawn around all use cases.
- The system's **name** is written at the top-left or top-center (e.g., "ATM System").
- Defines the *scope* of the system being modeled.

#### 2.1.4 Relationships
This is where most KTU questions test students. There are **four** types:

| Relationship | Notation | Meaning | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Association** | Solid line | Actor participates in a use case | Bi-directional (no arrow needed) |
| **«include»** | Dashed arrow + `«include»` | Base use case *always* invokes another | From base → included |
| **«extend»** | Dashed arrow + `«extend»` | Optional/conditional behavior added at an extension point | From extending → base |
| **Generalization** | Solid line with hollow triangle | Inheritance — a specialized actor or use case | Specialized → Parent |

### 2.2 «include» vs «extend» — The Most Tested Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Hot Spot:** 70% of 14-mark questions on Use Case Diagrams contain a sub-part asking the difference between `«include»` and `«extend»`. Memorize this!

- **«include»**: Mandatory reuse. *Every* execution of the base use case *must* trigger the included use case. Used to **factor out common behavior**. Example: `Withdraw Cash` **includes** `Validate PIN` (cannot withdraw without PIN).

- **«extend»**: Optional/Conditional reuse. The extending use case adds behavior *only under certain conditions* at a defined **extension point**. Example: `Apply Discount` **extends** `Place Order` (only if customer has a coupon).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | UML Element | Placement | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stick figure | Human Actor | Outside boundary | Singular Title Case |
| Rectangle + «actor» | System/Timer Actor | Outside boundary | Non-human roles |
| Ellipse | Use Case | Inside boundary | Verb + Noun |
| Rectangle | System Boundary | Encloses use cases | System name on top |
| Solid line | Association | Actor ↔ Use Case | No arrow |
| Dashed arrow | Dependency («include»/«extend») | Between use cases | Always labelled |
| Hollow triangle | Generalization | Specialized → Parent | Inheritance |
| Note (folded corner) | Constraint/Comment | Anywhere | Use to clarify |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Use Case Diagrams are used in KTU-aligned software projects to:
1. **Elicit requirements** from stakeholders before coding begins.
2. Serve as the **contract** between the client and the development team.
3. Form the **input** for identifying Classes (via noun extraction from use case names).
4. Provide a **scope boundary** to prevent feature creep in agile sprints.
5. Form the basis of **test case generation** — each use case path becomes a test scenario.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Construction & Worked Example

### 3.1 The Five-Step Methodology for Drawing a Use Case Diagram

Since a use case diagram has no mathematical derivation, the "derivation" here is the **systematic engineering process** to construct one. Follow these steps strictly to score full marks.

> **Problem Statement (Worked Example):** *"Design a Use Case Diagram for an Online Shopping System. The system allows customers to browse products, add items to a cart, place an order, make payment, and track delivery. The admin manages inventory and processes refunds."*

#### Step 1 — Identify Actors
Ask: *"Who or what interacts with the system from outside?"*

Identified actors:
- `Customer` (primary human actor)
- `Admin` (primary human actor)
- `Payment Gateway` (secondary system actor)
- `Delivery System` (secondary system actor)

> **Logic:** The Customer initiates purchases. The Admin maintains the catalog. The Payment Gateway is an external service the system contacts. The Delivery System is contacted for shipping status.

#### Step 2 — Identify Use Cases
Ask: *"What are the distinct goals each actor wants to achieve?"*

$$
\text{Use Cases} = \{\text{Browse Products},\ \text{Add to Cart},\ \text{Place Order},\ \text{Make Payment},\ \text{Track Delivery},\ \text{Manage Inventory},\ \text{Process Refund}\}
$$

> **Logic:** Every use case must deliver an *observable result of value*. "Add to Cart" alone may not be valuable, but combined with "Place Order" it is — keep it anyway because it represents a meaningful sub-goal.

#### Step 3 — Draw the System Boundary
Draw a rectangle labelled `Online Shopping System` at the top. All ellipses (use cases) must lie **inside** this rectangle. All actors lie **outside**.

#### Step 4 — Establish Associations
Connect each actor to the use cases they participate in using solid lines.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Customer} &\longrightarrow \{\text{Browse Products},\ \text{Add to Cart},\ \text{Place Order},\ \text{Make Payment},\ \text{Track Delivery}\} \\
\text{Admin} &\longrightarrow \{\text{Manage Inventory},\ \text{Process Refund}\} \\
\text{Payment Gateway} &\longrightarrow \{\text{Make Payment}\} \\
\text{Delivery System} &\longrightarrow \{\text{Track Delivery}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Apply Advanced Relationships (Include, Extend, Generalization)
Scan for common behaviors and conditional flows.

- **«include»**: `Make Payment`, `Place Order`, and `Track Delivery` *all* require user authentication.
  $$\text{Make Payment} \xrightarrow{\text{«include»}} \text{Authenticate User}$$
- **«extend»**: `Apply Coupon` is only triggered when a customer enters a valid promo code during `Place Order`.
  $$\text{Apply Coupon} \xrightarrow{\text{«extend»}} \text{Place Order}$$
- **Generalization**: `Registered User` and `Guest User` are both kinds of `Customer`.
  $$\text{Registered User},\ \text{Guest User} \longrightarrow \text{Customer}$$

### 3.2 Validation Checklist (Before Submission)

Use this matrix to verify the diagram against KTU 2024 valuation rubrics.

| Validation Rule | Check |
|---|---|
| All actors placed *outside* boundary? | ☐ |
| All use cases placed *inside* boundary? | ☐ |
| Every use case has at least one associated actor? | ☐ |
| Use case names follow Verb + Noun pattern? | ☐ |
| «include» arrows point from base to reusable? | ☐ |
| «extend» arrows point from extension to base? | ☐ |
| No implementation details (classes, methods) shown? | ☐ |
| System name labelled on the boundary? | ☐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Use Case Diagram — Online Shopping System

The Mermaid `flowchart` engine is used here to render a faithful representation of the UML Use Case Diagram for the worked example. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CustomerA((Customer))
    AdminA((Admin))
    PaymentA((Payment Gateway))
    DeliveryA((Delivery System))
    RegisteredA((Registered User))
    GuestA((Guest User))

    subgraph SYS [Online Shopping System]
        UC1[Browse Products]
        UC2[Add to Cart]
        UC3[Place Order]
        UC4[Make Payment]
        UC5[Track Delivery]
        UC6[Manage Inventory]
        UC7[Process Refund]
        UC8[Authenticate User]
        UC9[Apply Coupon]
    end

    RegisteredA --- SYS
    GuestA --- SYS
    SYS --- CustomerA

    CustomerA --- UC1
    CustomerA --- UC2
    CustomerA --- UC3
    CustomerA --- UC4
    CustomerA --- UC5
    AdminA --- UC6
    AdminA --- UC7
    PaymentA --- UC4
    DeliveryA --- UC5

    UC3 -. include .-> UC8
    UC4 -. include .-> UC8
    UC5 -. include .-> UC8

    UC9 -. extend .-> UC3

    UC3 -. extend .-> UC4
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Relationship Mapping

For a high-level view of how the relationships interact, the following block architecture illustrates the **dependency topology** between base use cases and their reusable extensions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Authenticate User]:::reused
    R2[Apply Coupon]:::optional
    R3[Make Payment]:::optional

    B1[Place Order]:::base
    B2[Make Payment]:::base
    B3[Track Delivery]:::base

    B1 -- include --> R1
    B2 -- include --> R1
    B3 -- include --> R1
    R2 -- extend --> B1
    B1 -- extend --> R3

    classDef base fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef reused fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef optional fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Notation Legend Table

| UML Notation | Mermaid Approximation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Ellipse with text | `[Text]` rectangle | Use Case |
| Stick figure | `((Text))` circle | Actor |
| Solid line | `---` | Association |
| Dashed arrow | `-. label .->` | «include» / «extend» |
| Hollow triangle | Hollow triangle in `flowchart` | Generalization |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Use Case Diagram. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Use Case Diagram is a UML behavioral diagram that represents the functional requirements of a system by depicting the interactions between external actors and the system's use cases. The main components are:
1. **Actors** — external entities that interact with the system.
2. **Use Cases** — specific functions/services provided by the system.
3. **System Boundary** — a rectangle defining the system's scope.
4. **Relationships** — associations, «include», «extend», and generalizations connecting the above.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between «include» and «extend» relationships in a use case diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | «include» | «extend» |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Mandatory reuse of common behavior | Optional/conditional addition of behavior |
| Trigger | Always executed when base use case runs | Only when a specific condition is met |
| Arrow Direction | Base use case → Included use case | Extending use case → Base use case |
| Example | `Place Order` includes `Authenticate User` | `Apply Coupon` extends `Place Order` |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Draw a detailed Use Case Diagram for a **Library Management System** where:
- A **Member** can search for books, issue a book, return a book, and view their loan history.
- A **Librarian** can add new books, remove books, and manage member records.
- A **Payment System** is an external system used to collect fines for late returns.
- The `Issue Book` use case must always include `Verify Membership`.
- The `Pay Fine` use case extends `Return Book` (only triggered if the book is overdue). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four types of relationships used in a use case diagram with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diagram Construction (7 Marks):**
- *Actors identified:* `Member` (primary), `Librarian` (primary), `Payment System` (secondary system actor).
- *Use cases identified:* `Search Book`, `Issue Book`, `Return Book`, `View Loan History`, `Add Book`, `Remove Book`, `Manage Members`, `Verify Membership`, `Pay Fine`.
- *System boundary:* Rectangle labelled `Library Management System`.
- *Relationships:*
  - Solid lines connecting each actor to their relevant use cases.
  - `Issue Book` → dashed arrow labelled `«include»` → `Verify Membership`.
  - `Pay Fine` → dashed arrow labelled `«extend»` → `Return Book`.

**Mark Allocation (7):** 2 marks actors, 2 marks use cases + boundary, 2 marks relationships, 1 mark proper UML notation.

**(b) Four Relationship Types (7 Marks):**
1. **Association (1.5 Marks):** A solid line between an actor and a use case. Indicates the actor participates in the use case. Example: `Member` is associated with `Issue Book`.
2. **«include» (2 Marks):** A dashed arrow with the «include» stereotype. It represents *mandatory* reuse of a common use case. The base use case always invokes the included one. Example: `Issue Book` «include» `Verify Membership`.
3. **«extend» (2 Marks):** A dashed arrow with the «extend» stereotype pointing from the extension to the base. Represents *optional/conditional* behavior inserted at an extension point. Example: `Pay Fine` «extend» `Return Book`.
4. **Generalization (1.5 Marks):** A solid line with a hollow triangular arrowhead. Shows inheritance between actors or use cases. Example: `Student Member` and `Faculty Member` both generalize to `Member`.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the components of a Use Case Diagram using a **Hospital Management System** as an example. Identify at least 3 actors and 5 use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a Use Case Diagram? Discuss the role of actors and the system boundary in requirement analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hospital Management System Diagram (7 Marks):**
- *Actors:* `Patient` (stick figure), `Doctor` (stick figure), `Receptionist` (stick figure), `Insurance System` («actor» rectangle, external system).
- *Use cases (5 minimum):* `Book Appointment`, `View Medical Records`, `Generate Bill`, `Admit Patient`, `Process Insurance Claim`.
- *System boundary:* Rectangle labelled `Hospital Management System`.
- *Sample relationships:* `Book Appointment` «include» `Verify Patient`. `Generate Bill` «extend» `Process Insurance Claim` (only if patient has insurance).

**Mark Allocation:** 2 marks actors, 2 marks use cases, 2 marks relationships & boundary, 1 mark neatness/notation.

**(b) Conceptual Answer (7 Marks):**
- A **Use Case Diagram** (2 Marks) is a UML behavioral diagram that captures the functional requirements of a system by showing how external entities (actors) interact with the system's services (use cases) within a defined scope (boundary).
- **Role of Actors (2.5 Marks):** Actors represent *who* or *what* interacts with the system. They help identify the system's users and external systems, which in turn drive the discovery of use cases. Actors are classified as primary (initiate use cases) and secondary (provide services).
- **Role of System Boundary (2.5 Marks):** The system boundary clearly demarcates what is *inside* the system (use cases, internal logic) from what is *outside* (actors, external systems). It sets the *scope* of the project, prevents scope creep, and helps stakeholders agree on what the system will and will not do.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Drawing **«include»** arrow in the *wrong direction* (must always point from base use case to the included reusable use case). Deducts 1–2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting to enclose use cases inside the **system boundary rectangle** — system becomes visually ambiguous. Deducts 1 mark.
> 3. Using **plural** actor names (`Customers` instead of `Customer`) — violates UML naming convention. Deducts 0.5 mark.
> 4. Adding **classes, attributes, or methods** inside the use case diagram — this is a behavioral diagram, not structural. Deducts 1–2 marks.
> 5. Failing to use the **«extend» stereotype label** on the dashed arrow — examiners specifically check for this stereotype text. Deducts 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **Use Case Diagram** is a *behavioral* UML diagram showing *what* the system does, not *how*.
- **Four components:** Actors, Use Cases, System Boundary, Relationships.
- **Actors** are *outside* the boundary; **Use Cases** are *inside*.
- Use Case naming: **Verb + Noun**, singular, Title Case.
- Actor naming: **Singular Noun** in Title Case (e.g., `Customer`, `Admin`).
- **«include»** = **mandatory** reuse; arrow points from base to included.
- **«extend»** = **optional/conditional** addition; arrow points from extending to base.
- **Association** = solid line (no arrow) between actor and use case.
- **Generalization** = solid line with **hollow triangle** for inheritance among actors or use cases.
- The diagram must have a **system boundary rectangle** with the system name labelled.
- Use Case Diagrams are the **first step** in OOP analysis — they feed into Class Diagrams (via noun extraction) and Sequence Diagrams (via use case flows).
- They are also called **"External View"** diagrams because they model the system from the user's perspective.
- UML 2.5 specification by **OMG** is the authoritative standard — there are no regional variants.
- Avoid implementation details in the diagram — no classes, no methods, no SQL, no pseudocode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class Diagram. Introduction to Java - Java Buzzwords
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Class Diagram. Introduction to Java - Java Buzzwords

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Class Diagram — Formal Definition

A **Class Diagram** is a static structure diagram in the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** that describes the system structure by showcasing the system's **classes**, their **attributes**, **operations (methods)**, and the **relationships** that exist among the objects of those classes. It is the most fundamental and widely used diagram in object-oriented modeling and is the backbone of any OOP-based software design under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for OOP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** Class Diagrams fall under *UML Diagrams (Structural)* and carry direct 14-mark ESE weightage when combined with Object-Oriented concepts.

In UML 2.5 (standardized by the **Object Management Group — OMG**), a class diagram visually represents the blueprint of a system. Each class is represented as a **rectangle divided into three compartments**:
1. **Top compartment** — Class name (in PascalCase, e.g., `Student`)
2. **Middle compartment** — Attributes (data members) with visibility
3. **Bottom compartment** — Operations (methods/functions) with visibility

### 1.2 Components of a UML Class

| Component | Description | Notation Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Class Name** | Identifier of the class | `Student` |
| **Attribute** | Data member (field) of the class | `-rollNo : int` |
| **Operation** | Function/Method exposed by the class | `+display() : void` |
| **Visibility** | Access modifier symbol | `+` `−` `#` `~` |

### 1.3 Visibility Modifiers (Standard UML Symbols)

| Symbol | Visibility | Java Equivalent | Access Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+` | Public | `public` | Accessible from anywhere |
| `−` | Private | `private` | Accessible only within the class |
| `#` | Protected | `protected` | Accessible within class, subclass, and same package |
| `~` | Package | *(default in Java)* | Accessible within the same package |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Class Diagram as a "House Blueprint":**
> Imagine you are an architect designing a housing colony. Before laying a single brick, you draw a **blueprint** that shows where the rooms (attributes) are, where the doors and windows (methods) are, and how different houses share a boundary wall (relationships). A **Class Diagram** is exactly this blueprint, but for a software system. The "rooms" are data fields, the "doors/windows" are functions that allow interaction, and the "shared walls" are relationships like *aggregation* and *inheritance*. Without this blueprint, software construction becomes chaotic — exactly why the OOP paradigm insists on designing class diagrams *before* coding.

## 1.4 Introduction to Java — Formal Definition

**Java** is a high-level, general-purpose, **object-oriented**, **platform-independent**, **robust**, **secure**, **architecturally neutral**, **multithreaded**, and **dynamic** programming language developed by **James Gosling** and his team at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991** (officially released in **1995**). Java follows the principle of **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** through its compiled **bytecode** that runs on the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Timeline (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **1991** — Project "Green" initiated at Sun Microsystems (James Gosling)
> - **1995** — Java 1.0 released (originally called "Oak")
> - **2009** — Sun Microsystems acquired by Oracle Corporation
> - **2014** — Java 8 released (Lambda expressions — major milestone)
> - **2024** — Java 23 (current LTS-aligned versions: 17, 21)

## 1.5 Java Buzzwords — The 11 Official Features

The **Java Buzzwords** are the **11 officially recognized features** of Java as defined in *The Java Language Specification* and as emphasized repeatedly in the KTU OOP module. They are the cornerstone conceptual question for **3-mark and short-answer questions**.

| # | Buzzword | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple** | Easy to learn, no pointers/explicit memory management |
| 2 | **Object-Oriented** | Based on classes and objects |
| 3 | **Platform-Independent** | Bytecode runs on any JVM |
| 4 | **Secured** | Sandboxed execution environment |
| 5 | **Robust** | Strong memory management & exception handling |
| 6 | **Architecture-Neutral** | No implementation-dependent features |
| 7 | **Portable** | Same bytecode works on all platforms |
| 8 | **Multi-threaded** | Concurrent execution via `Thread` class |
| 9 | **Interpreted** | Bytecode interpreted by JVM (also JIT compiled) |
| 10 | **High-Performance** | Uses JIT compiler for speed |
| 11 | **Distributed** | Supports RMI and internet-based applications |
| 12 | **Dynamic** | Supports runtime class loading via reflection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The original list given by James Gosling's white paper mentioned **11 buzzwords**. The "Dynamic" feature is often added as the 12th. KTU questions frequently ask students to *list and explain any 6–8 buzzwords*. Memorizing the 11 standard buzzwords with short definitions guarantees full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Java Buzzwords as a "Swiss Army Knife":**
> Think of Java as a **Swiss Army Knife** carried by a software engineer. Each blade/tool represents a buzzword:
> - **Simple** → The knife is easy to grip and open
> - **Object-Oriented** → Tools are organized in a modular case
> - **Platform-Independent** → The same knife works whether you are in India, USA, or Japan
> - **Secure** → The blade retracts safely (no accidental cuts)
> - **Robust** → The knife is made of stainless steel — does not rust easily
> - **Multi-threaded** → You can use the scissors and screwdriver at the same time
>
> Just as the Swiss Army Knife is preferred by explorers worldwide, Java is preferred by enterprises worldwide because of these built-in features.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Class Diagram — The Operational Theory

A UML Class Diagram is built from a finite set of structural primitives. The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on **classes, attributes, operations, and four key relationships**.

### 2.1.1 Class Representation

The class is drawn as a **rectangle with three sections**. The abstract class is represented with the class name in *italics*. Interfaces are shown with the stereotype `<<interface>>` above the name.

**Standard Class Syntax in UML:**
```
+-------------------------+
|        ClassName        |   ← Class Name (Noun, PascalCase)
+-------------------------+
| - attribute1 : type     |   ← Attributes (data members)
| + attribute2 : type     |
+-------------------------+
| + method1() : returnType|   ← Operations (methods)
| - method2() : returnType|
+-------------------------+
```

### 2.1.2 Relationship Types in Class Diagrams (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

There are **four primary relationships** a KTU student must master:

### A. Association (Uses-a / Knows-a)
- A general "uses" or "knows" relationship between two classes.
- Represented as a **simple solid line** between classes.
- May include multiplicity: `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, `n..m`.

### B. Aggregation (Has-a — Weak Ownership)
- A specialized form of association representing a **whole–part relationship** with **weak ownership**.
- The child can **exist independently** of the parent.
- Represented as a **hollow diamond (◇)** on the side of the whole.

### C. Composition (Has-a — Strong Ownership)
- A stronger form of aggregation where the child **cannot exist** without the parent.
- The parent's destruction implies the child's destruction.
- Represented as a a **filled diamond (◆)** on the side of the whole.

### D. Inheritance / Generalization (Is-a)
- One class inherits the structure and behavior of another.
- Represented as a **hollow triangle arrow (▷)** pointing from the subclass to the superclass.
- Achieved in Java using the `extends` keyword.

### 2.1.3 Multiplicity Notations

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) |
| `n..m` | Range from n to m |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.2.1 Class Diagram Components Reference Table

| Element | UML Notation | Java Realization | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class | Rectangle with 3 compartments | `class ClassName { }` | Blueprint for objects |
| Attribute | `visibility name : type` | Field declaration | Stores object state |
| Operation | `visibility name() : returnType` | Method declaration | Defines object behavior |
| Public | `+` | `public` | Universal access |
| Private | `−` | `private` | Class-restricted access |
| Protected | `#` | `protected` | Subclass access |
| Abstract Class | Class name in *italics* | `abstract class` | Cannot be instantiated |
| Interface | `<<interface>>` | `interface` | Contract definition |
| Association | Solid line | Field reference (loose) | "uses-a" relationship |
| Aggregation | Hollow diamond | Field reference (loose) | "has-a" (weak) |
| Composition | Filled diamond | Field reference (strong) | "has-a" (strong) |
| Inheritance | Hollow triangle arrow | `extends` / `implements` | "is-a" relationship |

### 2.2.2 Java Buzzwords Reference Matrix

| # | Buzzword | KTU-Definition | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple | No pointers, operator overloading, no preprocessor | Easy onboarding for developers |
| 2 | Object-Oriented | Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction | Code reuse, modularity |
| 3 | Platform-Independent | `.class` bytecode runs on any JVM | Cross-platform deployment |
| 4 | Secured | No explicit pointers, bytecode verifier, sandbox | Banking & enterprise apps |
| 5 | Robust | Strong type-checking, exception handling, GC | Mission-critical systems |
| 6 | Architecture-Neutral | Fixed size of primitive types (e.g., `int` = 32 bits) | Portability across CPUs |
| 7 | Portable | Same bytecode → any OS | Android, embedded systems |
| 8 | Multi-threaded | `Thread` class, `Runnable` interface | Web servers, real-time systems |
| 9 | Interpreted | Bytecode line-by-line execution by JVM | Quick prototyping |
| 10 | High-Performance | JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler optimizes bytecode | High-frequency trading |
| 11 | Distributed | RMI, EJB, Sockets for networked apps | Cloud-based applications |
| 12 | Dynamic | Reflection, runtime class loading | Frameworks (Spring, Hibernate) |

### 2.2.3 Java Compilation Pipeline (Reference)

$$
\text{Source Code (.java)} \xrightarrow{\text{javac (Java Compiler)}} \text{Bytecode (.class)} \xrightarrow{\text{JVM Interpreter + JIT}} \text{Machine Code (Native)}
$$

This **three-stage pipeline** is what makes Java platform-independent. The `.class` file is the **portable artifact** that any operating system with a JVM can execute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Class Diagrams** are used in software engineering to model **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** systems, **Banking Software**, and **Embedded System Architectures** before any code is written.
> - **Java** powers **Android (Dalvik/ART VM)**, **Apache Hadoop (Big Data)**, **Jenkins (CI/CD)**, and the entire **Spring Boot** microservice ecosystem. The buzzwords map directly to production requirements: *Security* for fintech, *Multi-threading* for web servers, *Portability* for IoT.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Designing a Class Diagram from a Real-World Scenario

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario (KTU-style 14-mark question framing):**
> *"Design a UML Class Diagram for a University Management System that includes the classes `Student`, `Course`, and `Department`. Show all relevant relationships, attributes, and methods."*

### Step 1: Identify the Nouns (Classes)

From the problem statement, the candidate classes are:
- `Student`
- `Course`
- `Department`

These are the **persistent entities** of the system. Auxiliary classes like `Faculty` or `Result` may be added, but for the KTU answer, we focus on the core three.

### Step 2: Identify the Attributes for Each Class

Following **information hiding** principles (encapsulation), attributes are marked `private` (`−`):

- `Student` → `−rollNo : int`, `−name : String`, `−marks : double`, `−email : String`
- `Course` → `−courseId : String`, `−courseName : String`, `−credits : int`
- `Department` → `−deptId : String`, `−deptName : String`

### Step 3: Identify the Methods (Operations)

Methods are typically `public` (`+`):

- `Student` → `+registerCourse(c : Course) : void`, `+calculateGPA() : double`, `+display() : void`
- `Course` → `+enrollStudent(s : Student) : void`, `+getCourseInfo() : void`
- `Department` → `+addCourse(c : Course) : void`, `+listCourses() : void`

### Step 4: Identify the Relationships

- A `Student` **enrolls in** one or more `Course`s → **Association (many-to-many)**
- A `Department` **offers** `Course`s → **Aggregation (whole–part weak)** — courses can exist without a department in some institutions
- A `Department` **is headed by** a `Faculty` (not in our simplified model, but commonly added)

### Step 5: Apply Multiplicity

- `Student` ↔ `Course` : `1..*` (a student takes at least one course) on Student side, `*` (a course has many students) on Course side.
- `Department` ↔ `Course` : `1` on Department side, `1..*` on Course side.

### Step 6: Verify the Design Against OOP Principles

- **Encapsulation** → Attributes are `private`.
- **Abstraction** → Only essential features exposed via `public` methods.
- **Modularity** → Each class has a single responsibility.

### Final Logical Model Representation

$$
\text{Class Diagram} = \{\text{Classes}\} \cup \{\text{Attributes}\} \cup \{\text{Methods}\} \cup \{\text{Relationships}\} \cup \{\text{Multiplicity}\}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Class Diagram} = \{Student, Course, Department\} \cup \{rollNo, name, marks, courseId, courseName, credits, deptId, deptName\} \cup \{registerCourse(), calculateGPA(), enrollStudent(), \ldots\}
$$

This **exhaustive derivation** is what a 14-mark KTU question expects in textual form, accompanied by the **diagram** in Section 4.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Java Buzzword Justification

For each buzzword, derive **why Java qualifies for that property**. This is a common 7-mark sub-part in KTU questions.

### Step-by-Step Justification for **Platform-Independent**:

$$
\text{Platform-Independence} = \frac{\text{Java Source Code}}{\text{JVM Dependency}} = \text{Bytecode-based execution}
$$

**Reasoning steps:**

1. Java source code is compiled by `javac` into **bytecode** (`.class` file).
2. Bytecode is **not native machine code** — it is a platform-neutral intermediate representation.
3. Each operating system has its **own JVM implementation** that translates bytecode into native instructions.
4. Therefore, the same `.class` file can run on Windows, Linux, macOS, or any device with a JVM — **WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere)**.

Mathematically, if $S$ = source code, $B$ = bytecode, $J_{OS}$ = JVM for OS:

$$
S \xrightarrow{javac} B \xrightarrow{J_{OS}} \text{Native Code}
$$

Since $B$ is identical for all OSes and $J_{OS}$ exists for every major platform, the portability property is **mathematically satisfied**.

### Step-by-Step Justification for **Robust**:

$$
\text{Robustness} = \text{Strong Type-Checking} + \text{Exception Handling} + \text{Automatic Garbage Collection}
$$

1. **Strong Type-Checking** — Compile-time validation prevents type mismatches.
2. **Exception Handling** — `try-catch-finally` and checked exceptions force error handling.
3. **Garbage Collection** — JVM automatically deallocates unreferenced objects, eliminating **dangling pointers** and **memory leaks** common in C/C++.

### Step-by-Step Justification for **Secure**:

$$
\text{Security} = \text{No Pointers} + \text{Bytecode Verifier} + \text{SecurityManager (Sandbox)}
$$

1. Absence of pointers prevents direct memory manipulation.
2. The **Bytecode Verifier** validates bytecode before execution, ensuring no stack overflow or illegal type casts.
3. The **Security Manager** enforces access policies, especially in applet environments.

### Step-by-Step Justification for **Multi-threaded**:

Java provides built-in support for concurrent execution through the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` interface. Multiple threads share the same memory space, enabling efficient concurrent operations — a critical feature for **web servers** like Tomcat.

### Step-by-Step Justification for **Object-Oriented**:

Java is "purely" object-oriented (with the exception of primitive types). All Java code resides within classes. The four pillars of OOP are natively supported:

$$
\text{OOP Pillars} = \{\text{Encapsulation}, \text{Inheritance}, \text{Polymorphism}, \text{Abstraction}\}
$$

Java realizes these via classes, `extends` keyword, method overloading/overriding, and abstract classes/interfaces respectively.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — University Management System

The following Mermaid diagram renders the complete class diagram derived in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        -rollNo : int
        -name : String
        -marks : double
        -email : String
        +registerCourse(c : Course) void
        +calculateGPA() double
        +display() void
    }

    class Course {
        -courseId : String
        -courseName : String
        -credits : int
        +enrollStudent(s : Student) void
        +getCourseInfo() void
    }

    class Department {
        -deptId : String
        -deptName : String
        +addCourse(c : Course) void
        +listCourses() void
    }

    class Faculty {
        -facultyId : int
        -facultyName : String
        -designation : String
        +assignCourse(c : Course) void
    }

    Student "1..*" --o "*" Course : enrolls in
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Course : offers
    Department "1" -- "1" Faculty : headed by
    Faculty "1" -- "*" Course : teaches
```

### Diagram Annotation (How to Read This in KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Notation:**
> - The `−` before attributes denotes **private** visibility.
> - The `+` before methods denotes **public** visibility.
> - The `--o` (hollow diamond) between `Student` and `Course` denotes **Aggregation** (weak ownership).
> - The `o--` between `Department` and `Course` denotes **Aggregation** (Department aggregates courses).
> - The `--` between `Department` and `Faculty` denotes a simple **Association**.
> - The multiplicities (`1..*`, `*`, `1`) define how many instances participate on each side.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Java Compilation & Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Source Code .java] --> B[Java Compiler javac]
    B --> C[Bytecode .class]
    C --> D[Class Loader Subsystem]
    D --> E[Bytecode Verifier]
    E --> F[Interpreter]
    F --> G[JIT Compiler]
    G --> H[Native Machine Code]
    H --> I[Operating System Hardware]
    J[JVM Architecture] -.-> D
    J -.-> E
    J -.-> F
    J -.-> G
    K[Java Runtime Environment JRE] --> J
    L[Java Development Kit JDK] --> K
```

### Architecture Annotation

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Flow Explanation (For KTU 14-mark answers):**
> 1. **`.java` file** — Programmer writes Java source code in any text editor or IDE.
> 2. **`javac` Compiler** — Compiles source into platform-neutral **bytecode (`.class`)**.
> 3. **Class Loader Subsystem** — Loads the `.class` file into JVM memory at runtime.
> 4. **Bytecode Verifier** — Validates that bytecode does not violate Java's safety rules (no forged pointers, no stack overflow, no illegal type casting).
> 5. **Interpreter** — Reads and executes bytecode **line-by-line** (slower path).
> 6. **JIT Compiler** — Converts **frequently executed ("hot") bytecode** into native machine code for performance (faster path).
> 7. **Native Code Execution** — Runs on the underlying hardware via the OS.
>
> This architecture is the **direct cause** of Java's Platform-Independence, Security, Robustness, and High-Performance buzzwords.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Java Buzzword Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Platform Independent] --> A1[Bytecode .class]
    B[Secure] --> B1[No Pointers]
    B --> B2[Bytecode Verifier]
    C[Robust] --> C1[Strong Typing]
    C --> C2[Garbage Collection]
    C --> C3[Exception Handling]
    D[Multi-threaded] --> D1[Thread class]
    D --> D2[Runnable interface]
    E[Dynamic] --> E1[Reflection API]
    E --> E2[Runtime Class Loading]
    F[Object Oriented] --> F1[Classes and Objects]
    F --> F2[Four Pillars Encapsulation Inheritance Polymorphism Abstraction]
```
