# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define a Java compiler. Why does Java use a two-stage compilation process while C/C++ uses a single stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Java compiler (`javac`) is a program supplied with the JDK that translates Java source code (`.java`) into platform-neutral bytecode (`.class`) as defined by the JVM Specification. Java adopts a **two-stage** model because the first stage produces an **intermediate, hardware-independent representation (bytecode)**, and the second stage (the JVM, optionally with JIT) converts this into **native machine code at runtime** on the host platform. This separation delivers **platform independence** ("Write Once, Run Anywhere") and enables **runtime optimizations** (JIT, dynamic class loading) that a single-stage, ahead-of-time C/C++ compiler cannot easily perform. C/C++ compilers must target a **specific CPU + OS combination** at compile time, hence the single stage.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **What is the significance of the magic number `0xCAFEBABE` in a Java `.class` file?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The magic number `0xCAFEBABE` is a **4-byte constant** placed as the very first field of every valid Java `.class` file. It serves three purposes:
1. **Identification:** It allows the JVM and class-loading tools to instantly recognize the file as a Java class file, distinguishing it from arbitrary binary data or other file formats.
2. **Validity Check:** A file without this magic number is rejected by the class loader before any further parsing, providing an early sanity check.
3. **Historical Trivia:** It was chosen by Java's original designers (James Gosling et al.) because hexadecimal `CAFEBABE` is an easily recognizable, playful pattern that fits into a 32-bit word. It is the **only** means of identifying a class file at the binary level; there is no filename convention that the JVM trusts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete architecture of the Java compilation and execution process**, from source code to native CPU execution. Clearly distinguish between the role of `javac`, the JVM, and the JIT compiler. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **internal structure of a Java `.class` file** with a labeled diagram. What is the role of the **constant pool**, and why does it use 1-based indexing? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Source Code to Bytecode (javac):** The programmer writes `MyApp.java`. The Java compiler `javac` performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, and emits bytecode stored in `MyApp.class`. This is the **development-time** stage and is platform-independent.

**Step 2 — Class Loading (JVM):** When `java MyApp` is invoked, the JVM's **Class Loader Subsystem** (Bootstrap → Platform → Application) locates `MyApp.class`, reads it, and stores its constant pool, method bytecodes, and field layouts in the **Method Area** of JVM memory.

**Step 3 — Verification:** The **Bytecode Verifier** statically analyzes the loaded bytecode to ensure type safety, stack balance, and that no illegal casts or jumps occur. This is a key Java security feature.

**Step 4 — Interpretation & JIT:** The **Interpreter** initially executes bytecode instruction-by-instruction. The **JIT Compiler** monitors method execution frequency; once a method becomes "hot" (default threshold ≈ 10,000 invocations), the JIT compiles it to **native machine code** and caches it in the **Code Cache**. Subsequent invocations execute directly on the host CPU.

**Step 5 — Distinction Table:**

| Compiler | When it runs | Input | Output | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `javac` | Development time | `.java` | `.class` | Portability |
| JIT | Runtime | Bytecode | Native code | Speed |

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

The `.class` file is a strict binary format defined in JVMS §4. Its layout, in order, is:
1. **Magic Number** — `0xCAFEBABE` (4 bytes)
2. **Minor & Major Version** — e.g., `0x0034` = Java 8
3. **Constant Pool Count** — number of entries `+ 1`
4. **Constant Pool** — a table of tagged entries: `Utf8`, `Integer`, `Float`, `Class`, `String`, `Fieldref`, `Methodref`, `InterfaceMethodref`, `NameAndType`, etc.
5. **Access Flags** — e.g., `ACC_PUBLIC`, `ACC_FINAL`, `ACC_SUPER`
6. **This Class** — index into the constant pool
7. **Super Class** — index into the constant pool (always `java/lang/Object` for `Object` itself)
8. **Interfaces** — count + index array
9. **Fields** — count + field_info structures
10. **Methods** — count + method_info structures (each containing `Code`, `LineNumberTable`, etc.)
11. **Attributes** — count + attribute_info structures (class-level metadata)

**Role of the Constant Pool:** It is the **literal/structural repository** of the class. All symbolic references (class names, method signatures, field names, string literals, numeric constants) are stored here and referenced by **1-based index** from elsewhere in the file. **1-based indexing** is a historical JVM design choice (JVM spec §4.4) — index `0` is reserved as an "invalid" sentinel that can be used by the verifier to indicate "no constant pool entry." This simplifies verification logic at the cost of a small indexing offset.

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the **Java compiler (`javac`)** with a **traditional C/C++ compiler (`gcc`)** across at least six parameters. Why is Java called a **platform-independent** language? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation**? Explain how it combines **portability with performance**, with reference to HotSpot's tiered compilation (C1 and C2 compilers). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (any 6 of these are required):**

| Parameter | `javac` (Java) | `gcc` (C/C++) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input | `.java` | `.c` / `.cpp` / `.h` |
| Output | `.class` (bytecode) | Native executable |
| Target | JVM (virtual, portable) | CPU + OS (physical, fixed) |
| Portability | High (WORA) | Low (recompile per platform) |
| Optimization | Minimal (deferred) | Aggressive (compile-time) |
| Linking | Runtime, dynamic | Compile-time (static or dynamic) |
| Memory safety | Compile + runtime verifier | Mostly programmer's responsibility |
| Tool name | `javac` | `gcc` / `clang` |

**Why Java is platform-independent:** The Java compiler does not produce machine code for a specific processor. It produces **bytecode**, an abstract instruction set defined by the JVM Specification. Every conforming JVM — whether on Windows x86-64, Linux ARM64, macOS Apple Silicon, or a Raspberry Pi — implements the same bytecode semantics. Therefore, the same `MyApp.class` file executes identically on any device with a JVM, achieving true **binary portability** (the same bytes run everywhere).

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Definition:** **JIT compilation** is a runtime optimization technique in which the JVM identifies frequently executed ("hot") bytecode and compiles it into **native machine code for the host CPU** at runtime, caching the result for future invocations.

**Why it exists:** Pure interpretation is portable but slow. Ahead-of-time native compilation is fast but not portable. JIT is a **hybrid** that retains portability *and* approaches native speed for long-running programs.

**HotSpot Tiered Compilation (C1 + C2):**
* **C1 (Client Compiler):** A fast, lightly optimizing compiler. Used in the early stages to quickly convert hot methods to native code with basic optimizations (inlining, simple range checks). Low compilation overhead, moderate performance.
* **C2 (Server Compiler):** A slow, aggressively optimizing compiler. Uses advanced techniques — **escape analysis**, **loop unrolling**, **intrinsics**, **profile-guided speculation** — based on runtime profiling data collected during interpretation and C1 execution. Produces highly optimized native code for steady-state performance.
* **Tiered Compilation Levels:** HotSpot blends 5 tiers — interpreted → C1 with profiling → C1 with counters → C2 — letting the JVM balance startup time, memory, and peak throughput dynamically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating `javac` and JIT:** Many answers wrongly credit "the Java compiler" with JIT. They are *two different compilers* working at *two different times*. Be explicit.
> 2. **Forgetting the magic number:** When asked about `.class` file structure, students often skip `0xCAFEBABE` — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Ignoring the constant pool:** Merely listing fields of a `.class` file without explaining the *role* of the constant pool loses at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Saying Java is "slow":** This is outdated. With modern HotSpot/Graal JIT, Java is within 10% of C++ for many server workloads. Examiners penalize blanket "Java is slow" statements.
> 5. **Confusing `JDK` and `JRE`:** `javac` lives in the **JDK**, not the JRE. A user with only the JRE *cannot* compile Java — they can only run it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Java Compiler = `javac`** — translates `.java` → `.class` (bytecode).
- **Output is platform-independent bytecode**, not native code.
- **Magic Number `0xCAFEBABE`** identifies a valid `.class` file (4 bytes, first field).
- **Two-stage model** is the basis of **WORA — Write Once, Run Anywhere**.
- **Constant Pool** is a 1-based indexed table of literals, names, and type descriptors.
- **`javac` does very little optimization** — the **JIT compiler** in the JVM does the heavy lifting at runtime.
- **JIT tiers:** C1 (fast, light) → C2 (slow, aggressive) for profile-guided optimization.
- **Class file structure** (in order): Magic → Version → Constant Pool → Access Flags → This Class → Super Class → Interfaces → Fields → Methods → Attributes.
- **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM ⊃ JIT** (JIT is *inside* the JVM).
- **Bytecode opcodes are 1 byte wide** — hence the name "bytecode."
- **`javac` exit code 0** = success; **non-zero** = compilation error.
- **The Bytecode Verifier** (a JVM component, not a `javac` component) ensures type safety at runtime — a major Java security feature.
- **Common KTU one-liner:** *"Java is compiled and interpreted — compiled by `javac` into bytecode, interpreted (and JIT-compiled) by the JVM."*
- **Compilation is per-type, not per-file-forward-references** in Java are limited to class-level, not method-level, in some cases.
- **Platform independence = JVM dependence:** Java is portable, but *requires* a JVM on every target platform — the trade-off cost.
- **Stack-based ISA:** The JVM uses a **stack machine** model, unlike x86 (which is register-based) — this further simplifies bytecode portability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bytecode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bytecode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bytecode** is an intermediate, low-level, platform-independent instruction set designed for execution by a software-based virtual machine (the **Java Virtual Machine / JVM**) rather than directly by a physical Central Processing Unit (CPU).

In Object Oriented Programming (OOP) with Java (the reference language for KTU's PEEET419 course), the source code written by the programmer (`.java` file) is NOT compiled into machine code native to the underlying hardware. Instead, the Java compiler (`javac`) translates the source code into **bytecode** — a highly optimized set of instructions stored in `.class` files. Each instruction in this intermediate language is exactly **1 byte (8 bits)** in length, hence the name *bytecode*. The full executable specification of this instruction set is defined in **The Java Virtual Machine Specification (JVMS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Bytecode is the *technological backbone* of Java's famous **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** paradigm. The KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly focuses on how OOP achieves platform independence, and Bytecode is the *mechanism* through which that promise is delivered.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a world-renowned chef who has written a **master recipe** in a language that only you understand (your native tongue). You want this exact recipe to be cooked perfectly in kitchens located in **Paris, Tokyo, New York, and Mumbai** — each with completely different stoves, ovens, and equipment.

Instead of translating your recipe four times into four different languages for four different kitchen setups (which would be expensive and error-prone), you do something clever:

1. You write the recipe in a **universal "kitchen-agnostic" language** (this is the **bytecode**).
2. In every city, you hire a **local interpreter-translator** who reads your universal language and converts it into precise instructions for *that specific* kitchen's stove and equipment (this local interpreter is the **JVM** — different for Windows, Linux, macOS, etc.).

**The Result:** Your master recipe (program) is written ONCE, but runs EVERYWHERE — because every kitchen has its own local translator (JVM) that knows how to read the universal language (bytecode) and execute it on the local hardware.

In this analogy:
- **Master Recipe** → Java Source Code (`.java` file)
- **Universal Kitchen Language** → **Bytecode** (`.class` file)
- **Local Interpreters** → Platform-Specific JVMs (Windows JVM, Linux JVM, macOS JVM)
- **Local Kitchens** → Physical CPUs and Operating Systems

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

The following technical constants define bytecode's operational envelope:

- **Instruction Length:** **1 byte (8 bits) per opcode** (though operands may follow, making a single instruction 1–5 bytes total).
- **Opcode Range:** **0 to 255 (decimal)** — i.e., 256 possible opcodes, though not all are defined.
- **File Extension:** **`.class`** for individual class files; **`.jar`** (Java ARchive) for packaged collections of `.class` files.
- **Magic Number:** Every `.class` file begins with the bytes **`0xCAFEBABE`** (in hexadecimal) — a signature used by the JVM to instantly identify a valid class file.
- **Total Defined Opcodes in Modern JVM (Java 17+):** approximately **200 opcodes**, with a theoretical maximum of **256**.
- **Stack-Based Execution:** Bytecode is fundamentally designed around a **stack-based execution model** (operand stack + local variables array), as opposed to register-based machine code used by physical CPUs.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The magic number `0xCAFEBABE` is a frequently asked KTU question. Remember it is written in **big-endian** format and is literally a "magic" signature — JVMs reject any file that does not start with these exact 4 bytes!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Compilation-Execution Model

Java's use of bytecode introduces a **two-stage** lifecycle for every program, fundamentally different from traditional compiled languages like C or C++.

### Stage 1: Compilation (Source Code → Bytecode)
The Java compiler `javac` performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, and optimization on the `.java` source file. The output is a `.class` file containing:
- The **constant pool** (literal strings, class/method names, type descriptors)
- **Method bytecode sequences** (the actual instructions)
- **Field and method metadata**
- **Class hierarchy information** (interfaces, superclass)

### Stage 2: Execution (Bytecode → Native Machine Code)
The JVM loads the `.class` file and executes the bytecode using one (or a combination) of:
- **Interpreter:** Reads bytecode one instruction at a time and dispatches it.
- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compiler:** Compiles "hot" (frequently executed) bytecode methods into native machine code at runtime for performance.
- **Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Compilation:** (Java 9+) Compiles bytecode to a native shared library at build time.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Source Creation:** The programmer writes `HelloWorld.java`.
2. **Compilation:** Running `javac HelloWorld.java` produces `HelloWorld.class` containing bytecode.
3. **Class Loading:** The JVM's **ClassLoader Subsystem** reads the `.class` file and stores it in the **Method Area** of JVM memory.
4. **Bytecode Verification:** The **Bytecode Verifier** checks the loaded code for safety, type correctness, stack overflows, and illegal operations. This is a *security-critical* step.
5. **Execution:** The **Execution Engine** (interpreter + JIT) processes instructions, pushing/popping values on the **Operand Stack** and reading/writing to **Local Variable Arrays**.
6. **Runtime Data Interaction:** The program interacts with memory, system calls, and the underlying OS via the **JNI (Java Native Interface)**.

## 2.3 Structural Anatomy of a Bytecode Instruction

Each instruction has the format:
```
[ opcode (1 byte) ]  [ operand(s) (0–4 bytes) ]
```

Example disassembly (using `javap -c HelloWorld`):
```
0: aload_0
1: invokespecial #1   // Method java/lang/Object."<init>":()V
4: return
```

- `aload_0` — opcode `0x2A` — load `this` reference onto the operand stack
- `invokespecial` — opcode `0xB7` — call instance initialization method
- `return` — opcode `0xB1` — return void from method

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

| **Property** | **Value / Formula** | **Significance** |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Opcode size | **1 byte (8 bits)** | Defines the name "bytecode" |
| Total possible opcodes | $2^8 = 256$ | Theoretical maximum instruction set |
| Magic number (`.class` file) | `0xCAFEBABE` | JVM file-identification signature |
| Class file version (Java 17) | Major = **61** | Indicates compiler version compatibility |
| Instruction categories | **9 categories** | See table below |
| Execution model | **Stack-based** | Operand stack + local variables |
| Native alternative model | **Register-based** | Used by physical CPUs (x86, ARM) |

### Instruction Categories (JVMS Classification)

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Function** |
|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Load/Store** | `iload`, `astore`, `ldc` | Move data between stack and local variables |
| **Arithmetic** | `iadd`, `imul`, `fdiv` | Perform mathematical operations |
| **Type Conversion** | `i2f`, `f2d`, `d2i` | Convert between primitive types |
| **Object Creation/Manipulation** | `new`, `newarray`, `getfield` | Instantiate and access objects |
| **Operand Stack Management** | `pop`, `dup`, `swap` | Manipulate the top of the stack |
| **Control Transfer** | `ifeq`, `goto`, `tableswitch` | Branching, loops, method returns |
| **Method Invocation/Return** | `invokevirtual`, `invokestatic`, `ireturn` | Call and return from methods |
| **Exception Handling** | `athrow`, `jsr` (deprecated) | Throw and catch exceptions |
| **Synchronization** | `monitorenter`, `monitorexit` | Implement `synchronized` blocks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bytecode is the cornerstone of **cross-platform software engineering**, **dynamic class loading**, **runtime reflection**, **aspect-oriented programming** (e.g., Spring AOP uses bytecode manipulation via libraries like ASM/CGLIB), and **mobile/sandboxed execution** (Android's Dalvik/ART uses a similar bytecode concept adapted from JVM).

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Bytecode: Platform Independence

The traditional compile-to-native approach binds a program to ONE specific (CPU + OS) combination. To target a new platform, **the entire compilation must be redone**, and sometimes the source code itself must be modified (e.g., 32-bit vs 64-bit pointer differences, endianness, system calls).

Bytecode solves this by introducing a **virtual instruction set** that is:
- **Stable** across decades (backward compatibility is a core JVM promise)
- **Compact** (1-byte opcodes are dense and network-friendly)
- **Verifiable** (provably type-safe before execution)
- **Portable** (the same `.class` file runs on any device with a JVM)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Java Source to Bytecode — A Worked Example

Consider the following Java source code:

```java
public class Calculator {
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
}
```

**Step 1 — Compilation:**
Running `javac Calculator.java` produces `Calculator.class`.

**Step 2 — Disassembly (using `javap -c -p Calculator`):**
The bytecode generated is:
```
public int add(int, int);
  Code:
    0: iload_1          // Push local variable a (int) onto operand stack
    1: iload_2          // Push local variable b (int) onto operand stack
    2: iadd             // Pop two ints, push their sum
    3: ireturn          // Return the top int from the method
```

**Step 3 — Trace the Stack Execution:**

| **Instruction** | **Opcode (Hex)** | **Operand Stack (after)** | **Locals Array** |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| *(method entry)* | — | `[ ]` (empty) | `[this, a, b]` |
| `iload_1` | `0x1B` | `[ a ]` | `[this, a, b]` |
| `iload_2` | `0x1C` | `[ a, b ]` | `[this, a, b]` |
| `iadd` | `0x60` | `[ a+b ]` | `[this, a, b]` |
| `ireturn` | `0xAC` | `[ ]` (returned) | — |

**Step 4 — Verification of Correctness:**

We can formally express the stack-state transition as a function. Let $S_n$ be the state of the operand stack after instruction $n$:

$$
S_0 = \varnothing \quad \text{(empty stack at method entry)}
$$

$$
S_1 = S_0 \cup \{a\} = \{a\} \quad \text{(after iload\_1)}
$$

$$
S_2 = S_1 \cup \{b\} = \{a, b\} \quad \text{(after iload\_2)}
$$

$$
S_3 = (S_2 \setminus \{a, b\}) \cup \{a + b\} = \{a + b\} \quad \text{(after iadd)}
$$

$$
S_4 = \varnothing \quad \text{(after ireturn, value handed to caller)}
$$

The **stack depth at any point** must never exceed `max_stack` declared in the `.class` file. The JVM verifies this constraint at load time.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Execution Models

| **Dimension** | **Native Machine Code (C/C++)** | **Bytecode (Java)** | **Hybrid JIT (Modern Java)** |
|---------------|----------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Generation Time | Before execution (AOT) | Before execution (compile) + JIT at runtime | Both |
| Target Hardware | Specific CPU (x86, ARM, etc.) | Abstract virtual machine | Specific CPU (after JIT) |
| Portability | Poor — recompile per platform | Excellent — same `.class` everywhere | Excellent |
| Startup Performance | Fast | Slow (interpreter overhead) | Moderate → Fast (after warmup) |
| Peak Performance | Fastest | Slowest | Near-native |
| Security Verification | None at runtime | Mandatory bytecode verification | Mandatory bytecode verification |
| File Size | Small | Small to medium (with metadata) | Same as bytecode |
| Reversibility (Decompilation) | Very hard | Easy (JVMs are designed for this) | Easy before JIT, hard after |

## 3.3 Derivation: Class File Internal Structure (Constant Pool Size)

A `.class` file follows a strict binary format defined by the JVMS. The structure is:

$$
\text{ClassFile} = \text{magic} \oplus \text{version} \oplus \text{constant\_pool} \oplus \text{access\_flags} \oplus \text{this\_class} \oplus \text{super\_class} \oplus \text{interfaces} \oplus \text{fields} \oplus \text{methods} \oplus \text{attributes}
$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes sequential concatenation. The `constant_pool` is a table of `CONSTANT_*` entries. Its size is declared in the class file as `constant_pool_count` (a 2-byte unsigned integer), and the actual number of entries is:

$$
N_{\text{entries}} = \text{constant\_pool\_count} - 1
$$

**Why subtract 1?** The index 0 is reserved (it indicates "no constant") — a common JVM convention inherited from early design decisions. The valid indices therefore range from 1 to 255 for `u1` indices, or 1 to 65535 for `u2` indices.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A Java class file declares `constant_pool_count = 47`. How many actual constant pool entries does it contain? Justify using the JVMS rule.

**Solution:**

$$
N_{\text{entries}} = \text{constant\_pool\_count} - 1 = 47 - 1 = 46
$$

Therefore, the class file contains **46 valid constant pool entries** indexed from 1 to 46.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Java Program Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[".java Source Code"] -->|javac compiler| B[".class Bytecode File"]
    B -->|Class Loader| C[JVM Method Area]
    C -->|Bytecode Verifier| D{Verified?}
    D -->|No| E[VerificationError Exception]
    D -->|Yes| F[Execution Engine]
    F --> G[Interpreter]
    F --> H[JIT Compiler]
    G --> I[Native Machine Code]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Operating System]
    J --> K[Physical CPU]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: JVM Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JVM [Java Virtual Machine]
        CL[Class Loader Subsystem]
        BA[Bytecode Verifier]
        ME[Method Area]
        H[Heap Memory]
        S[Stack Memory]
        EE[Execution Engine]
        INT[Interpreter]
        JIT[JIT Compiler]
        GC[Garbage Collector]
    end
    
    CL --> BA
    BA --> ME
    EE --> INT
    EE --> JIT
    INT --> S
    JIT --> S
    GC --> H
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: Bytecode Execution via Operand Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Method Called]) --> Load1[iload_1: Push a onto Stack]
    Load1 --> Load2[iload_2: Push b onto Stack]
    Load2 --> Add[iadd: Pop a,b; Push a+b]
    Add --> Return[ireturn: Hand result to caller]
    Return --> End([Method Returns])
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Native vs Bytecode Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Native [Native Compilation - C/C++]
        S1[".c Source"] -->|gcc| N1[".exe Native Binary"]
        N1 --> N2[Windows CPU]
        N1 --> N3[Recompile for Linux]
        N3 --> N4[Linux CPU]
    end
    
    subgraph Byte [Bytecode Compilation - Java]
        S2[".java Source"] -->|javac| B1[".class Bytecode"]
        B1 --> B2[Windows JVM]
        B2 --> B3[Windows CPU]
        B1 --> B5[Linux JVM]
        B5 --> B6[Linux CPU]
        B1 --> B7[macOS JVM]
        B7 --> B8[macOS CPU]
    end
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: .class File Binary Format

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Magic Number: CAFEBABE] --> B[Minor Version: u2]
    B --> C[Major Version: u2]
    C --> D[Constant Pool Count: u2]
    D --> E[Constant Pool Table]
    E --> F[Access Flags: u2]
    F --> G[This Class: u2]
    G --> H[Super Class: u2]
    H --> I[Interfaces Count: u2]
    I --> J[Interfaces Table]
    J --> K[Fields Count: u2]
    K --> L[Fields Table]
    L --> M[Methods Count: u2]
    M --> N[Methods Table]
    N --> O[Attributes Count: u2]
    O --> P[Attributes Table]
```

---
