The leading `1.` of the significand is the **implicit/hidden bit** and is not stored. The exponent range for normal numbers is $1 \le E \le 254$ (corresponding to true exponents $-126$ to $+127$).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** $|{-19.625}| = 19.625$ — apply the same conversion as in Section 3.1.

**Step 2:** $19.625_{(10)} = 10011.101_{(2)} = 1.0011101 \times 2^{4}$

**Step 3 — Fields:**

- Sign $S = \mathbf{1}$ (negative)
- True exponent $e = 4$
- Biased exponent $E = 4 + 127 = 131 = \mathbf{10000011_{(2)}}$
- Mantissa $M = \mathbf{00111010000000000000000}$ (padded to 23 bits)

**Step 4 — Final 32-bit word:**

$$ \boxed{1 \;|\; 10000011 \;|\; 00111010000000000000000} $$

**Two Special Values:**

1. `Float.NaN` — exponent = `11111111`, mantissa $\ne 0$
2. `Float.POSITIVE_INFINITY` — exponent = `11111111`, mantissa = `000...0`, sign = `0`

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Double Precision & Subnormals

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Compare `float` and `double` with respect to memory, range, precision, and default literal assignment. Draw the bit layout of the IEEE 754 double-precision format.

**(b)** Explain the concept of *denormalized (subnormal) numbers* in IEEE 754. Convert $85.125$ to IEEE 754 single-precision format with full working.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

| Property | `float` | `double` |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | 32 bits | 64 bits |
| Exponent field | 8 bits (bias 127) | 11 bits (bias 1023) |
| Mantissa | 23 bits | 52 bits |
| Decimal precision | 7 digits | 15–16 digits |
| Default literal? | No | **Yes** |
| Range | $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $\pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ |

**Bit layout of `double` (64 bits):**

$$ \underbrace{S}_{1} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{E}_{11} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{M}_{52} $$

A normalized double-precision value is computed as:

$$ V = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - 1023)} $$

The mantissa provides an effective **53 bits** of precision (52 stored + 1 implicit), which is why `double` is the default for high-accuracy scientific work.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept of Subnormals (5 marks content distributed in 7-mark answer):**

When the stored exponent $E = 0$ and the mantissa is **non-zero**, the number is **subnormal** (denormalized). The implicit leading 1 is now **0** instead of 1, and the true exponent is fixed at $1 - \text{bias}$ (i.e., $-126$ for `float`, $-1022$ for `double`). This allows the gradual underflow of tiny numbers down to $\pm 0$, preserving **uniform spacing** near zero and reducing the loss of precision that would otherwise occur.

**Conversion of $85.125$ (3 marks):**

- $85_{(10)} = 1010101_{(2)}$
- $0.125_{(10)} = 0.001_{(2)}$
- $85.125 = 1010101.001_{(2)} = 1.010101001 \times 2^{6}$

Fields:
- $S = 0$ (positive)
- $E = 6 + 127 = 133 = 10000101_{(2)}$
- $M = 010101001\underbrace{00000000000000}_{14 \text{ zeros}}$

**Final 32-bit word:**

$$ \boxed{0 \;|\; 10000101 \;|\; 01010100100000000000000} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias (–2 marks):** Many students store the *true* exponent directly. The IEEE 754 stored exponent is *always* biased. Adding the bias is mandatory.
> 2. **Including the leading 1 in the mantissa (–2 marks):** The implicit bit is **not** stored. The mantissa is the bits *after* the binary point.
> 3. **Padding errors (–1 mark):** Mantissa must be exactly 23 bits (float) or 52 bits (double). Trailing zeros must be appended on the **right**.
> 4. **Sign error on negative numbers (–1 mark):** A negative decimal number always sets $S = 1$, regardless of magnitude.
> 5. **Confusing 0.1 with exact representation (–1 mark):** $0.1_{(10)}$ is *non-terminating* in binary. In a `double`, it is stored as the nearest 53-bit approximation. Always be ready to explain this in viva.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two floating-point primitives in Java:** `float` (32-bit) and `double` (64-bit).
- **Default literal type:** `double` — use the `f` or `F` suffix to force `float`.
- **Standard:** Both conform to **IEEE 754**.
- **`float` layout:** $1$ sign + $8$ exponent + $23$ mantissa = 32 bits; bias = **127**.
- **`double` layout:** $1$ sign + $11$ exponent + $52$ mantissa = 64 bits; bias = **1023**.
- **Mathematical form:** $V = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{bias})}$.
- **Implicit bit:** The leading `1.` of the significand is not stored, giving 24/53 bits of effective precision.
- **Special values:** $\pm 0$ (exponent 0, mantissa 0), $\pm\infty$ (exponent all 1s, mantissa 0), **NaN** (exponent all 1s, mantissa non-zero), **subnormals** (exponent 0, mantissa non-zero).
- **Wrapper classes:** `java.lang.Float` and `java.lang.Double` — provide `MIN_VALUE`, `MAX_VALUE`, `NaN`, `POSITIVE_INFINITY`, `NEGATIVE_INFINITY`.
- **Conversion algorithm:** Decimal → binary integer + binary fraction → concatenate → normalize → fill sign, biased exponent, mantissa.
- **Avoid binary rounding surprises:** Use `BigDecimal` for monetary calculations; never compare floats with `==`.
- **Highest-yield KTU topics:** Bit layout, conversion with full working, special values, and the *exact* bias values (127 and 1023).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Characters # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **C++ Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a **Character** is a fundamental primitive data type used to store a single textual symbol—such as a letter, digit, punctuation mark, or control code. The C++ language represents characters using integral numeric codes standardized by the **American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII)** for narrow execution character sets, and **Unicode (ISO/IEC 10646)** for wide and universal character sets. The C++ standard defines the following character types: | Type | Size (Typical) | Purpose | Header | |------|----------------|---------|--------| | `char` | **1 byte (8 bits)** | Default character type, implementation-defined signedness | `` | | `signed char` | 1 byte | Explicitly signed small integer (-128 to 127) | `` | | `unsigned char` | 1 byte | Explicitly unsigned small integer (0 to 255) | `` | | `wchar_t` | 2 or 4 bytes | Wide character (locale-dependent) | `` | | `char16_t` | 2 bytes | UTF-16 code unit (C++11 onwards) | `` | | `char32_t` | 4 bytes | UTF-32 code unit (C++11 onwards) | `` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 OOP module explicitly emphasizes the difference between `char`, `signed char`, and `unsigned char`—these are **three distinct types** in C++, even though they share the same memory size. This is a high-yield KTU exam point frequently tested in Part A questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of characters as **the individual LEGO bricks of language**. Just as LEGO bricks come in different sizes and colors but snap together to form a sentence (a `std::string`), characters are the smallest meaningful textual unit a computer understands. - **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a **postal sorting office**. Every letter (mail piece) has a tiny code stamped on it (a character). The sorting machine doesn't read the actual shape of the letter—it reads the **numeric code** (ASCII number) and routes it accordingly. Similarly, a computer stores `'A'` not as the visual letter "A," but as the integer **65** in memory. - **Storage intuition:** A `char` variable is a **locker with exactly 8 compartments** (1 byte). It can hold any pattern from `00000000` (0) to `11111111` (255 unsigned / -128 signed). Each pattern represents a unique character per the ASCII table. ## 1.3 Character Literals and Escape Sequences A **character literal** is a single character enclosed in **single quotes** (apostrophes), e.g., `'A'`, `'7'`, `'?'`. This is distinct from a string literal, which uses double quotes. ```cpp char grade = 'A'; // Character literal char digit = '7'; // The character 7, NOT the number 7 char newline = '\n'; // Escape sequence ``` **Common C++ Escape Sequences (KTU High-Frequency):** | Escape | Meaning | ASCII Value (Decimal) | |--------|---------|----------------------| | `\n` | Newline | **10** | | `\t` | Horizontal tab | **9** | | `\r` | Carriage return | **13** | | `\\` | Backslash | **92** | | `\'` | Single quote | **39** | | `\"` | Double quote | **34** | | `\0` | Null character (string terminator) | **0** | | `\a` | Alert (bell) | **7** | | `\b` | Backspace | **8** | | `\f` | Form feed | **12** | | `\v` | Vertical tab | **11** | | `\?` | Question mark | **63** | > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — ASCII:** ASCII is a **7-bit character encoding** standard covering **128 characters** (0–127). It includes 95 printable characters (codes 32–126) and 33 non-printable control characters (codes 0–31 and 127). Extended ASCII (8-bit, codes 128–255) is **not standardized** and varies by code page. ## 1.4 Unicode and Wide Characters Modern C++ (C++11 and later, aligned with KTU 2024 expectations) supports **Unicode** through wide and universal character types: - **`wchar_t`**: Wide character type whose size is implementation-defined (typically 2 bytes on Windows, 4 bytes on Linux). Used for locale-specific wide character sets. - **Prefix `L`**: Wide character/string literal, e.g., `L'A'`, `L"Hello"`. - **Prefix `u8`**: UTF-8 encoded string literal (C++11), e.g., `u8"Hello"`. - **Prefix `u`**: UTF-16 encoded literal, e.g., `u"A"`. - **Prefix `U`**: UTF-32 encoded literal, e.g., `U"A"`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The OOP module in KTU 2024 places primary emphasis on `char` type, character I/O, and the `` library functions. Unicode/wide characters are an awareness-level topic, not heavily tested. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 Internal Storage Architecture A `char` variable occupies **exactly 1 byte (8 bits)** in memory. The interpretation of the bit pattern follows the **execution character set**, which is guaranteed by the C++ standard to be a superset of the basic character set (letters, digits, punctuation, control codes). The mathematical relationship between a `char` value and its ASCII code is given by: $$c_{ascii} = \text{value of bit pattern interpreted as unsigned integer}$$ For example, the character `'A'`: $$ \text{Bit pattern: } 01000001_2 = 0 \cdot 128 + 1 \cdot 64 + 0 \cdot 32 + 0 \cdot 16 + 0 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 65_{10} $$ ## 2.2 The `` Header — KTU High-Yield Functions The `` header (C-style ``) provides a rich set of **character classification** and **case conversion** functions. These are extensively tested in KTU exams. ### Character Classification Functions (Return `int` — nonzero if true, 0 if false) | Function | Purpose | Example | Returns | |----------|---------|---------|---------| | `isalpha(c)` | Is alphabetic? | `isalpha('A')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isdigit(c)` | Is digit (0–9)? | `isdigit('5')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isalnum(c)` | Is alphanumeric? | `isalnum('A')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isspace(c)` | Is whitespace? | `isspace(' ')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isupper(c)` | Is uppercase? | `isupper('Z')` | Nonzero (true) | | `islower(c)` | Is lowercase? | `islower('a')` | Nonzero (true) | | `ispunct(c)` | Is punctuation? | `ispunct('!')` | Nonzero (true) | | `iscntrl(c)` | Is control char? | `iscntrl('\n')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isprint(c)` | Is printable? | `isprint('A')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isxdigit(c)` | Is hex digit? | `isxdigit('F')` | Nonzero (true) | | `isgraph(c)` | Printable except space? | `isgraph('A')` | Nonzero (true) | ### Case Conversion Functions | Function | Purpose | Example | Returns | |----------|---------|---------|---------| | `toupper(c)` | To uppercase | `toupper('a')` | `'A'` (65) | | `tolower(c)` | To lowercase | `tolower('Z')` | `'z'` (122) | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** All `` functions take an `int` (promoted `char`) and return an `int` — **NOT** a `bool`. Passing a `char` with value outside `unsigned char` range + `EOF` results in **undefined behavior (UB)**. Always cast: `static_cast(c)`. ## 2.3 Character I/O Operations In C++, characters can be input/output using: ### Method 1: `std::cin` / `std::cout` with Extraction/Insertion Operators ```cpp char ch; std::cin >> ch; // Skips leading whitespace, reads ONE character std::cout << ch; // Outputs the character ``` ### Method 2: `get()` Function (Reads Whitespace Too) ```cpp char ch; std::cin.get(ch); // Reads ANY single character, including ' ', '\n', '\t' ``` ### Method 3: `getline()` for Strings of Characters ```cpp char name[50]; std::cin.getline(name, 50); // Reads a line of characters ``` ## 2.4 ASCII Table — KTU Quick Reference | Range (Decimal) | Category | |-----------------|----------| | 0–31 | Control characters (non-printable) | | 32 | Space | | 48–57 | Digits `'0'` to `'9'` | | 65–90 | Uppercase `'A'` to `'Z'` | | 97–122 | Lowercase `'a'` to `'z'` | | 127 | DEL (control character) | **Important ASCII Arithmetic Patterns (KTU Favorites):** $$ \text{Distance between uppercase and lowercase of same letter} = 32 $$ $$ \text{ASCII}('a') - \text{ASCII}('A') = 97 - 65 = 32 $$ To convert uppercase to lowercase **without** using library functions: $$ c_{lower} = c_{upper} + 32 $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Character processing is foundational in: - **Lexical analyzers** of compilers (tokenizing source code) - **Text editors and IDEs** (character-by-character rendering) - **Network protocols** (parsing headers character by character) - **Cryptographic systems** (Caesar cipher, ROT13, Vigenère cipher) - **Embedded systems** (reading sensor data encoded as ASCII streams) - **Database systems** (character encoding for internationalization) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In **ROT13** (a special case of the Caesar cipher used in web forums to hide spoilers), every alphabetic character is replaced by the letter 13 positions after it. This works perfectly in ASCII because there are exactly 26 letters, and 13 × 2 = 26. The formula is: > $$\text{ROT13}(c) = \begin{cases} ((c - \text{base}) + 13) \bmod 26 + \text{base} & \text{if } c \text{ is a letter} \\ c & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > where `base` = `'A'` (65) for uppercase or `'a'` (97) for lowercase. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ASCII Conversion Between Cases **Problem:** Derive the formula to convert an uppercase letter to lowercase using only arithmetic (no library function). **Step 1 — Establish the baseline.** The ASCII value of `'A'` is 65. The ASCII value of `'a'` is 97. $$ \text{ASCII}('A') = 65, \quad \text{ASCII}('a') = 97 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the offset.** $$ \text{offset} = 97 - 65 = 32 $$ **Step 3 — State the general relationship.** For any letter, the lowercase letter lies exactly **32 positions above** the uppercase letter in the ASCII table. $$ \text{ASCII}(\text{lower}(c)) = \text{ASCII}(c) + 32 $$ **Step 4 — Verify with 'Z'.** $$ \text{ASCII}('z') = \text{ASCII}('Z') + 32 = 90 + 32 = 122 \checkmark $$ **Step 5 — Verify with 'M'.** $$ \text{ASCII}('m') = \text{ASCII}('M') + 32 = 77 + 32 = 109 \checkmark $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Digit Character to Numeric Value **Problem:** Convert the character `'7'` (ASCII 55) to the integer 7. **Step 1 — Identify the ASCII range of digits.** All digits `'0'` to `'9'` occupy consecutive ASCII values from 48 to 57. $$ \text{ASCII}('0') = 48, \quad \text{ASCII}('9') = 57 $$ **Step 2 — Derive the conversion formula.** For any digit character `c`: $$ \text{numeric\_value}(c) = \text{ASCII}(c) - \text{ASCII}('0') = \text{ASCII}(c) - 48 $$ **Step 3 — Apply to '7'.** $$ \text{numeric\_value}('7') = 55 - 48 = 7 \checkmark $$ **Step 4 — Apply to '0' (edge case).** $$ \text{numeric\_value}('0') = 48 - 48 = 0 \checkmark $$ **Step 5 — Apply to '9' (edge case).** $$ \text{numeric\_value}('9') = 57 - 48 = 9 \checkmark $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Numeric Value to Digit Character **Inverse operation** — given integer `n` (0 ≤ n ≤ 9), produce the character: $$ \text{char}(n) = \text{char}(n + 48) = \text{char}(n + \text{ASCII}('0')) $$ For example, integer 5 → character `'5'`: $$ \text{char}(5) = 5 + 48 = 53 = \text{ASCII}('5') \checkmark $$ ## 3.4 Full Worked Example: Character Counter Program **Problem:** Count the number of vowels, consonants, digits, and spaces in a given string. **Analytical Matrix:** | Character Type | Detection Formula | KTU Logic | |----------------|-------------------|-----------| | Vowel (lowercase) | `c == 'a' \|\| c == 'e' \|\| c == 'i' \|\| c == 'o' \|\| c == 'u'` | Direct comparison | | Vowel (uppercase) | `c == 'A' \|\| c == 'E' \|\| c == 'I' \|\| c == 'O' \|\| c == 'U'` | Direct comparison | | Consonant | `isalpha(c) && !isVowel(c)` | Alpha minus vowels | | Digit | `c >= '0' && c <= '9'` | Range check | | Space | `c == ' '` or `isspace(c)` | Direct/range | **Complete C++ Solution:** ```cpp #include #include #include using namespace std; int main() { string text; cout << "Enter a line of text: "; getline(cin, text); int vowels = 0, consonants = 0, digits = 0, spaces = 0; for (size_t i = 0; i < text.length(); i++) { char c = text[i]; char lowerC = tolower(static_cast(c)); if (lowerC == 'a' || lowerC == 'e' || lowerC == 'i' || lowerC == 'o' || lowerC == 'u') { vowels++; } else if (isalpha(static_cast(c))) { consonants++; } else if (isdigit(static_cast(c))) { digits++; } else if (isspace(static_cast(c))) { spaces++; } } cout << "Vowels: " << vowels << endl; cout << "Consonants: " << consonants << endl; cout << "Digits: " << digits << endl; cout << "Spaces: " << spaces << endl; return 0; } ``` **Step-by-step trace for input `"OOP 2024 is Fun"`:** | Index | Char | `tolower` | isalpha? | isdigit? | isspace? | Vowel? | Action | |-------|------|-----------|----------|----------|----------|--------|--------| | 0 | `O` | `o` | Yes | No | No | Yes | vowels=1 | | 1 | `O` | `o` | Yes | No | No | Yes | vowels=2 | | 2 | `P` | `p` | Yes | No | No | No | consonants=1 | | 3 | ` ` | ` ` | No | No | Yes | No | spaces=1 | | 4 | `2` | `2` | No | Yes | No | No | digits=1 | | 5 | `0` | `0` | No | Yes | No | No | digits=2 | | 6 | `2` | `2` | No | Yes | No | No | digits=3 | | 7 | `4` | `4` | No | Yes | No | No | digits=4 | | 8 | ` ` | ` ` | No | No | Yes | No | spaces=2 | | 9 | `i` | `i` | Yes | No | No | Yes | vowels=3 | | 10 | `s` | `s` | Yes | No | No | No | consonants=2 | | 11 | ` ` | ` ` | No | No | Yes | No | spaces=3 | | 12 | `F` | `f` | Yes | No | No | No | consonants=3 | | 13 | `u` | `u` | Yes | No | No | Yes | vowels=4 | | 14 | `n` | `n` | Yes | No | No | No | consonants=4 | **Final Output:** ``` Vowels: 4 Consonants: 4 Digits: 4 Spaces: 3 ``` ## 3.5 Caesar Cipher Walkthrough **Problem:** Encrypt a character using Caesar cipher with shift = 3. **Formulas (modular arithmetic):** $$ E(c) = \begin{cases} ((c - \text{base} + k) \bmod 26) + \text{base} & \text{if } c \text{ is a letter} \\ c & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ where: - `base` = 65 (for uppercase) or 97 (for lowercase) - `k` = 3 (shift) - `c` = ASCII value of input character **Trace for input `'X'` (uppercase), shift = 3:** $$ E('X') = ((88 - 65 + 3) \bmod 26) + 65 $$ $$ = (26 \bmod 26) + 65 $$ $$ = 0 + 65 = 65 = \text{ASCII}('A') $$ So `'X'` → `'A'` (wraparound from end of alphabet). ✓ **C++ Implementation:** ```cpp char caesarEncrypt(char c, int shift) { if (isalpha(static_cast(c))) { char base = isupper(static_cast(c)) ? 'A' : 'a'; return (c - base + shift) % 26 + base; } return c; // Non-alphabetic characters unchanged } ``` # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ASCII Code Map — Hierarchical Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["ASCII Character Set 0-127"] Start --> Control["Control Characters 0-31 and 127"] Start --> Printable["Printable Characters 32-126"] Control --> C0["NUL SOX STX

Why are they considered three distinct types even though they have the same size?** **Model Answer:** In C++, `char`, `signed char`, and `unsigned char` are **three distinct fundamental types** even though all three occupy exactly **1 byte (8 bits)** of memory. | Type | Size | Range | Signedness | |------|------|-------|------------| | `char` | 1 byte | -128 to 127 **or** 0 to 255 | **Implementation-defined** | | `signed char` | 1 byte | -128 to 127 | Explicitly signed | | `unsigned char` | 1 byte | 0 to 255 | Explicitly unsigned | The C++ standard treats them as different types because the `char` type is meant for **character representation** (tied to the execution character set, e.g., ASCII), while `signed char` and `unsigned char` are meant for **small integer arithmetic**. A compiler can legally implement `char` as either signed or unsigned. You **cannot overload** a function with both `char` and `signed char` parameters as if they were the same—doing so causes a compilation error because the overload resolution treats them as distinct types. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Q: What is the difference between `cin >> ch` and `cin.get(ch)` in C++? When would you prefer one over the other?** **Model Answer:** Both statements read a single character into the variable `ch`, but they behave differently with **whitespace characters**. **`cin >> ch`:** - Uses the formatted extraction operator - **Skips leading whitespace** (spaces, tabs, newlines) before reading - Reads exactly one non-whitespace character - Leaves the whitespace in the input buffer - Use when: you want to ignore whitespace and read meaningful characters **`cin.get(ch)`:** - Uses the unformatted input member function - **Reads every character** including spaces, tabs, and newlines - Does NOT skip whitespace - Use when: you need to process whitespace explicitly (e.g., counting spaces, parsing character by character) **Example:** For input `"A B"`: - `cin >> ch` → reads `'A'`, whitespace `' '` remains in buffer - `cin.get(ch)` → reads `'A'`, then on next call reads `' '` (the space itself) **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain the various character classification functions available in the C++ `` header. Write a C++ program that reads a string from the user and counts the number of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, digits, whitespace characters, and punctuation marks using these functions. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The `` header provides the following character classification functions, each taking an `int` argument (a `char` promoted to `int`) and returning a non-zero value (`int`) for true and `0` for false: **Character Classification Functions:** | Function | Returns non-zero (true) if the character is | |----------|--------------------------------------------| | `isalpha(c)` | An alphabetic letter (A–Z or a–z) | | `isdigit(c)` | A decimal digit (0–9) | | `isalnum(c)` | An alphanumeric character (letter or digit) | | `isspace(c)` | A whitespace character (space, tab, newline, etc.) | | `isupper(c)` | An uppercase letter (A–Z) | | `islower(c)` | A lowercase letter (a–z) | | `ispunct(c)` | A punctuation character (printable but not space, letter, or digit) | | `iscntrl(c)` | A control character (0x00–0x1F or 0x7F) | | `isprint(c)` | A printable character (including space) | | `isxdigit(c)` | A hexadecimal digit (0–9, A–F, a–f) | | `isgraph(c)` | A printable character except space | **Complete C++ Program:** ```cpp #include #include #include using namespace std; int main() { string text; int upper = 0, lower = 0, digit = 0, space = 0, punct = 0; cout << "Enter a line of text: "; getline(cin, text); for (size_t i = 0; i < text.length(); i++) { unsigned char uc = static_cast(text[i]); if (isupper(uc)) upper++; else if (islower(uc)) lower++; else if (isdigit(uc)) digit++; else if (isspace(uc)) space++; else if (ispunct(uc)) punct++; } cout << "Uppercase letters: " << upper << endl; cout << "Lowercase letters: " << lower << endl; cout << "Digits: " << digit << endl; cout << "Whitespace: " << space << endl; cout << "Punctuation: " << punct << endl; return 0; } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter a line of text: Hello, World! 2024. Uppercase letters: 2 Lowercase letters: 8 Digits: 4 Whitespace: 2 Punctuation: 3 ``` **Explanation of Key Steps:** 1. The `getline(cin, text)` function reads the entire line including spaces, storing it in a `std::string`. 2. The `for` loop iterates over each character of the string using the `length()` method. 3. The `static_cast` ensures safe passing to `` functions, preventing undefined behavior for negative `char` values. 4. A cascade of `if-else` branches classifies each character into exactly one category. 5. Respective counters are incremented and the final report is displayed. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Write a C++ program to implement the **Caesar cipher** encryption for uppercase letters with a user-supplied shift value (1–25). The program should read a plaintext string and a shift key, then output the ciphertext. Show the trace of your algorithm for the input `"HELLO"` with shift = 3. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Caesar Cipher Theory:** The Caesar cipher shifts each letter by a fixed number of positions in the alphabet. For a letter `c` with ASCII value and base ASCII value of `'A'` (65): $$ E(c) = ((c - 65 + k) \bmod 26) + 65 $$ where `k` is the shift value (wrapped around using modulo 26). **Complete C++ Program:** ```cpp #include #include #include using namespace std; string caesarEncrypt(string plaintext, int shift) { string cipher = ""; for (size_t i = 0; i < plaintext.length(); i++) { char c = plaintext[i]; if (isupper(static_cast(c))) { cipher += static_cast((c - 'A' + shift) % 26 + 'A'); } else if (islower(static_cast(c))) { cipher += static_cast((c - 'a' + shift) % 26 + 'a'); } else { cipher += c; // Non-alphabetic characters unchanged } } return cipher; } int main() { string text; int shift; cout << "Enter plaintext (uppercase only): "; getline(cin, text); cout << "Enter shift value (1-25): "; cin >> shift; if (shift < 1 || shift > 25) { cout << "Invalid shift! Must be between 1 and 25." << endl; return 1; } string result = caesarEncrypt(text, shift); cout << "Ciphertext: " << result << endl; return 0; } ``` **Trace for `"HELLO"` with shift = 3:** | Position | Char | ASCII | Computation | Result | ASCII | |----------|------|-------|-------------|--------|-------| | 0 | `H` | 72 | `(72-65+3) % 26 + 65 = 10 % 26 + 65 = 0 + 65` | `K` | 75 | | 1 | `E` | 69 | `(69-65+3) % 26 + 65 = 7 % 26 + 65 = 7 + 65` | `L` | 76 | | 2 | `L` | 76 | `(76-65+3) % 26 + 65 = 14 % 26 + 65 = 14 + 65` | `O` | 79 | | 3 | `L` | 76 | `(76-65+3) % 26 + 65 = 14 + 65` | `O` | 79 | | 4 | `O` | 79 | `(79-65+3) % 26 + 65 = 17 + 65` | `R` | 82 | **Output:** `Ciphertext: KLOOR` **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain the **internal storage representation** of `char` data type in C++. How is the character `'Z'` stored in memory? Show the complete bit-level calculation. Discuss the difference between `char`, `wchar_t`, and `char32_t` with their typical sizes and use cases. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Internal Storage of `char`:** A `char` variable in C++ occupies **1 byte = 8 bits** in memory. The C++ standard does not mandate the encoding, but in virtually all modern platforms, characters are stored using the **ASCII** (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) encoding, where each printable character is assigned a unique 7-bit (or 8-bit) numeric code. **Bit-level Calculation for `'Z'`:** The ASCII value of `'Z'` is **90** in decimal. **Step 1:** Convert 90 to binary using repeated division by 2: $$ 90 \div 2 = 45 \text{ remainder } 0 $$ $$ 45 \div 2 = 22 \text{ remainder } 1 $$ $$ 22 \div 2 = 11 \text{ remainder } 0 $$ $$ 11 \div 2 = 5 \text{ remainder } 1 $$ $$ 5 \div 2 = 2 \text{ remainder } 1 $$ $$ 2 \div 2 = 1 \text{ remainder } 0 $$ $$ 1 \div 2 = 0 \text{ remainder } 1 $$ Reading remainders bottom-up: `1011010` **Step 2:** Pad to 8 bits (1 byte): `01011010` **Step 3:** Verify the bit weights: $$ 0 \cdot 128 + 1 \cdot 64 + 0 \cdot 32 + 1 \cdot 16 + 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 $$ $$ = 64 + 16 + 8 + 2 = 90 \checkmark $$ So the memory representation of `'Z'` is the bit pattern **`01011010`**, which the CPU and C++ runtime interpret as the character `'Z'`. **Difference Between `char`, `wchar_t`, and `char32_t`:** | Type | Size (Typical) | Encoding | Use Case | |------|----------------|----------|----------| | `char` | **1 byte** | ASCII / UTF-8 code unit | Default character type, English text, file I/O, C-strings | | `wchar_t` | **2 bytes** (Windows) or **4 bytes** (Linux) | Implementation-defined wide encoding | Legacy wide character I/O (`wcout`, `wcin`), Windows APIs | | `char32_t` | **Exactly 4 bytes** | UTF-32 code unit (one code point fits in one value) | C++11 onwards, fixed-width Unicode, internationalization | **Key Distinctions:** 1. **`char`** is **size-limited** to 1 byte by the C++ standard and is **implementation-defined** whether signed or unsigned. 2. **`wchar_t`** has a **size dependent on the platform** — on Windows it is 16 bits (UTF-16), on Linux/macOS it is 32 bits (UTF-32). This makes it non-portable. 3. **`char32_t`** has a **fixed, portable size** of exactly 4 bytes (guaranteed by the standard), making it ideal for representing any Unicode code point directly. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Write a C++ program that reads a character from the user and determines whether it is a **vowel, consonant, digit, or special symbol** without using any `` library functions. Use only direct ASCII comparisons. Provide the full source code and a sample run. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Algorithm:** 1. Read a character from the user. 2. Check the ASCII range: - Digits: ASCII 48 to 57 (`'0'` to `'9'`) - Uppercase letters: ASCII 65 to 90 (`'A'` to `'Z'`) - Lowercase letters: ASCII 97 to 122 (`'a'` to `'z'`) 3. If uppercase or lowercase, further check if it is a vowel by comparing against the set `{A, E, I, O, U, a, e, i, o, u}`. 4. Otherwise, classify as a special symbol. **Complete C++ Program (No `` Used):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; int main() { char ch; cout << "Enter a single character: "; cin >> ch; int ascii = static_cast(ch); bool isVowel = (ch == 'A' || ch == 'E' || ch == 'I' || ch == 'O' || ch == 'U' || ch == 'a' || ch == 'e' || ch == 'i' || ch == 'o' || ch == 'u'); if (ascii >= 48 && ascii <= 57) { cout << "'" << ch << "' is a DIGIT." << endl; } else if ((ascii >= 65 && ascii <= 90) || (ascii >= 97 && ascii <= 122)) { if (isVowel) { cout << "'" << ch << "' is a VOWEL." << endl; } else { cout << "'" << ch << "' is a CONSONANT." << endl; } } else { cout << "'" << ch << "' is a SPECIAL SYMBOL." << endl; } return 0; } ``` **Sample Runs:** **Run 1:** ``` Enter a single character: E 'E' is a VOWEL. ``` **Run 2:** ``` Enter a single character: k 'k' is a CONSONANT. ``` **Run 3:** ``` Enter a single character: 9 '9' is a DIGIT. ``` **Run 4:** ``` Enter a single character: @ '@' is a SPECIAL SYMBOL. ``` **ASCII Range Reference Used in the Code:** | Range | Constant | Meaning | |-------|----------|---------| | 48–57 | `'0'`–`'9'` | Digits | | 65–90 | `'A'`–`'Z'` | Uppercase letters | | 97–122 | `'a'`–`'z'` | Lowercase letters | **Explanation of the Logic:** 1. The expression `static_cast(ch)` retrieves the ASCII numeric value of the character. 2. The compound condition `(ch == 'A' || ch == 'E' || ... || ch == 'u')` checks vowel membership using OR (logical disjunction). 3. The nested `if-else` structure first classifies the broad category (digit/letter/symbol) and then refines letters into vowel vs. consonant. 4. The program uses only **direct ASCII comparisons** and **range checks**, satisfying the "no ``" requirement. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Forgetting to include ``** when using `isalpha`, `isdigit`, `tolower`, etc. — a guaranteed zero on the classification part. Always add `#include `. > > 2. **Confusing character `'7'` with integer `7`** — these are completely different. `'7'` has ASCII value 55; the integer `7` is stored directly. The conversion `ch - '0'` converts character to integer. > > 3. **Forgetting `static_cast`** before passing to `` functions. If `char` is signed and contains a negative value (e.g., extended ASCII 200+), the behavior is **undefined**. Examiners specifically look for this cast in higher-mark questions. > > 4. **Confusing `cin >> ch` with `cin.get(ch)`** — the former skips whitespace, the latter does not. This is a 3-mark KTU favorite. > > 5. **Writing `'A' + 32` instead of `'a'`** in case-conversion logic is correct but the KTU examiner expects you to state the ASCII arithmetic explicitly, not just hardcode. > > 6. **Treating `char`, `signed char`, `unsigned char` as the same type** — they are **three distinct types** in C++ overloading rules. This is a high-frequency KTU Part A trap. > > 7. **Off-by-one in range checks** — confusing `>=` and `>`, or using `>= 48 && <= 57` (correct) vs. `>= 0 && <= 9` (WRONG — these are integer ranges, not ASCII character ranges). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ A `char` is **1 byte (8 bits)**; `char`, `signed char`, and `unsigned char` are **three distinct types** in C++. - ✅ The signedness of plain `char` is **implementation-defined** — never assume. - ✅ Character literals use **single quotes** `'A'`; string literals use **double quotes** `"A"`. - ✅ ASCII range: digits `48–57`, uppercase `65–90`, lowercase `97–122`, space `32`, null `0`. - ✅ The difference between **uppercase and lowercase** of the same letter in ASCII is exactly **32**. - ✅ **Character-to-integer conversion:** `int n = ch - '0';` (works only for `'0'`–`'9'`). - ✅ **Integer-to-character conversion:** `char c = n + '0';` (works for `n` in `0`–`9`). - ✅ **Uppercase-to-lowercase** (without library): `tolower(c) = c + 32` (for letters only). - ✅ **Lowercase-to-uppercase** (without library): `toupper(c) = c - 32` (for letters only). - ✅ All `` functions take `int` and return `int` (nonzero for true, 0 for false). - ✅ Always cast to `unsigned char` before passing to ``: `isalpha(static_cast(c))`. - ✅ `cin >> ch` **skips whitespace**; `cin.get(ch)` **does not skip whitespace**. - ✅ Escape sequences: `\n` (newline, 10), `\t` (tab, 9), `\0` (null, 0), `\\` (backslash, 92). - ✅ `wchar_t` size is **platform-dependent** (2 bytes Windows, 4 bytes Linux); `char32_t` is **fixed at 4 bytes**. - ✅ Unicode string literal prefixes: `u8` (UTF-8), `u` (UTF-16), `U` (UTF-32), `L` (wide). - ✅ Caesar cipher formula: `E(c) = ((c - base + k) % 26) + base`, where `base` = `'A'` or `'a'`. - ✅ ROT13 = Caesar with `k = 13`; works perfectly because `13 × 2 = 26`. - ✅ `isalnum` = `isalpha || isdigit`; `ispunct` is printable but **not** alphanumeric and **not** space. - ✅ Character variables can participate in **integer arithmetic** because they ARE integers internally. - ✅ `static_cast('A')` returns 65; this dual nature (character + integer) is fundamental to OOP polymorphism concepts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** For Part A (3-mark) questions on characters, always state the **ASCII value** alongside the classification. For Part B (14-mark) questions, write **complete, compilable C++ code** with `#include` directives, `using namespace std;`, and demonstrate a sample run — KTU examiners award full marks only for working, well-commented programs.

topics.title: Boolean. Type Conversion and Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boolean. Type Conversion and Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The `boolean` Data Type

In Java (the language of KTU's OOP curriculum), the **boolean** is a primitive data type whose only legal values are the two literals `true` and `false`. It is the computational embodiment of **Boolean Algebra** (developed by George Boole in 1854), restricted to a **2-element set** $B = \{0, 1\}$ mapped to Java keywords $\{false, true\}$.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *A boolean variable is a 1-bit logical container that represents one of two mutually exclusive states — `true` (1) or `false` (0) — and is the operand type required by all conditional and looping control structures.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specs at a Glance**
> - **Size:** Conceptually **1 bit**, but the JVM physically allocates **1 byte** (with 7 unused bits) for array element alignment.
> - **Default Value:** `false` (for class/instance fields).
> - **Wrapper Class:** `java.lang.Boolean`
> - **Integral Promotion:** A `boolean` **CANNOT** be implicitly cast to `int` or any numeric type in Java (unlike C/C++).

---

### 1.2 Type Conversion (Widening / Implicit Conversion)

**Formal Definition:**
> *Type Conversion is the automatic, compiler-driven transformation of a value from a narrower primitive type to a wider one, performed only when no information loss is possible and no fractional-to-integral demotion occurs.*

It is **lossless**, runs at compile-time, and follows the chain:
`byte → short → int → long → float → double` and also `char → int`.

---

### 1.3 Type Casting (Narrowing / Explicit Conversion)

**Formal Definition:**
> *Type Casting is the programmer-forced conversion of a value from a wider type to a narrower type using the cast operator `(targetType)`, which may result in **truncation, modular wrap-around, or precision loss**.*

The syntax is: `(targetType) expression`.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Jar of Liquid" Model

Imagine you have **five glass jars** lined up from smallest to largest:

```
  [ byte ]  →  [ short ]  →  [ int ]  →  [ long ]  →  [ double ]
   50 mL        250 mL         1 L          5 L          20 L
```

- **Type Conversion (Widening):** Pouring a smaller jar into a larger one. **Always safe** — no liquid spills. The compiler does this automatically.
- **Type Casting (Narrowing):** Pouring a huge 5-L bucket into a tiny 50 mL cup. **You must explicitly grab a smaller cup and force the pour**. Some liquid will overflow (data loss), and you must take responsibility for the result.
- **boolean:** A jar that only ever contains **"Yes"** or **"No"** written on a label — you can never pour it into a numeric jar.

---

### 1.5 Type Promotion in Expressions

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Module 2**
> In any arithmetic expression, all `byte`, `short`, and `char` operands are **automatically promoted to `int`** *before* the operation is performed. This is called **Integer Promotion** (JLS §5.6.2).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why `boolean` is Non-Numeric in Java

Unlike C/C++ where `0 = false` and `non-zero = true`, Java enforces **strict type safety**. This was a deliberate design choice by James Gosling to eliminate entire categories of bugs (e.g., `if (x = 0)` accidentally being a valid assignment-as-boolean).

| Property | `boolean` (Java) | `bool` (C++) |
|---|---|---|
| Size | 1 byte (JVM) | ≥ 1 byte |
| Implicit to `int` | ❌ Forbidden | ✅ Allowed |
| Default | `false` | `false` (often 0) |
| Control-flow eligible | ✅ | ✅ |

---

### 2.2 Widening Conversion Rules (Lossless Path)

The compiler performs widening automatically **only if**:
1. Both types are **compatible** (numeric ↔ numeric, `char` ↔ integral).
2. The destination type is **strictly larger** (holds all values of source).

**Widening Ladder (ordered):**

$$
\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$
$$
\text{char} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

> The arrow direction $\rightarrow$ indicates "can be implicitly widened to".

---

### 2.3 Narrowing Casting Rules (Lossy Path)

Casting is required when:
1. Source type is **larger** than destination, OR
2. Converting between a **floating-point and an integral** type.

**Behaviour during casting:**

| Conversion | Result Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Floating → Integral | **Truncation** toward zero (fraction dropped) |
| Long → int / int → short | **Modular wrap-around** (last 32 / 16 bits kept) |
| `double` → `float` | **Rounding** to nearest representable `float` |
| Numeric → `char` | Modulo 65536, then mapped to Unicode |
| Any → `boolean` | **Compilation Error** (no casting exists) |

---

### 2.4 Type Promotion — The 4 Golden Rules

When evaluating a binary expression `a op b`:

1. If either operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`.
2. Else if either is `float`, the other is promoted to `float`.
3. Else if either is `long`, the other is promoted to `long`.
4. **Otherwise, both `byte`, `short`, and `char` are promoted to `int`**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Even `byte + byte` produces an `int`. Storing it back in a `byte` without an explicit cast causes `possible lossy conversion` compilation error.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Direction | Data Loss? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Widening Ladder | $T_s \subset T_d$ (subset) | Smaller $\rightarrow$ Larger | ❌ Never |
| Narrowing Truncation | $x \text{ cast to int} = \lfloor x \rfloor$ | `float/double` $\rightarrow$ `int` | ✅ Fractional part |
| Modular Wrap | $x \bmod 2^{n}$ where $n$ = bits of target | Wide int $\rightarrow$ Narrow int | ✅ High bits |
| Integer Promotion | $b + b \rightarrow \text{int}$ | `byte/short/char` $\rightarrow$ `int` | ⚠ Possible |
| Boolean-to-Int | Not permitted | — | N/A |
| `String` Concatenation | `+` with any primitive → `String` | Any $\rightarrow$ `String` | ❌ Never |
| Promotion of `char` | $\text{char} + \text{int} \rightarrow \text{int}$ | `char` $\rightarrow$ `int` | ❌ Never |
| Final Constant Exception | `final byte b = 10; byte c = b;` ✅ | Compile-time literal | ❌ Never |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Type promotion is the silent engine behind every numerical computation in embedded controllers (Arduino → `int` math), scientific simulations (CFD codes use `double`), and database indexes (`int` keys widened to `long` for shard IDs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Widening Conversion

```java
int    i = 100;
long   l = i;        // implicit widening: int → long
float  f = l;        // implicit widening: long → float
double d = f;        // implicit widening: float → double
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

$$
\text{Step 1: } i = 100_{10}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } l = (long)\, i = 100_{10}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } f = (float)\, l = 100.0_{10}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: } d = (double)\, f = 100.0_{10}
$$

**Verdict:** Zero precision loss at every step because each target type fully contains the source's representable range for the value 100.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Narrowing with Truncation

```java
double price = 99.987;
int    rupees = (int) price;     // explicit cast
System.out.println(rupees);
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

$$
\text{Step 1: } \text{price} = 99.987_{10}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } \text{rupees} = \lfloor 99.987 \rfloor = 99
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Output} \Rightarrow 99
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Truncation **does NOT round**. To round, the programmer must write `(int)(price + 0.5)` for positive numbers.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Modular Wrap-around

```java
int big = 257;
byte small = (byte) big;
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

$$
\text{Step 1: } \text{big} = 257_{10} = 00000000\,00000000\,00000001\,00000001_{2}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } \text{byte capacity} = 8 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow \text{keep last 8 bits}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } 00000001_{2} = 1_{10}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Output} \Rightarrow \text{small} = 1
$$

**Modular formula:**

$$
\text{small} = \text{big} \bmod 2^{8} = 257 \bmod 256 = 1
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — `boolean` Cannot be Cast

```java
boolean flag = true;
int x = (int) flag;      // ❌ Compile-time ERROR: incompatible types
```

**Analysis:** The Java Language Specification (JLS §5.1.3) **does not list any boolean conversion rule**. The compiler rejects the cast at the lexical-analysis phase.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Integer Promotion in `byte + byte`

```java
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
byte c = a + b;          // ❌ Compile Error
byte d = (byte)(a + b);  // ✅ Compiles, value = 30
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

$$
\text{Step 1: } a \text{ is promoted to } \text{int} \Rightarrow a' = 10
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } b \text{ is promoted to } \text{int} \Rightarrow b' = 20
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } a' + b' = 30 \;\;(\text{type: int})
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: } c = (byte)\,30 = 30 \;\; \text{(requires explicit cast back)}
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — All Primitive Conversions at a Glance

| Source | Destination | Conversion Type | Compile? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | `short` | Widening | ✅ Auto |
| `char` | `int` | Widening | ✅ Auto |
| `int` | `long` | Widening | ✅ Auto |
| `long` | `float` | Widening | ✅ Auto (may lose precision for very large longs) |
| `float` | `double` | Widening | ✅ Auto |
| `double` | `int` | Narrowing | ✅ Explicit |
| `int` | `byte` | Narrowing | ✅ Explicit |
| `boolean` | `int` | — | ❌ Forbidden |
| `int` | `boolean` | — | ❌ Forbidden |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Java Primitive Type Conversion Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    byteN["BYTE 8 bit"] --> shortN["SHORT 16 bit"]
    charN["CHAR 16 bit"] --> intN["INT 32 bit"]
    shortN --> intN
    intN --> longN["LONG 64 bit"]
    longN --> floatN["FLOAT 32 bit"]
    floatN --> doubleN["DOUBLE 64 bit"]
    boolN["BOOLEAN true false"] -.X NO CONVERSION.-> intN
    intN -.CAST ONLY.-> byteN
    intN -.CAST ONLY.-> charN
    doubleN -.CAST ONLY.-> intN
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows represent safe **implicit widening**. Dashed arrows represent **explicit narrowing** casts. The crossed-out dotted arrow confirms `boolean` is isolated.

---

### 4.2 Integer Promotion Flow in `a + b`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Operand A"] --> chkA{Is A byte short or char?}
    chkA -- YES --> promoteA["Promoted to INT"]
    chkA -- NO --> keepA["Keep type of A"]
    startB["Operand B"] --> chkB{Is B byte short or char?}
    chkB -- YES --> promoteB["Promoted to INT"]
    chkB -- NO --> keepB["Keep type of B"]
    promoteA --> op["Apply binary operator"]
    keepA --> op
    promoteB --> op
    keepB --> op
    op --> result["Result type is widest of A B promoted"]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Type System Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph compileTime[Compile Time Engine]
        lexer["Lexer Tokenizer"] --> typeCheck["Type Checker"]
        typeCheck --> promoteRule{"Widening possible without loss?"}
        promoteRule -- YES --> autoConv["Auto Insert Widening"]
        promoteRule -- NO --> needCast{"Cast operator present?"}
        needCast -- YES --> applyCast["Apply Narrowing Cast"]
        needCast -- NO --> err1["COMPILE ERROR"]
    end
    subgraph runtime[Runtime Engine JVM]
        stackOp["Operand Stack Push"] --> execOp["Execute Opcode iadd fadd etc"]
        execOp --> storeRes["Store result in local variable"]
    end
    autoConv --> stackOp
    applyCast --> stackOp
```

> **Architectural Note:** Widening conversions are resolved at compile time (bytecode already encodes the wider type). Narrowing casts add an explicit *checkcast* or *i2b / i2c / f2i* opcode that the JVM executes at runtime.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Differentiate between implicit type conversion and explicit type casting in Java. When is each performed? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Implicit Conversion | Explicit Casting |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Compiler | Programmer |
| Direction | Smaller $\rightarrow$ Larger | Any direction |
| Data Loss | Never | Possible |
| Syntax | No operator needed | `(type)` operator |

```java
// Implicit
int  i = 10;
long l = i;          // int → long (auto)

// Explicit
long x = 1000L;
int  y = (int) x;    // long → int (manual cast)
```

*(Examiner awards 1 mark each for: definition, direction, loss, and example.)*

---

#### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Explain why the statement `byte b = a + 2;` fails to compile when `byte a = 10;`, but `byte b = a + 10;` *also* fails unless `a` is declared `final`. Justify with the integer promotion rule.**

**Model Answer:**
In any binary expression, operands of type `byte`, `short`, and `char` are promoted to `int` **before** the operation.

- `a + 2` $\Rightarrow$ `int + int` $\Rightarrow$ result is `int`.
- Assigning an `int` to a `byte` requires narrowing — compile error unless cast.
- Even if `a` is `final byte a = 10`, the compiler treats the operand as a *constant expression* and folds it at compile time, allowing implicit narrowing to `byte` (only for assignment-context constant expressions, per JLS §5.2).

```java
final byte a = 10;
byte b = a + 2;     // ✅ compiles (compile-time constant folding)
```

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the rules of **type promotion** in Java expressions. Show with an example how the expression `char + double - int * long` is evaluated.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain with a code example how **narrowing casting** can lead to (i) truncation and (ii) modular wrap-around. Provide the output of the snippet.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Type Promotion Rules (7 marks)**

Java applies four cascading rules when evaluating an expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Rule 1: If } a \text{ or } b \text{ is } \texttt{double} \Rightarrow \text{other promoted to } \texttt{double} \\
&\text{Rule 2: Else if } a \text{ or } b \text{ is } \texttt{float} \Rightarrow \text{other promoted to } \texttt{float} \\
&\text{Rule 3: Else if } a \text{ or } b \text{ is } \texttt{long} \Rightarrow \text{other promoted to } \texttt{long} \\
&\text{Rule 4: Otherwise both } \texttt{byte/short/char} \Rightarrow \text{promoted to } \texttt{int}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Evaluation of `char + double - int * long`:**

According to Java operator precedence, `*` binds tighter than `+` and `-`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Inner product} \Rightarrow &\texttt{int * long} \\
&\text{By Rule 3} \Rightarrow \texttt{long * long} \\
&\text{Result } R_1 = (\texttt{long}) \\
\\
\text{Step 2: } &\texttt{char + double} \\
&\text{By Rule 1} \Rightarrow \texttt{double + double} \\
&\text{Result } R_2 = (\texttt{double}) \\
\\
\text{Step 3: } &\texttt{R_2 - R_1} \\
&\text{double - long} \Rightarrow \text{By Rule 1} \Rightarrow \texttt{double} \\
&\text{Final type: } \texttt{double}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Narrowing Casting Pitfalls (7 marks)**

```java
public class CastDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // (i) Truncation
        double d = 9.99;
        int    i = (int) d;
        System.out.println("Truncation: " + i);

        // (ii) Modular wrap-around
        int  big = 130;
        byte small = (byte) big;
        System.out.println("Wrap-around: " + small);
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i) Truncation} \Rightarrow (int)\,9.99 = \lfloor 9.99 \rfloor = 9 \\
&\text{(ii) Wrap-around} \Rightarrow 130 \bmod 2^{8} = 130 \bmod 256 = 130
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Many students write **9** is rounding of 9.99 — *wrong*. Java casts **truncate**, not round. KTU awards zero marks if "round" is used.
> 2. For wrap-around, students often compute `130 - 256 = -126`. This is correct for **signed** bytes when the bit pattern's MSB is 1. For 130 = `10000010`, the MSB is 1, so Java interprets it as `-126`. **Always check the MSB** to decide the sign.
> 3. Mark loss happens when students forget that **integer promotion occurs *before* the cast applies**.

**Output:**
```
Truncation: 9
Wrap-around: -126
```

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the `boolean` data type in Java? Why is it treated as a non-numeric type unlike C/C++? Explain with two code snippets demonstrating the difference.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the program below. Predict and justify the output. Mention any compilation errors.

```java
class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        boolean flag = true;
        int a = 10, b = 3;
        double result = a / b;
        int truncated = (int)(a / (double)b);
        System.out.println("Result: " + result);
        System.out.println("Truncated: " + truncated);
        System.out.println("Boolean: " + (flag && (a > b)));
    }
}
```

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Boolean Type in Java (7 marks)**

The `boolean` primitive represents **logical truth values** — exclusively `true` or `false`. Java deliberately excluded numeric interpretation to enforce type safety. In C/C++:

```c
int x = 0;
if (x) { /* executes because non-zero */ }   // C/C++
```

In Java, the equivalent fails at compile time:

```java
int x = 0;
if (x) { /* COMPILE ERROR: incompatible types */ }   // Java
```

Java forces the explicit `if (x != 0)` style, eliminating the historical bug where assignment (`x = 0`) was mistaken for comparison (`x == 0`). Additionally, a `boolean` **cannot be cast** to any numeric type and vice-versa.

**(b) Trace and Justify (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{a / b} = 10 \,/\, 3 \text{ (both int)} = 3 \text{ (integer division)} \\
&\texttt{result} = (\texttt{double})\,3 = 3.0 \\
\\
&\texttt{(double)b} = 3.0 \\
&\texttt{a / (double)b} = 10.0 \,/\, 3.0 = 3.3333... \\
&\texttt{truncated} = (int)\,3.3333... = \lfloor 3.3333 \rfloor = 3 \\
\\
&\texttt{flag \&\& (a > b)} = \texttt{true \&\& true} = \texttt{true}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:**
```
Result: 3.0
Truncated: 3
Boolean: true
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Examiner**
> - Missing the **integer division** pitfall in line 3 (students write `result = 3.33`).
> - Confusing `&` (bitwise) with `&&` (short-circuit logical AND).
> - Forgetting that `+` between a `String` and a `boolean` triggers `String` concatenation, not addition.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `boolean` has only **two values**: `true` and `false`. It **cannot be cast** to/from any numeric type.
- ✅ **Widening** = automatic, lossless, smaller $\rightarrow$ larger.
- ✅ **Narrowing** = explicit, may lose data, requires `(type)` cast.
- ✅ **Integer Promotion** lifts `byte`, `short`, `char` to `int` before arithmetic.
- ✅ **Truncation** drops the fractional part; it does **not round**.
- ✅ **Modular wrap-around** keeps the last $n$ bits where $n$ is the target's bit-width.
- ✅ `final` constants are an **exception** to the narrowing rule (compile-time folding).
- ✅ The `boolean` to `int` cast is **always a compile error** in Java — memorize this for MCQs.
- ✅ Operator precedence dictates the **order** of type promotion in compound expressions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) — specifically within the **Java** language ecosystem prescribed by the KTU PEEET419 syllabus — a **Variable** is a named storage location in the computer's memory (RAM) that holds a value which can be modified during program execution. A variable is characterized by three fundamental attributes:

1. **Name (Identifier):** A symbolic label used to reference the memory location.
2. **Data Type:** Determines the size, layout, and the range of values that can be stored.
3. **Value:** The actual data stored at the memory address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):** "A variable is a container that holds a value during the lifetime of a program. In Java, every variable must be declared with a specific data type before it can be used, which makes Java a strongly-typed language."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **variable as a labeled box in a warehouse**:
- The **box label** (e.g., `age`, `salary`, `studentName`) is the *variable name*.
- The **size of the box** (small, medium, large) is the *data type* — small for integers, large for decimals or complex objects.
- The **content inside the box** is the *value* (e.g., `21`, `55000.50`, `"Rahul"`).
- The **warehouse aisle number** is the *memory address* — hidden from you, but tracked internally.

When you write `int age = 21;`, you are telling the JVM: *"Reserve a small box, label it 'age', and put the number 21 inside it."*

## 1.3 Categories of Variables in Java (KTU High-Yield)

| Category | Scope | Lifetime | Declaration Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local Variable** | Inside a method, constructor, or block | Until the method/block finishes | Inside `{ }` of method |
| **Instance Variable** | Throughout the class (per object) | Until the object is garbage collected | Inside class, outside methods |
| **Static (Class) Variable** | Throughout the class (shared) | Until the class is unloaded | Inside class with `static` keyword |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the difference between **instance vs. static variables** and **local vs. instance variables**. Master the *default values* and *memory locations* (Stack vs. Heap).

## 1.4 Default Values of Primitive Data Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local variables** in Java do **NOT** get default values — they **must be initialized** before use, or the compiler throws an error. **Instance and static variables** receive default values automatically.

| Data Type | Size | Default Value | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | **1 byte** | `0` | -128 to 127 |
| `short` | **2 bytes** | `0` | -32,768 to 32,767 |
| `int` | **4 bytes** | `0` | -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 |
| `long` | **8 bytes** | `0L` | -9.22e18 to 9.22e18 |
| `float` | **4 bytes** | `0.0f` | ~ ±3.4e38 (7-digit precision) |
| `double` | **8 bytes** | `0.0d` | ~ ±1.8e308 (15-digit precision) |
| `char` | **2 bytes** | `'\u0000'` | 0 to 65,535 (Unicode) |
| `boolean` | **1 byte (JVM-dependent)** | `false` | `true` or `false` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Variable Declaration, Initialization, and Usage — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Lifecycle of a Variable
1. **Declaration:** The compiler allocates memory based on the data type. Syntax: `dataType variableName;`
2. **Initialization:** A value is assigned for the first time. Syntax: `variableName = value;` or combined: `dataType variableName = value;`
3. **Usage/Manipulation:** The variable is read, modified, or passed to methods.
4. **Destruction:** When the scope ends, the variable is removed from memory (Stack frame popped / Object GC'd).

### Memory Allocation Logic (Stack vs. Heap)
- **Local variables** (including method parameters) are stored in the **Stack memory** — fast, LIFO-managed, thread-safe.
- **Instance variables** are stored in the **Heap memory** as part of the object they belong to.
- **Static variables** are stored in the **Method Area** (a part of the Heap in modern JVMs), shared across all objects of that class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 7-mark question asks to draw the memory diagram showing the difference between primitive variables (value stored directly) and reference variables (address/reference stored). Primitive → value is in the box. Reference → the box holds an *arrow* to another box in the Heap.

## 2.2 Scope Rules in Java (Kerala Board Pattern)

### Variable Scope Hierarchy
- **Class/Static Scope:** Static variables — accessible everywhere in the class via the class name.
- **Instance Scope:** Instance variables — accessible from any non-static method/constructor/block of the class.
- **Method/Local Scope:** Local variables — accessible only inside the block where declared.
- **Block Scope:** Variables declared inside `if`, `for`, `while` blocks — accessible only inside that block.

### Shadowing Rule
If a local variable has the same name as an instance variable, the local variable **shadows** (hides) the instance variable within that method. Use `this.variableName` to refer to the instance variable.

## 2.3 The `final` Keyword — Constant Variables

When a variable is declared with the `final` keyword, its value cannot be changed after initialization. It becomes a **constant**.

- **Final local variable:** Must be initialized once; cannot be reassigned.
- **Final instance variable:** Must be initialized in every constructor or at the time of declaration (non-static final fields get no default value).
- **Final static variable (Class Constant):** Must be initialized in a static block or at the time of declaration. Conventionally written in **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE** (e.g., `MAX_LIMIT`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** `final` makes the **reference** immutable for reference variables, **not the object** itself. You cannot reassign `final Employee e = new Employee();` to a new object, but you can modify the fields inside the Employee object.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

| Concept | Syntax | Memory Location | Default Value | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Local Variable | `int x = 5;` (inside method) | Stack | None (must initialize) | Method execution |
| Instance Variable | `int x;` (in class, no static) | Heap (per object) | `0`, `false`, `null` | Object lifetime |
| Static Variable | `static int x;` (in class) | Method Area | `0`, `false`, `null` | Class lifetime |
| Final Variable | `final int MAX = 100;` | Depends on type | Must initialize explicitly | Same as scope type |
| Reference Variable | `Employee e = new Employee();` | Stack (ref) + Heap (obj) | `null` | Until ref is overwritten |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Variables form the backbone of **state management** in software systems:
- **Embedded Systems:** Sensors store readings in variables that are polled by control loops.
- **Web Applications:** Session variables track user state across HTTP requests.
- **Database Systems:** Variables cache query results for performance optimization.
- **Game Development:** Player position, score, health — all stored as instance variables of a `Player` class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Mechatronics and IoT systems** (relevant to KTU's interdisciplinary ethos), understanding variable lifetime and memory footprint is critical when writing firmware for microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino), where RAM is limited to **2 KB** on an ATmega328P.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Variable Type Resolution

The KTU 2024 examiners love to ask: *"Given a code snippet, classify each variable as local/instance/static and state its default value."* Below is a step-by-step analytical method to solve any such question.

### Worked Example Problem

```java
class Student {
    static int count = 0;           // Line 1
    String name;                     // Line 2
    final int ROLL_NO;               // Line 3
    
    Student(String n) {
        name = n;
        ROLL_NO = ++count;
    }
    
    void display() {
        int marks = 90;              // Line 4
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            int temp = i * 10;       // Line 5
            System.out.println(temp);
        }
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Rahul");
        Student s2 = new Student("Priya");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Classification

| Line | Variable | Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `count` | **Static (class) variable** | Declared with `static` keyword at class level. |
| 2 | `name` | **Instance variable** | Non-static, declared at class level. Default value: `null`. |
| 3 | `ROLL_NO` | **Final instance variable** | Marked `final` at class level, initialized in constructor. |
| 4 | `marks` | **Local variable** | Declared inside method `display()`. Must be initialized. |
| 5 | `temp` | **Local variable (block scope)** | Declared inside `for` block. Dies after each iteration. |
| (main) | `args` | **Local reference variable** | Method parameter, array of String references. |
| (main) | `s1`, `s2` | **Local reference variables** | Hold references to `Student` objects in the Heap. |

### Step-by-Step Memory Trace (Stack + Heap)

**Step 1:** `main()` is called → Stack frame created for `main`.

**Step 2:** `Student s1 = new Student("Rahul");` executes.
- Stack: `s1` (reference) → 0x100
- Heap: Object at 0x100 with `name = "Rahul"`, `ROLL_NO = 1`
- Method Area: `count = 1`

**Step 3:** `Student s2 = new Student("Priya");` executes.
- Stack: `s2` (reference) → 0x200
- Heap: Object at 0x200 with `name = "Priya"`, `ROLL_NO = 2`
- Method Area: `count = 2` (shared, incremented)

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Default Value Logic

The JVM specification defines default values based on the binary representation of memory:

$$
\text{For integral types} \Rightarrow \text{all bits set to } 0 \Rightarrow \text{integer value } 0
$$

$$
\text{For floating-point types} \Rightarrow \text{all bits set to } 0 \Rightarrow \text{value } +0.0
$$

$$
\text{For boolean} \Rightarrow \text{internally } 0 \text{ maps to } \text{false}
$$

$$
\text{For reference types} \Rightarrow \text{all bits set to } 0 \Rightarrow \text{null pointer}
$$

### Numerical Example — Memory Allocation for `int x = 175;`

A 32-bit `int` is stored in two's complement format. Converting `175` to binary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
175 \div 2 &= 87 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
87 \div 2 &= 43 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
43 \div 2 &= 21 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
21 \div 2 &= 10 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
10 \div 2 &= 5 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
5 \div 2 &= 2 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
2 \div 2 &= 1 \text{ remainder } 0 \\
1 \div 2 &= 0 \text{ remainder } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders from bottom to top:

$$
175_{10} = 10101111_2
$$

Pad to 32 bits:

$$
175_{10} = 00000000\,00000000\,00000000\,10101111_2
$$

This is the exact bit pattern stored in the 4-byte Stack memory for `x`.

## 3.3 Derivative Rule — Variable Shadowing Resolution

When a local variable shadows an instance variable, the compiler uses **lexical scoping** (closest enclosing block wins):

$$
\text{Variable Resolved} = \begin{cases} \text{Local variable} & \text{if local name matches instance name} \\ \text{Instance variable} & \text{if accessed via } \texttt{this.name} \end{cases}
$$

### Code Example Demonstrating Shadowing

```java
class Demo {
    int x = 10;  // instance variable
    
    void show() {
        int x = 20;  // local variable, shadows instance
        System.out.println(x);         // prints 20 (local)
        System.out.println(this.x);    // prints 10 (instance)
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If you forget to use `this` in the explanation, the examiner will deduct at least **2 marks** out of 7. Always explicitly mention `this` as the disambiguator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Architecture Diagram — Variable Storage Layout

```mermaid
