# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `while` Loop — Deep Analysis

The `while` loop is the **most general** iteration construct in Java. It is *entry-controlled*, meaning the condition is tested **before** the loop body is entered.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Step 1 — Initialisation:** A loop control variable (e.g., `int i = 0;`) is initialised **before** the `while` keyword. This is done outside the loop syntactically.
2. **Step 2 — Condition Test:** The boolean expression inside `while(...)` is evaluated.
3. **Step 3a — Body Execution:** If the condition is `true`, control enters the loop body and each statement executes sequentially.
4. **Step 3b — Termination Path:** If the condition is `false`, control immediately skips the body and proceeds to the next statement after the loop.
5. **Step 4 — Update:** Inside the body, the control variable is updated (e.g., `i++`). *Without this step, an **infinite loop** occurs — a common KTU pitfall.*
6. **Step 5 — Repeat:** Control returns to Step 2, and the cycle continues until the condition becomes `false`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `while` is "pre-test" (entry-controlled):**
> If the condition is `false` on the very first test, the body executes **zero times**. This is mathematically useful when iteration count is **data-dependent** (e.g., reading until EOF — End of File).

### Syntax

$$
\texttt{while ( \textit{condition} ) \{} \quad \texttt{// loop body} \quad \texttt{\}}
$$

---

## 2.2 The `do-while` Loop — Deep Analysis

The `do-while` loop is **exit-controlled**. The condition is tested **after** the body executes, guaranteeing **at least one execution**.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Step 1 — Body Execution:** Control enters the body unconditionally. The body executes once.
2. **Step 2 — Condition Test:** The boolean expression in `while(...)` is evaluated.
3. **Step 3a — Re-execution:** If `true`, the body executes again (return to Step 1).
4. **Step 3b — Termination:** If `false`, control exits the loop.
5. **Note:** The **semicolon** `;` after `while(condition)` is **mandatory** — a frequent KTU exam question.

### Syntax

$$
\texttt{do \{} \quad \texttt{// loop body} \quad \texttt{\} while ( \textit{condition} );}
$$

### Why `do-while` Exists

- **Menu-driven programs** (where the menu must display at least once).
- **Input validation** (where the user is re-prompted until valid input is given).

---

## 2.3 The `for` Loop — Deep Analysis

The `for` loop is the **most compact and counter-controlled** iteration construct. It consolidates initialisation, condition, and update into a single header.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

The `for` loop header is evaluated in this order:

1. **Step 1 — Initialisation:** The initialisation expression executes **exactly once** at the start.
2. **Step 2 — Condition Test:** The boolean expression is evaluated.
3. **Step 3a — Body:** If `true`, the body executes.
4. **Step 3b — Update:** After the body, the update expression executes.
5. **Step 4:** Return to Step 2 (Condition Test).
6. **Termination:** When condition is `false`, exit.

### Syntax

$$
\texttt{for ( \textit{init}; \textit{condition}; \textit{update} ) \{} \quad \texttt{// body} \quad \texttt{\}}
$$

### Java-Specific Enhancements (Tested in KTU)

| Enhancement | Syntax | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple initialisers | `for (int i=0, j=10; i<j; i++, j--)` | Two counters in one loop |
| Comma-separated | `int[] arr = {1,2,3}; for (int x : arr)` | **Enhanced for / for-each** (read-only traversal) |
| Infinite loop | `for (;;)` | Equivalent to `while(true)` |
| Empty sections | `for (; condition;)` | Conditional entry only |

---

## 2.4 Jump Statements — Deep Analysis

### `break` Statement

- **Role:** Immediately **terminates** the innermost enclosing loop or `switch` statement.
- **Transfer Target:** The statement immediately **after** the loop.
- **Labelled `break`:** In Java, `break` can target a *named label* placed before an outer loop, allowing exit from nested structures.

### `continue` Statement

- **Role:** **Skips** the remaining statements in the current iteration and proceeds to the **next iteration**.
- **In `for`:** The update expression still executes.
- **In `while`/`do-while`:** Control jumps directly to the condition test.
- **Labelled `continue`:** Resumes the **next iteration of the labelled (outer) loop**.

### `return` Statement

- **Role:** Exits from the **current method**, optionally returning a value to the caller.
- **For `void` methods:** `return;` (with no value).
- **For value-returning methods:** `return expression;`

---

## 2.5 Class Fundamentals — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 Class Declaration Anatomy

A Java class declaration has these components:

1. **Modifiers:** `public`, `abstract`, `final`, `strictfp` (access control & qualifiers).
2. **Keyword:** `class` (mandatory).
3. **Class Name:** A valid Java identifier, **PascalCase** by convention.
4. **Optional `extends` Clause:** For **single inheritance** (`class Child extends Parent`).
5. **Optional `implements` Clause:** For **multiple interface implementation** (`implements I1, I2`).
6. **Class Body:** Enclosed in `{ }`, containing fields, methods, constructors, nested classes, and initialiser blocks.

### 2.5.2 Fields (Instance Variables)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *field* is a variable declared **inside a class but outside any method or constructor*. It represents the **state** of an object.

Field declaration can include:
- **Access modifier:** `private`, `protected`, `public`, or *package-private* (no modifier).
- **Non-access modifier:** `static`, `final`, `transient`, `volatile`.
- **Type:** Primitive (`int`, `double`, etc.) or reference (`String`, custom class).
- **Initialiser:** Optional, defaults are: `0` for numeric, `false` for `boolean`, `null` for references.

### 2.5.3 Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *method* is a named block of statements declared inside a class that defines an **operation** on an object's state. It is the **behavioural unit** of a class.

Method signature: `[modifiers] returnType methodName(parameterList) [throws Clause] { body }`

### 2.5.4 The `this` Keyword

`this` is a **reference variable** in Java that refers to the **current invoking object**. Uses:

1. **Disambiguation:** Resolves shadowing between instance fields and parameters with the same name.
2. **Constructor chaining:** `this(args)` invokes another constructor of the same class.
3. **Passing current object:** As an argument to other methods.

### 2.5.5 Object Creation and Lifecycle

Objects are created using the `new` keyword. The `new` operator performs three actions:

1. **Memory allocation** in the **heap** for the object.
2. **Implicit initialisation** of instance variables to default values.
3. **Invocation of a constructor** to perform explicit initialisation.
4. The `new` expression **returns a reference** to the newly created object, which is stored in a reference-type variable.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield syntax and rules for Module 2. **Memorise this completely.**

| Concept | Syntax Template | Termination Condition | Min Executions |
|---|---|---|---|
| `while` | $\texttt{while (cond) \{ body; update; \}}$ | `cond == false` | **0** |
| `do-while` | $\texttt{do \{ body; update; \} while (cond);}$ | `cond == false` | **1** |
| `for` | $\texttt{for (init; cond; update) \{ body; \}}$ | `cond == false` | **0** |
| Enhanced `for` | $\texttt{for (T x : collection) \{ body; \}}$ | collection exhausted | **0** (empty collection) |
| `break` | $\texttt{break;}$ or $\texttt{break label;}$ | Immediate exit | — |
| `continue` | $\texttt{continue;}$ or $\texttt{continue label;}$ | Next iteration | — |
| `return` | $\texttt{return;}$ or $\texttt{return value;}$ | Method exit | — |
| Class | $\texttt{[mod] class Name [extends P] [impl I] \{ \}}$ | — | — |
| Object | $\texttt{ClassName obj = new ClassName(args);}$ | — | — |
| `this` | $\texttt{this.field}$ or $\texttt{this(args)}$ | — | — |

### Memory Layout (Runtime)

| Region | Contents |
|---|---|
| **Stack** | Local variables, method call frames, primitive values |
| **Heap** | All objects and instance variables |
| **Method Area (MetaSpace)** | Class metadata, static fields, constant pool |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Exhaustive Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in this section:** Every code block is followed by an *exhaustive line-by-line trace* showing the state of the loop control variable, the condition value, and the output produced. This mirrors the **dry-run** technique KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate for full marks.

---

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — `while` Loop

### Code Under Analysis

```java
int i = 1;
int sum = 0;
while (i <= 5) {
    sum = sum + i;
    i++;
}
System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Iteration | Initial State of `i` | Condition `i <= 5` | Body Action | `sum` after | `i` after update | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | true | sum = 0 + 1 = 1 | 1 | 2 | (none yet) |
| 2 | 2 | true | sum = 1 + 2 = 3 | 3 | 3 | (none yet) |
| 3 | 3 | true | sum = 3 + 3 = 6 | 6 | 4 | (none yet) |
| 4 | 4 | true | sum = 6 + 4 = 10 | 10 | 5 | (none yet) |
| 5 | 5 | true | sum = 10 + 5 = 15 | 15 | 6 | (none yet) |
| 6 | 6 | **false** | Exit loop | 15 | 6 | **Sum = 15** |

### Derivation of Final Result (Algebraic)

$$
\text{Sum} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} i = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15
$$

This is the closed-form arithmetic series formula:

$$
S_n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \cdot 6}{2} = 15
$$

The loop-derived result **15** matches the closed-form result — verification complete.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough — `for` Loop with Nested Logic

### Code Under Analysis

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for (int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print("* ");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Outer `i` | Inner Loop Range | Stars Printed on Row | Row Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | j = 1 to 1 | * | `* ` |
| 2 | j = 1 to 2 | * * | `* * ` |
| 3 | j = 1 to 3 | * * * | `* * * ` |

### Final Console Output

$$
\texttt{*}\quad \texttt{* *}\quad \texttt{* * *}
$$

This forms a **right-angled triangle pattern** — a classic KTU Module 2 program.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — `do-while` Loop (Menu System)

### Code Under Analysis

```java
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
int choice;
do {
    System.out.println("1. Deposit");
    System.out.println("2. Withdraw");
    System.out.println("3. Exit");
    System.out.print("Enter choice: ");
    choice = sc.nextInt();
} while (choice != 3);
```

### Trace (User Inputs: 1, 2, 3)

| Loop Pass | Body Displays Menu? | User Input | `choice` after | Condition `choice != 3` | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yes (first time, unconditional) | 1 | 1 | true | Re-iterate |
| 2 | Yes | 2 | 2 | true | Re-iterate |
| 3 | Yes | 3 | 3 | **false** | **Exit** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Even if the **first input** is `3` (Exit), the menu still displays once because the body executes *before* the condition is checked. This is the defining behaviour of `do-while`.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Walkthrough — Jump Statements in a Loop

### Code Under Analysis

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
    if (i == 5) {
        break;
    }
    if (i % 2 == 0) {
        continue;
    }
    System.out.print(i + " ");
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

| `i` | `i == 5`? | `i % 2 == 0`? | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | false | false (1 is odd) | Print | `1 ` |
| 2 | false | **true** | `continue` (skip print) | — |
| 3 | false | false | Print | `3 ` |
| 4 | false | **true** | `continue` (skip print) | — |
| 5 | **true** | — | `break` (exit loop) | — |

### Final Output

$$
\texttt{1 3}
$$

### Why This Matters (Analytical Reasoning)

- `continue` filtered out **even numbers** (2 and 4) — only odd numbers printed.
- `break` halted the loop the moment `i == 5`, so `5` was never printed.
- This is a **mini filter-and-terminate pattern** used in real search and data validation logic.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Walkthrough — Class and Object Demonstration

### Code Under Analysis

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    double cgpa;

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Roll No: " + rollNo);
        System.out.println("CGPA: " + cgpa);
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();
        s1.name = "Anu";
        s1.rollNo = 101;
        s1.cgpa = 9.2;
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Memory Trace

| Step | Action | Heap Allocation | Reference `s1` Points To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class loaded by JVM | `Student.class` metadata in Method Area | — |
| 2 | `new Student()` executes | Heap block allocated for one `Student` | — |
| 3 | Default field initialisation | `name = null`, `rollNo = 0`, `cgpa = 0.0` | New Student object |
| 4 | `s1.name = "Anu"` | Field updated to `"Anu"` | Same object |
| 5 | `s1.rollNo = 101` | Field updated to `101` | Same object |
| 6 | `s1.cgpa = 9.2` | Field updated to `9.2` | Same object |
| 7 | `s1.display()` | Stack frame created for `display()` | Same object accessed via `this` |

### Final Console Output

```
Name: Anu
Roll No: 101
CGPA: 9.2
```

### Why This Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> The phrase *"each object has its own copy of instance variables"* is tested. The heap block for `s1` is **independent** of any other `Student` object. Modifying `s1.name` does not affect any other object's `name` field.

---

## 3.6 Detailed Walkthrough — The `this` Keyword

### Code Under Analysis

```java
class Box {
    double length, width, height;

    Box(double length, double width, double height) {
        this.length = length;
        this.width = width;
        this.height = height;
    }

    double volume() {
        return this.length * this.width * this.height;
    }
}
```

### Trace Analysis

In the constructor call `new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0)`:
- The local parameter `length = 2.0` **shadows** the instance field `length`.
- `this.length = length` resolves the ambiguity:
  - LHS `this.length` → instance field of the current object.
  - RHS `length` → constructor parameter.
- After constructor: object state = `{length: 2.0, width: 3.0, height: 4.0}`.
- `volume()` returns:

$$
V = 2.0 \times 3.0 \times 4.0 = 24.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Without `this`**, the assignment `length = length;` would be a **no-op** — the parameter assigns to itself, and the instance field remains at its default value `0.0`. This is a very common KTU exam bug.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Loop?

| Criterion | `while` | `do-while` | `for` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test position** | Pre-test (entry) | Post-test (exit) | Pre-test (entry) |
| **Min executions** | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Best for** | Sentinel-terminated input | Menu systems | Counter-driven |
| **Syntax compactness** | Medium | Medium | **Highest** |
| **Risk of infinite loop** | High (if update missing) | High (if update missing) | Lower (update in header) |
| **Enhanced for variant?** | No | No | **Yes** (`for-each`) |
| **KTU exam frequency** | High | Medium | **Very High** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** syntax with **alphanumeric node IDs** (no reserved keywords). Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure successful compilation.

---

## 4.1 Flowchart — Generic `while` Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[INITIALISE COUNTER]
    B --> C{TEST CONDITION}
    C -->|TRUE| D[EXECUTE LOOP BODY]
    D --> E[UPDATE COUNTER]
    E --> C
    C -->|FALSE| F([END])
```

### Functional Interpretation

- **Node A:** Entry point of the program segment.
- **Node B:** Initialisation of the loop control variable (executes **once**).
- **Node C:** Diamond — the boolean decision gate.
- **Node D:** The loop body (all repeated statements).
- **Node E:** The update step (e.g., `i++`). Critical to avoid infinite loops.
- **Node F:** Exit point — control proceeds to the next statement.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Generic `do-while` Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[EXECUTE LOOP BODY]
    B --> C[UPDATE COUNTER]
    C --> D{TEST CONDITION}
    D -->|TRUE| B
    D -->|FALSE| E([END])
```

### Functional Interpretation

Note the **reversed** topology compared to `while`: body executes first, then condition is tested. This guarantees **at least one** execution pass.

---

## 4.3 Flowchart — Generic `for` Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[INIT EXPRESSION]
    B --> C{TEST CONDITION}
    C -->|TRUE| D[EXECUTE LOOP BODY]
    D --> E[UPDATE EXPRESSION]
    E --> C
    C -->|FALSE| F([END])
```

### Functional Interpretation

Structurally identical to `while`, but the **initialisation** and **update** are *visually localised* in the header, reducing the risk of forgetting either.

---

## 4.4 Flowchart — `break` and `continue` in a Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[INIT I = 1]
    B --> C{I <= 10}
    C -->|FALSE| K([END])
    C -->|TRUE| D{I == 5}
    D -->|TRUE| K
    D -->|FALSE| E{I % 2 == 0}
    E -->|TRUE| G[SKIP REMAINDER]
    E -->|FALSE| F[PRINT I]
    F --> H[I = I + 1]
    G --> H
    H --> C
```

### Functional Interpretation

- **Node D:** When `i == 5`, the `break` statement fires and control jumps **directly to K (END)**.
- **Node E:** When `i` is even, the `continue` statement fires and control jumps to **Node H** (update), skipping the print action.
- **Node F:** Only odd numbers (other than 5) are printed.
- The end result is the same as Section 3.4's trace: `1 3`.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Java Class

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASS["CLASS DECLARATION BLOCK"]
        A1[MODIFIERS e.g. PUBLIC]
        A2[KEYWORD CLASS]
        A3[CLASS NAME e.g. STUDENT]
        A4[EXTENDS CLAUSE OPTIONAL]
        A5[IMPLEMENTS CLAUSE OPTIONAL]
    end

    subgraph BODY["CLASS BODY INSIDE CURLY BRACES"]
        B1[FIELDS INSTANCE VARIABLES]
        B2[CONSTRUCTORS]
        B3[METHODS BEHAVIOURS]
        B4[STATIC BLOCKS]
        B5[NESTED CLASSES]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A5 --> BODY
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
```

### Functional Interpretation

This block diagram mirrors the **syntactic structure** of a Java class declaration. The header (modifiers → `class` → name → inheritance) precedes the body, which contains five categories of members in *any order* (though convention orders them: fields → constructors → methods).

---

## 4.6 Memory Architecture — Stack vs Heap for Objects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STACK["STACK MEMORY"]
        S1[MAIN SCOPE]
        S2[REFERENCE S1 POINTS TO HEAP]
        S3[REFERENCE S2 POINTS TO HEAP]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        H1[OBJECT 1 NAME ROLLNO CGPA]
        H2[OBJECT 2 NAME ROLLNO CGPA]
    end

    subgraph META["METHOD AREA METASPACE"]
        M1[STUDENT.CLASS METADATA]
        M2[STATIC FIELDS IF ANY]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 -.-> H1
    S3 -.-> H2
    H1 -.-> M1
    H2 -.-> M1
```

### Functional Interpretation

- The **stack** holds primitive variables and **references** (pointers) to objects.
- The **heap** holds the actual object data.
- The **method area** holds class-level metadata, shared by all instances.
- Multiple stack references can point to the **same** heap object (aliasing), or each can point to a **different** heap object.

---

## 4.7 Flowchart — Object Creation via `new`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([NEW EXPRESSION TRIGGERED]) --> B[ALLOCATE HEAP MEMORY]
    B --> C[DEFAULT INITIALISE FIELDS]
    C --> D[INVOKE CONSTRUCTOR]
    D --> E[RETURN REFERENCE]
    E --> F([ASSIGN TO REFERENCE VARIABLE])
```

### Functional Interpretation

This is the **four-step lifecycle** of every Java object creation: memory allocation → default initialisation → constructor execution → reference return. KTU examiners frequently test this as a *process-order* question.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions, **3 marks** each.
> - Part B: Long-answer questions, **14 marks** each, with **internal choice** (attempt any one of two alternatives).
> - Part B sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between `while` and `do-while` loops in Java. Which one guarantees at least one execution of the loop body?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `while` loop | `do-while` loop |
|---|---|---|
| Test type | Entry-controlled (pre-test) | Exit-controlled (post-test) |
| Minimum executions | Zero (if condition false initially) | One (body executes before test) |
| Syntax | $\texttt{while(cond)\{ body; \}}$ | $\texttt{do\{ body; \} while(cond);}$ |
| Semicolon | Not required after `)` | **Mandatory** after `while(cond);` |
| Typical use | Counter-driven, sentinel-terminated | Menu-driven, input-validation |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The `do-while` loop guarantees at least one execution of the loop body because the condition is tested **after** the body has executed.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**What is a class in Java? How is it different from an object?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **class** in Java is a blueprint or template that defines the **attributes (fields)** and **behaviours (methods)** of similar entities. It is declared using the `class` keyword and exists in source code as a logical construct.

An **object** is a **runtime instance** of a class, created using the `new` keyword. It occupies actual memory in the heap and has its own copies of instance fields.

**Key Differences (in tabular form for 1 mark):**

| Class | Object |
|---|---|
| Logical template | Physical instance |
| Declared once in source | Created many times at runtime |
| No memory for fields | Memory allocated in heap |
| Loaded once per JVM | Each has its own identity (hashCode) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question offers **two alternatives** (e.g., "OR"). The student must attempt **any ONE** of the two. Both alternatives are designed to test the **same CO** and **same Bloom's Level**.

---

### Question B-1 (Option A) — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the three iteration statements in Java with suitable syntax. Write a Java program to print the first **N** Fibonacci numbers using a `for` loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the `break` and `continue` statements in Java. Differentiate between labelled and unlabelled forms with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**The Three Iteration Statements:**

1. **`while` loop** — entry-controlled, condition tested first.
2. **`do-while` loop** — exit-controlled, body executed at least once.
3. **`for` loop** — counter-controlled, compact header.

**Java Program — First N Fibonacci Numbers:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Fibonacci {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter N: ");
        int n = sc.nextInt();

        int a = 0, b = 1;
        System.out.print("Fibonacci: ");
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            System.out.print(a + " ");
            int next = a + b;
            a = b;
            b = next;
        }
    }
}
```

**Dry Run for N = 6:**

| Iteration `i` | `a` (printed) | `b` (next term) | Update `a = b` | Update `b = next` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | a=1 | b=1 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | a=1 | b=2 |
| 3 | 1 | 2 | a=2 | b=3 |
| 4 | 2 | 3 | a=3 | b=5 |
| 5 | 3 | 5 | a=5 | b=8 |
| 6 | 5 | 8 | a=8 | b=13 |

**Output:** `Fibonacci: 0 1 1 2 3 5`

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**`break` Statement:**

The `break` statement terminates the **innermost** enclosing loop or `switch` immediately and transfers control to the statement following the loop.

**Example (Unlabelled `break`):**

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
    if (i == 10) {
        break; // exits the for loop
    }
    System.out.print(i + " ");
}
// Output: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9
```

**`continue` Statement:**

The `continue` statement skips the **remaining** statements in the current iteration and proceeds to the **next iteration** of the loop.

**Example (Unlabelled `continue`):**

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
    if (i == 3) {
        continue; // skips printing 3
    }
    System.out.print(i + " ");
}
// Output: 1 2 4 5
```

**Labelled `break` and `continue`:**

A **label** is an identifier followed by a colon placed before a loop. It allows the `break` or `continue` to target an **outer** loop in a nested structure.

```java
outer: for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for (int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        if (i == 2 && j == 2) {
            break outer; // exits the OUTER loop entirely
        }
        System.out.println(i + " " + j);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
1 1
1 2
1 3
2 1
```

**Comparative Table (Distinguishing Labelled vs Unlabelled):**

| Feature | Unlabelled | Labelled |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Innermost loop only | Any enclosing labelled loop |
| Syntax | `break;` | `break labelName;` |
| Use case | Single-loop exit | Nested loop exit / skip |
| Frequency in KTU | Very High | Moderate |

---

### Question B-1 (Option B) — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a class in Java with a general syntax. Write a Java program to create a class `Rectangle` with fields `length` and `width`, methods to calculate area and perimeter, and demonstrate object creation in the `main` method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the `this` keyword in Java? Explain its three primary uses with suitable code snippets. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Concept of a Class:**

A **class** is a user-defined reference type in Java that encapsulates **data (fields)** and **behaviour (methods)** into a single logical unit. It serves as a **template** from which **objects** (instances) are created at runtime.

**General Syntax:**

```java
[access_modifier] [non_access_modifier] class ClassName [extends Parent] [implements Interface1, Interface2] {
    // Fields
    [access_mod] dataType fieldName;

    // Constructors
    [access_mod] ClassName(parameters) { ... }

    // Methods
    [access_mod] returnType methodName(parameters) { ... }
}
```

**Java Program — `Rectangle` Class:**

```java
class Rectangle {
    double length;
    double width;

    double area() {
        return length * width;
    }

    double perimeter() {
        return 2 * (length + width);
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Length: " + length);
        System.out.println("Width: " + width);
        System.out.println("Area: " + area());
        System.out.println("Perimeter: " + perimeter());
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();
        r1.length = 10.0;
        r1.width = 5.0;
        r1.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Length: 10.0
Width: 5.0
Area: 50.0
Perimeter: 30.0
```

**Derivation of Area Formula:**

$$
A = \text{length} \times \text{width} = 10.0 \times 5.0 = 50.0
$$

**Derivation of Perimeter Formula:**

$$
P = 2 \times (\text{length} + \text{width}) = 2 \times (10.0 + 5.0) = 30.0
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**The `this` Keyword:**

`this` is a **reference variable** in Java that refers to the **current object** — the object on which a method or constructor is being invoked.

**Three Primary Uses:**

**Use 1 — Disambiguation (Resolving Shadowing):**

```java
class Student {
    String name;

    Student(String name) {
        this.name = name; // LHS = instance field, RHS = parameter
    }
}
```

If we write `name = name;`, the parameter assigns to itself and the instance field stays at default `null`. Using `this.name = name;` fixes this.

**Use 2 — Constructor Chaining:**

```java
class Box {
    double length, width, height;

    Box() {
        this(1.0, 1.0, 1.0); // calls the 3-arg constructor
        System.out.println("Default constructor called");
    }

    Box(double l, double w, double h) {
        this.length = l;
        this.width = w;
        this.height = h;
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** `this(args)` **must** be the **first statement** in a constructor. Otherwise, a compile-time error occurs.

**Use 3 — Passing the Current Object:**

```java
class Helper {
    void process(Box b) {
        System.out.println("Processing box of height: " + b.height);
    }
}

class Box {
    double height = 5.0;

    void sendToHelper() {
        Helper h = new Helper();
        h.process(this); // pass current object
    }
}
```

**Summary Table:**

| Use | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Disambiguation | `this.field = param` | Distinguish fields from parameters |
| Constructor chaining | `this(args)` | Invoke another constructor in the same class |
| Object passing | `method(this)` | Pass current object as argument |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalise (Frequently Resulting in 1–3 Mark Deductions):**
>
> 1. **Missing semicolon** after `do-while` condition. This is a **compile-time error**. The semicolon is **mandatory** because `do-while` is technically a single statement ending with the test.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the update expression** in `while` loops, causing **infinite loops** (e.g., `while(i < 10) { System.out.println(i); }` without `i++`).
>
> 3. **Confusing `break` with `continue`**: students often write `break` when they mean `continue` (or vice versa), producing incorrect outputs.
>
> 4. **Writing `=` instead of `==`** in loop conditions. In Java, `=` is assignment, not equality. A statement like `while(i = 5)` causes a compile error (in boolean context) or infinite loop.
>
> 5. **Declaring the loop counter inside the `for` header but then trying to access it outside the loop**. The scope of a `for` loop variable is limited to the loop itself.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to use `this` keyword** when parameter names shadow instance fields. The instance field remains at its default value (`0`, `null`, `false`), causing logic errors.
>
> 7. **Confusing stack and heap memory** in object-creation questions. References live on the stack; objects live in the heap. Examiners deduct marks for reversed statements.
>
> 8. **Using `goto`**: It is a **reserved keyword** in Java but has **no implementation**. Students who write `goto label;` in code lose full marks for the code section.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for last-hour revision before the KTU exam.**

### Iteration Statements
- `while` is **entry-controlled**, **0 or more** executions.
- `do-while` is **exit-controlled**, **1 or more** executions; semicolon after `while(...)` is **mandatory**.
- `for` loop executes **init** → **test** → **body** → **update** → **test** → ... sequence.
- The **enhanced for-each** loop (`for (T x : collection)`) is **read-only** — you cannot reassign the loop variable to modify the collection.
- A `for` loop with no condition (`for(;;)`) is an **infinite loop** — equivalent to `while(true)`.

### Jump Statements
- `break` exits the **innermost** enclosing loop or `switch` **immediately**.
- `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and proceeds to the **next**.
- In a `for` loop, `continue` still executes the **update** expression.
- **Labelled** `break`/`continue` use a label placed before an **outer** loop.
- `return` exits the **current method**; for `void` methods, no value is returned.
- `goto` is a reserved keyword with no functionality in Java.

### Class Fundamentals
- A **class** is a blueprint; an **object** is an instance.
- Class members include: **fields, methods, constructors, static blocks, nested classes**.
- Object creation follows: **heap allocation → default initialisation → constructor → reference return**.
- `this` keyword refers to the **current invoking object**.
- `this(args)` must be the **first statement** in a constructor.
- Reference variables hold **addresses** in the stack, pointing to objects in the heap.
- The **default initial value** of an uninitialised instance field is: `0` (numeric), `false` (boolean), `null` (reference).
- A class can **extend only one** parent (single inheritance) but **implement multiple** interfaces.
- A class declared as `final` **cannot be subclassed**; a method declared as `final` **cannot be overridden**.

### KTU-Specific Exam Pointers
- Always provide a **dry-run trace** in tabular form for any loop program — this fetches full marks.
- Always include **time complexity** comments in program answers when comparing loops.
- Always use **access modifiers** (`private`, `public`) explicitly in class definitions; avoid package-private unless asked.
- For object questions, **diagram the memory** (stack vs heap) — this is worth bonus marks.
- In definitions, **distinguish clearly** between class and object, between compile-time and runtime, between stack and heap.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Declaring Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Declaration** in Object-Oriented Programming is the two-step syntactic process of **declaring a reference variable** (a typed handle bound to a class or interface) and **instantiating the object** (allocating heap memory via the `new` operator and invoking a constructor) so that the reference variable points to a fully constructed instance residing in dynamic memory.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (mapped to **CO2 — Apply object-oriented constructs to model real-world entities**), an object is declared in **two distinct phases**:

1. **Declaration Phase** — A *reference variable* of a class type is created on the **stack memory**. This variable is a pointer (in Java's case, a managed reference; in C++, an actual address variable) capable of holding the memory address of an object.
2. **Instantiation Phase** — The `new` keyword dynamically allocates contiguous memory for the object on the **heap memory**, initializes the instance variables to their default values, executes the constructor, and returns the memory address which is then stored in the reference variable.

$$ \text{Declaration} = \underbrace{\text{Type}}_{\text{Class Name}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Identifier}}_{\text{Reference Name}} \;\; \underbrace{= \textbf{new}}_{\text{Memory Allocator}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Constructor()}}_{\text{Initialization Call}} $$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "House Key" Model**

Think of declaring an object like **getting the keys to a newly built house**:

| Step | Code Equivalent | Real-World Analogy |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | `House myHouse;` | You obtain an **empty keychain slot** labeled "myHouse" — but there is no house yet. |
| 2 | `= new House();` | A construction company **builds a house** at some address and hands you the keys. |
| 3 | `myHouse.openDoor();` | You **use the key** to access the actual house and perform actions. |

The key (reference) and the house (object) are **two separate entities**. Losing the key doesn't demolish the house — it just means you can't access it. Similarly, reassigning a reference variable to `null` (in Java) just discards the address; the object remains on the heap until the **Garbage Collector** notices no live references point to it.

## 1.3 Standardized Memory Metrics (Java JVM — Default HotSpot)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests these **default memory values** in 3-mark Part A questions. Memorize them.

- **Default object header size** (HotSpot, 64-bit, compressed oops) = **12 bytes** (or 16 with alignment padding)
- **Reference variable size on stack** = **4 bytes** (compressed) or **8 bytes** (uncompressed)
- **Default value of `int` field** = **0**
- **Default value of `boolean` field** = **false**
- **Default value of object reference field** = **null**
- **Default value of `char` field** = **'\u0000'**

These defaults are applied by the JVM during heap allocation *before* the constructor body executes, ensuring that no instance variable is ever left uninitialized in a defined-accessible state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — The 7 Logical Steps of Object Declaration

When the KTU examiner asks "Explain the steps involved in declaring and instantiating an object," these are the **7 universally accepted steps** the model answer must contain:

1. **Class Identification** — The programmer identifies a previously defined class (e.g., `Student`) whose blueprint will be used to manufacture a runtime instance.
2. **Reference Variable Declaration** — A variable of class type `Student` is declared. The compiler allocates **4 or 8 bytes** on the **stack frame** of the current method to hold a memory address. The variable is currently `null`.
3. **Memory Allocation Request** — The `new` keyword instructs the JVM to invoke the class loader (if not already loaded) and request a contiguous memory block from the heap of size = (sum of all instance variable sizes + object header).
4. **Default Initialization** — The JVM writes default values (0, false, null, '\u0000') into all instance variable slots of the newly allocated object. This is the **implicit zeroing** phase.
5. **Explicit Initialization** — Java executes the **instance initializer blocks** and **field initializers** in the order they appear in the source code (top-to-bottom).
6. **Constructor Invocation** — The matched constructor (based on argument signature) is executed. Inside the constructor, `super()` is implicitly called first (transitively reaching `Object()`), then field initializations, then the constructor's own body.
7. **Reference Assignment** — The memory address returned by the `new` expression is **stored** in the reference variable declared in step 2. The reference now points to the fully constructed object on the heap.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love the *why*. Don't just list steps — explain them.

- **Why two steps (declaration + instantiation)?** It enables **separating the existence of a handle from the existence of an object**, supporting polymorphism, late binding, and conditional object creation.
- **Why heap memory and not stack?** Objects have **dynamic lifetimes** — they may need to outlive the method that created them. The heap supports this; the stack does not.
- **Why default initialization?** The JVM specification mandates that no memory access should ever return arbitrary garbage. Safety first.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Memory Location | Default State | Lifetime |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|----------|
| Reference Declaration Only | `ClassName refVar;` | Stack | `null` (Java) / Undefined (C++) | Method scope |
| Object Instantiation | `refVar = new ClassName(args);` | Heap | Zeroed fields | Until GC / delete |
| Combined Declaration | `ClassName refVar = new ClassName(args);` | Stack + Heap | Constructed object | Method scope / GC |
| Anonymous Object | `new ClassName(args).methodCall();` | Heap (no ref) | Constructed object | Single statement |
| Array of Objects | `ClassName[] arr = new ClassName[5];` | Heap (array) | Each element = `null` | Until GC |
| Object Array + Init | `ClassName[] arr = {new A(), new A()};` | Heap | Constructed | Until GC |
| Reference Reassignment | `refVar = new ClassName();` | New heap obj | Old obj eligible for GC | Scoped |
| Final Reference | `final ClassName ref = new ClassName();` | Stack + Heap | Reassignment forbidden | Scoped |
| Class Variable (static) | `ClassName.staticField` | Method Area | Loaded with class | JVM lifetime |
| Local Variable | `ClassName ref = new ClassName();` | Stack + Heap | Constructed | Method block |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, all $\vert$ symbols (used in absolute-value contexts in your head) have been deliberately omitted to prevent markdown table breakage. Write `\vert` in LaTeX contexts only.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Object declaration is the **fundamental kernel** of every enterprise system. In **Android Development**, `Activity` objects are declared and instantiated via the framework's reflection-based `Class.forName(...).newInstance()` mechanism. In **Spring Boot (KTU's industry-relevant IoT/Cloud modules)**, the IoC container dynamically declares and injects objects using `@Autowired` — internally calling the same `new`-equivalent constructor invocation. In **embedded systems** (relevant to KTU's EEE/ECE streams), object pools pre-declare reusable objects to avoid costly heap fragmentation during real-time sensor polling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Object Lifecycle Analysis

**Source Code:**

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo = 101;                    // field initializer
    String name;                         // field declaration only

    {                                     // instance initializer block
        System.out.println("Init block");
    }

    Student() {                          // no-arg constructor
        name = "Default Name";
        System.out.println("Constructor");
    }

    Student(int r, String n) {           // parameterized constructor
        rollNo = r;
        name = n;
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();
        Student s2 = new Student(202, "Kavya");
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Execution of `Student s1 = new Student();`**

| Step | Action | Stack State | Heap State | Console Output |
|------|--------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Compile `Test.class` and `Student.class` | empty | empty | — |
| 2 | JVM invokes `main()` | `args` reference | empty | — |
| 3 | `Student s1` declaration | `s1 = null` (4 bytes) | empty | — |
| 4 | `new Student` — heap allocation request | `s1 = null` | 16-byte block reserved | — |
| 5 | Default initialization of fields | `s1 = null` | `rollNo = 0`, `name = null` | — |
| 6 | Explicit field initializers run | `s1 = null` | `rollNo = 101` | — |
| 7 | Instance initializer block runs | `s1 = null` | unchanged | `Init block` |
| 8 | Constructor `Student()` body runs | `s1 = null` | `name = "Default Name"` | `Constructor` |
| 9 | Address returned and assigned to `s1` | `s1 = 0xABC123` | object at 0xABC123 | — |

**Execution of `Student s2 = new Student(202, "Kavya");`**

| Step | Action | Stack State | Heap State | Console Output |
|------|--------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | `Student s2` declaration | `s2 = null` | object #1 at 0xABC123 | — |
| 2 | `new Student` heap allocation | `s2 = null` | new block reserved (0xDEF456) | — |
| 3 | Default initialization | `s2 = null` | `rollNo = 0`, `name = null` | — |
| 4 | Explicit field initializers | `s2 = null` | `rollNo = 101` | — |
| 5 | Instance initializer block | `s2 = null` | unchanged | `Init block` |
| 6 | Parameterized constructor with `r=202, n="Kavya"` | `s2 = null` | `rollNo = 202`, `name = "Kavya"` | — |
| 7 | Address assigned to `s2` | `s2 = 0xDEF456` | two distinct objects in heap | — |

## 3.3 Mathematical Memory Footprint Derivation

For a `Student` object with `int rollNo` (4 bytes) and `String name` (4-byte reference, 8-byte `String` object on heap):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object Size} &= \text{Object Header} + \text{Instance Variables Sum} + \text{Padding} \\
&= 12 \text{ bytes} + (4 \text{ bytes} + 4 \text{ bytes}) + 0 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 20 \text{ bytes (unaligned)} \rightarrow 24 \text{ bytes (8-byte aligned)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the class contains a `boolean` field (1 byte in JVM, but padded to 4 bytes for alignment):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object Size} &= 12 + (4 + 4 + 4) \\
&= 24 \text{ bytes (already aligned)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Reference Reassignment & Garbage Collection Trace

```java
Student s1 = new Student();        // Heap obj #1 created
Student s2 = new Student();        // Heap obj #2 created
s1 = s2;                            // s1 now points to obj #2
// Heap obj #1 is now eligible for Garbage Collection
```

**Mathematical condition for GC eligibility:**

$$
\text{Eligible for GC} \iff \text{Reference Count}(\text{object}) = 0
$$

$$
\text{Reference Count}(\text{Obj\#1}) = \underbrace{1}_{s1} - \underbrace{1}_{s1 = s2 \text{ (lost ref)}} = 0 \implies \text{GC Eligible}
$$

## 3.5 The Analytical Matrix — All Object Declaration Forms

| Form | Code | # of Stack Refs | # of Heap Objects | GC Eligible After Statement? |
|------|------|---------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Bare declaration | `A a;` | 1 (`null`) | 0 | N/A |
| Declaration + init | `A a = new A();` | 1 | 1 | No |
| Anonymous object | `new A().display();` | 0 | 1 (transient) | Yes, immediately after `;` |
| Two refs, one obj | `A a = new A(); A b = a;` | 2 | 1 | No |
| Ref reassignment | `a = new A();` | 1 | 2 (1 GC-eligible) | Yes, old object |
| Array declaration | `A[] arr = new A[3];` | 1 (array ref) | 1 array (3 nulls) | No |
| Array of init objects | `A[] arr = {new A(), new A()};` | 1 | 3 (1 array + 2 objs) | No |
| Static factory | `A a = A.create();` | 1 | 1 | No |
| Cloning | `A b = (A) a.clone();` | 2 | 2 (shallow dup) | No |
| Reflection | `A a = (A) Class.forName("A").newInstance();` | 1 | 1 | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs below are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) — no reserved keywords used. Labels are raw uppercase text, free of markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Memory Architecture Diagram — Stack vs Heap

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph STACK["STACK MEMORY - main method frame"]
        nodeA["s1 REFERENCE 0xABC123"]
        nodeB["s2 REFERENCE 0xDEF456"]
        nodeC["args REFERENCE 0xARR001"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY - Object instances"]
        nodeD["OBJECT 1 0xABC123 rollNo 101 name Default"]
        nodeE["OBJECT 2 0xDEF456 rollNo 202 name Kavya"]
        nodeF["STRING POOL name Kavya literal"]
    end

    subgraph METH["METHOD AREA - Class metadata"]
        nodeG["Student.class blueprint"]
        nodeH["Test.class bytecode"]
    end

    nodeA -. points to .-> nodeD
    nodeB -. points to .-> nodeE
    nodeD -. name ref .-> nodeF
    nodeG -. template for .-> nodeD
    nodeG -. template for .-> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Object Declaration Lifecycle Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["START: Source code statement"] --> stepA1["Compile class definition"]
    stepA1 --> stepA2["JVM loads .class into Method Area"]
    stepA2 --> stepA3["ClassName refVar DECLARED on stack"]
    stepA3 --> stepA4["refVar value equals NULL"]
    stepA4 --> stepA5["new keyword INVOKED"]
    stepA5 --> stepA6["Heap memory ALLOCATED"]
    stepA6 --> stepA7["DEFAULT initialization of fields"]
    stepA7 --> stepA8["EXPLICIT field initializers run"]
    stepA8 --> stepA9["INSTANCE initializer blocks execute"]
    stepA9 --> stepA10["CONSTRUCTOR body executes"]
    stepA10 --> stepA11["Return address assigned to refVar"]
    stepA11 --> stepA12["Object FULLY CONSTRUCTED and accessible"]
    stepA12 --> stepA13["END: refVar points to live heap object"]
```

## 4.4 Reference Reassignment & GC Eligibility Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph T1["INITIAL STATE"]
        refX1["refA -> 0xA1"]
        refY1["refB -> 0xB1"]
        objA1["Object at 0xA1"]
        objB1["Object at 0xB1"]
    end

    subgraph T2["AFTER refA = refB"]
        refX2["refA -> 0xB1"]
        refY2["refB -> 0xB1"]
        objA2["Object at 0xA1 ORPHANED"]
        objB2["Object at 0xB1 REF COUNT 2"]
    end

    T1 ==> T2
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Object Declaration Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE CODE LAYER"]
        nodeSrc1["Class Definition with fields and constructors"]
        nodeSrc2["Reference variable declaration statement"]
        nodeSrc3["new operator and constructor call"]
    end

    subgraph COMP["COMPILE TIME LAYER"]
        nodeC1["javac produces .class file"]
        nodeC2["Symbol table entry for class type"]
        nodeC3["Bytecode new and invokespecial instructions"]
    end

    subgraph RUN["RUNTIME LAYER"]
        nodeR1["ClassLoader subsystem"]
        nodeR2["Memory allocator in heap"]
        nodeR3["Default initializer routine"]
        nodeR4["Constructor execution engine"]
        nodeR5["Reference assignment on stack"]
    end

    nodeSrc1 --> nodeC1
    nodeSrc2 --> nodeC2
    nodeSrc3 --> nodeC3
    nodeC1 --> nodeR1
    nodeC3 --> nodeR2
    nodeR1 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: What is the difference between declaring an object and instantiating an object in Java? Give one example.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Declaring an object means creating a **reference variable** of a class type on the stack memory. The variable is capable of holding the address of an object but currently holds `null`. Instantiation means using the `new` keyword to **allocate heap memory**, initialize fields, and invoke the constructor. The address returned is then stored in the reference.
>
> Example:
> ```java
> Student s;        // declaration only - s is null
> s = new Student(); // instantiation - s now points to heap object
> ```
> Mark split: Definition 1, Distinction 1, Example 1.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What are anonymous objects in Java? When are they used?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Anonymous objects are objects that are **instantiated but not assigned to any reference variable**. They exist on the heap for only the duration of the single statement in which they are created, after which they become eligible for garbage collection.
>
> Example: `new Student(101, "Arjun").displayDetails();`
>
> **Uses:** (i) One-time method calls where the object is not needed later, (ii) Passing objects as arguments to methods without storing them, (iii) Reducing memory footprint in event-handling callbacks.
> Mark split: Definition 1, Example 1, Uses 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat diagram the process of object declaration and instantiation in Java. Discuss the role of stack and heap memory with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Concept Definition (2 marks):**
> Object declaration in Java is a two-phase operation: **declaration of a reference variable** and **instantiation using the `new` operator**. A reference variable is a typed handle that stores the memory address of an object. The `new` operator dynamically allocates memory on the heap, initializes the object, and returns its address.
>
> **Step 2 — Stack and Heap Roles (2 marks):**
> - **Stack memory** stores the reference variable (a local handle, typically 4 or 8 bytes), method parameters, and primitive local variables. It follows LIFO allocation with automatic deallocation when the method exits.
> - **Heap memory** stores the actual object instance, including all instance variables and the object header. It supports dynamic allocation and is managed by the Garbage Collector.
>
> **Step 3 — Diagram (1 mark):**
> *(Draw a stack box with `s1 → 0xABC` and a heap box with `Student Object at 0xABC: rollNo=101, name="Kavya"`. Show arrow from s1 to the heap object.)*
>
> **Step 4 — Code Example (1 mark):**
> ```java
> class Student {
>     int rollNo;
>     String name;
>     Student(int r, String n) { rollNo = r; name = n; }
> }
> public class Test {
>     public static void main(String[] args) {
>         Student s1 = new Student(101, "Kavya");
>     }
> }
> ```
>
> **Step 5 — Execution Flow Explanation (1 mark):**
> When `main()` is invoked, a stack frame is created. The statement `Student s1` allocates 4 bytes on the stack, initialized to `null`. The `new Student(...)` expression allocates 24 bytes on the heap, default-initializes fields (`rollNo=0, name=null`), runs the constructor, and returns the address. The address is stored in `s1`, completing the declaration.

**(b)** What are the different ways to declare and initialize objects in Java? Write a Java program to demonstrate the creation of multiple objects of the same class and explain how references are stored. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Enumerate Ways (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Reference declaration only:** `Student s;` — `s` is `null`.
> 2. **Declaration + instantiation:** `Student s = new Student();`
> 3. **Declaration + parameterized instantiation:** `Student s = new Student(101, "Kavya");`
> 4. **Multiple references to one object:** `Student s1 = new Student(); Student s2 = s1;`
> 5. **Anonymous object:** `new Student(101, "Arjun").display();`
> 6. **Array of objects:** `Student[] arr = new Student[3];` followed by `arr[0] = new Student(...);`
> 7. **Static factory method:** `Student s = Student.create();`
> 8. **Cloning:** `Student s2 = (Student) s1.clone();`
> 9. **Reflection-based:** `Student s = (Student) Class.forName("Student").newInstance();`
>
> **Step 2 — Java Program (2 marks):**
> ```java
> class Box {
>     int length, width;
>     Box(int l, int w) { length = l; width = w; }
>     int area() { return length * width; }
> }
> public class Demo {
>     public static void main(String[] args) {
>         Box b1 = new Box(10, 5);
>         Box b2 = new Box(7, 3);
>         Box b3 = b1;        // b3 references same object as b1
>         System.out.println("b1 area = " + b1.area());
>         System.out.println("b2 area = " + b2.area());
>         System.out.println("b3 area = " + b3.area());
>     }
> }
> ```
>
> **Step 3 — Explanation of Reference Storage (2 marks):**
> Three Box objects are created: `b1` and `b3` point to the same heap object (10×5), while `b2` points to a separate heap object (7×3). Modifying state through `b1` will be visible through `b3` because they share the same underlying instance. The references themselves are stored on the **stack frame of `main()`**, each 4 bytes (with compressed oops), holding the address of the heap-allocated Box.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the lifecycle of an object in Java with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of the Garbage Collector. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Lifecycle Phases (3 marks):**
> 1. **Creation** — Memory is allocated on the heap via `new`, fields are default-initialized, initializers and constructor execute.
> 2. **Usage** — The object is accessed and manipulated via its reference variable(s).
> 3. **Inaccessibility** — When all references to the object are lost (e.g., reassigned, set to `null`, or go out of scope), the object becomes **eligible for garbage collection**.
> 4. **Finalization** *(deprecated in Java 9+)* — The object's `finalize()` method may be called once before reclamation.
> 5. **Reclamation** — The Garbage Collector frees the heap memory, making it available for future allocations.
>
> **Step 2 — Diagram (1 mark):**
> *(Draw a horizontal flow: Created → In Use → Unreachable → Finalized → Collected, with arrows and labels indicating reference count and GC eligibility.)*
>
> **Step 3 — Garbage Collector Role (2 marks):**
> The **Garbage Collector (GC)** is a JVM subsystem that automatically reclaims memory occupied by unreachable objects, preventing memory leaks. It uses algorithms like **Mark-and-Sweep**, **Generational Collection** (Young/Old generations), and **G1GC**. The GC periodically traces the object graph starting from **GC roots** (stack references, static fields, JNI references) and marks all reachable objects; unmarked ones are swept. In Java, programmers cannot force GC (`System.gc()` is only a hint), ensuring deterministic cleanup is delegated to try-with-resources and explicit `close()` methods.
>
> **Step 4 — Example (1 mark):**
> ```java
> Student s = new Student();
> s = new Student();    // first object is now GC-eligible
> ```

**(b)** Differentiate between primitive variables and object reference variables in Java. Explain what happens when you assign one object reference to another with a code example. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Differences Table (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Primitive Variable | Object Reference Variable |
> |--------|-------------------|---------------------------|
> | Stored value | Actual data (e.g., `int = 25`) | Memory address of object |
> | Memory location | Stack (local) or Heap (instance field) | Stack (local) / Heap (field) |
> | Default value | `0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` |
> | Size | Fixed (4 bytes for int, 8 for long) | 4 bytes (compressed oops) or 8 bytes |
> | Operations | Arithmetic, comparison | Method invocation, reference comparison |
> | Passed to methods | By value (copy of data) | By value (copy of address — effectively call-by-sharing) |
>
> **Step 2 — Assignment Code (2 marks):**
> ```java
> class Account { double balance; Account(double b) { balance = b; } }
> public class Bank {
>     public static void main(String[] args) {
>         Account a1 = new Account(5000.0);
>         Account a2 = a1;          // reference copy
>         a2.balance = 10000.0;
>         System.out.println(a1.balance);   // prints 10000.0
>     }
> }
> ```
>
> **Step 3 — Explanation (2 marks):**
> When `a2 = a1` executes, the **address stored in `a1` is copied into `a2`**. Both `a1` and `a2` now point to the **same heap object**. Modifying the object through `a2` (e.g., `a2.balance = 10000.0`) is visible through `a1` because they share the same underlying instance. This is fundamentally different from primitive assignment (`int x = 5; int y = x;` — `y` gets its own copy of `5`). The original object is not duplicated; only the reference (address) is duplicated. This is why Java references are said to be **pass-by-value**, where the value passed *is* a reference — a frequent KTU viva question.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Declaring Objects" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention default initialization** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if you skip the JVM's implicit zeroing of fields.
> 2. **Confusing "declaration" with "instantiation"** — Treat them as ONE step → lose 1 mark. Always say "declaration AND instantiation."
> 3. **Ignoring the stack-vs-heap distinction** — A 7-mark question without a memory diagram loses at least 2 marks. Draw it explicitly.
> 4. **Saying Java is "pass-by-reference"** — WRONG. Java is **pass-by-value** where the value is a reference. Examiners WILL deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning constructor invocation** — The `new` operator *implicitly* calls a constructor. If you miss this, lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Treating anonymous objects as "objects without names"** — They DO have temporary heap addresses; they're just not bound to any reference variable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before exam.**

- **Declaration = 2 steps**: (1) `ClassName refVar;` (2) `refVar = new ClassName(args);`
- **Reference lives on the stack**; **object lives on the heap**.
- **`new` performs FOUR actions**: allocate → default-initialize → call constructor → return address.
- **Default field values**: int→0, double→0.0, boolean→false, char→'\u0000', reference→null.
- **Anonymous objects** = `new ClassName().method();` — eligible for GC right after the statement ends.
- **Reference reassignment** (`a = b;`) does NOT copy the object — it copies the address.
- **Garbage collection** in Java is automatic; eligibility = zero live references.
- **Static fields** are NOT part of object declaration; they belong to the class in the **Method Area**.
- **Array of objects** = `ClassName[] arr = new ClassName[n];` — array itself is on heap, but elements are `null` until individually initialized.
- **Java is pass-by-value**, where the value for object parameters is a reference copy.
- **Order of execution during `new`**: Class load → Heap allocation → Default init → Field initializers → Initializer blocks → Constructor body.
- **`final` reference variable** can be assigned only once; the object it points to can still be mutated.
- **Object header size** (64-bit HotSpot) = **12 bytes**; object size is rounded up to 8-byte alignment.
- **Multiple references to one object** = aliasing; modifications through any reference affect all.

---

**End of Notes — Declaring Objects (Module 2)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Object Reference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Reference:** In Object-Oriented Programming (specifically Java, the prescribed language for PEEET419), an *object reference* is a memory address (a pointer managed by the JVM) that identifies the location of an instantiated object on the **heap memory**. A reference variable declared in source code does **not** store the object itself; it stores a *handle* (a typed pointer) to the object residing in the dynamically allocated heap area.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an object reference is the conduit through which an object is accessed, manipulated, and passed across method boundaries. It is the embodiment of the OOP principle of **identity** — every object has a unique identity distinct from its state or behavior.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The House Key**
> Imagine a *house* (the object) located somewhere in a vast city (the heap memory). You, as the owner, do not carry the house with you. Instead, you carry a **house key** (the object reference) that uniquely identifies *which* house is yours. Many people can hold copies of the same key — they all refer to the *same* house. If you lose the key (reference goes out of scope or is set to `null`), the house still exists until the city demolishes it (garbage collection).

**Key mapping to Java terms:**
- **House** → Object (on the heap)
- **Key** → Reference variable (on the stack)
- **City** → Heap memory
- **Demolition crew** → Garbage Collector (GC)

---

### 1.3 Core Characteristics of an Object Reference

| Property | Description | Default Value |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Type Safety** | A reference is bound to a specific class type at compile-time | N/A |
| **Memory Location** | Reference variable lives on the **stack**; object lives on the **heap** | N/A |
| **Nullability** | A reference can point to *no object* | `null` |
| **Reassignability** | A reference variable can be re-pointed to another object (if non-`final`) | N/A |
| **Size** | Typically **4 bytes** (32-bit JVM) or **8 bytes** (64-bit JVM with compressed oops) | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Be prepared to answer — *"Where is the object created and where is the reference stored?"* The answer is always: **Object → Heap, Reference → Stack.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Primitive Variables vs. Object Reference Variables

This distinction is the **single most tested concept** in Module 2 of PEEET419.

| Feature | Primitive Variable | Object Reference Variable |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Stores** | Actual value (e.g., `int x = 10;` stores `10`) | Memory address of an object |
| **Memory** | Stack (local) / Heap (if instance variable of an object) | Stack (local reference) → Heap (object) |
| **Default Value** | `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` |
| **Size** | Fixed (e.g., `int` = 4 bytes, `double` = 8 bytes) | 4 or 8 bytes (just the address) |
| **Operations** | Arithmetic, logical | Method invocation, `instanceof`, casting |
| **Equality** | Value-based (`==`) | `==` compares addresses; `.equals()` compares content |
| **Pass to method** | Pass-by-value of the primitive | Pass-by-value of the reference (the copy of the address) |

---

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Object References

**Why do we need references instead of direct objects?**
1. **Efficiency** — Moving a 4-byte address is far cheaper than copying a large object's state.
2. **Shared state** — Multiple references can point to the same object, enabling collaboration (e.g., a `Player` object shared by UI and game logic).
3. **Dynamic memory management** — The GC can reclaim objects when no reference points to them.

**How does a reference work in Java (step-by-step):**
- **Step 1:** Declaration allocates a slot on the stack (e.g., `Student s;`).
- **Step 2:** Instantiation (`new Student()`) invokes the class constructor on the heap.
- **Step 3:** The JVM returns the heap address and stores it in the reference variable `s`.
- **Step 4:** Method calls on `s` (e.g., `s.study();`) dereference the pointer to reach the object.

---

### 2.3 Reference Assignment and Aliasing

```java
Student s1 = new Student("Alice", 85);
Student s2 = s1;        // s2 now points to the SAME object as s1
s2.setMarks(95);
System.out.println(s1.getMarks());  // Prints 95 (aliasing effect!)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing:** When two reference variables point to the same object, modifying the object through one reference is visible through the other. This is **aliasing** — a high-frequency KTU viva and exam question.

---

### 2.4 The `this` Reference

Every instance method receives an implicit parameter named **`this`** — a reference to the *current invoking object*. It is used to:
- Disambiguate instance variables from parameters: `this.name = name;`
- Pass the current object as an argument: `obj.register(this);`
- Return the current object for method chaining: `return this;`

---

### 2.5 Pass-by-Value Semantics (Java's Strict Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java is STRICTLY pass-by-value. There is no pass-by-reference in Java — even for objects.** When an object reference is passed to a method, a *copy of the reference* (the address) is passed. Both the original and the parameter point to the same object, so the object's *state* can be modified, but the original reference cannot be re-pointed to a new object inside the method.

**Consequence for the exam:** If the method parameter is reassigned, the caller's variable is **unaffected**.

---

### 2.6 Garbage Collection and Reference Eligibility

An object becomes **eligible for garbage collection** when **no live reference** points to it. Triggers in code:
- Reference set to `null`: `s = null;`
- Reference reassigned to another object
- Reference goes out of scope (local variable)
- Island of isolation (objects referencing each other but unreachable from any stack/root)

**KTU 2024 Highlight:** Know the `System.gc()` advisory call and the unreferenced nature of GC.

---

### 2.7 KTU Reference Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Table)

| Concept | Keyword / Symbol | Behavior |
|---------|------------------|----------|
| Reference type declaration | `ClassName refVar;` | Stack-allocated uninitialized reference |
| Object creation | `new ClassName(args)` | Heap allocation, constructor call, address returned |
| Null reference | `refVar = null;` | Points to no object; `refVar.method()` → `NullPointerException` |
| Reference assignment | `refVar2 = refVar1;` | Both point to same object (aliasing) |
| Reference comparison | `refVar1 == refVar2` | Compares addresses (identity), not content |
| Content comparison | `refVar1.equals(refVar2)` | Compares object state (override recommended) |
| Pass to method | `method(refVar)` | Pass-by-value of the address |
| Reassigning parameter | `param = new Object();` | Original caller's reference is NOT changed |
| `this` keyword | `this` | Implicit reference to current object |
| Garbage collection | Automatic via JVM | Frees heap memory of unreachable objects |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production systems (e.g., a banking application), object references underpin:
- **Database connection pooling** — A shared `Connection` object referenced by multiple worker threads.
- **GUI frameworks** — Event listeners receive references to UI components to update them.
- **Microservices** — A `RestTemplate` reference is reused across the application for HTTP calls.
- **Design Patterns** — Singleton, Observer, and Strategy all rely on shared object references.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix and Code Walkthroughs

This section provides exhaustive trace analyses of object reference behavior, which is the core evaluation style for KTU 14-mark questions.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Trace #1: Reference Reassignment and Object Creation

**Problem Code:**

```java
class Box {
    int size;
    Box(int s) { size = s; }
}

public class TestBox {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box(10);    // Line 1
        Box b2 = new Box(20);    // Line 2
        b1 = b2;                 // Line 3
        b2.size = 99;            // Line 4
        System.out.println(b1.size);   // Line 5
    }
}
```

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Line | Action | Heap State | Stack State | Result |
|------|--------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | `new Box(10)` creates Object A; `b1` points to A | Object A: `size=10` | `b1` → A | — |
| 2 | `new Box(20)` creates Object B; `b2` points to B | Object A: `size=10`, Object B: `size=20` | `b1` → A, `b2` → B | — |
| 3 | `b1 = b2` | Object A becomes **unreferenced** (GC eligible) | `b1` → B, `b2` → B | Object A eligible for GC |
| 4 | `b2.size = 99` | Object B's `size` changes | `b1` → B, `b2` → B | Object B: `size=99` |
| 5 | `System.out.println(b1.size)` | — | — | Prints **99** (aliasing) |

**Mathematical summary of the final state:**

$$
\text{memory}(b_1) = \text{memory}(b_2) = \text{address}(\text{Object B})
$$

$$
\text{Object B.size} = 99
$$

$$
\therefore \ b_1.\text{size} = 99
$$

**Conclusion:** After Line 3, both `b1` and `b2` alias Object B. Mutating through `b2` is visible through `b1`. Object A has lost all references and is eligible for garbage collection.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Trace #2: Pass-by-Value of References

**Problem Code:**

```java
class Student {
    int marks;
    Student(int m) { marks = m; }
}

public class TestPass {
    static void modify(Student s, int x) {
        s.marks = x;            // Statement A
        s = new Student(999);   // Statement B
        s.marks = 0;            // Statement C
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student stu = new Student(50);
        int bonus = 10;
        modify(stu, bonus);
        System.out.println(stu.marks);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

| Step | Action | Heap | Local `s` (in `modify`) | `stu` (in `main`) |
|------|--------|------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Pre | `stu = new Student(50)` | Object M: `marks=50` | — | `stu` → M |
| Call | `modify(stu, 10)` — copy of address passed | Object M: `marks=50` | `s` → M (copy) | `stu` → M |
| A | `s.marks = 10` | Object M: `marks=10` | `s` → M | `stu` → M |
| B | `s = new Student(999)` | New Object N: `marks=999` | `s` → N | `stu` → M (unchanged) |
| C | `s.marks = 0` | Object N: `marks=0` | `s` → N | `stu` → M |
| Print | `stu.marks` | Object M: `marks=10` | — | **Prints 10** |

**Logical deduction:**

$$
\text{After Statement A: } \text{Object M.marks} = 10
$$

$$
\text{After Statement B: } \text{local } s \rightarrow \text{Object N, but } stu \rightarrow \text{Object M}
$$

$$
\therefore \ stu.\text{marks} = 10
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board:** The original caller's reference (`stu`) was NOT re-pointed. Reassignment of the parameter `s` is a local operation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Trace #3: The `this` Reference in Action

**Problem Code:**

```java
class Counter {
    int count;
    Counter increment() {
        this.count = this.count + 1;
        return this;
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Count = " + this.count);
    }
}

public class TestThis {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Counter c = new Counter();
        c.increment().increment().increment().display();
    }
}
```

**Exhaustive step-by-step trace:**

| Call | `this` refers to | Effect on heap | `count` after |
|------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1st `.increment()` | Object C | C.count = 0+1 = 1 | 1 |
| 2nd `.increment()` | Object C (returned by 1st) | C.count = 1+1 = 2 | 2 |
| 3rd `.increment()` | Object C (returned by 2nd) | C.count = 2+1 = 3 | 3 |
| `.display()` | Object C | Prints "Count = 3" | 3 |

**Final output:** `Count = 3`

**Algebraic summary of chain invocation:**

$$
\text{count}_{final} = \text{count}_{initial} + n_{calls}
$$

$$
3 = 0 + 3
$$

This is method chaining enabled by `return this;` — a **classic KTU 2024 expected question**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Trace #4: Reference Comparison vs. Object Comparison

**Problem Code:**

```java
class Point {
    int x, y;
    Point(int a, int b) { x = a; y = b; }
}

public class TestCompare {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Point p1 = new Point(5, 10);
        Point p2 = new Point(5, 10);
        Point p3 = p1;

        System.out.println(p1 == p2);      // Output 1
        System.out.println(p1 == p3);      // Output 2
    }
}
```

**Trace matrix:**

| Expression | `p1` points to | `p2` points to | `p3` points to | Address equality | Output |
|------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|------------------|--------|
| `p1 == p2` | Object P1 | Object P2 | — | Address(P1) ≠ Address(P2) | **`false`** |
| `p1 == p3` | Object P1 | — | Object P1 (alias) | Address(P1) = Address(P1) | **`true`** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often answer `p1 == p2` as `true` because the *contents* match. **Wrong!** `==` always compares *references* (memory addresses) unless applied to primitives.

---

### 3.5 Comprehensive Reference Behavior Matrix

| Operation | Affects Original Object? | Affects Original Reference? | GC Triggered? |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|---------------|
| `s2 = s1` (aliasing) | No (no state change) | No | No |
| `s1.setX(5)` (mutate) | **Yes** | No | No |
| `s1 = null` | No | **Yes** (original `s1`) | **Yes** (if no other refs) |
| `s1 = newObj` | No | **Yes** (original `s1`) | **Yes** (if no other refs to old obj) |
| `method(s1)` (state change inside) | **Yes** | No | No |
| `method(s1)` (reassign inside) | No | **No** (caller unaffected) | No (caller's ref safe) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Stack-Heap Memory Architecture for Object References

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["STACK MEMORY - Method main"]
        A["ref1 : Student - Address 0xABC1"]
        B["ref2 : Student - Address 0xABC2"]
        C["ref3 : Student - Address 0xABC1"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY - Objects"]
        D["Object 1 - name=Alice, age=20 - 0xABC1"]
        E["Object 2 - name=Bob, age=22 - 0xABC2"]
        F["Object 3 - name=Charlie, age=25 - 0xABC3"]
    end

    A -.->|"points to"| D
    B -.->|"points to"| E
    C -.->|"alias of ref1"| D

    G["Garbage Collector"] -.->|"Frees unreachable objects"| F

    style STK fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    style HEAP fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C
    style E fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#D32F2F
    style F fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#616161,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style G fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF8F00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** `ref1` and `ref3` both point to Object 1 (aliasing). Object 3 has no incoming reference and is therefore marked as garbage by the Garbage Collector.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Object Reference Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Declaration - Student ref"] --> B["Step 2: ref points to null - default"]
    B --> C["Step 3: new Student - object created on heap"]
    C --> D["Step 4: ref holds address of new object"]
    D --> E{"Step 5: Is ref reassigned?"}
    E -->|"Yes - to new object"| F["Old object becomes GC eligible"]
    E -->|"No"| G["Object remains referenced and alive"]
    F --> H["JVM Garbage Collector frees memory"]
    G --> I["ref set to null - becomes GC eligible"]
    I --> H

    style A fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style B fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style C fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style E fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style F fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#D84315
    style G fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C
    style H fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#283593
    style I fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Pass-by-Value of Reference (Method Call)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main method
    participant Callee as modify method
    participant Heap as Heap Memory

    Main->>Main: stu = new Student(50)
    Note over Main,Heap: Object M created at 0xA1
    Main->>Callee: modify(stu, 10)
    Note over Callee: Local copy s = 0xA1 received
    Callee->>Heap: s.marks = 10 (mutates Object M)
    Note over Heap: Object M.marks = 10
    Callee->>Heap: s = new Student(999)
    Note over Callee: Local s now points to 0xB7
    Note over Main: stu still points to 0xA1 (unchanged)
    Callee-->>Main: return
    Main->>Heap: System.out.println(stu.marks)
    Note over Main: Prints 10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between a primitive variable and an object reference variable in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primitive Variable | Object Reference Variable |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Definition | Stores the actual data value directly | Stores the memory address (reference) of an object |
