| Memory | Allocated on stack (local) | Reference on stack; object on heap |
| Default value | `0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` |
| Example | `int age = 20;` | `Student s = new Student();` |

A primitive variable holds the **literal value**, while an object reference variable holds a **handle** to an object allocated on the heap. The reference itself is the size of a memory address (typically 4 or 8 bytes), regardless of the object's actual size.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
**What is aliasing in the context of object references? Write a small Java code snippet to demonstrate it.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which two or more reference variables refer to the **same object** in memory. As a result, modifications made to the object through one reference variable are visible through all other reference variables that alias the same object.

**Code snippet:**

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    Box(int l) { length = l; }
}

public class AliasingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box(10);
        Box b2 = b1;          // b2 aliases b1
        b2.length = 50;
        System.out.println(b1.length);   // Prints 50, not 10
    }
}
```

**Output:** `50`

**Explanation:** Both `b1` and `b2` point to the **same Box object**. When `b2.length` is changed, the change is reflected when accessed via `b1.length` because they are the same object — this is aliasing.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of object reference in Java. With suitable diagrams, describe how an object is allocated on the heap and accessed via a reference variable on the stack.**

**Model Answer:**

An **object reference** in Java is a typed handle that holds the memory address of an object stored on the heap. When a class is instantiated using the `new` keyword, the JVM:
1. Allocates memory on the **heap** for the object.
2. Invokes the class constructor to initialize the object's state.
3. Returns the starting address of the allocated memory.
4. Stores this address in the **reference variable** declared on the stack.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
STACK (main method)              HEAP MEMORY
┌─────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────┐
│ Student ref         │ ───────► │ [name="Karthik"]     │
│ Address: 0x4A2F     │          │ [rollNo=42]          │
└─────────────────────┘          │ [marks=88]           │
                                 └──────────────────────┘
                                   (Object at 0x4A2F)
```

**Key points:**
- The reference `ref` occupies only 4–8 bytes (the address), regardless of the object's size.
- Accessing `ref.name` involves: dereference the pointer → reach heap → read field.
- If `ref` is set to `null`, the reference no longer points to any heap object.

**Code example:**

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    Student(String n, int r) { name = n; rollNo = r; }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student ref = new Student("Karthik", 42);
        System.out.println(ref.name);  // Access via reference
    }
}
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain with a code example how Java passes object references to methods. Justify why Java is called strictly "pass-by-value" even for objects.**

**Model Answer:**

When an object reference is passed to a method, Java creates a **copy of the reference (the address)** and passes that copy to the method parameter. The original reference and the parameter both point to the **same object**, so the object's state can be modified. However, reassigning the parameter inside the method does not affect the caller's original reference.

**Code example:**

```java
class Account {
    double balance;
    Account(double b) { balance = b; }
}

public class BankTest {
    static void update(Account acc, double amt) {
        acc.balance = acc.balance + amt;   // (A) Modifies shared object
        acc = new Account(0);              // (B) Reassigns local copy only
        acc.balance = 9999;                // (C) Affects new object only
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account myAcc = new Account(1000);
        update(myAcc, 500);
        System.out.println(myAcc.balance);  // Output: 1500
    }
}
```

**Trace:**
- (A): `acc` and `myAcc` both point to the same object → balance becomes 1500.
- (B): `acc` is re-pointed to a new object. `myAcc` is **unaffected** — still points to original.
- (C): Only the new object's balance becomes 9999.
- Final output: `myAcc.balance` = **1500**.

**Why pass-by-value:**
- The method receives a **copy of the address**, not the original reference variable.
- Reassigning the parameter only changes the local copy, never the caller's variable.
- This is the textbook definition of pass-by-value applied to references.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "Java is pass-by-reference" — this is a **2-mark deduction** guaranteed.
> 2. **Always** clarify that a *copy of the reference* is passed, not the reference itself.
> 3. Emphasize that *object state mutation* is visible to the caller, but *reference reassignment* is not.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2025, Expected Alternate]**

**(a) [7 Marks] What is the `this` reference in Java? Explain its uses with a suitable example. How does it differ from a regular object reference?**

**Model Answer:**

The **`this` reference** is an implicit, final reference variable that the JVM passes to every **non-static instance method** and **constructor**. It refers to the **current invoking object** — i.e., the object on which the method was called.

**Uses of `this` reference:**

1. **Disambiguating instance variables from parameters:**

```java
class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;
    Employee(int id, String name) {
        this.id = id;        // LHS is instance variable, RHS is parameter
        this.name = name;
    }
}
```

2. **Passing the current object as an argument:**

```java
class Team {
    void register(Player p) {
        p.joinTeam(this);    // 'this' is the current Team object
    }
}
```

3. **Returning the current object (method chaining):**

```java
class Builder {
    Builder setName(String n) { this.name = n; return this; }
    Builder setAge(int a) { this.age = a; return this; }
}

// Usage: new Builder().setName("X").setAge(20);
```

4. **Invoking one constructor from another (constructor chaining):**

```java
class Point {
    Point() { this(0, 0); }            // calls Point(int, int)
    Point(int x, int y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
}
```

**Difference from regular reference:**

| Feature | `this` reference | Regular reference |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Declaration | Implicit (provided by JVM) | Explicit (`Student s = new Student();`) |
| Reassignable | No (`final` implicitly) | Yes (unless `final`) |
| Scope | Current method/constructor | Block-level |
| Can it be `null`? | No | Yes |

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain garbage collection in Java. Under what circumstances does an object become eligible for garbage collection? Write a program to demonstrate at least two scenarios.**

**Model Answer:**

**Garbage Collection (GC)** is the automatic memory management process by which the JVM reclaims heap memory occupied by objects that are no longer reachable through any live reference. It eliminates the need for explicit `free()` or `delete()` calls.

**When an object becomes eligible for GC:**

1. **Reference is set to `null`:**
   ```java
   Student s = new Student();
   s = null;        // Original object is now unreachable
   ```

2. **Reference is reassigned:**
   ```java
   Student s1 = new Student();
   Student s2 = new Student();
   s1 = s2;         // Object originally held by s1 is now unreachable
   ```

3. **Reference goes out of scope:**
   ```java
   {
       Student s = new Student();   // 's' is local
   }
   // After this block, 's' is out of scope → object GC eligible
   ```

4. **Island of isolation:**
   ```java
   class Node { Node next; }
   Node a = new Node();
   Node b = new Node();
   a.next = b;
   b.next = a;
   a = null;
   b = null;
   // Both objects reference each other but are unreachable externally
   ```

**Demonstration program:**

```java
class Resource {
    int id;
    Resource(int i) { id = i; System.out.println("Created: " + id); }
    protected void finalize() {
        System.out.println("GC collected: " + id);
    }
}

public class GCDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Resource r1 = new Resource(1);
        r1 = null;                       // Scenario 1: null assignment
        Resource r2 = new Resource(2);
        Resource r3 = new Resource(3);
        r2 = r3;                         // Scenario 2: reassignment
        System.gc();                     // Request GC (advisory only)
        System.out.println("End of main");
    }
}
```

**Expected output (order may vary):**
```
Created: 1
Created: 2
Created: 3
End of main
GC collected: 1
GC collected: 2
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (GC Questions):**
> 1. Do NOT claim `System.gc()` *guarantees* GC — it is only an **advisory request**.
> 2. Do NOT confuse **eligibility** with **actual collection** — eligibility only means *the object can be collected when GC runs*.
> 3. Avoid stating exact GC algorithms (e.g., mark-and-sweep) unless specifically asked in Module 2.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Object Reference (Module 2, PEEET419):**

- ✅ **Definition:** A reference is a memory address (handle) to an object on the heap.
- ✅ **Memory layout:** Object → **Heap**; Reference variable → **Stack**.
- ✅ **Default value:** Object references default to `null`; primitives default to `0`/`false`.
- ✅ **`new` keyword:** Allocates heap memory, calls constructor, returns address.
- ✅ **Reference size:** 4 or 8 bytes — independent of object size.
- ✅ **Aliasing:** Multiple references to the same object → mutations visible across all.
- ✅ **Pass-by-value:** Java is **strictly** pass-by-value; object arguments pass a *copy of the address*.
- ✅ **State vs. reference mutation:** State changes via parameter are visible to caller; reference reassignment is not.
- ✅ **`this` reference:** Implicit, final, refers to the current invoking object.
- ✅ **`==` vs. `.equals()`:** `==` compares references (addresses); `.equals()` compares content (overridable).
- ✅ **NullPointerException:** Dereferencing a `null` reference throws this runtime exception.
- ✅ **Garbage Collection:** Triggered when an object has **zero live references**.
- ✅ **GC eligibility triggers:** `null` assignment, reassignment, scope exit, island of isolation.
- ✅ **`System.gc()`:** Advisory only — does NOT guarantee immediate collection.
- ✅ **Stack vs. Heap:** Local primitive variables and references live on the stack; objects live on the heap.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Mantra for KTU Viva:**
> *"References don't carry objects — they carry directions to objects."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Introduction to Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**, a **method** is a named, reusable block of statements (code) defined inside a class that performs a specific, well-defined task. Methods encapsulate behavior within objects, enabling **modularity**, **code reuse**, and **abstraction**. In Java, methods are the primary mechanism through which objects interact and modify their state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A method is a member of a class that combines the attributes (data/parameters) and the operations (logic) on that data into a single unit. It is the **behavioral counterpart** to a class's attributes (variables).

The general formal syntax of a method declaration in Java is:

$$
\underbrace{\text{access\_modifier}}_{\text{visibility}} \ \underbrace{\text{static\_keyword}}_{\text{optional}} \ \underbrace{\text{return\_type}}_{\text{mandatory}} \ \underbrace{\text{method\_name}}_{\text{identifier}} \ \underbrace{(\text{parameter\_list})}_{\text{input}} \ \underbrace{\{ \ \text{body} \ \}}_{\text{implementation}}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Vending Machine" 🍵

Imagine a **coffee vending machine**:

| Vending Machine Part | Java Method Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The machine itself | The **class/object** |
| The button label (e.g., "Espresso") | The **method name** |
| Coins you insert | The **arguments/parameters** |
| The internal brewing process | The **method body** |
| The coffee cup it dispenses | The **return value** |

You press a labeled button (call the method), optionally insert coins (pass arguments), and the machine does its internal work (executes the body) and gives you back a result (returns a value). You don't need to know *how* it brews the coffee — that's **abstraction**. You can press the same button thousands of times — that's **code reuse**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A method that does not return a value uses the keyword **`void`** as its return type. Methods may or may not take parameters, and may or may not return a value — yielding four logical combinations (the classic "method categories" question).

### 1.3 Why Methods Matter in OOP
- **Encapsulation of Behavior:** Operations on data live with the data.
- **Reusability:** Write once, call many times.
- **Maintainability:** Bugs are localized to specific methods.
- **Readability:** Self-documenting code through meaningful method names.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Method — The Six Core Components

A Java method declaration is composed of **six distinct parts**. Examiners frequently ask students to identify each.

| # | Component | Purpose | Example |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Access Modifier** | Controls visibility (visibility scope) | `public`, `private`, `protected`, default |
| 2 | **Non-Access Modifier** (optional) | Defines special properties | `static`, `final`, `abstract` |
| 3 | **Return Type** | Declares the data type of value returned | `int`, `double`, `String`, `void` |
| 4 | **Method Name** | Identifier used to invoke the method | `calculateArea` |
| 5 | **Parameter List** | Comma-separated typed input variables | `(double radius, int precision)` |
| 6 | **Method Body** | Block of statements enclosed in `{ }` | The actual logic |

### 2.2 Method Signature — The Uniqueness Key

The **method signature** in Java is uniquely defined by **two components only**:

$$
\text{Method Signature} = \text{Method Name} \; + \; \text{Parameter List (types and order)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Return type is NOT part of the method signature in Java.** Two methods cannot have the same name and identical parameter lists with different return types — this causes a compilation error. This is a classic KTU true/false or "identify the error" question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Return Statement** | `return value;` must match declared return type | Missing `return` in non-void methods → compile error |
| **Void Methods** | Return type is `void`; may or may not use `return;` (no value) | Cannot return a value from `void` |
| **Parameters vs Arguments** | Parameters = variables in declaration; Arguments = actual values passed at call | Often confused in exams — remember "parameters are placeholders" |
| **Pass-by-Value (Java Rule)** | Java **always** passes a **copy** of the value/reference | For primitives → copy of value; For objects → copy of reference |
| **Static Method** | Belongs to the class, called via `ClassName.method()` | Cannot use `this` keyword |
| **Instance Method** | Belongs to an object, requires object reference to call | Has access to `this` |
| **Overloading** | Same method name, different parameter lists (compile-time polymorphism) | Differ only by return type → **error** |
| **Recursion** | A method that calls itself | Must have a **base case** to terminate |

### 2.4 Access Modifiers — The Visibility Spectrum

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff. pkg) | Other Packages |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *default* (no keyword) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In large-scale software systems (e.g., banking, embedded controllers), access modifiers enforce the **Principle of Least Privilege** — only exposing what is absolutely necessary for the API contract, hiding internal implementation details.

### 2.5 Categories of Methods (Based on Parameters and Return)

| Type | Parameters | Returns Value | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Type 1** | No | No | `void greet()` |
| **Type 2** | No | Yes | `int getRandomNumber()` |
| **Type 3** | Yes | No | `void setName(String n)` |
| **Type 4** | Yes | Yes | `int add(int a, int b)` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Execution Trace

Since OOP is a **CS topic**, Java code walkthroughs replace algebraic derivations. The "derivation" here is the **execution trace** through the JVM call stack.

## 3.1 Worked Example: Method Declaration, Definition, and Invocation

Consider the following Java program. We will trace **every** execution step.

```java
class Calculator {
    // Type 4 method: takes 2 parameters, returns a value
    int add(int a, int b) {
        int sum = a + b;
        return sum;
    }
    
    // Type 2 method: no parameter, returns a value
    String getVersion() {
        return "Calc v1.0";
    }
    
    // Type 1 method: no parameter, no return
    void displayWelcome() {
        System.out.println("Welcome to Calculator!");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator c = new Calculator();   // Step 1: object creation
        c.displayWelcome();                  // Step 2: call Type 1
        int result = c.add(10, 20);         // Step 3: call Type 4
        System.out.println("Sum = " + result);
        System.out.println(c.getVersion()); // Step 4: call Type 2
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Object Instantiation**
- `new Calculator()` allocates memory on the **heap** for a `Calculator` object.
- The reference `c` is stored on the **stack** pointing to the heap object.

**Step 2: `c.displayWelcome()` Invocation**
- JVM pushes a new stack frame for `displayWelcome()`.
- No parameters are passed.
- Method executes `System.out.println(...)`.
- Stack frame is **popped** (method returns void → control returns to caller).

**Step 3: `c.add(10, 20)` Invocation**
- JVM pushes a stack frame for `add(int a, int b)`.
- `a` ← 10, `b` ← 20 (pass-by-value copies).
- `sum = 10 + 20 = 30` is computed.
- `return sum` → value 30 is returned to caller, popped from stack.
- Caller's local variable `result` ← 30.

**Step 4: `c.getVersion()` Invocation**
- JVM pushes stack frame for `getVersion()`.
- Returns the `String` literal `"Calc v1.0"`.
- Stack frame popped.

### Output Trace

$$
\text{Welcome to Calculator!} \rightarrow \text{Sum = 30} \rightarrow \text{Calc v1.0}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism)

```java
class Printer {
    void print(int n) {
        System.out.println("Integer: " + n);
    }
    
    void print(double d) {              // Overload 1: different parameter type
        System.out.println("Double: " + d);
    }
    
    void print(int n1, int n2) {        // Overload 2: different parameter count
        System.out.println("Two ints: " + n1 + ", " + n2);
    }
    
    void print(String s, int n) {       // Overload 3: different order
        System.out.println("String+int: " + s + n);
    }
    
    // int print(int x) { ... }          // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: same signature as print(int n)
}
```

### Binding Logic — Which `print()` Gets Called?

| Call Expression | Resolved Method | Binding Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `print(5)` | `print(int n)` | Exact match — int literal |
| `print(5.5)` | `print(double d)` | Exact match — double literal |
| `print(5, 10)` | `print(int n1, int n2)` | Match on arity |
| `print("Hi", 5)` | `print(String s, int n)` | Order-based resolution |
| `print(5.0f)` | `print(double d)` | float auto-promotes to double |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compiler Resolution Rule:** Java compiler chooses the **most specific** applicable method. If `print(int)` is called with a `short`, automatic widening to `int` occurs — this is **method promotion**, distinct from overloading.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Pass-by-Value Demonstration (A KTU Favorite!)

```java
class Swapper {
    void modify(int x) {
        x = x + 100;
        System.out.println("Inside method, x = " + x);
    }
}

public class TestSwap {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 5;
        Swapper s = new Swapper();
        s.modify(x);
        System.out.println("After method call, x = " + x);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state:} \quad & x_{main} = 5 \\
\text{Call } s.modify(x): \quad & \text{Copy is made: } x_{param} \leftarrow 5 \\
\text{Inside method:} \quad & x_{param} = 5 + 100 = 105 \\
\text{After return:} \quad & x_{param} \text{ is destroyed (out of scope)} \\
\text{Original } x_{main}: \quad & \text{Unchanged! } x_{main} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Output

$$
\text{Inside method, x = 105} \\
\text{After method call, x = 5}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Java is pass-by-reference." This is **FALSE**. Java is strictly **pass-by-value**. For object references, the *reference itself* is copied — the object it points to is shared. Modifying the object's internal state (via setters) IS visible, but reassigning the parameter to a new object is NOT visible to the caller.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Method Declaration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ACCESS MODIFIER\npublic / private / protected / default] --> B[NON ACCESS MODIFIER\nstatic / final / abstract - optional]
    B --> C[RETURN TYPE\nvoid / int / double / String / class]
    C --> D[METHOD NAME\nvalid identifier - camelCase]
    D --> E[PARAMETER LIST\ntype1 name1, type2 name2 ...]
    E --> F[METHOD BODY\n{ statements }]
    F --> G[RETURN STATEMENT\nmandatory if non void]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Method Invocation Lifecycle (JVM Call Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Caller invokes method]) --> P1[Push new Stack Frame onto JVM Stack]
    P1 --> P2[Copy arguments into parameter variables]
    P2 --> P3[Execute method body line by line]
    P3 --> Q{Is return type void?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Pop Stack Frame]
    Q -- No --> S[Evaluate return expression]
    S --> T[Copy return value to caller]
    T --> R
    R --> End([Control returns to caller])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Four Method Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CatA[Type 1 No Param No Return]
        A1[void methodName]
        A1 --> A2[Statements execute]
        A2 --> A3[End - returns nothing]
    end
    
    subgraph CatB[Type 2 No Param Returns Value]
        B1[returnType methodName]
        B1 --> B2[Statements execute]
        B2 --> B3[return value]
    end
    
    subgraph CatC[Type 3 Param No Return]
        C1[void methodName params]
        C1 --> C2[Statements use parameters]
        C2 --> C3[End - returns nothing]
    end
    
    subgraph CatD[Type 4 Param Returns Value]
        D1[returnType methodName params]
        D1 --> D2[Statements process parameters]
        D2 --> D3[return computed value]
    end
```

## 4.4 Recursive Method — Call Stack Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[main calls factorial 3] --> B[factorial 3: n=3, n greater than 1, call factorial 2]
    B --> C[factorial 2: n=2, n greater than 1, call factorial 1]
    C --> D[factorial 1: BASE CASE, return 1]
    D --> E[factorial 2: returns 2 times 1 = 2]
    E --> F[factorial 3: returns 3 times 2 = 6]
    F --> G[main receives 6]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a method in Java. List and explain any four components of a method declaration.**

**Model Answer:**

A **method** in Java is a named block of code declared inside a class that performs a specific task and may return a value. It encapsulates behavior and promotes code reusability.

**Four Components of a Method Declaration:**

1. **Access Modifier:** Specifies the visibility of the method (e.g., `public`, `private`, `protected`). It controls which other classes can invoke the method.

2. **Return Type:** Declares the data type of the value the method will return (e.g., `int`, `double`, `String`). If no value is returned, the keyword `void` is used.

3. **Method Name:** A valid Java identifier following camelCase convention (e.g., `calculateTotal`). It is used to invoke the method.

4. **Parameter List:** A comma-separated list of typed input variables enclosed in parentheses (e.g., `(int a, int b)`). Parameters act as placeholders for values passed during the call.

*Example:*
```java
public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
```

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a method signature? Explain with an example why the return type is not part of the method signature in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

A **method signature** uniquely identifies a method for the Java compiler and consists of:
- The **method name**
- The **parameter list** (number, types, and order of parameters)

The **return type is NOT part of the method signature.** This is why Java does not allow two methods in the same class to differ only in return type.

**Example illustrating the rule:**
```java
class Demo {
    int show(int x) { return x; }       // Valid
    // double show(int x) { return x; } // ❌ COMPILE ERROR
}
```
The second method has the **same signature** (`show(int)`) but a different return type — this produces the error: *"method show(int) is already defined in class Demo"*.

The compiler cannot resolve ambiguity based solely on return type because the caller may ignore the returned value.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Part B questions carry internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]
**(a)** Explain the different categories of methods based on parameters and return type with suitable Java examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is method overloading? Write a Java program to demonstrate method overloading with three different `area()` methods (square, rectangle, circle). Show the output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Four Categories of Methods

| Category | Parameters | Returns | Syntax Pattern |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| Type 1 | No | No | `void methodName() { }` |
| Type 2 | No | Yes | `returnType methodName() { }` |
| Type 3 | Yes | No | `void methodName(params) { }` |
| Type 4 | Yes | Yes | `returnType methodName(params) { }` |

**Java Examples:**

```java
class MethodDemo {
    // Type 1
    void greet() {
        System.out.println("Hello!");
    }
    
    // Type 2
    String getDate() {
        return "2024-01-15";
    }
    
    // Type 3
    void setName(String name) {
        System.out.println("Name set to: " + name);
    }
    
    // Type 4
    int multiply(int a, int b) {
        return a * b;
    }
}
```

#### Part (b) — Method Overloading Program

**Definition:** Method overloading is defining multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (different number, types, or order of parameters) within the same class. It is an example of **compile-time (static) polymorphism**.

```java
class Geometry {
    // Overload 1: area of square
    double area(double side) {
        return side * side;
    }
    
    // Overload 2: area of rectangle
    double area(double length, double breadth) {
        return length * breadth;
    }
    
    // Overload 3: area of circle
    double area(int radius) {   // using int to show type-based overloading
        return 3.14159 * radius * radius;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Geometry g = new Geometry();
        System.out.println("Square area: " + g.area(5.0));
        System.out.println("Rectangle area: " + g.area(4.0, 6.0));
        System.out.println("Circle area: " + g.area(7));
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Square area: 25.0
Rectangle area: 24.0
Circle area: 153.93809
```

**Explanation of Binding:**
- `g.area(5.0)` → matches `area(double)` → 25.0
- `g.area(4.0, 6.0)` → matches `area(double, double)` → 24.0
- `g.area(7)` → matches `area(int)` → 153.93809 (using π ≈ 3.14159)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]
**(a)** Explain the concept of call by value in Java with a suitable example program. Justify that Java does not support call by reference. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **static methods** and **instance methods** in Java. Write a program demonstrating both. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Call by Value in Java

Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. When a method is invoked:
- For **primitive types** → a copy of the value is passed.
- For **object references** → a copy of the reference (address) is passed, not the object itself.

**Example Program:**

```java
class ValueDemo {
    void modifyPrimitive(int num) {
        num = num * 2;
        System.out.println("Inside method, num = " + num);
    }
    
    void modifyArray(int[] arr) {
        arr[0] = 999;
        System.out.println("Inside method, arr[0] = " + arr[0]);
    }
}

public class TestValue {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ValueDemo obj = new ValueDemo();
        
        // Case 1: Primitive
        int x = 50;
        obj.modifyPrimitive(x);
        System.out.println("After call, x = " + x);   // x is unchanged
        
        // Case 2: Array (Object reference)
        int[] numbers = {1, 2, 3};
        obj.modifyArray(numbers);
        System.out.println("After call, numbers[0] = " + numbers[0]);  // modified!
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Inside method, num = 100
After call, x = 50
Inside method, arr[0] = 999
After call, numbers[0] = 999
```

**Justification — Java is NOT Call-by-Reference:**
- In the primitive case, the original `x` remains **50**, proving no in-place modification occurred.
- In the array case, although the reference was copied, both the original and the copy point to the **same object on the heap**. Changes to the object's contents are visible, but if we had `arr = new int[]{0};` inside the method, the original reference would be **unaffected** — this confirms reference copying, not reference passing.

#### Part (b) — Static vs Instance Methods

| Feature | Static Method | Instance Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Keyword** | Declared with `static` | No `static` keyword |
| **Belongs to** | The **class** | An **object** (instance) |
| **Call syntax** | `ClassName.method()` | `objectRef.method()` |
| **`this` keyword** | Cannot use | Can use |
| **Access to instance vars** | Cannot directly access | Can access |
| **Memory allocation** | Once, at class loading | Per object creation |

**Demonstration Program:**

```java
class Student {
    String name;            // instance variable
    static String college = "KTU University";  // static variable
    
    // Instance method
    void setName(String n) {
        name = n;
    }
    
    // Instance method - can access both
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", College: " + college);
    }
    
    // Static method
    static void changeCollege(String c) {
        college = c;
        // System.out.println(name);  // ❌ ERROR: cannot access instance var
    }
}

public class TestStatic {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student.changeCollege("APJ KTU");  // call static without object
        
        Student s1 = new Student();
        s1.setName("Arun");
        s1.display();
        
        Student s2 = new Student();
        s2.setName("Meera");
        s2.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Name: Arun, College: APJ KTU
Name: Meera, College: APJ KTU
```

**Key Observation:** The static method `changeCollege()` modified `college` for **all instances** because static variables are shared. Instance methods like `setName()` operate on a **specific object's** data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing parameters with arguments** — parameters are in the *declaration*; arguments are in the *call*. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Stating "Java is pass-by-reference"** — this is **factually wrong** and may cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting base case in recursion** — always leads to `StackOverflowError`; examiner deducts for missing base case.
> 4. **Returning a value from a `void` method** — syntax error; –2 marks.
> 5. **Overloading methods differing only in return type** — does NOT compile; –2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to write the class header / `main` method** in program-based questions — partial marking allowed only if logic is correct; ensure boilerplate is included.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **method** encapsulates behavior inside a class; declared with `access_modifier return_type name(params) { body }`.
- ✅ A method **signature** = `method_name + parameter_list`; return type is **NOT** included.
- ✅ Java has **four method categories** based on whether they take parameters and/or return values.
- ✅ Java is **strictly pass-by-value** (primitives → value copy; objects → reference copy).
- ✅ **Static methods** belong to the class; **instance methods** belong to objects. Static methods **cannot use `this`** and cannot directly access instance variables.
- ✅ **Method overloading** = same name, different parameter lists; resolved at **compile time**.
- ✅ **Recursion** requires a **base case** to terminate; otherwise → `StackOverflowError`.
- ✅ Access modifiers (in increasing visibility): `private < default < protected < public`.
- ✅ Use **camelCase** for method names; class names use **PascalCase**.
- ✅ A method declared `void` may use `return;` (bare) to exit early but cannot return a value.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Constructors - Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard)**
> A **Constructor** is a special member function (or method) of a class that is **automatically invoked** at the time of object creation. It is used to **initialize** the data members of an object to meaningful initial values. A constructor shares the exact same name as its enclosing class, has **no return type** (not even `void`), and executes before any other operation on the newly allocated object.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Object Oriented Programming*, constructors are positioned as the **first line of defense for object lifecycle management**. They ensure that no object ever exists in an undefined or invalid state.

### The Three Mandatory Properties of Any Constructor

| # | Property | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Same name as class** | The identifier of the constructor must match the class name exactly (case-sensitive). |
| 2 | **No return type** | Unlike methods, constructors cannot return a value, so they are declared without any return type (not even `void`). |
| 3 | **Implicit invocation** | A constructor is triggered automatically by the `new` operator (Java) or implicit allocation (C++). The programmer does **not** call it explicitly. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy - The "Hospital Admission Form" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a class as a **"Hospital Ward"** blueprint, and an object as a **"Patient Bed"**. When a new patient is admitted (object created), the hospital **does not leave the bed empty** — it must fill in a mandatory **Admission Form** with critical info like Name, Age, Blood Group, and Allergies. That form is the **Constructor**. It runs the *moment* the patient is admitted and guarantees the bed has valid information before any treatment (method call) begins.

If the Admission Form is skipped, the hospital has a "blank patient" — dangerous and invalid. Similarly, an object without a properly executed constructor may hold garbage values, leading to unpredictable program behavior.

## 1.3 Why Constructors Matter in Real Engineering Software

In production engineering systems (such as embedded controllers, SCADA systems, or sensor-fusion modules), constructors are responsible for:

* **Hardware Pin Initialization** — Setting default logic levels on GPIO pins.
* **Default Calibration Values** — Loading factory calibration constants into IMU / ADC objects.
* **Safe Default State** — Ensuring a relay or actuator starts in a known "OFF" state to prevent hardware damage.
* **Resource Pre-allocation** — Reserving buffer sizes, communication channels, or memory pools before runtime operations begin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly tests the student's ability to *distinguish between multiple constructor types* and *apply constructor overloading* in problem statements. Mastery of this topic is a prerequisite for Module 3 topics like Inheritance and Polymorphism.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Constructors - Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Constructors (KTU Taxonomy)

Constructors are broadly classified into **four** canonical types in the KTU OOP syllabus:

### A. Default Constructor (No-Argument Constructor)

A constructor that takes **zero** parameters. If the programmer writes no constructor at all, the compiler auto-generates one — but with an **empty body**.

$$ C_{default} : \text{ClassName}() \;\; \{ \;\; \text{// empty or default initialization} \;\; \} $$

### B. Parameterized Constructor

A constructor that accepts one or more arguments, allowing the caller to supply initial values at the moment of instantiation.

$$ C_{param} : \text{ClassName}(T_1 \, a_1, T_2 \, a_2, \dots, T_n \, a_n) \;\; \{ \;\; \text{// assign a}_i \to \text{member}_i \;\; \} $$

### C. Copy Constructor

A constructor that takes a **single argument** of the same class type passed **by reference**, and creates a new object as a clone of the source object.

$$ C_{copy} : \text{ClassName}(\text{const ClassName} \, \& \, \text{source}) \;\; \{ \;\; \text{// copy all members from source} \;\; \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The parameter **MUST be a reference**. If it were passed by value, the compiler would need to call the copy constructor to pass the argument — creating infinite recursion and a **stack overflow**.

### D. Constructor Overloading

Defining **multiple constructors** within the same class, differentiated by their **parameter signature** (number, type, or order of parameters). This follows the same compile-time polymorphism rules as method overloading.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formal Rule / Signature | Notes & Constraints |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Default Constructor** | `ClassName()` | Auto-provided only if **no** constructor is explicitly defined. |
| **Parameterized Constructor** | `ClassName(T1 a, T2 b)` | Eliminates the auto default constructor. |
| **Copy Constructor** | `ClassName(const ClassName &obj)` | Pass-by-reference is mandatory. |
| **Constructor Chaining** | `this(args)` must be the **first statement** | Used to invoke another constructor from within a constructor. |
| **Destructor Symmetry** | `~ClassName()` | Called when object goes out of scope (C++ only). |
| **Constructor Name Rule** | Identical to class name | Case-sensitive match required. |
| **Return Type Rule** | **None allowed** | Writing `void` makes it a regular method, not a constructor. |
| **Access Specifier** | Can be `public`, `protected`, or `private` | Private constructors enforce Singleton pattern. |
| **Static Keyword** | **Cannot** be combined with a constructor | Constructors are inherently instance-bound. |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Constructor Type

* **Default Constructor** → Ensures an object is **always usable** the moment it is allocated. Used heavily in collections like `ArrayList`, `HashMap` entries.
* **Parameterized Constructor** → Eliminates the anti-pattern of creating a "blank object, then assigning fields". Reduces lines of code and enforces **mandatory field population** at construction time.
* **Copy Constructor** → Enables safe **pass-by-value semantics** and object duplication. Critical in C++ where the default shallow copy can cause double-free memory bugs.
* **Constructor Overloading** → Provides **flexibility** for different initialization scenarios (e.g., a `Point` class can be created with no args, 2D args, or 3D args).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

* **Embedded Systems (Arduino / STM32):** The `HardwareSerial` constructor initializes baud rate, TX/RX pins, and buffer sizes before any `begin()` call.
* **Database Drivers:** A `Connection` object's constructor establishes the socket, validates credentials, and sets the default transaction isolation level.
* **GUI Frameworks:** `JButton("Submit")` invokes a parameterized constructor that sets the label text in one step, while `JButton()` creates an unlabeled button for later configuration.
* **Robotics:** A `MotorController` constructor configures PWM frequency, direction pins, and stall-current thresholds — preventing the motor from being commanded before safe limits are set.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Constructors - Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: A `Student` Class with All Constructor Types

Below is the complete Java implementation that the KTU board expects a student to produce when asked to "demonstrate all types of constructors."

```java
class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private double cgpa;

    // (1) DEFAULT CONSTRUCTOR
    public Student() {
        this.name = "Not Assigned";
        this.rollNo = 0;
        this.cgpa = 0.0;
    }

    // (2) PARAMETERIZED CONSTRUCTOR
    public Student(String name, int rollNo, double cgpa) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.cgpa = cgpa;
    }

    // (3) COPY CONSTRUCTOR
    public Student(Student other) {
        this.name = other.name;
        this.rollNo = other.rollNo;
        this.cgpa = other.cgpa;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name 
                         + ", Roll: " + rollNo 
                         + ", CGPA: " + cgpa);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

Suppose the `main` method is:

```java
public static void main(String[] args) {
    Student s1 = new Student();
    Student s2 = new Student("Ananya", 47, 9.12);
    Student s3 = new Student(s2);

    s1.display();
    s2.display();
    s3.display();
}
```

**Step 1 →** `new Student()` invokes the **default constructor**. After execution:

$$ s1.\text{name} = \text{"Not Assigned"}, \;\; s1.\text{rollNo} = 0, \;\; s1.\text{cgpa} = 0.0 $$

**Step 2 →** `new Student("Ananya", 47, 9.12)` invokes the **parameterized constructor**. Java's overload resolution matches the call to the 3-argument signature:

$$ s2.\text{name} = \text{"Ananya"}, \;\; s2.\text{rollNo} = 47, \;\; s2.\text{cgpa} = 9.12 $$

**Step 3 →** `new Student(s2)` invokes the **copy constructor**. The reference `s2` is passed and its fields are duplicated bit-for-bit:

$$ s3.\text{name} = s2.\text{name}, \;\; s3.\text{rollNo} = s2.\text{rollNo}, \;\; s3.\text{cgpa} = s2.\text{cgpa} $$

### Final Output

$$ \text{Name: Not Assigned, Roll: 0, CGPA: 0.0} $$
$$ \text{Name: Ananya, Roll: 47, CGPA: 9.12} $$
$$ \text{Name: Ananya, Roll: 47, CGPA: 9.12} $$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Constructor vs Method

| Attribute | Constructor | Regular Method |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Name** | Same as class | Any valid identifier |
| **Return Type** | None | Required (or `void`) |
| **Invocation** | Implicit via `new` | Explicit by programmer |
| **Inheritance** | Not inherited | Inherited (subject to access) |
| **Polymorphism** | Overloaded (not overridden) | Both overloaded and overridden |
| **Purpose** | Object initialization | Behavior definition |
| **When Called** | Once per object lifetime | Any number of times |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Constructor Chaining Using `this()`

Constructor chaining allows one constructor to delegate to another, eliminating code duplication.

```java
class Box {
    int length, width, height;

    public Box() {
        this(1, 1, 1);              // chains to 3-arg constructor
    }

    public Box(int side) {
        this(side, side, side);     // chains to 3-arg constructor
    }

    public Box(int l, int w, int h) {
        this.length  = l;
        this.width   = w;
        this.height  = h;
    }
}
```

**Rule derivation:**

$$ \text{If } C_1() \text{ calls } C_2(\dots) \text{ via this(), then } C_2 \text{ is the only constructor} $$
$$ \text{that performs the actual field assignments, ensuring a single source of truth.} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** A `this(args)` call **MUST be the very first executable statement** inside a constructor body. Placing it later causes a **compile-time error** in Java.

## 3.4 Analytical Case: What Happens If No Constructor Is Defined?

Let $D_c$ denote the **default constructor availability** and $U_c$ denote the **user-defined constructor presence**.

$$ D_c = \begin{cases} \text{Auto-generated} & \text{if } U_c = 0 \\ \text{NOT auto-generated} & \text{if } U_c \geq 1 \end{cases} $$

**Implication:** If you define **only** a parameterized constructor, then `new MyClass()` will throw a **compile-time error** in Java, because the compiler refuses to provide a default constructor once any constructor is user-defined.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Constructors - Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructor Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Constructor Types] --> nodeB[Default Constructor]
    startA --> nodeC[Parameterized Constructor]
    startA --> nodeD[Copy Constructor]
    startA --> nodeE[Constructor Overloading]

    nodeB --> nodeF[Zero Parameters]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Auto provided if NO user constructor]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Initializes fields to default values]

    nodeC --> nodeI[One or more parameters]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Enables custom initialization at object creation]

    nodeD --> nodeK[Takes reference of same class]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Used for object duplication]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Must use reference parameter to avoid infinite recursion]

    nodeE --> nodeN[Multiple constructors in one class]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Differentiated by parameter signature]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Resolved at compile time static polymorphism]
```

## 4.2 Object Creation Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Program requests new MyClass] --> step2[Memory allocation on heap]
    step2 --> step3[Default field initialization to zero or null]
    step3 --> step4[Constructor matching by signature]
    step4 --> step5{Match found?}
    step5 -->|Yes| step6[Execute constructor body]
    step5 -->|No| step7[Compile time error no suitable constructor]
    step6 --> step8[Assign reference to variable]
    step8 --> step9[Object ready for method calls]
```

## 4.3 Constructor Chaining Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    caller[Main calls new Box] --> c1[Invokes Box no arg constructor]
    c1 --> chain1[Executes this 1 1 1 as first statement]
    chain1 --> c2[Invokes Box int int int constructor]
    c2 --> assign[Assigns length width height fields]
    assign --> ret[Returns to original call site]
```

## 4.4 Class Diagram Showing Constructor Overloading

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        -String name
        -int rollNo
        -double cgpa
        +Student()
        +Student(String n, int r, double c)
        +Student(Student other)
        +display() void
    }
    note for Student "Three constructors demonstrate overloading nand ncopy semantics"
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Constructors - Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: Define a constructor. List any two properties that differentiate a constructor from a regular member function.**

**Model Answer:**

A constructor is a **special member function** of a class that is automatically invoked when an object of that class is created. It is primarily used to initialize the data members of the object to valid initial values.

**Two distinguishing properties:**

1. **Name Identity:** A constructor has the **same name as the class**, whereas a regular member function can have any valid identifier name.

2. **No Return Type:** A constructor has **no return type** (not even `void`), while every regular member function must declare a return type (`void`, `int`, `float`, etc.).

*(Additional property for credit: A constructor is **called implicitly** at object creation time, whereas regular methods must be **called explicitly** by the programmer using the dot operator.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: What is a copy constructor? Why must its parameter always be passed by reference?**

**Model Answer:**

A **copy constructor** is a constructor that creates a new object as a copy of an existing object of the same class. Its signature typically takes a single argument of the same class type.

**Reason for pass-by-reference:**

The parameter must be passed by reference (not by value) because if it were passed by value, the compiler would need to **invoke the copy constructor itself** to create the local copy of the argument. This would trigger an infinite recursive chain of copy constructor calls, ultimately resulting in a **stack overflow** at runtime.

In formal notation:

$$ C_{copy}(X \, x) \Rightarrow \text{infinite recursion} \quad ; \quad C_{copy}(\text{const X} \, \& \, x) \Rightarrow \text{safe single call} $$

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) - Internal Choice Format

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`**

**Q: Explain the different types of constructors in Java with a suitable example. Also write a program to demonstrate constructor overloading.**

#### Part (a) - 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of constructors with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

In Java (and OOP in general), constructors are classified into the following types:

**(i) Default Constructor:**
A constructor that takes no arguments. If the programmer does not write any constructor, the compiler automatically provides one with an empty body.

```java
class Demo {
    int x;
    public Demo() {       // default constructor
        x = 100;
    }
}
```

**(ii) Parameterized Constructor:**
A constructor that accepts one or more parameters to initialize object fields with custom values supplied by the caller at the time of object creation.

```java
public Demo(int value) {
    x = value;
}
```

**(iii) Copy Constructor:**
A constructor that takes another object of the same class as a parameter and copies its field values into the new object.

```java
public Demo(Demo other) {
    this.x = other.x;
}
```

**(iv) Constructor Overloading:**
Defining multiple constructors within the same class with different parameter lists (different number, type, or order of parameters). This is a form of compile-time polymorphism.

#### Part (b) - 7 Marks
**Write a Java program demonstrating constructor overloading for a `Rectangle` class.**

**Model Answer:**

```java
class Rectangle {
    double length, breadth;

    // (1) Default constructor
    public Rectangle() {
        this.length = 1.0;
        this.breadth = 1.0;
    }

    // (2) Parameterized constructor (2 args)
    public Rectangle(double length, double breadth) {
        this.length = length;
        this.breadth = breadth;
    }

    // (3) Square convenience constructor
    public Rectangle(double side) {
        this.length = side;
        this.breadth = side;
    }

    public double area() {
        return this.length * this.breadth;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(5.0, 3.0);
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle(4.0);

        System.out.println("Area r1 = " + r1.area());
        System.out.println("Area r2 = " + r2.area());
        System.out.println("Area r3 = " + r3.area());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$ \text{Area r1} = 1.0, \quad \text{Area r2} = 15.0, \quad \text{Area r3} = 16.0 $$

**Explanation:** Three different constructors are invoked based on the arguments passed during `new Rectangle(...)`. The compiler selects the correct constructor at **compile time** using the parameter signature — this is **static (compile-time) polymorphism**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`**

**Q: What is constructor chaining? Explain with a Java program using the `this()` keyword. Also discuss what happens when no constructor is defined in a class.**

#### Part (a) - 7 Marks
**Explain constructor chaining using `this()`.**

**Model Answer:**

**Constructor chaining** is the process of calling one constructor from another constructor within the same class. In Java, this is achieved using the `this()` keyword, which must be the **first statement** in the calling constructor's body.

**Program demonstrating chaining:**

```java
class Employee {
    private String name;
    private int id;
    private double salary;

    // Constructor 1: Default
    public Employee() {
        this("Unknown", 0, 0.0);        // chains to Constructor 3
    }

    // Constructor 2: Partial
    public Employee(String name, int id) {
        this(name, id, 25000.0);         // chains to Constructor 3
    }

    // Constructor 3: Master / Terminal constructor
    public Employee(String name, int id, double salary) {
        this.name   = name;
        this.id     = id;
        this.salary = salary;
    }

    public void show() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name 
                         + ", ID: " + id 
                         + ", Salary: " + salary);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee();
        Employee e2 = new Employee("Rahul", 101);
        Employee e3 = new Employee("Priya", 102, 55000.0);

        e1.show();
        e2.show();
        e3.show();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$ \text{Name: Unknown, ID: 0, Salary: 0.0} $$
$$ \text{Name: Rahul, ID: 101, Salary: 25000.0} $$
$$ \text{Name: Priya, ID: 102, Salary: 55000.0} $$

**Key Insight:** All field assignments happen **only** in the master (3-argument) constructor. The other constructors merely **delegate** to it, ensuring a **single point of initialization logic** and eliminating code duplication.

#### Part (b) - 7 Marks
**Discuss the behavior when no constructor is defined.**

**Model Answer:**

When a class in Java contains **no user-defined constructor**, the Java compiler **automatically inserts** a public no-argument default constructor with an empty body. This is sometimes called the **implicit default constructor** or **synthesized constructor**.

**Formal rule:**

$$ \text{If } U_c = 0 \implies D_{auto} = \text{ClassName}() \; \{ \; \text{empty body} \; \} $$

**Consequences:**

1. **Object instantiation works seamlessly:** `new MyClass()` is valid because the auto default constructor is provided.
2. **Fields receive Java's default values:** numeric fields become `0`, boolean fields become `false`, and object references become `null`.
3. **The moment you write ANY constructor**, the compiler **stops** providing the auto default constructor. This is a frequent source of compile-time errors in KTU exams:

```java
class Demo {
    public Demo(int x) {     // only parameterized defined
        // ...
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Demo d = new Demo();   // COMPILE ERROR: no suitable constructor found
    }
}
```

**Resolution:** Explicitly define `public Demo() {}` if you wish to retain the no-argument instantiation capability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall - Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Writing `void` before a constructor name** — Many students mistakenly write `public void Student()`. The examiner deducts **2 full marks** because the compiler treats it as a regular method, not a constructor.
> 2. **Forgetting `this.` in constructor overloading** — When parameter names match field names (e.g., `Student(String name)`), students often write `name = name;` which silently assigns the parameter to itself. Always use `this.name = name;`.
> 3. **Placing `this(args)` as the second statement** — Java enforces that constructor chaining calls must be the **very first statement**. Even an empty line before it triggers a compile-time error.
> 4. **Confusing "default constructor" with "no-arg constructor written by the user"** — A common viva question. Clarify: the *auto-generated* one is the *implicit default*; a *user-written* no-arg constructor is called an *explicit default constructor*.
> 5. **Forgetting `const` and `&` in copy constructor parameter** — In C++, omitting `const` works but is bad practice; omitting `&` causes infinite recursion. Both together is the gold standard.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

* A **constructor** is a special member function with the **same name as the class** and **no return type**.
* Constructors are **invoked automatically** when an object is created using the `new` keyword.
* The **four canonical types** are: Default, Parameterized, Copy, and Overloaded constructors.
* A **default constructor** takes no arguments; if absent in code, the compiler provides one automatically — but **only if no other constructor is defined**.
* A **parameterized constructor** accepts arguments for custom field initialization.
* A **copy constructor** takes `const ClassName &` (reference) as its single argument to safely duplicate objects.
* **Constructor overloading** allows multiple constructors in one class, resolved by the parameter signature at **compile time**.
* **Constructor chaining** via `this(args)` must be the **first statement** in the constructor body.
* Constructors **cannot be `static`, `final`, or `abstract`**, and they **cannot be inherited or overridden**.
* In KTU exam answers, always pair each constructor type with a **short code snippet** — definitions alone fetch only partial marks.
* The **golden viva line:** *"A constructor does not allocate memory — it initializes it. Memory is allocated by the `new` operator."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: this Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** this Keyword

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Java (the language mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PEEET419 – Object Oriented Programming**), the `this` keyword is a **reference variable** that refers to the **currently invoking object** of a class. It is an implicit, non-static instance reference automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and is available inside every **non-static** method, constructor, or instance initializer block of a class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall this verbatim for 2-mark questions):**
> "`this` is a final reference variable in Java, implicitly defined in every instance method and constructor, that points to the object on which the current method or constructor has been invoked."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a teacher inside a classroom. A student asks: *"Ma'am, who do I submit **my** assignment to?"* You (the teacher) point to yourself and say, **"Me."**

In this analogy:

| Real-world element | Java equivalent |
|---|---|
| The teacher (a person) | The object currently executing code |
| The word **"Me"** | The `this` keyword |
| Classroom context | The class definition |
| The student calling out | The method being executed |

So, `this` is simply a way for an object to say: *"I am referring to MYSELF."* When dozens of objects of the same class exist in memory, `this` disambiguates **which** object's instance members the code should access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "final"?** The variable `this` is declared `final` internally — you can never reassign it. Writing `this = newObject();` is a **compile-time error** in Java.

### 1.3 Where `this` is Available (Scope Boundaries)

| Location | Can `this` be used? |
|---|---|
| Instance (non-static) method | ✅ **Yes** |
| Constructor | ✅ **Yes** |
| Instance initializer block | ✅ **Yes** |
| Static method | ❌ **No** (no object context) |
| Static block | ❌ **No** |
| Local variable | ❌ **No** (compile error) |

---

## 1.4 Primary Usages of `this` (KTU-Mapped)

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus specifically tests **six (6)** major uses of the `this` keyword. Each one is a high-yield exam topic:

1. Referring to **current class instance variables** (resolving shadowing).
2. Invoking the **current class method** (implicit/explicit).
3. Invoking the **current class constructor** (constructor chaining).
4. Returning the **current object** from a method.
5. Passing the **current object as a method argument**.
6. Used as a **constructor parameter** to call another constructor.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' favourite phrasing:** *"Explain any FIVE uses of `this` keyword in Java with examples."* (10-mark / 14-mark slot). Memorize all six — the sixth gives you a bonus edge.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Use #1 — Differentiating Instance Variables from Local/Parameter Variables (Shadowing Resolution)

When a constructor or method parameter has the **same name** as an instance variable, the parameter **shadows** the instance variable. The keyword `this.fieldName` breaks the shadowing and explicitly points to the **object's own field**.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. JVM creates a brand-new object in heap memory.
2. The constructor `Student(int roll, String name)` is called.
3. Parameters `roll` and `name` are local stack variables.
4. Instance variables `this.roll` and `this.name` exist in the heap.
5. Assignment `roll = roll;` is **ambiguous** (no effect on instance variable).
6. Assignment `this.roll = roll;` **unambiguously** assigns the parameter to the object's field.

### 2.1.2 Reference Java Snippet (Model Program)

```java
class Student {
    int roll;
    String name;

    Student(int roll, String name) {       // parameters shadow fields
        this.roll = roll;                   // LHS = object's field, RHS = parameter
        this.name = name;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Roll: " + this.roll + ", Name: " + this.name);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Anand");
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Roll: 101, Name: Anand`

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing `roll = roll;` — this is a **no-op** (assigns parameter to itself). KTU examiners frequently plant a setter/getter program with this trap. You will lose **2 marks** for not using `this`.

---

## 2.2 Use #2 — Invoking Current Class Methods

You may call an instance method using `this.methodName()`, although the `this.` prefix is **optional** because the compiler inserts it automatically.

```java
class Demo {
    void m1() { System.out.println("m1"); this.m2(); }   // explicit
    void m2() { System.out.println("m2"); m3();       }   // implicit (compiler adds this.)
    void m3() { System.out.println("m3"); }
}
```

---

## 2.3 Use #3 — Constructor Chaining Using `this()` (High-Yield!)

`this(args)` is used inside a constructor to invoke **another overloaded constructor** of the **same class**. This is called **explicit constructor invocation**.

```java
class Box {
    int l, b, h;
    Box()                       { this(0, 0, 0);       }
    Box(int side)               { this(side, side, side); }
    Box(int l, int b, int h)    { this.l = l; this.b = b; this.h = h; }
}
```

### 2.3.1 Rules Mandated by the Compiler

| Rule | Constraint |
|---|---|
| R1 | `this()` **must** be the **first statement** in the constructor. |
| R2 | You **cannot** use `this()` inside a regular method. |
| R3 | Recursive constructor invocation is a **compile-time error**. |
| R4 | At least one constructor in the chain must **not** call `this()` (termination). |
| R5 | `this()` and `super()` **cannot** coexist in the same constructor. |

---

## 2.4 Use #4 — Returning the Current Object

A method can return `this` to enable **method chaining** (fluent interface style).

```java
class Builder {
    Builder setA(int a) { /*...*/ return this; }
    Builder setB(int b) { /*...*/ return this; }
}
// Usage:
new Builder().setA(10).setB(20);
```

---

## 2.5 Use #5 — Passing `this` as a Method Argument

```java
class A {
    void m1() { B b = new B(); b.process(this); }
}
class B {
    void process(A a) { System.out.println("Got: " + a); }
}
```

---

## 2.6 Use #6 — Using `this` as a Constructor Parameter

A constructor can accept `this` of another class only indirectly through references; more commonly, a class accepts its **own reference** for registration patterns (event listeners, observer design pattern).

```java
class Button {
    Button() { EventBus.register(this); }   // passes current object for callback
}
```

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula-Style Summary)

| # | Usage | Syntax Template | Mandatory Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instance variable resolution | `this.field = param;` | None |
| 2 | Method invocation | `this.method();` | Must be non-static context |
| 3 | Constructor chaining | `this(arg1, arg2);` | **First line** of constructor |
| 4 | Return current object | `return this;` | Method return type = ClassName |
| 5 | Pass as argument | `obj.method(this);` | Receiving method must accept ClassName |
| 6 | Constructor parameter | Implicit `this` available | `this` is a final reference |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of `this` |
|---|---|
| **Android Development (Java/Kotlin interop)** | Builder patterns for `AlertDialog`, `Retrofit` clients return `this` for chaining. |
| **Swing GUI Programming** | `addActionListener(this)` passes current frame to event dispatcher. |
| **Design Patterns** | Singleton's `getInstance()` sometimes compares `this == instance`. |
| **Spring / Hibernate Frameworks** | Self-injection (`@Autowired private Self self;`) to trigger proxies for transactional self-calls. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Analytical Matrix & Memory Model

## 3.1 Exhaustive Memory Model — How `this` is Wired

Let us trace, in a **board-exam-friendly** step-by-step manner, exactly what happens when an object invokes a method. We will use the equation-driven analytical style preferred by KTU valuer, Dr. Pillai (CSE Dept., Model Engineering College — *fictional reference for pedagogy*).

### 3.1.1 Notation & Assumptions

Let:
- $C$ = a class named `Student`.
- $O_i$ = the $i$-th object instantiated from $C$, for $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.
- $M$ = an instance method of $C$.
- $R$ = the implicit reference `this` injected by the JVM.
- $H$ = JVM heap memory region.
- $S$ = JVM stack memory region (per thread).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Class Loading**

When the class loader loads $C$, the JVM creates a **method area** entry containing the bytecode of $M$, but **no** `this` is created yet (no object exists).

**Step 2 — Object Instantiation**

When `new Student(...)` is executed:

$$H \;\leftarrow\; \text{allocate}(\text{sizeof}(C))$$

A new heap block is created. Its **starting address** is stored in the reference variable on the stack.

**Step 3 — Reference Binding (The Birth of `this`)**

When $M$ is invoked on $O_1$ via `O1.M()`, the JVM:

1. Pushes a new stack frame $F$ for $M$.
2. Inserts an **implicit local variable** named `this` as the **first slot** of $F$.
3. Initializes `this` with the address of $O_1$.

Mathematically:

$$\text{this}_F \;\equiv\; \text{address}(O_1), \quad \text{where } F \text{ is the stack frame of } M$$

**Step 4 — Field Access Resolution**

Whenever the bytecode `aload_0` (load reference at slot 0) is encountered, the JVM pushes `this` onto the operand stack. Every instance field access `field` in $M$ is internally rewritten as:

$$\text{this.field} \;\equiv\; \text{getfield}(\text{this}, \text{fieldName})$$

**Step 5 — Method Return / Frame Pop**

When $M$ returns, the frame $F$ is destroyed. The `this` reference **ceases to exist** for that invocation. If a *different* object $O_2$ later calls $M$, a brand-new `this` is created and bound to $O_2$.

### 3.1.3 Summary Equation

For any instance method invocation `obj.M()`:

$$\boxed{\;\text{this}_{\text{inside } M} \;\equiv\; \text{obj}\;}$$

The variable `this` is **syntactic sugar** for the receiver of the message — a concept rooted in Alan Kay's original Smalltalk OOP paradigm.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Resolving the Shadowing Equation

Consider the following general constructor signature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class } C: \quad & \text{instance fields } \{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n\} \\
& \text{constructor } C(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Inside the constructor body, we encounter the assignment `p_i = p_i;`. The compiler resolves this as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_i &= p_i \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{local} \leftarrow \text{local} \quad (\text{no-op}) \\[4pt]
\text{this.}p_i &= p_i \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{field} \leftarrow \text{param} \quad (\text{correct}) \\[4pt]
p_i &= \text{this.}p_i \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{param} \leftarrow \text{field} \quad (\text{reversed, usually wrong}) \\[4pt]
\text{this.}p_i &= \text{this.}p_i \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{field} \leftarrow \text{field} \quad (\text{no-op})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The compiler can detect cases 1 and 4 only if the variable is `final`; otherwise, it silently compiles but yields wrong semantics — this is a **favourite KTU 2-mark trick question**.

---

## 3.3 Constructor Chaining — Tree of Calls

When class $C$ has $k$ overloaded constructors $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k$, the `this()` calls form a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** rooted at the most-parameterized constructor. The compiler enforces:

$$
\exists \; C_j \in \{C_1, \ldots, C_k\} \; : \; \neg\;(\text{this}() \in C_j)
$$

**Worked Example — Cube Class:**

```java
class Cube {
    int side, volume;
    Cube()                { this(1);              }   // calls Cube(int)
    Cube(int s)           { this.side = s; compute(); }   // terminal
    void compute()        { this.volume = side * side * side; this.show(); } // chain methods
    void show()           { System.out.println(volume); }
    public static void main(String[] a) { new Cube(); }
}
```

**Trace Table:**

| Invocation | Statement Executed | Effect on `this` |
|---|---|---|
| `new Cube()` | `this(1)` invoked | `this` shifts to `Cube(int)` frame |
| Inside `Cube(int)` | field `side = 1` | sets object's `side` |
| `compute()` | computes `volume = 1` | `this` refers to same object throughout |
| `show()` | prints `1` | frame popped after return |

**Output:** `1`

---

## 3.4 Constructing a Bounded Cost Equation for Method Chaining

Suppose we have $m$ chained setter calls of the form `obj.setA().setB().setC() ... `. Each call returns `this` in $O(1)$ time. The total time complexity is:

$$
T(n) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{m} O(1) \;=\; O(m)
$$

This justifies why fluent builder APIs in production frameworks (e.g., `OkHttpClient.Builder()`) are considered **constant-amortized** in performance per call.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — `this` Memory Binding

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Stack Frame of main()"] --> B["Reference variable: s1"]
    A --> C["Reference variable: s2"]
    B --> D["Heap: Student Object 1"]
    C --> E["Heap: Student Object 2"]
    D --> F["this pointer (binding)"]
    E --> G["this pointer (binding)"]
    F --> H["Inside display() method"]
    G --> I["Inside display() method"]
    H --> J["this points to Student 1"]
    I --> K["this points to Student 2"]
```

**Caption:** When `s1.display()` is executed, the JVM creates a new stack frame with `this` bound to **Student Object 1**; when `s2.display()` is called later, a fresh frame's `this` binds to **Student Object 2**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constructor Chaining DAG

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Cube() no-arg"] -->|this int| A2["Cube(int s)"]
    A2 --> A3["compute() method"]
    A3 --> A4["show() method"]
    A2 --> B1["side assignment via this.side"]
    A3 --> B2["this.volume = side^3"]
    style A1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style A2 fill:#b0e0e6
    style A3 fill:#98fb98
    style A4 fill:#dda0dd
```

**Caption:** This Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) shows the flow of `this()` calls. The **terminal** constructor `Cube(int s)` performs the actual work; others delegate to it.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — `this` Usage Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["this Keyword - 6 Major Uses"]
    u1["Use 1: Resolve Shadowing"]
    u2["Use 2: Invoke Method"]
    u3["Use 3: Constructor Chaining"]
    u4["Use 4: Return Current Object"]
    u5["Use 5: Pass as Argument"]
    u6["Use 6: Constructor Parameter"]
    root --> u1
    root --> u2
    root --> u3
    root --> u4
    root --> u5
    root --> u6
    u1 --> e1["this.field = param"]
    u2 --> e2["this.method()"]
    u3 --> e3["this(args)"]
    u4 --> e4["return this"]
    u5 --> e5["obj.method(this)"]
    u6 --> e6["register(this)"]
    style root fill:#ffcc00
```

**Caption:** Taxonomy of the **6 KTU-prescribed** uses of the `this` keyword, mapped to canonical syntax.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — `this` Re-binding Across Method Calls

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main()
    participant Obj1 as Student s1
    participant Obj2 as Student s2
    participant Disp as display()
    Main ->> Obj1: new Student(101, Anand)
    Main ->> Obj1: s1.display()
    Note over Disp: this = s1 (address 0xAA)
    Disp -->> Main: returns void
    Main ->> Obj2: new Student(102, Balu)
    Main ->> Obj2: s2.display()
    Note over Disp: this = s2 (address 0xBB)
    Disp -->> Main: returns void
```

**Caption:** Each invocation of `display()` creates a **fresh** `this` bound to the calling object. The keyword `this` is **not** a global constant — it is per-invocation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is the `this` keyword in Java? Why is it not applicable inside a `static` method?

**Model Answer:**

> The `this` keyword in Java is an **implicit reference variable** that refers to the **current invoking object** of a class. It is automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine and passed as a hidden first parameter to every non-static method and constructor.
>
> It is **not applicable inside a `static` method** because static methods belong to the **class itself**, not to any specific object. Since there is no object context at the time of execution, no `this` reference can be bound. Attempting to use `this` inside a static method results in a **compile-time error**: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context."*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List any **four** uses of the `this` keyword in Java with one-line examples.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Use | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolving instance-variable shadowing | `this.roll = roll;` |
| 2 | Invoking current class method | `this.display();` |
| 3 | Constructor chaining | `this(0, 0);` *(must be first line)* |
| 4 | Returning the current object | `return this;` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) Part B Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice**. You are required to answer either **Question A** or **Question B**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the `this` keyword in Java. Discuss any **three** uses of `this` with suitable code snippets. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **constructor chaining**? Write a Java program to demonstrate constructor chaining using `this()` for a class `Rectangle` having three constructors: no-argument (default 1x1), single-argument (square), and three-argument (length, width, height treated as length × width). Compute and display the area. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Definition:** The `this` keyword is a final reference variable implicitly available in every non-static context, referring to the current object on which the method or constructor has been invoked.

**Three Uses with Code:**

```java
// USE 1: Resolving Shadowing
class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;
    Employee(int id, String name) {
        this.id = id;       // this.id = instance var
        this.name = name;
    }
}

// USE 2: Method Chaining via return this
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    Counter increment() {
        count++;
        return this;        // returns current object
    }
    void show() { System.out.println("Count = " + count); }
}
// Usage: new Counter().increment().increment().show();  // Count = 2

// USE 3: Constructor Chaining
class Point {
    int x, y;
    Point()              { this(0, 0);      }   // delegates
    Point(int x, int y)  { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
}
```

**Explanation (for 7 marks):**

- **3 marks** for crisp definition + memory model statement.
