- **4 marks** distributed across the three uses — 1.5 marks each for code + 0.3 for explanation per use.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Definition:** Constructor chaining is the technique of invoking one constructor from another constructor of the **same class** using the `this(args)` call. It promotes **code reusability** and ensures that the most-parameterized constructor (the *canonical* one) holds the initialization logic.

**Code:**

```java
class Rectangle {
    int length, width, area;

    // No-arg constructor → defaults to 1x1 square
    Rectangle() {
        this(1, 1);                      // chains to two-arg
    }

    // Single-arg constructor → square
    Rectangle(int side) {
        this(side, side);                // chains to two-arg
    }

    // Canonical two-arg constructor
    Rectangle(int length, int width) {
        this.length = length;
        this.width  = width;
        this.area   = this.length * this.width;   // USE OF this
        this.display();                          // USE OF this
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("L=" + length + " W=" + width + " Area=" + area);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(5);
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle(4, 6);
        r1.display();
        r2.display();
        r3.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
L=1 W=1 Area=1
L=5 W=5 Area=25
L=4 W=6 Area=24
```

**Mark Distribution:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Definition of constructor chaining | 2 |
| Correct use of `this(args)` as **first statement** | 2 |
| All three constructors correctly chained | 2 |
| `this` used inside canonical constructor (lines 17–18) | 1 |

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss with examples how the `this` keyword can be used to: (i) return the current object, (ii) pass the current object as an argument to another method, and (iii) invoke a current class method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Java class `Account` with fields `accountNo` (int), `holderName` (String), and `balance` (double). Demonstrate **constructor overloading** with three constructors and use the `this` keyword for shadowing resolution in each. Include a method `transfer(Account target, double amount)` that uses the current object (`this`) as the source of funds. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a)

**(i) Returning the current object — Method Chaining**

```java
class Pen {
    String color;
    Pen setColor(String c) { this.color = c; return this; }
    void write() { System.out.println("Writing in " + this.color); }
}
// new Pen().setColor("Blue").write();
```

**(ii) Passing `this` as argument**

```java
class Helper {
    void show(Account a) { System.out.println(a.holderName); }
}
class Account {
    String holderName;
    void callHelper() { new Helper().show(this); }   // 'this' = current Account
}
```

**(iii) Invoking current class method**

```java
class Logger {
    void log()    { System.out.println("Logged"); }
    void process(){ this.log(); log(); }   // both valid
}
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b)

```java
class Account {
    int accountNo;
    String holderName;
    double balance;

    // Constructor 1
    Account(int accountNo) {
        this(accountNo, "Unknown", 0.0);              // chain
    }

    // Constructor 2
    Account(int accountNo, String holderName) {
        this(accountNo, holderName, 0.0);             // chain
    }

    // Constructor 3 — canonical
    Account(int accountNo, String holderName, double balance) {
        this.accountNo = accountNo;        // shadowing resolution
        this.holderName = holderName;
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    // Uses 'this' as the source of transfer
    void transfer(Account target, double amount) {
        if (this.balance >= amount) {
            this.balance -= amount;       // current object = source
            target.balance += amount;
            System.out.println(this.holderName + " transferred " + amount
                               + " to " + target.holderName);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Insufficient funds in " + this.holderName);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a1 = new Account(101, "Anand", 5000);
        Account a2 = new Account(102, "Balu", 2000);
        a1.transfer(a2, 1500);
        System.out.println("A1 balance: " + a1.balance);
        System.out.println("A2 balance: " + a2.balance);
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Anand transferred 1500.0 to Balu
A1 balance: 3500.0
A2 balance: 3500.0
```

**Mark Distribution:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Three constructors with `this` chaining | 3 |
| Shadowing resolution in canonical constructor | 2 |
| `transfer()` correctly using `this` as source | 2 |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on `this` questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to write `this.field`** in the shadowed constructor → **−2 marks** (logic is wrong, output is wrong).
> 2. **Placing `this(args)` on the SECOND line** of a constructor → **−3 marks** (compile-time error, full marks lost in some strict valuers).
> 3. **Using `this` inside a `static main()`** → compile error, entire program fails.
> 4. **Confusing `this()` with `super()`** in interviews — remember, `this()` calls a constructor in the **same** class; `super()` calls the **parent's** constructor.
> 5. **Returning `this` from a method whose return type is `void`** → compile error.
> 6. **Writing `this = null;`** → compile error (`this` is `final`).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 `this` is an **implicit final reference** to the **current invoking object**.
- 🔑 Available in **non-static** methods, constructors, and instance initializer blocks **only**.
- 🔑 **Not available** in `static` methods, `static` blocks, or local inner classes of static methods.
- 🔑 **6 KTU-prescribed uses** — memorize all six verbatim.
- 🔑 When parameter name = field name, **`this.field = param`** is mandatory to avoid silent no-ops.
- 🔑 `this(args)` must be the **FIRST statement** of a constructor (compile-time rule).
- 🔑 `this()` and `super()` **cannot coexist** in the same constructor (only one first-statement is allowed).
- 🔑 Recursive `this()` chaining is a **compile-time error** — you must ensure the chain terminates.
- 🔑 Returning `this` enables **fluent/builder patterns** widely used in Android & Enterprise Java.
- 🔑 `this` is **per-invocation**, **not** per-class — each method call creates a fresh binding.
- 🔑 You **cannot reassign** `this` — it is `final` by JVM design.
- 🔑 In **lambda expressions** (Java 8+), `this` refers to the **enclosing class object**, not the lambda itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overloading. Inheritance - Super Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overloading. Inheritance - Super Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Method Overloading** is a mechanism in object-oriented programming that allows a class to declare **two or more methods** with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (varying in the number, type, or order of parameters) within the same class. It is also known as **Static Binding**, **Early Binding**, or **Compile-Time Polymorphism** because the compiler resolves the call at compile time based on the method signature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Signature in Java:** A method signature consists of the **method name** and the **parameter list** (number, type, and order of parameters). The return type is **NOT** part of the method signature for overloading purposes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **coffee vending machine** in your college canteen. The button labeled "Coffee" can produce:
- A small coffee (less water, less powder) → different in quantity
- A large coffee (more water, more powder) → different in quantity
- A black coffee vs. a coffee with sugar and milk → different in ingredients

The action "**Get Coffee**" is **one name**, but it has **multiple forms** based on *what* you ask for. Similarly, in method overloading, the name "calculate" can have multiple forms based on the inputs you pass.

### Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Method Signature:** Method name + parameter list (types, number, order).
> - **Static/Early Binding:** Compiler decides which method to invoke at compile time.
> - **Argument List:** The actual list of values passed during method invocation.
> - **Promotions in Overloading:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double` (widening conversions are automatically applied by the compiler if no exact match is found).

---

## 1.2 Inheritance – Super Class

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Inheritance** is an OOP mechanism by which one class (called the **subclass**, **derived class**, or **child class**) acquires the properties (fields) and behaviors (methods) of another class (called the **super class**, **base class**, or **parent class**). The keyword used in Java to refer to the immediate parent class is `super`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Super Class:** The class whose features are inherited is known as the **super class** (or base class / parent class). The class that inherits is called the **sub class** (or derived class / child class). In Java, the reserved keyword `super` is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **family tree**. A child inherits certain physical traits, last name, and family customs from the parents — but the child can also have their own unique qualities. Similarly, in inheritance, the subclass inherits reusable code from the superclass but can also add new features or override existing ones.

### Why `super` Keyword?

> [!NOTE]
> The `super` keyword in Java is used for three primary purposes:
> 1. **Calling the superclass constructor** → `super()` or `super(arguments)`.
> 2. **Accessing superclass methods** that have been overridden in the subclass → `super.methodName()`.
> 3. **Accessing superclass fields (data members)** that have been shadowed in the subclass → `super.fieldName`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Method Overloading – Rules and Logic

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Same Class Scope:** Overloading must occur within the **same class** (or be inherited into the subclass).
2. **Unique Signature:** Each overloaded method must have a unique parameter list differing in:
   - Number of parameters
   - Type of parameters
   - Order of parameters
3. **Return Type Irrelevance:** Changing only the return type **does NOT** constitute overloading — this causes a compile-time error.
4. **Access Modifier Freedom:** Overloaded methods can have any access modifier (public, private, protected, default).
5. **Exception Flexibility:** Overloaded methods can throw different checked or unchecked exceptions.
6. **Automatic Type Promotion:** If no exact match is found, the compiler promotes smaller types to larger types:
   - `byte → short → int → long → float → double`
   - `char → int`
7. **Var-Args (Java 5+):** A method can accept variable number of arguments using `...` (e.g., `int... x`). Var-args have **lowest priority** in overload resolution.

### High-Yield KTU Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Rule / Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Method Signature | `name + parameter list` | Return type NOT included |
| Overload by count | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(int a, int b, int c)` | Number of parameters differs |
| Overload by type | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)` | Data type differs |
| Overload by order | `display(int a, String b)` vs `display(String a, int b)` | Parameter order differs |
| Return type alone | ❌ NOT a valid overload | Compile-time error |
| Type promotion order | `byte → short → int → long → float → double` | Compiler auto-promotes |
| Var-args priority | Lowest in resolution | Exact match wins first |
| Binding time | Compile-time | Also called Static / Early Binding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Var-Args Caveat:** `display(int... a)` and `display(int a, int... b)` will cause ambiguity. Always place var-args as the **last parameter** if combined with fixed parameters.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering
Method overloading is heavily used in:
- **Math libraries** (e.g., `Math.max(int, int)`, `Math.max(double, double)`, `Math.max(float, float)`).
- **Console printing** (e.g., `System.out.println()` is overloaded for `int`, `float`, `String`, `Object`, `char[]`, etc.).
- **Constructor overloading** — initializing objects in different ways (covered later in Module 2).

---

## 2.2 Inheritance and Super Class – Rules and Logic

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Use `extends` keyword:** In Java, inheritance is declared using `class SubClass extends SuperClass`.
2. **Single Inheritance:** Java supports **single inheritance** for classes (a class can extend only one parent).
3. **Multilevel Inheritance:** A class can be a super class to another class and a sub class of yet another (A → B → C).
4. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** One super class can have multiple sub classes (A → B, A → C).
5. **Constructor Chaining:** When a subclass object is created, the superclass constructor is invoked **first** (implicitly or explicitly via `super()`).
6. **Default `super()` Call:** If the subclass constructor does not explicitly call `super()`, the compiler **automatically inserts** a call to the **no-argument constructor** of the super class.
7. **`super()` Must Be First Statement:** The call to the super class constructor must be the **first statement** in the subclass constructor.
8. **Method Overriding & `super`:** When a method is overridden, the subclass version hides the super class version. To invoke the hidden version, use `super.methodName()`.

### High-Yield KTU Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Syntax / Rule** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance Declaration | `class Sub extends Super` | Java uses `extends` keyword |
| Single Inheritance | One parent class only | Java does not support multiple inheritance for classes |
| `super()` call | `super();` or `super(args);` | Must be first line in constructor |
| Implicit `super()` | Auto-inserted by compiler | Calls no-arg constructor of parent |
| Access parent method | `super.methodName(args);` | Used for overridden methods |
| Access parent field | `super.fieldName;` | Used when field is shadowed |
| Constructor execution order | Parent → Child | Top-down in inheritance hierarchy |
| `this()` vs `super()` | Both must be first statement | Cannot be used together in same constructor |

### Real-World Utility in Engineering
Inheritance powers the **"IS-A"** relationship in software design:
- A `SavingsAccount` **IS-A** `BankAccount`.
- A `ElectricCar` **IS-A** `Car`.
- A `Square` **IS-A** `Rectangle` (in OOP modeling).
This enables **code reuse**, **polymorphic behavior**, and forms the backbone of design patterns like **Template Method**, **Factory**, and **Strategy**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method Overloading – Exhaustive Worked Example

### Java Program: Demonstrating All Forms of Overloading

```java
class Calculator {
    // Overload 1: Two int parameters
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // Overload 2: Three int parameters (differs in count)
    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }

    // Overload 3: Two double parameters (differs in type)
    double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // Overload 4: Order of parameters reversed
    void display(int a, String b) {
        System.out.println("int: " + a + ", String: " + b);
    }

    void display(String a, int b) {
        System.out.println("String: " + a + ", int: " + b);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator c = new Calculator();
        System.out.println(c.add(2, 3));          // calls add(int, int) -> 5
        System.out.println(c.add(1, 2, 3));       // calls add(int, int, int) -> 6
        System.out.println(c.add(2.5, 3.5));     // calls add(double, double) -> 6.0
        c.display(10, "Hello");                   // calls display(int, String)
        c.display("World", 20);                   // calls display(String, int)
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1:** `c.add(2, 3)` — Compiler matches **two `int` literals** with `add(int, int)`.
$$\text{Result} = 2 + 3 = 5$$

**Step 2:** `c.add(1, 2, 3)` — Compiler matches **three `int` literals** with `add(int, int, int)`.
$$\text{Result} = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6$$

**Step 3:** `c.add(2.5, 3.5)` — Compiler matches **two `double` literals** with `add(double, double)`.
$$\text{Result} = 2.5 + 3.5 = 6.0$$

**Step 4:** `c.display(10, "Hello")` — Exact match with `display(int, String)`.
$$\text{Output} = \text{"int: 10, String: Hello"}$$

**Step 5:** `c.display("World", 20)` — Exact match with `display(String, int)`.
$$\text{Output} = \text{"String: World, int: 20"}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix for Overload Resolution:**
>
> | Priority | Match Type | Description |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 (Highest) | Exact Match | Parameter types exactly match |
> | 2 | Widening Primitive Conversion | `int → long → float → double` |
> | 3 | Autoboxing / Unboxing | `int ↔ Integer` |
> | 4 (Lowest) | Var-Args | Variable argument match |
>
> The compiler applies these rules in strict order. If two methods are equally applicable at the same level, an **ambiguity error** occurs.

---

## 3.2 Inheritance & Super Class – Exhaustive Worked Example

### Java Program: Demonstrating `super` in All Three Contexts

```java
// Super class
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";

    Animal() {
        System.out.println("Animal constructor called");
    }

    Animal(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Animal parameterized constructor: " + name);
    }

    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

// Sub class
class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Dog (subclass field)";

    Dog() {
        super();  // calls Animal() - must be first statement
        System.out.println("Dog constructor called");
    }

    Dog(String name) {
        super(name);  // calls Animal(String)
        System.out.println("Dog constructor with: " + name);
    }

    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Subclass name field: " + name);          // this.name
        System.out.println("Superclass name field: " + super.name); // parent name
    }

    void callBothSounds() {
        sound();         // calls Dog's sound() (current class)
        super.sound();   // calls Animal's sound() (parent class)
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d1 = new Dog();
        System.out.println("---");
        Dog d2 = new Dog("Bruno");
        System.out.println("---");
        d2.display();
        System.out.println("---");
        d2.callBothSounds();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1:** `new Dog()` invokes the no-arg Dog constructor.

$$\text{Dog()} \rightarrow \text{super()} \rightarrow \text{Animal()}$$

**Step 1.1:** `Animal()` executes → prints `Animal constructor called`.

**Step 1.2:** Control returns to `Dog()` → prints `Dog constructor called`.

**Step 2:** `new Dog("Bruno")` invokes the parameterized Dog constructor.

$$\text{Dog("Bruno")} \rightarrow \text{super("Bruno")} \rightarrow \text{Animal("Bruno")}$$

**Step 2.1:** `Animal("Bruno")` sets `this.name = "Bruno"` and prints `Animal parameterized constructor: Bruno`.

**Step 2.2:** Control returns to `Dog("Bruno")` → prints `Dog constructor with: Bruno`.

**Step 3:** `d2.display()` demonstrates **field shadowing resolution**.

$$\text{this.name (Dog's field)} = \text{"Dog (subclass field)"}$$
$$\text{super.name (Animal's field)} = \text{"Bruno"}$$

**Step 4:** `d2.callBothSounds()` demonstrates **method overriding resolution**.

$$\text{sound()} \rightarrow \text{Dog's overridden version} = \text{"Dog barks"}$$
$$\text{super.sound()} \rightarrow \text{Animal's parent version} = \text{"Animal makes a sound"}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructor Chaining Rule:** In any inheritance hierarchy, when a subclass object is created, the constructor of the **root-most super class** is executed **first**, followed by descendants in order down to the **current class**. This is enforced by the implicit or explicit `super()` call at the start of every constructor.

### Analytical Matrix – `super` Keyword Usage

| **Usage** | **Syntax** | **When to Use** | **KTU Exam Tip** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Call parent constructor | `super();` or `super(args);` | Always as first statement in subclass constructor | If parent has no default constructor, must call explicitly |
| Access parent method | `super.methodName();` | When subclass has overridden the method | Common in overriding to extend parent behavior |
| Access parent field | `super.fieldName;` | When subclass field shadows parent field | Rare but important for disambiguation |
| `super` in static context | ❌ Not allowed | `super` refers to instance context | Compiler error if used in `static` method |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Method Overloading – Resolution Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Method Call Encountered] --> B{Exact Match Found?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Bind to Exact Match Method]
    B -- No --> D{Widening Primitive Conversion Possible?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply Widening and Bind]
    D -- No --> F{Autoboxing or Unboxing Possible?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Autoboxing or Unboxing and Bind]
    F -- No --> H{Var-Args Method Exists?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Bind to Var-Args Method]
    H -- No --> J[Compile Time Error: No Suitable Method]
```

## 4.2 Inheritance Hierarchy with `super` Reference Paths

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Animal - Super Class] --> B[Dog - Sub Class]
    A --> C[Cat - Sub Class]
    B --> D[Puppy - Sub Sub Class]

    A1[name: field in Animal]:::parentField
    A2[sound: method in Animal]:::parentMethod
    B1[name: field in Dog shadows parent]:::childField
    B2[sound: method in Dog overrides parent]:::childMethod
    D1[play: method in Puppy]:::grandChildMethod

    A --- A1
    A --- A2
    B --- B1
    B --- B2
    D --- D1

    classDef parentField fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000
    classDef parentMethod fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000
    classDef childField fill:#add8e6,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef childMethod fill:#add8e6,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef grandChildMethod fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Constructor Chaining – Execution Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main method
    participant DogCtor as Dog Constructor
    participant SuperCall as super call
    participant AnimalCtor as Animal Constructor
    participant ObjectCtor as Object Constructor

    Main->>DogCtor: new Dog
    activate DogCtor
    DogCtor->>SuperCall: super
    activate SuperCall
    SuperCall->>AnimalCtor: invoke Animal
    activate AnimalCtor
    AnimalCtor->>ObjectCtor: implicit super
    activate ObjectCtor
    ObjectCtor-->>AnimalCtor: return
    deactivate ObjectCtor
    AnimalCtor-->>SuperCall: return
    deactivate AnimalCtor
    SuperCall-->>DogCtor: return
    deactivate SuperCall
    DogCtor-->>Main: object ready
    deactivate DogCtor
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – `super` Keyword Roles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SubClass[Sub Class Context]
        A1[Constructor Body] --> A2[First Statement: super call]
        A2 --> A3[Remaining Statements: this usage]
        A4[Overridden Method] --> A5[super.methodName to invoke parent]
        A6[Shadowed Field Access] --> A7[super.fieldName to access parent]
    end

    subgraph ParentClass[Super Class Context]
        B1[Parent Constructor]
        B2[Parent Method Version]
        B3[Parent Field Version]
    end

    A2 -.calls.-> B1
    A5 -.invokes.-> B2
    A7 -.reads.-> B3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define method overloading. Write the rules that must be followed while overloading methods in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Method overloading is the process of defining **two or more methods with the same name** but **different parameter lists** within the same class. It is an example of **compile-time polymorphism**.

Rules: **(1 mark each, total 3 marks)**
1. Methods must have the **same name** but **different number, type, or order of parameters**.
2. **Return type alone cannot distinguish** overloaded methods — only the parameter list matters.
3. Overloaded methods can have **different access modifiers** and may throw **different exceptions**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the `super` keyword in Java? List any three uses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. **(1 mark)**

Three uses: **(2 marks – 1 each for first two, third for completion)**
1. **Calling the parent class constructor** → `super();` or `super(arguments);`
2. **Accessing the parent class method** that has been overridden → `super.methodName();`
3. **Accessing the parent class field** that has been shadowed in the subclass → `super.fieldName;`

---

## 📗 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) Explain method overloading in Java with a suitable example. Discuss type promotion in overloading. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Method overloading allows a class to have **multiple methods with the same name** but **different signatures** (parameter list). The compiler determines which method to invoke at **compile time** based on the arguments passed — this is also called **static binding** or **early binding**.

**Example Program (3 marks):**
```java
class OverloadDemo {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("int: " + a);
    }
    void show(double a) {
        System.out.println("double: " + a);
    }
    void show(int a, int b) {
        System.out.println("two ints: " + a + ", " + b);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        OverloadDemo obj = new OverloadDemo();
        obj.show(5);         // calls show(int)
        obj.show(5.5);       // calls show(double)
        obj.show(5, 10);     // calls show(int, int)
    }
}
```

**Type Promotion Explanation (2 marks):** If no exact match exists, the compiler applies **automatic type promotion** following this order:
$$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$
For example, if `show(byte b)` does not exist but `show(int a)` exists, calling `show((byte) 5)` will promote the byte to int.

---

**(b) Explain inheritance in Java with a suitable example. How is the `super` keyword used to call a superclass constructor? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Inheritance Definition (2 marks):** Inheritance is the OOP mechanism by which one class (**subclass**) acquires the properties and behaviors of another class (**super class**) using the `extends` keyword. It promotes **code reuse** and establishes an **"IS-A"** relationship.

**Example (3 marks):**
```java
class Vehicle {
    String brand = "Generic";
    Vehicle() { System.out.println("Vehicle created"); }
    void run() { System.out.println("Vehicle is running"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int wheels = 4;
    Car() {
        super();  // calls Vehicle() constructor
        System.out.println("Car created");
    }
    void run() {
        super.run();  // calls Vehicle's run()
        System.out.println("Car is running safely");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c = new Car();
        c.run();
    }
}
```

**`super()` for Constructor Call (2 marks):**
- `super()` invokes the **no-argument constructor** of the parent class.
- `super(arguments)` invokes the **parameterized constructor** of the parent class.
- The call must be the **first statement** in the subclass constructor.
- If not written explicitly, the compiler **automatically inserts** `super()` as the first line.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) What is method overloading? Explain the different ways to overload a method with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Method overloading is defining multiple methods in the same class with the **same name** but **different parameter lists**, resolved at **compile time**.

**Ways to Overload (6 marks – 2 each):**

**1. By changing the number of parameters:**
```java
int sum(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
int sum(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
```

**2. By changing the data type of parameters:**
```java
double sum(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
float sum(float a, float b) { return a + b; }
```

**3. By changing the order of parameters:**
```java
void info(String name, int id) { /* ... */ }
void info(int id, String name) { /* ... */ }
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **DO NOT** write that **changing the return type** is a valid way to overload — this causes a **compile-time error**.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** confuse **method overloading** with **method overriding** (overriding happens between super and sub classes, not within the same class).
> - ❌ **DO NOT** forget that the compiler applies **type promotion** if no exact match is found — this can change which method gets called.
> - ✅ **ALWAYS** show the **method signature rule** clearly: name + parameter list = signature.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of a super class in inheritance. With an example, illustrate how `super` is used to (i) call the parent constructor and (ii) access the overridden method. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Super Class Concept (2 marks):**
A **super class** (also called **base class** or **parent class**) is the class whose attributes and methods are inherited by another class. The class that inherits is the **sub class**. Java uses the `extends` keyword to establish this relationship. The `super` keyword in Java refers to the **immediate parent class object**.

**(i) Calling Parent Constructor (3 marks):**
```java
class Person {
    String name;
    Person(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Person constructor: " + name);
    }
}
class Student extends Person {
    int rollNo;
    Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        super(name);              // calls Person(String) constructor
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        System.out.println("Student constructor: " + rollNo);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student("Alice", 101);
    }
}
```
**Output:**
```
Person constructor: Alice
Student constructor: 101
```

**(ii) Accessing Overridden Method (2 marks):**
```java
class Shape {
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Shape"); }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    void draw() {
        super.draw();             // calls Shape's draw()
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();
        c.draw();
    }
}
```
**Output:**
```
Drawing Shape
Drawing Circle
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **DO NOT** place `super()` anywhere other than the **first statement** in the constructor — causes compile error.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** use `super` inside a `static` method or `static` block — `super` refers to an instance.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** forget that if the parent class has **only a parameterized constructor**, the compiler will NOT auto-insert `super()` and you will get a **compile error** unless you write it explicitly.
> - ✅ **ALWAYS** remember: constructor execution order in inheritance is **Parent → Child** (top-down).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Method overloading** = same name + different parameter list (number, type, or order).
- ✅ **Return type alone** does **NOT** constitute method overloading.
- ✅ Overloading is resolved at **compile time** (static/early binding).
- ✅ Java applies **automatic type promotion** when no exact match exists: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`.
- ✅ **Var-args** have the **lowest priority** in overload resolution.
- ✅ **Inheritance** uses the `extends` keyword in Java (supports **single**, **multilevel**, and **hierarchical** inheritance for classes).
- ✅ The **`super` keyword** has **three uses**: call parent constructor, access overridden method, access shadowed field.
- ✅ `super()` (or `super(args)`) **must be the first statement** in a subclass constructor.
- ✅ The compiler **automatically inserts** `super()` if no explicit call is written, but only for the **no-argument** parent constructor.
- ✅ **Constructor chaining** ensures the parent constructor runs **before** the child constructor (top-down execution).
- ✅ `super` **cannot be used in static contexts** (methods or blocks).
- ✅ Overloaded methods **can have different access modifiers and exception lists**.
- ✅ `this()` and `super()` **cannot be used together** in the same constructor — both demand to be the first statement.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for `super` Uses:** **"CAM"** → **C**onstructor invocation, **A**ccess overridden method, **M**ember (field) access.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sub Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub Class (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **Sub Class** (also called a **Derived Class**, **Child Class**, or **Extended Class**) is a class that **inherits** attributes (data members) and behaviors (member functions) from another class, known as the **Super Class** (Base Class / Parent Class). The subclass mechanism is the direct implementation of the **"IS-A" relationship** in Object-Oriented Programming, enabling **code reusability**, **extensibility**, and the construction of a logical class hierarchy.

Formally, if class `B` is a subclass of class `A`, we write:
$$ B \subseteq A $$
This means every object of type `B` is *logically* also an object of type `A`, inheriting its public and protected interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *Sub Class* appears in Module 2 under the broader theme of **Inheritance**. Students must distinguish between **subclassing** (a relationship between classes) and **subtyping** (a relationship between types/interfaces).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Family Tree"

Imagine a real-world **biological family**:

- Your **Parent** possesses certain traits — eye color, last name, family property, and skills.
- As a **Child (Sub Class)**, you automatically **inherit** these traits without rewriting them.
- However, you may also **add your own unique traits** (your degree, your own bank account) and in some cases **override** inherited behavior (e.g., you pronounce the family name differently).

| Biological Concept | OOP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Parent | Super Class / Base Class |
| Child | Sub Class / Derived Class |
| Inherited surname | Inherited data members |
| Inherited mannerisms | Inherited methods |
| Child's own achievements | New data members/methods added in subclass |
| Child behaves differently from parent | **Method Overriding** |

This **"Parent → Child"** mental model is the foundation of all subclass-based designs in C++, Java, C#, and Python.

---

## 1.3 Why Subclasses Exist — The Three Pillars

1. **Reusability** — Common code is written **once** in the super class and reused across all subclasses.
2. **Extensibility** — New features are added in subclasses **without modifying** (and thus risking) the original super class.
3. **Polymorphism** — Subclass objects can be treated as super class objects at runtime, enabling dynamic method dispatch.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Why do we use subclasses?"* — always answer with these **three keywords in this exact order**: **Reusability, Extensibility, Polymorphism**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inheritance Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

When a subclass `Derived` is created from a super class `Base`, the following sequence of logical operations occurs:

1. **Declaration of Inheritance** — The subclass explicitly names its super class using language-specific syntax (e.g., `:` in C++/Java, `extends` in Java, `class Derived(Base):` in Python).
2. **Memory Layout** — Each object of the subclass contains a *complete sub-object* of the super class as its prefix in memory. This is the **"Base-class sub-object"** model.
3. **Member Visibility Resolution** — The compiler enforces access rules:
   - `public` members of Base → accessible in Derived.
   - `protected` members of Base → accessible in Derived.
   - `private` members of Base → **NOT directly accessible** in Derived (but exist in memory).
4. **Constructor Chaining** — When a Derived object is constructed, the **Base constructor runs first**, then the Derived constructor body executes.
5. **Destructor Chaining (Reverse Order)** — When destroyed, the **Derived destructor runs first**, then the Base destructor (in C++).
6. **Method Binding** — By default, methods are **statically bound**. If a method is declared `virtual` (C++) or overridden with `@Override` (Java), it becomes **dynamically bound** (polymorphism).

---

## 2.2 Access Specifier Matrix (THE MOST ASKED KTU TABLE)

The following table governs **who can see what** in a subclass — this is among the highest-weightage topics in KTU exams.

| Base Class Member | Access in Derived (as `public` inheritance) | Access in Derived (as `protected` inheritance) | Access in Derived (as `private` inheritance) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `public` | `public` | `protected` | `private` |
| `protected` | `protected` | `protected` | `private` |
| `private` | **Not accessible** | **Not accessible** | **Not accessible** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The accessibility of `private` members is *never* promoted — they remain inaccessible directly from the subclass regardless of inheritance mode. The data **physically exists** in the derived object but can only be touched through `public`/`protected` accessors of the base class.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Relationship Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Inheritance | `class B : public A { }` | Most common form — linear hierarchy. |
| 2 | Multilevel Inheritance | `A → B → C` | Grandparent → Parent → Child chain. |
| 3 | Multiple Inheritance | `class C : public A, public B { }` | Mixin-style reuse; C++ only directly. |
| 4 | Hierarchical Inheritance | One Base, many Derived | Common framework design. |
| 5 | Hybrid Inheritance | Diamond pattern | Combine ≥2 of above. |
| 6 | IS-A Test | `SubClass` is a kind of `SuperClass` | Liskov Substitution Principle check. |
| 7 | HAS-A Test | `SubClass` contains a `SuperClass` object | Composition (NOT inheritance). |
| 8 | Constructor Order | `Base()` then `Derived()` | Stack-style initialization. |
| 9 | Destructor Order | `~Derived()` then `~Base()` | Stack-style cleanup. |
| 10 | Upcasting (Implicit) | `Base* p = new Derived();` | Polymorphic pointer/reference. |
| 11 | Downcasting (Explicit) | `Derived* d = (Derived*) basePtr;` | Unsafe; needs `dynamic_cast`. |
| 12 | Abstract Class | Contains ≥1 pure virtual function | Cannot be instantiated; force subclassing. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** In C++, a subclass inheriting from a class with **pure virtual functions** remains abstract unless **all** pure virtuals are overridden. Marks are lost when students assume the class becomes concrete automatically.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Subclass Use Case |
|---|---|
| **GUI Frameworks (Qt, JavaFX)** | `MyButton` extends `Button` extends `Control` — every widget is a subclass chain. |
| **Game Development** | `Enemy`, `Boss`, `Minion` all subclass `Character` to reuse `health`, `position`, `render()`. |
| **Embedded Systems** | `Sensor` super class with subclasses `TemperatureSensor`, `PressureSensor` — uniform interface, device-specific logic. |
| **Database ORMs** | Hibernate/JPA entities form inheritance trees: `Vehicle` → `Car`, `Truck`, `Bike`. |
| **Compiler Design** | AST node hierarchy: every node type subclasses the base `Node` class. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Memory Layout Derivation of a Subclass Object

Let us **mathematically derive** the memory footprint of a subclass object using the **Base-Subobject Model**.

### Assumptions
Let:
- $\text{sizeof(Base)} = B$ bytes (sum of base class data members + padding).
- $\text{sizeof(Derived-only data)} = D$ bytes (members declared only in subclass).
- The compiler adds no virtual table overhead for now (we will extend later).

### Step 1 — Construction of Derived Object
When `Derived d;` is executed, the compiler allocates a contiguous memory block.

### Step 2 — Layout Formula
The memory block is partitioned as:

$$
\text{Memory}(d) = \underbrace{\text{Base Subobject}}_{B \text{ bytes}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Derived Members}}_{D \text{ bytes}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{sizeof}(d) = B + D
$$

### Step 3 — Adding Virtual Dispatch (Polymorphism)
If the base class declares any `virtual` function, the compiler injects a hidden pointer `vptr` (typically **8 bytes on 64-bit systems**), pointing to a virtual method table (vtable).

$$
\text{sizeof}(d) = B + D + \text{sizeof}(vptr) = B + D + 8
$$

> The vptr is **inherited** — the subclass's vtable contains pointers to overridden methods if any.

### Step 4 — Multiple Inheritance Extension
If `Derived` inherits from both `Base1` and `Base2`:

$$
\text{sizeof}(d) = B_1 + B_2 + D + 8 + 8 = B_1 + B_2 + D + 16
$$

### Step 5 — General Formula (n base classes)

$$
\boxed{\;\text{sizeof}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i + D + n \cdot \text{sizeof}(vptr)\;}
$$

Where $B_i$ is the size of the $i^{\text{th}}$ base subobject, $D$ is the derived-only data, and the factor $n$ accounts for one vptr per polymorphic base.

---

## 3.2 Constructor/Destructor Execution Order — Full Derivation

Let the inheritance chain be: `A → B → C` (multilevel).

**Hypothesis:** Construction follows the chain top-down; destruction follows bottom-up.

### Step 1 — Construction Trace
When `C obj;` is invoked:

$$
\text{Execution Order} = \text{Constructor}(A) \rightarrow \text{Constructor}(B) \rightarrow \text{Constructor}(C)
$$

**Reasoning:** The compiler injects an implicit call to `A()` and `B()` at the start of `C::C()`. In C++ this can be explicit via the *member initializer list*:
```cpp
C::C() : B(), A() { /* body of C */ }
```
Order is dictated by **declaration order of base classes**, not by the order written in the initializer list.

### Step 2 — Destruction Trace
When `obj` goes out of scope:

$$
\text{Execution Order} = \text{Destructor}(C) \rightarrow \text{Destructor}(B) \rightarrow \text{Destructor}(A)
$$

**Reasoning:** This guarantees the *derived part* is dismantled before the *base part* it depends on — a strict LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) symmetry.

### Step 3 — General Formula for $n$-Level Chain

$$
\text{Construction} = \text{CTOR}(L_1) \to \text{CTOR}(L_2) \to \cdots \to \text{CTOR}(L_n)
$$

$$
\text{Destruction} = \text{DTOR}(L_n) \to \text{DTOR}(L_{n-1}) \to \cdots \to \text{DTOR}(L_1)
$$

Where $L_i$ is the class at inheritance depth $i$ (root = $L_1$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Subclass Capability Map

| Capability | Inherited as-is? | Overridable? | Hides Base Member? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Public data member | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (data not overridden) | ⚠️ Yes if redeclared |
| Protected data member | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠️ Yes if redeclared |
| Public method | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (`virtual`/`@Override`) | ⚠️ Yes (method hiding) |
| Protected method | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Yes |
| Private method | ❌ Not accessible | ❌ Cannot override | N/A |
| Static method | ✅ Yes (hidden) | ⚠️ Cannot override, can hide | ✅ Yes (hiding) |
| Constructor | ❌ Not inherited | N/A (each class defines its own) | N/A |
| Destructor | ❌ Not inherited | ✅ Yes (always virtual in base for polymorphism) | N/A |
| Friend function/class | ❌ Not inherited | N/A | N/A |
| Operator overload | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Method *overriding* (runtime polymorphism) is fundamentally different from method *hiding* (compile-time). Students frequently confuse the two — losing **2–3 marks** per instance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy — Single Inheritance

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
      +String name
      +int age
      +eat() void
      +sleep() void
      +makeSound() void
    }
    class Dog {
      +String breed
      +fetch() void
      +makeSound() void
      +wagTail() void
    }
    class Puppy {
      +String motherName
      +whine() void
    }
    Animal <|-- Dog
    Dog <|-- Puppy
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Hollow triangle `◁` denotes **inheritance** (generalization).
- `Dog` is a *direct* subclass of `Animal`.
- `Puppy` is a *transitive* subclass (multilevel chain: `Animal → Dog → Puppy`).

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Subclass Object in Memory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["Derived Object d in Memory (64-bit system)"]
        A1["vptr (8 bytes) — pointer to Derived vtable"]
        A2["Base Subobject of Animal (B bytes): name, age"]
        A3["Derived Subobject of Dog (D bytes): breed"]
        A4["Puppy-only data (P bytes): motherName"]
    end

    subgraph VTABLE["Derived vtable (read-only)"]
        T1["&makeSound() → Dog::makeSound()"]
        T2["&sleep()   → Animal::sleep()"]
        T3["&eat()     → Animal::eat()"]
    end

    A1 -. points to .-> VTABLE
    A2 --> A1
    A3 --> A2
    A4 --> A3
```

---

## 4.3 Lifecycle Flow — Object Construction & Destruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1([User code: Dog d]) --> S2[Compiler allocates sizeof d]
    S2 --> S3[Animal constructor runs]
    S3 --> S4[Dog constructor runs]
    S4 --> S5[d is ready to use]
    S5 --> S6([d goes out of scope])
    S6 --> S7[Dog destructor runs]
    S7 --> S8[Animal destructor runs]
    S8 --> S9([Memory released])
```

---

## 4.4 Access Visibility Flow in Inheritance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BASE["Base Class Members"]
    BASE --> P["public members"]
    BASE --> PR["protected members"]
    BASE --> PV["private members"]

    P --> P1["Visible in Derived"]
    PR --> PR1["Visible in Derived (protected inheritance promotes them to private)"]
    PV --> PV1["NOT directly visible in Derived — accessible only via public/protected getters"]

    P1 --> USE["Subclass code can use freely"]
    PR1 --> USE
    PV1 --> GET["Must call public/protected methods to read or write"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a subclass. How is it different from a sub-object?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **subclass** (also called a derived class) is a class that inherits attributes and behaviors from another class called the **super class** using an inheritance mechanism. It represents an **"IS-A"** relationship at the class level.

A **sub-object**, in contrast, refers to a **base-class instance embedded as a member** within a derived object (memory-level concept). The sub-object exists *inside* every instance of the subclass, occupying a contiguous region at the start of the derived object's memory layout.

| Subclass | Sub-object |
|---|---|
| Class-level concept | Object-level (memory) concept |
| Declared via inheritance syntax | Implicitly present in every instance |
| Establishes IS-A relationship | Establishes HAS-A (containment) at memory level |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sub-object definition, 1 mark for distinction.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is method overriding? How is it different from method overloading in the context of subclasses?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Method Overriding** occurs when a subclass provides a **new implementation** for a method already defined in its super class, using the same name, return type, and parameter list. It enables **runtime polymorphism** (dynamic dispatch).

**Method Overloading** occurs when **multiple methods share the same name** but differ in the **number or types of parameters**, within the *same* class (or in a subclass that adds a new version). It is resolved at **compile time** (static binding).

| Feature | Overriding | Overloading |
|---|---|---|
| Where | Across super–sub classes | Within the same class (or sibling) |
| Signature | Must be identical | Must differ in parameters |
| Binding time | Runtime (dynamic) | Compile time (static) |
| Polymorphism type | Runtime | Compile time |

*(1 mark overriding definition + example, 1 mark overloading definition, 1 mark comparison table.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1). **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of inheritance with suitable diagrams. Show how a subclass is declared in C++ and Java for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C++ program that defines a class `Shape` with a virtual function `area()`. Create two subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle` that override `area()`. Demonstrate runtime polymorphism using a base class pointer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Inheritance Types — Definitions and Diagrams:**

1. **Single Inheritance** — One base, one derived.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    A[Base A] <|-- B[Derived B]
    class A { +methodA() }
    class B { +methodB() }
```

2. **Multilevel Inheritance** — Chain of inheritance.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    A[A] <|-- B[B]
    B <|-- C[C]
```

3. **Multiple Inheritance** — One derived, many bases.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    A[A] <|-- C[C]
    B[B] <|-- C
```

4. **Hierarchical Inheritance** — One base, multiple derived.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    A[A] <|-- B[B]
    A <|-- D[D]
    A <|-- E[E]
```

5. **Hybrid Inheritance** — Combination of the above (e.g., diamond).

```mermaid
classDiagram
    A[A] <|-- B[B]
    A <|-- C[C]
    B <|-- D[D]
    C <|-- D
```

**C++ Declaration Syntax (1 mark):**
```cpp
class Derived : public Base1, public Base2 { /* ... */ };
```

**Java Declaration Syntax (1 mark):**
```java
class Derived extends Base1 { /* Java does not support multiple class inheritance */ }
class Derived extends Base1 implements IBase2 { /* Use interfaces for multiple inheritance */ }
```

> *(1 mark for listing the 5 types, 3 marks for diagrams, 1 mark for syntax each language.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**C++ Program Demonstrating Runtime Polymorphism via Subclassing:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Shape {
public:
    virtual double area() const {
        cout << "Base Shape area (undefined)\n";
        return 0.0;
    }
    virtual ~Shape() {}   // virtual destructor is mandatory
};

class Circle : public Shape {
    double r;
public:
    Circle(double radius) : r(radius) {}
    double area() const override {
        return 3.14159 * r * r;
    }
};

class Rectangle : public Shape {
    double l, w;
public:
    Rectangle(double length, double width) : l(length), w(width) {}
    double area() const override {
        return l * w;
    }
};

int main() {
    Shape* s1 = new Circle(5.0);
    Shape* s2 = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);

    cout << "Circle area: "    << s1->area() << endl;   // Circle::area() called
    cout << "Rectangle area: " << s2->area() << endl;   // Rectangle::area() called

    delete s1;
    delete s2;
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Circle area: 78.5398
Rectangle area: 24
```

**Explanation of Key Concepts (1 mark each):**
- `virtual` keyword in base enables dynamic dispatch.
- Base-class pointer `Shape*` holds addresses of subclass objects (upcasting).
- `override` keyword is optional but increases code safety.
- `virtual ~Shape()` ensures correct destructor chaining during `delete`.

---

### Question B (Choice 2). **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is the order of execution of constructors and destructors in a multilevel inheritance hierarchy? Derive the general formula and illustrate with a three-level example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Diamond Problem** in multiple inheritance. How is it resolved in C++ and Java? Provide a clean program snippet for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Order of Construction (Top-Down):**
When a derived object is created, the constructors are called in the order: **root base → intermediate base → most-derived class**.

**Order of Destruction (Bottom-Up):**
When the object is destroyed, the destructors are called in reverse: **most-derived → intermediate → root base**.

**General Formula for an n-level chain $L_1 \to L_2 \to \cdots \to L_n$:**

$$
\text{Construction} = \text{CTOR}(L_1) \to \text{CTOR}(L_2) \to \cdots \to \text{CTOR}(L_n)
$$

$$
\text{Destruction} = \text{DTOR}(L_n) \to \text{DTOR}(L_{n-1}) \to \cdots \to \text{DTOR}(L_1)
$$

**Three-Level Illustration (1 mark for code, 2 marks for output, 1 mark for explanation):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A {
public:
    A() { cout << "A ctor\n"; }
    ~A() { cout << "A dtor\n"; }
};
class B : public A {
public:
    B() { cout << "B ctor\n"; }
    ~B() { cout << "B dtor\n"; }
};
class C : public B {
public:
    C() { cout << "C ctor\n"; }
    ~C() { cout << "C dtor\n"; }
};
int main() {
    C obj;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output Trace:**
```
A ctor
B ctor
C ctor
C dtor
B dtor
A dtor
```

**Reasoning (1 mark):** Construction proceeds from the *most general* (root) to the *most specific* (leaf) class. Destruction is symmetric and LIFO. This guarantees that the *base part* is alive whenever the *derived part* needs to access it during construction, and dismantled *after* the derived part is no longer needed during destruction.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**The Diamond Problem (2 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    A[Base A] <|-- B[B]
    A <|-- C[C]
    B <|-- D[D]
    C <|-- D
```

When class `D` inherits from both `B` and `C`, and both inherit from `A`, the class `A` exists in `D` **twice** in memory. This causes:
- **Ambiguity** — which `A::method()` to call?
- **Memory waste** — duplicate base subobjects.
- **Inconsistent state** — modifying one copy does not affect the other.

---

**C++ Resolution — `virtual` Inheritance (2 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A {
public:
    int data;
    A() : data(0) {}
    void show() { cout << "A::data = " << data << endl; }
};

class B : virtual public A { };
class C : virtual public A { };
class D : public B, public C { };

int main() {
    D obj;
    obj.data = 42;     // unambiguous — single A subobject
    obj.show();        // A::data = 42
    return 0;
}
```

The keyword `virtual` makes `A` a **virtual base class**, ensuring only **one shared subobject** of `A` exists inside `D`.

---

**Java Resolution — Interfaces (2 marks):**

Java avoids the diamond problem *at the class level* by **disallowing multiple class inheritance**. Multiple inheritance is achieved only through **interfaces**, which carry no state (no data members, only abstract methods), so there is no duplication.

```java
interface A { void show(); }
interface B extends A { }    // B inherits A's contract
interface C extends A { }    // C inherits A's contract
class D implements B, C {    // D resolves show() once
    public void show() { System.out.println("D resolves A's show()"); }
}
```

Since interfaces declare *no instance fields*, only one implementation of `show()` is needed in `D`, eliminating ambiguity entirely.

**Summary Comparison (1 mark):**
| Language | Mechanism | State allowed in base? |
|---|---|---|
| C++ | `virtual` inheritance | Yes (shared subobject) |
| Java | `interfaces` only | No (interfaces are stateless) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *overriding* with *overloading*** — both have similar names; the parameter list is the deciding factor. Marks lost: ~2 per instance.
> 2. **Forgetting the `virtual` keyword on the base destructor** — if a base pointer deletes a derived object, the base destructor must be `virtual`, otherwise the derived destructor is **never called**, leading to memory leaks. Marks lost: ~3.
> 3. **Assuming private members are inherited** — they are physically present but *not directly accessible* in the subclass. Marks lost: ~2.
> 4. **Writing the wrong constructor order** — it is **root-to-leaf**, not leaf-to-root. Marks lost: ~2.
> 5. **Forgetting the `override` keyword** — although optional in C++, omitting it when the signature drifts causes silent bugs. Marks lost: ~1 (style/quality).
> 6. **Diamond problem answer without `virtual` keyword in C++** — the *entire* resolution mechanism hinges on this single keyword. Marks lost: ~3.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Sub Class (Module 2):**

- A **subclass** inherits from exactly **one or more** super classes; it represents the **IS-A relationship**.
- Inheritance modes in C++: `public` (most common), `protected`, `private` — they govern *how inherited members are seen inside the subclass*.
- **`private` members of the base are NOT directly accessible in the subclass**, regardless of inheritance mode.
- **Constructor order:** root base → leaf derived (top-down).
- **Destructor order:** leaf derived → root base (bottom-up, LIFO).
- **Single inheritance** — one base, one derived.
- **Multilevel inheritance** — chain (grandparent → parent → child).
- **Multiple inheritance** — one derived, many bases (C++ only directly; Java uses interfaces).
- **Hierarchical inheritance** — one base, many derived (common in frameworks).
- **Hybrid inheritance** — combination, often produces the **diamond problem**.
- **Diamond problem** is resolved in C++ via `virtual` inheritance; in Java by **disallowing multiple class inheritance** and using stateless interfaces.
- **Method overriding** requires the same signature and enables **runtime polymorphism** (needs `virtual` in C++ or non-`final` in Java).
- **Method overloading** is resolved at **compile time** and lives *within* a class.
- An **abstract class** contains ≥1 **pure virtual function** (C++: `virtual void f() = 0;`) and **cannot be instantiated** — it forces subclassing.
- **Upcasting** (Base pointer ← Derived object) is **implicit and safe**; **downcasting** requires an explicit `dynamic_cast` in C++.
- The **Liskov Substitution Principle** states: any property provable about the base type must also be provable about the subclass.
- **HAS-A** (composition) is **not** inheritance — it is a separate design relationship achieved by embedding an object as a data member.
- Memory layout of a derived object: **`[vptr] [Base subobject] [Derived-only data]`**, and the vptr enables dynamic dispatch.
- KTU 2024 expects students to draw class diagrams using **UML notation** (hollow triangle arrow for inheritance) — practice this regularly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overriding - super Keyword. Input/Output - I/O Basics
Reading Console Input
Writing Console Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Writing Console Output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Object Oriented Programming, **Console Output** refers to the mechanism by which a Java program transmits data (text, numbers, or formatted strings) from the program's memory to the **standard output stream** (`stdout`), which is typically displayed on the user's terminal, command prompt, or IDE console.

In Java, console output is achieved through a statically-accessed reference chain rooted at the `java.lang.System` class. The canonical statement used for writing output is:

```java
System.out.println("Hello, World!");
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, students must demonstrate proficiency in writing console output using the `System.out` object, understanding the role of the `PrintStream` class, and applying escape sequences and format specifiers for structured terminal output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing in front of a **public announcement speaker system** in a railway station. The announcer (your **Java program**) wants to broadcast a message. To do this, three things are required:

1. **The Announcement Room** → represents the `System` class (the entry point into Java's runtime environment).
2. **The Microphone on the desk** → represents the `out` object (a pre-configured output device).
3. **The Speaker/Amplifier** → represents the `PrintStream` class (the device that actually converts electrical signals into audible sound).

When the announcer speaks into the microphone, the message travels through the amplifier and emerges as sound at the speakers. Similarly, when you invoke `System.out.println("...")`, the message travels from your code, through the `out` object (a `PrintStream` instance), and emerges as text on the console.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Java Console Output

The expression `System.out.println(...)` is a compound reference that resolves through three layers:

| Layer | Component | Type | Role |
|-------|-----------|------|------|
| 1 | `System` | `final class` | Provides access to system resources |
| 2 | `out` | `static PrintStream` | The standard output destination |
| 3 | `println(...)` | Instance method | Prints data and appends a newline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is `out` declared `static` and `public`?**
> Because there is exactly **one** standard output stream per running JVM, and it must be accessible without instantiating the `System` class. This design follows the **Singleton-like static access pattern** — a recurring OOP concept that KTU examiners frequently test.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `System` Class — The Root of Output

The `System` class is a `final` class declared inside the `java.lang` package. It cannot be instantiated, and its constructor is private. It exposes three pre-defined static fields that correspond to the three standard I/O streams:

- `System.in` → Standard Input (keyboard) — of type `InputStream`
- `System.out` → Standard Output (console) — of type `PrintStream`
- `System.err` → Standard Error (error console) — of type `PrintStream`

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:**
> *"Why is `System` a final class?"* — It is marked `final` so that no subclass can alter the behavior of critical system-level resources such as standard input, output, and error streams.

## 2.2 The `out` Object — A Static `PrintStream`

The `out` field is declared as:

```java
public static final PrintStream out;
```

It is initialized by the JVM at startup to point to the host environment's standard output stream. Once initialized, the same `PrintStream` object handles every console write during the program's lifetime.

## 2.3 The `PrintStream` Class — The Output Engine

`PrintStream` is an `extends FilterOutputStream` class that provides a rich set of overloaded `print()` and `println()` methods. Every Java primitive type, plus `String`, `Object`, and character arrays, has a dedicated overloaded version of these methods.

### KTU Cheat Sheet — Console Output Methods

| Method Signature | Behavior |
|------------------|----------|
| `print(int i)` | Prints the integer as decimal text |
| `print(double d)` | Prints the double using its `Double.toString` representation |
| `print(boolean b)` | Prints `"true"` or `"false"` |
| `print(char c)` | Prints a single character |
| `print(char[] s)` | Prints an array of characters |
| `print(String s)` | Prints a string literal |
| `print(Object obj)` | Invokes `obj.toString()` and prints the result |
| `println()` | Prints a platform-dependent line separator |
| `println(<any>)` | Same as `print(<any>)` followed by a newline |
| `printf(String format, Object... args)` | C-style formatted output |
| `format(String format, Object... args)` | Identical to `printf` |
| `flush()` | Forces buffered output to be written immediately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Difference for KTU Exams:**
> - `print()` → does **NOT** move the cursor to a new line.
> - `println()` → prints the argument **AND** appends the line separator (`\n` on Unix, `\r\n` on Windows).
> - `printf()` → prints formatted output **WITHOUT** a trailing newline (unless `%n` is included).

## 2.4 Escape Sequences in Java

Escape sequences are special character combinations beginning with a backslash (`\`) used to represent non-printable or hard-to-type characters in console output.

| Escape Sequence | Meaning |
|-----------------|---------|
| `\n` | Newline (line feed) |
| `\r` | Carriage return |
| `\t` | Horizontal tab |
| `\\` | Backslash literal |
| `\"` | Double quote literal |
| `\'` | Single quote literal |
| `\b` | Backspace |
| `\f` | Form feed |

## 2.5 Format Specifiers Used with `printf()`

| Specifier | Data Type |
|-----------|-----------|
| `%d` | Decimal integer |
| `%f` | Floating-point number |
| `%s` | String |
| `%c` | Character |
| `%b` | Boolean |
| `%n` | Platform-independent newline |
| `%x` | Hexadecimal integer |
| `%o` | Octal integer |
| `%e` | Scientific notation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Console output is the foundation of every debugging tool, server log, embedded system diagnostic, and CLI utility. Mastery of `System.out.printf` is essential for generating structured reports (e.g., bank statements, sensor logs, telemetry dashboards).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace of `System.out.println("Hello");`

Let us dissect the statement `System.out.println("Hello");` to understand exactly what happens during execution:

**Step 1 — Class Loading:**
The JVM loads the `java.lang.System` class into memory. This class contains the static field `out`.

**Step 2 — Static Initialization:**
During class initialization, the static block inside `System` invokes `initializeSystemClass()`, which calls `setOut0()` to bind the `out` field to a `PrintStream` object wrapping the host's standard output file descriptor.

**Step 3 — Field Resolution:**
The dot operator `.` accesses the public static field `out`, returning the `PrintStream` reference.

**Step 4 — Method Resolution:**
The dot operator `.` again accesses the instance method `println(String x)` on the `PrintStream` object.

**Step 5 — Method Dispatch (Overload Resolution):**
The compiler selects the matching overload:

$$\text{println}(\text{String x}) : \text{void}$$

This overload internally calls `print(x)` followed by `newLine()`.

**Step 6 — Conversion to Bytes:**
The string `"Hello"` is converted to its byte representation using the platform's default character encoding, then written to the underlying output file descriptor.

**Step 7 — Flush:**
The `PrintStream` flushes its internal buffer to the OS, which then writes the bytes to the terminal.

Let us visualize the call chain as a mathematical/algorithmic trace:

$$\text{Source Code} \rightarrow \text{System} \rightarrow \text{out} \rightarrow \text{println} \rightarrow \text{print} \rightarrow \text{write} \rightarrow \text{flush} \rightarrow \text{OS} \rightarrow \text{Terminal}$$

## 3.2 Mathematical Trace of an Integer Output

Suppose we execute the statement `System.out.println(255);`. The internal dispatch logic is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{Compiler selects } \texttt{println(int i)} \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \texttt{println(int i)} \rightarrow \texttt{print(int i)} \rightarrow \texttt{newLine()} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \texttt{print(int i)} \text{ internally invokes } \texttt{Integer.toString(255)} \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \texttt{Integer.toString(255)} \text{ returns the } \texttt{String} \text{ "255"} \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{The string "255" is encoded into UTF-8 bytes} \\
\text{Step F:} \quad & \text{Bytes are written to the file descriptor} \\
\text{Step G:} \quad & \text{newLine()} \text{ writes the line separator} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The expression `System.out.println(255)` and `System.out.println("255")` produce **identical visible output**, but the underlying code paths differ entirely. The first triggers numeric-to-string conversion inside the JVM, while the second simply outputs an already-stringified constant. Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between these two execution paths.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Based on Argument Type

| Code Statement | Selected Overload | Internal Action |
|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| `System.out.println(100);` | `println(int)` | `Integer.toString(100)` |
| `System.out.println(3.14);` | `println(double)` | `Double.toString(3.14)` |
| `System.out.println('A');` | `println(char)` | Direct character write |
| `System.out.println(true);` | `println(boolean)` | Writes `"true"` literal |
| `System.out.println("Hi");` | `println(String)` | Direct string write |
| `System.out.println(obj);` | `println(Object)` | Invokes `obj.toString()` |

## 3.4 Detailed Example — Formatted Output with `printf`

Consider the following Java statement:

```java
System.out.printf("Name: %-10s | Age: %03d | Salary: %.2f%n", "Anu", 21, 45300.5);
```

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compiler validates the format string syntax} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Argument list is packaged into an Object array} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{\%-10s is applied to "Anu" \rightarrow left-aligned, padded to 10 chars: "Anu       "} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{\%03d is applied to 21 \rightarrow zero-padded to 3 digits: "021"} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{\%.2f is applied to 45300.5 \rightarrow rounded to 2 decimals: "45300.50"} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{\%n writes a platform-independent newline} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Name: Anu        \mid Age: 021 \mid Salary: 45300.50} \\
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Console Output

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the layered architecture through which a Java console output statement reaches the terminal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Java Source Code"] --> B["javac Compiler"]
    B --> C["Bytecode with System.out.println call"]
    C --> D["JVM Class Loader"]
    D --> E["System Class Loaded"]
    E --> F["Static Field out initialized to PrintStream"]
    F --> G["Method Resolution println overload selected"]
    G --> H["PrintStream Internal Buffer"]
    H --> I["Byte Conversion using default charset"]
    I --> J["Operating System File Descriptor 1 stdout"]
    J --> K["Terminal or Console Display"]
```

## 4.2 Method Overloading Hierarchy for `println`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PrintStream instance out"] --> B["println void no arg"]
    A --> C["println void boolean"]
    A --> D["println void char"]
    A --> E["println void int"]
    A --> F["println void long"]
    A --> G["println void float"]
    A --> H["println void double"]
    A --> I["println void char array"]
    A --> J["println void String"]
    A --> K["println void Object"]
```

## 4.3 Execution Flow of a `printf` Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Source: System.out.printf format args"] --> P2["Compiler validates format string"]
    P2 --> P3["Box primitive args into Object array"]
    P3 --> P4["Parse format specifiers left to right"]
    P4 --> P5{"Specifier Type"}
    P5 --> Q1["d integer"] --> R1["Apply width precision flags"]
    P5 --> Q2["f floating point"] --> R2["Round to specified decimal places"]
    P5 --> Q3["s string"] --> R3["Pad or truncate to width"]
    P5 --> Q4["n newline"] --> R4["Write line separator"]
    R1 --> S["Assemble final output string"]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    S --> T["Write bytes to stdout"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the `System` class and its `out` field in Java console output.**

**Model Answer:**

The `System` class is a `final` class in the `java.lang` package that provides access to standard I/O streams and other system-level resources. It cannot be instantiated.

The `out` field is a `public static final` reference of type `PrintStream`, automatically initialized by the JVM at program startup. It represents the standard output stream connected to the console. Because it is `static`, it is accessed directly via the class name (`System.out`), and because it is `final`, the reference cannot be reassigned to point to a different stream during runtime.

Together, `System.out` provides the entry point for invoking methods like `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` to write data to the console.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` methods in Java with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Behavior | Example | Output |
|--------|----------|---------|--------|
| `print()` | Prints the argument without a newline | `System.out.print("Hi");` | `Hi` (no newline) |
| `println()` | Prints the argument and appends a newline | `System.out.println("Hi");` | `Hi\n` |
| `printf()` | Prints formatted output using format specifiers | `System.out.printf("%-5d", 42);` | `42   ` (left-aligned) |

`printf()` allows width, precision, padding, and alignment controls, while `print()` and `println()` only perform direct conversion using the default `toString()` representation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of Java console output from source code to terminal display. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` with at least three different data types. Show the expected output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The layered architecture consists of the following stages:

1. **Source Code Layer** — Programmer writes `System.out.println(...)` in a `.java` file.
2. **Compilation Layer** — `javac` compiles it into bytecode, preserving the symbolic reference to `System.out`.
3. **Class Loading Layer** — JVM loads `java.lang.System` and resolves the static `out` field.
4. **Method Dispatch Layer** — Compiler-chosen `println` overload is invoked on the `PrintStream` object.
5. **Conversion Layer** — The argument is converted to its string representation via `toString()` or platform charset.
6. **Buffering Layer** — Characters are accumulated in the `PrintStream`'s internal byte buffer.
7. **OS Layer** — Buffer is flushed to the OS's standard output file descriptor (fd 1).
8. **Terminal Layer** — The OS forwards the bytes to the active terminal emulator for display.

A reference diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** above.

**(b)** Java Program:

```java
public class OutputDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Demonstration of print()
        System.out.print("Name: ");
        System.out.print("Anu");
        System.out.print("\n");

        // Demonstration of println()
        System.out.println("Age: 21");
        System.out.println("City: Kochi");

        // Demonstration of printf()
        System.out.printf("Salary: %.2f INR%n", 45300.567);
        System.out.printf("Hex Code: %#X%n", 255);
        System.out.printf("Padded: |%-10s|%05d|%n", "KTU", 7);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Name: Anu
Age: 21
City: Kochi
Salary: 45300.57 INR
Hex Code: 0XFF
Padded: |KTU       |00007|
```

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of method overloading in `PrintStream` with respect to `println()`. List at least six overloaded forms with their signatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss escape sequences and format specifiers in detail. Write a program that generates a formatted bill receipt using `printf()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Method overloading allows multiple methods to share the same name but differ in their parameter list. The `PrintStream` class provides numerous overloaded forms of `println()` so that any Java data type can be printed directly without manual conversion.

**Overloaded Forms of `println`:**

| # | Method Signature |
|---|------------------|
| 1 | `public void println()` |
| 2 | `public void println(boolean x)` |
| 3 | `public void println(char x)` |
| 4 | `public void println(int x)` |
| 5 | `public void println(long x)` |
| 6 | `public void println(float x)` |
| 7 | `public void println(double x)` |
| 8 | `public void println(char[] x)` |
| 9 | `public void println(String x)` |
| 10 | `public void println(Object x)` |

The Java compiler selects the appropriate overload at compile time based on the **static type** of the argument.

**(b)** **Escape Sequences** are special two-character combinations starting with `\` that represent non-printable characters (e.g., `\n`, `\t`, `\\`). **Format Specifiers** are placeholders inside format strings (e.g., `%d`, `%f`, `%s`) that are replaced by corresponding arguments at runtime.

**Formatted Bill Receipt Program:**

```java
public class BillReceipt {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("========== KTU CAFE BILL ==========");
        System.out.printf("%-20s %5s %10s%n", "Item", "Qty", "Price");
```
