        System.out.println("-----------------------------------");
        System.out.printf("%-20s %5d %10.2f%n", "Coffee", 2, 50.00);
        System.out.printf("%-20s %5d %10.2f%n", "Sandwich", 1, 120.50);
        System.out.printf("%-20s %5d %10.2f%n", "Pastry", 3, 45.75);
        System.out.println("-----------------------------------");
        System.out.printf("%-20s %5s %10.2f%n", "TOTAL", "", 357.75);
        System.out.println("===================================");
        System.out.printf("Thank you! Visit again.%n");
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
========== KTU CAFE BILL ==========
Item                     Qty      Price
-----------------------------------
Coffee                     2      50.00
Sandwich                   1     120.50
Pastry                     3     137.25
-----------------------------------
TOTAL                          357.75
===================================
Thank you! Visit again.
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing `System.out` with `System.in`** — Students frequently write `System.in.println()` which is a **compilation error**. `System.in` is an `InputStream`, not a `PrintStream`.
> - **Forgetting `%n` in `printf()`** — Unlike `println()`, `printf()` does **NOT** automatically append a newline. Omitting `%n` causes all outputs to merge on the same line.
> - **Using `\n` inside `printf()` instead of `%n`** — While `\n` works, it produces the Unix-style line feed regardless of platform. `%n` is **platform-independent** and is the KTU-recommended choice.
> - **Mismatched format specifiers and arguments** — Passing a `String` where `%d` is expected throws `IllegalFormatConversionException` at runtime.
> - **Assuming `print()` and `println()` accept format specifiers** — They do NOT interpret `%d` or `%s`. Only `printf()` and `format()` perform format substitution.
> - **Treating `System.out` as reassignable** — It is `final`, so `System.out = something;` is a **compilation error**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Console output in Java** is performed using the `System.out` object, which is a static `PrintStream` reference.
- The `System` class is **final** and cannot be instantiated; its static fields `in`, `out`, and `err` provide access to the three standard I/O streams.
- **`print()`** writes without a newline; **`println()`** appends a platform-specific line separator; **`printf()`** supports C-style format specifiers without auto-appending a newline.
- **`PrintStream` provides 10+ overloaded versions of `println()`**, one for each Java primitive type, plus `String`, `Object`, and `char[]`.
- **Escape sequences** like `\n`, `\t`, and `\\` allow non-printable and special characters to be embedded in output.
- **Format specifiers** like `%d`, `%f`, `%s`, `%c`, `%b`, and `%n` enable structured, aligned, and precision-controlled output.
- **Use `%n` instead of `\n` inside `printf()`** for cross-platform newline behavior.
- **`printf()` does NOT throw an exception** when argument count mismatches format specifiers in older Java versions but does throw `MissingFormatArgumentException` in modern versions — a common KTU trick question.
- **The `System.out` reference is `final`** and cannot be reassigned.
- **`flush()`** is automatically called when `println()` executes, ensuring immediate visibility on the console.
- **Buffering is enabled by default** in `PrintStream`; calling `System.out.flush()` forces immediate write.
- For KTU 2024 exams, always write code in **proper Java syntax**, use **meaningful variable names**, and include **expected output** with your program answers to score full marks.

topics.title: Packages - Defining Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages - Defining Package

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — PACKAGES: DEFINING A PACKAGE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Package (Java / KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **package** in Java is a *namespace* that organizes a set of related *classes*, *interfaces*, *enumerations*, and *sub-packages*. It is a **compile-time and runtime mechanism** used to group semantically or logically related types, prevent naming collisions, enforce access protection, and enable modular, maintainable, and reusable object-oriented program design.

A package is declared in a Java source file using the keyword **`package`** as the **first statement** (excluding comments and the `package` declaration itself). The general KTU prescribed syntax is:

```java
package <package_name>.<subpackage_name>;
```

A Java program can contain **only one `package` statement**, and it must appear **before any `import` statement or type declaration**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Think of a **package** like a *labeled filing cabinet* in a large office:

- The **office building** = your entire project / file system.
- Each **drawer in the cabinet** = a *package* (e.g., `accounts`, `employees`, `inventory`).
- Each **folder inside a drawer** = a *sub-package* (e.g., `accounts.salary`, `accounts.tax`).
- Each **document inside a folder** = a *class* or *interface* (e.g., `Employee.java`, `Payroll.java`).

Without labeled cabinets, two clerks might each file a document named `Report.java` — causing **chaos (name collision)**. With labeled cabinets (packages), every document is uniquely located at: `office/accounts/salary/Report.java`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Just like the file system uses *folders*, Java uses *packages*. The **directory hierarchy in your file system MUST mirror the package hierarchy** for the Java compiler (`javac`) and JVM to locate classes correctly.

### 1.3 Why Packages Are Essential (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Problem | Solution via Packages |
|---|---|
| Two classes with the same simple name (e.g., `Date`) in one project | Place them in different packages → `java.util.Date` vs `java.sql.Date` |
| Uncontrolled global access to classes | Packages provide **default (package-private)** access control |
| Poor modularity in large codebases | Packages enforce **logical grouping** of related types |
| Difficulty in locating & maintaining classes | Hierarchical naming (`com.company.project.module`) provides structure |
| No way to distribute reusable code | `JAR` files package entire packages for redistribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO3** — *Apply object-oriented principles such as encapsulation, modularity, and reusability using Java constructs* — and aligns with **Bloom’s Level: Apply / Analyze**.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Maximum recommended package depth:** 4 to 6 levels (industry standard, per Oracle Java Conventions).
- **Naming convention:** All **lowercase**, reverse-domain style — e.g., `in.ac.ktu.cse.s3.oop`.
- **Reserved package prefixes:** `java.*` and `javax.*` (Java SE), `org.*` (open-source), `com.*` (commercial).
- **Default package:** If no `package` statement is written, the class belongs to the **unnamed default package** — discouraged in professional and KTU-graded code.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules of the `package` Statement

The `package` keyword is a **compile-time directive** that tells the Java compiler:
> *"Every type declared in this `.java` source file belongs to the named package."*

### 2.1.1 Mandatory Rules (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Placement Rule** → The `package` statement must be the **first non-comment, non-whitespace statement** in the source file. Comments above it are permitted.
2. **Uniqueness Rule** → Only **one** `package` declaration is allowed per `.java` file.
3. **Naming Rule** → Package names are **dot-separated identifiers** (e.g., `kerala.ktu.oop.module3`).
4. **Case Convention** → Package names are conventionally **all lowercase** to avoid OS-level case-sensitivity conflicts (Windows is case-insensitive, Linux is case-sensitive).
5. **No Class-Name Collision** → Two types in the same package cannot share the same simple name.
6. **Directory Mapping Rule** → The **physical folder structure** of the source code **must exactly match** the package hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** The most commonly tested sub-concept is the **directory structure ↔ package mapping rule**. Always draw both the package tree and the corresponding folder tree in exam answers.

### 2.2 Types of Packages in Java

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Built-in / Predefined Packages** | Bundled with the JDK | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql`, `javax.swing` |
| **User-defined Packages** | Created by the programmer using the `package` keyword | `mypack`, `kerala.ktu.oop.lab3` |
| **Default (Unnamed) Package** | Has no `package` statement — used only for small demos | (No name) |

> [!NOTE]
> `java.lang` is **automatically imported** into every Java program — you never need `import java.lang.*;`.

### 2.3 The `package` vs `import` vs `class` Declaration Order

The order in any Java source file is **strictly**:

```java
// 1. (optional) package statement
package mypack;

// 2. (optional) import statements
import java.util.Scanner;

// 3. (optional) other type declarations
class MyClass { ... }
```

> [!WARNING]
> Placing an `import` statement **before** the `package` statement causes a **compilation error**: `error: expected package, found import`.

### 2.4 Compilation & Runtime Resolution of Packages

When you compile `MyClass.java` containing `package kerala.ktu.oop;`:

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Run `javac MyClass.java` | Produces `MyClass.class` in the **current working directory** |
| 2 | Use `javac -d . MyClass.java` | Places `.class` inside a folder matching the package name |
| 3 | Run `java kerala.ktu.oop.MyClass` | JVM searches `CLASSPATH` for the folder `kerala/ktu/oop/`, then loads `MyClass.class` |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Java (Spring, Jakarta EE):** Heavy reliance on packages like `org.springframework.boot`, `com.mysql.cj.jdbc`.
- **Android Development:** `androidx.appcompat.app`, `com.google.android.material` packages.
- **API Design:** Public packages form the *contract* of a library; private packages hide implementation details.
- **Software Engineering:** Packages enable **Information Hiding** at the module level, supporting the *Open/Closed Principle* of SOLID design.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula-Style Summary)

| Element | Syntax | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Declare a package | `package name1.name2;` | `package com.ktu.oop;` |
| Sub-package (hierarchical) | `package a.b.c;` | `package in.ac.ktu.cse.s3;` |
| Default (unnamed) | *(omit `package` statement)* | `class Demo { }` |
| Compile with directory creation | `javac -d . File.java` | `javac -d . Student.java` |
| Run a packaged class | `java fully.qualified.Name` | `java com.ktu.oop.Student` |
| Maximum `package` per file | **1** | — |
| Allowed positions | **First** statement | — |
| Naming convention | All **lowercase**, reverse-DNS | `org.apache.commons.lang3` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Defining a Package

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **exhaustive, line-by-line logical derivation** of how a package is defined, compiled, stored, and executed. Although not a numerical derivation, the same rigorous step-by-step discipline is applied to the Java compilation pipeline.

## 3.1 Worked Example: Defining `mypack` with a `Student` Class

### Step 1 — Create the directory structure

The directory structure **must mirror** the package name. Since the package is `mypack`, we need a folder named `mypack`.

```text
project_root/
└── mypack/
    └── Student.java
```

### Step 2 — Write the source file with `package` declaration

**File:** `mypack/Student.java`

```java
// First statement → package declaration
package mypack;

public class Student {
    private int rollNo;
    private String name;

    public Student(int rollNo, String name) {
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.name  = name;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Roll No : " + rollNo);
        System.out.println("Name    : " + name);
        System.out.println("Package : mypack");
    }
}
```

**Derivation of each component:**

| Line | Code | Logical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `// First statement → package declaration` | A **comment** — permitted *above* the `package` statement. |
| 2 | `package mypack;` | The **compile-time directive** that binds the `Student` class to namespace `mypack`. |
| 3 | `public class Student { ... }` | Declares a publicly accessible class inside the package. |
| 4–5 | `private int rollNo; private String name;` | **Encapsulation** — fields are hidden from outside the class. |
| 7–10 | `public Student(...)` | **Constructor** — initializes object state. |
| 12–16 | `public void display()` | **Public method** — accessible across packages (since class is `public`). |

### Step 3 — Create a consumer class in a *different* package (or default package)

**File:** `TestStudent.java` (placed in the `project_root` folder, *outside* `mypack`)

```java
import mypack.Student;   // Explicitly import the class from mypack

public class TestStudent {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Ananya");
        Student s2 = new Student(102, "Rahul");
        s1.display();
        s2.display();
    }
}
```

### Step 4 — Compile the package member

Run the following from the `project_root` directory:

```bash
javac -d . mypack/Student.java
```

- **`-d .`** tells the compiler to **create directory structure** (`.` = current directory) and place the `.class` file inside it.
- Result: `project_root/mypack/Student.class` is generated.

### Step 5 — Compile the consumer

```bash
javac TestStudent.java
```

### Step 6 — Execute

```bash
java TestStudent
```

**Expected Output:**

```text
Roll No : 101
Name    : Ananya
Package : mypack
Roll No : 102
Name    : Rahul
Package : mypack
```

### Step 3.2 — Full Analytical Matrix of the Process

| Phase | Action | Tool | Input Location | Output Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source Creation | Write `package mypack;` + class | Text Editor / IDE | `mypack/Student.java` | — |
| Package Compilation | Generate `.class` inside matching folder | `javac -d .` | `mypack/Student.java` | `mypack/Student.class` |
| Import Resolution | Compiler reads `import mypack.Student;` | `javac` | `TestStudent.java` | Resolves to `mypack/Student.class` |
| Bytecode Execution | JVM locates class via **fully-qualified name** | `java` | `mypack/Student.class` | Console output |

### Step 3.3 — Derivation of the CLASSPATH Resolution Rule

When the JVM runs `java mypack.Student`, the following resolution algorithm executes:

1. Read the **fully-qualified class name** → `mypack.Student`.
2. Convert dots to path separators → `mypack/Student`.
3. Append `.class` extension → `mypack/Student.class`.
4. Search each entry in the **CLASSPATH** variable (and the `-cp` flag, if provided).
5. If found → load bytecode into memory and execute `main()`.
6. If not found → throw `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException`.

This can be expressed compactly as:

$$
\text{ClassPath} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Entry}_i
$$

$$
\text{Find}(C) = \text{Entry}_i \oplus \text{PathOf}(C) \oplus \text{".class"}
$$

where $C$ is the fully-qualified class name, $\oplus$ is path concatenation, and $\text{PathOf}(C)$ replaces every dot `.` in $C$ with the OS path separator (`/` on Unix, `\` on Windows).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams comply with the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (no markdown formatting tags inside quoted labels).

## 4.1 Package Declaration Flow — Compilation & Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Write Source Code] --> A1[Add package statement as FIRST line]
    A1 --> A2[Save file in matching directory structure]
    A2 --> B0[Run javac -d . FileName.java]
    B0 --> B1{JVM Compiler parses package directive}
    B1 --> B2[Create folder hierarchy matching package]
    B2 --> B3[Generate Bytecode FileName.class]
    B3 --> C0[Run java fully.qualified.ClassName]
    C0 --> C1[Resolve fully qualified name to file path]
    C1 --> C2{Search CLASSPATH entries}
    C2 -->|Found| C3[Load class into JVM memory]
    C2 -->|Not Found| C4[Throw ClassNotFoundException]
    C3 --> D0[Execute main method]
    C4 --> E0[End with error]
    D0 --> F0[End with output]
```

## 4.2 Directory Structure ↔ Package Hierarchy Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FS[File System Tree]
        F1[Project Root] --> F2[mypack folder]
        F2 --> F3[Student.java source]
        F2 --> F4[Student.class bytecode]
        F1 --> F5[TestStudent.java source]
        F1 --> F6[TestStudent.class bytecode]
    end

    subgraph PK[Java Package Tree]
        P1[Default Package unnamed] --> P2[mypack]
        P2 --> P3[Student class]
        P1 --> P4[TestStudent class]
    end

    F3 -. compiles to .-> P3
    F5 -. compiles to .-> P4
    F4 -. imported by .-> P4
```

## 4.3 Source File Structure Order (Strict)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line 1: Optional Comments] --> B[Line 2: package statement ONLY ONE]
    B --> C[Line 3: import statements OPTIONAL MULTIPLE]
    C --> D[Line 4: Class or Interface declarations]
    D --> E[Line 5: end of file]
```

## 4.4 Sub-Package Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Root Package com] --> KT[ktu]
    KT --> OE[oop]
    OE --> M3[module3]
    M3 --> EX1[examples]
    M3 --> EX2[utilities]
    M3 --> EX3[tests]
    EX1 --> C1[Hello.java]
    EX1 --> C2[Demo.java]
    EX2 --> C3[Helper.java]
    EX3 --> C4[TestRunner.java]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Mapping:** Each leaf node above represents a `.java` file. The *full path* on disk would be:
> `com/ktu/oop/module3/examples/Hello.java` — matching the package declaration:
> `package com.ktu.oop.module3.examples;`

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a package in Java. List any two advantages of using packages.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **package** in Java is a *namespace* that groups a set of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single logical unit. It is declared using the `package` keyword as the first statement of a source file.
>
> **Two Advantages:**
> 1. **Namespace Management:** Prevents name conflicts by allowing classes with the same simple name to coexist in different packages (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`).
> 2. **Access Control:** Packages provide *package-private* (default) access level, enabling controlled visibility and information hiding.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three rules that must be followed while defining a package in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. The `package` statement must be the **first non-comment statement** in the source file.
> 2. **Only one** `package` statement is permitted per `.java` file.
> 3. The **directory structure** of the source file must match the package hierarchy (e.g., `package a.b;` → `a/b/ClassName.java`).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, each Part B question contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** with **internal choice** between alternatives. Two fully-independent question alternatives are provided below.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a package in Java. Write the syntax for defining a user-defined package and list the rules to be followed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **package** is a Java mechanism for grouping related classes and interfaces into a single namespace. It provides *modularity*, *access control*, and *name conflict resolution*.

**Syntax:**

```java
package <package_name>;
```

Example: `package kerala.ktu.oop;`

**Rules:**
- The `package` statement must be the **first** statement in the source file.
- Only **one** `package` statement is allowed per file.
- Package names are written in **lowercase** by convention.
- The **directory path** must mirror the package name (dots → folders).
- The default (unnamed) package is used when no `package` statement is written.

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a user-defined package named `mypack` containing a class `Circle` with members to calculate the area. Demonstrate how to use this class from another package. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**File 1:** `mypack/Circle.java`

```java
package mypack;

public class Circle {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double radius) {
        this.radius = radius;
    }

    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Radius : " + radius);
        System.out.printf("Area   : %.2f%n", area());
    }
}
```

**File 2:** `TestCircle.java` (in the project root, *outside* `mypack`)

```java
import mypack.Circle;

public class TestCircle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle(5.0);
        c.display();
    }
}
```

**Compilation & Execution:**

```bash
javac -d . mypack/Circle.java
javac TestCircle.java
java TestCircle
```

**Output:**

```text
Radius : 5.0
Area   : 78.54
```

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Differentiate between **built-in** and **user-defined** packages. Give one example of each with its purpose. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Built-in Package | User-defined Package |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Provided by JDK | Created by the programmer |
| Import needed? | Most require explicit import; `java.lang` is auto-imported | Must be explicitly imported |
| Purpose | Provide standard reusable functionality | Modularize the programmer’s own code |
| Example | `java.util` — provides `ArrayList`, `Scanner`, `HashMap` | `mypack` — contains project-specific classes |
| Location | Inside `JDK/jmods/java.base.jmod` | Inside the programmer’s project directory |

**(b)** Explain the significance of the **`CLASSPATH` environment variable** and the **`-d`** flag used with `javac` in the context of Java packages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**`CLASSPATH`:**
- It is an **environment variable** that tells the JVM and compiler *where* to look for user-defined package class files.
- It consists of a list of directories, JAR files, or ZIP files separated by `:` (Unix) or `;` (Windows).
- Example: `CLASSPATH=.;C:\myproject\lib\*`

**`javac -d` flag:**
- The `-d` flag specifies the **destination directory** for the generated `.class` files.
- The compiler **automatically creates sub-directories** matching the package hierarchy inside the destination.
- Example: `javac -d . Circle.java` creates `./mypack/Circle.class` if the source declares `package mypack;`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to place the `package` statement as the **first** statement → loses 2 marks.
> 2. ❌ Writing `package MyPack;` with uppercase — Java allows it, but the **directory must match exactly** (case-sensitive on Linux). Examiners mark this as a *partial* answer.
> 3. ❌ Drawing the *directory tree* but forgetting the *package tree* (or vice versa) → loses 3 marks in diagram-based questions.
> 4. ❌ Compiling without `-d .` flag and assuming `java mypack.Circle` will work without the folder structure → loses 2 marks.
> 5. ❌ Confusing `import` (type-level) with `package` (file-level) — a frequent conceptual error in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Package** = a *namespace* for grouping related Java types.
- ✅ Declared using `package` keyword.
- ✅ Must be the **first statement** of a source file (comments above it are okay).
- ✅ Only **one** `package` statement per `.java` file.
- ✅ Package names are **lowercase, dot-separated, reverse-DNS** style.
- ✅ **Directory structure MUST match the package hierarchy** (dots → folders).
- ✅ Use `javac -d . File.java` to auto-create folder structure for `.class` files.
- ✅ Use `java fully.qualified.ClassName` to run a packaged class.
- ✅ `CLASSPATH` tells the JVM *where* to find package classes.
- ✅ `java.lang` is **auto-imported**; every other package needs an `import`.
- ✅ **Default (unnamed) package** has no `package` statement — avoid in real projects.
- ✅ Packages provide **default (package-private) access control** in addition to `public`, `protected`, `private`.
- ✅ Built-in packages: `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql`, `java.awt`, `javax.swing`.
- ✅ User-defined packages: created by the programmer using `package` keyword.
- ✅ KTU CO Mapping: **CO3** — Apply OOP modularity principles.
- ✅ Bloom’s Level Tested: **Apply / Analyze** (defining, compiling, importing).
- ✅ Common exam trick: ask you to **predict the error** if `package` is placed *after* `import`.
- ✅ Common exam trick: ask you to **draw the folder structure** given a package name.
- ✅ Common exam trick: ask you to **write the fully-qualified name** to run a packaged class.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Packages. Access Control - public
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Importing Packages. Access Control - public ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Package in Java? **Formal KTU Definition:** A *package* in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, enumerations, and sub-packages. It acts as a **container** (a folder/directory) that prevents naming conflicts, controls access, and provides a search path for locating classes during compilation and runtime. Java packages follow a hierarchical, dot-separated naming convention (e.g., `java.util`, `java.io`, `com.ktu.oop`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Packages are the **fundamental unit of access control, modularity, and reusability** in Java. The `public` access modifier determines what is visible *across* package boundaries. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library System" Imagine a **massive national library** (the Java API). Without organization, finding a book would be impossible. The library solves this using: | Library Concept | Java Equivalent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Library Building** | Package | Logical container of related resources | | **Section / Wing** | Sub-package (`java.util`, `java.sql`) | Finer grouping inside the library | | **Book** | Class / Interface | The actual usable entity | | **"Public Reference Section"** | `public` modifier | Accessible to *any* visitor from *any* section | | **"Staff Only" sticker** | `default` (package-private) | Accessible only within that wing | | **Card swipe** | `protected` | Restricted but extends to members of the same "trust circle" (subclasses) | | **Locked vault** | `private` | Accessible *only* inside the room where it is kept | So, when we say **"importing a package"**, it is like **getting a library card that lets us borrow books from a specific wing** of the national library without having to walk to that wing every time. ### 1.3 What is the `public` Access Modifier? **Formal Definition:** The `public` keyword is an **access specifier** in Java that grants the **most permissive visibility level**. A class, method, variable, or constructor declared `public` is accessible **from any other class in any package** within the Java universe, subject only to class-loading rules. > [!NOTE] > **Key Fact:** `public` is the **least restrictive** of Java's four access levels: `private` < `default` < `protected` < `public`. In KTU 2024 OOP grading, confusing these four levels is one of the **top causes of 2–3 mark deductions** in viva and written exams. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Importing Packages — The Three Forms Java provides **three syntactic ways** to bring external types into the current namespace. Each has a different **scope of resolution** and **readability trade-off**. #### 2.1.1 Single-Type Import (Recommended by KTU) ```java import java.util.Scanner; // imports ONLY the Scanner class import java.util.ArrayList; // imports ONLY the ArrayList class ``` - **Why use it?** High readability, no ambiguity, preferred in industry and academic submissions. - **How it works:** The compiler adds a single reference to `java.util.Scanner` in the symbol table. No runtime cost. #### 2.1.2 Type-Import-on-Demand (Wildcard Import) ```java import java.util.*; // imports ALL public types of java.util ``` - **Why use it?** Convenient for exploratory coding, prototyping, and when many classes from a package are used. - **How it works?** The compiler still performs **on-demand resolution** — only the classes actually referenced are loaded. It does **not** bloat bytecode. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Wildcard imports can cause **name-collision compile-time errors** if two packages contain classes with the same name (e.g., `java.util.Date` vs `java.sql.Date`). #### 2.1.3 Implicit / Automatic Import ```java // No statement needed! public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { String s = "KTU"; // works without import System.out.println(s); // works without import } } ``` - **Why?** The `java.lang` package is **auto-imported** by every Java compilation unit. It contains `String`, `System`, `Object`, `Math`, `Thread`, etc. #### 2.1.4 Static Import (KTU Frequently Tested) ```java import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.Math.sqrt; import static java.lang.System.out; class Circle { public static void main(String[] args) { out.println("Area = " + PI * 5 * 5); // PI, out used directly out.println("Root = " + sqrt(16)); // sqrt used directly } } ``` - **Why?** Lets us refer to **static members** of a class directly, without the class-name prefix. Useful with constants (`PI`, `MAX_VALUE`). ### 2.2 The `public` Access Modifier — Deep Rules #### 2.2.1 Top-Level Classes and Interfaces A **top-level** class or interface (one declared directly inside a file, not nested) can only be declared `public` or **package-private** (no modifier). It **cannot** be `protected` or `private`. ```java // File: Student.java package university.dept; public class Student { // ← public: visible everywhere // ... } ``` #### 2.2.2 Members (Fields, Methods, Constructors) | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff. pkg) | Other Packages | |---|---|---|---|---| | `public` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **✓** | | `protected` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | | `default` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | | `private` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | #### 2.2.3 Default Public Class Requirement > [!IMPORTANT] > In **any** `.java` source file, **at most one** top-level class/interface can be `public`, and its name **must match the file name**. This is a compile-time rule under JLS §7.6, and KTU exam questions often test this rule. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Syntax | Visibility Scope | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Single-class import | `import p.q.C;` | One class only | ★★★★★ | | 2 | Wildcard import | `import p.q.*;` | All public types of package | ★★★★ | | 3 | Static import | `import static p.q.C.m;` | One static member | ★★★★ | | 4 | Auto import (`java.lang`) | None required | `String`, `System`, etc. | ★★★★★ | | 5 | `public` class | `public class C {}` | All packages | ★★★★★ | | 6 | `public` method | `public void m(){}` | All packages | ★★★★★ | | 7 | `public` field | `public int x;` | All packages | ★★★★ | | 8 | `public` constructor | `public C(){}` | Instantiated from anywhere | ★★★ | | 9 | `public` interface | `public interface I{}` | All packages | ★★★ | | 10 | File-name rule | `public class` name = filename | Compile-time enforced | ★★★★★ | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Microservices & API Design:** A service's *public methods* form its **Application Programming Interface (API)** — the contract consumers depend on. Anything not `public` is an *implementation detail* that can be refactored freely. - **Library Development (e.g., Apache Commons, Jackson):** Library authors use `public` to expose stable APIs and leave the rest `package-private` or `private`. - **Plugin Systems:** Frameworks like Spring or Eclipse use `public` to discover extension points via reflection. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolving an External Class Without Import **Problem:** Demonstrate the *full path import* technique and contrast it with `import` statements. **Step 1 — Original code without import:** ```java package app; public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { // Without import, we must use the Fully Qualified Name (FQN) java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList<>(); list.add("KTU"); list.add("B.Tech"); System.out.println(list); } } ``` **Step 2 — Logic of FQN usage:** - The compiler resolves `java.util.ArrayList` by walking the **classpath** directory tree. - This is the **most explicit** and the **least likely to collide**, but it is verbose. **Step 3 — Refactored with single-type import:** ```java package app; import java.util.ArrayList; // ← now ArrayList is in scope public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { ArrayList list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add("KTU"); list.add("B.Tech"); System.out.println(list); } } ``` **Step 4 — Refactored with wildcard import:** ```java package app; import java.util.*; // ← every public type of java.util is available public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { ArrayList list = new ArrayList<>(); Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); // also from java.util list.add(sc.nextLine()); System.out.println(list); } } ``` **Output (for all three versions):** $$ \text{Output} = [\text{user input}] $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proving `public` Crosses Package Boundaries **Step 1 — Create a reusable class in package `utility`:** ```java // File: /utility/Calculator.java package utility; public class Calculator { public int add(int a, int b) { // ← public method return a + b; } public int square(int x) { // ← public method return x * x; } } ``` **Step 2 — Use it from a *different* package `app`:** ```java // File: /app/Main.java package app; import utility.Calculator; // explicit single-type import public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator c = new Calculator(); int sum = c.add(10, 20); int sq = c.square(sum); System.out.println("Sum = " + sum); System.out.println("Square of sum = " + sq); } } ``` **Step 3 — Output derivation:** $$ \text{sum} = 10 + 20 = 30 $$ $$ \text{sq} = 30 \times 30 = 900 $$ $$\boxed{\text{Output: Sum = 30}\quad \text{Square of sum = 900}}$$ **Step 4 — Proof of accessibility:** - The `app` package is **separate** from the `utility` package. - The `add()` and `square()` methods are `public`. - Therefore they are **accessible** to `Main` in a different package. ✓ **Step 5 — Counter-example (what fails):** If we change `add` from `public` to **package-private** (no modifier), then `Main` in package `app` will get: ``` error: add(int,int) is not public in utility.Calculator; cannot be accessed from outside package ``` This proves `public` is the *only* modifier that allows cross-package access for ordinary (non-subclass) code. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Compile-Time Rules Tested by KTU | # | Scenario | Verdict | KTU Rule Tested | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `public class A {}` in `B.java` | ❌ Compile error | Filename must match public class name | | 2 | Two `public` top-level classes in one file | ❌ Compile error | At most one `public` top-level type per file | | 3 | `public` top-level class marked `private` | ❌ Compile error | Illegal combination | | 4 | `protected` top-level class | ❌ Compile error | Only `public` or default allowed at top level | | 5 | `public` constructor in a non-`public` class | ✅ Compiles | Constructor access level can exceed class? **No** — must be ≤ class's access | | 6 | `public` field accessed via `obj.fieldName` across packages | ✅ Compiles | `public` is universally visible | | 7 | `import java.util.*; import java.sql.*; Date d;` | ❌ Ambiguous | Wildcard collision | | 8 | `import static java.lang.Math.*;` | ✅ Compiles | Static wildcard is legal | | 9 | `public interface I { void m(); }` (implicit `public abstract`) | ✅ Compiles | Interface methods are `public` by default | | 10 | Accessing `public` nested class via `Outer.Inner` from another package | ✅ Compiles | Nested classes follow the same rules | ### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Static Import: Mathematical Demonstration **Problem:** Compute the circumference and area of a circle of radius $r$ using static import. **Step 1 — Formulas:** $$ C = 2 \pi r \qquad A = \pi r^2 $$ **Step 2 — Java code with static import:** ```java package geometry; import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.Math.pow; public class Circle { public static void main(String[] args) { double r = 7.0; double c = 2 * PI * r; double a = PI * pow(r, 2); System.out.println("Circumference = " + c); System.out.println("Area = " + a); } } ``` **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:** $$ C = 2 \times \pi \times 7 = 14\pi \approx 43.982 $$ $$ A = \pi \times 7^2 = 49\pi \approx 153.938 $$ **Output:** ``` Circumference = 43.982297150257104 Area = 153.93804002589985 ``` **Why static import helps here:** - `PI` and `pow` are used repeatedly. - Removing the `Math.` prefix reduces visual clutter. - The code reads like a **mathematical expression**, which matches the domain. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Java Package Hierarchy (Classpath Tree) ```mermaid graph TD classDef auto fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000 classDef user fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef ext fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 root((Java API)):::auto lang["java.lang (auto-imported)"]:::auto util["java.util"]:::ext io["java.io"]:::ext sql["java.sql"]:::ext userpkg["com.ktu.app (user-defined)"]:::user subpkg["com.ktu.app.model"]:::user string["String"]:::auto system["System"]:::auto math["Math"]:::auto scanner["Scanner"]:::ext arraylist["ArrayList"]:::ext file["File"]:::ext date1["Date (util)"]:::ext date2["Date (sql) ⚠"]:::ext main["Main.java"]:::user calc["Calculator.java"]:::user student["Student.java"]:::user root --> lang root --> util root --> io root --> sql root --> userpkg lang --> string lang --> system lang --> math util --> scanner util --> arraylist util --> date1 sql --> date2 io --> file userpkg --> subpkg userpkg --> main userpkg --> calc subpkg --> student ``` > [!NOTE] > The ⚠ marker on `Date (sql)` highlights a classic KTU exam trap: importing both `java.util.*` and `java.sql.*` causes an *ambiguous-Date* error. ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Visibility Matrix of `public` Across Packages ```mermaid graph LR classDef yes fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 classDef no fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000 A["Caller in
Package P1"]:::yes B["Caller in
Package P2"]:::yes C["Subclass in
Package P2"]:::yes D["Non-related class
in P2"]:::yes T["Target class / member
declared PUBLIC
in Package P1"]:::yes A -->|"obj.add()"| T B -->|"obj.add()"| T C -->|"obj.add()"| T D -->|"obj.add()"| T ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow: Choosing an Import Style ```mermaid graph TD classDef start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000 classDef stepA fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef stepB fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#000 classDef endN fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 classDef endE fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000 Q{"Need a class
from another package?"}:::start S1{"Is it from
java.lang?"}:::stepA S2{"How many classes
from the package?"}:::stepB A1["Use it directly,
no import needed"]:::endN A2["Use single-type
import (BEST)"]:::endN A3["Use wildcard
import carefully"]:::endN A4["Use Fully Qualified
Name inline"]:::endN A5["Name collision -
avoid wildcard"]:::endE Q -->|Yes| S1 Q -->|No| A1 S1 -->|Yes| A1 S1 -->|No| S2 S2 -->|"1 or 2 classes"| A2 S2 -->|"many classes,
no collisions"| A3 S2 -->|"name clash
with another pkg"| A5 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture: How the Compiler Resolves `import` ```mermaid graph TD classDef io fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef mem fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#000 classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 src[".java source file
with import statements"]:::io tok["Tokenizer / Lexer
reads source"]:::proc sym["Symbol Table
(per file)"]:::mem res["Resolver phase
'Where is ArrayList?'"]:::proc cp["Search CLASSPATH
directories & JARs"]:::proc bind["Bind reference:
java/util/ArrayList.class"]:::mem gen["Generate .class bytecode
with CONSTANT_Class refs"]:::out src --> tok --> sym --> res --> cp --> bind --> gen ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the role of the `import` statement in Java. How is the `java.lang` package different from other packages with respect to importing?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `import` statement in Java makes classes and interfaces from other packages available to the current source file **without using their Fully Qualified Name (FQN)**. It improves code readability and reduces verbosity. There are three forms: single-type (`import p.q.C;`), on-demand (`import p.q.*;`), and static import (`import static p.q.C.m;`). The `java.lang` package is **automatically imported** into every Java source file by the compiler (JLS §7.3). Therefore classes such as `String`, `System`, `Object`, and `Math` can be used directly without an explicit `import` statement. All other packages must be imported explicitly or referenced via FQN. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List the four access specifiers in Java and arrange them in increasing order of accessibility. What is the highest visibility a top-level class can have?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four access specifiers in Java, in **increasing order of accessibility**, are: $$ \text{private} \;<\; \text{default (package-private)} \;<\; \text{protected} \;<\; \text{public} $$ A **top-level class or interface** can only be declared either `public` or *package-private* (no modifier). It **cannot** be `protected` or `private`. Therefore the highest visibility a top-level class can have is `public`, which makes it accessible from any package in the application. --- ### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)** --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of packages in Java. With suitable examples, illustrate *single-type import*, *type-import-on-demand*, and *static import* statements. Discuss any two advantages of using packages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the `public` access modifier in Java? Explain its visibility scope with a neat diagram or table. Write a Java program that demonstrates a `public` class in one package being accessed and instantiated from a class in a different package. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Packages in Java (7 marks):** A *package* in Java is a namespace that groups related types. It maps directly to a folder/directory on disk and provides: 1. **Logical organization** of large codebases. 2. **Name-conflict resolution** (two classes with the same name can coexist in different packages). 3. **Access control** boundary. 4. **Reusability** and **distribution** (JAR files). **Three forms of import — code demonstration:** ```java // File: ImportDemo.java package demo; // (i) Single-type import import java.util.ArrayList; // (ii) Type-import-on-demand (wildcard) import java.util.*; // (iii) Static import import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.System.out; public class ImportDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { ArrayList list = new ArrayList<>(); // (i) Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); // (ii) wildcard out.println("PI value = " + PI); // (iii) static out.print("Enter name: "); list.add(sc.nextLine()); out.println("List: " + list); } } ``` **Two advantages of packages:** - **Modularity:** Code is organized into logical units, easing maintenance. - **Reusability:** A well-designed package can be distributed (as a JAR) and reused across projects without modification. --- **(b) The `public` modifier (7 marks):** The `public` keyword is the **most permissive** access specifier in Java. A `public` class, method, or field is accessible **from any other class in any package**, without restriction. **Visibility scope table:** | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Other Pkg) | Other Packages | |---|---|---|---|---| | `private` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | `default` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | | `protected` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | | **`public`** | **✓** | **✓** | **✓** | **✓** | **Demonstration program (two files in two packages):** ```java // File: utility/Converter.java package utility; public class Converter { public double celsiusToFahrenheit(double c) { return (c * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32.0; } } ``` ```java // File: app/Main.java package app; import utility.Converter; // public class imported across packages public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Converter conv = new Converter(); // public ctor access double result = conv.celsiusToFahrenheit(100); System.out.println("100 C in F = " + result); } } ``` **Output derivation:** $$ F = (100 \times 9/5) + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212 $$ $$\boxed{\text{Output: 100 C in F = 212.0}}$$ This proves that a `public` class (`Converter`) in package `utility` is **instantiable and usable** from package `app`, confirming the universal visibility of `public`. --- #### **Question B — [Alternative Choice, KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Differentiate between *package-private* (default) and `public` access in Java. Provide a code example showing a compile-time error when default-access members are accessed across packages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with an example how `static import` improves code readability. Write a Java program that uses `static import` to compute the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle using the formula $h = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$. Show the calculation for $a = 3$, $b = 4$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) `public` vs package-private (7 marks):** | Aspect | `public` | Package-Private (default) | |---|---|---| | Keyword required | Yes (`public`) | No (omitted) | | Visibility | All packages | Only same package | | Subclass in another pkg | ✓ | ✗ | | Use case | Public APIs, library classes | Internal helpers, tightly-coupled classes | **Compile-time error demonstration:** ```java // File: geometry/Box.java package geometry; public class Box { int length = 10; // PACKAGE-PRIVATE (default) public int width = 20; // PUBLIC } ``` ```java // File: app/UseBox.java package app; import geometry.Box; public class UseBox { public static void main(String[] args) { Box b = new Box(); System.out.println(b.width); // ✓ Compiles: width is public System.out.println(b.length); // ✗ Compile error } } ``` **Compile error produced:** ``` error: length is not public in geometry.Box; cannot be accessed from outside package ``` This is the classic KTU proof that **only `public` crosses package boundaries for non-subclasses**. --- **(b) Static Import for Hypotenuse (7 marks):** **Mathematical formula:** $$ h = \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}} $$ **Given:** $a = 3$, $b = 4$ **Java program:** ```java package triangles; import static java.lang.Math.sqrt; // static import of method import static java.lang.Math.pow; import static java.lang.System.out; public class Hypotenuse { public static void main(String[] args) { double a = 3.0, b = 4.0; double h = sqrt(pow(a, 2) + pow(b, 2)); out.println("Hypotenuse = " + h); } } ``` **Step-by-step numerical derivation:** $$ a^{2} = 3^{2} = 9 $$ $$ b^{2} = 4^{2} = 16 $$ $$ a^{2} + b^{2} = 9 + 16 = 25 $$ $$ h = \sqrt{25} = 5.0 $$ $$\boxed{\text{Output: Hypotenuse = 5.0}}$$ **How static import improved readability:** - Without it: `Math.sqrt(Math.pow(a,2) + Math.pow(b,2))` — three `Math.` prefixes. - With it: `sqrt(pow(a, 2) + pow(b, 2))` — reads as the math formula itself. - Particularly useful in **scientific, statistical, and engineering** Java code where many math utilities are used. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions** > 1. **Forgetting `import java.util.Scanner;`** when using `Scanner` in code → **−1 mark** (compilation error in viva). > 2. **Writing `protected class A {}` at top level** → **−1 mark** (illegal combination). > 3. **Filename mismatch** with the public class name → entire program treated as **non-compilable**, often 0 in practical. > 4. **Confusing `public` with `protected`** when explaining cross-package access for non-subclasses → **−2 marks** (conceptual error). > 5. **Forgetting that `java.lang` is auto-imported** and writing `import java.lang.String;` → minor deduction, but reflects lack of clarity. > 6. **Using wildcard import `import java.util.*;` then `import java.sql.*;`** and referring to `Date` → ambiguous-error deduction if asked to identify bug. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Package = folder = namespace.** Use lowercase, dot-separated, reverse-domain names for user packages (e.g., `com.ktu.oop`). - ✅ **Three import forms:** single-type (`import p.q.C;`), on-demand (`import p.q.*;`), and static (`import static p.q.C.m;`). - ✅ **`java.lang` is auto-imported** — no statement needed for `String`, `System`, `Math`, `Object`. - ✅ **`import` does NOT embed code** — it only tells the compiler where to look. Bytecode still uses FQNs (visible in `javap -v`). - ✅ **Static import** reduces verbosity of repeated `ClassName.member` calls but should be used sparingly. - ✅ **`public` is the most permissive modifier** and the only one that allows cross-package access for non-subclasses. - ✅ **Top-level class can be only `public` or default** — never `protected` or `private`. - ✅ **Filename rule:** at most one `public` top-level class per `.java` file, and its name must match the file. - ✅ **Constructors inherit class visibility constraints** — a `public` class can have non-public constructors (limiting instantiation), but a non-public class cannot have a `public` constructor. - ✅ **Wildcard import does not slow runtime** — Java uses on-demand resolution at compile time. - ✅ **Always prefer single-type import** in KTU submissions for clarity and to avoid naming collisions.

topics.title: private
protected. Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** protected. Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The `protected` Access Modifier — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > In Java, `protected` is an **access control modifier** applied to classes, methods, and fields. A `protected` member is accessible: > 1. **Within the same package** (package-private / default behavior is included), AND > 2. **From subclasses** in any package (via inheritance). This makes `protected` strictly more permissive than the **default (package-private)** access level, but **more restrictive than `public`**. ### The Four Access Levels in Java (Strict Hierarchy) | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Different Package) | Other Classes | |----------|:----------:|:------------:|:----------------------------:|:-------------:| | `private` | Yes | No | No | No | | **default** (no keyword) | Yes | Yes | No | No | | **`protected`** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes (via inheritance only)** | **No** | | `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Photo Album" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of `protected` as a *Family Photo Album*:** > - **Private** = Your personal diary locked in your drawer → only you can read it. > - **Default** = Photos shared inside your house → only family members living in the same house can see them. > - **Protected** = Photos you share with **all your relatives**, including cousins living in **different cities/houses** (subclasses in other packages), but **not** with random neighbors or strangers. > - **Public** = Photos posted on social media → the entire world can see them. So `protected` essentially says: *"Visible to my package-mates AND to anyone who inherits from me, regardless of where they live."* --- ## 1.2 Checked Exceptions — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **Checked Exception** in Java is a subclass of `java.lang.Exception` (excluding `RuntimeException` and its subclasses) that the **compiler verifies at compile time**. The programmer is **mandatorily required** to either: > 1. **Handle** the exception using a `try-catch` block, OR > 2. **Declare** it in the method's signature using the `throws` clause. If neither is done, the code **fails to compile**. This compile-time enforcement is the defining feature of checked exceptions. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Government Permit" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Checked Exceptions are like a Government Permit for a Construction Project:** > - Before you can **demolish a building** (perform a risky operation like reading a file or connecting to a database), you **must obtain a permit** from the municipal corporation (the compiler). > - You either **submit a safety plan upfront** (`throws` declaration), or you **have a supervisor handle problems on-site** (`try-catch` block). > - If you have **neither**, the project is **shut down before it even begins** (compilation fails). > - **Unchecked exceptions** (RuntimeExceptions) are like *earthquakes* — they are sudden, often caused by programmer mistakes, and the government cannot predict them in advance, so no permit is required. ### Categories of Checked Exceptions (Commonly Tested in KTU) | Exception Class | When It Occurs | Common Scenario | |-----------------|----------------|-----------------| | `IOException` | Input/Output operation fails | File not found, network failure | | `FileNotFoundException` | Specific file path does not exist | Reading `data.txt` that doesn't exist | | `ClassNotFoundException` | Class cannot be loaded at runtime | Dynamic class loading via reflection | | `InterruptedException` | Thread is interrupted while waiting/waiting/sleeping | `Thread.sleep()` interrupted | | `SQLException` | Database access error | Invalid SQL query, DB connection lost | | `ParseException` | String parsing fails in expected format | Date string `"abc"` passed to date parser | > [!WARNING] > **`RuntimeException` and its subclasses (e.g., `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`) are UNCHECKED — they are NOT checked exceptions**, even though their parent class `Exception` is checked. KTU questions often test this distinction. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of `protected` — Step-by-Step Breakdown ### Rule 1: Package-Level Visibility A `protected` member behaves **exactly like default (package-private)** access for any class residing in the **same package**. There is no functional difference between `protected` and default within the same package. ### Rule 2: Cross-Package Inheritance Access A `protected` member becomes accessible to a subclass in a **different package** **only through inheritance** — meaning the subclass can access it via its own instance reference or through the inheritance chain, but **not** via a reference of the superclass type held by some unrelated class. ### Rule 3: Non-Subclass External Access is Denied If a class is in a different package AND is **not** a subclass of the protected member's owning class, the member is **inaccessible** — same as private. ### Why Was `protected` Designed This Way? The `protected` modifier exists to support the **OOP principle of encapsulation with controlled extensibility**. It allows library/framework designers to expose internal helper methods to subclass implementors without exposing them to end-users. This is heavily used in Java's standard library (e.g., `AbstractList`, `AbstractMap` in the Collections Framework). ### Practical KTU-Relevant Code Rule ```java package university.dept; public class Faculty { protected int employeeId = 101; // accessible to subclasses anywhere protected void displayId() { // accessible to subclasses anywhere System.out.println("ID: " + employeeId); } } ``` ```java package university.examcell; // DIFFERENT package import university.dept.Faculty; public class ExamController extends Faculty { // subclass public void accessDemo() { System.out.println(employeeId); // ALLOWED — inherited access displayId(); // ALLOWED — inherited access } public void indirectAccess(Faculty f) { // System.out.println(f.employeeId); // NOT ALLOWED — different package, not via inheritance chain } } ``` --- ## 2.2 Operational Logic of Checked Exceptions — The "Three-Party Contract" Checked exceptions operate through a **mandatory contract** enforced by `javac` (the Java compiler): ### The Three Compliance Options 1. **`try-catch` (Handle internally)** - The method catches the exception, recovers (if possible), and prevents it from propagating. - Use when the calling context **can meaningfully respond** to the failure. 2. **`throws` (Delegate upward)** - The method declares the exception in its signature, transferring responsibility to the caller. - Use when the method **cannot reasonably handle** the exception and the caller has more context. 3. **Wrap as Unchecked (Modern Best Practice)** - Catch the checked exception and rethrow it wrapped inside an unchecked exception (e.g., `RuntimeException`). - Use in frameworks where checked exceptions create excessive boilerplate. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Exception Handling Reference:** The table below summarizes the complete exception taxonomy and the rules you must memorize for exams. ### Table A: Java Exception Class Hierarchy | Level | Class | Type | Compiler Enforced? | |:-----:|-------|:----:|:------------------:| | 0 | `java.lang.Throwable` | Root (checked conceptually) | N/A — root | | 1 | `java.lang.Exception` | Checked parent | **Yes** | | 1 | `java.lang.Error` | Unchecked | No | | 2 | `RuntimeException` (extends Exception) | **Unchecked** | **No** | | 3 | `IOException` (extends Exception) | **Checked** | **Yes** | | 3 | `SQLException` (extends Exception) | **Checked** | **Yes** | | 3 | `ClassNotFoundException` (extends Exception) | **Checked** | **Yes** | ### Table B: Checked vs Unchecked — Decision Matrix | Property | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception (RuntimeException) | Error | |----------|:----------------:|:--------------------------------------:|:-----:| | Compile-time checking | **Yes (mandatory)** | No | No | | Inherits from | `Exception` (directly) | `RuntimeException` | `Error` | | Recoverable? | Usually yes | Usually programmer bug | System-level, fatal | | Mandatory handling? | **Yes** | No | No | | Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException` | `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` | ### Table C: The Five Keywords of Java Exception Handling | Keyword | Purpose | Where Used | |---------|---------|------------| | `try` | Defines a monitored block of code | Wraps risky code | | `catch` | Handles a specific exception type | Immediately after `try` | | `finally` | Always executes (cleanup code) | Optional, after `catch` | | `throw` | Manually throws an exception object | Inside method body | | `throws` | Declares exceptions a method may propagate | In method signature | ### Table D: Protected Access Decision Rules | Scenario | Access Allowed? | Reason | |----------|:---------------:|--------| | Same class | Yes | Direct member access | | Same package, different class | Yes | Package-level visibility | | Different package, subclass (via inheritance) | Yes | Subclass privilege | | Different package, subclass (via superclass reference) | No | Must access via subclass type | | Different package, non-subclass | No | No privilege | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **`protected`**: Used extensively in **framework design** — e.g., `HttpServlet` in Java EE exposes `protected doGet()` and `doPost()` methods that application developers override but end-users never call directly. - **Checked Exceptions**: Promote **robust I/O programming** — JDBC drivers, file APIs, and network libraries use checked exceptions to force programmers to handle realistic failure modes like network drops or missing files. This is why Java is preferred for **enterprise backend systems** where reliability is critical. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Java Exception Hierarchy Tree The Java exception hierarchy is a **single-inheritance tree** rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`. Below is the formal step-by-step derivation of why checked exceptions behave as they do. ### Step 1: The Root — `Throwable` `Throwable` is the root class. It has two direct subclasses: - `Exception` - `Error` ### Step 2: Branching into `Exception` `Exception` represents conditions that **reasonable applications should catch and handle**. It further branches into: - **Direct subclasses** → These are **CHECKED** exceptions. - `RuntimeException` and its subclasses → These are **UNCHECKED**. ### Step 3: The Compiler's Decision Rule The Java compiler (`javac`) enforces the following rule: $$ \text{IsChecked}(E) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } E \text{ is a subclass of } \texttt{Exception} \text{ but NOT a subclass of } \texttt{RuntimeException} \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 4: Formal Application Let us verify with `IOException`: $$ \texttt{IOException} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{extends Exception} \;\rightarrow\; \text{NOT a subclass of RuntimeException} $$ $$ \therefore \text{IsChecked}(\texttt{IOException}) = \text{true} $$ Let us verify with `NullPointerException`: $$ \texttt{NullPointerException} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{extends RuntimeException} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{extends Exception} $$ $$ \therefore \text{IsChecked}(\texttt{NullPointerException}) = \text{false} \quad (\text{unchecked}) $$ ### Step 5: The `throws` Keyword Propagation When a method declares `throws X`, the rule cascades upward: $$ \text{Method}_A() \;\xrightarrow{\texttt{throws X}}\; \text{Method}_B() \;\xrightarrow{\texttt{throws X}}\; \text{Method}_C() $$ Each method in the call chain must either: 1. Add `throws X` to its own signature (delegate upward), OR 2. Wrap the call in a `try-catch(X e)` block (handle locally). The obligation terminates only at a `try-catch` block or at `main()` (after which the JVM terminates). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Protected Access Resolution Algorithm The compiler resolves a protected member access using the following decision logic: $$ \text{Access}(M, C, T) = \begin{cases} \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } C \equiv T \\ \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \text{Package}(M) \equiv \text{Package}(C) \\ \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } T \text{ inherits from } M\text{'s class AND accessed via } T\text{'s type} \\ \text{DENY} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Where: - $M$ = the member being accessed - $C$ = the accessing class - $T$ = the reference type used for access - $\text{Package}(X)$ = the package containing class $X$ ### Step-by-Step Application **Case 1:** Same package, any reference type → ALLOW (package visibility). **Case 2:** Different package, subclass, accessed via subclass reference → ALLOW. **Case 3:** Different package, subclass, accessed via superclass reference → DENY. **Case 4:** Different package, non-subclass → DENY. This explains why Java's protected access is **context-sensitive** — the same code can compile or fail to compile based on the **reference type** used to access the member. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Combining Both Topics | Topic | Enforcement Level | Failure Consequence | OOP Principle | |-------|:-----------------:|:-------------------:|---------------| | `protected` | Compile-time | Compilation error | Encapsulation + Extensibility | | Checked Exceptions | Compile-time | Compilation error | Robust error handling | | `RuntimeException` | Runtime | Program crash (if unhandled) | Indicates programmer error | | `Error` | Runtime | Program termination | System-level fatal failure | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Java Exception Class Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD node1["Throwable"] node2["Exception"] node3["Error"] node4["IOException"] node5["SQLException"] node6["ClassNotFoundException"] node7["RuntimeException"] node8["NullPointerException"] node9["ArithmeticException"] node10["ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"] node11["OutOfMemoryError"] node12["StackOverflowError"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node2 --> node4 node2 --> node5 node2 --> node6 node2 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node7 --> node9 node7 --> node10 node3 --> node11 node3 --> node12 classDef checked fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef unchecked fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef error fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef root fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class node1 root class node2,node4,node5,node6 checked class node7,node8,node9,node10 unchecked class node3,node11,node12 error ``` > [!NOTE] > **Color Legend:** Blue = Root, Yellow = Checked Exceptions, Green = Unchecked Exceptions (RuntimeException family), Red = Error family. --- ## 4.2 `protected` Access Visibility Matrix ```mermaid graph LR subgraph pkgA["Package A"] classA["Class A
protected int data"] end subgraph pkgB["Package B"] classB["Class B
Non-Subclass"] classC["Class C
extends Class A
SUBCLASS"] end classA -.->|"SAME PACKAGE: ALLOW"| classA classA -.->|"DIFFERENT PKG, SUBCLASS via inheritance: ALLOW"| classC classA -.->|"DIFFERENT PKG, NON-SUBCLASS: DENY"| classB classC -.->|"DIFFERENT PKG, via superclass ref: DENY"| classA classDef allowed fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef denied fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef neutral fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class classA,classC neutral class classB denied ``` --- ## 4.3 Checked Exception Handling Flow — `try-catch-finally` Execution ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Method Entry"]) --> tryBlock["Execute try block"] tryBlock --> checkError{"Exception
thrown?"} checkError -->|NO| finallyBlock["Execute finally block"] checkError -->|YES, matches catch| catchBlock["Execute matching catch block"] checkError -->|YES, no match| propagate["Propagate to caller
via throws"] catchBlock --> finallyBlock finallyBlock --> end1(["Method Exit Normally"]) propagate --> callerCheck{"Caller has
try-catch or throws?"} callerCheck -->|YES, try-catch| catchBlock callerCheck -->|YES, throws| propagate callerCheck -->|NO| jvmCrash["Uncaught Exception
JVM terminates"] classDef normal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef error fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF class start,end1,finallyBlock,catchBlock normal class checkError,callerCheck decision class propagate,jvmCrash error ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`** > **Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception | |---------|:-----------------:|:-------------------:| | Parent class | Direct subclass of `Exception` | Subclass of `RuntimeException` | | Compile-time check | **Yes — compiler enforces handling** | No — only occurs at runtime | | Handling requirement | Must use `try-catch` or `throws` | Optional | | Indicates | External/resource failures | Programmer logic errors | | Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException` | --- ### Question A2 > **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]`** > **Explain the `protected` access specifier in Java. Can a `protected` member be accessed from a non-subclass in a different package? Justify.** **Model Answer:** The `protected` access specifier allows a member to be accessed: 1. **Within the same package** by any class (package-private behavior included). 2. **From subclasses in any package** via inheritance. A `protected` member **cannot** be accessed from a non-subclass residing in a different package, regardless of the reference type used. This is because non-subclasses lack both the package-level and the inheritance-based privilege required. **Example Justification:** ```java package p1; public class Super { protected int x = 10; } ``` ```java package p2; import p1.Super; public class Other { // NOT a subclass of Super Super s = new Super(); // System.out.println(s.x); // COMPILE ERROR: x has protected access } ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **"Explain the Java Exception Handling mechanism in detail. With suitable code examples, describe how checked exceptions are handled using `try-catch-finally` and `throws` clauses."** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Exception Handling Mechanism with `try-catch-finally` **Model Answer:** Java's exception handling is built upon five keywords: `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws`. The mechanism works through **object propagation**: when an error occurs, the JVM creates an exception object and hands it to the runtime system, which searches the call stack for a handler. **Code Example:** ```java import java.io.*; public class FileReadDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { BufferedReader br = null; try { br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("data.txt")); String line = br.readLine(); System.out.println("Read: " + line); } catch (FileNotFoundException e) { System.out.println("Error: File not found - " + e.getMessage()); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("Error: I/O failure - " + e.getMessage()); } finally { try { if (br != null) br.close(); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("Error closing stream."); } System.out.println("Cleanup complete in finally block."); } } } ``` **Explanation:** 1. The `try` block contains risky code: file opening and reading. `FileNotFoundException` and `IOException` are **checked**, so they **must** be handled. 2. The first `catch` block handles `FileNotFoundException` (a subclass of `IOException`) — placed before the generic `IOException` catch because Java matches `catch` blocks in order. 3. The second `catch` block handles any other I/O issues. 4. The `finally` block executes **regardless** of whether an exception occurred — used here for resource cleanup (`br.close()`). #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Using the `throws` Clause **Model Answer:** The `throws` keyword **declares** that a method may propagate an exception, transferring the handling responsibility to the caller. It does NOT handle the exception itself. **Code Example:** ```java import java.io.*; public class ThrowsDemo { // Declaring checked exception in method signature static void readFile(String path) throws FileNotFoundException, IOException { BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader(path)); String line = br.readLine(); System.out.println(line); br.close(); } public static void main(String[] args) { try { readFile("input.txt"); } catch (FileNotFoundException e) { System.out.println("File missing: " + e.getMessage()); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("I/O error: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Explanation:** 1. The `readFile()` method declares `throws FileNotFoundException, IOException`. The caller (`main`) is now **obligated** to handle them. 2. `main()` chooses to handle both via a multi-catch sequence. 3. `throws` is preferred when the method **cannot meaningfully recover** from the failure and the caller has more context. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **"Discuss the various access specifiers in Java with emphasis on the `protected` specifier. Illustrate with examples how `protected` differs from default and `public` access. Also explain checked exceptions with examples."** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Access Specifiers in Java **Model Answer:** Java provides **four** access levels (three explicit specifiers + default), each controlling the visibility of classes, methods, and fields. **Comparison Table:** | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Other Pkg) | Other Classes | |----------|:----------:|:------------:|:--------------------:|:-------------:| | `private` | Yes | No | No | No | | default | Yes | Yes | No | No | | `protected` | Yes | Yes | Yes (via inheritance) | No | | `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Code Example Demonstrating `protected`:** ```java package university.dept; public class Employee { protected String name = "Dr. Sharma"; protected double salary = 75000.0; protected void displayDetails() { System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Salary: " + salary); } } ``` ```java package university.admin; // DIFFERENT package import university.dept.Employee; public class HRAdmin extends Employee { // SUBCLASS public void showInherited() { System.out.println("Inherited name: " + name); // OK via inheritance displayDetails(); // OK via inheritance } } class Outsider { // NOT a subclass public void attempt() { Employee e = new Employee(); // System.out.println(e.name); // COMPILE ERROR } } ``` **Key Insight:** The `protected` modifier strikes a **balance between encapsulation and extensibility** — internal helpers can be exposed to subclass implementors across package boundaries, but hidden from unrelated classes. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Checked Exceptions Explained **Model Answer:** A **checked exception** is a subclass of `java.lang.Exception` that is **not** a subclass of `RuntimeException`. The Java compiler enforces mandatory handling at compile time. This forces developers to anticipate and plan for realistic failure scenarios like file errors, network drops, and database failures. **Three Real-World Examples:** 1. **`IOException`** — Thrown when an I/O operation fails. Common with file, network, and stream operations. 2. **`ClassNotFoundException`** — Thrown when the JVM tries to load a class dynamically (via `Class.forName()`) and the class is not found on the classpath. 3. **`SQLException`** — Thrown when a database access error occurs (invalid query, lost connection, constraint violation). **Handling Demonstration:** ```java import java.sql.*; public class DatabaseDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { try { Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver"); // throws ClassNotFoundException Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection( // throws SQLException "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/test", "user", "pass"); Statement stmt = con.createStatement(); // throws SQLException ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students"); while (rs.next()) { System.out.println(rs.getString("name")); } con.close(); } catch (ClassNotFoundException e) { System.out.println("JDBC Driver not found: " + e.getMessage()); } catch (SQLException e) { System.out.println("Database error: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `RuntimeException` with `Exception`:** Writing "RuntimeException is a checked exception" will cost full marks. `RuntimeException` is the parent of **unchecked** exceptions. > 2. **Forgetting to handle subclass-before-parent ordering:** Writing `catch (Exception e)` before `catch (IOException e)` causes an **unreachable code compile error**. > 3. **Thinking `throws` handles the exception:** `throws` only **declares** propagation; it does NOT catch or recover. > 4. **Treating `protected` as `public`:** Believing that ANY class can access a `protected` member in a different package. The access requires **subclass inheritance** AND access via the subclass reference type. > 5. **Forgetting `finally` always executes:** Even if `System.exit(0)` is called inside `try`, `finally` may NOT execute — this is a classic KTU trick question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **`protected`** = package access + inheritance access (across packages). NOT same as `public`. - **`protected`** accessed via **superclass reference** from a subclass in another package → **DENIED**. - **Checked Exceptions** inherit from `java.lang.Exception` but **NOT** from `RuntimeException`. - **Unchecked Exceptions** = subclasses of `RuntimeException` (e.g., `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException`). - The **compiler** enforces handling for checked exceptions — failure to do so causes a **compile-time error**. - Five exception-handling keywords: **`try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, `throws`**. - `try-catch` **handles** an exception; `throws` **declares** propagation to the caller. - `finally` block executes **always** (except on `System.exit()` or JVM crash). - Java exception hierarchy root: **`java.lang.Throwable`** → branches into `Exception` (recoverable) and `Error` (fatal). - `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `InterruptedException`, `ParseException` are the **most commonly tested** checked exceptions. - `OutOfMemoryError` and `StackOverflowError` are **`Error`** subclasses — not `Exception` — and represent unrecoverable system failures.

topics.title: Unchecked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unchecked Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Java Exception Handling Hierarchy**, an **Unchecked Exception** is defined as any exception class that directly or indirectly extends `java.lang.RuntimeException` (or `java.lang.Error`). The Java compiler does **not** enforce mandatory handling (try-catch) or declaration (throws clause) for these exceptions at compile-time. This is why they are termed "unchecked" — the verification responsibility is deferred to the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** which checks them only at **runtime**.

The formal KTU 2024 Scheme definition states:
