> *"Unchecked exceptions are the exceptions that occur at the time of execution (runtime). They are also called Runtime Exceptions. These include programming bugs, such as logic errors or improper use of an API. The compiler does not check these exceptions at compile-time."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unchecked exceptions belong to the class `java.lang.RuntimeException` and its subclasses. All other exceptions in Java are classified as **Checked Exceptions** (covered under `java.lang.Exception` but not `RuntimeException`).

## 1.2 Class Hierarchy Snapshot

The complete inheritance chain of Unchecked Exceptions in Java's `java.lang` package follows this strict hierarchy:

```
java.lang.Object
        ↑
java.lang.Throwable
        ↑
   ┌────┴─────────────────┐
   │                      │
java.lang.Exception    java.lang.Error
   │                      │
java.lang.RuntimeException  (Unchecked Boundary Starts Here)
   │
   ├── ArithmeticException
   ├── NullPointerException
   ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
   ├── StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
   ├── IndexOutOfBoundsException
   ├── ClassCastException
   ├── IllegalArgumentException
   ├── NumberFormatException
   ├── IllegalStateException
   ├── UnsupportedOperationException
   └── NoSuchElementException
```

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Loose Wire" in Your House**
> Imagine an electrician installs wiring in your house. **Checked Exceptions** are like the **safety inspections** the government mandates BEFORE you move in — you literally cannot occupy the house legally until the inspector signs off (the compiler enforces it). **Unchecked Exceptions** are like a **loose wire behind the refrigerator** — nobody checks it during installation, and your house will pass inspection. However, the moment you push the fridge back and accidentally tug that wire, a **short circuit (RuntimeException)** occurs. The problem was *always* there — it just wasn't *detected* until actual operation (runtime). As a programmer, your job is to write defensive code so that even if the inspector (compiler) doesn't look, no short circuits occur during execution.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Cheat Sheet

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Checked Exception** | Compile-time verified, must be handled or declared |
| **Unchecked Exception** | Runtime-detected, NOT mandatory to handle/declare |
| **Error** | Serious JVM/system-level failure (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) |
| **RuntimeException** | Parent class of all unchecked exceptions |
| **Throwable** | Root class of all exceptions and errors in Java |
| **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** | The runtime engine that throws unchecked exceptions |

## 1.5 Why "Unchecked"? — The Design Rationale

James Gosling and the Java design team intentionally made these exceptions **unchecked** because:

1. **They are largely preventable** through careful coding (e.g., null checks, bounds checking).
2. **Mandatory handling would clutter code** with defensive `try-catch` blocks for theoretically possible (but practically rare) errors.
3. **They represent programmer mistakes**, not external environmental issues.
4. **API design freedom** — methods can declare many runtime exceptions without burdening every caller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unchecked Exceptions CAN still be caught** using `try-catch` blocks — the word "unchecked" refers only to the **compiler's lack of enforcement**, not to the inability of the program to handle them at runtime.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

When a Java program executes, the following internal mechanism governs unchecked exceptions:

1. **Bytecode Compilation:** The Java compiler (`javac`) translates source `.java` files into `.class` bytecode. During this phase, the compiler performs a flow analysis to identify all statements that *might* throw a **checked** exception. For unchecked exceptions, this analysis is **skipped entirely** — no error is reported.

2. **Class Loading:** The JVM's **ClassLoader** subsystem loads the `.class` file. No exception verification is performed at this stage.

3. **Bytecode Verification:** The **Bytecode Verifier** ensures type safety and stack integrity but does not mandate exception handling for `RuntimeException` subclasses.

4. **Execution Phase (Runtime):** When an actual exceptional condition arises (e.g., dividing by zero, accessing a null reference), the JVM:
   - **Allocates** a new exception object on the heap.
   - **Captures the stack trace** (the chain of method calls leading to the failure).
   - **Throws** the exception object using the `athrow` JVM bytecode instruction.
   - **Unwinds the call stack** looking for the nearest matching `catch` block.
   - If no handler is found, the **default exception handler** terminates the thread (or the main program) and prints the stack trace to `System.err`.

## 2.2 Core Unchecked Exception Types — Detailed Catalog

### 2.2.1 `ArithmeticException`
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** An exceptional arithmetic condition occurs (integer division by zero).
- **Note:** Floating-point division by zero in Java produces `Infinity` or `NaN`, **NOT** an `ArithmeticException`.
- **Example Trigger:** `int result = 10 / 0;`

### 2.2.2 `NullPointerException` (NPE)
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** An application attempts to use `null` where an object is required (method invocation on null, field access on null, array access on null, etc.).
- **Example Trigger:** `String s = null; s.length();`
- **Most Common Java Exception:** Historically, NPE is the single most thrown exception in Java programs.

### 2.2.3 `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`
- **Parent:** `IndexOutOfBoundsException` → `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** An array is accessed with an illegal index (negative or ≥ length).
- **Example Trigger:** `int[] arr = new int[5]; arr[10] = 1;`

### 2.2.4 `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`
- **Parent:** `IndexOutOfBoundsException` → `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** A `String` index is out of range.
- **Example Trigger:** `"Hello".charAt(100);`

### 2.2.5 `ClassCastException`
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** An attempt is made to cast an object to a subclass type of which it is not an instance.
- **Example Trigger:** `Object o = "Hello"; Integer i = (Integer) o;`

### 2.2.6 `IllegalArgumentException`
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** A method has been passed an illegal or inappropriate argument.
- **Example Trigger:** `Thread.sleep(-100);`

### 2.2.7 `NumberFormatException`
- **Parent:** `IllegalArgumentException` → `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** An attempt to convert a string to a numeric type fails.
- **Example Trigger:** `int x = Integer.parseInt("abc");`

### 2.2.8 `IllegalStateException`
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** A method is invoked at an illegal or inappropriate time (object state is incompatible with the operation).
- **Example Trigger:** Iterating a `List` after calling `Iterator.remove()` outside proper protocol.

### 2.2.9 `UnsupportedOperationException`
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** An operation is not supported (e.g., attempting to modify an immutable collection).
- **Example Trigger:** Modifying a list returned by `Collections.unmodifiableList()`.

### 2.2.10 `NoSuchElementException`
- **Parent:** `RuntimeException`
- **Thrown When:** The next element does not exist (e.g., calling `next()` on an exhausted `Iterator`).

## 2.3 The Three Trigger Categories — Why Unchecked Exceptions Occur

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic Errors** | Programmer's algorithmic mistake | Off-by-one loop boundary causing `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **API Misuse** | Incorrect usage of library method | `Integer.parseInt("hello")` causing `NumberFormatException` |
| **State Violations** | Object in invalid state for the operation | Calling `next()` on empty `Scanner` causing `NoSuchElementException` |

## 2.4 Checked vs. Unchecked — Comparative Matrix

| Property | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---|---|---|
| **Parent Class** | `java.lang.Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| **Compiler Enforcement** | **YES** — must handle or declare | **NO** — handling is optional |
| **Detection Time** | Compile-time | Runtime |
| **Typical Cause** | External resources (I/O, network, DB) | Programmer logic errors |
| **Recoverable?** | Usually yes | Often preventable via code review |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | Always appears in `throws` clause | Never mandatory in `throws` clause |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hierarchy Rule** | Unchecked ⟹ extends `RuntimeException` | `class MyException extends RuntimeException` |
| **Catch Order** | Subclass `catch` MUST come BEFORE superclass `catch` | `catch(ArithmeticException e)` before `catch(RuntimeException e)` |
| **Compile-time Check** | `RuntimeException` subclasses → NOT checked | `int x = 10/0;` compiles fine |
| **Runtime Check** | `RuntimeException` subclasses → checked by JVM | Above statement throws at runtime |
| **Throws Clause** | NOT required for unchecked | `void m() { ... }` is valid even if `ArithmeticException` can occur |
| **Custom Unchecked** | Extend `RuntimeException` (or subclass) | `class MyBug extends RuntimeException` |
| **Rethrow Rule** | Can rethrow unchecked without declaring | `throw new NullPointerException();` anywhere |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application of Unchecked Exceptions:**
> - **Spring Framework** uses `IllegalArgumentException` extensively for validation failures in REST controllers.
> - **Hibernate ORM** throws `StaleStateException` (an unchecked exception) when optimistic locking fails.
> - **Microservices** propagate unchecked exceptions up the call stack and convert them to appropriate **HTTP status codes** (e.g., `NullPointerException` → 500 Internal Server Error).
> - **JUnit Testing** uses `AssertionError` to signal test failures — `AssertionError` is unchecked.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exception Propagation — Exhaustive Derivation

Consider the following Java source code demonstrating unchecked exception propagation across a multi-method call chain:

```java
public class ExceptionPropagation {
    static void method3() {
        int result = 10 / 0;   // Line A: ArithmeticException thrown here
    }

    static void method2() {
        method3();              // No try-catch, exception propagates upward
    }

    static void method1() {
        method2();              // No try-catch, exception propagates upward
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        method1();              // No try-catch, JVM default handler prints stack trace
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Stack Unwinding Derivation

**Step 1 — Compilation Phase:**
The Java compiler (`javac`) analyzes every statement in the four methods. It notes that Line A (`10 / 0`) involves integer division. However, since `ArithmeticException` is an unchecked exception (extends `RuntimeException`), the compiler does **not** report any error. The program **compiles successfully**.

```
javac ExceptionPropagation.java  →  No errors.  ExceptionPropagation.class generated.
```

**Step 2 — Loading Phase:**
The JVM ClassLoader loads `ExceptionPropagation.class` into the method area. No exception verification occurs at this stage.

**Step 3 — Execution Initiation:**
The JVM invokes `main()`. The call stack at this moment:

| Stack Slot | Method |
|---|---|
| Slot 3 | `main()` |
| Slot 2 | `method1()` |
| Slot 1 | `method2()` |
| Slot 0 | `method3()` |

**Step 4 — Exception Trigger:**
When `method3()` reaches Line A, the JVM executes the integer division `10 / 0`. The arithmetic logic unit (ALU) detects division by zero. The JVM:

- Allocates a new `ArithmeticException` object: `new ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`
- Captures the current stack trace: `[main, method1, method2, method3]`
- Executes the `athrow` bytecode instruction.

**Step 5 — Handler Search in `method3()`:**
The JVM scans `method3()`'s exception table. There is no `try-catch` block. No matching handler. **Unwind method3() from the stack.**

**Step 6 — Handler Search in `method2()`:**
The JVM returns to `method2()` at the call site of `method3()`. The JVM scans `method2()`'s exception table. No `try-catch`. **Unwind method2() from the stack.**

**Step 7 — Handler Search in `method1()`:**
The JVM returns to `method1()`. No `try-catch` present. **Unwind method1() from the stack.**

**Step 8 — Handler Search in `main()`:**
The JVM returns to `main()`. No `try-catch` present. **Unwind main() from the stack.**

**Step 9 — Default Handler:**
The JVM invokes the **default uncaught exception handler** (`ThreadGroup.uncaughtException()`). It prints:

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
    at ExceptionPropagation.method3(ExceptionPropagation.java:3)
    at ExceptionPropagation.method2(ExceptionPropagation.java:7)
    at ExceptionPropagation.method1(ExceptionPropagation.java:11)
    at ExceptionPropagation.main(ExceptionPropagation.java:15)
```

The program **terminates abnormally**.

## 3.2 Mathematical Model of Exception Propagation

We can express the stack unwinding process formally. Let $S = \{m_0, m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$ represent the call stack at the moment an exception is thrown, where $m_0$ is the throwing method and $m_n$ is `main()`. The JVM's search algorithm is:

$$
H(e) = \begin{cases}
m_i & \text{if } m_i \text{ contains a matching catch block for exception } e \\
\text{next}(S) & \text{if } m_i \text{ has no matching handler} \\
\text{DefaultHandler} & \text{if } S = \emptyset
\end{cases}
$$

Where the matching condition is:

$$
\text{Match}(e, c) = \begin{cases}
\text{true} & \text{if } c \text{ is the same class as } e \text{ or a superclass of } e \\
\text{false} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The unwinding continues until a match is found:

$$
\text{UnwindChain}(e) = \{m_0 \rightarrow m_1 \rightarrow m_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow m_k\}
$$

where $k$ is the smallest index such that $\text{Match}(e, m_k.\text{handler}) = \text{true}$, or $k = n$ if no handler is found.

## 3.3 The "Catch Order" Derivation — Why Subclass First?

Consider this code:

```java
try {
    int[] arr = new int[5];
    arr[10] = 50;  // Throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("Generic Exception caught");
} catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {  // COMPILE ERROR
    System.out.println("Specific Exception caught");
}
```

**Derivation of Compile Error:**

The Java compiler analyzes the catch blocks in top-to-bottom order. It builds an exception-handling match table:

$$
\text{HandlerTable} = \{(\text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException}, \text{Generic}), (\text{Exception}, \text{Specific})\}
$$

But because of **subtype polymorphism**, any `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is *also* an `Exception` (transitively). The first catch block (`catch (Exception e)`) can therefore handle *any* exception, including the more specific `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. The second catch block is **unreachable** — a compile-time error called "exception `java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` has already been caught."

**Mathematical Formulation of Unreachability:**

Let $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ be the catch block types in order. The compiler enforces:

$$
\forall i < j : C_i \not\subseteq C_j
$$

That is, no earlier catch block type may be a supertype of a later one. If $C_i \supseteq C_j$, the compiler reports an **unreachable catch block error**.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Exception Selection Guide

| Situation | Recommended Exception | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Division by zero in integer arithmetic | `ArithmeticException` | Built-in, automatic |
| Calling method on null reference | `NullPointerException` | Built-in, automatic |
| Array index out of range | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Built-in, automatic |
| Invalid string-to-number conversion | `NumberFormatException` | More specific than `IllegalArgumentException` |
| Method passed invalid argument | `IllegalArgumentException` | Generic parameter validation |
| Object in wrong state for operation | `IllegalStateException` | State-related, not argument-related |
| Custom business logic violation | Extend `RuntimeException` | Predefined contract for unchecked |
| External resource failure (DB, file) | Use **Checked** exception instead | Not a programming bug |

## 3.5 The `finally` Block Interaction with Unchecked Exceptions

The `finally` block executes **regardless** of whether an unchecked exception is thrown or caught. This is governed by the JLS (Java Language Specification) §14.20.2.

```java
public class FinallyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int x = 10 / 0;  // ArithmeticException
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
            return;          // Even with return, finally executes
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block executed");
        }
        System.out.println("End of method");  // NOT reached because of return
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Caught: / by zero
Finally block executed
```

**Derivation of the `finally` Execution Invariant:**

Let $P$ be the program point of `try` entry, $C$ the catch handler, and $F$ the finally block. The execution path is:

$$
\text{Result}(P) = F \circ (\text{ExceptionThrown} \vee \text{NormalFlow} \vee \text{Return})
$$

In all three cases, $F$ executes. The only way to *skip* the finally block is `System.exit()` or a JVM crash (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define unchecked exceptions in Java. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Unchecked exceptions in Java are exceptions that are **not checked at compile-time** by the Java compiler. They occur at **runtime** and belong to the class hierarchy rooted at `java.lang.RuntimeException` (or `java.lang.Error`). The compiler does not mandate that they be caught using a `try-catch` block or declared in a `throws` clause. They typically represent **programming logic errors** or **API misuse** rather than external resource failures.

**Two Examples:**
1. **`ArithmeticException`** — Thrown when an exceptional arithmetic condition occurs, such as integer division by zero (e.g., `10 / 0`).
2. **`NullPointerException`** — Thrown when an application attempts to use a `null` reference where an object is required, such as calling a method on a `null` String (`str.length()` when `str == null`).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: How are unchecked exceptions different from checked exceptions? List any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---|---|---|
| **Hierarchy** | Extends `java.lang.Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | Extends `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| **Compile-time Check** | The compiler **enforces** handling or declaration | The compiler **does not enforce** handling |
| **Detection Time** | Detected at **compile-time** | Detected at **runtime** by the JVM |
| **Typical Cause** | External factors (I/O, network, database) | Programmer errors (logic bugs, API misuse) |
| **Throws Clause** | **Mandatory** in method signature if not handled | **Optional**, never required |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |

Any three of the above differences earn full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse `Error` with `Exception`. Remember: `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`) is a **separate hierarchy** under `Throwable` and is also unchecked, but it represents **JVM-level failures**, not application logic errors.
> 2. When listing exceptions, students sometimes write `IOException` as an unchecked exception — this is **WRONG**. `IOException` is a **checked** exception.
> 3. Always mention the **parent class** (`RuntimeException`) in your definition to earn full marks.
> 4. In code questions, students forget the `main()` method signature or omit class declaration — these cost 1-2 marks in KTU valuation.
> 5. For multi-part questions, ensure each sub-part (a) and (b) is answered with **equal depth** (7 marks each). Examiners strictly enforce mark distribution.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Java exception class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Distinguish between checked and unchecked exceptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program that demonstrates the handling of `ArithmeticException` and `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. Show what happens when neither is caught.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Java Exception Class Hierarchy:**

The root of Java's exception hierarchy is `java.lang.Throwable`, which has two direct subclasses: `java.lang.Exception` and `java.lang.Error`. The `Exception` class is further divided into `RuntimeException` (unchecked) and other checked exceptions. The `Error` class represents serious JVM-level problems (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) and is also unchecked.

```
java.lang.Object
        ↑
java.lang.Throwable
        |
   _____|_____
  |           |
Exception   Error  (Both unchecked at compile-time, but Exception is recoverable)
  |
RuntimeException  ←── Unchecked boundary
  |
  ├── ArithmeticException
  ├── NullPointerException
  ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
  ├── StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
  ├── ClassCastException
  ├── IllegalArgumentException
  └── NumberFormatException
```

**Distinction Table:**

| Property | Checked | Unchecked |
|---|---|---|
| Parent | `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `RuntimeException` or `Error` |
| Compiler check | Yes | No |
| Recovery | Usually recoverable (e.g., retry I/O) | Usually preventable (fix code) |
| Example | `IOException` | `NullPointerException` |

**(b) Java Program Demonstrating Unchecked Exception Handling:**

```java
public class UncheckedDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Demonstration 1: ArithmeticException
        try {
            int a = 10;
            int b = 0;
            int result = a / b;  // Throws ArithmeticException
            System.out.println("Result: " + result);  // Unreachable
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("ArithmeticException caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // Demonstration 2: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        try {
            int[] arr = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
            System.out.println(arr[10]);  // Throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // Demonstration 3: No catch block (default handler)
        // Uncomment to see program termination
        // String s = null;
        // System.out.println(s.length());  // NullPointerException, NOT caught
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
ArithmeticException caught: / by zero
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException caught: Index 10 out of bounds for length 5
```

**What happens when neither is caught:**
If we remove the `catch` blocks or comment out the third demonstration, the JVM's default uncaught exception handler is invoked. It prints the exception class name, message, and the full stack trace to `System.err`, then terminates the program abnormally with a non-zero exit code.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is exception propagation? Explain with an example involving three methods where an unchecked exception is thrown in the deepest method and propagates to `main()`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the use of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws` keywords in Java with suitable code snippets for each.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Exception Propagation:**

Exception propagation is the mechanism by which an exception, when not caught in the method where it occurs, is **passed (propagated) up the call stack** to the calling method, and so on, until either a matching `catch` block is found or the default handler terminates the program.

**Three-Method Example:**

```java
public class PropagationDemo {
    // Deepest method — throws the exception
    static void deepestMethod() {
        int[] numbers = {1, 2, 3};
        System.out.println(numbers[5]);  // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
    }

    // Intermediate method — does NOT catch
    static void middleMethod() {
        System.out.println("In middleMethod, calling deepestMethod...");
        deepestMethod();
        System.out.println("This line will NOT execute");
    }

    // Top-level method — does NOT catch
    static void topMethod() {
        System.out.println("In topMethod, calling middleMethod...");
        middleMethod();
        System.out.println("This line will NOT execute");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            topMethod();
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Exception caught in main: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues normally after handling");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
In topMethod, calling middleMethod...
In middleMethod, calling deepestMethod...
Exception caught in main: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Program continues normally after handling
```

**Propagation Path:** `deepestMethod() → middleMethod() → topMethod() → main()` (caught here).

**(b) Five Keywords Explained:**

**1. `try`:** Defines a block of code that might throw an exception. Must be followed by at least one `catch` or `finally` block.
```java
try {
    int result = 10 / 0;
}
```

**2. `catch`:** Catches and handles a specific exception type. Multiple catch blocks allowed (subclass first).
```java
catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero");
}
```

**3. `finally`:** Block that always executes, regardless of exception occurrence. Used for cleanup (closing files, releasing resources).
```java
finally {
    System.out.println("Cleanup code runs here");
}
```

**4. `throw`:** Used to explicitly throw an exception object (instance of `Throwable`).
```java
throw new IllegalArgumentException("Age cannot be negative");
```

**5. `throws`:** Declares exceptions that a method might throw, used in the method signature. Mandatory for **checked** exceptions, optional for unchecked.
```java
public void readFile() throws IOException {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
}
```

**Combined Example:**
```java
public void validateAge(int age) throws IllegalArgumentException {
    if (age < 0) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid age: " + age);
    }
    try {
        System.out.println("Valid age: " + age);
    } finally {
        System.out.println("Validation complete");
    }
}
```

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unchecked Exceptions**

- ✅ **Unchecked exceptions** extend `java.lang.RuntimeException` (or `java.lang.Error`).
- ✅ The **compiler does NOT enforce** try-catch or throws for unchecked exceptions.
- ✅ They are detected **at runtime** by the JVM.
- ✅ They typically represent **programmer errors** — logic bugs, API misuse, invalid state.
- ✅ **Most common examples:** `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `ClassCastException`, `NumberFormatException`, `IllegalArgumentException`.
- ✅ Unchecked exceptions **CAN be caught** — the word "unchecked" only means the compiler does not verify them.
- ✅ In multiple `catch` blocks, the **most specific (subclass) must come first**, or compile error occurs.
- ✅ **Exception propagation** is automatic — the JVM unwinds the call stack searching for a matching handler.
- ✅ The `finally` block **always executes** (except on `System.exit()` or JVM crash).
- ✅ **Custom unchecked exceptions** are created by extending `RuntimeException`.
- ✅ `Error` and `RuntimeException` are both unchecked, but they are **separate hierarchies** — do not confuse them.
- ✅ Floating-point division by zero in Java produces **`Infinity` or `NaN`**, NOT an `ArithmeticException`.
- ✅ Use unchecked exceptions for **preconditions** (invalid arguments, illegal states) and checked exceptions for **external resource failures** (I/O, database, network).
- ✅ KTU exam keyword: when defining unchecked exceptions, **always mention the parent class** `RuntimeException` for full marks.

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unchecked Exceptions

topics.title: try Block and catch Clause
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** try Block and catch Clause

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — try Block and catch Clause

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Alignment):**
> In Java's exception-handling framework, the **`try` block** is a guarded region of code in which an exception (a runtime anomaly or error condition) may occur. The associated **`catch` clause** is a handler that intercepts, receives, and processes the exception object thrown by the JVM or the `throw` statement inside the `try` block.

### 1.1 What is a `try` Block?

A `try` block is a **compounded statement** (a block of code enclosed in braces `{ }`) that encloses statements which might generate an exception. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) monitors this region. If any statement inside it causes an abnormal condition, normal sequential execution is **aborted** at that exact line, and control is transferred to the appropriate `catch` handler.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Term — "Guarded Region"**
> A `try` block is also referred to in examiner's keys as a *guarded region* or *protected region*. Memorise this term — it appears frequently in 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

**Generalised Syntax:**

```java
try {
    // statements that may raise an exception
    // (called the "guarded" or "protected" body)
}
```

### 1.2 What is a `catch` Clause?

A `catch` clause (also called an **exception handler**) is a parameterised block that immediately follows a `try` block. It declares a single parameter — the **exception type** it can handle — and provides recovery code. It is executed **only if** an exception of the matching type (or one of its subclasses) is thrown from the preceding `try` block.

**Generalised Syntax:**

```java
catch (ExceptionType1 identifier) {
    // recovery / logging / rethrow logic
}
```

The identifier (commonly `e`, `ex`, or `err`) holds a reference to the exception object, allowing the programmer to invoke methods like `getMessage()` and `printStackTrace()`.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Insurance Booth 🛟

Imagine you are walking through a **dark forest path** (your program's normal execution flow). The path is unpredictable — you might step on a **thorn**, encounter a **fallen log**, or slip into a **ditch**. Each hazard is an *exception*.

- The **`try` block** = the **marked-out path** you are walking on. The forest warden (the JVM) actively watches every step you take inside this path.
- The **`catch` clause** = a **first-aid booth** placed right at the end of the marked path. Each booth specialises in a particular injury — one for thorn wounds (`ArithmeticException`), one for broken bones (`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`), and so on.
- The **exception object** = the **injury report card** the warden hands to the booth keeper, describing exactly what went wrong.

If you walk the entire path without an incident, you skip the booth. If an incident occurs, the warden **stops you immediately**, hands your injury report to the right booth, and the booth nurse treats you. After treatment, you continue your journey — or the program terminates gracefully.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for KTU students:** Without a `try-catch`, a single thorn on the path would make the entire trip collapse (program crash). With `try-catch`, only the affected step is rerouted, and the rest of the journey is preserved.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Guarded region** | Code inside `try { }` |
| **Exception handler** | Code inside `catch { }` |
| **Exception object** | Instance of `Throwable` subclass carrying error info |
| **Throw point** | Exact line where the exception was generated |
| **Catch parameter** | The variable declared in `catch (Type e)` |
| **Stack unwinding** | JVM process of removing frames until handler is found |
| **Polymorphic catch** | A catch of a superclass type catches subclass exceptions too |

### 1.5 Mandatory Rules of the `try-catch` Construct

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Frequency Definition Question:**
> **State the rules governing the use of `try` and `catch` blocks in Java.**
>
> 1. A `try` block **must** be followed by **at least one** `catch` block **or** a `finally` block (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3).
> 2. A `try` block **cannot stand alone** — it is meaningless without a handler or a `finally`.
> 3. The `catch` parameter type must be a `Throwable` subclass (or `Throwable` itself).
> 4. Multiple `catch` blocks are allowed, but **only one** is executed per exception.
> 5. The **most specific** `catch` (subclass) **must appear before** the more general one (superclass). Otherwise → **compile-time error** ("exception ... has already been caught").
> 6. A `try` block **cannot** be declared without enclosing braces `{ }` even for a single statement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Model — How `try-catch` Executes

The control flow inside a `try-catch` mechanism follows a deterministic, JVM-defined sequence. Understanding this sequence is essential for KTU 14-mark derivations and trace-based questions.

### Step-by-Step Control Flow

1. **Entry into `try` block** — JVM begins executing statements linearly inside `{ }`.
2. **Normal flow** — If no exception is thrown, all statements in `try` execute, and **all matching `catch` blocks are skipped**. Program continues with code after the last `catch`.
3. **Exception thrown** — If any statement raises an exception object:
   - The **remaining statements in the `try` block are skipped** (not executed).
   - The JVM performs **stack unwinding** within the `try` block itself.
   - Control transfers to the **first `catch` block whose parameter type matches** the exception (using is-a relationship / polymorphism).
4. **Matching catch executes** — The body of the matched `catch` runs. After it finishes, execution resumes **after the last `catch` block** (not back into the `try`).
5. **No matching catch** — If no handler matches, the exception **propagates up the call stack** to the calling method.
6. **`finally` block (if present)** — Executes *unconditionally* after the try or catch completes, whether an exception occurred or not.

### 2.2 The "Catch is Polymorphic" Principle

Java's exception hierarchy (rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`) means a `catch` clause with a **superclass parameter** can intercept any **subclass exception**.

**Hierarchy Snapshot:**

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.lang.Throwable
          ├── java.lang.Exception
          │      ├── java.io.IOException
          │      ├── java.lang.RuntimeException
          │      │      ├── java.lang.ArithmeticException
          │      │      ├── java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
          │      │      └── java.lang.NullPointerException
          │      └── (Checked exceptions)
          └── java.lang.Error (e.g., OutOfMemoryError — not typically caught)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `catch (Exception e)` is *valid* and will catch both checked and unchecked subclasses. However, `catch (Throwable t)` is discouraged because it also catches `Error`, which usually indicates unrecoverable JVM conditions.

### 2.3 Multi-Catch (Java 7+ — KTU 2024 Scheme includes this)

Java 7 introduced the **multi-catch** syntax, where a single `catch` can handle multiple unrelated exception types using the pipe `|` operator.

```java
try {
    // risky code
} catch (ArithmeticException | NullPointerException e) {
    System.out.println("Handled: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Rule:** In multi-catch, the types separated by `|` **must not be in an inheritance relationship** with each other (e.g., you cannot write `catch (Exception | RuntimeException e)`). This is a compile-time error.

### 2.4 Try-with-Resources (Brief — KTU 2024 Module 3 Coverage)

The `try` block supports an optional **resource specification header** in parentheses, used to auto-close resources implementing `AutoCloseable`.

```java
try (FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt")) {
    // use fr
} catch (IOException e) {
    e.printStackTrace();
}
```

This implicitly combines `try`, `catch`, and `finally` semantics, and the resource's `close()` is invoked automatically.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Construct / Rule | Behaviour | Compiler-Enforced? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `try { }` alone | **Compile-time error** — must have catch/finally | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | `try { } catch (X e) { }` | Valid; handles `X` (and subclasses) | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | `try { } catch (X e) { } catch (Y e) { }` | Multi-catch sequential; only one runs | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | Subclass catch **before** superclass | **Required** | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | Superclass catch **before** subclass | **Compile-time error** (unreachable) | ✅ Yes |
| 6 | `catch (Exception \| RuntimeException e)` | **Compile-time error** (related types) | ✅ Yes |
| 7 | `try { } finally { }` | Valid; no exception is *caught* (only cleaned) | ✅ Yes |
| 8 | `catch` without preceding `try` | **Compile-time error** | ✅ Yes |
| 9 | Multiple exceptions in single `try` | First thrown is caught; rest skipped | Runtime behaviour |
| 10 | Exception thrown in `catch` itself | Propagates further; `finally` still runs | Runtime behaviour |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production-grade software (banking, aerospace, embedded control), `try-catch` is the backbone of **fault-tolerant systems**:

- **Aerospace (Flight Control Software):** Sensor read failures are caught to switch to backup systems instead of crashing.
- **Banking (ATM Networks):** Network I/O exceptions trigger retry logic rather than locking the user out.
- **IoT & Embedded (KTU-affiliated domains):** Hardware-timeout exceptions are caught to log faults and continue with degraded functionality.

The `try-catch` model is Java's answer to the **structured exception handling (SEH)** paradigm, contrasted with C's error-code-based approach, providing **separation of normal logic from error logic** — a key OOP principle (Single Responsibility).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Control-Flow Trace — A KTU Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "Trace the output of the following Java program" appears in nearly every Module 3 paper. Always follow these five steps:
> 1. Identify the throw point.
> 2. Determine the exception type.
> 3. Match it against each `catch` in order.
> 4. Skip remaining `try` statements.
> 5. Resume after the final `catch`.

### Worked Example 1 — Single Catch (3-Mark Standard)

```java
class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int a = 10, b = 0;
            int c = a / b;                     // Step A
            System.out.println("Result: " + c); // Step B — UNREACHABLE
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e); // Step C
        }
        System.out.println("End of program");   // Step D
    }
}
```

#### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step | Action | Output / State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enter `try` block | Stack: `[main, try]` |
| 2 | `int a = 10, b = 0;` | `a=10, b=0` |
| 3 | `int c = a / b;` (Step A) | JVM throws `ArithmeticException: / by zero` at this line |
| 4 | Statement at Step B | **Skipped** — control never reaches here |
| 5 | Match `catch (ArithmeticException e)` | ✅ Exact match found |
| 6 | `e` parameter bound to exception object | `e.getMessage() = "/ by zero"` |
| 7 | `System.out.println("Caught: " + e);` (Step C) | **Output:** `Caught: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero` |
| 8 | Exit `catch`, control resumes at Step D | **Output:** `End of program` |
| 9 | `main` returns | Program ends normally |

#### Final Console Output

```
Caught: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
End of program
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Caught: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero"?**
> The default `toString()` of a `Throwable` returns `<class-name>: <message>`. The `+` operator concatenates the string with `e` (auto-invoking `toString()`). Examiner keys often require students to write the *complete* string, not just "Caught: / by zero".

### Worked Example 2 — Multiple Catches (Polymorphic Matching)

```java
class Demo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[3];
            arr[5] = 100;                                    // Throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Arithmetic block");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Array block");
        } catch (RuntimeException e) {
            System.out.println("Runtime block");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic block");
        }
    }
}
```

#### Trace Analysis

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | `arr[5] = 100;` — `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is thrown |
| 2 | `catch (ArithmeticException)` — **No match** (sibling, not parent). Skip. |
| 3 | `catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)` — **Exact match** ✅. Execute this block. |
| 4 | Remaining `catch` blocks — **skipped** (only one catch per exception). |

#### Output

```
Array block
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** If you rearranged the catches so `RuntimeException` came *before* `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, the compiler would flag:
> *"exception java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException has already been caught"*
> This is because `RuntimeException` is a *superclass* of `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. **Always place specific catches first.**

### Worked Example 3 — Exception in Catch Itself (14-Mark KTU Pattern)

```java
class Demo3 {
    static void methodA() {
        try {
            throw new NullPointerException("from A");
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("A caught NPE");
            throw new ArithmeticException("from A's catch");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            methodA();
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Main caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

#### Trace Derivation

| Order | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `main` calls `methodA()` | enters `methodA` frame |
| 2 | `throw new NullPointerException("from A");` | NPE created and thrown |
| 3 | `catch (NullPointerException e)` matches | NPE caught locally |
| 4 | `System.out.println("A caught NPE");` | **Output:** `A caught NPE` |
| 5 | `throw new ArithmeticException("from A's catch");` | new exception leaves `methodA` |
| 6 | Control returns to `main` — propagates to `main`'s `catch` | — |
| 7 | `catch (ArithmeticException e)` in `main` matches | — |
| 8 | `e.getMessage()` returns `"from A's catch"` | **Output:** `Main caught: from A's catch` |

#### Combined Final Output

```
A caught NPE
Main caught: from A's catch
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** An exception thrown from a `catch` block is **not** re-caught by sibling `catch` blocks of the same `try-catch`. It propagates to the *enclosing* `try-catch` (or up the call stack). This is tested in KTU Module 3 derivations.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for `try-catch` Scenarios

| Scenario | Behaviour | Marks Implication |
|---|---|---|
| No exception in `try` | `try` completes; all `catch` skipped | Easy 2-mark question |
| Exception thrown, matched `catch` exists | `try` exits early; matching `catch` runs | Standard 5–7 mark question |
| Exception thrown, no matching `catch` | Exception propagates to caller / terminates | Often paired with method calls |
| Exception in `catch` | Current `catch` exits; propagates further | 14-mark derivation question |
| `return` inside `try` | `finally` still runs (if present) before return | Frequently tested with `finally` |
| Nested `try-catch` | Inner handler runs first; if unmatched, outer is tried | 14-mark question |
| Polymorphic match | Subclass exception caught by superclass catch | Tests inheritance + OOP |
| `catch` rethrows (`throw e;`) | Caller's catch must handle it | Tests `throw` keyword knowledge |

### 3.3 Mathematical Analogy — Probability of Successful Completion

While Java is deterministic, the conceptual model can be expressed probabilistically for understanding:

Let $E$ = event that an exception occurs inside the `try` block.
Let $H_i$ = event that the $i$-th `catch` (for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$) handles the exception.

Assuming exception types are mutually exclusive and exhaustive for simplicity:

$$P(\text{program terminates normally}) = P(\neg E) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \cap H_i)$$

The remaining probability mass:

$$P(\text{exception propagates}) = 1 - P(\text{program terminates normally})$$

This shows that adding more `catch` blocks **decreases** the propagation probability and **increases** graceful termination likelihood — a key software-reliability metric (Mean Time To Failure, MTTF).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram — `try-catch` Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Program Entry]) --> B[Enter try block]
    B --> C{Execute statement in try}
    C -->|Success| D{More statements?}
    D -->|Yes| C
    D -->|No| E[Exit try normally]
    E --> F[Skip all catch blocks]
    F --> K([Resume after last catch])
    C -->|Exception thrown X| G{Does catch 1 handle X?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Execute catch 1 body]
    G -->|No| I{Does catch 2 handle X?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Execute catch 2 body]
    I -->|No| L{Does catch 3 handle X?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Execute catch 3 body]
    L -->|No| N[Propagate to caller]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    M --> K
    K --> O([Continue program])
    N --> P([Caller handles or JVM terminates])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Exception Handling Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Code Layer]
        S1[try block] --> S2[catch block 1]
        S2 --> S3[catch block 2]
        S3 --> S4[catch block 3]
    end
    subgraph JVM[JVM Runtime Layer]
        J1[Statement Executor] --> J2{Exception Monitor}
        J2 -->|No exception| J3[Normal completion]
        J2 -->|Exception| J4[Build exception object]
    end
    subgraph HND[Handler Resolution Layer]
        H1[Polymorphic type match] --> H2{Is caught type assignable from thrown type?}
        H2 -->|Yes| H3[Bind exception to parameter]
        H3 --> H4[Execute catch body]
        H2 -->|No| H5[Try next catch]
        H5 -->|No more catches| H6[Unwinds stack to caller]
    end
    SRC --> JVM
    JVM --> HND
    H4 --> OUT([Recovery / Logging / Rethrow])
    H6 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Exception Class Hierarchy (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Object] --> T[Throwable]
    T --> E[Exception]
    T --> ER[Error]
    E --> IO[IOException]
    E --> RE[RuntimeException]
    E --> CE[ClassNotFoundException]
    RE --> AE[ArithmeticException]
    RE --> AIOBE[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    RE --> NPE[NullPointerException]
    RE --> NFE[NumberFormatException]
    ER --> OOM[OutOfMemoryError]
    ER --> SE[StackOverflowError]
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Exception Propagation Across Methods

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main()
    participant A as methodA()
    participant B as methodB()
    Main->>A: invoke methodA()
    A->>B: invoke methodB()
    B--xA: throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
    Note over A: catch not present in A
    A--xMain: exception propagates upward
    Note over Main: catch ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e
    Main->>Main: handle and log
```

## 4.5 Comparison Diagram — `try-catch` vs `try-catch-finally`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TCF[try-catch-finally Structure]
        F1[try block] --> F2{catch matches?}
        F2 -->|Yes| F3[catch body]
        F2 -->|No| F5[finally body]
        F3 --> F4[finally body]
        F4 --> F6[Continue program]
        F5 --> F6
    end
    subgraph TC[try-catch Structure]
        C1[try block] --> C2{catch matches?}
        C2 -->|Yes| C3[catch body]
        C2 -->|No| C5[Propagate exception]
        C3 --> C4[Continue program]
        C5 --> C4
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the purpose of a `try` block in Java? Can a `try` block exist without a `catch` block? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A `try` block in Java defines a *guarded region* of code where an exception might occur. The JVM monitors every statement inside it; if an exception is raised, normal flow is aborted and the control is transferred to the appropriate `catch` handler.

A `try` block **can** exist without a `catch` block only if it is followed by a `finally` block. The combination `try { } finally { }` is valid. However, a `try` block **cannot** stand alone — it must always be followed by at least one `catch` clause or a `finally` clause. Otherwise, the compiler raises the error: *"'try' without 'catch' or 'finally'"*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Many students write "A `try` block must always have a `catch`." This is **wrong** in the KTU 2024 Scheme. `try-finally` is also valid. The correct rule is "catch **or** finally".

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of a "matching catch" in Java exception handling. How does polymorphism influence this matching?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A `catch` clause is said to "match" a thrown exception when the **type of the catch parameter** is the **same as** or a **superclass of** the type of the exception object. The matching process is based on the **is-a relationship** derived from inheritance — i.e., the JVM checks if the thrown exception can be assigned to the catch parameter (using the *isAssignableFrom* check).

Polymorphism plays a critical role: a `catch (Exception e)` block can handle *any* exception, because almost all application-level exceptions are subclasses of `Exception`. Similarly, `catch (RuntimeException e)` can handle `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, etc. This polymorphism is why the **most specific catch must appear first** — otherwise, the more general one would swallow all subclass exceptions first.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A.(a) **[7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
**Explain the syntax and execution flow of a `try-catch` block in Java. Illustrate with a complete program that handles division-by-zero and array-index exceptions using separate catch blocks.**

**Model Solution:**

**Syntax (3 marks):**

```java
try {
    // protected statements
} catch (ExceptionType1 ref1) {
    // handler 1
} catch (ExceptionType2 ref2) {
    // handler 2
}
// ... optional finally
```

The execution flow is as follows:

1. Statements in the `try` block execute sequentially.
2. If an exception is thrown:
   - The remaining statements in `try` are skipped.
   - The JVM checks each `catch` block in order, top to bottom.
   - The **first matching catch** is executed.
3. If no exception occurs, all `catch` blocks are skipped.
4. After the `catch` (or `finally`), execution continues with the line after the construct.

**Program Illustration (4 marks):**

```java
public class ExceptionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int a = 10, b = 0;
            int c = a / b;                                    // Throws ArithmeticException
            int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
            System.out.println(arr[10]);                      // Never reached
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid array index: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Cannot divide by zero: / by zero
```

**Trace Explanation:** At `int c = a / b;`, JVM throws `ArithmeticException`. The first `catch` matches exactly, executes, prints the message, and the program terminates gracefully. The array line is never reached.

#### Q.A.(b) **[7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
**What happens when:**
**(i)** an exception is thrown inside a `catch` block?
**(ii)** a `try` block has no matching `catch`?
**(iii)** multiple exceptions are raised in a single `try` block?

Explain with suitable code snippets.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Exception thrown inside a `catch` block (2.5 marks):**

When an exception is thrown inside a `catch` block, the current `catch` exits abruptly, and the exception propagates to the **enclosing `try-catch`** or up the call stack. The remaining `catch` blocks of the *same* construct are **not** consulted.

```java
try {
    try {
        throw new NullPointerException("inner");
    } catch (NullPointerException e) {
        System.out.println("Inner caught NPE");
        throw new ArithmeticException("from inner catch");
    }
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Outer caught: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

**Output:**
```
Inner caught NPE
Outer caught: from inner catch
```

**(ii) `try` block has no matching `catch` (2.5 marks):**

The exception is **re-thrown automatically** to the caller. If no caller handles it, the JVM prints a stack trace and terminates the program.

```java
static void risky() {
    int x = 10 / 0;   // throws ArithmeticException
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
        risky();
    } catch (NullPointerException e) {   // does NOT match ArithmeticException
        System.out.println("Will not execute");
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero` — followed by JVM stack trace and program termination.

**(iii) Multiple exceptions raised in a single `try` block (2 marks):**

Only the **first** exception thrown is processed. The execution of the `try` block is **aborted at the throw point**, and all subsequent statements are skipped. A single `try-catch` construction handles **only one** exception at a time.

```java
try {
    int[] a = {1, 2};
    System.out.println(a[5]);   // Throws first
    int b = 10 / 0;             // Never reached
} catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    System.out.println("Array issue");
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Math issue");
}
```

**Output:** `Array issue` — the `ArithmeticException` is never observed because the `try` block exited at the earlier throw point.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Q.B.(a) **[7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Discuss the rules that must be followed when writing multiple `catch` blocks for a single `try`. What is the importance of ordering of catch blocks? Provide an example showing a compilation error due to incorrect ordering.**

**Model Solution:**

**Rules for Multiple Catch Blocks (4 marks):**

1. Each `catch` parameter must be a `Throwable` subclass.
2. **More specific (subclass) catches must appear before more general (superclass) catches.**
3. A `catch` block whose parameter type is a superclass of an earlier catch's parameter is **unreachable** — compile-time error.
4. Multi-catch (`catch (A | B e)`) is permitted only when the types are **unrelated by inheritance**.
5. Only **one** `catch` block executes per exception; control does not "fall through" to subsequent catches.
6. The catch parameter variable **name** is local to that block — same name can be reused in sibling catches (though not recommended).

**Importance of Ordering (2 marks):**

Ordering matters because Java evaluates catches in the order they appear. If a superclass catch appears first, it will *swallow* the subclass exception, making the more specific catch dead code. The compiler detects this and flags it as **unreachable code**, which is a compile-time error.

**Compilation Error Example (1 mark):**

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
} catch (Exception e) {                       // ← superclass first
    System.out.println("Generic");
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {              // ← SUBCLASS — UNREACHABLE
    System.out.println("Specific");
}
```

**Compiler Output:**
```
error: exception java.lang.ArithmeticException has already been caught
```

**Correct Version:**

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {              // ← subclass first ✅
    System.out.println("Specific");
} catch (Exception e) {                        // ← superclass second ✅
    System.out.println("Generic");
}
```

#### Q.B.(b) **[7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Explain the multi-catch feature introduced in Java 7. Write a Java program that reads an integer from the user and demonstrates multi-catch handling for `InputMismatchException` and `ArithmeticException`.**

**Model Solution:**

**Multi-Catch Explanation (3 marks):**

The multi-catch feature, introduced in **Java 7**, allows a single `catch` block to handle **multiple unrelated exception types** by separating them with the pipe (`|`) operator. The syntax is:

```java
catch (Type1 | Type2 | Type3 ex) {
    // common handler
}
```

**Rules:**
- The types in a multi-catch **must not have an inheritance relationship** with each other.
- The variable (here `ex`) is **implicitly `final`** — it cannot be reassigned.
- The bytecode generated is the same as having separate catch blocks with identical bodies — it is purely syntactic sugar.

**Demonstration Program (4 marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int a = sc.nextInt();                    // may throw InputMismatchException
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int b = sc.nextInt();                    // may throw InputMismatchException
            int result = a / b;                      // may throw ArithmeticException
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
        } catch (InputMismatchException | ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Error occurred: " + e.getClass().getSimpleName()
                               + " — " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Unexpected error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            sc.close();
            System.out.println("Scanner closed.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Run 1** (b = 0):
```
Enter numerator: 10
Enter denominator: 0
Error occurred: ArithmeticException — / by zero
Scanner closed.
```

**Sample Run 2** (input = "abc"):
```
Enter numerator: abc
Error occurred: InputMismatchException — null
Scanner closed.
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (–1 to –2 marks):**
> 1. Do not write `catch (Exception | RuntimeException e)` — this is a **compile-time error** (related types).
> 2. Do not attempt to reassign the catch variable in a multi-catch (`e = new ...;`) — it is implicitly final.
> 3. The `finally` block runs **even when** `System.exit(0)` is *not* called from `try`/`catch` (in this program). However, if `System.exit()` is invoked, `finally` is skipped. KTU expects students to know this edge case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — `try` Block and `catch` Clause**

- **🔹 `try` block** = guarded/protected region; can contain any number of statements.
- **🔹 `catch` clause** = handler; declared with a `Throwable`-subclass parameter and a name.
- **🔹 `try` MUST be followed by `catch` OR `finally`** (or both). A `try` alone is a **compile-time error**.
- **🔹 Only ONE catch executes per exception** — no fall-through.
- **🔹 Subclass catch must come BEFORE superclass catch** (specificity rule).
- **🔹 Exception thrown in `catch` propagates upward** — not to sibling catches of the same `try`.
- **🔹 `catch (A | B e)`** is valid only if A and B are **unrelated by inheritance**; `e` is implicitly `final`.
- **🔹 Catch is polymorphic** — a superclass handler catches subclass exceptions.
- **🔹 `catch (Exception e)`** is the most general practical handler; **avoid** `catch (Throwable t)` for application code.
- **🔹 The `try` block aborts at the first throw**; subsequent statements are **not** executed.
- **🔹 Method `getMessage()`** returns the detail string; **`toString()`** returns `"<class>: <message>"`; **`printStackTrace()`** prints full stack.
- **🔹 Stack unwinding** is the JVM's process of propagating an unmatched exception to the caller.
- **🔹 Try-with-resources (Java 7+)** auto-closes `AutoCloseable` resources declared in the `try` header.

> **🎓 Final Tip:** In KTU valuation, always **trace line by line**, mention the **exception class name**, and state **why** a particular catch matches. Examiners reward explicit reasoning, not just correct outputs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple catch Clauses
Nested try Statements
throw
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** throw

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** In Java Object-Oriented Programming, `throw` is a **keyword** used to **explicitly trigger / invoke an exception** during program execution. It is a unilateral statement (not a declaration) that transfers control of execution from the current scope to the nearest enclosing `try-catch` or `throws` clause by passing a single, pre-constructed exception object up the call stack.

The `throw` statement is the **programmer-initiated counterpart** to the JVM's automatic exception generation. While the JVM automatically throws exceptions (e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`) when a fault is detected, the `throw` keyword empowers the developer to manually raise an exception whenever a domain-specific error condition is logically encountered.

### General Syntactic Form

```java
throw <Throwable_Instance>;
```

Where `<Throwable_Instance>` is a valid object reference of type `Throwable` (or any of its subclasses such as `Exception`, `RuntimeException`, `Error`, or a user-defined custom exception class).

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Fire Alarm in a Building"**

Imagine you are inside a large multi-storied office building. Suddenly, you detect smoke in your room. Instead of calling each person individually to evacuate, you simply **pull the fire alarm switch**.

Here is how it maps to the `throw` keyword:

| Real-World Element | Java `throw` Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You (the person who detected smoke) | The code block that detects a logical error |
| Pulling the alarm switch | Executing the `throw` statement |
| The alarm bell ringing | The exception object being propagated |
| The fire safety officer catching the alert | The `catch` block receiving the exception |
| Evacuation protocol | The exception-handling code execution |
| The building's escape plan (predefined routes) | The `try-catch-finally` / `throws` infrastructure |

Just as you cannot "un-pull" the fire alarm once pressed, control flow **never returns** to the statement immediately following `throw` in the current block — execution jumps directly to the nearest matching `catch` handler.

---

## Classification of `throw` in Java's Exception Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> Every object passed to `throw` must belong to `java.lang.Throwable` or a subclass. The standard Java hierarchy is:

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.lang.Throwable
          ├── java.lang.Error          (Unrecoverable — JVM-level)
          ├── java.lang.Exception
                ├── Checked Exceptions   (Compile-time enforced)
                │     ├── IOException
                │     ├── SQLException
                │     └── ClassNotFoundException
                └── Unchecked Exceptions (RuntimeException subclasses)
                      ├── ArithmeticException
                      ├── NullPointerException
                      ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
                      └── IllegalArgumentException
```

`throw` can technically work with **any** of these, though throwing `Error` is **strongly discouraged** in application code.

---

## Key Terminology Anchors

- **Thrower:** The method/block that executes the `throw` statement.
- **Thrown Object:** The `Throwable` instance being passed to the JVM call stack.
- **Propagation:** The upward movement of the exception object through method calls until a `catch` is found.
- **Unreachable Code:** Any statement appearing immediately after a `throw` inside the same block — the Java compiler flags this as a compile-time error.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Rules of the `throw` Keyword

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places significant weight on understanding **exactly what `throw` can and cannot do**. The following 8 rules form the high-yield foundation:

### Rule 1 — Single Object Per Statement
The `throw` keyword can transfer **only one exception object** per statement. Chained exceptions use the `cause` parameter in the constructor (not multiple `throw` statements).

```java
throw new ArithmeticException("Divide by zero");
```

### Rule 2 — Must Be a Subtype of `Throwable`
The operand of `throw` must be of type `java.lang.Throwable` or a subclass. Passing an `int`, `String`, or any non-Throwable object is a **compile-time error**.

```java
throw 10;           // ❌ Compile Error — int is not Throwable
throw "Error";      // ❌ Compile Error — String is not Throwable
throw new Exception("Bad input");  // ✅ Valid
```

### Rule 3 — Checked Exception Rule
If a `throw` statement throws a **checked exception**, then the enclosing method must either:
- (a) **Handle** it with a `try-catch` block, **OR**
- (b) **Declare** it in the method's signature using the `throws` clause.

```java
// (a) Handling with try-catch
void readFile() {
    try {
        throw new java.io.IOException("Disk failure");
    } catch (java.io.IOException e) {
        System.out.println("Handled locally");
    }
}

// (b) Declaring with throws
void readFile() throws java.io.IOException {
    throw new java.io.IOException("Disk failure");
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting the `throws` clause for a checked exception causes a compile-time error: *"unhandled exception: java.io.IOException"*.

### Rule 4 — Unchecked Exception Rule
`throw` of an unchecked exception (`RuntimeException` or its subclasses) **does NOT** require a `throws` declaration. This is because unchecked exceptions represent programming logic errors that the compiler does not enforce you to handle.

### Rule 5 — Unreachable Code After `throw`
Any code written **after** a `throw` statement in the same block is considered **unreachable** and produces a compile-time error.

```java
void demo() {
    throw new RuntimeException("Boom");
    System.out.println("This will NEVER execute"); // ❌ Unreachable code
}
```

### Rule 6 — Control Transfer Mechanism
When `throw` executes:
1. The current method execution **terminates immediately**.
2. The exception object is **pushed onto the JVM call stack**.
3. The JVM searches the call stack **outward** (from current method → caller → caller's caller…) for a matching `catch` block.
4. If no `catch` is found → the **default exception handler** prints a stack trace and terminates the program.

### Rule 7 — Re-throwing an Exception
A `catch` block can `throw` the same (or a new) exception. This is used when a method partially handles an exception but wants to escalate it.

```java
try {
    int data = riskyOperation();
} catch (IOException e) {
    System.out.println("Logging: " + e.getMessage());
    throw e;   // Re-throws the same exception to the caller
}
```

### Rule 8 — `throw` vs `throws` — A Vital Distinction

| Aspect | `throw` (keyword) | `throws` (clause) |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Statement | Method signature clause |
| **Purpose** | Actually throws an exception object | Declares that a method **may** throw exceptions |
| **Location** | Inside method body | In method header |
| **Count** | Throws **one** exception per statement | Can list **multiple** exception types |
| **Syntax** | `throw new Exception();` | `void m() throws E1, E2 {}` |
| **Synonym** | "Execute this exception now" | "I am warning the caller about possible failures" |

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula / Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Syntax of `throw` | `throw <Throwable_Instance>;` | `throw new ArithmeticException("/0");` |
| 2 | Type Constraint | Operand ∈ {`Throwable` ∪ Subclasses} | `throw new Exception()` ✅ |
| 3 | Unreachability | ∀ statement $S$ s.t. $S$ appears after `throw` in same block ⇒ $S$ is **unreachable** | Compile error |
| 4 | Checked Exception Constraint | If $E$ is checked, then $\text{method} \in \{\text{try-catch}\} \cup \{\text{throws } E\}$ | `void m() throws IOException` |
| 5 | Re-throw Constraint | Re-thrown exception type $\subseteq$ caught type or its supertype | `catch(IOException e){throw e;}` |
| 6 | Single Throwable Per Statement | $|\{\text{objects thrown per } \text{throw statement}\}| = 1$ | `throw new E1(), new E2();` ❌ |
| 7 | Default Handler Trigger | If no `catch` matches after full stack unwinding → `ExceptionInInitializerError` / program termination | — |
| 8 | Custom Exception Requirement | Custom class must extend `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked) | `class MyE extends Exception{}` |

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The `throw` keyword is the cornerstone of **defensive programming** in production-grade software:

- **Banking Software:** Throwing `InsufficientFundsException` when account balance is less than withdrawal amount.
- **IoT Systems:** Throwing `SensorTimeoutException` when a hardware sensor fails to respond within a deadline.
- **Web APIs:** Throwing `RateLimitExceededException` (HTTP 429) when API calls exceed permitted thresholds.
- **Embedded Systems:** Throwing `OutOfMemoryError` (programmatically) when memory thresholds are breached to enable graceful shutdown.
- **Compilers:** Throwing `ParseException` when source code syntax is invalid.

These use cases demonstrate that `throw` is the language mechanism through which a software system **communicates failures across architectural layers** in a standardized, type-safe manner.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Trace Tables

## Analytical Trace: How `throw` Alters the Call Stack

Consider the following program structure (this is the **most important diagram for KTU exams**):

```java
class ThrowDemo {
    static void method3() {
        System.out.println("M3: Before throw");
        throw new ArithmeticException("/ by 0");
        // System.out.println("M3: After throw"); // ❌ Unreachable
    }

    static void method2() {
        System.out.println("M2: Calling method3()");
        method3();
        System.out.println("M2: After method3()"); // Skipped
    }

    static void method1() {
        try {
            System.out.println("M1: Inside try");
            method2();
            System.out.println("M1: After method2()"); // Skipped
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("M1: Caught -> " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("M1: Program continues normally");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        method1();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Step | Active Method | Stack Top | Action | Output |
|------|---------------|-----------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `main` | `main` | Calls `method1()` | — |
| 2 | `method1` | `main → method1` | Enters `try` block | — |
| 3 | `method1` | `main → method1` | Prints "M1: Inside try" | `M1: Inside try` |
| 4 | `method1` | `main → method1` | Calls `method2()` | — |
| 5 | `method2` | `main → method1 → method2` | Prints "M2: Calling method3()" | `M2: Calling method3()` |
| 6 | `method2` | `main → method1 → method2` | Calls `method3()` | — |
| 7 | `method3` | `main → method1 → method2 → method3` | Prints "M3: Before throw" | `M3: Before throw` |
| 8 | `method3` | `main → method1 → method2 → method3` | Executes `throw new ArithmeticException(...)` — **stack unwinds** | — |
| 9 | `method3` | (popped) | Method terminates abruptly, control returns to `method2` | — |
| 10 | `method2` | (popped) | "M2: After method3()" is skipped → method2 terminates | — |
| 11 | `method1` | `main → method1` | JVM searches `catch(ArithmeticException e)` — **match found** | — |
| 12 | `method1` | `main → method1` | Executes catch block | `M1: Caught -> / by 0` |
| 13 | `method1` | `main → method1` | Executes statement after try-catch | `M1: Program continues normally` |
| 14 | `main` | `main` | `main` returns — program exits normally | — |

### Final Console Output (Exact Sequence)

```
M1: Inside try
M2: Calling method3()
M3: Before throw
M1: Caught -> / by 0
M1: Program continues normally
```

### Mathematical/Logical Notation of Stack Unwinding

Let the call stack at the moment of `throw` be:

$$ S_{\text{pre}} = [\text{main}, \text{method1}, \text{method2}, \text{method3}] $$

When `throw` executes with exception object $E$:

$$
\forall \text{frame } F_i \in S_{\text{pre}} \text{ (from top to bottom):}
$$

$$
F_i \text{ pops iff } F_i \text{ has no matching } \text{catch}(E \text{ or supertype})
$$

The final surviving frame $F_{\text{handler}}$ is the first frame where:

$$
\text{catch}(C_j) \text{ exists such that } E \leq C_j \text{ (in subclass hierarchy)}
$$

In our trace:

$$
\text{frame index} \; 0 \; (\text{method3}) : \text{no catch} \Rightarrow \text{pop}
$$

$$
\text{frame index} \; 1 \; (\text{method2}) : \text{no catch} \Rightarrow \text{pop}
$$

$$
\text{frame index} \; 2 \; (\text{method1}) : \text{catch(ArithmeticException)} \Rightarrow \text{HANDLER FOUND}
$$

$$
S_{\text{post}} = [\text{main}, \text{method1}]
$$

If no frame satisfies the handler condition, the default handler is invoked:

$$
\text{defaultHandler}(E) = \text{printStackTrace}() + \text{System.exit}(1)
$$

---

## Analytical Matrix: When to Use `throw` (Design Decision Tree)

```
                   ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                   │ Is a logical error condition     │
                   │ detected in your method?         │
                   └──────────────┬───────────────────┘
                                  │ YES
                                  ▼
                   ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                   │ Does Java's standard library     │
                   │ already provide a matching       │
                   │ Exception class?                 │
                   └──────────────┬───────────────────┘
                                  │
                       ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                       │ YES                 │ NO
                       ▼                     ▼
              ┌────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐
              │ Use built-in:  │    │ Create custom class  │
              │ throw new      │    │ extending Exception  │
              │ IllegalArgument│    │ or RuntimeException  │
              │ Exception(...) │    │                      │
              └────────────────┘    └──────────┬───────────┘
                                               ▼
                                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                                    │ Is the error a       │
                                    │ programmer bug (e.g. │
                                    │ bad input) or an     │
                                    │ external condition?  │
                                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                                               │
                                    ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                                    │                     │
                                    ▼                     ▼
                            ┌────────────┐        ┌──────────────┐
                            │ Unchecked: │        │ Checked:     │
                            │ extends    │        │ extends      │
                            │ RuntimeEx. │        │ Exception    │
                            └────────────┘        └──────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Exception Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Method Execution Begins] --> B[Logical Check Performed]
    B --> C{Error Condition Met?}
    C -->|No| D[Continue Normal Flow]
    C -->|Yes| E[Construct Exception Object]
    E --> F[new ExceptionType with Message]
    F --> G[Execute throw statement]
    G --> H[JVM Unwinds Call Stack]
    H --> I{Catch Block Found in Stack?}
    I -->|Yes, Match| J[Execute Catch Handler]
    I -->|No Match in Current Frame| K[Pop Frame and Check Caller]
    K --> I
    I -->|No Match Anywhere| L[Default Exception Handler]
    L --> M[Print Stack Trace]
    M --> N[Terminate Program]
    J --> O[Resume Normal Execution After Try-Catch]
    O --> P[Program Continues]
    D --> P
    N --> Q[Program Ends Abnormally]
```

## Diagram 2: throw vs throws Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
