```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THROW["throw Statement"]
        T1[Inside Method Body] --> T2[Single Throwable Object]
        T2 --> T3[Transfers Control Immediately]
        T3 --> T4[Requires try-catch OR throws clause for checked]
    end

    subgraph THROWS["throws Clause"]
        R1[In Method Signature] --> R2[Declares Multiple Exception Types]
        R2 --> R3[Does NOT Transfer Control]
        R3 --> R4[Forces Caller to Handle Exceptions]
    end

    THROW -.Companion.-> THROWS
```

## Diagram 3: Throwable Class Hierarchy (Applicable to `throw`)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.lang.Throwable]
    B --> C[java.lang.Error]
    B --> D[java.lang.Exception]
    C --> C1[OutOfMemoryError]
    C --> C2[StackOverflowError]
    D --> E[Checked Exceptions]
    D --> F[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    E --> E1[IOException]
    E --> E2[SQLException]
    E --> E3[ClassNotFoundException]
    F --> F1[ArithmeticException]
    F --> F2[NullPointerException]
    F --> F3[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    F --> F4[IllegalArgumentException]
    F --> F5[NumberFormatException]
```

## Diagram 4: Checked vs Unchecked Exception Decision Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Custom Exception Required] --> Q1{Does error occur due to programmer mistake?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Bad Input or Logic| Unchecked[Extend RuntimeException]
    Q1 -->|No, External Resource or Env| Q2{Should compiler force handling?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Checked[Extend Exception]
    Q2 -->|No| Unchecked

    Unchecked --> Result1[throw new MyRuntimeException]
    Checked --> Result2[throw new MyCheckedException]
    Result2 --> Note1[Caller MUST use try-catch or declare throws]
    Result1 --> Note2[Caller NOT forced to handle]
```

## Diagram 5: Programmatic Re-Throw Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as Calling Method
    participant Callee as Called Method
    participant Handler as Catch Handler

    Caller->>Callee: invoke method()
    activate Callee
    Callee->>Callee: Detect exception condition
    Callee->>Callee: throw new CustomException
    deactivate Callee
    Note over Callee: Method aborts immediately

    Callee-->>Handler: Exception propagates
    activate Handler
    Handler->>Handler: Partial handling (e.g., logging)
    Handler->>Handler: throw e (re-throw)
    deactivate Handler
    Note over Handler: Re-thrown to outer scope
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between `throw` and `throws` in Java. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `throw` | `throws` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Statement / keyword | Clause in method signature |
| Purpose | Explicitly throws a single exception | Declares that a method may throw one or more exceptions |
| Count | One exception per statement | Can declare multiple exceptions |
| Location | Method body | Method header |

**Example of `throw`:**
```java
throw new ArithmeticException("Division by zero");
```

**Example of `throws`:**
```java
void readFile() throws IOException, FileNotFoundException {
    // method body
}
```

*(3 marks — 1 mark for syntax, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What happens if a `throw` statement is followed by additional code in the same block? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Any code written **after a `throw` statement** within the same block becomes **unreachable code**. The Java compiler produces a compile-time error: *"unreachable statement"*.

**Example:**
```java
void demo() {
    throw new RuntimeException("Failure");
    System.out.println("This line is unreachable"); // ❌ Compile error
}
```

The `throw` statement transfers control out of the method immediately, so no subsequent statement in that block can ever execute.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for concept, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for explanation of why)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of exception handling in Java. With a suitable example, describe how the `throw` keyword is used to explicitly raise an exception. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that validates a user's age for voter registration. If the age is below 18, the program should `throw` a custom `InvalidAgeException`. Demonstrate the use of `try`, `catch`, and `finally` to handle this exception. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Concept and `throw` Usage

**Exception Handling** is a robust mechanism in Java to handle runtime errors, ensuring the normal flow of the program is maintained. The five keywords involved are: `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws`.

**Need for Exception Handling:**
- Prevents abrupt program termination
- Separates error-handling code from regular business logic
- Enables graceful recovery from unexpected conditions

**The `throw` keyword** is used to **explicitly transfer control** to the nearest enclosing `try-catch` block by passing a `Throwable` object.

**Example Program Demonstrating `throw`:**

```java
public class ThrowExample {
    static void checkEligibility(int age) {
        if (age < 18) {
            throw new ArithmeticException("Not eligible to vote");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Eligible to vote");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            checkEligibility(15);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Exception caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues after exception");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Exception caught: Not eligible to vote
Program continues after exception
```

**Working Explanation:**
1. The `checkEligibility` method checks if `age < 18`.
2. If true, `throw new ArithmeticException(...)` creates and propagates the exception.
3. The exception travels up the call stack to `main`, where the `catch` block handles it.
4. After the `catch` block, the program continues normally.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for concept, 3 marks for program, 2 marks for explanation of working)*

---

#### Part (b) — Custom Exception with try-catch-finally

**Custom Exception Class Definition:**

```java
// Custom checked exception
class InvalidAgeException extends Exception {
    public InvalidAgeException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}
```

**Main Program:**

```java
public class VoterRegistration {
    static void registerVoter(String name, int age) throws InvalidAgeException {
        if (age < 18) {
            throw new InvalidAgeException("Age " + age + " is below 18. Registration denied for " + name);
        }
        System.out.println("Registration successful for " + name);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            registerVoter("Arjun", 16);
        } catch (InvalidAgeException e) {
            System.out.println("Registration Failed: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Voter registration system cleanup complete.");
        }

        System.out.println("End of program.");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Registration Failed: Age 16 is below 18. Registration denied for Arjun
Voter registration system cleanup complete.
End of program.
```

**Code Explanation:**

| Construct | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `class InvalidAgeException extends Exception` | Defines a custom **checked** exception |
| `throw new InvalidAgeException(...)` | Explicitly raises the custom exception |
| `throws InvalidAgeException` in method signature | Declares that the method may throw the checked exception |
| `try` block | Wraps the risky method call |
| `catch (InvalidAgeException e)` | Catches and handles the custom exception |
| `finally` block | Executes regardless of whether the exception was thrown (cleanup logic) |

*(7 marks — 2 marks for custom class, 3 marks for main program logic, 2 marks for output and explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Java's exception handling mechanism, clearly distinguishing between checked and unchecked exceptions. Illustrate how `throw` interacts with the call stack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to perform division of two integers entered by the user. If the denominator is zero, the program should `throw` an `ArithmeticException` with an appropriate message and demonstrate exception propagation across multiple methods. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Exception Architecture and `throw` Interaction

**Exception Hierarchy Diagram:**

```
              java.lang.Object
                    │
              java.lang.Throwable
              /                \
        java.lang.Error      java.lang.Exception
        (Unrecoverable)         /           \
                       Checked Exceptions   java.lang.RuntimeException
                       (Compile-time)       (Unchecked - Runtime)
```

**Checked vs Unchecked:**

| Aspect | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Direct subclasses of `Exception` (not RuntimeException) | Subclasses of `RuntimeException` |
| Compile-time check | Yes — compiler enforces handling | No — compiler does not enforce |
| Examples | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| When to use | External resource failures | Programming logic errors |
| `throws` required? | Yes, if not handled locally | No |

**Interaction of `throw` with the Call Stack:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Stack Frame: main()                                      │
│   try {                                                  │
│       methodA();    ──────────────┐                      │
│   } catch (Exception e) {         │                      │
│       handle(e);                  │                      │
│   }                               │                      │
├───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
│ Stack Frame: methodA()            │                      │
│   methodB();    ──────────┐       │                      │
│   (no catch)              │       │                      │
├───────────────────────────┼───────┼──────────────────────┤
│ Stack Frame: methodB()    │       │                      │
│   throw new E;  ◄─────────┘       │                      │
│   (Exception created and          │                      │
│    propagated upward) ────────────┘                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

When `throw new E` executes in `methodB()`:
1. The exception object $E$ is **pushed onto the call stack**.
2. `methodB()` is **popped** (no catch present).
3. `methodA()` is searched — no catch — **popped**.
4. `main()` is searched — **catch found** — handler executes.
5. If no catch exists anywhere → **default handler** prints stack trace and terminates.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for hierarchy diagram, 2 marks for checked/unchecked distinction, 2 marks for call stack interaction explanation)*

---

#### Part (b) — Division with `throw` and Exception Propagation

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class DivisionDemo {

    static int computeQuotient(int numerator, int denominator) {
        if (denominator == 0) {
            throw new ArithmeticException("Denominator cannot be zero — division aborted.");
        }
        return numerator / denominator;
    }

    static void performDivision(int a, int b) {
        int result = computeQuotient(a, b);  // Exception may propagate from here
        System.out.println("Result: " + result);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int num = sc.nextInt();
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int den = sc.nextInt();
            performDivision(num, den);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("ArithmeticException caught in main: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            sc.close();
            System.out.println("Scanner closed. Program terminating safely.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Run (Denominator = 0):**

```
Enter numerator: 25
Enter denominator: 0
ArithmeticException caught in main: Denominator cannot be zero — division aborted.
Scanner closed. Program terminating safely.
```

**Exception Propagation Path:**

```
computeQuotient()  ──throws──▶  performDivision()  ──propagates──▶  main() catch block
   (no try-catch)                  (no try-catch)                    (handler executes)
```

The exception **originates** in `computeQuotient()`, **passes through** `performDivision()` (which has no `try-catch`), and is finally **caught** in `main()`.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for throw logic, 3 marks for program, 2 marks for propagation explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing `throw` and `throws` syntax:** Students often write `void m() throw Exception` (incorrect) instead of `void m() throws Exception` (correct). The first is a syntax error.
> 2. **Forgetting `throws` clause for checked exceptions:** When a method `throw`s a checked exception, students forget to declare it in the method signature using `throws`, leading to a compile-time error worth **2 marks lost** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Throwing multiple exceptions in one `throw` statement:** Writing `throw new E1(), new E2();` is a common mistake. The `throw` keyword supports **only one** object per statement.
> 4. **Catching the wrong supertype:** Writing `catch (Exception e)` when the thrown exception is `IOException` works due to polymorphism, but KTU examiners want students to catch the **most specific** exception type. Catching the supertype always loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Ignoring unreachable code:** Statements after `throw` cause compile errors. Some students wrongly believe the program will compile. Examiners specifically test this — failing to mention it costs **2 marks**.
> 6. **Re-throwing with a narrowed type:** Writing `catch (IOException e) { throw new SQLException(e); }` is **not allowed** without declaring `throws SQLException` in the method signature.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `throw` Keyword in Java**

- ✅ `throw` is a **keyword**, NOT a method. It is a **statement** used to explicitly throw an exception object.
- ✅ The operand of `throw` **must be** an object of type `Throwable` or its subclass.
- ✅ Only **ONE** exception object can be thrown per `throw` statement.
- ✅ For **checked exceptions**, the method must either **handle** the exception with `try-catch` OR **declare** it using the `throws` clause.
- ✅ For **unchecked exceptions** (subclasses of `RuntimeException`), **no `throws` clause** is required.
- ✅ Any code written **after** a `throw` statement in the same block is **unreachable** — this is a **compile-time error**.
- ✅ `throw` causes the current method to **terminate immediately** and the exception to **propagate up the call stack**.
- ✅ If no matching `catch` block is found in the call stack, the **default exception handler** terminates the program with a stack trace printout.
- ✅ A `catch` block can **re-throw** the same exception (or a different one) using `throw e;`.
- ✅ `throw` (statement) ≠ `throws` (clause) — KTU frequently tests this distinction. Remember: `throw` **does**, `throws` **declares**.
- ✅ Custom exception classes must extend `Exception` (for checked) or `RuntimeException` (for unchecked).
- ✅ The exception object is **constructed first**, then **thrown** — order is critical for understanding stack traces.
- ✅ The `throw` keyword is the **primary tool** for enforcing preconditions, business rules, and validation logic in OOP systems.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 exams, expect `throw` to appear combined with **custom exceptions**, **`finally` blocks**, and **multi-method propagation** scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throws and finally. Multithreaded Programming - Thread Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** throws and finally. Multithreaded Programming - Thread Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Exception Handling (Advanced) & Multithreaded Programming

This module unifies two pivotal pillars of robust Java programming: the **defensive cleanup mechanisms** (`throws` and `finally`) that ensure a program never leaks resources, and the **Thread Model** that allows a single Java Virtual Machine (JVM) to execute multiple logical flows of control concurrently.

---

## 1.1 The `throws` Clause — Delegating Exception Responsibility

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim):**
> The `throws` clause is a method-signature-level declaration used to *propagate* (forward) a checked exception to the calling method instead of handling it locally. It informs the compiler that the method may generate one or more exceptions listed, and the caller is responsible for handling or further propagating them.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Forwarding the Problem Letter":**
> Imagine a junior clerk at a government office who receives a complaint he is not authorized to resolve. Instead of throwing the complaint in the dustbin (crashing the program), he writes *"Forwarded to Senior Officer"* on the envelope and passes it up the chain. The `throws` keyword plays exactly this role — it is a **formal declaration of incompetence** at the current level, declaring: *"I cannot handle this; whoever called me must do so."*

### Why `throws` Exists — The Checked Exception Mandate

Java divides exceptions into two categories:
1. **Checked Exceptions** — verified at **compile-time** (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`). The compiler *forces* the programmer to either **handle** them with `try-catch` OR **declare** them using `throws`.
2. **Unchecked Exceptions** — subclasses of `RuntimeException` (e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`). The compiler does *not* force handling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The `throws` keyword applies **only meaningfully** to checked exceptions. Using it for unchecked exceptions is legal but redundant.

### Syntax of `throws`

```java
accessModifier returnType methodName(parameters) throws ExceptionType1, ExceptionType2 {
    // method body that may throw the declared exceptions
}
```

---

## 1.2 The `finally` Block — Guaranteed Execution Guardian

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> `finally` is an optional block that follows `try` (and optionally `catch`) which contains code guaranteed to execute regardless of whether an exception is thrown, caught, or never occurs. Its primary purpose is **resource cleanup** — closing files, releasing database connections, freeing network sockets — to prevent resource leaks.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Table Cleanup":**
> Picture a waiter who takes your order (the `try` block). Whether you love the food, hate it, or even walk out mid-meal, the table **must** be cleared and wiped (the `finally` block) before the next customer can be seated. If `finally` did not exist, half-eaten meals (open files, leaked memory) would accumulate forever, eventually crashing the restaurant (the JVM).

---

## 1.3 Multithreaded Programming — The Thread Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. The **Thread Model** in Java defines the lifecycle, scheduling, priorities, and communication mechanisms of threads managed by the JVM, enabling concurrent execution of multiple tasks within a single program.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Single Chef, Multiple Recipes" vs. "Multiple Chefs":**
> A **single-threaded program** is one chef cooking one dish from start to finish before touching the next. A **multithreaded program** is multiple chefs (or one chef juggling multiple burners) working on different dishes simultaneously, sharing the same kitchen (memory space). Java's Thread Model is the *rulebook* that governs how these chefs take turns, who gets priority, and how they share ingredients.

### Two Ways to Create a Thread in Java

| Approach | Mechanism | Limitation |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Extend `Thread` class** | Override `public void run()` | Cannot extend any other class (single inheritance) |
| **Implement `Runnable` interface** | Implement `public void run()`, pass to `Thread` constructor | Cannot directly call `start()` on the runnable object |

### Java's Default Thread Scheduler

Java uses a **preemptive, priority-based** scheduler. When multiple threads are runnable, the thread with the **higher priority** is generally chosen by the OS. Priorities are integers from **`Thread.MIN_PRIORITY = 1`** to **`Thread.MAX_PRIORITY = 10`**, with default **`Thread.NORM_PRIORITY = 5`**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `throws` Clause — Rules & Mechanics

### Operational Rules

1. **Applies to methods and constructors only** — cannot be used on classes or blocks.
2. **Multiple exceptions** can be listed, separated by commas.
3. The method body must *actually be capable* of throwing the declared exception (either explicitly via `throw` or by calling a method that declares it).
4. **Subclass override rule:** If a subclass overrides a superclass method, the overriding method can **only declare a subset** (or none) of the exceptions declared in the parent — it cannot introduce *new* or *broader* checked exceptions.
5. Unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException` and its subclasses, plus `Error`) **need not** be declared, even if thrown.

### `throw` vs `throws` — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | `throw` | `throws` |
|---------|---------|----------|
| **Type** | Keyword statement | Keyword clause |
| **Purpose** | Actually *invokes* an exception object | *Declares* possibility of exception |
| **Location** | Inside method body | In method signature |
| **Number** | Throws **one** exception at a time | Can declare **multiple** exceptions |
| **Syntax** | `throw new ArithmeticException("/ by zero");` | `void m() throws IOException { }` |

---

## 2.2 The `finally` Block — Execution Guarantees

### The Four Possible Execution Paths

| Scenario | Will `finally` execute? |
|----------|------------------------|
| Exception **not** thrown in `try`, no `catch` | ✅ Yes |
| Exception **thrown**, **caught** by `catch` | ✅ Yes |
| Exception **thrown**, **not caught** (propagates) | ✅ Yes (before propagating) |
| `try` or `catch` executes `return` | ✅ Yes (BEFORE the return actually happens) |
| `System.exit(0)` called | ❌ **No** — JVM terminates |
| An **infinite loop** or fatal `Error` halts JVM | ❌ No |
| Thread executing `try` is `stop()`-ed abruptly (deprecated) | ❌ No |

### The 4 Valid `try` Combinations

1. `try` — `catch` ✅
2. `try` — `finally` ✅
3. `try` — `catch` — `finally` ✅
4. `try` alone ❌ (Compile-time error)

### Critical Edge Case: `finally` with `return`

> [!WARNING]
> If both `try` and `finally` contain a `return` statement, the value returned is the one from `finally` — **not** `try`. This is one of the most commonly tested KTU pitfalls.

```java
public static int demo() {
    try {
        return 10;
    } finally {
        return 20;  // This OVERRIDES the try's return!
    }
}
// demo() always returns 20.
```

---

## 2.3 The Thread Model — Lifecycle, States & Scheduling

### Thread Lifecycle States (5 States)

A thread can exist in exactly one of the following states at any moment:

| State | Description | Entry Trigger |
|-------|-------------|---------------|
| **New** | Thread object created via `new Thread()`, `start()` not yet called | Object instantiation |
| **Runnable** | `start()` invoked; thread is ready to run, waiting for CPU | Call to `start()` |
| **Running** | Scheduler has selected this thread; `run()` is executing | Scheduler dispatch |
| **Blocked / Waiting / Sleeping** | Thread temporarily inactive (I/O wait, `sleep()`, `wait()`, `join()`) | `sleep()`, `wait()`, I/O block, lock contention |
| **Dead / Terminated** | `run()` completed normally or threw uncaught exception | Natural completion or uncaught throw |

### Key Thread Methods (KTU High-Yield)

| Method | Purpose | Static? | Throws? |
|--------|---------|---------|---------|
| `start()` | Spawns new call stack, calls `run()` | No | No |
| `run()` | Entry point of thread's task | No | No |
| `sleep(ms)` | Pauses current thread for given ms | **Yes** | `InterruptedException` (checked) |
| `join()` | Caller waits for this thread to die | No | `InterruptedException` |
| `yield()` | Hint to scheduler to give other threads a chance | **Yes** | No |
| `interrupt()` | Sends interrupt signal to target thread | No | No |
| `isAlive()` | Returns `true` if thread started but not yet dead | No | No |
| `setPriority(int)` | Sets thread priority (1–10) | No | `IllegalArgumentException` |
| `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` | Inter-thread communication (defined in `Object`) | No | `InterruptedException` |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Boundary Condition |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| `throws` propagation | Depth of call stack = n levels | All n−1 intermediate methods must handle or declare |
| `finally` execution | `Pr(finally executes) ≈ 1.0` for normal flow | Excludes `System.exit()`, fatal `Error`, thread death |
| Priority range | $1 \leq \text{priority} \leq 10$ | Default = 5 |
| Single vs Multithread speedup (ideal) | $T_n = T_1 / n$ | Amdahl's Law ceiling due to serial portion |
| Thread states | $N = 5$ canonical states | New, Runnable, Running, Blocked, Dead |
| `Thread.sleep()` accuracy | $\text{min granularity} \geq \text{OS-dependent}$ | Cannot guarantee exact wake-up time |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — `throws` Propagation Across the Call Stack

### Problem Statement
A program reads an integer from a file. Design the method hierarchy such that the file-reading exception is propagated all the way up to `main()`.

### Complete Annotated Code

```java
import java.io.*;

class FileProcessor {

    // Level 3: Lowest-level method that actually performs the risky I/O.
    // It declares 'throws IOException' so it does NOT handle it locally.
    static int readNumber(String fileName) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(fileName);
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(fr);
        String line = br.readLine();
        br.close();
        return Integer.parseInt(line);
    }

    // Level 2: Intermediate wrapper. Either handles or re-declares.
    // We choose to re-declare, propagating the exception upward.
    static int processFile(String fileName) throws IOException {
        int value = readNumber(fileName);  // delegates further up
        return value * 2;
    }

    // Level 1: main() is the ultimate handler in many KTU programs.
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int result = processFile("data.txt");
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("File error caught in main: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

### Execution Flow Matrix

$$
\text{Call Stack at the moment of exception:}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{main()} &\rightarrow \text{processFile()} \rightarrow \text{readNumber()} \\
\text{Exception originates in readNumber()} &\rightarrow \text{propagates up through processFile()} \\
&\rightarrow \text{caught in main()}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

1. `main()` enters the `try` block and invokes `processFile("data.txt")`.
2. `processFile()` invokes `readNumber("data.txt")`.
3. Inside `readNumber()`, the JVM throws an `IOException` because `data.txt` does not exist.
4. Since `readNumber()` does **not** contain a `catch` for `IOException`, the JVM unwinds the stack to `processFile()`.
5. `processFile()` also has no `catch`; only the `throws` declaration. The exception is propagated again.
6. The exception reaches `main()`, where the `catch (IOException e)` block successfully handles it.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `finally` Execution Across All Control Paths

### Problem Statement
Demonstrate that `finally` executes in **all four** normal control scenarios.

```java
class FinallyDemo {

    static void caseA() {
        try {
            System.out.println("Case A: try only, no exception");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Case A: finally executed");
        }
    }

    static void caseB() {
        try {
            int x = 10 / 0;  // ArithmeticException
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Case B: caught " + e);
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Case B: finally executed");
        }
    }

    static void caseC() {
        try {
            int x = 10 / 0;  // ArithmeticException, NOT caught here
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Case C: finally executed even though uncaught");
        }
    }

    static int caseD() {
        try {
            return 100;
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Case D: finally executed BEFORE return");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        caseA();
        caseB();
        try { caseC(); } catch (ArithmeticException e) { System.out.println("Caught in main"); }
        int val = caseD();
        System.out.println("Returned value = " + val);
    }
}
```

### Predicted Output Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Case A: try only, no exception} \\
\text{Case A: finally executed} \\
\text{Case B: caught java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero} \\
\text{Case B: finally executed} \\
\text{Case C: finally executed even though uncaught} \\
\text{Caught in main} \\
\text{Case D: finally executed BEFORE return} \\
\text{Returned value = 100}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of the "return override" rule:**
> The Java Language Specification guarantees that when `finally` contains a `return`, the compiler-generated bytecode for the `try` block's `return` is **suppressed** because the `finally` block's `return` writes to the return register *after* the `try` block's return value is computed but *before* control leaves the method. Hence, `finally`'s `return` wins.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Thread Lifecycle Trace

### Problem Statement
Trace the state transitions of a thread in the following program.

```java
class LifecycleDemo extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("State inside run(): " + getState());
        try { Thread.sleep(2000); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        System.out.println("Woke up. State now: " + getState());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        LifecycleDemo t = new LifecycleDemo();
        System.out.println("After new Thread(): " + t.getState());   // NEW
        t.start();
        System.out.println("After start(): " + t.getState());       // RUNNABLE
        Thread.sleep(500);
        System.out.println("Mid-execution: " + t.getState());       // TIMED_WAITING
        t.join();
        System.out.println("After join(): " + t.getState());        // TERMINATED
    }
}
```

### State Transition Sequence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_0 &= \text{NEW} \quad (\text{after } \texttt{new LifecycleDemo()}) \\
\text{State}_1 &= \text{RUNNABLE} \quad (\text{after } \texttt{start()}) \\
\text{State}_2 &= \text{TIMED\_WAITING} \quad (\text{inside } \texttt{sleep(2000)}) \\
\text{State}_3 &= \text{RUNNABLE} \quad (\text{after sleep expires}) \\
\text{State}_4 &= \text{TERMINATED} \quad (\text{after } \texttt{join()} \text{ returns})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Key Insight

> [!NOTE]
> The JVM **does not guarantee** that you will observe `RUNNING` state because modern OS schedulers are extremely fast — between the `start()` call and your `System.out.println`, the thread may have already yielded. The **observable** states in a typical KTU lab exercise are `NEW`, `RUNNABLE`, `TIMED_WAITING` (during sleep), and `TERMINATED`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | `extends Thread` | `implements Runnable` |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| Inheritance flexibility | Locks the class to `Thread` parent | Class can extend any other class |
| Object reusability | Each thread is its own object | Same `Runnable` can be passed to many `Thread`s |
| Recommended by KTU | Acceptable | **Preferred** (industry best practice) |
| Access to `Thread` methods directly | Yes (`this.start()`) | No (must wrap in `Thread` instance) |

| Feature | `sleep()` | `wait()` |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| Class | `java.lang.Thread` | `java.lang.Object` |
| Releases lock? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Must be in synchronized block? | No | **Yes** |
| Wakes on `notify()`? | No | Yes |
| Wakes on timeout? | Yes | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exception Propagation Diagram with `throws` and `finally`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Caller Method main"] -->|invokes| B["Method B declares throws IOException"]
    B -->|invokes| C["Method C declares throws IOException"]
    C -->|invokes| D["Method D actual risky I/O"]
    D -->|throws IOException| E["Exception generated"]
    E -->|propagates up| C
    C -->|propagates up| B
    B -->|propagates up| A
    A -->|caught in catch block| F["Recovery via catch"]
    F -->|control flows to| G["finally block executes ALWAYS"]
    G -->|either way| H["Method continues or terminates normally"]
    styleA["A"]:::hi
    styleG["G"]:::hi
    classDef hi fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow-highlighted boxes (`A` and `G`) are the two critical "anchors" — `A` is where the exception is finally caught, and `G` is the `finally` block which executes *regardless* of whether the exception was caught at `A` or whether it propagated further.

## 4.2 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : new Thread()
    New --> Runnable : start()
    Runnable --> Running : Scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Runnable : yield() or time slice ends
    Running --> Blocked : sleep / wait / I/O / lock wait
    Blocked --> Runnable : timer expires / notify / I/O ready
    Running --> Dead : run() completes or uncaught exception
    Dead --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Java Thread Stack vs. Shared Heap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JVM
        H["Shared Heap - objects, class data"]
        subgraph Thread1
            S1["Stack1 - local variables, frames"]
        end
        subgraph Thread2
            S2["Stack2 - local variables, frames"]
        end
        subgraph Thread3
            S3["Stack3 - local variables, frames"]
        end
    end
    S1 -->|references| H
    S2 -->|references| H
    S3 -->|references| H
    styleH["H"]:::shared
    classDef shared fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each thread has its **own private stack** (so local variables are thread-safe), but all threads share the **same heap** (so object instances are *not* automatically thread-safe — this is why `synchronized` blocks exist, covered in Module 4).

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — When to use `throws` vs `try-catch`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the exception checked?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Checked| Q2{"Is your method the right level to recover?"}
    Q1 -->|No, Unchecked| R1["Handle optionally or let propagate naturally"]
    Q2 -->|Yes, can recover meaningfully| R2["Use try catch locally"]
    Q2 -->|No, delegate to caller| R3["Declare throws in method signature"]
    Q2 -->|Mixed| R4["try catch for known ones, throws for unknown"]
    styleR1["R1"]:::note
    styleR2["R2"]:::note
    styleR3["R3"]:::note
    styleR4["R4"]:::note
    classDef note fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*What is the difference between `throw` and `throws` in Java? Give one example of each.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**`throw`** is a statement used **inside a method body** to explicitly **invoke** (raise) a single exception object at runtime.

**`throws`** is a clause used in the **method signature** to **declare** that the method may pass one or more checked exceptions to its caller.

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Location | Inside method body | Method signature |
| Function | Actually creates and throws an exception | Informs compiler of possible exceptions |
| Count | One exception per statement | Multiple, comma-separated |

```java
throw new ArithmeticException("/ by zero");           // throw example

void readFile() throws IOException {                  // throws example
    FileReader f = new FileReader("a.txt");
}
```
*(Award 1 mark for each correct difference + 1 mark for valid example syntax.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Explain the purpose of the `finally` block in Java. Is it always executed?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Purpose:** The `finally` block contains code that must execute regardless of whether the `try` block succeeded, failed, or encountered an exception. Its primary role is **resource cleanup** — closing files, releasing database connections, freeing sockets — to prevent leaks.

**Execution guarantee:** `finally` executes in almost all scenarios: exception not thrown, exception caught, exception propagated uncaught, and even when `return` is used in `try`.

**Exceptions when `finally` does NOT execute:**
1. `System.exit(0)` is called — JVM halts immediately.
2. A fatal `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) crashes the JVM.
3. The thread executing `try` is killed abruptly via deprecated `stop()`.

*(Award 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for typical execution, 1 mark for non-execution cases.)*

---

## 📘 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the different states in the **lifecycle of a thread** in Java with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to create two threads — one prints even numbers from 1 to 20, and the other prints odd numbers from 1 to 20. Use the `Thread` class extension method. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

A thread in Java moves through **five canonical states**:

1. **New:** The thread object is created via `new MyThread()` but `start()` has not been called. The thread is not yet alive.

2. **Runnable:** After calling `start()`, the thread is ready to run. It waits in the queue for the CPU scheduler to pick it. This state includes the thread actually executing on the CPU.

3. **Blocked / Waiting / Sleeping:** The thread is alive but temporarily inactive. Causes include:
   - `Thread.sleep(milliseconds)`
   - `wait()` called on a monitor
   - Waiting for I/O completion
   - Trying to acquire a `synchronized` lock held by another thread

4. **Dead / Terminated:** The `run()` method returns normally, or an uncaught exception terminates `run()`. Once dead, a thread cannot be restarted.

**ASCII Diagram (5 marks for diagram + explanation):**

```
       ┌────────┐  start()   ┌──────────┐  scheduler   ┌─────────┐
       │  New   │ ─────────► │ Runnable │ ───────────► │ Running │
       └────────┘            └──────────┘              └─────────┘
                                   ▲                        │
                                   │ yield()/time slice    │
                                   │                        ▼
                            ┌──────────────┐         ┌────────┐
                            │  Blocked /   │◄────────│  Dead  │
                            │  Waiting     │  run()  └────────┘
                            └──────────────┘ completes
```

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

```java
class EvenThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 2; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Even: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

class OddThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Odd:  " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        EvenThread et = new EvenThread();
        OddThread  ot = new OddThread();
        et.start();   // starts even thread
        ot.start();   // starts odd thread
    }
}
```

**Mark allocation:**
- Correct class definitions: 2 marks
- Proper `run()` method override: 2 marks
- `start()` invocation in `main()`: 1 mark
- Proper exception handling for `sleep()`: 1 mark
- Output and explanation: 1 mark

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the `throws` clause in Java with a suitable example. How does it differ from a `try-catch` block in handling checked exceptions? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **Java Thread Model**. Also list any **four** methods of the `Thread` class with their purpose. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** The `throws` clause is a declaration in a method's signature used to propagate one or more checked exceptions to the caller method, instead of handling them locally.

**Example (3 marks):**

```java
import java.io.*;

class Demo {
    static void readFile() throws IOException, FileNotFoundException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");  // may throw
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(fr);
        System.out.println(br.readLine());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            readFile();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Handled in main: " + e);
        }
    }
}
```

**Difference between `throws` and `try-catch` (2 marks):**

| Aspect | `throws` | `try-catch` |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Strategy | Delegates (passes upward) | Handles locally |
| Location | Method signature | Method body |
| Granularity | Whole method | Specific code block |
| Use case | When current method cannot recover meaningfully | When recovery is possible at this layer |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Java Thread Model — Working (4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for explanation):**

```
            ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
            │              JAVA VIRTUAL MACHINE        │
            │                                          │
            │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐       │
            │   │Thread1│  │Thread2│  │Thread3│       │
            │   │ Stack │  │ Stack │  │ Stack │       │
            │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘       │
            │         ↓         ↓         ↓           │
            │      ┌──────────────────────┐           │
            │      │    SHARED HEAP       │           │
            │      │ (Objects, Class Data)│           │
            │      └──────────────────────┘           │
            └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↑
                  ┌───────────────┐
                  │ Thread        │
                  │ Scheduler     │
                  │ (priority-    │
                  │  based)       │
                  └───────────────┘
```

- Each thread gets a **private stack** for local variables and method frames.
- All threads share the **heap** where objects are allocated.
- The **Thread Scheduler** (part of the JVM/OS) decides which runnable thread executes next based on **priority (1–10)** and OS time-slicing.

**Four Methods of `Thread` Class (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `start()` | Creates a new call stack and invokes `run()` on it. |
| `run()` | Entry point for the thread's task code. |
| `sleep(long ms)` | Suspends current thread for the specified milliseconds. |
| `join()` | Calling thread waits until this thread finishes. |

*(Bonus acceptable: `yield()`, `interrupt()`, `setPriority()`, `isAlive()`.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `throw` with `throws`** — many students lose 2–3 marks by writing `throw` in method signatures or `throws` inside method bodies. Memorize: *throw = action; throws = announcement.*
> 2. **Forgetting `start()` vs `run()`** — calling `run()` directly executes the code in the *current* thread (no new thread created). This is the #1 thread-programming mistake.
> 3. **Assuming `finally` always runs** — forgetting that `System.exit(0)` bypasses it. Examiners specifically test this edge case.
> 4. **Forgetting to declare `InterruptedException`** — both `sleep()` and `join()` throw checked `InterruptedException`; omitting the `throws` or `try-catch` is a compile-time error.
> 5. **Confusing `sleep()` with `wait()`** — `sleep()` keeps the lock; `wait()` releases it. Module 4 covers this, but examiners often introduce it as a "compare-and-contrast" question.
> 6. **Not handling `RuntimeException` propagation** — unchecked exceptions propagate *automatically* without needing `throws`; students sometimes over-declare.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ `throws` = method-signature clause that **propagates** checked exceptions to the caller.
- ✅ `throw` = statement that **actually creates and raises** an exception object.
- ✅ `finally` = block that executes **regardless of exception flow**, except on `System.exit()` or fatal JVM errors.
- ✅ Valid combinations: `try-catch`, `try-finally`, `try-catch-finally` (never `try` alone).
- ✅ `finally`'s `return` **overrides** `try`'s `return`.
- ✅ A thread has **5 states**: New, Runnable, Running, Blocked/Waiting, Dead.
- ✅ `start()` spawns a new call stack; calling `run()` directly runs in the *current* thread.
- ✅ Two thread creation mechanisms: **extend `Thread`** OR **implement `Runnable`** (latter preferred).
- ✅ Priority range is **1–10**, default **5**.
- ✅ `sleep()` keeps the lock; `wait()` releases it.
- ✅ `InterruptedException` is **checked** — must be caught or declared.
- ✅ Each thread has its **own stack**; all threads **share the heap**.
- ✅ Java scheduler is **preemptive, priority-based**.
- ✅ Thread cannot be restarted once `TERMINATED` — calling `start()` again throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Creating Threads # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** and Object-Oriented Programming under the KTU 2024 syllabus, a **Thread** is defined as the smallest independent unit of execution within a process. Every Java program implicitly has at least one thread — the *main thread* — created automatically by the JVM to execute the `main()` method. **Creating Threads** is the explicit act of instantiating additional concurrent execution paths so that multiple blocks of code can run seemingly in parallel, thereby enabling **multithreaded programming**. In Java (Kotlin/JDK), threads are represented by objects of the class `java.lang.Thread` or by objects whose class implements the marker functional interface `java.lang.Runnable`. The KTU 2024 OOP module emphasises thread creation as the gateway concept to **concurrent**, **asynchronous**, and **parallel** problem solving. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The official syllabus mandates coverage of *"creating threads, extending Thread class, implementing Runnable interface, thread priorities, and thread life-cycle."* This note treats **Creating Threads** as the foundational sub-topic. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a single restaurant kitchen with **one chef** (a *single-threaded program*). The chef can only do one task at a time: chop vegetables, then boil pasta, then plate the dish. Now imagine hiring **three chefs** (a *multi-threaded program*). Each chef works on their own station simultaneously. In Java terms: | Kitchen Concept | Java Equivalent | |---|---| | Restaurant | Process (JVM instance) | | Chef | Thread | | Recipe card held by the chef | Runnable task (`run()` method) | | Kitchen Manager's whistle | `start()` method | | Chef actually cooking | `run()` method execution | > [!NOTE] > **Why two ways to create a thread?** In Java, a class can extend only **one** parent (no multiple inheritance). By providing both *extending `Thread`* and *implementing `Runnable`*, Java lets the class remain free to extend another business class while still being a thread — a classic OOP design trade-off. ## 1.3 Why Threads Matter in Engineering Applications - **Responsive UIs** — keep a GUI from freezing during heavy computation. - **Server throughput** — web servers like Tomcat spawn a thread per request. - **Embedded/IoT** — sensor polling vs. main control loop separation. - **Scientific computing** — parallel matrix multiplication, Monte-Carlo simulations. The default metric the JVM uses for thread scheduling is the **native OS thread priority**, with Java's range bounded between **MIN_PRIORITY = 1** and **MAX_PRIORITY = 10**, the default being **NORM_PRIORITY = 5**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Canonical Ways to Create a Thread in Java The KTU 2024 OOP module insists on a clear, structured understanding of the two creation pathways. Both pathways ultimately cause the JVM to call the no-argument `run()` method on a new stack-of-execution. ### 2.1.1 Pathway A — Extending the `java.lang.Thread` Class **Operational Steps:** 1. Declare a subclass of `Thread`. 2. Override the `public void run()` method — this is the *task body*. 3. In the calling code, instantiate the subclass. 4. Invoke `start()` on the instance. **Never call `run()` directly** if concurrency is desired. **Why this works:** The `Thread` superclass already implements `Runnable`, so the subclass inherits the threading machinery; we only supply the *what-to-do* logic in `run()`. ### 2.1.2 Pathway B — Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` Interface **Operational Steps:** 1. Declare a class that `implements Runnable`. 2. Implement the `public void run()` method. 3. Create an instance of your class (the *task*). 4. Pass that instance to a `new Thread(task).start()` call. **Why this works:** The `Runnable` interface is purely a *task contract* — it has no inherited threading state. We *compose* it with a `Thread` object. This preserves the host class's ability to extend any other business class. ### 2.1.3 Modern Variants (Beyond KTU Prescribed — For Completeness) - **Anonymous inner class:** `new Thread(new Runnable() { public void run() { ... } }).start();` - **Lambda expression (Java 8+):** `new Thread(() -> { /* task */ }).start();` - **Executor framework:** Production-grade alternative using `ExecutorService` pools. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbol / API | Value / Range | Unit / Type | |---|---|---|---| | Minimum thread priority | `MIN_PRIORITY` | **1** | integer constant | | Default thread priority | `NORM_PRIORITY` | **5** | integer constant | | Maximum thread priority | `MAX_PRIORITY` | **10** | integer constant | | States in thread life-cycle | — | **6** states | `New, Runnable, Blocked, Waiting, Timed-Waiting, Terminated` | | Memory overhead per thread (approx.) | `Stack size` | **512 KB – 1 MB** | bytes (JVM dependent) | | Active method to launch thread | `start()` | returns `void` | asynchronous | | Trap to avoid | `run()` direct call | no new thread | synchronous call on caller's stack | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners *love* asking the difference between `start()` and `run()`. Memorise that `start()` allocates a **new call stack** and then invokes `run()` on that fresh stack, whereas calling `run()` directly executes on the **caller's** existing stack — producing no concurrency at all. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking software** — separate threads handle UI rendering, transaction logging, and database connection pooling. - **Telecommunications** — each phone call is a thread in a VoIP server. - **Robotics** — sensor reading, motor control, and path planning run as distinct threads with carefully assigned priorities. - **Scientific computing** — matrix operations split into chunks processed by worker threads (parallel for-loops in libraries like Apache Commons Math). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Extending `Thread` (Pathway A) Below is an exhaustive, KTU-board-style complete program with line-by-line analysis. ```java // File: MyThreadDemo.java class MyThread extends Thread { // (1) Override run() to define the task of THIS thread public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(getName() + " -> Count: " + i); try { Thread.sleep(500); // Pause for 500 ms } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted."); } } } public static void main(String[] args) { MyThread t1 = new MyThread(); // (2) Object in 'New' state MyThread t2 = new MyThread(); // (3) A second 'New' thread t1.setName("Worker-A"); t2.setName("Worker-B"); t1.start(); // (4) Transitions to 'Runnable'; calls run() on NEW stack t2.start(); // (5) Same for t2 } } ``` **Step-by-step logical derivation:** - **Step 1:** `MyThread extends Thread` — inherits the `start()`, `sleep()`, `getName()` machinery. - **Step 2:** `public void run()` override supplies the *what-to-do* logic; this is the **only** callback the JVM invokes when `start()` is called. - **Step 3:** `new MyThread()` creates a *New* state thread; **no OS resource is consumed yet** beyond a Java object on the heap. - **Step 4:** `setName()` assigns a human-readable label; purely diagnostic, no effect on scheduling. - **Step 5:** `t1.start()` performs three actions internally: - The thread is registered with the JVM thread scheduler. - A fresh native call stack is allocated (~512 KB by default). - On that new stack, `t1.run()` is invoked. - **Step 6:** `Thread.sleep(500)` does **not release the lock**; it merely parks the *current* thread for ~500 ms and yields to the scheduler. The `InterruptedException` catch is mandatory — checked exception. > [!NOTE] > **Output ordering is non-deterministic** because the OS scheduler decides which runnable thread executes first. A KTU answer must explicitly state: *"Interleaving of output depends on the JVM thread scheduler and is not guaranteed."* ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Implementing `Runnable` (Pathway B) ```java // File: MyRunnableDemo.java class MyTask implements Runnable { public void run() { Thread t = Thread.currentThread(); for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(t.getName() + " runs iteration " + i); } } public static void main(String[] args) { MyTask task = new MyTask(); // (1) Plain object, NOT a thread yet Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Alpha"); Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Beta"); t1.start(); // (2) Wrap task in a Thread and launch t2.start(); // (3) Same task instance, two threads — share the run() } } ``` **Step-by-step derivation:** - **Step 1:** `MyTask implements Runnable` — the class is now a *task contract*; it could also `extends SomeBusinessClass` (Java allows *one* interface + *one* class). - **Step 2:** `new Thread(task, "Alpha")` calls the `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` constructor; the `Runnable` reference is stored, the name is registered. - **Step 3:** `t1.start()` triggers `Thread.run()` internally, which then **delegates** to `task.run()` — confirming the *decoupling* of *task* (Runnable) from *worker* (Thread). - **Step 4:** Both `t1` and `t2` share the *same* `task` object. They will execute `task.run()` concurrently on independent stacks. The `Runnable` object itself is **stateless** in this example, so concurrent access is safe. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Criterion | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` | |---|---|---| | Inheritance used | Class inheritance (single) | Interface implementation | | Can class extend another class? | **No** | **Yes** | | Object is itself a Thread? | Yes | No (must be wrapped) | | Reusability of task | Low (each thread is a new object) | High (one task, many threads) | | Suits Executor framework? | Awkward | Natural (designed for it) | | KTU recommendation | Good for simple demos | **Preferred in industry & OOP design** | | `start()` call syntax | `t1.start();` | `new Thread(task).start();` | | Memory footprint per task | Larger (inherits `Thread` fields) | Smaller (only task fields) | ## 3.4 Algebraic-Style Summary of the `start()` Mechanism The transition of a thread through its creation states can be written formally: $$ S_{\text{new}} \xrightarrow{\text{start()}} S_{\text{runnable}} \xrightarrow{\text{scheduler picks}} S_{\text{running}} \xrightarrow{\text{run() returns}} S_{\text{terminated}} $$ Where each $S$ denotes the thread's logical state, and the arrows are transitions triggered by JVM/OS events rather than algebraic operators. This notation is helpful when answering KTU theory questions on the **Thread Life-Cycle**. ## 3.5 Common Pitfalls (Step-by-Step Pit-Trace) 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`** — The program runs sequentially on the *caller's* stack. No new thread is born. This is the #1 KTU mark-loser. 2. **Calling `start()` twice on the same thread object** — Throws `IllegalThreadStateException` at runtime. 3. **Forgetting to override `run()`** — The base `Thread.run()` does nothing; the program appears to "succeed" silently with zero output. 4. **Blocking the main thread** — Using `t1.run()` synchronously and then `t1.start()` afterwards gives a false sense of concurrency; the second call throws the exception mentioned above. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Two Pathways of Thread Creation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Application Code"] --> B{"Which creation strategy?"} B -- Extends Thread --> C1["class MyThread extends Thread"] C1 --> C2["Override public void run() with task body"] C2 --> D1["Instantiate MyThread t = new MyThread()"] D1 --> E1["State of t equals NEW"] E1 --> F1["Call t.start()"] F1 --> G1["JVM allocates new call stack"] G1 --> H1["Invokes t.run() on that new stack"] H1 --> I1["State becomes RUNNABLE then RUNNING"] I1 --> J1["On completion equals TERMINATED"] B -- Implements Runnable --> C2A["class MyTask implements Runnable"] C2A --> D2A["Implement public void run() with task body"] D2A --> E2A["Instantiate MyTask task = new MyTask()"] E2A --> F2A["Wrap inside new Thread task, new Thread task"] F2A --> G2A["Call thread.start()"] G2A --> H2A["JVM allocates new call stack"] H2A --> I2A["Internally calls task.run() on that stack"] I2A --> J2A["Thread state RUNNABLE then RUNNING then TERMINATED"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Thread State Transitions (Contextual View) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> New : new Thread New --> Runnable : start Runnable --> Blocked : acquire failed lock Blocked --> Runnable : lock acquired Runnable --> Waiting : wait join park Waiting --> Runnable : notify notifyAll unpark Runnable --> TimedWaiting : sleep ms wait ms join ms TimedWaiting --> Runnable : timeout elapses Runnable --> Terminated : run returns Terminated --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — `start()` Internals ```mermaid flowchart LR Caller["Caller stack: main thread"] -- t1.start --> Checker{"Thread state check
already started?"} Checker -- Yes --> Throw["throw IllegalThreadStateException"] Checker -- No --> Add["Register with ThreadGroup"] Add --> Alloc["Allocate native stack ~512 KB"] Alloc --> Spawn["Spawn OS-level thread via JNI"] Spawn --> NewStack["Fresh Java call stack"] NewStack --> Invoke["Invoke t1.run on that stack"] Invoke --> Task["User-defined task body executes"] Task --> Cleanup["On return: state equals TERMINATED"] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ExtendsThread["PATHWAY A: Extends Thread"] A1["class MyThread extends Thread"] --> A2["@Override run"] A2 --> A3["MyThread t = new MyThread()"] A3 --> A4["t.start()"] end subgraph ImplementsRunnable["PATHWAY B: Implements Runnable"] B1["class MyTask implements Runnable"] --> B2["@Override run"] B2 --> B3["MyTask task = new MyTask()"] B3 --> B4["Thread t = new Thread task"] B4 --> B5["t.start()"] end FinalBoth["Both end at: parallel run execution on independent stacks"] -.-> ExtendsThread FinalBoth -.-> ImplementsRunnable ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B] **Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class. Why is it wrong to invoke `run()` directly for achieving multithreading?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - `start()` is responsible for **registering the thread with the JVM scheduler**, allocating a **new call stack**, and then invoking `run()` on that *fresh* stack. This is what makes the code run **concurrently**. - `run()` is simply a *normal method* inherited from `Runnable`. Calling it directly executes the body on the **caller's** existing stack — there is no new thread, no concurrency, and the program behaves like a single-threaded sequential call. - Therefore, invoking `run()` defeats the entire purpose of multithreading; one must use `start()` to spawn a true new thread of execution. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain any two ways of creating a thread in Java. State one advantage of using the `Runnable` interface over extending the `Thread` class.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Way 1:** Define a class that *extends* `java.lang.Thread` and override the `run()` method. Create its object and call `start()`. - **Way 2:** Define a class that *implements* `java.lang.Runnable` and override `run()`. Pass the object to a `new Thread(...).start()` call. - **Advantage of `Runnable`:** It allows the class to *inherit from another business class*, since Java supports only single class inheritance, whereas `Runnable` is just an interface. It also enables the **same task object to be reused by multiple threads**, which is impossible when extending `Thread` directly. **(3 marks)** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Examiners routinely deduct **2–3 marks** when students: > 1. Forget to mention that `run()` must be `public` (visibility matters for JNI dispatch). > 2. Confuse `Runnable` (interface, task-only) with `Thread` (class, has the start() machinery). > 3. State that "sleep() releases the lock" — it does **not**. Only `wait()` releases the lock. > 4. Omit the catch block for `InterruptedException` — it is a *checked* exception and **must** be handled. > Avoid these to score full marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Extends-Thread Pathway **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OOP Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the steps involved in creating a thread by **extending the `Thread` class** with a suitable Java program. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the significance of the `run()` method? What happens if we override `start()` instead of `run()`? Justify with a code snippet. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. Declare a subclass `MyThread extends Thread`. 2. Override `public void run()` with the task body. 3. In the main method, instantiate `MyThread t = new MyThread();` — the thread is in the *New* state. 4. Call `t.start();` — the JVM allocates a new call stack and asynchronously invokes `t.run()`. 5. The thread transitions to *Runnable* → *Running* → *Terminated* on completion. ```java class MyThread extends Thread { public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i); } public static void main(String[] args) { MyThread t = new MyThread(); t.setName("Worker-1"); t.start(); } } ``` *(Award full 7 marks: 2 marks for class declaration, 2 marks for run() override, 2 marks for start() invocation, 1 mark for correct output commentary.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) - The `run()` method acts as the **entry point for a new thread** — analogous to `main()` for the main thread. The JVM calls it automatically once `start()` is invoked. - If a programmer **overrides `start()`** instead of `run()`, the threading machinery breaks. The overridden `start()` would be executed on the *caller's* stack rather than spawning a new one, **unless** the override explicitly calls `super.start()`. - **Correct practice** is: override `run()` for the task; leave `start()` untouched so that the inherited logic (JNI call to spawn OS thread) is preserved. ```java class BadThread extends Thread { public void start() { // BUG: overrides threading logic System.out.println("Fake start, no new thread!"); } public void run() { System.out.println("Run will never be auto-invoked."); } public static void main(String[] a) { new BadThread().start(); // prints "Fake start" only } } ``` *(Award full 7 marks: 3 marks for explaining run()'s role, 2 marks for stating the bug in overriding start(), 2 marks for the illustrative snippet.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Implements-Runnable Pathway **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OOP Module 3 Alternative]** **(a)** Write a Java program to create a thread by **implementing the `Runnable` interface** that prints numbers 1 to 5 with a 200 ms delay. Explain each line. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare both methods of creating threads. Under what circumstances would you prefer `Runnable` over extending `Thread`? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) ```java class NumberPrinter implements Runnable { public void run() { // (1) task contract for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { // (2) loop body System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i); try { Thread.sleep(200); // (3) 200 ms pause } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Interrupted"); } } } public static void main(String[] args) { NumberPrinter task = new NumberPrinter(); // (4) task object Thread t = new Thread(task, "NumThread"); // (5) wrap in Thread t.start(); // (6) launch } } ``` **Line-by-line explanation (7 marks):** - Line 1 — Implements the `Runnable` interface, fulfilling the *task* contract. - Line 2 — `run()` is the *only* method that the new thread will execute. - Line 3 — Loop bounds 1 to 5, prints current thread name plus counter. - Line 4 — `Thread.sleep(200)` parks the *current* (worker) thread for 200 ms. - Line 5 — `InterruptedException` is checked, must be caught or declared. - Line 6 — A `NumberPrinter` is *not* a thread; we wrap it inside a `Thread` instance. - Line 7 — `start()` registers with the scheduler, allocates a new stack, and calls `task.run()` on it. #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Aspect | Extends `Thread` | Implements `Runnable` | |---|---|---| | Inheritance | Uses class inheritance; cannot extend another class | Frees the class to extend any other class | | Object type | The object itself *is* a thread | The object is a *task*; must be wrapped | | Code reuse | Poor (each thread is a new subclass) | Excellent (one task reused by N threads) | | Separation of concerns | Task and worker entangled | Clean separation of *what* and *how* | | Best for | Quick demos, trivial tasks | Production code, thread pools, framework design | **Circumstances where `Runnable` is preferred (3 extra marks):** - When the host class must extend a business/framework class. - When the same task logic must be executed by many threads (e.g., server workers). - When integrating with the `Executor` framework, which accepts only `Runnable`/`Callable` tasks, not raw `Thread` subclasses. - When applying OOP principles of *favouring composition over inheritance*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **Thread** in Java is an object of `java.lang.Thread` (or an object whose class implements `Runnable`). - There are **two creation pathways**: *extend `Thread`* or *implement `Runnable`*. - **Always** call `start()` to launch a new thread; calling `run()` directly yields **no concurrency**. - `start()` does three things: registers with the scheduler, allocates a new call stack, invokes `run()` on that stack. - `run()` must be `public void` with **no parameters** and **no checked exceptions declared**. - `Runnable` is **preferred** in OOP design — it allows inheritance, promotes reuse, and is compatible with the Executor framework. - Java thread priorities are integer constants **1 (MIN)**, **5 (NORM)**, **10 (MAX)**; the default is **5**. - The thread life-cycle has **six states**: *New, Runnable, Blocked, Waiting, Timed-Waiting, Terminated*. - `Thread.sleep(ms)` does **not release locks**; `Object.wait()` does. - Calling `start()` **twice** on the same `Thread` object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. - Interleaving of output from multiple threads is **non-deterministic** — never assume a fixed order in exam answers. - Lambda expressions (`new Thread(() -> {...}).start();`) are a concise modern alternative permitted by Java 8+. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When answering a 7-mark sub-question, always include **(i) a complete code snippet, (ii) a state-transition explanation, and (iii) one line about why `start()` is preferred over `run()`**. This trifecta covers most of the marking scheme for Module 3 — Creating Threads.

topics.title: Creating Multiple Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Multiple Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OOP – Module 3)**, **Multiple Threads** refers to the concurrent execution of two or more lightweight sub-processes (threads) within a single Java program, where each thread represents an independent path of execution that shares the same process resources (heap memory, file handles) but maintains its own **program counter**, **stack**, and **local variables**.

A **Thread** in Java is fundamentally an instance of `java.lang.Thread` or an object whose class implements `java.lang.Runnable`. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) permits every program to implicitly possess at least one thread (the **main thread**), and developers can explicitly spawn additional threads to perform tasks asynchronously, thereby improving CPU utilization, responsiveness, and throughput of I/O-bound or compute-bound applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET419 – Module 3):**
> The module explicitly requires the student to learn **"Creating Multiple Threads"** using:
> 1. **Extending the `Thread` class**
> 2. **Implementing the `Runnable` interface**
>
> The concept of `Thread` class methods (`start()`, `run()`, `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()`, `interrupt()`, `setPriority()`, `getName()`, `isAlive()`) and **thread priorities (MIN_PRIORITY = 1, NORM_PRIORITY = 5, MAX_PRIORITY = 10)** are part of the assessment outcomes mapped to **CO3 / CO4 (Apply concurrency concepts in OOP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single restaurant kitchen with one chef**:
- The chef must cook rice, then bake bread, then prepare the curry — **sequentially**.
- The total time = (rice time) + (bread time) + (curry time).

Now imagine **three chefs in the same kitchen**:
- Each chef handles one dish simultaneously, sharing the same pantry (memory) but working with their own cutting board and knife (stack).
- Total time ≈ the slowest dish's time, not the sum of all.

These three chefs are **"multiple threads"** — they live in the **same process (kitchen)**, share resources, and execute concurrently. In Java, we "hire" these chefs by **extending the `Thread` class** OR by **implementing the `Runnable` interface** and assigning each to a `Thread` worker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Threaded vs. Multi-Threaded Program:**
> - **Single-threaded:** One sequential flow of control. The program waits for one task to finish before starting the next.
> - **Multi-threaded:** Multiple flows of control run concurrently. The CPU (or multiple cores) alternates or parallelizes their execution via **time-slicing**.

## 1.3 Key Performance & Design Metrics

- **MIN_PRIORITY = 1**
- **NORM_PRIORITY = 5** (default for any new thread)
- **MAX_PRIORITY = 10**
- **Main thread priority** when a program starts: typically **5** (inherited from the parent).
- **Thread ID:** A unique positive `long` value assigned by the JVM when the thread is created.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU viva question: *"Can we start a thread twice?"* — **No.** Invoking `start()` on a thread that has already been started results in an **`IllegalThreadStateException`**. This is a **favourite 3-mark question** in the Part A section.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Strategies for Creating Multiple Threads

The Java API provides **two canonical strategies** to introduce multiple threads of execution into a program. Both ultimately rely on the internal `Thread.start()` native method, but they differ in **inheritance flexibility** and **design purity**.

### Strategy A — Extending the `java.lang.Thread` Class

The class declares itself as a subclass of `Thread` and overrides the `public void run()` method. Each instance, when started, executes its own `run()` body concurrently.

**Why this works:**
- `Thread` already implements `Runnable`.
- The subclass inherits thread-management methods (`start()`, `sleep()`, `join()`, etc.).
- Calling `start()` allocates a new call stack, invokes the JVM thread scheduler, and then asynchronously calls the overridden `run()`.

### Strategy B — Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` Interface

The class implements `Runnable` by providing a `public void run()` method. A `Thread` object is then constructed by passing the `Runnable` instance to the `Thread` constructor. Calling `start()` on this `Thread` causes the `run()` of the `Runnable` to execute on a new thread.

**Why this works:**
- Java does not support multiple class inheritance; by implementing `Runnable`, the worker class is free to **extend any other class** (e.g., a GUI component or a domain entity).
- Promotes **composition over inheritance**, a key OOP principle aligned with **KTU 2024 NEP-2020 OOP design philosophy**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Define the task** as the body of `run()`.
2. **Choose a creation strategy** (extend `Thread` OR implement `Runnable`).
3. **Instantiate** the worker object.
4. **Wrap** the worker inside a `Thread` object (Strategy B only — automatic in Strategy A).
5. **Invoke `start()`** on the `Thread` object → this **does NOT** call `run()` directly. Instead, it asks the JVM to spawn a new call stack and then call `run()` on it.
6. **Concurrency begins** — the main thread and the new thread are scheduled by the OS/JVM.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Criterion | Extends `Thread` | Implements `Runnable` |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance used | Class inheritance (`extends`) | Interface implementation (`implements`) |
| Can extend another class? | **No** (Java single inheritance) | **Yes** (more flexible) |
| Object shared between threads? | Each thread needs its **own** subclass instance | **One** `Runnable` object can be passed to **many** `Thread`s |
| Design pattern followed | Inheritance-based | Composition-based (preferred) |
| Code reusability | Lower | Higher |
| Best for KTU exam scenario | Simple, single-purpose threads | Production-grade, reusable task logic |
| `start()` call location | On the subclass object directly | On the `Thread` wrapper object |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently write `t.run()` instead of `t.start()`. Calling `run()` directly executes the method on the **current thread (no new thread is created)** — it is just a normal method invocation. Only `start()` triggers true multithreading.

## 2.4 Thread Class — Key Methods (KTU Mandatory List)

| Method | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Creates a new call stack and invokes `run()`. |
| `run()` | `void run()` | Entry point for the new thread's task. |
| `sleep(ms)` | `static void sleep(long ms)` | Pauses current thread for `ms` milliseconds. |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Calling thread waits for this thread to die. |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints the scheduler to give other threads a chance. |
| `interrupt()` | `void interrupt()` | Interrupts a sleeping/waiting thread. |
| `setPriority(int)` | `void setPriority(int p)` | Sets priority, where $p \in \lbrack 1, 10 \rbrack$ |
| `getName()` | `String getName()` | Returns the thread's name (e.g., `Thread-0`). |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | Returns `true` if the thread has started and not yet died. |
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns a reference to the currently executing thread. |

> **Mathematical Form of Priority Bound:**
> $$\text{setPriority}(p) \quad \text{where} \quad 1 \le p \le 10$$
> Any value $p \notin \lbrack 1, 10 \rbrack$ throws **`IllegalArgumentException`**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX):** Background tasks (file I/O, network) run on worker threads to prevent UI freezing.
- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Each HTTP request handled by a separate thread from a thread pool.
- **Real-time Data Pipelines:** Sensor data acquisition, financial tick processing, video streaming.
- **Parallel Computation:** Matrix multiplication, image processing, scientific simulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO3: "Apply multithreading concepts to solve concurrency problems in OOP"** at Bloom Level **L3 (Apply)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Strategy A (Extending `Thread`)

Consider the following canonical KTU-style program:

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) {
        super(name);
    }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " — Count: " + i);
```
