```java
            try {
                Thread.sleep(400);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " exiting.");
    }
}

public class ThreadDemoA {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        System.out.println("Main thread finished launching workers.");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

1. The JVM starts the **main thread**, which executes `main()`.
2. `t1` and `t2` are instantiated — **no threads spawned yet**; they are mere `MyThread` objects on the heap.
3. `t1.start()` is invoked:
   - JVM allocates a **new call stack** for `t1`.
   - The native `start0()` calls `t1.run()` on this new stack.
4. `t2.start()` similarly allocates a second stack.
5. The main thread continues and prints `"Main thread finished launching workers."`
6. Now three threads run concurrently: **Main**, **Worker-A**, **Worker-B**.
7. The scheduler interleaves their outputs. Each `sleep(400)` yields the CPU to other threads.

### Sample Output (Order is Non-Deterministic)

```
Main thread finished launching workers.
Worker-A — Count: 1
Worker-B — Count: 1
Worker-A — Count: 2
Worker-B — Count: 2
Worker-A — Count: 3
Worker-B — Count: 3
Worker-A — Count: 4
Worker-B — Count: 4
Worker-A — Count: 5
Worker-B — Count: 5
Worker-A exiting.
Worker-B exiting.
```

### Analytical Summary

$$
\text{Output sequence} = \mathcal{S}_{\text{scheduler}} \left( \text{Main}, \text{Worker-A}, \text{Worker-B} \right)
$$

where $\mathcal{S}_{\text{scheduler}}$ is a non-deterministic interleaving operator governed by the OS thread scheduler. The **only guarantee** is that the program will terminate with all three threads finishing their loops.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Strategy B (Implementing `Runnable`)

```java
class Task implements Runnable {
    private String label;
    public Task(String label) { this.label = label; }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(label + " — Iteration " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadDemoB {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Task shared = new Task("SharedTask");
        Thread t1 = new Thread(shared, "Thread-One");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(shared, "Thread-Two");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

1. `shared` is a **single `Runnable` object** holding the label "SharedTask".
2. Two `Thread` objects (`t1`, `t2`) are constructed — both reference the same `shared` object.
3. When `t1.start()` and `t2.start()` are called, **two separate stacks** are allocated, but both stacks execute the same `run()` method on the same `shared` object.
4. The fields of `shared` (e.g., `label`) are in the **shared heap memory**, accessible by both threads.

### Key Insight — Same Task, Multiple Threads

$$
\text{Number of threads} = \text{Number of } \texttt{Thread} \text{ wrappers}
$$

$$
\text{Number of task objects} = 1 \quad (\text{the single } \texttt{Runnable} \text{ instance})
$$

This demonstrates why `Runnable` is **preferred for resource sharing** (e.g., a shared ticket counter, bank account, or sensor buffer).

## 3.3 Comparative Execution Timeline (Analytical Matrix)

| Time Slice (≈) | Main Thread | Worker-A (Strategy A) | Worker-B (Strategy B) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | Constructs objects | (Not started) | (Not started) |
| $t_1$ | Calls `start()` on both | Stack allocated, `run()` invoked | Stack allocated, `run()` invoked |
| $t_2$ | Prints launch message | Prints count = 1 | Prints iteration = 1 |
| $t_3$ | (Idle / may exit) | `sleep(400)` — yields CPU | Prints iteration = 2 |
| $t_4$ | (Terminates) | Resumes — prints count = 2 | Prints iteration = 3 |
| $t_5$ | — | Loop continues | Thread terminates |
| $t_6$ | — | Loop completes — exits | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determinism Theorem:** Java's memory model makes the **interleaving** of thread instructions non-deterministic in the general case. The only ordering guarantees come from:
> - `Thread.start()` **happens-before** the first action in the new thread.
> - `Thread.join()` **happens-before** any action in the joining thread after the call.
> - A thread's `run()` exit **happens-before** another thread successfully returns from `join()`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two Strategies of Thread Creation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Starts in Main Thread] --> B{Need Multiple Threads?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Define Task Logic]
    C --> D{Choose Strategy}
    D -- Strategy A --> E[Class extends Thread]
    E --> E1[Override run method]
    E1 --> F1[Instantiate subclass object]
    F1 --> G1[Call start on subclass]
    G1 --> H1[JVM allocates new call stack]
    H1 --> I1[run executes concurrently]
    D -- Strategy B --> F[Class implements Runnable]
    F --> F2[Provide run method]
    F2 --> G2[Instantiate Runnable object]
    G2 --> H2[Wrap inside Thread constructor]
    H2 --> I2[Call start on Thread]
    I2 --> J2[JVM allocates new call stack]
    J2 --> K2[Runnable run executes concurrently]
    I1 --> L[Multiple Threads Running in Parallel]
    K2 --> L
    L --> M[Scheduler Interleaves CPU Time]
    M --> N[Threads Complete run and Terminate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Thread Lifecycle (Creation → Termination)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : object instantiated
    New --> Runnable : start invoked
    Runnable --> Running : scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Blocked : sleep, wait, or I/O
    Blocked --> Runnable : sleep elapses or lock released
    Running --> Runnable : yield invoked
    Running --> Dead : run method returns
    Dead --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **"New"** state corresponds to a `Thread` object that has been created but `start()` not yet called. The **"Dead"** state is reached after `run()` completes normally or via an uncaught exception. Once dead, a thread **cannot be restarted**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Shared Resource via Runnable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Heap[Shared Heap Memory]
        R[Runnable Task Object - label field]
    end
    subgraph Stack1[Call Stack - Thread 1]
        S1[run method executing]
    end
    subgraph Stack2[Call Stack - Thread 2]
        S2[run method executing]
    end
    T1[Thread Object 1] --> S1
    T2[Thread Object 2] --> S2
    S1 --> R
    S2 --> R
    R --> R1[Shared state accessible by both stacks]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Why `start()` ≠ `run()`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Developer calls t.start] --> Y{Is this thread already started?}
    Y -- No --> Z[JVM start0 native method]
    Z --> W[New call stack allocated]
    W --> V[Thread becomes Runnable]
    V --> U[Scheduler picks the thread]
    U --> T[t.run invoked on new stack]
    T --> S[Concurrent execution begins]
    Y -- Yes --> E1[Throws IllegalThreadStateException]
    A[Developer calls t.run directly] --> B[Normal method call on CURRENT thread]
    B --> C[No new thread created]
    C --> D[Sequential execution only]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Insight for KTU Theory:** This block diagram is the visual answer to the most-asked viva question: *"What is the difference between `start()` and `run()`?"*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class. Why is it incorrect to call `run()` directly to begin multithreaded execution? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Registers the thread with the thread scheduler | A normal instance method |
| New thread? | **Yes** — creates a new call stack | **No** — executes in the current thread |
| Invocation | `void start()` (inherited from `Thread`) | `void run()` (overridden by subclass) |
| Multiple calls? | Throws `IllegalThreadStateException` if called twice | Can be called any number of times (just a method) |

Calling `run()` directly simply invokes a method on the current thread — no new thread of execution is created, defeating the purpose of multithreading. Only `start()` triggers the JVM's native thread creation routine, which subsequently invokes `run()` on a **newly allocated call stack**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**List and briefly explain any three methods of the `Thread` class used to control thread execution. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`sleep(long millis)`** — A `static` method that suspends the currently executing thread for the specified number of milliseconds. The thread transitions to the **Blocked** state and resumes automatically after the timer expires. *Example: `Thread.sleep(1000);`*

2. **`join()`** — An instance method that causes the **calling** thread to wait until the target thread on which `join()` was invoked completes (i.e., its `run()` method returns). Useful for enforcing execution order.

3. **`setPriority(int p)`** — Sets the thread's priority to a value $p$ in the range $\lbrack 1, 10 \rbrack$. Higher-priority threads are scheduled preferentially by the OS, although priority-based scheduling is **not strictly guaranteed** in all JVMs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a Java program how to create multiple threads by **extending the `Thread` class**. Show the output structure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of this approach and justify why **implementing the `Runnable` interface** is generally preferred in real-world OOP design.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

```java
class PrintThread extends Thread {
    public PrintThread(String name) {
        super(name);
    }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " : value = " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(300);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " has finished.");
    }
}

public class ThreadExtendDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PrintThread p1 = new PrintThread("Alpha");
        PrintThread p2 = new PrintThread("Beta");
        p1.start();
        p2.start();
        System.out.println("Main thread has launched both workers.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation (Key Points for 7 Marks):**

1. `PrintThread` extends `Thread` and overrides `run()`.
2. The `super(name)` call sets the thread's name via the `Thread(String name)` constructor.
3. Two `PrintThread` objects are created on the heap.
4. `p1.start()` and `p2.start()` cause the JVM to allocate two new call stacks and invoke `run()` on each concurrently.
5. The `sleep(300)` introduces interleaving, producing a non-deterministic output order.
6. The main thread continues independently and may finish before, during, or after the worker threads.

**Expected Output (order may vary):**
```
Main thread has launched both workers.
Alpha : value = 1
Beta : value = 1
Alpha : value = 2
Beta : value = 2
...
Alpha has finished.
Beta has finished.
```

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Limitations of extending `Thread` (3 sub-points):**
- **No multiple inheritance** — If `PrintThread` already extends `Thread`, it cannot extend any other class (e.g., a domain class like `Employee`). This violates the Open-Closed Principle of OOP.
- **Tight coupling** — The thread-management logic is fused with the task logic, reducing reusability and increasing maintenance cost.
- **Poor resource sharing** — Each thread requires its **own** subclass instance; the same task logic cannot be reused across multiple threads with shared state easily.

**Why `Runnable` is preferred (3 sub-points):**
- **Decoupling** — Task logic (in `Runnable`) is separated from thread management (in `Thread`). The same `Runnable` instance can be wrapped by many `Thread` objects, enabling **shared state** access from multiple stacks.
- **Design flexibility** — A `Runnable`-implementing class can extend another class freely, supporting real OOP hierarchies.
- **Composability** — Aligns with the **"favor composition over inheritance"** principle, a core KTU 2024 NEP-2020 design guideline.

```java
// Cleaner design using Runnable
class PrintTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i);
        }
    }
}
```

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program that creates **three threads** by implementing the `Runnable` interface, where all three threads share the **same `Runnable` object** and print numbers from 1 to 5 with a delay of 250 ms between prints.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of `Thread.sleep()`, `join()`, and `setPriority()` methods. Also discuss the `MIN_PRIORITY`, `NORM_PRIORITY`, and `MAX_PRIORITY` constants.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

```java
class NumberPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(t.getName() + " prints: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(250);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(t.getName() + " was interrupted.");
                return;
            }
        }
        System.out.println(t.getName() + " exiting.");
    }
}

public class MultiRunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NumberPrinter task = new NumberPrinter();   // Single shared object
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "T-One");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "T-Two");
        Thread t3 = new Thread(task, "T-Three");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (Key Points for 7 Marks):**

1. A single `NumberPrinter` object `task` is created.
2. Three `Thread` objects (`t1`, `t2`, `t3`) wrap the same `task` but carry **distinct names**.
3. Each `start()` spawns a new call stack that executes the same `run()`.
4. `Thread.sleep(250)` introduces latency, allowing the scheduler to interleave outputs.
5. The output is non-deterministic but each thread prints exactly the integers 1 through 5.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Method | Purpose | Signature |
|---|---|---|
| `sleep(long ms)` | Pauses the **current** thread for `ms` milliseconds; releases CPU but not locks. | `static void sleep(long ms) throws InterruptedException` |
| `join()` | Calling thread blocks until the target thread finishes. | `void join() throws InterruptedException` |
| `setPriority(int p)` | Suggests a priority for the thread, where $1 \le p \le 10$. | `void setPriority(int p)` |

**Priority Constants — Mandatory for 7-Mark Questions:**

$$
\text{MIN\_PRIORITY} = 1 \qquad \text{NORM\_PRIORITY} = 5 \qquad \text{MAX\_PRIORITY} = 10
$$

- `MIN_PRIORITY` (value **1**): The lowest scheduling hint. Reserved for background tasks.
- `NORM_PRIORITY` (value **5**): The **default** priority assigned to a newly created thread. Also the default for the main thread.
- `MAX_PRIORITY` (value **10**): The highest scheduling hint. Used for time-critical threads (e.g., real-time UI updates).

> **Mathematical Constraint:**
> $$p \in \lbrack 1, 10 \rbrack \implies \text{setPriority}(p) \text{ is valid}$$
> $$p \notin \lbrack 1, 10 \rbrack \implies \text{throws } \texttt{IllegalArgumentException}$$

**Significance Discussion:**
- `sleep()` enables **timed suspension**, vital for animation loops, polling, and rate-limiting.
- `join()` enforces **synchronization** between threads — e.g., a main thread waiting for a worker to finish computing a result.
- `setPriority()` allows **soft real-time hints** to the OS scheduler, though Java does not guarantee strict priority preemption.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (3-5 marks lost per paper):**
> 1. **Writing `t.run()` instead of `t.start()`** — This is the **#1 error** in KTU OOP papers. It does NOT create a new thread; the examiner will deduct **2 full marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `super(name)`** in the `Thread` subclass constructor — threads will get auto-generated names like `Thread-0`, `Thread-1`, which still works, but the examiner expects explicit naming for clarity.
> 3. **Not handling `InterruptedException`** inside `run()` — if `sleep()` or `join()` is called without a try-catch, the program **will not compile**. This is a **compile-time error** worth 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing `Thread.sleep()` (static) with `t.sleep()` (instance)** — `sleep()` is a static method; calling it on an instance is misleading style. Use `Thread.sleep(ms)` explicitly.
> 5. **Attempting to start a thread twice** — results in `IllegalThreadStateException` at runtime. This is a **favourite 3-mark Part A question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- [x] A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Java supports multithreading natively via `java.lang.Thread`.
- [x] **Two ways to create multiple threads:** (1) extend `Thread`, (2) implement `Runnable`.
- [x] **Always override `public void run()`** — this is the entry point of the new thread.
- [x] **Call `start()`, never `run()`** — `start()` allocates a new call stack; `run()` does not.
- [x] **Calling `start()` twice** on the same thread throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- [x] **Priorities:** $\text{MIN}=1$, $\text{NORM}=5$, $\text{MAX}=10$. Default for any new thread is **5**.
- [x] **Implementing `Runnable` is preferred** — allows extending other classes, supports shared objects across multiple threads, and follows OOP composition principles.
- [x] **Thread lifecycle:** `New → Runnable → Running → Blocked/Waiting → Dead`.
- [x] **Key control methods:** `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()`, `interrupt()`, `setPriority()`, `getName()`, `isAlive()`, `currentThread()`.
- [x] **Output order is non-deterministic** — the thread scheduler's behaviour is platform-dependent.
- [x] **Memory model guarantees:** `start()` *happens-before* the new thread's first action; `join()` return *happens-after* the joined thread's termination.
- [x] **Naming convention:** Constructor `Thread(Runnable obj, String name)` sets a custom thread name; otherwise auto-names like `Thread-0`, `Thread-1` are assigned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always accompany thread-creation code with a **try-catch for `InterruptedException`** around `sleep()` and `join()`. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme specifically check for this in 14-mark questions. A missing catch is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thread Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔒 Module 3 — Thread Synchronization in Java

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread Synchronization** is the mechanism that ensures **mutually exclusive access** to shared resources (critical sections) by multiple concurrent threads, thereby preserving **data integrity**, **consistency**, and **atomicity** in a multi-threaded Java program.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET419 — Object Oriented Programming, Module 3), synchronization is studied under three pillars:

1. **The `synchronized` keyword** — intrinsic/monitor locks.
2. **The `java.util.concurrent.locks` package** — explicit `Lock` interface and `ReentrantLock`.
3. **Inter-thread communication** — `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` defined inside `Object` class.

Mathematically, a synchronized block enforces a *serializability constraint* on parallel operations: if operations $O_1$ and $O_2$ access a shared variable $V$, and at least one of them is a **write**, then the partial order $\prec$ defined by the lock-acquisition time must hold for all observable executions (this is the **happens-before consistency model** of the Java Memory Model — JMM, JSR-133).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Single-Stall Public Restroom 🚻**
>
> Imagine a public restroom with **exactly one stall** but **many people** wanting to use it. To prevent chaos, the building manager places a **lock on the door**. The rules are:
>
> - A person **enters**, **locks the door**, does their business, and **unlocks** it.
> - Anyone else who tries to enter while it's locked must **wait outside**.
> - When the person leaves and unlocks, the **next person in queue** can lock and enter.
>
> In this analogy:
> - The **stall** = the **critical section** (shared resource).
> - The **lock** = the Java **`synchronized`** monitor.
> - The **people** = **threads**.
> - The **queue outside** = the **thread wait-set**.

This is precisely what thread synchronization achieves — it converts *concurrent chaos* into an *ordered, serialized* execution of critical code.

## 1.3 Why Synchronization Is Needed — The Race Condition Problem

A **race condition** occurs when two or more threads access shared data **simultaneously**, and the final result depends on the unpredictable timing/ordering of their execution. The KTU syllabus explicitly lists this as a high-weightage sub-topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The KTU 2024 module outcomes state that the learner must be able to *"implement Java programs that handle multi-threading issues such as race conditions, deadlocks, and inter-thread communication."*

---

## 1.4 Java Memory Model (JMM) — The "Why" Behind Synchronization

Modern CPUs have multiple cache levels (L1, L2, L3), and each thread may cache a variable locally. Without synchronization, one thread's writes may **never become visible** to another thread (cache coherency issues). The JMM defines rules:

- **Visibility:** A write by thread T1 to a shared variable must be visible to T2 under certain ordering rules.
- **Atomicity:** Operations like `count++` (read-modify-write) are **not atomic** in Java — they expand to three bytecode instructions: `iload`, `iadd`, `istore`.
- **Ordering:** Without synchronization, the JVM/CPU may **reorder** instructions for performance.

**Synchronization provides all three guarantees simultaneously** by leveraging **memory barriers** (fences) inserted at monitor entry (`lock`) and exit (`unlock`).

---

## 1.5 Three Core Problems Synchronization Solves

| # | Problem | KTU Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Race Condition** | Outcome depends on thread scheduling order | Two ATM withdrawals reading the same balance |
| 2 | **Memory Inconsistency** | One thread's write invisible to another | Master-slave replication in distributed systems |
| 3 | **Reordering** | JVM/CPU reorders instructions, breaking logic | Compiler optimization of loop-invariant code |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **distinguish** between these three problems and **map each** to the correct Java construct (`synchronized`, `volatile`, or `final`).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `synchronized` Keyword — Two Forms

### Form A: Synchronized Method (Instance-Level)

When applied to a non-static method, the lock acquired is on **the current object** (`this`).

```java
class SharedCounter {
    private int count = 0;
    public synchronized void increment() {  // lock = `this`
        count++;  // read-modify-write — now ATOMIC
    }
}
```

### Form B: Synchronized Block (Fine-Grained)

Locks on an **explicit object**, allowing more precise control. This is the **KTU-recommended** form because it minimizes the critical section.

```java
class SharedCounter {
    private int count = 0;
    private final Object lock = new Object();

    public void increment() {
        synchronized (lock) {        // lock = `lock` object
            count++;
        }                            // monitor released
    }
}
```

### Form C: Synchronized Static Method (Class-Level)

The lock is acquired on the **Class object** itself (`SharedCounter.class`), not on instances.

```java
class SharedConfig {
    public static synchronized void reset() {
        // lock = SharedConfig.class
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** A thread holding the monitor on object `O` can re-enter synchronized blocks on `O` recursively. This is why Java's built-in lock is called a **`ReentrantLock`**.

---

## 2.2 The Java Object Monitor — Internal Architecture

Every Java object has an **intrinsic lock (monitor)** consisting of three components (as defined in `java.lang.Object` and the JVM specification):

| Component | Purpose | KTU Buzzword |
|-----------|---------|--------------|
| **Owner** | The thread that currently holds the lock | "Monitor owner" |
| **Entry Set** | Queue of threads blocked trying to enter | "Contention queue" |
| **Wait Set** | Queue of threads that called `wait()` | "Condition queue" |

State transitions:
1. Thread T1 calls `synchronized(obj)` → moves from *Running* to *Owner*.
2. Thread T2 calls `synchronized(obj)` while T1 holds it → moves to **Entry Set** (BLOCKED state).
3. T1 calls `obj.wait()` → T1 releases the lock and moves to **Wait Set** (WAITING state).
4. T1 calls `obj.notify()` → a thread from Wait Set is moved to Entry Set, where it must re-acquire the lock to proceed.

---

## 2.3 The `volatile` Keyword — Lightweight Synchronization

`volatile` provides **visibility** and **ordering** guarantees, but **NOT atomicity**.

```java
private volatile boolean running = true;  // visible to all threads
```

| Feature | `synchronized` | `volatile` |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Visibility | ✅ | ✅ |
| Atomicity | ✅ (for the block) | ❌ |
| Ordering | ✅ (full memory barrier) | ✅ (acquire/release semantics) |
| Blocking | ✅ (can cause BLOCKED state) | ❌ (non-blocking) |
| Use Case | Compound actions (`count++`) | Single-flag reads/writes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing `volatile int count;` and then `count++` — this is **NOT** thread-safe! `count++` is read-modify-write, which requires atomicity that `volatile` alone cannot provide.

---

## 2.4 Explicit Locks — `java.util.concurrent.locks.Lock`

Introduced in Java 5, the `Lock` interface provides more flexible synchronization than `synchronized`.

```java
import java.util.concurrent.locks.Lock;
import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock;

class SafeCounter {
    private int count = 0;
    private final Lock lock = new ReentrantLock();

    public void increment() {
        lock.lock();
        try {
            count++;
        } finally {
            lock.unlock();   // MUST be in finally
        }
    }
}
```

### Advantages over `synchronized`:
- ✅ `tryLock()` — non-blocking attempt.
- ✅ `lockInterruptibly()` — allows thread interruption while waiting.
- ✅ Fair lock option — `new ReentrantLock(true)` honors FIFO order.
- ✅ Multiple condition variables (`newCondition()`).

---

## 2.5 Inter-Thread Communication — `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()`

These three methods are defined in `java.lang.Object` and **must be called from within a synchronized context**, otherwise `IllegalMonitorStateException` is thrown.

| Method | Effect on Calling Thread | Effect on Other Threads |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| `wait()` | Releases lock, enters Wait Set | None directly |
| `wait(long ms)` | Same as `wait()` with timeout | None directly |
| `notify()` | None | **One** waiting thread moves to Entry Set |
| `notifyAll()` | None | **All** waiting threads move to Entry Set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Always use `notifyAll()` in preference to `notify()`. Using `notify()` can cause **missed signals** (a critical exam pitfall — see Section 5).

---

## 2.6 The `java.util.concurrent` Higher-Level Utilities

For advanced scenarios, KTU Module 3 expects familiarity with:

| Class | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `Semaphore` | Allow N concurrent accesses (e.g., DB connection pool) |
| `CountDownLatch` | Wait for N operations to complete |
| `CyclicBarrier` | Synchronize a fixed group of threads at a barrier point |
| `Phaser` | Reusable multi-phase barrier |
| `ConcurrentHashMap` | Thread-safe Map without locking the whole structure |
| `BlockingQueue` | Thread-safe queue for producer-consumer pattern |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **cheat sheet** for the KTU ESE. Memorize it.

| # | Concept | Key Point | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | `synchronized` | Lock on `this` (instance) or `Class` (static) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | `volatile` | Visibility only, no atomicity | ⭐⭐ |
| 3 | `wait()` | Releases lock, must be in synchronized | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | `notify()` | Wakes ONE thread (use `notifyAll()`) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | `ReentrantLock` | Explicit, supports `tryLock`, fair lock | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Deadlock | Mutual circular wait, 4 Coffman conditions | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Livelock | Threads keep yielding, no progress | ⭐ |
| 8 | Starvation | Thread never gets CPU/lock | ⭐ |
| 9 | Happens-before | JMM ordering relation, basis of visibility | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Critical section | Code accessing shared resource | ⭐⭐⭐ |

### The Four Coffman Conditions for Deadlock

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **four** must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur:
>
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Resource is non-shareable.
> 2. **Hold and Wait** — Thread holds one resource while waiting for another.
> 3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly taken.
> 4. **Circular Wait** — A cycle exists in the wait-for graph: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_n \rightarrow T_1$.

A deadlock can be broken by **negating any one** of these four conditions.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Database Transaction Systems** — Row-level locks ensure ACID properties.
- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Netty)** — Thread pools with bounded concurrent access.
- **Real-Time Trading Systems** — `ReentrantLock` with `tryLock` to avoid blocking order execution.
- **Producer-Consumer Pipelines** — `BlockingQueue` decouples thread rates.
- **Operating System Kernels** — Spinlocks and mutexes for kernel data structures.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Why `count++` Is Not Thread-Safe

**Step 1 — Java Source Code**
The increment operator `count++` is **syntactic sugar** for a three-step read-modify-write sequence.

```java
count++;  // apparent atomic operation
```

**Step 2 — Java Bytecode (using `javap -c`)**
The compiler expands this into three separate JVM instructions:

```
0: aload_0
1: dup
2: getfield      #2   // Field count:I     → READ
5: iconst_1
6: iadd                                  → MODIFY
7: dup_x1
8: putfield      #2   // Field count:I     → WRITE
11: return
```

**Step 3 — The Race Condition Derivation**

Let two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ both execute `count++` concurrently. The state of `count` evolves as follows (assuming initial `count = 5`):

$$
\text{Initial:} \quad \text{count} = 5
$$

$$
T_1 \text{ reads: } \text{count} = 5
$$

$$
T_2 \text{ reads: } \text{count} = 5 \quad (\text{before } T_1 \text{ writes})
$$

$$
T_1 \text{ computes: } 5 + 1 = 6, \text{ writes count} = 6
$$

$$
T_2 \text{ computes: } 5 + 1 = 6, \text{ writes count} = 6
$$

$$
\text{Final:} \quad \text{count} = 6 \quad (\text{expected } 7)
$$

**Step 4 — Generalized Form**

For $N$ threads each performing $K$ increments, the **expected correct value** is:

$$
\text{count}_{\text{expected}} = \text{count}_{\text{initial}} + N \cdot K
$$

The **race-induced loss** is:

$$
\Delta = N \cdot K - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{increments}_{\text{actually applied}}
$$

**Step 5 — Synchronized Solution**

```java
public synchronized void increment() {
    count++;  // now atomic
}
```

The `synchronized` keyword inserts a **monitorenter** and **monitorexit** bytecode instruction around the block, ensuring the three sub-operations execute **atomically** with respect to all other threads competing for the same lock.

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough: The Producer-Consumer Problem

This is the **canonical KTU problem** for `wait()` / `notifyAll()`.

### Problem Statement
A producer thread generates data items and places them in a bounded buffer. A consumer thread removes them. The buffer has a fixed capacity $C$. Both threads must:
- **Never** add to a full buffer.
- **Never** remove from an empty buffer.

### Solution Using `wait()` / `notifyAll()`

```java
class BoundedBuffer {
    private final int[] buffer;
    private int count = 0, in = 0, out = 0;

    public BoundedBuffer(int capacity) {
        buffer = new int[capacity];
    }

    public synchronized void produce(int item) throws InterruptedException {
        while (count == buffer.length) {  // (A) guard against spurious wakeup
            wait();                       // release lock, wait for space
        }
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % buffer.length;
        count++;
        notifyAll();                      // wake consumers
    }

    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (count == 0) {              // (B) guard against spurious wakeup
            wait();                       // release lock, wait for item
        }
        int item = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % buffer.length;
        count--;
        notifyAll();                      // wake producers
        return item;
    }
}
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — State Invariant**
The condition $0 \leq \text{count} \leq C$ is the **safety invariant**.

$$
\text{Invariant:} \quad 0 \leq \text{count} \leq C, \quad \forall t \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 — Wait-Release Semantics**
When `wait()` is called inside a synchronized method, **two atomic actions** occur in sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Thread releases monitor on } \textit{this}. \\
&\text{2. Thread enters the wait-set and is suspended.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wake-Notify Semantics**
`notifyAll()` does **not** release the lock immediately. The notifying thread continues execution; the lock is released only when the synchronized block exits, after which the woken thread(s) compete to re-acquire it.

**Step 4 — The Spurious Wakeup Problem**

> [!WARNING]
> A thread can wake up from `wait()` **without** being notified — this is called a **spurious wakeup** (mandated by the Java specification for `Object.wait()`). The `while` loop guard is **mandatory**, not optional. Replacing `while` with `if` is one of the **most common KTU valuation penalties**.

**Step 5 — Why `notifyAll()` over `notify()`?**

Using `notify()` could wake the **wrong thread**:
- Producer wakes → finds buffer full → waits again → no one else wakes.
- System **deadlocks** even though no resource is held.

Using `notifyAll()` is **always correct** (though sometimes less efficient), which is why KTU examiners prefer it.

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough: Deadlock Demonstration & Resolution

### Deadlock Code

```java
class Account {
    private double balance;
    public synchronized void withdraw(double amt) {
        balance -= amt;
    }
    public synchronized void deposit(double amt) {
        balance += amt;
    }
}

class Transfer {
    public static void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amt) {
        synchronized (a) {           // STEP 1: lock A
            synchronized (b) {       // STEP 2: lock B
                a.withdraw(amt);
                b.deposit(amt);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

If Thread T1 calls `transfer(A, B)` and Thread T2 calls `transfer(B, A)` **simultaneously**:
- T1 holds lock on A, waits for B.
- T2 holds lock on B, waits for A.
- **Circular wait** → **Deadlock** 🔒

### Resolution 1 — Lock Ordering (Acquire locks in a global order)

```java
public static void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amt) {
    Account first  = (a.hashCode() < b.hashCode()) ? a : b;
    Account second = (a.hashCode() < b.hashCode()) ? b : a;
    synchronized (first) {
        synchronized (second) {
            a.withdraw(amt);
            b.deposit(amt);
        }
    }
}
```

This **breaks the Circular Wait condition**, which is sufficient to prevent deadlock.

### Resolution 2 — `tryLock` with Timeout

```java
import java.util.concurrent.locks.*;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

private final Lock lockA = new ReentrantLock();
private final Lock lockB = new ReentrantLock();

public void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amt) throws InterruptedException {
    while (true) {
        if (lockA.tryLock(50, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)) {
            try {
                if (lockB.tryLock(50, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)) {
                    try {
                        a.withdraw(amt);
                        b.deposit(amt);
                        return;
                    } finally { lockB.unlock(); }
                }
            } finally { lockA.unlock(); }
        }
        Thread.sleep(10);  // back-off to reduce contention
    }
}
```

This approach **breaks the Hold and Wait** condition by releasing the first lock if the second cannot be acquired.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: The Happens-Before Relation

The Java Memory Model defines a partial order $\prec$ on actions called **happens-before**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Program order rule:} \quad a \prec b \text{ if } a, b \text{ in same thread and } a \text{ before } b. \\
&\text{2. Monitor lock rule:} \quad \text{unlock}(m) \prec \text{lock}(m) \text{ on same monitor.} \\
&\text{3. Volatile rule:} \quad \text{write}(v) \prec \text{read}(v) \text{ if read sees the write.} \\
&\text{4. Thread start:} \quad \text{start}(t) \prec \text{any action in } t. \\
&\text{5. Thread join:} \quad \text{any action in } t \prec \text{join}(t) \text{ returns.} \\
&\text{6. Transitivity:} \quad a \prec b \land b \prec c \Rightarrow a \prec c.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Implication for Visibility:**
If action $a$ happens-before action $b$ ($a \prec b$), then all memory writes visible to $a$ are also visible to $b$. This is the **mathematical foundation** that allows Java programmers to reason about thread safety without considering low-level CPU cache behavior.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Java Object Monitor — Internal State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Runnable: start()
    Runnable --> Blocked: synchronized(obj) failed
    Runnable --> Waiting: wait()
    Runnable --> TimedWaiting: wait(timeout)
    Blocked --> Runnable: lock acquired
    Waiting --> Blocked: notify() / notifyAll()
    TimedWaiting --> Blocked: timeout elapsed
    Runnable --> Terminated: run() returns
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Synchronized Method Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread T1 calls synchronized method] --> B{Is monitor free?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Acquire monitor on this]
    C --> D[Execute critical section]
    D --> E[Release monitor]
    E --> F[Continue normal execution]
    B -- No --> G[Add T1 to Entry Set]
    G --> H[Thread state: BLOCKED]
    H --> I[Wait for monitor owner to release]
    I --> J{Owner released?}
    J -- Yes --> C
    J -- No --> H
```

## 4.3 Inter-Thread Communication — wait / notify Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer Thread
    participant B as BoundedBuffer
    participant C as Consumer Thread

    P->>B: produce(item) [acquires lock]
    B-->>P: executes inside synchronized
    P->>B: notifyAll() [lock still held]
    P->>B: exits synchronized [releases lock]
    C->>B: consume() [competes for lock]
    B-->>C: acquires lock, resumes
    Note over C: was in WAITING state<br/>now RUNNABLE
```

## 4.4 Deadlock Visual — Circular Wait Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Thread T1] -- holds --> LA[Lock A]
    T1 -- waits for --> LB[Lock B]
    T2[Thread T2] -- holds --> LB
    T2 -- waits for --> LA
    LA -. held by T1 .-> T1
    LB -. held by T2 .-> T2
    style T1 fill:#ffcccb
    style T2 fill:#ffcccb
    style LA fill:#ffeb99
    style LB fill:#ffeb99
```

## 4.5 Producer-Consumer — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProducerSide [Producer Thread]
        P1[Generate Item]
        P2[Check Buffer Full]
        P3[Add to Buffer]
    end
    subgraph SharedBuffer [BoundedBuffer capacity C]
        Q[(Ring Queue)]
    end
    subgraph ConsumerSide [Consumer Thread]
        C1[Check Buffer Empty]
        C2[Remove from Buffer]
        C3[Process Item]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 -- synchronized put --> Q
    Q -- synchronized take --> C1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    Q -. wait() / notifyAll() .-> P2
    Q -. wait() / notifyAll() .-> C1
```

## 4.6 Comparison Matrix — `synchronized` vs `ReentrantLock`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Java Thread Synchronization] --> B[Intrinsic synchronized]
    A --> C[Explicit java.util.concurrent.locks]
    B --> B1[Monitor lock on this]
    B --> B2[Automatic release on exception]
    B --> B3[No tryLock option]
    B --> B4[No fairness option]
    C --> C1[ReentrantLock]
    C --> C2[ReadWriteLock]
    C1 --> C1a[tryLock available]
    C1 --> C1b[Fair lock option new ReentrantLock true]
    C1 --> C1c[Multiple condition variables]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of a race condition in Java. Give one example.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
A **race condition** occurs when two or more threads access shared data concurrently, and the final outcome depends on the unpredictable ordering of their execution.

**Example:** Two threads simultaneously call `counter.increment()`. The statement `count++` is internally a read-modify-write sequence. If both threads read the same value of `count` before either writes, the increments are **lost**, producing an incorrect final value.

```java
class Counter { int count = 0; void increment() { count++; } }
```

Without `synchronized`, the KTU-expected observation is that the final value of `count` may be **less than the expected** $2N$ (where $N$ is the number of increments per thread). The fix is to declare `increment()` as `synchronized`.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between `wait()` and `sleep()` in Java.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | `wait()` | `sleep()` |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| Defined in | `java.lang.Object` | `java.lang.Thread` |
| Releases lock? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Must be in synchronized? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Wakes via | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` or timeout | Timeout only |
| Purpose | Inter-thread communication | Pausing execution |
| Exception on interrupt | `InterruptedException` | `InterruptedException` |

> **1 mark** for the table or key distinction + **2 marks** for the explanation and synchronization context.

---

## 📌 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Java Object Monitor** and describe how `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` work for inter-thread communication. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. The Java Object Monitor (3 marks)**

Every Java object has an **intrinsic lock (monitor)** with three components:

- **Owner field** — the thread currently holding the lock.
- **Entry Set** — a queue of threads blocked trying to acquire the lock.
- **Wait Set** — a queue of threads that have called `wait()`.

**2. Methods (3 marks)**

- `wait()`: The calling thread **releases the monitor lock** and enters the Wait Set. It remains there until awakened by `notify()`, `notifyAll()`, or a `wait(timeout)` expiration.
- `notify()`: Selects **one arbitrary thread** from the Wait Set and moves it to the Entry Set, where it must re-acquire the lock to proceed.
- `notifyAll()`: Moves **all** threads from the Wait Set to the Entry Set.

**3. Diagram (1 mark) — As shown in Section 4.1 above.**

> The state transitions among Runnable → Blocked → Waiting are governed by lock acquisition, `wait()`, and `notify()` calls.

---

**Q. (b)** Write a Java program to solve the **Producer-Consumer problem** using `wait()` and `notifyAll()`. Explain why `notifyAll()` is preferred over `notify()`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Program (5 marks)**

```java
class BoundedBuffer {
    private final int[] buffer;
    private int count = 0, in = 0, out = 0;

    public BoundedBuffer(int capacity) { buffer = new int[capacity]; }

    public synchronized void produce(int item) throws InterruptedException {
        while (count == buffer.length)
            wait();
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % buffer.length;
        count++;
        notifyAll();
    }

    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (count == 0)
            wait();
        int item = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % buffer.length;
        count--;
        notifyAll();
        return item;
    }
}
```

**2. Why `notifyAll()` over `notify()` (2 marks)**

`notify()` wakes **only one** waiting thread, but the JVM gives **no guarantee** about which one is chosen. If the **wrong type** of thread is woken (e.g., a producer wakes when buffer is full), it re-enters `wait()`, and no further `notify()` will occur, leading to **deadlock**. `notifyAll()` wakes all waiters, allowing each to re-check its guard condition — this is always safe.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **four Coffman conditions** necessary for a deadlock. With a Java code example, show how a deadlock can occur and how it can be prevented using **lock ordering**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Four Coffman Conditions (3 marks)**

A deadlock requires **all four** conditions to hold simultaneously:

1. **Mutual Exclusion** — At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode.
2. **Hold and Wait** — A thread holding resources is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other threads.
3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly removed; they are only released voluntarily.
4. **Circular Wait** — A circular chain of threads exists, where each thread holds a resource the next one needs.

Negating **any one** of these conditions prevents deadlock.

**2. Java Deadlock Code (2 marks)**

```java
class Transfer {
    public static void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amt) {
        synchronized (a) {              // T1 holds A
            synchronized (b) {          // T1 waits for B
                a.withdraw(amt);
                b.deposit(amt);
            }
        }
    }
}
// T1 calls transfer(A, B), T2 calls transfer(B, A) → DEADLOCK
```

**3. Prevention via Lock Ordering (2 marks)**

Acquire locks in a **globally consistent order** (e.g., by `System.identityHashCode`):

```java
public static void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amt) {
    Account first  = (System.identityHashCode(a) < System.identityHashCode(b)) ? a : b;
    Account second = (System.identityHashCode(a) < System.identityHashCode(b)) ? b : a;
    synchronized (first) {
        synchronized (second) { a.withdraw(amt); b.deposit(amt); }
    }
}
```

This **breaks the Circular Wait** condition, guaranteeing deadlock freedom.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare `synchronized` and `ReentrantLock`. Write a Java program demonstrating the use of `ReentrantLock` with `tryLock()` to **avoid deadlock**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Comparison (3 marks)**

| Feature | `synchronized` | `ReentrantLock` |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Lock release | Automatic (on exit/exception) | Manual (must be in `finally`) |
| `tryLock()` | ❌ Not available | ✅ Non-blocking attempt |
| Interruptible | ❌ Cannot interrupt | ✅ `lockInterruptibly()` |
| Fairness | ❌ Unfair | ✅ Optional FIFO |
| Condition vars | One (implicit) | Multiple (`newCondition()`) |
| Performance | Optimized in modern JVM | Comparable; better in high contention |

**2. Program using `tryLock()` (4 marks)**

```java
import java.util.concurrent.locks.*;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

class SafeTransfer {
    private final Lock lockA = new ReentrantLock();
    private final Lock lockB = new ReentrantLock();

    public void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amt) throws InterruptedException {
        while (true) {
            if (lockA.tryLock(50, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)) {
                try {
                    if (lockB.tryLock(50, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)) {
                        try {
                            a.withdraw(amt);
                            b.deposit(amt);
                            return;        // success
                        } finally { lockB.unlock(); }
                    }
                } finally { lockA.unlock(); }
            }
            Thread.sleep(10);  // back-off reduces contention
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** `tryLock()` returns `false` immediately if the lock is unavailable, instead of blocking. By releasing `lockA` if `lockB` cannot be acquired, the **Hold-and-Wait** condition is broken, preventing deadlock.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Thread Synchronization Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using `if` instead of `while` with `wait()`** — Misses the guard against spurious wakeups. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to call `wait()`/`notify()` inside a synchronized block** — Causes `IllegalMonitorStateException` at runtime. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Claiming `volatile` makes `count++` thread-safe** — It does NOT. `volatile` provides visibility, not atomicity. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting the `finally` block in explicit Lock** — Leaves the lock unreleased on exception, causing deadlock. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing `notify()` with `notifyAll()`** — Using `notify()` in producer-consumer is a deadlock risk; `notifyAll()` is the safe default. **−1 mark.**
>
> **Bonus Tip:** Always draw the **monitor state diagram** (Owner, Entry Set, Wait Set) for full-marks answers on inter-thread communication. It earns 1–2 extra marks even if your explanation is brief.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Thread Synchronization**

- 🔹 **Race Condition** — Outcome depends on thread scheduling; fix with `synchronized` or atomic classes.
- 🔹 **Critical Section** — Code segment accessing shared resources; minimize its size for performance.
- 🔹 **`synchronized` method** — Lock is on `this` (instance) or `Class` object (static).
- 🔹 **`synchronized` block** — Lock is on explicitly specified object; **preferred** for fine-grained locking.
- 🔹 **`volatile`** — Visibility + ordering, **no atomicity**; use for single-flag reads/writes only.
- 🔹 **`ReentrantLock`** — Explicit, supports `tryLock()`, fair lock, and `lockInterruptibly()`.
- 🔹 **Always release explicit locks in `finally`** — Non-negotiable best practice.
- 🔹 **`wait()` releases the lock; `sleep()` does not** — Critical distinction.
- 🔹 **Always use `while` (not `if`) to guard `wait()`** — Prevents spurious wakeup bugs.
- 🔹 **Prefer `notifyAll()` over `notify()`** — Avoids the "lost wakeup" / "wrong thread" deadlock.
- 🔹 **Deadlock requires all 4 Coffman conditions** — Break any one to prevent it.
- 🔹 **Lock ordering prevents deadlock** — Acquire locks in a globally consistent sequence.
- 🔹 **`tryLock()` with timeout prevents deadlock** — Breaks the Hold-and-Wait condition.
- 🔹 **`join()` enables happens-before** — A thread joining on another sees all its writes.
- 🔹 **Happens-before relation** — The mathematical foundation of JMM visibility guarantees.
- 🔹 **`BlockingQueue`** — The modern, recommended approach for producer-consumer; eliminates manual `wait`/`notify` bugs.
- 🔹 **`ConcurrentHashMap`** — Use instead of `Hashtable` for thread-safe maps.
- 🔹 **Common interview/KTU question** — *"Why is `String` used as a lock object a bad practice?"* → Strings are interned; using `"lock"` creates a global lock, reducing concurrency and creating surprising contention.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swings Fundamentals - Swing Key Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swings Fundamentals - Swing Key Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Swing** is a GUI (Graphical User Interface) widget toolkit for Java, forming the lightweight component layer of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**. It is part of the `javax.swing` package (extended from `java.awt`) and provides a richer, more flexible set of UI components than the original Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Object Oriented Programming, **Swing fundamentals** refers to the architectural foundation upon which Java's desktop GUI applications are built, including the component hierarchy, the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern, the lightweight/heavyweight component distinction, and the *pluggable look-and-feel* mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> The expected outcomes require students to *describe the key features of Swing, differentiate it from AWT, and understand the MVC architecture along with the class hierarchy rooted at `java.awt.Container` and `javax.swing.JComponent`.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are designing a **modern car dashboard**:

- The **AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit)** is like an old-school dashboard where every gauge (speedometer, fuel indicator, temperature) is *welded directly onto the car's metal frame* using the operating system's native parts. If you want a different style, you have to physically replace the metal. AWT components are called **heavyweight** because they rely on the host OS (peer components) for rendering.
- **Swing**, on the other hand, is like a **digital, software-painted dashboard**. Every gauge is drawn entirely by Java itself, sitting on a canvas. You can reskin the entire dashboard (change fonts, colors, themes) at runtime, and even make it look like Windows, macOS, or Linux — all without touching the underlying car.

This *paint-it-yourself* nature is what makes Swing components **lightweight** and gives them the famous **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF)** feature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Definition for Exam:**
> *"Swing is a lightweight, Java-native, MVC-based GUI toolkit in `javax.swing` that provides platform-independent, pluggable UI components built entirely in Java."*

### 1.3 Key Constants / Metrics (For Exam Reference)

| Property | Value/Description |
|---|---|
| **Package** | `javax.swing` (extends `java.awt`) |
| **Base Class** | `javax.swing.JComponent` (extends `java.awt.Container` → `java.awt.Component`) |
| **Java Version** | Introduced in **JDK 1.2 (1998)** as part of JFC |
| **MVC Pattern** | Model-View-Controller (introduced by Swing for UI separation) |
| **Default L&F** | **Metal** (Cross-platform) |
| **Thread Policy** | *Single-threaded rule* — all UI updates on **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** |
| **Renderer** | 100% Java (no native peer — except for top-level containers) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Was Swing Introduced? — The AWT Limitations

Before understanding Swing's *key features*, students must understand *why* it was created. The pre-Swing era used **AWT**, which had these core problems:

- **Heavyweight Dependency:** Every AWT component (Button, TextField) mapped 1-to-1 to a native OS peer. Behavior varied across platforms ("Write Once, Run Anywhere" was broken).
- **Limited Component Set:** AWT had only ~10 basic components (no tree, no table, no tabbed pane).
- **No Pluggable Look-and-Feel:** The appearance was locked to the host OS.
- **No MVC:** UI and data were tightly fused inside a single class.

Swing solved each of these — and its **key features** are precisely the architectural solutions to these problems.

## 2.2 The Key Features of Swing (The "Big 7")

### Feature 1: **Lightweight Components**
- A **lightweight component** does *not* rely on the host OS for rendering. It is drawn by Java onto a parent heavyweight container (typically `JFrame`, which extends AWT's `Frame`).
- **Engineering Utility:** Cross-platform pixel-identical rendering; smaller binary footprint; supports overlapping/translucent components.

### Feature 2: **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)**
- The *appearance* and *behavior* of components is decoupled from the components themselves.
- You can switch the entire UI at runtime using `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`.
- **Standard L&F options in Java:**
  - `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` (Default, Java's own)
  - `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel`
  - `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif.MotifLookAndFeel`
  - GTK on Linux (added later)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for PLAF classes — "MWN":** **M**etal, **W**indows, **N**otif (GTK is bonus).

### Feature 3: **MVC Architecture (Model-View-Controller)**
- Swing was the first Java GUI toolkit to formally adopt **MVC** to separate:
  - **Model:** The data (e.g., `DefaultTableModel` holds rows of a `JTable`).
  - **View:** The visual rendering (e.g., the cells painted on screen).
  - **Controller:** The listener/interaction logic (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`).
- **Engineering Utility:** Allows the *same data* to be displayed in multiple views (e.g., a `JTable` and a `JChart` reading the same model) and easier unit testing.

> [!NOTE]
> Swing's actual implementation is closer to a **"Model-Delegate"** (a simplified MVC) where the View and Controller are combined into a *UI Delegate* object, but the *conceptual* MVC separation is what KTU examiners test.

### Feature 4: **Rich, Expandable Component Set**
- Swing provides **~40+ components**, far beyond AWT.
- **Critical Components for KTU:**
  `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JPasswordField`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JDialog`, `JOptionPane`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`.

### Feature 5: **Double Buffering (Built-in)**
- Swing automatically uses **double buffering** — drawing to an off-screen image buffer and then copying to screen in one pass.
- **Result:** Eliminates flicker during repaints (critical for animations and drag-resize).

### Feature 6: **Key-Binding & Accessibility (Java Accessibility API)**
- Components support **mnemonics** (e.g., `setMnemonic('S')` for Alt+S) and **accelerators** (Ctrl+S).
- Built-in support for screen readers, Braille, etc., via the `javax.accessibility` package.

### Feature 7: **Event Delegation Model (Inherited from AWT 1.1)**
- Events are *delegated* to listener objects (Observer pattern), eliminating the inheritance-based event handling of Java 1.0.
- **Why this matters in KTU:** Exam questions frequently test `addActionListener`, `addMouseListener`, `addKeyListener`, etc.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **highest-yield comparison** for KTU Part-A 3-mark questions. Memorize it.

| Property | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| **Package** | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| **Component Type** | Heavyweight (native peer) | Lightweight (Java-painted) |
| **Pluggable L&F** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (`UIManager`) |
| **MVC Architecture** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Model-Delegate) |
| **Component Count** | ~10 basic | ~40+ rich |
| **Top-Level Container** | `Frame`, `Dialog` | `JFrame`, `JDialog` |
| **Button Class** | `Button` | `JButton` |
| **Double Buffering** | Manual | Built-in |
| **Thread Safety** | Not thread-safe | Not thread-safe (use EDT) |
| **Introduced** | JDK 1.0 | **JDK 1.2 (1998)** |
| **Platform Dependence** | High (OS-dependent look) | Low (Java-rendered) |
| **Button Prefix Convention** | None | All start with **"J"** (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JFrame`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *All Swing components start with **J** — think **"J" for Java-rendered"**.*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Swing Features

- **PLAF:** Used in IDEs (NetBeans, IntelliJ) to allow user theme switching.
- **MVC:** Foundation for modern JavaFX (which is pure MVC) and web frameworks (Spring MVC).
- **Lightweight Rendering:** Concept extended to HTML5 Canvas and Android's `View` system.
- **Double Buffering:** The same technique is used in game engines and embedded graphics (STM32 LTDC layers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> In this section, since the topic is **architectural/conceptual** rather than formulaic, "derivations" are replaced with **structured analytical reasoning flows** that derive *why* each Swing feature is engineered the way it is — exactly how a KTU board examiner expects a 14-mark answer to be built up.

## 3.1 Analytical Step-By-Step: Why Swing Components Are Lightweight

**Step 1 — Identify the AWT Problem:**
AWT's `Button` class delegates rendering to the OS. Therefore, on Windows it looks like a Windows button, on Mac it looks like a Mac button, and on Linux it looks like a Motif button. This breaks the *Write Once, Run Anywhere* promise.

**Step 2 — Define the Architectural Goal:**
The goal is a component whose pixels are drawn **by Java itself**, using `java.awt.Graphics` primitives (`drawString`, `fillRect`, `drawImage`).

**Step 3 — Identify the Required Inheritance Trick:**
Java cannot draw "into nothing" — there must be a *native window* to receive the OS-level paint events. Swing solves this by:

$$
\text{JComponent} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{extends } \texttt{java.awt.Container} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{extends } \texttt{java.awt.Component}
$$

The `Component` class eventually holds a *single* native peer (the top-level `JFrame`). All child Swing components are painted by Java into this one native canvas.

**Step 4 — Logical Deduction:**
Therefore, a Swing `JButton` is a **lightweight component** because it does not own a native peer. It "lives" inside the peer of its `JFrame` ancestor and is rendered by Java's `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method.

**Step 5 — Final Engineering Property:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Lightweight} = \text{Drawn by Java's } paintComponent(Graphics)\ \text{method, no native peer.}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Step-By-Step: How Pluggable Look-and-Feel Works (UI Delegate Pattern)

**Step 1 — Decouple the "What" from the "How":**
Swing separates the component's *data/behavior* (e.g., is it pressed? what is its label?) from its *rendering logic* (how to draw a pressed button).

**Step 2 — Introduce the UI Delegate Object:**
Each Swing component has a private `ComponentUI` delegate object that *knows how to draw it*.

$$
\text{JButton} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{button.setUI}(\texttt{new WindowsButtonUI()})
$$

**Step 3 — Look up the Delegate at Construction Time:**
When a Swing component is created, the `UIManager` looks up the current global L&F and asks it to install the appropriate UI delegate.

$$
\texttt{UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \texttt{UIManager.setUI(new MetalButtonUI())}
$$

**Step 4 — Switch L&F Dynamically:**
Calling `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)` after a `setLookAndFeel` call forces every component to swap its delegate, achieving a runtime theme change.

**Step 5 — Final Engineering Property:**

$$
\boxed{\text{PLAF} = \text{Swap the } \texttt{ComponentUI} \text{ delegate at runtime via } \texttt{UIManager}.}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Step-By-Step: MVC in Swing (Using JSlider as the Canonical Example)

**Step 1 — Model Class:** `BoundedRangeModel` (interface), typically `DefaultBoundedRangeModel`.
Holds the data: `value`, `minimum`, `maximum`, `extent`.
Provides `addChangeListener(...)` for state-change notifications.

**Step 2 — View + Controller:** `BasicSliderUI` (the UI delegate) and the `JSlider` class itself.
The UI delegate **draws** the track, thumb, and ticks. The `JSlider` class processes **mouse drags** and translates them to `setValue()` calls on the model.

**Step 3 — Communication Flow:**

$$
\text{User drags mouse} \;\xrightarrow{\text{event}}\; \text{JSlider (Controller)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{setValue}}\; \text{BoundedRangeModel} \;\xrightarrow{\text{fireStateChanged}}\; \text{UI Delegate (View) repaints}
$$

**Step 4 — Independence Proof:** You can change the model (e.g., `model.setValue(50)`) and the slider thumb moves on screen **without any mouse event** — proving the View is decoupled from the data source.

**Step 5 — Final Engineering Property:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Model-Delegate} = \text{Data (Model)} \longleftrightarrow \text{Draw+Listen (Delegate)} \longleftrightarrow \text{User (Controller)}.}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Swing Features to Problems They Solve

| # | AWT Problem | Swing Feature | Code-Level Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OS-dependent rendering | Lightweight Components | `paintComponent(Graphics g)` override |
| 2 | Fixed OS appearance | Pluggable Look-and-Feel | `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` |
| 3 | Tight UI-Data coupling | MVC (Model-Delegate) | `setModel(...)`, `ComponentUI` |
| 4 | Few components | Rich Component Set | `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane` etc. |
| 5 | Flicker on repaint | Double Buffering | Automatic in `RepaintManager` |
| 6 | No keyboard shortcuts | Key Binding API | `setMnemonic(...)`, `getInputMap()` |
| 7 | Inherited event handling | Event Delegation | `addXxxListener(...)` (from AWT 1.1) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The single most important takeaway is that `JComponent` is the **root of *most*** Swing components (everything except top-level windows like `JFrame` and `JDialog`, which inherit from `java.awt` because they need a native peer). Drawing this hierarchy correctly scores full marks.

## 4.2 MVC (Model-Delegate) Flow in Swing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[USER] -->|MOUSE OR KEY EVENT| C[CONTROLLER - LISTENER OBJECT]
    C -->|UPDATE DATA| M[MODEL - EG DEFAULTBOUNDEDRANGEMODEL]
    M -->|FIRESTATECHANGED EVENT| V[VIEW - COMPONENTUI DELEGATE]
    V -->|PAINTCOMPONENT CALL| SC[SCREEN PIXELS]
    SC -->|VISUAL FEEDBACK| U
```

## 4.3 Pluggable Look-and-Feel Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[JAVA APPLICATION] -->|SETLOOKANDFEEL CALL| UIM[UIMANAGER SINGLETON]
    UIM -->|LOOKUP CLASS| LAF1[METAL LOOK AND FEEL]
    UIM -->|LOOKUP CLASS| LAF2[WINDOWS LOOK AND FEEL]
    UIM -->|LOOKUP CLASS| LAF3[MOTIF LOOK AND FEEL]
    LAF1 --> DEL1[BUTTONUI DELEGATE A]
    LAF2 --> DEL2[BUTTONUI DELEGATE B]
    LAF3 --> DEL3[BUTTONUI DELEGATE C]
    DEL1 --> JB[JBUTTON INSTANCE]
    DEL2 --> JB
    DEL3 --> JB
    JB -->|UPDATECOMPONENTTREEUI| SCREEN[REPAINT WITH NEW THEME]
```

## 4.4 AWT vs Swing — Component Layering Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph OS_LAYER[OPERATING SYSTEM LAYER]
        OS1[WINDOWS WIN32 API]
        OS2[MACOS COCOA]
        OS3[LINUX GTK]
    end
    subgraph AWT_LAYER[AWT HEAVYWEIGHT PEER LAYER]
        AW1[BUTTON PEER]
        AW2[TEXTFIELD PEER]
        AW3[CANVAS PEER]
    end
    subgraph SWING_LAYER[SWING LIGHTWEIGHT LAYER]
        SW1[JBUTTON - PAINTED BY JAVA]
        SW2[JTEXTFIELD - PAINTED BY JAVA]
        SW3[JTABLE - PAINTED BY JAVA]
    end
    OS1 --- AW1
    OS2 --- AW1
    OS3 --- AW1
    AW1 --- SW1
    AW2 --- SW2
    AW3 --- SW3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** AWT peers sit *between* Java and the OS, calling native APIs. Swing sits *entirely above* AWT and is painted in pure Java — it never touches the OS directly (except via the one peer owned by `JFrame`).
