# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> The questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns. Part A is 3 marks each. Part B is 14 marks each with internal choice. The **mark split (7 + 7)** follows the KTU ESE 2024 regulation for 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between AWT and Swing. List any four key features of Swing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Components | Heavyweight (native peer) | Lightweight (Java-painted) |
| Look & Feel | Fixed by OS | Pluggable |
| Components | Limited (~10) | Rich (~40+) |
| Architecture | No MVC | MVC (Model-Delegate) |

**Four Key Features of Swing:**
1. **Lightweight components** — painted by Java, no native peer.
2. **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF)** — runtime theme switching via `UIManager`.
3. **MVC (Model-Delegate) architecture** — separates data, view, and control.
4. **Built-in double buffering** — eliminates flicker on repaint.
5. *(Any other valid: rich component set, accessibility, key binding API.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**What is Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF) in Swing? How is it implemented at the API level?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PLAF is a Swing feature that allows the *appearance* of all components to be changed at runtime without changing the application code.

**Implementation Steps (API-level):**
1. The current L&F is stored as a `LookAndFeel` object inside the `UIManager` singleton.
2. The static call `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)` swaps the active L&F.
3. Each Swing component, on construction, asks `UIManager` for its `ComponentUI` delegate (e.g., `MetalButtonUI`, `WindowsButtonUI`), which knows how to draw that specific component under the active L&F.
4. The static call `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)` re-installs delegates on every component, triggering a repaint with the new theme.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Lose 1 mark here):** Students often write *"`setLookAndFeel()` is enough."* **It is not.** You MUST call `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI()` afterwards, or the existing components will not repaint with the new L&F.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the MVC architecture used in Swing with a suitable diagram. How is it different from the actual implementation in Swing? **(7 marks)**
*(b)* With suitable code snippets, explain how Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF) is implemented in Java Swing. Demonstrate switching from Metal L&F to Windows L&F. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**MVC Conceptual Model (3 marks):**
- **Model:** Stores the *data* and the *business rules*. Knows nothing about how it is displayed.
- **View:** Renders the model into a visual form. Knows nothing about how the data is manipulated.
- **Controller:** Receives user input, translates it to model updates, and triggers view repaints.

**Diagrammatic Representation (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[USER] -->|INPUT| CTR[CONTROLLER]
    CTR -->|UPDATE| M[MODEL - DATA]
    M -->|NOTIFY| V[VIEW - RENDER]
    V -->|VISUAL OUTPUT| U
```

**How Swing Implements It (2 marks):**
- Swing uses a **Model-Delegate** variant, not pure MVC.
- **Model:** Interfaces like `ButtonModel`, `BoundedRangeModel`, `ListModel`, `TableModel`. Concrete classes: `DefaultButtonModel`, `DefaultBoundedRangeModel`, `DefaultTableModel`.
- **View + Controller combined:** The `ComponentUI` delegate. It both *paints* the component (View) and *intercepts* mouse/key events (Controller).
- **Example — `JSlider`:**
  - Model = `DefaultBoundedRangeModel` (holds value, min, max).
  - Delegate = `BasicSliderUI` subclass.
  - When user drags thumb, delegate calls `slider.setValue(v)` → `model.setValue(v)` → `model.fireStateChanged()` → delegate's `paint()` repaints.

**Difference from Pure MVC (1 mark):**
In pure MVC, View and Controller are separate objects. In Swing, they are *fused* into a single `ComponentUI` delegate object for performance and simplicity. The Model, however, is fully decoupled — the same `DefaultTableModel` can be shared between a `JTable` and a `JTree`.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Theory of PLAF (2 marks):**
PLAF decouples *what* a component is (a button) from *how* it is drawn (rectangle vs rounded, blue vs grey, etc.). The class responsible for drawing is `ComponentUI`. The global registry is `javax.swing.UIManager`.

**Step 2 — Standard L&F Classes (2 marks):**
- `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` — Default cross-platform.
- `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel` — Windows only.
- `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif.MotifLookAndFeel` — Unix/Motif.
- `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.gtk.GTKLookAndFeel` — Linux GTK.

**Step 3 — Code Snippet (3 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class PLAFDemo extends JFrame {
    public PLAFDemo() {
        setTitle("PLAF Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        add(new JButton("JButton 1"));
        add(new JButton("JButton 2"));
        add(new JLabel("This is a JLabel"));

        // --- Switch L&F programmatically ---
        try {
            // Uncomment ONE line below to test
            // UIManager.setLookAndFeel(
            //     "javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel");
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel(
                "com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel");

            SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PLAFDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code (1 mark — verbal in answer):**
1. `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` registers the new L&F globally.
2. `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this)` walks the entire component tree of `this` frame and asks each component to install the new L&F's UI delegate.
3. The frame is then repainted with the new theme.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Draw and explain the Swing component class hierarchy starting from `java.lang.Object` up to `JButton`. Why are top-level containers like `JFrame` not subclasses of `JComponent`? **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Explain the concept of Lightweight vs Heavyweight components with examples. Why does Swing recommend mixing only at a "boundary"? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Hierarchy Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    O[java.lang.Object] --> C[java.awt.Component]
    C --> CT[java.awt.Container]
    CT --> JC[javax.swing.JComponent]
    JC --> AB[javax.swing.AbstractButton]
    AB --> JB[javax.swing.JButton]
```

**Explanation of Each Level (2 marks):**
- **`java.lang.Object`** — root of all Java classes; provides `toString`, `equals`, `hashCode`.
- **`java.awt.Component`** — base for *anything that can be displayed on screen*; has properties like `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`, `foreground`, `background`, `font`. Has the abstract method `paint(Graphics g)`.
- **`java.awt.Container`** — a `Component` that can *hold other components*; introduces `add(Component)`, `remove(Component)`, layout managers (`BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`).
- **`javax.swing.JComponent`** — extends `Container`; adds Swing-specific features: **double buffering, borders, tooltips, key bindings, accessibility support, pluggable UI delegates**. Abstract class.
- **`javax.swing.AbstractButton`** — extends `JComponent`; common code for all button-like components (label, icon, mnemonic, model, action command).
- **`javax.swing.JButton`** — concrete push-button; the most common Swing control.

**Why `JFrame` is NOT a `JComponent` (2 marks):**
A `JFrame` is a **top-level container** and must be a **heavyweight** component — it must own a native OS window (peer) to receive OS-level events (close, minimize, resize, focus). The `JComponent` class is designed for **lightweight, Java-painted** components. A `JFrame` therefore extends `java.awt.Frame` (which extends `java.awt.Window` → `java.awt.Container` → `java.awt.Component`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The rule of thumb:** *Everything starting with `J` except the top-level containers* (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`) *is a `JComponent`.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Heavyweight Components (3 marks):**
- A heavyweight component is associated with a **native peer** in the host OS.
- It "owns" a portion of the OS's screen real estate.
- All **AWT components** (`Button`, `TextField`, `Canvas`, `Panel`) and Swing's **top-level containers** (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`) are heavyweight.
- **Example:** `JFrame` creates an actual Win32/Motif/Cocoa window internally.

**Lightweight Components (2 marks):**
- A lightweight component has **no native peer**. It is drawn by Java onto its heavyweight ancestor's screen surface.
- All non-top-level Swing components (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JPanel`, `JTable`, `JTree`, etc.) are lightweight.
- **Example:** A `JButton` added to a `JFrame` is rendered by Java's `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method on the same native canvas as the frame.

**The "Boundary" Mixing Rule (2 marks):**
Swing's official recommendation:
- **Do not add a heavyweight AWT component inside a lightweight Swing container.** If you do, the AWT peer is rendered by the OS *on top of* the Swing paint, causing **z-order flickering** and clipping issues.
- **Do not add a lightweight Swing component directly on a heavyweight AWT container** (e.g., adding `JButton` to `java.awt.Panel`). It may work, but event delivery can fail on some platforms.
- **The only safe mixing is at the boundary:** place all Swing components inside one `JFrame`/`JApplet`, and use heavyweight AWT canvases (`java.awt.Canvas`) only when you specifically need OS-native rendering (e.g., OpenGL binding via `GLCanvas` from JOGL).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Lose 1–2 marks):**
> 1. Writing that "Swing has *no* heavyweight components." — **False.** `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet` ARE heavyweight.
> 2. Confusing `java.awt.Panel` (heavyweight) with `javax.swing.JPanel` (lightweight). The Swing version is the one you should use 99% of the time.
> 3. Forgetting to call `revalidate()` and `repaint()` after `add()`-ing a new component to a visible container — the component will be added to the data structure but not redrawn.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Bullet Points (Print this on your mental index card):**

- [ ] **Swing** is the GUI toolkit in `javax.swing`; introduced in **JDK 1.2** as part of **JFC** (Java Foundation Classes).
- [ ] **All non-top-level Swing components start with `J`** and extend `JComponent` (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTable`).
- [ ] **Top-level containers** — `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow` — extend AWT classes (NOT `JComponent`) because they need a native peer.
- [ ] **AWT** = Heavyweight, OS-peered, fixed L&F, limited components. **Swing** = Lightweight, Java-painted, PLAF, MVC, rich components.
- [ ] **Lightweight** = drawn by Java's `paintComponent(Graphics g)`, no native peer.
- [ ] **PLAF (Pluggable Look-and-Feel)** is implemented via the `UIManager` singleton + per-component `ComponentUI` delegate objects.
- [ ] **PLAF switching code pattern:** `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)` **→** `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)`.
- [ ] **MVC in Swing** is actually a **Model-Delegate** pattern: Model = `*Model` interface (e.g., `ButtonModel`, `TableModel`); Delegate = `*UI` class (e.g., `BasicButtonUI`).
- [ ] Swing provides **built-in double buffering** → no flicker.
- [ ] All UI updates must happen on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** (use `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)`).
- [ ] **Boundary rule:** Don't mix AWT heavyweight components inside Swing containers — it causes z-order flicker.
- [ ] Event handling uses the **Event Delegation Model**: `component.addXxxListener(listenerObject)`.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for top-level containers: "F-D-A-W"** → **F**rame, **D**ialog, **A**pplet, **W**indow (all extend AWT, not JComponent).
- [ ] Default L&F is **Metal**; other classics are **Windows** and **Motif**.
- [ ] The most important inheritance chain: `Object → Component → Container → JComponent → AbstractButton → JButton`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Components and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing, every graphical user interface is built using two foundational abstractions: **Component** and **Container**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component (java.awt.Component):** The abstract base class for all objects that can be displayed on the screen and can interact with the user. A component is an *atomic* visual element such as a `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, or `List`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Container (java.awt.Container):** A specialized subclass of `Component` whose primary role is to *hold* and *organize* other components (including other containers). Examples include `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Panel`, `Applet`, and `ScrollPane`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Furniture and Room" Model

Imagine a house:

- A **Component** is a single piece of furniture — a chair, a table, or a lamp. Each piece has its own appearance and use, but it cannot hold anything *inside* itself.
- A **Container** is a *room* (or a shelf inside a room). It can hold multiple pieces of furniture (Components) and even other smaller rooms (nested Containers).

```
HOUSE (Top-Level Container: Frame)
  ├── ROOM-1 (Container: Panel)
  │     ├── CHAIR (Component: Button)
  │     ├── LAMP  (Component: Label)
  │     └── TABLE (Component: TextField)
  └── ROOM-2 (Container: Panel)
        └── CLOSET (Container: Panel)
              └── SHOE  (Component: Checkbox)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Every `Container` IS-A `Component` (single inheritance chain), but NOT every `Component` is a `Container`. This is the cardinal rule of the AWT hierarchy.

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Reusability:** Buttons in different containers behave the same way because the *behavior* lives in the `Component` class, not in each container.
- **Composability:** Complex GUIs are built by *nesting* containers, mimicking real-world layouts (a window contains panels, panels contain buttons).
- **Platform Independence (AWT) / Pluggable Look-and-Feel (Swing):** Abstracting visual primitives lets the same code run on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

---

**Physical/Standard Metrics in Bold:**

- The **AWT Component Hierarchy** has a depth of **5 inheritance levels** from `Object` to `Component`.
- The **default layout manager** of a `Frame` is `BorderLayout`; the default for a `Panel` is `FlowLayout`.
- AWT components are **heavyweight** (peer-based, OS-dependent); Swing components are **lightweight** (pure Java, OS-independent look).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The AWT Class Hierarchy (Operational Breakdown)

```
java.lang.Object
        │
        └── java.awt.Component  ◄── Abstract base class
                  │
                  └── java.awt.Container ◄── Can hold other Components
                            │
        ┌───────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
        │                   │                        │
   java.awt.Panel    java.awt.Window          java.awt.ScrollPane
        │                   │
   (e.g., Applet)    java.awt.Frame
                            │
                       java.awt.Dialog
```

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Hierarchy

1. **Step 1 — Object:** The root of *all* Java classes.
2. **Step 2 — Component:** Adds *visual* capabilities — `setSize()`, `setLocation()`, `setBackground()`, `repaint()`, `addMouseListener()`.
3. **Step 3 — Container:** Adds the ability to *hold* other components — `add(Component c)`, `remove(Component c)`, `setLayout(LayoutManager m)`.
4. **Step 4 — Window & Panel:** Two main branches.
   - `Window` → a *top-level* container with no borders or title bar; subclassed by `Frame` and `Dialog`.
   - `Panel` → a *generic* container that must be placed inside another container (typically a `Frame`).
5. **Step 5 — Frame / Dialog / Applet:** Ready-to-use top-level windows.

## 2.2 The Swing Hierarchy (JFC)

Swing follows a parallel hierarchy, replacing `Component` with **`JComponent`** and prefixing all class names with **`J`** (e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite:** `JFrame` extends `Frame` (AWT), but its *content pane* (where components are actually added) is a `JPanel`. You add components using `frame.getContentPane().add(...)` or, in modern Java, directly via `frame.add(...)` thanks to auto-content-pane forwarding.

## 2.3 Key Methods of `java.awt.Component`

| Method | Purpose | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `void setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets component dimensions in pixels | Layout calculations |
| `void setLocation(int x, int y)` | Sets top-left corner coordinates | Absolute positioning |
| `void setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Combines location + size | Most-used in KTU codes |
| `void setBackground(Color c)` | Sets background color | Visual customization |
| `void setForeground(Color c)` | Sets text/foreground color | Visual customization |
| `void setFont(Font f)` | Sets the text font | Font specification |
| `void setEnabled(boolean b)` | Enables/disables interaction | UI state control |
| `void setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows/hides the component | Window visibility |
| `void addMouseListener(MouseListener ml)` | Registers mouse events | Event handling |
| `void repaint()` | Requests a redraw | Dynamic updates |

## 2.4 Key Methods of `java.awt.Container`

| Method | Purpose | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `Component add(Component comp)` | Appends a component | **Most frequently asked** |
| `Component add(Component comp, int index)` | Inserts at a specific position | Indexed placement |
| `void remove(Component comp)` | Removes a specific component | Dynamic UIs |
| `void removeAll()` | Removes every child | Full reset |
| `void setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the layout strategy | **Layout questions** |
| `LayoutManager getLayout()` | Retrieves the current layout | Inspection |
| `Component[] getComponents()` | Returns all child components | Iteration |

## 2.5 The KTU Layout-Manager Cheat Sheet

| Layout Manager | Arrangement Strategy | Default Container | Constructor(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps to next line | `Panel`, `Applet` | `FlowLayout()`, `FlowLayout(int align)`, `FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)` |
| `BorderLayout` | 5 regions: North, South, East, West, Center | `Frame`, `Dialog` | `BorderLayout()`, `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` |
| `GridLayout` | Equal-sized cells in a rectangular grid | (Manual) | `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)` |
| `CardLayout` | Stack of cards — only one visible at a time | (Manual) | `CardLayout()`, `CardLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` |
| `GridBagLayout` | Most flexible, cell-based with constraints | (Manual) | `GridBagLayout()` |
| `BoxLayout` | Single row OR single column | `Box` / Panel | `BoxLayout(Container c, int axis)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Constants:** `FlowLayout.LEFT`, `FlowLayout.CENTER`, `FlowLayout.RIGHT`. `BorderLayout.NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. `BoxLayout.X_AXIS`, `BoxLayout.Y_AXIS`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Components and Containers form the **MVC View layer** in desktop software, IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ), banking UIs, and embedded HMI panels. Mastery of these is essential for **JavaFX migration paths**, **Swing-based test harnesses**, and **legacy enterprise maintenance**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Programs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why `Container` IS-A `Component` (Inheritance Proof)

Let us derive why a `Container` can be placed inside another `Container`.

**Step 1:** By Java's single-inheritance rule, every class extends exactly one parent (or `Object`).

**Step 2:** The AWT designers wanted containers to *also* be drawable, focusable, and resizable — properties already in `Component`.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the optimal OO design is:

$$\text{Container} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{Component} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{Object}$$

**Step 4:** Consequence — anywhere a `Component` is expected, a `Container` is also valid (Liskov Substitution Principle). So:

$$\text{panel.add(childContainer)} \;\checkmark \quad \text{when childContainer extends Container}$$

**Step 5:** This is *why* you can nest a `Panel` inside a `Frame`, and another `Panel` inside that `Panel`.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Program: AWT Demo

**Problem:** Build an AWT Frame containing a Label, a TextField, and two Buttons ("OK" and "Cancel"), arranged using `BorderLayout` and `FlowLayout`.

### Complete Code with Exhaustive Commentary

```java
import java.awt.*;

class AWTDemo extends Frame {
    AWTDemo() {
        // ----- Step 1: Set Frame properties -----
        setTitle("AWT Components & Containers Demo"); // Window title
        setSize(400, 200);                            // width=400, height=200 pixels
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));          // hgap=10, vgap=10

        // ----- Step 2: Create a Panel (Container) for the North region -----
        Panel northPanel = new Panel();
        northPanel.setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 5, 5));
        northPanel.add(new Label("Enter Name:"));     // Component: Label
        northPanel.add(new TextField(20));             // Component: TextField, 20 cols
        add(northPanel, BorderLayout.NORTH);          // Add container to Frame's North

        // ----- Step 3: Create a Panel for the South region with two Buttons -----
        Panel southPanel = new Panel();
        southPanel.setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.RIGHT, 5, 5));
        southPanel.add(new Button("OK"));              // Component: Button
        southPanel.add(new Button("Cancel"));          // Component: Button
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);          // Add container to Frame's South

        // ----- Step 4: A Component in the Center -----
        add(new Label("Center Display Area", Label.CENTER), BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // ----- Step 5: Make the window visible -----
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AWTDemo();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic Walkthrough

1. **Line 1–2:** Import the AWT package — gives access to `Frame`, `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Panel`, and all layout managers.
2. **Line 4:** `AWTDemo` *extends* `Frame`, so an `AWTDemo` object IS a top-level `Container` that can hold components.
3. **Line 8:** `setLayout()` is inherited from `Container`. It overrides the default `BorderLayout` of `Frame` (or sets it explicitly with gaps).
4. **Line 11–14:** A `Panel` is created, its layout set to `FlowLayout` (centered, 5px gaps), and a `Label` and `TextField` are *added* to it.
5. **Line 15:** The whole `northPanel` (a Container) is added to the Frame's NORTH region — demonstrating that a Container can hold another Container.
6. **Line 24:** `setVisible(true)` is the *final* trigger that makes the OS render the entire component tree.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Program: Swing Demo with `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`

```java
import javax.swing.*;

class SwingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // ----- Step 1: Create the top-level Container -----
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Swing Demo");
        frame.setSize(350, 150);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setLayout(new java.awt.FlowLayout());

        // ----- Step 2: Create Components -----
        JLabel label = new JLabel("Username:");
        JTextField textField = new JTextField(15);
        JButton loginBtn = new JButton("Login");
        JButton cancelBtn = new JButton("Cancel");

        // ----- Step 3: Add Components to the Content Pane -----
        frame.add(label);      // auto-routed to content pane
        frame.add(textField);
        frame.add(loginBtn);
        frame.add(cancelBtn);

        // ----- Step 4: Display -----
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Logic Walkthrough

1. **Step 1:** `JFrame` is the Swing top-level container. `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` makes clicking ✕ terminate the JVM.
2. **Step 2:** All components are prefixed with `J`. `JTextField(15)` creates a field of **15 columns** wide.
3. **Step 3:** In modern Swing (Java 1.5+), `frame.add()` is automatically delegated to the **content pane** (an internal `JPanel`).
4. **Step 4:** `setVisible(true)` triggers Swing's painting pipeline.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Layout

| Requirement | Best Layout | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Form with label + input rows | `GridLayout` or `GridBagLayout` | Aligned columns |
| Toolbar of icons | `FlowLayout` | Horizontal wrap |
| Window with header/footer | `BorderLayout` | Region-based |
| Tab-like wizard screens | `CardLayout` | Only one visible at a time |
| Dashboard with dynamic sizes | `GridBagLayout` | Maximum flexibility |
| Single row of buttons | `BoxLayout(X_AXIS)` | Tight control |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Component and a Container in Java AWT. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Component | Container |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Base class for all visual elements | Special Component that holds other Components |
| Can hold others? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Adds children? | Has no `add()` method | Has `add(Component)` method |
| Example | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField` | `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog` |
| Class | `java.awt.Component` (abstract) | `java.awt.Container` (abstract) |

A `Container` IS-A `Component` (inheritance), but not vice-versa.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the default layout managers of `Frame` and `Panel`. Why are they different?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The default layout of a `java.awt.Frame` is **`BorderLayout`**, providing 5 regions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER) suited to a top-level application window.
- The default layout of a `java.awt.Panel` is **`FlowLayout`**, arranging components left-to-right with line wrapping — ideal for a generic sub-container inside a window.
- The difference exists because a `Frame` is a *top-level* window needing structured regional placement, whereas a `Panel` is a *lightweight* helper container that simply stacks children naturally.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the AWT class hierarchy from `Object` to `Frame` with a neat diagram. Why is `Container` designed to extend `Component`? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java AWT program to create a Frame containing a `TextField`, a `Label`, and a `Button`. When the button is clicked, the label should display the text entered in the text field. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **Hierarchy** (3 marks):
   `Object → Component (abstract) → Container (abstract) → Window → Frame`. Also `Container → Panel`.
2. **Why Container extends Component** (4 marks):
   - A `Container` must itself be **drawable, focusable, resizable, and event-aware** — all features defined in `Component`.
   - This satisfies the **"is-a"** relationship: a Container *is a* Component that can additionally hold children.
   - It enables **Liskov Substitution**: anywhere `Component` is expected, a `Container` works, allowing nested containers.
   - It avoids **code duplication** by reusing the visual & event-handling infrastructure of `Component`.

```mermaid
graph TD
    O["Object"] --> C["Component"]
    C --> CO["Container"]
    CO --> P["Panel"]
    CO --> W["Window"]
    W --> F["Frame"]
    W --> D["Dialog"]
    style O fill:#FFE4B5
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style CO fill:#90EE90
    style P fill:#FFB6C1
    style W fill:#FFB6C1
    style F fill:#DDA0DD
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Complete Program:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class CopyDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextField tf;
    Label lbl;

    CopyDemo() {
        setTitle("Copy Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        tf  = new TextField(20);
        lbl = new Label("Result will appear here");
        Button btn = new Button("Copy");

        add(tf);
        add(btn);
        add(lbl);

        btn.addActionListener(this); // Register event

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        lbl.setText("You entered: " + tf.getText());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CopyDemo();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Line 1–2:** Imports AWT classes and event-handling interfaces.
2. **Line 4:** The class `CopyDemo` extends `Frame` (top-level Container) and implements `ActionListener` to handle button clicks.
3. **Line 5–6:** Instance variables for the TextField and Label are declared so they are accessible inside `actionPerformed`.
4. **Line 9–11:** `setLayout(FlowLayout)` is set; `setSize` defines the window's pixel dimensions.
5. **Line 13–15:** Components (`TextField`, `Button`, `Label`) are instantiated.
6. **Line 17–19:** Components are added to the Frame (Container).
7. **Line 21:** `btn.addActionListener(this)` registers the current object as the event handler.
8. **Line 25–27:** When the button is clicked, the Label's text is updated with the TextField's content using `getText()` and `setText()`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **ALTERNATIVE**

**(a)** Compare AWT and Swing in terms of weight, look-and-feel, and class hierarchy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program using `JFrame`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, and `JButton` arranged in a `GridLayout(2, 2)`. When the button is clicked, the label must show the sum of two numbers entered in the text fields. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

| Aspect | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt.*` | `javax.swing.*` |
| Component weight | **Heavyweight** (relies on OS peers) | **Lightweight** (pure Java, drawn by Java) |
| Class prefix | None (`Button`, `Frame`) | `J` prefix (`JButton`, `JFrame`) |
| Look & Feel | OS-dependent (fixed) | **Pluggable** (Metal, Nimbus, Windows, GTK) |
| Base class | `Component` | `JComponent` (extends `Container`) |
| Performance | Faster on simple UIs | Slightly slower, but richer widgets |
| Portability | Limited by platform peers | Fully portable |
| Pluggable MVC | No | Yes (e.g., `JTable`, `JTree`) |

**Why Swing is preferred in modern KTU curricula:** Pluggable look-and-feel, richer component set (tables, trees, tabs), and full Java portability.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class SumApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField t1, t2;
    JLabel result;

    SumApp() {
        setTitle("Sum of Two Numbers");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        t1 = new JTextField();
        t2 = new JTextField();
        JButton btn = new JButton("Add");
        result = new JLabel("Result: ");

        add(new JLabel("Number 1:"));
        add(t1);
        add(new JLabel("Number 2:"));
        add(t2);

        // Second row of grid
        // We pack everything in 2x2; let's redo to fit
        setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));
        add(new JLabel("Number 1:")); add(t1);
        add(new JLabel("Number 2:")); add(t2);
        add(btn);                    add(result);

        btn.addActionListener(this);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            int a = Integer.parseInt(t1.getText());
            int b = Integer.parseInt(t2.getText());
            result.setText("Sum = " + (a + b));
        } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
            result.setText("Invalid input");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SumApp();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Line 4:** `JFrame` is used (Swing top-level container) and `ActionListener` is implemented.
2. **Line 7–11:** Two text fields, a button, and a result label are declared.
3. **Line 13–15:** `GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5)` is set initially; the layout is later updated to `GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5)` to accommodate 6 cells (2 labels + 2 fields + 1 button + 1 result label).
4. **Line 17–22:** Components are added cell-by-cell, left-to-right, top-to-bottom, as per `GridLayout` rules.
5. **Line 24:** The button's action listener is registered.
6. **Line 27–33:** On click, both text fields are parsed into integers using `Integer.parseInt()`. If parsing fails, a `NumberFormatException` is caught and "Invalid input" is displayed. The sum is then shown in the label.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** → Window never displays (-2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting `addActionListener(this)`** in button-based programs → Button click does nothing (-3 marks).
> 3. **Calling `frame.add()` in Swing without realizing it goes to the content pane** — usually fine, but if you bypass it using `getContentPane().setLayout(...)`, you must remember the layout applies to the content pane, not the frame itself.
> 4. **Mixing up `setSize()` and `pack()`** — `setSize` uses pixel values, `pack()` sizes the window to fit preferred sizes of contained components.
> 5. **Not closing the program** — In AWT, the window won't close on the ✕ button. Use `addWindowListener` with `System.exit(0)`, or use Swing's `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`.
> 6. **Confusing AWT and Swing class names** — `Button` vs `JButton`, `Frame` vs `JFrame` — mixing them throws compilation errors or behavior mismatches.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`Component` is the abstract superclass of all AWT UI elements**; `Container` is its only direct abstract subclass that *holds* other components.
- **Every `Container` IS-A `Component`**, but NOT every `Component` is a `Container` — this is the **most-tested KTU concept**.
- **AWT is heavyweight** (OS-peered); **Swing is lightweight** (pure Java) with **pluggable look-and-feel**.
- **Default layouts:** `Frame` → `BorderLayout`; `Panel` → `FlowLayout`; `JFrame` → `BorderLayout` (on the content pane).
- **Five regions of `BorderLayout`:** NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER — at most **one** component per region.
- **`GridLayout(rows, cols)`** divides the container into a uniform grid; components are added cell-by-cell.
- **`FlowLayout`** wraps to the next line when components exceed the row width.
- **`CardLayout`** shows only one of several "cards" at a time — used in tabbed wizards.
- **`add(Component)`** is defined in `Container`, not in `Component` — proving the parent-child role.
- **`setVisible(true)`** must be called *after* adding all components; otherwise some may not appear.
- **`JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE`** is a Swing convenience that calls `System.exit(0)` on window close.
- **Event handling** (next module hook): components register listeners; the listener's method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) is invoked on user interaction.
- **Lightweight vs Heavyweight** is a guaranteed 3-mark question — memorize the table in Section 2.6.
- **`pack()`** vs `setSize()`: prefer `pack()` for production, but KTU codes typically use `setSize()` for predictability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Java Swing** is a set of class libraries built on top of the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT) API, designed to provide a richer, more flexible, and platform-independent set of GUI (Graphical User Interface) components for Java applications. Swing is part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**, which also include Accessibility APIs, the 2D API, and the Pluggable Look and Feel support.

The Swing API is contained primarily in the package **`javax.swing`**, which provides classes such as `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`, and `JTable`. The `javax` prefix (rather than `java`) signifies that Swing is an **extension** to the core Java platform, though it has been bundled with the JDK since version 1.2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Swing is a **lightweight** component framework — components are rendered entirely by Java rather than relying on the underlying operating system's native peers. This is the single most important conceptual difference between Swing and AWT.

## 1.2 The Swing Package Family

Swing is not just one package — it is a **collection of co-operating packages** that together form the JFC:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `javax.swing` | Core component classes (`JButton`, `JLabel`, etc.) |
| `javax.swing.event` | Swing-specific event classes and listener interfaces |
| `javax.swing.border` | Classes to draw borders around components |
| `javax.swing.plaf` | Pluggable Look-and-Feel architecture (base classes) |
| `javax.swing.plaf.basic` | Basic L&F implementation |
| `javax.swing.plaf.metal` | Cross-platform "Metal" L&F |
| `javax.swing.table` | Classes for `JTable` (tabular data) |
| `javax.swing.tree` | Classes for `JTree` (hierarchical data) |
| `javax.swing.text` | Text component support (Document, StyledDocument) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Modern Flat-Pack Furniture vs The Carved Wooden Chair"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of building a chair.
> - **AWT** is like carving a chair out of a single block of wood taken from your local forest. The chair is **tied to the forest (operating system)** — its grain, knots, and shape depend on which tree the wood came from. Move to a different forest, and the chair looks and feels different.
> - **Swing** is like ordering a **flat-pack chair from IKEA**. All the pieces (panels, screws, bolts) are manufactured identically in a Java factory. You assemble them in your own home, and they look the **same on Windows, macOS, and Linux**. You can even swap the fabric (Look-and-Feel) without changing the structure.

This is exactly why Swing is called **lightweight** — its components are not carved from native OS peers; they are *drawn* by Java itself using the underlying `java.awt.Graphics` primitives.

## 1.4 Why Swing was Introduced — Limitations of AWT

1. **Limited Component Set** — AWT had only a handful of components (Button, Label, TextField, etc.).
2. **Heavyweight Components** — Each AWT component had a native peer; bugs in the OS would surface in the Java program.
3. **No Pluggable Look-and-Feel** — AWT components always looked like the host OS.
4. **No MVC Architecture** — AWT mixed data and presentation.

Swing was designed to solve all four problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic for Exam:** Swing contains roughly **40+ top-level component classes** (the ones starting with `J`) versus AWT's **~12 heavyweight component classes**. This expansion is what enabled rich, modern GUIs in Java.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Swing Class Hierarchy — "The Royal Family Tree"

The single most important concept in the KTU module is the **Swing inheritance hierarchy**. Every Swing component ultimately inherits from `java.awt.Component`. Let us walk through it logically, layer by layer:

1. **`java.lang.Object`** — The cosmic root. All Java classes descend from this.
2. **`java.awt.Component`** — Adds position, size, painting (`paint(Graphics)`), and event-enabling methods.
3. **`java.awt.Container`** — A Component that can *contain* other Components. Adds `add()`, `remove()`, `validate()`, `layout` management.
4. **`javax.swing.JComponent`** — The **base of all Swing components**. It is an **abstract** class that adds:
   - Pluggable Look-and-Feel support (`setUI()`)
   - Tooltips (`setToolTipText()`)
   - Borders (`setBorder()`)
   - Double-buffering for flicker-free painting
   - Keyboard actions and `ActionMap`/`InputMap`
5. **Concrete Swing classes** — `JLabel`, `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JPanel`, `JFrame`, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, and `JApplet` are the **only four** Swing top-level containers that are **heavyweight** because they must interact with the OS window manager. All other Swing components are lightweight.

## 2.2 The MVC Architecture in Swing

Every Swing component is built on the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** design pattern. However, Swing uses a slightly relaxed variant often called the **Model-Delegate** or **Separable Model** architecture.

$$\text{Component} = \text{Model} + \text{UI Delegate (View + Controller)}$$

- **Model** — Stores the *data* (e.g., `ButtonModel` stores selected/pressed state).
- **View** — Draws the visual representation.
- **Controller** — Handles user input (mouse, keyboard) and updates the model.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This separation is invaluable. You can change the data model (e.g., a `DefaultListModel`) without rebuilding the UI. The same `JList` can be repopulated instantly. This is a textbook example of the **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID OOP design.

## 2.3 Lightweight vs Heavyweight — A Numerical Comparison

| Property | Heavyweight (AWT) | Lightweight (Swing) |
|---|---|---|
| Native peer required | Yes | No |
| OS-dependent rendering | Yes | No |
| Memory per component | Higher (peer + Java) | Lower (Java only) |
| Pluggable L&F | No | Yes |
| Transparency support | Limited | Full |
| Z-ordering across HW/LW | Problematic | Reliable (within Swing) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Essential Swing Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU ESE. Every component below has appeared in past papers.**

| Class | Type | Superclass | Key Constructor | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `JFrame` | Top-level container (HW) | `java.awt.Frame` | `JFrame()`, `JFrame(String title)` | Main application window |
| `JPanel` | Generic container (LW) | `JComponent` | `JPanel()` | Holds groups of components |
| `JLabel` | Display (non-editable) | `JComponent` | `JLabel(String text)`, `JLabel(Icon i)` | Displays text or icon |
| `JButton` | Command trigger | `AbstractButton` | `JButton(String text)` | Clickable button |
| `JTextField` | Single-line text | `JTextComponent` | `JTextField(int cols)` | Editable text field |
| `JTextArea` | Multi-line text | `JTextComponent` | `JTextArea(int rows, int cols)` | Multi-line text area |
| `JCheckBox` | Toggle | `AbstractButton` | `JCheckBox(String label)` | Checkbox state |
| `JRadioButton` | Group toggle | `AbstractButton` | `JRadioButton(String label)` | Radio button |
| `JComboBox` | Drop-down selector | `JComponent` | `JComboBox(E[] items)` | Drop-down list |
| `JList` | Scrollable list | `JComponent` | `JList(E[] items)` | List of items |
| `JTable` | Tabular data | `JComponent` | `JTable(Object[][] data, Object[] cols)` | Grid display |
| `JTree` | Hierarchy | `JComponent` | `JTree(Object[] nodes)` | Tree display |
| `JMenuBar`/`JMenu`/`JMenuItem` | Menu system | `JComponent` | `JMenu(String s)` | Pull-down menus |
| `JDialog` | Secondary window (HW) | `java.awt.Dialog` | `JDialog(Frame owner, String title)` | Modal/Modeless dialog |
| `JScrollPane` | Scrollable view | `JComponent` | `JScrollPane(Component view)` | Adds scrollbars |
| `JSplitPane` | Resizable split | `JComponent` | `JSplitPane(orient, c1, c2)` | Splits two components |
| `JTabbedPane` | Tab interface | `JComponent` | `JTabbedPane()` | Tabbed panels |

## 2.5 Layout Managers — The Invisible Architects

Swing components must be placed within containers using a **Layout Manager** (the **Strategy Pattern** in action):

| Layout | Arrangement | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps to next line | **Very High** — default for `JPanel` |
| `BorderLayout` | North, South, East, West, Center | **Very High** — default for `JFrame` content pane |
| `GridLayout` | Equal-sized cells in a grid | High |
| `BoxLayout` | Horizontal or vertical stack | Medium |
| `GridBagLayout` | Variable-sized cells with constraints | Low (advanced) |
| `CardLayout` | Stack of cards, one visible at a time | Medium |
| `GroupLayout` | Used by GUI builders (NetBeans) | Low for written exams |
| `null` (Absolute) | Manual x,y placement | Rarely used, not portable |

## 2.6 The Event Delegation Model — "The Newspaper Subscription"

Swing uses the **Event Delegation Model** (inherited from AWT 1.1 onwards). Think of it like a newspaper subscription:

- **Source** (the newspaper) — Fires events when something interesting happens.
- **Event Object** (the article) — Carries details (which button, mouse coordinates, etc.).
- **Listener** (the subscriber) — Implements a listener interface to receive notifications.
- **Registration** (`addXxxListener`) — The subscription itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A `JButton` is the source. When clicked, it fires an `ActionEvent`. The class wanting to react must implement `ActionListener` and register via `button.addActionListener(this)`.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise desktop applications** (legacy banking, ERP) — Swing was the backbone of Java desktop for two decades.
- **IDE development** — IntelliJ IDEA, Eclipse, and NetBeans use Swing variants (or JavaFX) extensively.
- **Scientific visualization** — Custom Swing rendering for plotting libraries (JFreeChart).
- **Embedded kiosks** — Java's sandboxed runtime with Swing is used in ATM UIs and information kiosks.
- **Education** — Swing remains a popular teaching tool for OOP, event-driven programming, and design patterns.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Structural Logic

## 3.1 Derivation of the Swing Class Hierarchy (Symbolic Form)

We can formally express the Swing hierarchy as a chain of `extends` relationships. Each line below is an **inheritance step** documented in the JDK source.

$$\text{java.lang.Object} \;\xrightarrow{\text{extends}}\; \text{java.awt.Component} \;\xrightarrow{\text{extends}}\; \text{java.awt.Container} \;\xrightarrow{\text{extends}}\; \text{javax.swing.JComponent}$$

**Reasoning for each link:**

1. **Object → Component** — `Component` adds the *position* and *rendering* contract: `getX()`, `getY()`, `getWidth()`, `getHeight()`, `paint(Graphics g)`, `repaint()`. Without these, a thing cannot appear on screen.
2. **Component → Container** — A `Container` *is-a* `Component` (it inherits all of the above) **and** adds the *aggregation* contract: `add(Component)`, `remove(Component)`, `doLayout()`, `getLayout()`. This is the only difference: a Container can hold other Components.
3. **Container → JComponent** — `JComponent` adds the **Swing-specific** contract: `setBorder(Border)`, `setToolTipText(String)`, `setUI(ComponentUI)`, `getActionMap()`, `setOpaque(boolean)`, and the `paintComponent(Graphics)` template method (the standard hook for custom painting).

## 3.2 Derivation of a `JFrame` (Top-Level Container) Relationship

`JFrame` does *not* extend `JComponent` — this is a frequent KTU trap:

$$\text{java.lang.Object} \;\to\; \text{java.awt.Component} \;\to\; \text{java.awt.Container} \;\to\; \text{java.awt.Window} \;\to\; \text{java.awt.Frame} \;\to\; \text{javax.swing.JFrame}$$

**Why this detour?** A `JFrame` needs OS-level window-manager cooperation (title bar, minimize, maximize, close). Those capabilities live in `java.awt.Window` and `java.awt.Frame`. Bypassing `JComponent` means `JFrame` does **not** automatically support tooltips or borders — you must add a `JPanel` (or another `JComponent` subclass) to the content pane to use those features.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often call `jframe.setToolTipText(...)` directly. This **does not compile to a useful call** because `JFrame` does not extend `JComponent`. Always add a `JPanel` first and set the tooltip on the panel.

## 3.3 Derivation of Button Hierarchy

Buttons in Swing share a common abstract base:

$$\text{JComponent} \;\to\; \text{AbstractButton} \;\to\; \begin{cases} \text{JButton} \\ \text{JToggleButton} \;\to\; \begin{cases} \text{JCheckBox} \\ \text{JRadioButton} \\ \text{JCheckBoxMenuItem} \\ \text{JRadioButtonMenuItem} \end{cases} \\ \text{JMenuItem} \;\to\; \begin{cases} \text{JMenu} \\ \text{JCheckBoxMenuItem} \\ \text{JRadioButtonMenuItem} \end{cases} \end{cases}$$

**Reasoning:** `AbstractButton` consolidates all common button behavior — text, icon, mnemonic (Alt+key shortcut), action command, model (`ButtonModel`), and `Action` support. Each concrete button class only adds its own rendering and state-management logic. This is a textbook example of **code reuse through abstract classes** in OOP.

## 3.4 Derivation of the LayoutManager Interface (Strategy Pattern)

Layouts are *strategies* that can be swapped at runtime:

$$\text{java.awt.LayoutManager} \; \text{interface} \; \to \; \begin{cases} \text{FlowLayout} \\ \text{BorderLayout} \\ \text{GridLayout} \\ \text{GridBagLayout} \\ \text{CardLayout} \\ \text{BoxLayout} \\ \text{GroupLayout} \end{cases}$$

The contract requires two core methods:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{void addLayoutComponent(String name, Component comp)} \\
\text{void layoutContainer(Container parent)} \\
\text{Dimension preferredLayoutSize(Container parent)} \\
\text{Dimension minimumLayoutSize(Container parent)} \\
\text{void removeLayoutComponent(Component comp)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

`LayoutManager2` extends this to support constraints (e.g., `BorderLayout.NORTH`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{void addLayoutComponent(Component comp, Object constraints)} \\
\text{float getLayoutAlignmentX(Container target)} \\
\text{float getLayoutAlignmentY(Container target)} \\
\text{void invalidateLayout(Container target)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Pluggable Look-and-Feel Resolution Order

When a Swing program starts, the UI manager (`UIManager`) searches for an L&F in this exact order:

1. **Programmatic override** — `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)` called in code.
2. **Command-line argument** — `-Dswing.defaultlaf=com.sun.java.swing.plaf.gtk.GTKLookAndFeel`.
3. **`swing.properties` file** in `${java.home}/lib`.
4. **Cross-platform default** — `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` (since Java 5, the cross-platform **Nimbus** is also available: `javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel`).

The lookup logic is:

$$\text{Chosen L\&F} = \max_{\text{priority}} \; \big\{ \text{Code}, \text{CommandLine}, \text{PropertiesFile}, \text{JREDefault} \big\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Mnemonic:** "**C-C-P-D**" — **C**ode, **C**ommandLine, **P**roperties, **D**efault. Memorize this four-step resolution order.

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: How a `JButton` Paints Itself

When the user clicks a `JButton`, the following sequence executes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \; \text{OS delivers mouse-press event to `JButton`.} \\
&\text{Step 2:} \; \text{`ButtonModel` (the M of MVC) flips state: armed = true.} \\
&\text{Step 3:} \; \text{`JButton` calls `repaint()` on the affected region.} \\
&\text{Step 4:} \; \text{Swing's RepaintManager coalesces and schedules a paint call.} \\
&\text{Step 5:} \; \text{`paint(Graphics)` (inherited from `JComponent`) calls the template method chain:} \\
&\qquad \text{`paintComponent(g)` → `paintBorder(g)` → `paintChildren(g)`.} \\
&\text{Step 6:} \; \text{`paintComponent` delegates to the current `ButtonUI` (the V+C).} \\
&\text{Step 7:} \; \text{`ButtonUI` looks up colors from `UIManager` (L&F tables) and draws the button.} \\
&\text{Step 8:} \; \text{On mouse release, the model fires an `ActionEvent` to all `ActionListener`s.} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Template Method Pattern** (`paintComponent` is overridable) combined with **Strategy** (`ButtonUI` is swappable) and **Observer** (the listener notification).

**Reading Guide:** Follow the arrows from `Object` down. The right branch (Container → Window → Frame → JFrame) shows why top-level containers are heavyweight. The central branch (Container → JComponent) is the lightweight family.

## 4.2 The MVC / Model-Delegate Flow Inside a Swing Component

```mermaid
graph LR
    user[User Input]
    ui[UI Delegate View plus Controller]
    comp[JButton or JComponent]
    model[ButtonModel Data]
    listener[ActionListener Observer]
    user -- mouse and key events --> ui
    ui -- mutates --> model
    ui -- repaints --> comp
    model -- state changed --> ui
    model -- fires ActionEvent --> listener
    listener -- application logic --> comp
```

**Reading Guide:** The model is the *truth*. The UI delegate renders the truth. The user mutates the model *through* the UI delegate. Observers (listeners) are notified when the model changes. This keeps the data decoupled from the presentation.

## 4.3 Container-Component Composition Tree (A Typical KTU Application)

```mermaid

    jf[JFrame My App]
    cp[Content Pane BorderLayout]

    tf1[JTextField]
    lbl2[JLabel Password]

    btn1[JButton Login]
    btn2[JButton Cancel]
    jf --> cp
    cp --> np
    cp --> sp
    cp --> cp1
    np --> lbl1
    np --> tf1
    sp --> btn1
    sp --> btn2
    cp1 --> lbl2
    cp1 --> tf2
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the *exact* kind of diagram you should draw in the KTU 14-mark answer. The outer `JFrame` uses `BorderLayout` in its content pane; the inner `JPanel`s use `FlowLayout` or `GridLayout`. Each `add()` call corresponds to one edge in the tree.

## 4.4 Event Delegation Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Event Source JButton]
    evt[Event Object ActionEvent]
    src1[Listener 1 implements ActionListener]
    src2[Listener 2 implements ActionListener]
    src3[Listener N]
    src -- fires --> evt
    src -- notifies --> src1
    src -- notifies --> src2
    src -- notifies --> src3
    evt -- carries data --> src1
    evt -- carries data --> src2
    evt -- carries data --> src3
```

**Reading Guide:** A single source can have **many** listeners (1-to-N relationship). All listeners receive the *same* event object but can react differently. This is multi-cast notification, not a callback function.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing AWT and Swing package names** — `java.awt.Button` vs `javax.swing.JButton`. The `J` prefix is *not* optional.
> 2. **Forgetting `getContentPane()`** — In older Java, you had to write `frame.getContentPane().add(...)`. From Java 5 onwards, `frame.add(...)` is auto-forwarded, but you still cannot set a layout on `JFrame` directly; you must do it on the content pane or a child `JPanel`.
> 3. **Setting tooltips on `JFrame`/`JDialog`** — They do not extend `JComponent`; tooltips will not appear.
> 4. **Mixing AWT and Swing in the same container** — Causes Z-order and painting bugs. Use *either* AWT *or* Swing in a given window.
> 5. **Not calling `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** — The window closes, but the JVM does not terminate unless the last frame is disposed.
> 6. **Confusing `JFrame.setLayout()`** — `JFrame` does have a layout, but it acts on the *content pane*. Setting it on the frame is accepted but discouraged.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of `JComponent` in the Swing hierarchy?**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: ~80 words):**

`JComponent` is the **abstract base class** of all Swing components (except the four top-level containers `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`). It is declared in the package `javax.swing` and inherits from `java.awt.Container`. It provides the foundation for Swing-specific features such as:
- **Pluggable Look-and-Feel** (`setUI()`)
- **Tooltips** (`setToolTipText()`)
- **Borders** (`setBorder()`)
- **Double-buffering** for flicker-free painting
- **Keyboard bindings** via `InputMap`/`ActionMap`
- **Custom painting** via the template method `paintComponent(Graphics)`

*(Mentioning 4–5 features is enough for 3 marks.)*

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**a)** Explain the Swing class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Discuss why `JComponent` is considered the heart of the Swing architecture. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java Swing program to design a simple calculator that has two `JTextField`s for input, four `JButton`s for `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and a `JLabel` to display the result. Use `BorderLayout` for the frame and a `JPanel` with `GridLayout` for the buttons. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The Swing class hierarchy is a layered inheritance chain rooted in `java.lang.Object`:

**Layer 1 — `java.awt.Component`:** Provides position, size, and painting primitives (`paint(Graphics)`, `repaint()`, `getX()`, `setBounds(int, int, int, int)`).

**Layer 2 — `java.awt.Container`:** Inherits from `Component` and adds the *aggregation* contract — it can hold other components. Methods: `add(Component)`, `remove(Component)`, `setLayout(LayoutManager)`, `validate()`.

**Layer 3 — `javax.swing.JComponent`:** The abstract base of all lightweight Swing components. It introduces:
- `setUI(ComponentUI)` — installs the Pluggable L&F
- `setBorder(Border)` — draws decorative borders
- `setToolTipText(String)` — hover hints
- `paintComponent(Graphics)` — template method for custom painting
- `setOpaque(boolean)` — controls transparency
- `getActionMap()` / `getInputMap()` — keyboard handling
- Automatic **double-buffering** to eliminate flicker

**Why `JComponent` is the "Heart" of Swing:**

1. It unifies all lightweight components under a common contract.
2. It implements the **Strategy Pattern** for the L&F (the UI delegate can be swapped at runtime).
3. It implements the **Template Method Pattern** — subclasses override `paintComponent` to customize appearance without touching the rest of the painting chain.
4. It enables **keyboard-driven accessibility** through the `InputMap`/`ActionMap` pair, a precursor to modern accessibility APIs.
5. It provides **storage for client properties** (`putClientProperty`/`getClientProperty`) — a generic extension hook.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Object} \to \text{Component} \to \text{Container} \to \text{JComponent} \\
&\text{JComponent} \to \{\text{JLabel, JButton, JPanel, JTable, JTree, JList, JTabbedPane, JScrollPane, ...}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for hierarchy description, 3 for features of JComponent, 2 for diagram.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Java Swing Calculator — Complete Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SwingCalculator extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField t1, t2;
    JLabel result;
    JButton bAdd, bSub, bMul, bDiv;

    SwingCalculator() {
        setTitle("Swing Calculator");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));

        // North panel: input fields
        JPanel north = new JPanel(new FlowLayout());
        t1 = new JTextField(8);
        t2 = new JTextField(8);
        north.add(new JLabel("Num1:"));
        north.add(t1);
        north.add(new JLabel("Num2:"));
        north.add(t2);

        // Center panel: buttons in a 1x4 grid
        JPanel center = new JPanel(new GridLayout(1, 4, 5, 5));
        bAdd = new JButton("+");
        bSub = new JButton("-");
        bMul = new JButton("*");
        bDiv = new JButton("/");
        bAdd.addActionListener(this);
        bSub.addActionListener(this);
        bMul.addActionListener(this);
        bDiv.addActionListener(this);
        center.add(bAdd);
        center.add(bSub);
        center.add(bMul);
        center.add(bDiv);

        // South panel: result label
        JPanel south = new JPanel(new FlowLayout());
        result = new JLabel("Result: ");
        south.add(result);

        add(north, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(center, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(south, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            double a = Double.parseDouble(t1.getText());
            double b = Double.parseDouble(t2.getText());
            double r = 0;
            if (e.getSource() == bAdd) r = a + b;
            else if (e.getSource() == bSub) r = a - b;
            else if (e.getSource() == bMul) r = a * b;
            else if (e.getSource() == bDiv) r = a / b;
            result.setText("Result: " + r);
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            result.setText("Invalid input!");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SwingCalculator();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (for 7 marks):**

1. `extends JFrame implements ActionListener` — Frame for the window and the class itself handles button clicks.
2. `setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10))` — 10-pixel horizontal and vertical gaps.
3. `new JTextField(8)` — Column width of 8 characters.
4. `new JPanel(new GridLayout(1, 4, 5, 5))` — 1 row, 4 columns, 5px gaps.
5. `addActionListener(this)` — Registers the frame as a listener on each button.
6. `e.getSource() == bAdd` — Identifies *which* button was clicked.
7. `Double.parseDouble(...)` — Converts text to number; `try-catch` handles invalid input.
8. `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — Exits JVM on close.

*(7 marks: 3 for layout + component creation, 2 for event handling, 2 for arithmetic logic and exception handling.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set C]`

**a)** With examples, explain the **Event Delegation Model** in Java Swing. How is it different from the legacy event-inheritance model? **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java Swing program that creates a registration form with `JLabel`s and corresponding `JTextField`s for *Name, Email, Phone, Address*, a `JPasswordField` for *Password*, and a `JButton` labeled "Submit". Use appropriate layout managers. On clicking Submit, display all entered values in a `JOptionPane` message dialog. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Event Delegation Model (EDM) — Definition:**

The Event Delegation Model is a post-Java 1.1 event-handling architecture in which:
- **Event sources** (UI components) maintain a **list of registered listeners**.
- When an event occurs, the source **delegates** the event to **all** registered listeners.
- Listeners are objects whose classes **implement** specific listener interfaces (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`).
- The event itself is encapsulated in an **Event Object** subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent`).

**Three Participants in EDM:**

1. **Event Source** — The component that *generates* the event. Example: `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, `JTextField`.
2. **Event Object** — Carries event-specific data. All extend `java.util.EventObject`. Examples: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`.
3. **Event Listener** — An interface implemented by the class that wants to react. Examples: `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`.

**Example Code:**

```java
JButton b = new JButton("Click");
b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Button clicked!");
    }
});
```

Here, the `JButton` is the source, `ActionEvent` is the event object, and the anonymous inner class is the listener.

**Difference from Event-Inheritance Model:**

| Property | Event-Inheritance (Java 1.0) | Event Delegation (Java 1.1+) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Subclass the component and override `handleEvent(...)` | Implement a listener interface |
| Coupling | Tight — class must inherit from the component | Loose — any class can be a listener |
| Extensibility | Single inheritance limits listeners | One source can have many listeners |
| Reusability | Hard — subclassing the button is heavy | Easy — listener class can be reused across components |
| Performance | Event dispatch chain traverses parent components | Direct call to registered listeners |

**Why EDM is Better:**

- Supports **multiple listeners** per event (multi-cast).
- Honors the **Single Responsibility Principle** — the UI class focuses on UI, the listener class focuses on logic.
- Aligns with the **Observer Design Pattern** in OOP.
- Avoids the fragile base-class problem of 1.0.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for three participants, 1 for code, 2 for comparison table.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Java Swing Registration Form — Complete Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class RegistrationForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField nameF, emailF, phoneF, addrF;
    JPasswordField passF;
    JButton submit;

    RegistrationForm() {
        setTitle("Registration Form");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new GridLayout(6, 2, 5, 5));

        add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        nameF = new JTextField(15);
        add(nameF);

        add(new JLabel("Email:"));
        emailF = new JTextField(15);
        add(emailF);

        add(new JLabel("Phone:"));
        phoneF = new JTextField(15);
        add(phoneF);

        add(new JLabel("Address:"));
        addrF = new JTextField(15);
        add(addrF);

        add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        passF = new JPasswordField(15);
        add(passF);

        submit = new JButton("Submit");
        submit.addActionListener(this);
        add(new JLabel("")); // Empty cell for grid alignment
        add(submit);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String msg = "Name: " + nameF.getText()
                   + "\nEmail: " + emailF.getText()
                   + "\nPhone: " + phoneF.getText()
                   + "\nAddress: " + addrF.getText()
                   + "\nPassword: " + new String(passF.getPassword());
        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, msg, "Registration Details",
                                      JOptionPane.INFORMATION_MESSAGE);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new RegistrationForm();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Constructs (for 7 marks):**

1. `GridLayout(6, 2, 5, 5)` — 6 rows, 2 columns, 5px gaps. Each label-field pair occupies one row.
2. `JPasswordField(15)` — Echoes `*` instead of actual characters; retrieve via `getPassword()`.
3. `new String(passF.getPassword())` — `getPassword()` returns `char[]` for security; convert for display only.
4. `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(parent, message, title, messageType)` — Built-in modal dialog.
5. Empty `JLabel("")` cell in the last row — Preserves grid alignment before the Submit button.
6. `setVisible(true)` placed at the end — All components must be added *before* showing the frame.
7. `getText()` for `JTextField` — Retrieves the entered text as `String`.

*(7 marks: 3 for layout + component setup, 2 for event handling, 2 for JOptionPane display and password handling.)*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU ESE**

- [x] **Swing package** is `javax.swing` (note the `javax`, *not* `java`).
- [x] Swing is **lightweight**; AWT is **heavyweight**.
- [x] All Swing components (except `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) are descendants of `JComponent`.
- [x] `JComponent` extends `java.awt.Container` and provides L&F, tooltips, borders, double-buffering.
- [x] **MVC** is implemented as **Model + UI Delegate** (the Model-Delegate variant).
- [x] **Event Delegation Model** has three participants: Source, Event Object, Listener.
- [x] Default layout of `JFrame` content pane is `BorderLayout`; default of `JPanel` is `FlowLayout`.
- [x] **`setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** must be set, else the JVM keeps running.
- [x] Pluggable L&F resolution order: **C-C-P-D** (Code, CommandLine, Properties, Default).
- [x] `JFrame.setToolTipText()` does **not** work — set the tooltip on a child `JComponent`.
- [x] Common components: `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JMenuBar`, `JScrollPane`, `JTabbedPane`.
- [x] Abstract button hierarchy: `JComponent → AbstractButton → {JButton, JToggleButton, JMenuItem}`.
- [x] `JPasswordField.getPassword()` returns `char[]` (not `String`) for security.
- [x] Never mix AWT and Swing in the same container — causes Z-order and painting anomalies.
- [x] **Layout Managers** are Strategy Pattern implementations — swappable at runtime via `setLayout(...)`.
- [x] `JOptionPane` provides built-in dialogs: `showMessageDialog`, `showConfirmDialog`, `showInputDialog`.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for the 14-Mark Question:** Always begin with a one-sentence *purpose statement*, then a *hierarchy diagram*, then a *working code block*, then a *line-by-line explanation of tricky parts*. This 4-part structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and consistently scores 12–14 out of 14.
