topics.title: Swing Layout Managers. Event Handling in Swings - Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers. Event Handling in Swings - Delegation Event Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Swing Layout Managers — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing is an object that implements the `LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface and dictates the **size and position of components** inside a container. The layout manager automatically arranges child components based on the container's size, the components' preferred/minimum/maximum sizes, and the layout-specific rules, thereby providing **platform-independent GUI design**.

In Java AWT/Swing, layout management decouples the geometric placement of widgets from the application logic. Instead of calling `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` manually, the programmer delegates the responsibility to a layout object, and the AWT/Swing toolkit invokes the layout algorithm during the container's *layout pass*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for KTU 2024:** Layout managers are **Container-level strategies** in the **Strategy design pattern**. Every `Container` (e.g., `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JApplet`) has a default layout, but it can be replaced using `container.setLayout(new BorderLayout())`.

### 🔑 The LayoutManager Interface Hierarchy

The complete inheritance of Swing layout management is rooted in `java.awt.LayoutManager`:

```
java.awt.LayoutManager           ← defines addLayoutComponent, layoutContainer, minimumLayoutSize, preferredLayoutSize, removeLayoutComponent
        │
        ├── java.awt.LayoutManager2  ← adds addLayoutComponent(Component, Object), maximumLayoutSize, getLayoutAlignmentX/Y, invalidateLayout
        │
        │   (BorderLayout, GridBagLayout, BoxLayout, CardLayout, GroupLayout, GridLayout, SpringLayout, OverlayLayout all extend one of these)
        │
        └── java.awt.FlowLayout (directly extends LayoutManager)
```

---

## 1.2 Event Handling in Swing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Event Handling** in Java is the mechanism by which a component reacts to user interactions (mouse clicks, key presses, window events, etc.). Java uses the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**, where an *event source* delegates the processing of an event to a separate *event listener* (also called *event handler*). The listener implements a callback method prescribed by a *listener interface* corresponding to the event type.

In the **Delegation Event Model** (introduced in JDK 1.1, replacing the legacy JDK 1.0 *inheritance event model*), three actors collaborate:

| Actor | Role |
|---|---|
| **Event Object** | Encapsulates the *what*, *where*, and *when* of the user action (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`). |
| **Event Source** | The component that **fires** the event (e.g., a `JButton`). |
| **Event Listener** | An object that **receives** the event and provides a callback method. |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

### 🍱 Analogy 1: Layout Manager = Restaurant Table Setting

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You don't rearrange the chairs yourself; the **maitre d' (layout manager)** decides how the tables are arranged:

- If the room is narrow, they use a **single-file layout** (`BoxLayout`).
- If it's a banquet, they use a **grid of identical tables** (`GridLayout`).
- If the centerpiece is the wedding cake, they place it in the **center, with side dishes around** (`BorderLayout`).

Likewise, when the JFrame is resized, the layout manager **rearranges** the components automatically. You never write `component.setLocation(...)` after a resize event.

### 🛎️ Analogy 2: Event Delegation = Hotel Concierge Service

Think of a **hotel concierge** (the *event source*). When a guest presses the room service button (the *event*), the concierge doesn't handle the food preparation herself. She **delegates** the order to the **kitchen staff** (the *event listener*), who knows the recipe (the *callback method*).

- The concierge doesn't care *how* the kitchen cooks, only that it executes the `actionPerformed()` method.
- Multiple staff members (multiple listeners) can register for different events (room service, housekeeping, wake-up calls).
- Each listener **implements a specific contract** (`ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`).

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Delegation"?** Because the source component *delegates* the responsibility of responding to events to external objects. The source itself contains no business logic for the response. This decouples UI from business code — a hallmark of OOP design.

### ⚖️ Key Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Default Layout of `JFrame` (Content Pane)** | `BorderLayout` |
| **Default Layout of `JPanel`** | `FlowLayout` |
| **Default Layout of `JApplet`** | `FlowLayout` (in AWT) |
| **JDK 1.0 Event Model** | Inheritance-based (deprecated) |
| **JDK 1.1+ Event Model** | **Delegation Event Model** (current) |
| **Package for AWT events** | `java.awt.event` |
| **Package for Swing events** | `javax.swing.event` (extends AWT events) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layout Managers — The Core Catalog

### 2.1.1 `BorderLayout`

A `BorderLayout` divides the container into **five regions**: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, and `CENTER`. Each region can hold **at most one component** (or a `JPanel` containing sub-components). When the container is resized, the **center region absorbs** all extra space.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderDemo extends JFrame {
    public BorderDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5)); // hgap=5, vgap=5
        add(new JButton("North"),  BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JButton("South"),  BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new JButton("East"),   BorderLayout.EAST);
        add(new JButton("West"),   BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new JButton("Center"), BorderLayout.CENTER);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CENTER is the only region that grows** when the container is resized. If you don't add a component to CENTER, one of the surrounding regions gets its space.

### 2.1.2 `FlowLayout`

The simplest manager: components are placed in a **row, left to right**, and wrap to the next line when the row is full. Alignment can be `LEFT`, `CENTER` (default), or `RIGHT`. Components keep their **preferred size**.

```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 20));
// hgap = 10 pixels, vgap = 20 pixels
p.add(new JButton("Btn1"));
p.add(new JButton("Btn2"));
p.add(new JButton("Btn3"));
```

### 2.1.3 `GridLayout`

Divides the container into a **rectangular grid of equal-sized cells**. Each cell holds one component. All cells are forced to be the **same size** — the largest component's preferred size determines the cell size.

```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5)); // 3 rows, 2 cols
```

### 2.1.4 `GridBagLayout`

The most **powerful and flexible** (and complex) layout manager. It places components in a grid of *cells*, but unlike `GridLayout`, a single component can **span multiple rows/columns**, and individual components can have their own **fill, anchor, weight, insets, and padding**. The position of each component is specified via a `GridBagConstraints` object.

```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new GridBagLayout());
GridBagConstraints c = new GridBagConstraints();
c.gridx = 0; c.gridy = 0;
c.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
c.weightx = 0.5; c.weighty = 0.5;
p.add(new JButton("Btn 1"), c);
```

**Key `GridBagConstraints` fields:**

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `gridx, gridy` | Cell position (0-based). Use `RELATIVE` for next cell. |
| `gridwidth, gridheight` | Number of cells spanned. |
| `fill` | `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH` |
| `weightx, weighty` | How extra space is distributed (0 = no growth). |
| `anchor` | Where to place component when smaller than cell. |
| `insets` | External padding (top, left, bottom, right). |
| `ipadx, ipady` | Internal padding. |

### 2.1.5 `BoxLayout`

Stacks components either **vertically** (`Y_AXIS`) or **horizontally** (`X_AXIS`). Components keep their preferred size, and **glue** or **struts** can be added to control spacing.

```java
JPanel p = new JPanel();
p.setLayout(new BoxLayout(p, BoxLayout.Y_AXIS));
p.add(new JButton("Top"));
p.add(Box.createVerticalStrut(10));
p.add(new JButton("Bottom"));
```

### 2.1.6 `CardLayout`

Stacks components like a **deck of cards**; only one card is visible at a time. Use `cardPanel.next(cardPanel)`, `first()`, `last()`, `show(name)` to flip between cards.

```java
JPanel cards = new JPanel(new CardLayout());
cards.add(panel1, "Login");
cards.add(panel2, "Register");
((CardLayout) cards.getLayout()).show(cards, "Register");
```

### 2.1.7 `GroupLayout`

Introduced in Java SE 6, used by the **NetBeans GUI Builder (Matisse)**. It groups components horizontally and vertically into sequential or parallel groups. Powerful but verbose when written by hand.

### 2.1.8 `SpringLayout`

Lays out components according to a set of **springs (constraints)** — minimum, preferred, and maximum distances between component edges. Used internally by NetBeans; rarely coded manually.

### 2.1.9 `GridBagLayout` vs `GroupLayout` — Comparison

| Feature | `GridBagLayout` | `GroupLayout` |
|---|---|---|
| Introduced in | JDK 1.0 | Java SE 6 |
| Flexibility | Very High | Very High |
| Verbosity | High | High |
| Used by | Hand-coded GUIs | NetBeans Matisse |
| Preferred in modern Swing | Yes (for hand code) | Yes (for GUI builders) |

---

## 2.2 Delegation Event Model — The Three Pillars

### 2.2.1 Event Classes (java.awt.event, javax.swing.event)

All event classes extend `java.util.EventObject`, which provides `getSource()` and `getID()`. Common events include:

| Event Class | Triggered By | Listener Interface |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | Button click, menu selection, text field Enter | `ActionListener` |
| `MouseEvent` | Mouse press, release, click, enter, exit, move, drag | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` |
| `KeyEvent` | Key press, release, type | `KeyListener` |
| `WindowEvent` | Window open, close, iconify, activate | `WindowListener` |
| `ItemEvent` | Checkbox/radio button toggle, list selection | `ItemListener` |
| `TextEvent` | Text field/area content change | `TextListener` |
| `FocusEvent` | Component gains/loses focus | `FocusListener` |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | Scrollbar movement | `AdjustmentListener` |
| `ChangeEvent` (Swing) | Slider, spinner value change | `ChangeListener` |

### 2.2.2 Event Source

A source is **any object that can fire events**. It must provide methods to **register** and **deregister** listeners:

```java
public void addActionListener(ActionListener l);
public void removeActionListener(ActionListener l);
```

When the user interacts, the source calls a protected method (e.g., `fireActionPerformed(new ActionEvent(this, ...))`) which **iterates through the registered listener list** and invokes the callback.

### 2.2.3 Event Listener Interface

A listener is an **interface** that defines callback methods. To receive events, a class **implements** the listener and **registers** itself with the source.

```java
class MyHandler implements ActionListener {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Button clicked!");
    }
}

JButton btn = new JButton("Click");
btn.addActionListener(new MyHandler());
```

### 2.2.4 Adapter Classes (Convenience)

Many listener interfaces have **multiple methods** (e.g., `MouseListener` has 5). To avoid implementing empty methods, Java provides **adapter classes** with empty default implementations:

```java
class MyMouseHandler extends MouseAdapter {
    @Override
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked at " + e.getX() + "," + e.getY());
    }
}
```

Common adapters: `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `MouseMotionAdapter`.

### 2.2.5 The Four Steps of Event Handling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite — The Four Steps:**
> 1. Implement the appropriate **Listener interface** (or extend an Adapter).
> 2. Override the **callback method(s)** to specify the response.
> 3. Create the **Event Source** (e.g., `JButton`).
> 4. **Register the listener** with the source using `source.addXxxListener(listener)`.

### 2.2.6 Inner Classes for Event Handling

A common KTU pattern uses **anonymous inner classes** or **lambda expressions** (Java 8+):

```java
btn.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Lambda click!"));

btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Anonymous inner class click!");
    }
});
```

### 2.2.7 Multiple Listeners on a Single Source

A single source can register **multiple listeners**; all of them will be notified in registration order. Conversely, **one listener can register with multiple sources** by adding itself to each.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Cheat Sheet — Key Concepts to Memorize for KTU Exams**

| # | Concept | Key Fact |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default layout of `JFrame` content pane | `BorderLayout` |
| 2 | Default layout of `JPanel` | `FlowLayout` |
| 3 | `BorderLayout` regions | 5: N, S, E, W, CENTER |
| 4 | Center region behavior | Grows when container is resized |
| 5 | `GridLayout` cell size | All cells equal-sized |
| 6 | `GridBagLayout` positioning | Uses `GridBagConstraints` |
| 7 | `BoxLayout` axes | `X_AXIS` or `Y_AXIS` |
| 8 | `CardLayout` visibility | Only one card visible at a time |
| 9 | `FlowLayout` default alignment | `CENTER` |
| 10 | Event base class | `java.util.EventObject` |
| 11 | JDK that introduced DEM | JDK 1.1 |
| 12 | Listener registration method | `source.addXxxListener(listener)` |
| 13 | Adapter purpose | Provide empty default implementations |
| 14 | `ActionEvent` source | Buttons, menus, text fields |
| 15 | `WindowEvent` listener | `WindowListener` / `WindowAdapter` |
| 16 | `MouseListener` methods | 5: clicked, pressed, released, entered, exited |
| 17 | `MouseMotionListener` methods | 2: dragged, moved |
| 18 | `KeyListener` methods | 3: typed, pressed, released |
| 19 | Event getter | `getSource()` and `getID()` |
| 20 | Anonymous inner class | Inline class implementing interface |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Building a Swing Application with Layout Manager + Event Handling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** Walk through a complete, real-world KTU-style problem. Every step is explicit, every line explained.

### Problem Statement (KTU Model)

> Design a Java Swing application that displays a window containing a `JTextField` at the top (using `BorderLayout.NORTH`) and three `JButton`s labeled *"Red"*, *"Green"*, *"Blue"*. When a button is clicked, the background of a `JLabel` placed at the `CENTER` should change to the corresponding color. Use the **Delegation Event Model**.

### Step 1 — Import Required Packages

```java
import javax.swing.*;   // For JFrame, JButton, JTextField, JLabel, etc.
import java.awt.*;      // For BorderLayout, Color, Container
import java.awt.event.*;// For ActionEvent, ActionListener
```

**Explanation:**
- `javax.swing.*` provides Swing components (lightweight, pluggable look-and-feel).
- `java.awt.*` provides AWT layout managers and `Color`.
- `java.awt.event.*` provides event classes and listener interfaces.

### Step 2 — Define the Class Extending `JFrame`

```java
public class ColorChangerApp extends JFrame {
```

**Explanation:** Inheriting from `JFrame` gives us a top-level window with a default `BorderLayout` on its *content pane*. We override its constructor.

### Step 3 — Declare the Components as Instance Fields

```java
    private JTextField inputField;
    private JLabel    colorPanel;
    private JButton   redBtn, greenBtn, blueBtn;
```

**Explanation:** Declaring components as instance fields (rather than local variables) is essential because **the event listeners (defined later) must be able to access them**.

### Step 4 — Initialize the Frame Properties in the Constructor

```java
    public ColorChangerApp() {
        setTitle("Color Changer - KTU Demo");
        setSize(450, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));
    }
```

**Explanation:**
- `setTitle()` sets the window title.
- `setSize(450, 300)` sets initial dimensions in pixels.
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` makes the close button terminate the JVM.
- `setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10))` explicitly sets the layout with **hgap = 10** and **vgap = 10** pixels of spacing between regions. (This step is technically redundant, since `JFrame` already uses `BorderLayout`; it is shown for clarity.)

### Step 5 — Build the North Region (`JTextField`)

```java
        inputField = new JTextField("Type your name here");
        add(inputField, BorderLayout.NORTH);
```

**Explanation:** A `JTextField` is added to the NORTH region. The text field automatically stretches to fill the width.

### Step 6 — Build the Center Region (`JLabel`)

```java
        colorPanel = new JLabel("  Watch the background change!", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        colorPanel.setOpaque(true);          // REQUIRED to show background color
        colorPanel.setBackground(Color.WHITE);
        add(colorPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
```

**Explanation:** By default, a `JLabel` is **transparent** (`setOpaque(false)`). To display a background color, we must call `setOpaque(true)`. The label is centered in the **CENTER** region, which grows when the window is resized.

### Step 7 — Build the South Region (Three Buttons in a `JPanel`)

```java
        JPanel southPanel = new JPanel();
        southPanel.setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 15, 10));

        redBtn   = new JButton("Red");
        greenBtn = new JButton("Green");
        blueBtn  = new JButton("Blue");

        southPanel.add(redBtn);
        southPanel.add(greenBtn);
        southPanel.add(blueBtn);

        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
```

**Explanation:** Since `BorderLayout.SOUTH` accepts only one component, we wrap the three buttons in a `JPanel` whose own layout is `FlowLayout`. This is the **canonical "nested layout" pattern** in Swing.

### Step 8 — Register the Event Listeners (The Core of DEM)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the heart of the Delegation Event Model.** We instantiate a single listener object and register it with all three buttons. The listener inspects the **command string** of the `ActionEvent` to decide which color to apply.

```java
        // Create ONE listener object
        ColorActionHandler handler = new ColorActionHandler();

        // Register the SAME listener with THREE sources
        redBtn.addActionListener(handler);
        greenBtn.addActionListener(handler);
        blueBtn.addActionListener(handler);
```

### Step 9 — Make the Frame Visible

```java
        setVisible(true);
    } // end constructor
```

### Step 10 — Implement the Listener as an Inner Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why an inner class?** It can directly access the enclosing class's instance fields (e.g., `colorPanel`, `inputField`) **without needing a reference to the outer object**.

```java
    private class ColorActionHandler implements ActionListener {
        @Override
        public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
            String cmd = e.getActionCommand();  // returns the button's label
            switch (cmd) {
                case "Red":
                    colorPanel.setBackground(Color.RED);
                    colorPanel.setText("Background set to RED");
                    break;
                case "Green":
                    colorPanel.setBackground(Color.GREEN);
                    colorPanel.setText("Background set to GREEN");
                    break;
                case "Blue":
                    colorPanel.setBackground(Color.BLUE);
                    colorPanel.setText("Background set to BLUE");
                    break;
            }
        }
    }
```

### Step 11 — The `main` Method

```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Event Dispatch Thread (best practice)
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ColorChangerApp());
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` ensures the GUI is created on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**, which is the thread-safe way to start Swing applications.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Listener Interface Map

| Event Type | Listener Interface | Methods (Callback) | Adapter Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | — (only 1 method) |
| `MouseEvent` (clicks) | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked, mousePressed, mouseReleased, mouseEntered, mouseExited` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseEvent` (motion) | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged, mouseMoved` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped, keyPressed, keyReleased` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened, windowClosing, windowClosed, windowIconified, windowDeiconified, windowActivated, windowDeactivated` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained, focusLost` | `FocusAdapter` |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged` | — (only 1 method) |
| `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged` | — (only 1 method) |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | — (only 1 method) |
| Swing `ChangeEvent` | `ChangeListener` (javax.swing.event) | `stateChanged` | — (only 1 method) |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layout Manager Quick-Reference

| Layout | Constructor Examples | Cell Resizing | Component Count per Region | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `BorderLayout` | `new BorderLayout()`, `new BorderLayout(h, v)` | Center grows | 1 per region | Top-level frames, toolbars |
| `FlowLayout` | `new FlowLayout()`, `new FlowLayout(align, h, v)` | None (preferred size) | Many | Button rows, simple panels |
| `GridLayout` | `new GridLayout(r, c)`, `new GridLayout(r, c, h, v)` | All cells equal | 1 per cell | Calculators, keypads |
| `GridBagLayout` | `new GridBagLayout()` | Per-component weights | Many | Complex forms |
| `BoxLayout` | `new BoxLayout(c, axis)` | Preferred size | Many | Toolbars, stacks |
| `CardLayout` | `new CardLayout()`, `new CardLayout(h, v)` | N/A | One visible | Wizards, tab-like views |
| `GroupLayout` | `new GroupLayout(c)` | Per-group | Many | GUI-builder output |
| `SpringLayout` | `new SpringLayout()` | Per-spring | Many | Precise constraints |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Lambda Expression Form (Java 8+)

The same listener registration can be written in three progressively compact forms:

```java
// Form 1: Named class
redBtn.addActionListener(new RedHandler());

// Form 2: Anonymous inner class
redBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        colorPanel.setBackground(Color.RED);
    }
});

// Form 3: Lambda expression
redBtn.addActionListener(e -> colorPanel.setBackground(Color.RED));
```

**Explanation:** Since `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** (has exactly one abstract method), it is eligible for lambda replacement. KTU exams may ask students to convert between these forms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Delegation Event Model — Block-Level Architecture

> This block diagram illustrates the three actors and the flow of an event from user action to callback execution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Action<br/>Mouse Click / Key Press]
    ES[Event Source<br/>e.g. JButton]
    EO[Event Object<br/>e.g. ActionEvent]
    EL[Event Listener<br/>e.g. ActionListener]
    CB[Callback Method<br/>actionPerformed]
    RE[Response<br/>UI / Logic Update]

    U -- triggers --> ES
    ES -- creates & fires --> EO
    EO -- dispatched to --> EL
    EL -- invokes --> CB
    CB -- executes --> RE

    R1[Registration Step]
    EL -. addActionListener .-> R1
    R1 -. registered with .-> ES
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *solid arrows* show the runtime event flow (when the user clicks). The *dotted arrows* show the design-time registration (when the application is constructed). This separation is the essence of **delegation**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hierarchy of Layout Manager Interfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[java.awt.LayoutManager]
    B[java.awt.LayoutManager2]
    C[java.awt.FlowLayout]
    D[java.awt.BorderLayout]
    E[java.awt.GridLayout]
    F[java.awt.CardLayout]
    G[java.awt.GridBagLayout]
    H[javax.swing.BoxLayout]
    I[javax.swing.GroupLayout]
    J[javax.swing.SpringLayout]
    K[javax.swing.OverlayLayout]

    A --> C
    A --> B
    B --> D
    B --> E
    B --> F
    B --> G
    B --> H
    B --> I
    B --> J
    B --> K
```

**Explanation:**
- `FlowLayout` is the only major layout that implements `LayoutManager` directly.
- All other modern layouts implement `LayoutManager2`, which adds support for *constraints* (e.g., `BorderLayout.NORTH`, `GridBagConstraints`).

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: BorderLayout Region Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JF["JFrame (BorderLayout)"]
        direction TB
        subgraph N["NORTH region"]
            N1["Component A"]
        end
        subgraph MID["MIDDLE ROW (3 regions)"]
            direction LR
            subgraph W["WEST region"]
                W1["Component B"]
            end
            subgraph C["CENTER region (grows)"]
                C1["Component C"]
            end
            subgraph E["EAST region"]
                E1["Component D"]
            end
        end
        subgraph S["SOUTH region"]
            S1["Component E"]
        end
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual takeaway:** The CENTER region is the only one that expands when the user resizes the window. NORTH and SOUTH consume their **preferred height**; EAST and WEST consume their **preferred width**. The CENTER absorbs the remainder.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Event Listener Registration Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application Code
    participant Source as JButton (Event Source)
    participant Listener as MyListener (Implements ActionListener)
    participant User as User

    App->>Source: new JButton("OK")
    App->>Listener: new MyListener()
    App->>Source: addActionListener(listener)
    Note over Source,Listener: Listener registered in source's internal list

    User->>Source: Clicks the button
    Source->>Source: fireActionPerformed(new ActionEvent(this, ...))
    Source->>Listener: actionPerformed(event)
    Listener-->>User: Displays response (e.g., dialog box)
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Nested Layout Pattern (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Frame["JFrame — BorderLayout"]
        direction TB
        subgraph N["NORTH"]
            N1["JTextField"]
        end
        subgraph C["CENTER"]
            C1["JLabel (opaque)"]
        end
        subgraph S["SOUTH"]
            subgraph SPanel["JPanel — FlowLayout"]
                direction LR
                Btn1["JButton Red"]
                Btn2["JButton Green"]
                Btn3["JButton Blue"]
            end
        end
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why nesting is essential:** `BorderLayout.SOUTH` can hold only *one* component. To put three buttons there, you wrap them in a `JPanel` with its own `FlowLayout`. This **nested layout** pattern is a frequently tested KTU concept.

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java. List any three event listener interfaces and their corresponding event classes.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is Java's mechanism for handling GUI events, introduced in JDK 1.1. It involves three actors:

1. **Event Source:** A component (e.g., `JButton`) that generates events in response to user actions.
2. **Event Object:** An object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) that encapsulates details of the event.
3. **Event Listener:** An object that implements a listener interface and provides a callback method to handle the event.

The source does not handle the event itself; it **delegates** the responsibility to any registered listener(s). To register, the listener is added via `source.addXxxListener(listener)`.

**Three Listener Interfaces:**

| Listener Interface | Event Class | Callback Method |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `MouseListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, etc. |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyEvent` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, and `GridLayout`. State the default layout of `JFrame` and `JPanel`.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `FlowLayout` | `BorderLayout` | `GridLayout` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arrangement | Row-wise, wraps to next line | 5 regions (N, S, E, W, Center) | Rectangular grid of equal cells |
| Cell resizing | No | Only Center grows | All cells resize equally |
| Components per region | Unlimited | 1 per region | 1 per cell |
| Default of `JFrame` | — | **Yes (default)** | — |
| Default of `JPanel` | **Yes (default)** | — | — |

**Defaults:**
- `JFrame` content pane → `BorderLayout`
- `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the major layout managers available in Java Swing with suitable code examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a window with a `JTextField` and a `JButton` labeled "Greet". When the button is clicked, the program should display a `JOptionPane` message dialog showing "Hello, <name>!" where `<name>` is the text from the `JTextField`. Use the **Delegation Event Model**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Part (a) — Layout Managers

The major layout managers in Java Swing are:

**1. `BorderLayout`** — Divides the container into five regions:
```java
JFrame f = new JFrame();
f.setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));
f.add(new JButton("Top"),    BorderLayout.NORTH);
f.add(new JButton("Center"), BorderLayout.CENTER);
f.add(new JButton("Bottom"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
```
Best for top-level windows. The **CENTER** region grows when resized.

**2. `FlowLayout`** — Lays out components in a row, wrapping at the boundary:
```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 20));
p.add(new JButton("A"));
p.add(new JButton("B"));
```
Components keep their **preferred size**; alignment can be `LEFT`, `CENTER`, or `RIGHT`.

**3. `GridLayout`** — Equal-sized cells in a grid:
```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new GridLayout(3, 3, 5, 5)); // 3x3 grid with gaps
```
Useful for calculators and keypads.

**4. `GridBagLayout`** — Most flexible; uses `GridBagConstraints`:
```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new GridBagLayout());
GridBagConstraints c = new GridBagConstraints();
c.gridx = 0; c.gridy = 0; c.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
c.weightx = 1.0; c.weighty = 1.0;
p.add(new JButton("Click"), c);
```
Supports per-component resizing, spanning, and anchoring.

**5. `BoxLayout`** — Vertical or horizontal stacking:
```java
JPanel p = new JPanel();
p.setLayout(new BoxLayout(p, BoxLayout.Y_AXIS));
p.add(new JButton("Top"));
p.add(Box.createVerticalGlue());
p.add(new JButton("Bottom"));
```

**6. `CardLayout`** — Stack of cards, only one visible:
```java
JPanel cards = new JPanel(new CardLayout());
cards.add(panel1, "Page1");
cards.add(panel2, "Page2");
((CardLayout) cards.getLayout()).show(cards, "Page2");
```

**Conclusion:** Choosing the right layout manager ensures the GUI remains **platform-independent** and **resizable** without manual repositioning.

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Part (b) — Greeting Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends JFrame {
    private JTextField nameField;
    private JButton greetBtn;

    public GreetApp() {
        setTitle("Greet Application");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 30));

        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        nameField.setText("Enter your name");
        greetBtn  = new JButton("Greet");

        add(nameField);
        add(greetBtn);

        // Step 4: Register the listener (Delegation Event Model)
        greetBtn.addActionListener(new GreetHandler());

        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Step 1-2: Listener class implementing ActionListener
    private class GreetHandler implements ActionListener {
        @Override
        public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
            String name = nameField.getText().trim();
            if (name.isEmpty()) {
                JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null,
                    "Please enter your name first!",
                    "Empty Name",
                    JOptionPane.WARNING_MESSAGE);
            } else {
                JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null,
                    "Hello, " + name + "!",
                    "Greeting",
                    JOptionPane.INFORMATION_MESSAGE);
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new GreetApp());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of DEM Steps:**
1. **Listener:** `GreetHandler` implements `ActionListener`.
2. **Callback:** `actionPerformed()` overridden to read text and show dialog.
3. **Source:** `greetBtn` (a `JButton`).
4. **Registration:** `greetBtn.addActionListener(new GreetHandler())`.

---

### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe the four essential steps of event handling in Java. Explain the role of **Adapter Classes** with an example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Swing program that uses `BorderLayout` to add a `JLabel` at `CENTER` and three `JCheckBox`es (`Red`, `Green`, `Blue`) at `SOUTH` (wrapped in a `FlowLayout` panel). When a checkbox is selected, change the label's background to the corresponding color; when deselected, revert to white. Use `ItemListener`. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Part (a) — Four Steps of Event Handling + Adapters

**The Four Steps of Event Handling in Java:**

1. **Implement the Listener Interface:** Create a class that implements an event-listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`).
2. **Override the Callback Method:** Provide the response logic by overriding the method(s) declared in the interface.
3. **Create the Event Source:** Instantiate the GUI component that will generate events (e.g., `JButton`).
4. **Register the Listener:** Use the source's `addXxxListener()` method to register the listener object.

**Role of Adapter Classes:**

Adapter classes provide **default empty implementations** of listener interfaces that have more than one method. They eliminate the need to write empty stubs for unused callbacks.

| Listener Interface | # Methods | Adapter Class |
|---|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | 5 | `MouseAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | 3 | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | 7 | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | 2 | `FocusAdapter` |

**Example using `WindowAdapter`:**
```java
public class MyFrame extends JFrame {
    public MyFrame() {
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0); // Handle close event only
            }
        });
    }
}
```
Here, we override only `windowClosing()`; the other six methods are inherited as empty.

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Part (b) — Checkbox Color Mixer

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CheckboxColorMixer extends JFrame {
    private JLabel colorLabel;
    private JCheckBox redChk, greenChk, blueChk;

    public CheckboxColorMixer() {
        setTitle("Checkbox Color Mixer");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));

        // CENTER: a color-displaying label
        colorLabel = new JLabel("  Mix colors using checkboxes", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        colorLabel.setOpaque(true);
        colorLabel.setBackground(Color.WHITE);
        add(colorLabel, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // SOUTH: nested panel with three checkboxes
        JPanel southPanel = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 15, 10));
        redChk   = new JCheckBox("Red");
        greenChk = new JCheckBox("Green");
        blueChk  = new JCheckBox("Blue");
        southPanel.add(redChk);
        southPanel.add(greenChk);
        southPanel.add(blueChk);
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        // Register the same ItemListener with all three checkboxes
        ColorMixHandler handler = new ColorMixHandler();
        redChk.addItemListener(handler);
        greenChk.addItemListener(handler);
        blueChk.addItemListener(handler);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    private class ColorMixHandler implements ItemListener {
        @Override
        public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
            int r = redChk.isSelected()   ? 255 : 0;
            int g = greenChk.isSelected() ? 255 : 0;
            int b = blueChk.isSelected()  ? 255 : 0;
            colorLabel.setBackground(new Color(r, g, b));
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new CheckboxColorMixer());
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- `ItemEvent` fires on checkbox toggle. The `ItemListener`'s `itemStateChanged()` is invoked.
- The handler reads all three checkbox states and constructs a new `Color(r, g, b)` where each channel is either 0 or 255.
- The `CENTER` label's background updates accordingly, demonstrating the **additive RGB model**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `setOpaque(true)` on `JLabel`:** A label's background color will be invisible unless `setOpaque(true)` is called. *(-1 to -2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener`:** Mouse click/release/enter/exit → `MouseListener`. Mouse drag/move → `MouseMotionListener`. *(-1 mark)*
> 3. **Not registering the listener:** Writing `implements ActionListener` and overriding `actionPerformed()` but forgetting `button.addActionListener(this)` means the code will compile but **never execute the handler**. *(-3 marks — full handler logic lost)*
> 4. **Adding multiple components to the same `BorderLayout` region:** Only the **last-added** component is shown. The previous ones are silently replaced. *(-2 marks)*
> 5. **Creating GUI on `main()` thread:** Forgetting `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` is acceptable in KTU answers, but if asked about best practices, this is a deduction point. *(-1 mark)*
> 6. **Mixing up `getSource()` and `getActionCommand()`:** `getSource()` returns the object reference; `getActionCommand()` returns the string label. For buttons with the same listener, comparing `getActionCommand()` is more flexible. *(-1 mark)*

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

### 🔹 Layout Managers
- [ ] `BorderLayout`: 5 regions, only **CENTER grows**, default of `JFrame`.
- [ ] `FlowLayout`: row-wise, wraps, alignment ∈ {LEFT, CENTER, RIGHT}, default of `JPanel`.
- [ ] `GridLayout`: r × c equal cells, all cells resize uniformly.
- [ ] `GridBagLayout`: most flexible; uses `GridBagConstraints` (`gridx, gridy, gridwidth, gridheight, fill, weightx, weighty, anchor, insets, ipadx, ipady`).
- [ ] `BoxLayout`: `X_AXIS` / `Y_AXIS`; uses glue, strut, rigid area for spacing.
- [ ] `CardLayout`: stack of cards; methods `first()`, `next()`, `previous()`, `last()`, `show(name)`.
- [ ] `GroupLayout`: NetBeans GUI Builder; groups components horizontally/vertically.
- [ ] `SpringLayout`: constraint-based; rarely hand-coded.
- [ ] **Nested layouts** are required when a region needs to hold multiple components (wrap in a `JPanel`).
- [ ] `setLayout(null)` enables absolute positioning, **not recommended** for cross-platform GUIs.

### 🔹 Delegation Event Model
- [ ] Three pillars: **Event Source**, **Event Object**, **Event Listener**.
- [ ] All events extend `java.util.EventObject` (which provides `getSource()` and `getID()`).
- [ ] **Registration is mandatory** — `source.addXxxListener(listener)`.
- [ ] **Adapter classes** provide empty implementations for multi-method listener interfaces.
- [ ] **Inner classes / anonymous classes** are commonly used to implement listeners.
- [ ] **Lambda expressions** can replace anonymous inner classes for functional interfaces (single-method).
- [ ] `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` is the **best practice** for thread-safe GUI creation.
- [ ] Always call `setOpaque(true)` on `JLabel` before setting a background color.
- [ ] `ActionEvent.getActionCommand()` returns the button's text; useful when one listener handles multiple buttons.
- [ ] The Delegation Event Model replaced the JDK 1.0 **inheritance event model** (which required subclassing components to override `handleEvent()`).

### 🔹 Frequently Asked KTU Sub-Topics
- [ ] Diagram of `BorderLayout` regions — **MUST memorize**.
- [ ] Code snippet with `JButton` + `ActionListener`.
- [ ] Adapter class — example and justification.
- [ ] Comparison table of layout managers.
- [ ] The four-step event-handling process.
- [ ] Event types and their corresponding listener interfaces.
- [ ] Difference between `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener`.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 JFrame — The Top-Level Window Container

In the Java Swing (and AWT) graphical library, a **window** is the fundamental object on which all visual components (buttons, text fields, labels) are painted. The class `javax.swing.JFrame` is the official Swing implementation of a **top-level window** with a title bar, border, and the standard minimize / maximize / close icons seen on every operating system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A `JFrame` is a top-level container defined in the `javax.swing` package that extends `java.awt.Frame` and provides a window with title, borders, and built-in support for menu bars. It serves as the **root container** of any standalone Swing desktop application and uses its content pane (obtained via `getContentPane()`) to host child components. The default layout of a `JFrame`'s content pane is `BorderLayout`.

### Class Hierarchy (Inheritance Chain)

$$ JFrame \;\rightarrow\; java.awt.Frame \;\rightarrow\; java.awt.Window \;\rightarrow\; java.awt.Container \;\rightarrow\; java.awt.Component \;\rightarrow\; java.lang.Object $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy matters:** Because `JFrame` inherits from `java.awt.Component`, it is itself a Swing component and supports all painting, sizing, and event-handling APIs of its parent classes. This is what allows a `JFrame` to receive events (mouse, keyboard, window) directly.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Picture Frame for Your Program"

Think of a `JFrame` as the **physical wooden picture frame** that you buy from a shop, while the picture itself (buttons, text boxes, labels) is drawn on a piece of paper that you stick inside the frame. The wood (title, borders, close button) is provided by the operating system through the `Frame` superclass — the **Swing class `JFrame` is just the elegant Java wrapper around it**.

Real-world analogy:
- **Picture Frame (Wooden Border)** = `JFrame`'s title bar, borders, system menu, minimize/maximize/close buttons (provided by the OS).
- **The Glass / Picture Itself** = The `Content Pane` (the inner area where Swing components are actually painted).
- **The Painter** = The application's main thread (`Event Dispatch Thread`).

## 1.3 Event Handling — "The Mailbox System"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition of Event Handling:**
> Event Handling is the mechanism by which a graphical component **reacts** to user interactions (clicking a button, typing a key, resizing a window). Java uses the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**: an event generated by an **Event Source** is dispatched to a registered **Event Listener** whose callback method is then invoked.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Doorbell

| Swing Concept | Real-World Analogy (Doorbell) |
|---|---|
| **Event Source** (e.g., a `JButton`) | The doorbell button on your doorstep |
| **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`) | The electrical signal travelling through the wire |
| **Event Listener** (e.g., `ActionListener`) | The person inside the house registered to respond |
| **Callback method** (e.g., `actionPerformed()`) | The action the person takes (opens the door) |
| **Registering the listener** (`btn.addActionListener(...)`) | Telling the person "when the bell rings, please open the door" |

The button **does not know** what will happen when clicked. It simply delegates the event to whoever is listening — hence the name **Delegation Event Model**.

## 1.4 Key Constants for Window Behaviour

When configuring a `JFrame`, three standard constants are essential for KTU exam answers:

| Constant | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | `3` | Terminates the application when window is closed |
| `JFrame.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | `2` | Closes the window but keeps the JVM alive |
| `JFrame.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | `1` | Hides the window (default for `JFrame`) |
| `JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | `0` | Ignores the close button click |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** `JFrame`'s *default* close operation is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` — unlike `JDialog` or `JInternalFrame`, simply closing a `JFrame` does **not** stop the JVM. For a single-window program you almost always set it to `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Delegation Event Model

The Delegation Event Model has exactly **three roles**, and understanding them guarantees marks in any KTU theory question:

### Pillar 1 — Event Source
The object on which the event **occurs**. It must:
1. Maintain a list of registered listeners internally.
2. Provide `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()` methods (e.g., `addActionListener()`, `addKeyListener()`).
3. Provide a method (usually `processXxxEvent()`) that constructs an event object and forwards it to every registered listener.

### Pillar 2 — Event Listener
The object that **wants to be notified**. It is created by implementing a *Listener interface* (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`). When the source fires an event, the corresponding callback method of the listener is invoked.

### Pillar 3 — Event Object
An object of a class that **describes the event** (e.g., `ActionEvent` carries the source and the action command). It is automatically constructed by the source and passed as an argument to the listener's callback method.

## 2.2 Common Event Types and Their Listeners (High-Yield Table)

| Event Class | Triggered By | Listener Interface | Key Callback Method(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | Button click, Menu item, Enter in text field | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseEvent` | Mouse press / release / click / enter / exit | `MouseListener` | `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseClicked`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `MouseEvent` (motion) | Mouse move / drag | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseMoved`, `mouseDragged` |
| `KeyEvent` | Key press / release / type | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` |
| `WindowEvent` | Window open / close / iconify / activate | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` |
| `ItemEvent` | Checkbox toggle, list selection change | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A KTU favourite — when an interface declares more than one abstract method (e.g., `MouseListener` has 5, `KeyListener` has 3, `WindowListener` has 7), implementing the interface forces you to provide *all* the methods even if you only need one. Java solves this via **Adapter classes** (`MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, etc.) which provide empty default implementations.

## 2.3 Three Styles of Providing a Listener Body (Step-by-Step Logic)

A KTU 14-mark question frequently asks you to demonstrate *all three* ways of writing an event handler. Mastery of these is essential.

### Style 1 — Separate Class Implementing the Listener
- Create a new class (e.g., `MyHandler`) that implements `ActionListener`.
- Override the callback method.
- Instantiate the class and pass it to `addActionListener()`.
- **Why use it:** Reusable across multiple components; separates GUI code from business logic.

### Style 2 — The Class Itself Implements the Listener
- Make the outer class (often the one containing `main`) implement the interface (e.g., `implements ActionListener`).
- The whole class is the listener; pass `this` to `addActionListener()`.
- Inside, use `if (e.getSource() == btn1) { ... }` to discriminate between sources.
- **Why use it:** Compact for small programs; keeps everything in one file.

### Style 3 — Anonymous Inner Class (or Lambda)
- Provide the listener body **inline** at the call to `addActionListener()`.
- For a *single-method* interface (functional interface) such as `ActionListener`, Java 8+ allows a **lambda expression** as a syntactic shortcut.
- **Why use it:** Localized, readable, modern; ideal when the handler is short and used only once.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a programming topic, the "formulas" are the canonical constructor / method signatures. Memorise these for full marks.

| Construct | Syntax / Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Create frame | `JFrame frame = new JFrame("Title");` | Constructs a window with title bar text "Title" |
| Default constructor | `JFrame frame = new JFrame();` | Creates a window with no title |
| Set size | `frame.setSize(int width, int height);` | Sets window dimensions in **pixels** |
| Set location | `frame.setLocation(int x, int y);` | Sets top-left corner position (pixels from top-left of screen) |
| Combined | `frame.setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h);` | Sets location + size in one call |
| Show window | `frame.setVisible(true);` | **Required** — windows are invisible by default |
| Close behaviour | `frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);` | Controls what `X` does |
| Get content pane | `Container c = frame.getContentPane();` | Returns the inner container |
| Set layout | `c.setLayout(new FlowLayout());` | Replaces default `BorderLayout` |
| Add component | `c.add(new JButton("OK"));` | Adds child component to content pane |
| Set bg color | `c.setBackground(Color.CYAN);` | Sets background color of content pane |
| Register listener | `btn.addActionListener(this);` | Wires the event source to a listener |
| Lambda form | `btn.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Clicked"));` | Functional-interface shortcut (Java 8+) |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Event-driven Swing applications are the backbone of:
- **SCADA / HMI dashboards** for industrial control rooms (operator clicks a button, a PLC command is sent over the network).
- **Medical imaging software** (CT/MRI viewers) where mouse-drag events pan and zoom the image.
- **CAD tools** where `KeyListener` enables keyboard shortcuts and `MouseMotionListener` enables free-hand drawing.
- **Banking front-ends** where every `ActionEvent` triggers a database transaction.

The Delegation Event Model is the foundational design pattern that paved the way for modern frameworks (JavaFX, Android's `OnClickListener`, web DOM events, and even `addEventListener` in JavaScript) — so the principles you learn here are universally transferable.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Code Walkthrough

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Building a Swing `JFrame` from Scratch

The canonical "Hello-Swing" program, dissected line-by-line exactly as a KTU board examiner expects.

```java
import javax.swing.*;            // 1. Imports all Swing classes (JFrame, JButton, JLabel)
import java.awt.*;               // 2. Imports AWT (FlowLayout, Color, Container)
import java.awt.event.*;         // 3. Imports AWT event classes (ActionEvent, ActionListener)

public class SwingDemo implements ActionListener {  // 4. Class implements the listener interface
    JLabel label;                                          // 5. Field for a label component

    SwingDemo() {                                          // 6. Constructor builds the GUI
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("My First Swing App");   // 7. Create the top-level window
        frame.setSize(400, 200);                           // 8. 400 px wide, 200 px tall
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // 9. JVM exits on 'X'

        Container c = frame.getContentPane();              // 10. Get the inner container
        c.setLayout(new FlowLayout());                     // 11. Set a flow layout manager
        c.setBackground(Color.CYAN);                       // 12. Cyan background

        JButton btn = new JButton("Click Me");             // 13. Create a button
        btn.addActionListener(this);                       // 14. Register `this` as the listener

        label = new JLabel("Hello, World!");               // 15. Create a label with initial text
        c.add(btn);                                        // 16. Add button to content pane
        c.add(label);                                      // 17. Add label to content pane

        frame.setVisible(true);                            // 18. Make the window visible
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {           // 19. Mandatory callback
        label.setText("Button was clicked!");              // 20. Change label text on click
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {               // 21. Standard entry point
        new SwingDemo();                                   // 22. Construct the GUI
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic Explanation

| Line | Action | Why It Is Required |
|---|---|---|
| 1–3 | Import packages | Java's `java.lang` is auto-imported; everything else needs explicit import |
| 4 | `implements ActionListener` | Authorises the class to receive `ActionEvent`s |
| 7 | `new JFrame("Title")` | Allocates the OS window |
| 8 | `setSize(400, 200)` | Without this, the window opens at **0 × 0 pixels** (invisible in practice) |
| 9 | `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Prevents the JVM from lingering after the window closes |
| 10 | `getContentPane()` | In **modern Java 5+**, this method is rarely required — you can call `frame.add(...)` directly — but exam answers and legacy code still use it |
| 11 | `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` | Overrides the default `BorderLayout` of the content pane |
| 14 | `addActionListener(this)` | Wires the button to the current object — the only listener |
| 18 | `setVisible(true)` | Until this is called, the window is not painted (the JFrame is created but **not displayed**) |
| 19 | `actionPerformed` | Mandatory method from `ActionListener` interface |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — All Three Listener Styles Compared

Consider a button `btn` and a label `lbl`. The following matrix shows the **exact** code for each style.

### Style 1 — Separate Class

```java
class MyHandler implements ActionListener {
    JLabel lbl;                                   // 1. Reference to the label to update
    MyHandler(JLabel lbl) { this.lbl = lbl; }     // 2. Constructor stores the reference

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { // 3. Callback
        lbl.setText("Clicked (Style 1)");        // 4. Update the label
    }
}

// Registration
btn.addActionListener(new MyHandler(lbl));
```

### Style 2 — Class Implements Listener

```java
class App extends JFrame implements ActionListener { // 1. Outer class is the listener
    JLabel lbl;                                       // 2. Field
    App() {                                           // 3. Constructor builds GUI
        JButton btn = new JButton("Click");
        btn.addActionListener(this);                  // 4. Pass `this` as the listener
        // ...
    }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {     // 5. Callback
        if (e.getSource() == btn)                    // 6. Discriminate between sources
            lbl.setText("Clicked (Style 2)");
    }
}
```

### Style 3 — Anonymous Inner Class (or Lambda)

```java
// Anonymous inner class form
btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        lbl.setText("Clicked (Style 3 - AIC)");
    }
});

// Lambda form (Java 8+)
btn.addActionListener(e -> lbl.setText("Clicked (Style 3 - Lambda)"));
```

> [!NOTE]
> The lambda form works because `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** (exactly one abstract method). The compiler implicitly substitutes the lambda for the entire anonymous class.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Adapter Class to Reduce Boilerplate

Suppose we want to handle only the **closing** of a window. Using `WindowListener` would require writing **all seven** methods. The `WindowAdapter` provides empty bodies for them; we override only the one we need.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AdapterDemo extends JFrame {
    AdapterDemo() {
        setSize(300, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE); // 'X' only hides window
        setVisible(true);

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {            // 1. Anonymous subclass of adapter
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {     // 2. Override ONE method
                System.out.println("Window is closing");   // 3. Custom close logic
                System.exit(0);                            // 4. Force JVM termination
            }
        });
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AdapterDemo();
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Derivation of "Why an Adapter?"

1. `WindowListener` interface contains **7 abstract methods**.
2. Implementing it directly forces 7 overrides — **5 of which are empty** in our case.
3. `WindowAdapter` is a class that **implements `WindowListener` with empty bodies**.
4. By extending `WindowAdapter` and overriding only `windowClosing`, we honour the contract with **minimal code**.
5. The same logic applies to `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `ComponentAdapter`.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If the question says "implement an event listener", check whether the interface has > 1 method. If yes, the **adapter pattern** is the elegant answer.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Full Multi-Component JFrame Application

A typical KTU Part-B (14 mark) question expects a *complete working program* with two or more components wired to event handlers.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextCopier extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField tf;        // 1. Single-line input
    JButton copyBtn, clearBtn; // 2. Two action sources
    JLabel resultLbl;     // 3. Output label

    TextCopier() {
        setTitle("Text Copier");                            // 4. Window title
        setSize(350, 150);                                  // 5. Dimensions
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);            // 6. Exit on close
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());                        // 7. Layout

        tf = new JTextField(15);                            // 8. 15-column text field
        copyBtn = new JButton("Copy");                      // 9. Copy button
        clearBtn = new JButton("Clear");                    // 10. Clear button
        resultLbl = new JLabel("Type something above");     // 11. Initial label text

        copyBtn.addActionListener(this);                    // 12. Register `this` for Copy
        clearBtn.addActionListener(this);                   // 13. Register `this` for Clear

        add(tf); add(copyBtn); add(clearBtn); add(resultLbl); // 14. Add all to frame
        setVisible(true);                                   // 15. Show
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {            // 16. Callback
        if (e.getSource() == copyBtn) {                     // 17. Discriminate source
            resultLbl.setText("You typed: " + tf.getText());
        } else if (e.getSource() == clearBtn) {             // 18. Handle second source
            tf.setText("");                                 // 19. Empty the text field
            resultLbl.setText("Cleared");                   // 20. Update label
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new TextCopier();
    }
}
```

### Analytical Table — Trace of User Actions

| Step | User Action | Source | Listener Invoked | Resulting State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Types "Hello" into `tf` | `JTextField` (no listener) | — | `tf` text = "Hello" |
| 2 | Clicks **Copy** | `JButton copyBtn` | `actionPerformed(e)` | `resultLbl` = "You typed: Hello" |
| 3 | Clicks **Clear** | `JButton clearBtn` | `actionPerformed(e)` | `tf` text = "", `resultLbl` = "Cleared" |
| 4 | Clicks window's **X** | `JFrame` | `WindowListener` (default) | JVM exits (because of `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting to call `setVisible(true)` is the **#1 reason** Swing programs "don't work". A window is created but stays invisible. Always place `setVisible(true)` **at the end of the constructor**, after all components are added.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy for `JFrame` and Listeners

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Object] --> B[Component]
    B --> C[Container]
    C --> D[Window]
    D --> E[Frame]
    E --> F[JFrame]
    F --> G[UserDefinedFrame]

    H[EventObject] --> I[AWTEvent]
    I --> J[ActionEvent]
    I --> K[WindowEvent]
    I --> L[MouseEvent]
    I --> M[KeyEvent]

    N[ActionListener] --> O[UserHandler]
    P[WindowListener] --> Q[WindowAdapter]
    P --> R[UserWindowHandler]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Delegation Event Model — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[JButton Event Source] -- addActionListener --> LIST[ActionListener Object]
    LIST -- implements --> IFACE[ActionListener interface]
    IFACE -- contains --> CB[actionPerformed ActionEvent e]
    SRC -- user clicks --> EVT[ActionEvent created automatically]
    EVT -- passed to --> CB
    CB -- updates --> UI[JLabel new text]
```

## 4.3 JFrame Internal Structure — Components of a Window

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[JFrame Window]
    F --> ROPT[Root Pane]
    ROPT --> LAYER[Layered Pane]
    LAYER --> CP[Content Pane]
    LAYER --> GP[Glass Pane]
    LAYER --> MB[JMenuBar if any]
    CP --> B1[JButton]
    CP --> L1[JLabel]
    CP --> TF1[JTextField]
    F --> TITLE[Title Bar OS Provided]
    F --> BORD[Border OS Provided]
```

## 4.4 Event Flow Lifecycle (Block Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Program starts main method] --> B[Construct JFrame]
    B --> C[setSize setDefaultCloseOperation]
    C --> D[getContentPane setLayout]
    D --> E[Create JButton and JLabel]
    E --> F[addActionListener register]
    F --> G[setVisible true]
    G --> H{User clicks button}
    H -- Yes --> I[JVM fires ActionEvent]
    I --> J[actionPerformed invoked]
    J --> K[label setText updated]
    K --> H
    H -- No --> L{User clicks X}
    L -- Yes --> M[WindowEvent handled]
    M --> N[EXIT ON CLOSE JVM ends]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is the Delegation Event Model in Java? List its three components.*

**Model Answer (expected length: 3–4 lines + bullets):**

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is Java's standard mechanism for handling events generated by GUI components. Instead of a component handling its own events, the event is *delegated* to one or more registered listener objects. It has three components:

1. **Event Source** — the object that generates the event (e.g., a `JButton`). It maintains a list of listeners and provides `addXxxListener()` / `removeXxxListener()` methods.
2. **Event Listener** — an object that implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`). It contains the callback method invoked when the event occurs.
3. **Event Object** — an object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) that encapsulates details of the event and is passed as an argument to the listener's callback method.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between `JFrame.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` and `JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE`.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (default) | `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` |
|---|---|---|
| Effect of pressing **X** | Window is hidden; JVM still alive | Window is closed; JVM terminates |
| Use case | Multi-window apps where the user may re-open the window | Single-window apps that should end when the user clicks **X** |
| Implementation | Calls `setVisible(false)` on the frame | Calls `System.exit(0)` after closing the window |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — the program compiles but "doesn't work". Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong import** — using `java.awt.event.*` correctly but importing only `javax.swing.*` is fine; the reverse is **not** (Swing depends on AWT events).
> 3. **Implementing a multi-method interface without an adapter** — losing 1 mark for verbosity. Use `MouseAdapter` / `WindowAdapter` when only one method is needed.
> 4. **Not calling `getContentPane()`** in legacy code — for Java 5+ it is implicit, but KTU expects its explicit use in textbook answers.
> 5. **Mixing up `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` and `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`** — `DISPOSE` only releases the window; the JVM keeps running if other non-daemon threads exist.

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Delegation Event Model** in Java. List any **four** common listener interfaces and the events they handle. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program that creates a `JFrame` titled *"Calculator Layout Demo"*. The frame must contain **two `JTextField`s** for entering two numbers, **four `JButton`s** labelled *Add, Sub, Mul, Div*, and **one `JLabel`** for displaying the result. Implement the event handling using a **single class that implements `ActionListener`**, distinguishing between sources using `e.getSource()`. Handle division by zero gracefully. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Delegation Event Model** is built on three components — **Event Source, Event Listener, Event Object** — as illustrated below:

```
   USER CLICKS BUTTON
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────┐   addActionListener( )
  │ Event Source │ ─────────────────────►┌───────────────┐
  │  (JButton)   │                       │ Event Listener│
  └──────┬───────┘                       │ (ActionListener)│
         │                               └───────┬───────┘
         │ on click                              │
         ▼                                       │
  ┌──────────────┐     delivered to     ┌───────▼────────┐
  │ Event Object │ ────────────────────►│ actionPerformed│
  │ (ActionEvent)│                      │ (callback)     │
  └──────────────┘                      └────────────────┘
```

**Four common listener interfaces:**

| Listener Interface | Event Class | Callback Method |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyEvent` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowEvent` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, etc. |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CalculatorDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField t1, t2;
    JButton addBtn, subBtn, mulBtn, divBtn;
    JLabel result;

    CalculatorDemo() {
        setTitle("Calculator Layout Demo");
        setSize(420, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        t1 = new JTextField(8);
        t2 = new JTextField(8);
        addBtn  = new JButton("Add");
        subBtn  = new JButton("Sub");
        mulBtn  = new JButton("Mul");
        divBtn  = new JButton("Div");
        result  = new JLabel("Result will appear here");

        addBtn.addActionListener(this);
        subBtn.addActionListener(this);
        mulBtn.addActionListener(this);
        divBtn.addActionListener(this);

        add(t1); add(t2);
        add(addBtn); add(subBtn); add(mulBtn); add(divBtn);
        add(result);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            double a = Double.parseDouble(t1.getText());
            double b = Double.parseDouble(t2.getText());
            double r = 0;
            if (e.getSource() == addBtn)      r = a + b;
            else if (e.getSource() == subBtn) r = a - b;
            else if (e.getSource() == mulBtn) r = a * b;
            else if (e.getSource() == divBtn) {
                if (b == 0) { result.setText("Cannot divide by zero"); return; }
                r = a / b;
            }
            result.setText("Result = " + r);
        } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
            result.setText("Invalid input");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CalculatorDemo();
    }
}
```

**Key marking points the examiner checks:**
- `implements ActionListener` (1 mark)
- `setVisible(true)` present (1 mark)
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` (0.5 mark)
- Four distinct buttons with their own actions (2 marks)
- Use of `e.getSource()` to discriminate (1 mark)
- Division-by-zero handling (1 mark)
- Compilation-readability, indentation (0.5 mark)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **class hierarchy of `JFrame`** in Java Swing. Why is `getContentPane()` used to add components? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates a `JFrame` containing a `JTextField` and a `JButton` labelled *"Submit"*. Implement the `ActionListener` using a **separate class** (Style 1). When the button is clicked, the label (already added) should display the text from the text field. Use `WindowAdapter` to print *"Goodbye!"* when the window is closing. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The class hierarchy of `JFrame` is:

$$
\text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Component} \rightarrow \text{Container} \rightarrow \text{Window} \rightarrow \text{Frame} \rightarrow \text{JFrame}
$$

- `Component` — the abstract superclass of *all* AWT/Swing visual objects. Provides paint, sizing, event support.
- `Container` — a `Component` that can contain other components. Provides `add()`, `remove()`, layout manager support.
- `Window` — a top-level window with **no** title bar or border; pure OS window.
- `Frame` — a `Window` *with* a title bar, border, and menu bar.
- `JFrame` — Swing's lightweight, pluggable-look-and-feel implementation of `Frame`.

**Why `getContentPane()`?**
A `JFrame` actually consists of several **layered panes** — the *root pane*, *layered pane*, *content pane*, and *glass pane*. Components must be added to the **content pane** because Swing's painting architecture expects components there (it ensures correct z-ordering and event propagation). The convenience method `frame.add(comp)` is a shortcut for `frame.getContentPane().add(comp)`. In KTU answers, *always* show the explicit form to score full marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class SubmitHandler implements ActionListener {
    JTextField tf; JLabel lbl;
    SubmitHandler(JTextField tf, JLabel lbl) { this.tf = tf; this.lbl = lbl; }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        lbl.setText("You entered: " + tf.getText());
    }
}

public class SubmitApp extends JFrame {
    SubmitApp() {
        setTitle("Submit App");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        JTextField tf = new JTextField(15);
        JButton btn  = new JButton("Submit");
        JLabel lbl   = new JLabel("Type and submit");

        Container c = getContentPane();
        c.add(tf); c.add(btn); c.add(lbl);

        btn.addActionListener(new SubmitHandler(tf, lbl));

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Goodbye!");
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SubmitApp();
    }
}
```

**Key marking points:**
- Separate `SubmitHandler` class implementing `ActionListener` (1 mark)
- Constructor stores references to the source and target components (1 mark)
- `WindowAdapter` used (not raw `WindowListener`) (1 mark)
- `windowClosing` overridden to print "Goodbye!" (1 mark)
- `setVisible(true)` (1 mark)
- `getContentPane()` used explicitly (1 mark)
- Code compiles mentally — imports correct (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things you MUST memorise for the KTU exam (5-minute revision list):**

- ✅ `JFrame` is in `javax.swing`; extends `java.awt.Frame`; imports needed: `javax.swing.*`, `java.awt.*`, `java.awt.event.*`.
- ✅ A `JFrame` is invisible until you call `setVisible(true)` — the #1 cause of "code doesn't work".
- ✅ Default close operation is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`; for terminal apps, set `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`.
- ✅ Default layout of content pane is `BorderLayout`; override with `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` etc.
- ✅ `getContentPane()` returns the inner container where components must be added.
- ✅ Delegation Event Model = **Event Source + Event Listener + Event Object**.
- ✅ To add a listener: `source.addXxxListener(listenerObject)`.
- ✅ Three styles: separate class, class implements listener, anonymous inner class / lambda.
- ✅ For multi-method listener interfaces (`MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`), use the corresponding **Adapter** class to avoid boilerplate.
- ✅ `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** → lambda expressions are allowed (`btn.addActionListener(e -> { ... })`).
- ✅ Common methods to remember: `setSize(w, h)`, `setLocation(x, y)`, `setBounds(x, y, w, h)`, `setTitle(String)`, `setBackground(Color)`, `setForeground(Color)`, `setResizable(boolean)`, `setLayout(LayoutManager)`.
- ✅ `e.getSource()` is used inside a single listener to discriminate between multiple sources.
- ✅ `e.getActionCommand()` returns the button label (alternative to `getSource()` for `ActionEvent`).

> **🎯 End of Module 4 — JFrame & Event Handling Notes** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JLabel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** JLabel # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of JLabel ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **JLabel** is a fundamental class within the `javax.swing` package of Java's Standard Edition (Java SE). It extends the `JComponent` class and is primarily used to **display read-only text, images, or both** within a GUI (Graphical User Interface) container such as a `JFrame`, `JPanel`, or `JApplet`. According to the official Java API specification, `JLabel` is a **display area** for a short text string, an image, or both. The text and/or image can be aligned horizontally and vertically within the label's display area. By default, labels are vertically centered, and text labels are leading-edge aligned (left-aligned for left-to-right directions). Labels are also **non-focusable** by default, which means the user cannot navigate to them using the keyboard focus traversal. ### Class Declaration ```java public class JLabel extends JComponent implements SwingConstants, Accessible ``` ### Package and Hierarchy | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | **Package** | `javax.swing` | | **Direct Superclass** | `javax.swing.JComponent` | | **Implemented Interfaces** | `SwingConstants`, `javax.accessibility.Accessible` | | **Introduced in** | **JDK 1.2** (with the Swing API) | | **Common Use Case** | Static text display, image display, mnemonic keys | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of a JLabel as a "name tag" or a "sign board" in the real world.** Imagine you walk into a classroom. On the wall, there is a fixed **name plate** that says "Dr. Sharma's Office." This name plate: - Displays information (text) but you **cannot edit it** directly. - It is **passive** — it does not respond to clicks (unlike a button). - It has a **fixed size and position** on the wall. A **`JLabel` behaves exactly like this name plate** inside a Java application window: - It **displays static information** (text like "Username:" or an image like a logo). - It is **not interactive** — the user cannot click it to trigger actions (that's the job of a `JButton`). - It can be **positioned and aligned** within its parent container (the JFrame or JPanel). **Real-World Mapping Table:** | Real-World Object | JLabel Equivalent | |-------------------|-------------------| | Name plate on a door | `JLabel label = new JLabel("Name:");` | | Logo on a website header | `JLabel label = new JLabel(icon);` | | Caption under a photo | `JLabel label = new JLabel("Sunset", icon, JLabel.CENTER);` | | Signboard in a museum | `JLabel label = new JLabel("Do Not Touch");` | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember:** **L**abel = **L**ook-only, **L**ocked-in-place component. Users can **look** at it, but they cannot **lock onto it** with focus or change its contents via direct interaction. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & JLabel Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind JLabel Before Swing, Java used the **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)**. Labels in AWT were instances of the `java.awt.Label` class. However, AWT components were **heavyweight** (relying on the underlying OS's native peers).
