Swing's `JLabel` is a **lightweight** component — it is rendered entirely by Java, ensuring **consistent appearance across platforms** (Write Once, Run Anywhere — WORA). ### Core Functional Roles of JLabel 1. **Text Display** — Show descriptive text such as "Enter Password:" near a `JPasswordField`. 2. **Image Display** — Display an `ImageIcon` (e.g., company logo). 3. **Combined Display** — Show both text and image simultaneously (e.g., a button-shaped label with an icon and caption). 4. **Mnemonic Association** — Define a keyboard shortcut (e.g., **Alt + U**) that transfers focus to an associated component (like a `JTextField`). 5. **Accessibility Support** — Implements the `Accessible` interface, allowing screen readers to interpret the label's content for visually impaired users. ## 2.2 Constructors of JLabel The `JLabel` class provides **six constructors** in the `javax.swing` package. Each constructor offers a different level of initialization flexibility. | # | Constructor | Purpose | Example | |---|-------------|---------|---------| | 1 | `JLabel()` | Creates an **empty label** with no text or icon. | `JLabel l = new JLabel();` | | 2 | `JLabel(String text)` | Creates a label with the **specified text**. | `JLabel l = new JLabel("Hello");` | | 3 | `JLabel(Icon image)` | Creates a label with the **specified image**. | `JLabel l = new JLabel(myIcon);` | | 4 | `JLabel(String text, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with text and a **specific horizontal alignment**. | `JLabel l = new JLabel("Hi", JLabel.CENTER);` | | 5 | `JLabel(Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with an image and alignment. | `JLabel l = new JLabel(myIcon, JLabel.LEFT);` | | 6 | `JLabel(String text, Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with **both text and image** plus alignment. | `JLabel l = new JLabel("Save", saveIcon, JLabel.CENTER);` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alignment Constants (from `SwingConstants` interface):** > - `JLabel.LEFT` (or `SwingConstants.LEFT`) — Aligns to the left edge. > - `JLabel.CENTER` (or `SwingConstants.CENTER`) — Centers the content. > - `JLabel.RIGHT` (or `SwingConstants.RIGHT`) — Aligns to the right edge. > - `JLabel.LEADING` — Edge appropriate for the writing system (default). > - `JLabel.TRAILING` — Opposite of leading. ## 2.3 Key Methods of JLabel (The "Cheat Sheet") The following table lists the **most frequently tested methods** in KTU examinations. The **"Effect"** column describes what the method actually does at runtime. | Method Signature | Return Type | Effect | |------------------|-------------|--------| | `setText(String text)` | `void` | Sets the **displayed text** of the label. | | `getText()` | `String` | Returns the **current text** of the label. | | `setIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | Sets the **icon (image)** displayed by the label. | | `getIcon()` | `Icon` | Returns the **current icon** of the label. | | `setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)` | `void` | Sets the **horizontal position** of the content. | | `setVerticalAlignment(int alignment)` | `void` | Sets the **vertical position** of the content. | | `setHorizontalTextPosition(int textPosition)` | `void` | Sets the **text position relative to the icon** (LEFT, CENTER, RIGHT, LEADING, TRAILING of icon). | | `setVerticalTextPosition(int textPosition)` | `void` | Sets the **vertical text position** (TOP, CENTER, BOTTOM relative to icon). | | `setIconTextGap(int iconTextGap)` | `void` | Sets the **number of pixels** between the icon and the text. | | `setDisplayedMnemonic(int mnemonic)` | `void` | Sets the **keyboard mnemonic** (Alt + key) for the label. | | `setLabelFor(Component c)` | `void` | Associates the label with another **focusable component** so that the mnemonic transfers focus to it. | | `setFont(Font font)` | `void` | Sets the **font** (typeface, style, size) of the label's text. | | `setForeground(Color fg)` | `void` | Sets the **text color** (foreground). | | `setBackground(Color bg)` | `void` | Sets the **background color** (only visible if `setOpaque(true)` is called). | | `setOpaque(boolean isOpaque)` | `void` | When `true`, the label **fills its background** with its background color. | | `setEnabled(boolean enabled)` | `void` | When `false`, the label is **grayed out** and inactive. | | `setDisabledIcon(Icon disabledIcon)` | `void` | Sets the **icon shown when the label is disabled**. | | `getHorizontalTextPosition()` | `int` | Returns the current **horizontal text position** relative to the icon. | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Exams:** The most commonly confused pair is `setHorizontalAlignment()` vs. `setHorizontalTextPosition()`. > - `setHorizontalAlignment()` positions the **entire label content (text + icon together)** within the label's bounds. > - `setHorizontalTextPosition()` positions the **text relative to the icon** when both are displayed. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility JLabel is a **cornerstone component** in: 1. **Enterprise Desktop Applications** (Banking software, ERP systems) — Displaying field names next to input boxes. 2. **Medical Imaging Software** — Displaying patient information overlays. 3. **Educational Software** — Showing captions, instructions, and image annotations. 4. **Kiosk Interfaces** — Static informational displays in airports and malls. 5. **IDE Plugins and Developer Tools** — Status bar messages and tool descriptions. In **KTU 2024 Scheme labs**, JLabel is typically used as part of a `JFrame` setup where students must build a complete GUI form (e.g., a Login Screen, a Calculator, or a Student Registration form). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Practical Demonstrations Since `JLabel` is a **programming component** (not a physics/math concept), the "derivations" here take the form of **step-by-step code walkthroughs** showing how each method affects the visual output. ## 3.1 Example 1: Basic JLabel with Text ### Program Code ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; public class BasicLabelDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // Step 1: Create the main window (JFrame) JFrame frame = new JFrame("JLabel Demo - Basic"); frame.setSize(400, 200); frame.setLayout(null); // Absolute positioning for clarity frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // Step 2: Create a JLabel with simple text JLabel label = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU OOP Course!"); // Step 3: Set the position and size of the label label.setBounds(50, 50, 300, 30); // Step 4: Add the label to the frame frame.add(label); // Step 5: Make the frame visible frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis 1. **Step 1 — Window Creation:** `JFrame` is the top-level container. Setting `setSize(400, 200)` creates a window of 400 pixels wide and 200 pixels tall. 2. **Step 2 — Label Construction:** `new JLabel("Welcome...")` invokes the `JLabel(String text)` constructor. 3. **Step 3 — Positioning:** `setBounds(50, 50, 300, 30)` places the label at coordinates (x=50, y=50) with width 300 and height 30. 4. **Step 4 — Attachment:** `frame.add(label)` attaches the label to the frame's content pane. 5. **Step 5 — Rendering:** `setVisible(true)` triggers the painting of all child components. > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A window appears with the text "Welcome to KTU OOP Course!" displayed at the specified position. ## 3.2 Example 2: JLabel with Icon and Text Positioning ### Program Code ```java import javax.swing.ImageIcon; import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; public class IconLabelDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { JFrame frame = new JFrame("JLabel with Icon"); frame.setSize(500, 300); frame.setLayout(null); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // Step 1: Create an ImageIcon from a file // Ensure "logo.png" is in the project root directory ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("logo.png"); // Step 2: Create a JLabel with both text and icon JLabel label = new JLabel("KTU University", icon, JLabel.CENTER); // Step 3: Position the text relative to the icon label.setHorizontalTextPosition(JLabel.CENTER); label.setVerticalTextPosition(JLabel.BOTTOM); // Step 4: Set the gap between icon and text label.setIconTextGap(10); // Step 5: Customize font label.setFont(new java.awt.Font("Arial", java.awt.Font.BOLD, 16)); // Step 6: Set position and add to frame label.setBounds(50, 50, 400, 150); frame.add(label); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis 1. **Step 1 — Image Loading:** `ImageIcon` is a concrete implementation of the `Icon` interface. The string `"logo.png"` is the **relative path** to the image file. 2. **Step 2 — Dual-Content Label:** The third constructor `JLabel(String, Icon, int)` is used. The horizontal alignment `JLabel.CENTER` centers the entire label content (text + icon) within the label's bounding rectangle. 3. **Step 3 — Text Position:** `setHorizontalTextPosition(JLabel.CENTER)` and `setVerticalTextPosition(JLabel.BOTTOM)` place the text **directly below** the icon, centered horizontally. 4. **Step 4 — Gap Control:** `setIconTextGap(10)` adds a **10-pixel** space between the icon's bottom edge and the text's top edge. 5. **Step 5 — Font Customization:** The `Font` class is from `java.awt`. The constructor takes (font name, style, size). Here, "Arial Bold 16pt" is applied. 6. **Step 6 — Positioning:** The label occupies the area from (50, 50) to (450, 200) within the frame. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Output:** The image appears at the top, and below it, centered, is the bold text "KTU University", with a 10-pixel gap. ## 3.3 Example 3: JLabel with Mnemonic and LabelFor ### Program Code ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.JTextField; public class MnemonicDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { JFrame frame = new JFrame("Mnemonic Demo"); frame.setSize(400, 150); frame.setLayout(null); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // Create the text field JTextField nameField = new JTextField(); nameField.setBounds(150, 50, 200, 30); // Create the label JLabel nameLabel = new JLabel("Name:"); nameLabel.setBounds(50, 50, 90, 30); // Set mnemonic 'N' on the label nameLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('N'); // Associate the label with the text field nameLabel.setLabelFor(nameField); frame.add(nameLabel); frame.add(nameField); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis 1. **Mnemonic Setup:** `setDisplayedMnemonic('N')` assigns **Alt + N** as the keyboard shortcut. The character 'N' in "Name:" will be visually underlined automatically. 2. **Association:** `setLabelFor(nameField)` tells Swing: *"When the user presses Alt + N, transfer focus to `nameField`."* 3. **User Experience:** The user does not need to click the label. They can simply press **Alt + N** to jump directly to the text field. > [!NOTE] > This pattern is **extremely important for accessibility** and is a common KTU exam question. Always remember: **Mnemonic + LabelFor = Focus Transfer to a Component.** ## 3.4 Example 4: HTML Rendering in JLabel ### Program Code ```java JLabel htmlLabel = new JLabel("Bold Text, Italic, Underlined"); htmlLabel.setBounds(50, 50, 300, 30); frame.add(htmlLabel); ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis 1. **HTML Support:** JLabel supports a **subset of HTML** tags (b, i, u, font, html, body, p, h1-h6, br, hr, ul, ol, li, img, table). 2. **Detection Mechanism:** The constructor checks if the text starts with ``. If so, it uses a `BasicHTML` renderer internally. 3. **Use Case:** Useful for displaying **rich text** like multi-styled labels in about dialogs or form headers. > [!WARNING] > Do NOT use complex HTML (like CSS or JavaScript). JLabel's HTML support is **intentionally limited** for security and performance reasons. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: JLabel Class Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.awt.Component"] B --> C["java.awt.Container"] C --> D["javax.swing.JComponent"] D --> E["javax.swing.JLabel"] I1["Interface: java.io.Serializable"] -.-> D I2["Interface: SwingConstants"] -.-> E I3["Interface: Accessible"] -.-> E E --> F["Display Text"] E --> G["Display Icon"] E --> H["Set Alignment"] E --> J["Mnemonic Support"] E --> K["LabelFor Association"] E --> L["HTML Rendering"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** > - **Solid arrows (`-->`)** represent `extends` (inheritance). > - **Dashed arrows (`-.->`)** represent `implements` (interface realization). > - `JLabel` inherits from `JComponent`, which inherits from `java.awt.Container`, which inherits from `java.awt.Component`, the ultimate base of all AWT/Swing components. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: JLabel's Method Categories (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph CONTENT["Content Management"] C1["setText(String)"] C2["getText()"] C3["setIcon(Icon)"] C4["getIcon()"] end subgraph ALIGN["Alignment Control"] A1["setHorizontalAlignment(int)"] A2["setVerticalAlignment(int)"] A3["setHorizontalTextPosition(int)"] A4["setVerticalTextPosition(int)"] A5["setIconTextGap(int)"] end subgraph KEYBOARD["Keyboard Interaction"] K1["setDisplayedMnemonic(char)"] K2["setDisplayedMnemonic(int)"] K3["setLabelFor(Component)"] end subgraph VISUAL["Visual Customization"] V1["setFont(Font)"] V2["setForeground(Color)"] V3["setBackground(Color)"] V4["setOpaque(boolean)"] V5["setEnabled(boolean)"] V6["setDisabledIcon(Icon)"] end subgraph HTML["HTML Features"] H1["setText with html prefix"] end JLabelObj["JLabel Object"] --> CONTENT JLabelObj --> ALIGN JLabelObj --> KEYBOARD JLabelObj --> VISUAL JLabelObj --> HTML ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Visual Layout of a JLabel with Text + Icon ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Label["JLabel Bounding Box (400x150)"] direction TB subgraph Top["Top Section"] ICON["ImageIcon (logo.png) - 80x80 px"] end subgraph Middle["Gap of 10 pixels"] GAP["setIconTextGap(10)"] end subgraph Bottom["Bottom Section"] TXT["Text: KTU University - Bold 16pt"] end end HAlign["Horizontal Alignment = CENTER"] -.-> Label VTextPos["Vertical Text Position = BOTTOM"] -.-> Bottom ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The label's bounding box is **centered** (horizontal alignment). Within that box, the icon is drawn at the top, followed by a 10-pixel gap, and then the text appears at the bottom — as specified by `setVerticalTextPosition(JLabel.BOTTOM)`. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]** ### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** **"Explain the role of `javax.swing.JLabel` in Java GUI programming. List any three constructors of the JLabel class."** ### **Model Answer (A1):** **Role of JLabel:** `JLabel` is a lightweight Swing component used to display a **short text string, an image, or both** within a GUI. It is a **read-only, non-focusable** display area, primarily used to: 1. Provide **descriptions** for other input components (e.g., labeling a text field). 2. Display **static images** such as logos. 3. Act as a **mnemonic anchor** to provide keyboard shortcuts for associated components. **Three Constructors:** | # | Constructor | Description | |---|-------------|-------------| | 1 | `JLabel()` | Creates an empty label with no text and no icon. | | 2 | `JLabel(String text)` | Creates a label with the specified text string. | | 3 | `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with text, icon, and horizontal alignment. | --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Question]** ### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** **"Differentiate between `setHorizontalAlignment()` and `setHorizontalTextPosition()` methods of JLabel with suitable examples."** ### **Model Answer (A2):** | Aspect | `setHorizontalAlignment()` | `setHorizontalTextPosition()` | |--------|---------------------------|-------------------------------| | **Purpose** | Positions the **entire label content (text + icon)** within the label's display area. | Positions the **text relative to the icon** when both are present. | | **Default Value** | `JLabel.LEADING` (left for LTR languages) | `JLabel.TRAILING` | | **Valid Constants** | `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING` | `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING` | | **Example** | `label.setHorizontalAlignment(JLabel.CENTER);` | `label.setHorizontalTextPosition(JLabel.RIGHT);` | **Illustrative Example:** ```java JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Save", saveIcon, JLabel.CENTER); // Entire (text + icon) is centered in the label lbl.setHorizontalTextPosition(JLabel.LEFT); // Text "Save" appears to the LEFT of the icon ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 typically offers an internal choice between two questions. The model answers below are exhaustive, step-by-step, and align with the **14-mark split (7 + 7)**. --- ### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]** **"Write a Java Swing program to create a JFrame containing:** **(a)** A JLabel displaying the text "Student Registration Form" in **Arial Bold 24pt**, centered horizontally, with **red foreground color**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A JLabel displaying an image (assume `student.png` exists) **to the left** and the text "Upload Photo" **to the right of the icon**, with a **15-pixel gap** between them. **(7 Marks)"** ### **Model Answer (B1):** **Complete Program:** ```java import java.awt.Color; import java.awt.Font; import javax.swing.ImageIcon; import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; public class StudentRegistrationGUI { public static void main(String[] args) { // -------- Frame Setup -------- JFrame frame = new JFrame("Student Registration"); frame.setSize(600, 400); frame.setLayout(null); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // ===== (a) Title Label ===== JLabel titleLabel = new JLabel("Student Registration Form"); // Set font: Arial, Bold, 24pt titleLabel.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 24)); // Set foreground color to RED titleLabel.setForeground(Color.RED); // Center the label content horizontally titleLabel.setHorizontalAlignment(JLabel.CENTER); // Position and size: (x=50, y=20, w=500, h=40) titleLabel.setBounds(50, 20, 500, 40); frame.add(titleLabel); // ===== (b) Image + Text Label ===== // Load the image ImageIcon photoIcon = new ImageIcon("student.png"); // Create label with text, icon, and center alignment JLabel photoLabel = new JLabel("Upload Photo", photoIcon, JLabel.CENTER); // Position the text to the RIGHT of the icon photoLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(JLabel.RIGHT); // Set vertical text position to CENTER (aligned with icon) photoLabel.setVerticalTextPosition(JLabel.CENTER); // Set 15-pixel gap between icon and text photoLabel.setIconTextGap(15); // Position and size photoLabel.setBounds(50, 100, 400, 100); frame.add(photoLabel); // -------- Show Frame -------- frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation:** **(a) Title Label (7 Marks Breakdown):** 1. **Constructor Used:** `JLabel(String text)` — passes the title text. 2. **Font Customization:** `new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 24)` — Font name, style constant `Font.BOLD`, and size 24. 3. **Color:** `Color.RED` is a static field of `java.awt.Color` (predefined colors: RED, BLUE, GREEN, BLACK, WHITE, etc.). 4. **Alignment:** `setHorizontalAlignment(JLabel.CENTER)` centers the entire text within the label's display area. 5. **Positioning:** `setBounds(50, 20, 500, 40)` reserves a 500x40 region starting at (50, 20). 6. **Mark Allocation:** Font setup (2 marks), color (1 mark), alignment (2 marks), positioning & addition (2 marks) = **7 marks**. **(b) Image + Text Label (7 Marks Breakdown):** 1. **Image Loading:** `ImageIcon` class with a relative file path `"student.png"`. 2. **Constructor:** `JLabel(String, Icon, int)` — three-argument constructor for combined display. 3. **Text-Icon Positioning:** `setHorizontalTextPosition(JLabel.RIGHT)` — places the text to the right of the icon. 4. **Vertical Alignment:** `setVerticalTextPosition(JLabel.CENTER)` — vertically aligns text with the icon's center. 5. **Gap Setting:** `setIconTextGap(15)` — exactly 15 pixels of space between icon and text. 6. **Mark Allocation:** Image loading (2 marks), text-icon positioning (3 marks), gap (1 mark), frame addition (1 mark) = **7 marks**. --- ### **Question B2 — Option B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]** **"(a)** Explain the concept of **mnemonics** in JLabel. Write a Java program to create a login form with a JLabel "Username:" that uses **Alt + U** as a mnemonic to transfer focus to an adjacent JTextField. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain how JLabel supports **HTML rendering**. Write a code snippet to display a label with bold red text containing the message "**Invalid Login!**" wrapped in an `` tag. **(7 Marks)"** ### **Model Answer (B2):** **(a) Mnemonics in JLabel (7 Marks):** **Conceptual Explanation:** A **mnemonic** is a keyboard shortcut (usually **Alt + a key**) that allows users to quickly navigate a GUI. When set on a JLabel, the corresponding character in the label's text is automatically **underlined**. When the user presses the mnemonic key combination, focus is transferred to a **target component** specified by `setLabelFor()`. **Program Code:** ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.JTextField; public class LoginMnemonicDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { JFrame frame = new JFrame("Login Form"); frame.setSize(350, 150); frame.setLayout(null); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // Username text field JTextField userField = new JTextField(); userField.setBounds(130, 40, 180, 30); // Username label JLabel userLabel = new JLabel("Username:"); userLabel.setBounds(30, 40, 90, 30); // Set mnemonic 'U' — the 'U' in "Username" will be underlined userLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('U'); // Associate the label with the text field // Pressing Alt+U will move focus to userField userLabel.setLabelFor(userField); frame.add(userLabel); frame.add(userField); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` **Explanation (7 marks):** 1. **Mnemonic Method:** `setDisplayedMnemonic(char mnemonic)` — sets the shortcut key (2 marks). 2. **LabelFor Method:** `setLabelFor(Component c)` — links the label to a focusable component (2 marks). 3. **Visual Indicator:** The character 'U' in "Username" is automatically underlined (1 mark). 4. **Focus Transfer Behavior:** Alt+U moves keyboard focus to `userField` (2 marks). --- **(b) HTML Rendering in JLabel (7 Marks):** **Conceptual Explanation:** `JLabel` supports a **limited subset of HTML 3.2** tags. If the text passed to the JLabel constructor starts with ``, Swing's `BasicHTML` class parses the text and renders it using multiple font styles. Supported tags include ``, ``, ``, ``, ``, ``, `
`, `

`, `

` to `

`, `
    `, `
      `, `
    1. `, ``, ``, `
      `, and `` (with restrictions). **Code Snippet:** ```java import java.awt.Color; import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; public class HTMLErrorLabel { public static void main(String[] args) { JFrame frame = new JFrame("Error Display"); frame.setSize(400, 150); frame.setLayout(null); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // HTML-formatted label JLabel errorLabel = new JLabel( "Invalid Login!" ); errorLabel.setBounds(50, 50, 300, 30); frame.add(errorLabel); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` **Explanation (7 marks):** 1. **HTML Detection Mechanism:** JLabel checks if text starts with `` (1 mark). 2. **Bold Tag:** `...` makes the text bold (2 marks). 3. **Font Color Tag:** `` sets the text color to red (2 marks). 4. **Security Note:** Only a limited HTML subset is supported; JavaScript and CSS are not allowed (2 marks). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `frame.setLayout(null)`** — Without this, `setBounds()` will not work, and the label will not appear at the specified position. (Lose 1-2 marks) > 2. **Confusing alignment methods** — Students often write `setHorizontalAlignment()` when asked about text-relative-to-icon positioning. Read the question carefully! > 3. **Missing `setLabelFor()`** — When a question asks for mnemonic focus transfer, simply calling `setDisplayedMnemonic()` is **not enough**. You **must** call `setLabelFor()` to complete the association. > 4. **Image path errors** — If the image file is not in the correct directory, the icon will not load silently, and the examiner may mark the image-handling portion as incorrect. Always mention the file path clearly. > 5. **Forgetting to import** — `javax.swing.JLabel` and `javax.swing.JFrame` must both be imported; otherwise, compilation fails. Also, `java.awt.Color` and `java.awt.Font` for color and font customization. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — JLabel** - ✅ **JLabel** is a **lightweight** Swing component in the **`javax.swing`** package. - ✅ It **extends `JComponent`** and implements **`SwingConstants`** and **`Accessible`**. - ✅ JLabel is **non-focusable** and **read-only** by default. - ✅ It can display **text, image, or both**. - ✅ **Six constructors** exist — ranging from empty to fully configured. - ✅ **`setHorizontalAlignment()`** positions the **entire content** within the label. - ✅ **`setHorizontalTextPosition()`** positions the **text relative to the icon**. - ✅ **`setIconTextGap(int)`** controls the **pixel gap** between icon and text. - ✅ **Mnemonics** require **two steps**: `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` + `setLabelFor(Component)`. - ✅ **HTML rendering** is enabled by prefixing text with ``. - ✅ **`setOpaque(true)`** is required to make the **background color** visible. - ✅ **`setEnabled(false)`** grays out the label and its icon. - ✅ JLabel **does not generate ActionEvents** — it is purely a display component. - ✅ Use **AWT constants** like `Color.RED`, `Font.BOLD`, and `SwingConstants.CENTER` for full marks. - ✅ Always import: `javax.swing.JLabel`, `javax.swing.JFrame`, `java.awt.Color`, `java.awt.Font`, `javax.swing.ImageIcon`. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Memory Aid:** > **"JLabel = Just a Label"** — it shows information, doesn't act on it. Pair it with `JTextField` (input) or `JButton` (action) for a complete interactive form.

topics.title: JButton
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** JButton

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**`JButton`** is a concrete subclass of `javax.swing.AbstractButton` that resides in the `javax.swing` package of the Java Standard Edition (SE) API. It is the fundamental **push-button** component in Java's Swing GUI toolkit, used to trigger a discrete `ActionEvent` when the user activates it (typically via a mouse click, keyboard mnemonic, or `Enter` key while focused).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Format):**
> *"JButton is a concrete class in the `javax.swing` package that extends `AbstractButton` and implements the `Accessible` interface. It is a lightweight Swing component that displays a label, an icon, or both, and dispatches `ActionEvent` notifications to registered `ActionListener` objects upon user activation."*

### Complete Class Hierarchy (Inheritance Chain)

```java
java.lang.Object
    └── java.awt.Component
            └── java.awt.Container
                    └── javax.swing.JComponent
                            └── javax.swing.AbstractButton
                                    └── javax.swing.JButton
```

**Implemented Interfaces (Direct/Indirect):**

- `javax.swing.SwingConstants`
- `javax.accessibility.Accessible`
- `java.awt.image.ImageObserver`
- `java.awt.ItemSelectable`
- `java.awt.MenuContainer`
- `java.io.Serializable`
- `javax.swing.ButtonModel`

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Physical Bell at a Reception Desk"

Imagine you walk into a hotel reception desk. The receptionist has a small **bell** placed on the counter. This bell is inert — it does nothing on its own.

| Real-World Element | Java Swing Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| The bell itself | `JButton` object | A visible, clickable UI element |
| The label engraved on the bell ("Service") | `setText("Service")` | Provides textual identity |
| A logo or symbol on the bell | `setIcon(icon)` | Provides graphical identity |
| The person listening for the ring | `ActionListener` | Reacts when the bell is pressed |
| The actual ringing event | `ActionEvent` | The signal/message passed to the listener |
| The action performed (calling waiter) | `actionPerformed()` method | The business logic executed |

**The Intuition:** A `JButton` is *dumb* by itself. It has no idea what should happen when clicked. You (the programmer) must **register a listener** (attach a person to listen for the ring) and **define the response** (tell them what to do when they hear it). This is the **Observer / Event-Delegation Model** that KTU examiners love to test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** A `JButton` is the **source** of the event; the `ActionListener` you register is the **subscriber**. This is the textbook **Event Delegation Model**.

---

## 1.3 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus

In Module 4 (GUI Programming with Swing / Event Handling), `JButton` is typically the **first concrete component** introduced because it is the simplest, most intuitive interactive widget, and it serves as the gateway to understanding:
1. Event Delegation Model
2. `ActionListener` interface
3. Lambda expressions (Java 8+) used as listeners
4. Anonymous inner classes
5. Method references

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 OBE, the `JButton` topic maps directly to **CO3 (Design event-driven GUI applications using Swing)** and tests cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** in Bloom's Revised Taxonomy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy — How a JButton Works Internally

When a user clicks a `JButton`, a precise sequence of events unfolds. Understanding this sequence is **critical** for both KTU theory and lab viva questions.

### Step-by-Step Internal Flow

1. **Mouse Press Event** → The AWT event queue receives a `MouseEvent` (button pressed).
2. **Component Identification** → AWT identifies the source component (the `JButton` instance).
3. **State Transition in `ButtonModel`** → The internal `DefaultButtonModel` flips its state to `ARMED` (mouse pressed over button) and later to `PRESSED`.
4. **Visual Feedback** → The UI delegate (`ButtonUI`, e.g., `BasicButtonUI`) repaints the button in its pressed appearance.
5. **Mouse Release Event** → A `MouseEvent.MOUSE_RELEASED` is fired.
6. **Action Event Generation** → If the release occurred *within* the button's bounds, the button's model calls `fireActionPerformed()`.
7. **Listener Notification** → All registered `ActionListener` objects receive the `ActionEvent` via their `actionPerformed()` method.
8. **State Reset** → The button returns to its normal appearance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** If a user clicks a button, then drags the mouse off the button before releasing, **no `ActionEvent` is fired**. This is the difference between `MOUSE_RELEASED` (raw) and the `ActionEvent` (semantic). Examiners frequently frame this as: *"Will the button's action be triggered if the user drags the mouse away?"* — **Answer: No.**

---

## 2.2 Constructors of `JButton` (Board-Favorite)

| Constructor | Signature | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| No-arg | `JButton()` | Create an empty button; set properties later |
| Text-only | `JButton(String text)` | Most common — button with a label |
| Icon-only | `JButton(Icon icon)` | Graphical button without text |
| Text + Icon | `JButton(String text, Icon icon)` | Rich button with both label and image |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write `new JButton("Submit")` and then try to add a label inside — no! The string passed to the constructor **is the button's own label**. There is no separate label component.

---

## 2.3 Inherited Methods (Key API Surface)

Since `JButton` extends `AbstractButton` and `JComponent`, the following methods form the **must-know** API:

### Methods Inherited from `AbstractButton`

| Method (using $\vert$ for clarity) | Description |
|---|---|
| `void setText(String text)` | Sets the button's label |
| `String getText()` | Returns the current label |
| `void setIcon(Icon icon)` | Sets the displayed icon |
| `Icon getIcon()` | Returns the current icon |
| `void setMnemonic(int mnemonic)` | Sets keyboard shortcut (use `KeyEvent.VK_X`) |
| `void setMnemonic(char mnemonic)` | Char-based shortcut setter |
| `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Registers an event listener |
| `void removeActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Unregisters a listener |
| `void setEnabled(boolean b)` | Enables/disables the button |
| `boolean isEnabled()` | Checks if the button is enabled |
| `void setSelected(boolean b)` | Sets toggle state (for check-box behavior) |
| `void doClick()` | Programmatically simulates a click |
| `void doClick(int pressTime)` | Simulates a click held for `pressTime` ms |
| `void setActionCommand(String cmd)` | Sets a custom command string for the event |

### Methods Inherited from `JComponent`

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `void setToolTipText(String text)` | Sets hover tooltip |
| `void setBackground(Color c)` | Sets background color (may be ignored by Look & Feel) |
| `void setForeground(Color c)` | Sets text color |
| `void setFont(Font f)` | Sets the font |
| `void setOpaque(boolean b)` | Controls transparency (relevant for L&F) |
| `void setBorder(Border b)` | Sets the border |

### `JButton`-Specific Methods

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `boolean isDefaultButton()` | Checks if it's the default button for its root pane |
| `void setDefaultCapable(boolean b)` | Sets whether the button can be the default |
| `String getUIClassID()` | Returns `"ButtonUI"` (UI delegate class name) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

`JButton` is the foundational widget in virtually every desktop application:
- **NetBeans IDE / IntelliJ IDEA** → Every toolbar action is a button.
- **Banking software** → "Submit", "Cancel", "Reset" buttons in transaction forms.
- **Industrial SCADA systems** → "Start Machine", "Emergency Stop" buttons.
- **Medical equipment UI** → Patient-record systems with action triggers.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Lab Viva:** When asked *"Why do we use `JButton` and not `Button` (AWT)?"*, the answer is:
> *"AWT `Button` is a heavyweight component (peers with the OS), giving inconsistent cross-platform appearance. `JButton` is lightweight, fully Java-rendered, pluggable Look & Feel capable, and supports accessibility, icons, mnemonics, and rich customization."*

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / API Cheat Sheet

There are no mathematical formulas, but here is the **high-density API reference** that students should memorize for the 14-mark questions:

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Event Generated | Listener Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create text button | `JButton b = new JButton("OK");` | — | — |
| Create icon button | `JButton b = new JButton(icon);` | — | — |
| Register listener | `b.addActionListener(this);` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` |
| Trigger manually | `b.doClick();` | `ActionEvent` | — |
| Disable | `b.setEnabled(false);` | — (no clicks) | — |
| Mnemonic | `b.setMnemonic('S');` | — | — |
| Default button | `getRootPane().setDefaultButton(b);` | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The "Login Form" Use Case

We will build a **complete, compilable Java Swing program** featuring a `JButton`, demonstrating every concept above. This is the model answer style expected for KTU 14-mark questions.

### Problem Statement

> *"Write a Java Swing program using `JButton` that, when the user clicks a button labeled 'Greet', displays a personalized greeting in a `JLabel` based on the name entered in a `JTextField`."*

### Step 1: Import Statements (Explanation of Each)

```java
import javax.swing.*;      // Core Swing components: JButton, JLabel, JTextField, JFrame, JPanel
import java.awt.*;          // AWT layout managers and Color class
import java.awt.event.*;    // ActionListener interface and ActionEvent class
```

> **Why each?** `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JFrame`, `JPanel` all live in `javax.swing`. `FlowLayout` and `BorderLayout` come from `java.awt`. The event classes/interfaces come from `java.awt.event`.

### Step 2: Class Declaration with Interface Implementation

```java
public class GreetApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // Step 2a: Declare the components as instance fields (NOT local variables!)
    private JLabel nameLabel, resultLabel;
    private JTextField nameField;
    private JButton greetButton;
```

**Explanation:**
- We extend `JFrame` because our application needs a top-level window.
- We `implements ActionListener` because the **same class** will handle the button click. This is one of three valid registration strategies.
- Fields are declared `private` for encapsulation (a KTU OOP-favorite concept).

### Step 3: Constructor — Building the UI

```java
    public GreetApp() {
        setTitle("Greeting Application");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));
```

**Explanation:** Sets the title, dimensions (400×200 pixels), the close behavior, and a `FlowLayout` manager that centers components horizontally with 10px gaps.

```java
        // Step 3a: Initialize components
        nameLabel   = new JLabel("Enter Name:");
        nameField   = new JTextField(15);
        greetButton = new JButton("Greet");
        resultLabel = new JLabel("Result will appear here");
```

**Explanation:** A label for instruction, a text field 15 columns wide, the action button, and a result label to display the greeting.

```java
        // Step 3b: Register the listener (this class implements ActionListener)
        greetButton.addActionListener(this);
```

**Explanation:** **The `this` keyword** is passed because the current class implements `ActionListener`. This is **Strategy 1** of listener registration. Other strategies (anonymous inner class, lambda) are discussed in Section 3.3.

```java
        // Step 3c: Set mnemonic (Alt+G as keyboard shortcut)
        greetButton.setMnemonic('G');
```

**Explanation:** When the user presses **Alt+G**, the button is activated as if clicked. The character is automatically underlined in the label if present.

```java
        // Step 3d: Add components to the frame
        add(nameLabel);
        add(nameField);
        add(greetButton);
        add(resultLabel);
```

**Explanation:** Components are added in the order they should appear (FlowLayout flows left-to-right, wrapping at the edge).

```java
        setLocationRelativeTo(null); // Center the window on screen
        setVisible(true);            // Make the window appear
    }
```

### Step 4: Override `actionPerformed` — The Event Handler

```java
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Step 4a: Identify source for robustness
        if (e.getSource() == greetButton) {
```

**Explanation:** Even though we know only one button is registered, KTU best practice is to **always verify the event source** when multiple components share one handler. This prevents logic errors.

```java
            // Step 4b: Extract the name from the text field
            String name = nameField.getText().trim();
            
            // Step 4c: Validation
            if (name.isEmpty()) {
                resultLabel.setText("Please enter a name!");
            } else {
                resultLabel.setText("Hello, " + name + "! Welcome.");
            }
```

**Explanation:**
- `getText()` retrieves the current contents; `trim()` removes leading/trailing whitespace.
- An empty check (defensive programming) sets an error message.
- The greeting is built using **string concatenation** and displayed in the `JLabel`.

```java
        }
    }
```

### Step 5: The `main` Method

```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Event Dispatch Thread — best practice for Swing apps
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new GreetApp());
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` ensures the GUI is created on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**, preventing concurrency issues. This is mandatory knowledge for advanced KTU questions.

### Complete Consolidated Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JLabel nameLabel, resultLabel;
    private JTextField nameField;
    private JButton greetButton;

    public GreetApp() {
        setTitle("Greeting Application");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));

        nameLabel   = new JLabel("Enter Name:");
        nameField   = new JTextField(15);
        greetButton = new JButton("Greet");
        resultLabel = new JLabel("Result will appear here");

        greetButton.addActionListener(this);
        greetButton.setMnemonic('G');

        add(nameLabel);
        add(nameField);
        add(greetButton);
        add(resultLabel);

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == greetButton) {
            String name = nameField.getText().trim();
            if (name.isEmpty()) {
                resultLabel.setText("Please enter a name!");
            } else {
                resultLabel.setText("Hello, " + name + "! Welcome.");
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new GreetApp());
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Three Listener-Registration Strategies

KTU examiners **frequently** ask: *"Explain the different ways to register an `ActionListener` with a `JButton`."* Here is the exhaustive matrix:

| Strategy | Code Pattern | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Implement in the same class** | `class MyFrame extends JFrame implements ActionListener { ... button.addActionListener(this); }` | Centralized handler; easy to share logic across components | Class must implement interface; can grow large | When many components share the same handler |
| **2. Anonymous Inner Class** | `button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { ... } });` | Self-contained; handler logic stays next to creation | Verbose; creates a new class file per button | When each button has unique logic |
| **3. Lambda Expression (Java 8+)** | `button.addActionListener(e -> { ... });` | Most concise; modern best practice | Requires Java 8+; cannot be used with anonymous class state | Default choice in modern code |

### Strategy 1 Code (Reference)

```java
public class Strategy1 extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JButton b = new JButton("Click");
    public Strategy1() {
        b.addActionListener(this);
        add(b); setSize(200, 200); setVisible(true);
    }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked via Strategy 1");
    }
}
```

### Strategy 2 Code (Reference)

```java
JButton b = new JButton("Click");
b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked via Strategy 2 (Anonymous)");
    }
});
```

### Strategy 3 Code (Reference)

```java
JButton b = new JButton("Click");
b.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Clicked via Strategy 3 (Lambda)"));
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda Eligibility Rule:** The lambda form works only because `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** — it has *exactly one abstract method* (`actionPerformed`). The compiler treats the lambda as an instance of `ActionListener`. This is a KTU-favorite conceptual question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Event Flow (Logical Steps)

Let us **derive** the event propagation chain when a `JButton` is clicked:

1. **User clicks the button** at coordinates $(x, y)$ on the screen.
2. The OS dispatches a native `MouseEvent` to the JVM via JNI.
3. The JVM's event queue stores the event in `java.awt.EventQueue`.
4. The EDT picks the event and calls `Component.processMouseEvent()` on the topmost component at $(x, y)$.
5. The `JButton`'s UI delegate (`BasicButtonUI`) handles the visual repaint.
6. The `ButtonModel` state changes: `armed = true`, `pressed = true`, then on release, `pressed = false`.
7. If the release is inside the button's bounds, `ButtonModel.fireActionPerformed()` is invoked.
8. This loops over the listener list and calls `actionPerformed(e)` on each.
9. The handler method executes the user-defined logic.
10. The EDT returns to waiting for the next event.

> [!NOTE]
> This 10-step chain is a high-yield answer for the question: *"Explain the event-delegation model with reference to a `JButton` click."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — JButton Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java lang Object"] --> B["java awt Component"]
    B --> C["java awt Container"]
    C --> D["javax swing JComponent"]
    D --> E["javax swing AbstractButton"]
    E --> F["javax swing JButton"]
    F --> F1["TEXT button"]
    F --> F2["ICON button"]
    F --> F3["TEXT plus ICON button"]
    E --> E1["JToggleButton"]
    E --> E2["JCheckBox"]
    E --> E3["JRadioButton"]
    E --> E4["JMenuItem"]
    D --> D1["JLabel"]
    D --> D2["JPanel"]
    D --> D3["JTextField"]
    style F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Reading Guide:** The gold node is our focus — `JButton`. Its parent `AbstractButton` (blue) provides 90% of its functionality. Sibling classes (`JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, etc.) share the same parent, demonstrating **code reuse via inheritance** — a classic OOP concept tested alongside Swing.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Event Delegation Model Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    U["USER"] -->|1 mouse click| EB["EVENT QUEUE"]
    EB -->|2 dispatch| BTN["JButton instance"]
    BTN -->|3 update model| BM["DefaultButtonModel"]
    BM -->|4 fireActionPerformed| AL["ActionListener list"]
    AL -->|5 invoke actionPerformed| H["HANDLER METHOD"]
    H -->|6 execute logic| R["APPLICATION RESPONSE"]
    R -.->|7 visual update| U
    style BTN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AL fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **Observer Pattern in action**. The `JButton` (subject) maintains a list of `ActionListener`s (observers) and notifies them when the state changes (click event). This pattern is the heart of all Swing event handling.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete JButton Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SRC["EVENT SOURCE"]
        JBTN["JButton object"]
        BMODEL["ButtonModel"]
        JUI["ButtonUI delegate"]
    end
    subgraph EVT["EVENT OBJECT"]
        AEVT["ActionEvent"]
    end
    subgraph LST["EVENT LISTENER"]
        IFACE["ActionListener interface"]
        IMPL1["Strategy 1: implements in main class"]
        IMPL2["Strategy 2: anonymous inner class"]
        IMPL3["Strategy 3: lambda expression"]
    end
    JBTN --> BMODEL
    JBTN --> JUI
    BMODEL -->|fires| AEVT
    AEVT -->|passed to| IFACE
    IFACE -.implemented by.-> IMPL1
    IFACE -.implemented by.-> IMPL2
    IFACE -.implemented by.-> IMPL3
    style JBTN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AEVT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style IFACE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **Source** generates the **Event**, which is delivered to a registered **Listener** that has implemented the **ActionListener** interface. All three implementation strategies ultimately satisfy the same interface contract.

---

## 4.4 Visual Layout — The Login Form GUI

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph WIN["JFrame: Greeting Application 400x200"]
        L1["JLabel: Enter Name"]
        TF["JTextField (15 cols)"]
        BTN["JButton Greet (Alt+G)"]
        L2["JLabel: Result will appear here"]
    end
    BTN -->|click event| LOGIC["actionPerformed handler"]
    LOGIC -->|reads| TF
    LOGIC -->|updates| L2
    style BTN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style LOGIC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a block-level functional map showing how the `JButton` acts as the **trigger** that causes data flow between the text field (input) and the result label (output) via the event handler.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define `JButton`. List any two methods of `JButton` class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> `JButton` is a concrete class in the `javax.swing` package that extends `AbstractButton`. It is a lightweight Swing component used to create push buttons that trigger `ActionEvent` notifications when activated.
>
> **Two methods:**
> 1. `void setText(String text)` — Sets the button's label.
> 2. `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` — Registers an event listener for click events.
>
> *(Alternatively: `setIcon()`, `setMnemonic()`, `setEnabled()`, `doClick()`)*

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Two methods with purpose: 2 marks (1 each)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the role of the `ActionListener` interface with respect to `JButton`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The `ActionListener` interface (from `java.awt.event`) contains a single abstract method `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`. When a `JButton` is clicked, it dispatches an `ActionEvent` to every registered `ActionListener`. The listener's `actionPerformed()` method contains the business logic that executes in response to the click. The `JButton` itself does not define any action; it merely **delegates** the event to its listeners — hence the name *Event Delegation Model*.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Interface location & method signature: 1 mark
- Event dispatch mechanism: 1 mark
- Event Delegation Model concept: 1 mark

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the inheritance hierarchy of `JButton` in Java Swing. List any four constructors of `JButton` with their signatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` containing two `JButton`s labeled "Red" and "Blue". When the user clicks a button, the background color of the `JFrame` should change accordingly. Use the **anonymous inner class** approach for event handling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Inheritance Hierarchy of `JButton`:**

```java
java.lang.Object
   ↓ extends
java.awt.Component
   ↓ extends
java.awt.Container
   ↓ extends
javax.swing.JComponent
   ↓ extends
javax.swing.AbstractButton
   ↓ extends
javax.swing.JButton
```

`JButton` also implements the `Accessible` interface and indirectly inherits from `SwingConstants` and `ImageObserver`.

**Four Constructors:**

| # | Constructor | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `JButton()` | Creates a button with no text or icon |
| 2 | `JButton(String text)` | Creates a button with the given label |
| 3 | `JButton(Icon icon)` | Creates a button with the given icon |
| 4 | `JButton(String text, Icon icon)` | Creates a button with both label and icon |

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Hierarchy diagram: 3 marks
- Four constructors with signatures & purpose: 4 marks (1 each)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ColorChangerApp extends JFrame {
    private JButton redButton, blueButton;

    public ColorChangerApp() {
        setTitle("Color Changer");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 20));

        redButton  = new JButton("Red");
        blueButton = new JButton("Blue");

        // Anonymous inner class for Red button
        redButton.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                getContentPane().setBackground(Color.RED);
            }
        });

        // Anonymous inner class for Blue button
        blueButton.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                getContentPane().setBackground(Color.BLUE);
            }
        });

        add(redButton);
        add(blueButton);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ColorChangerApp());
    }
}
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Correct class declaration & imports: 1 mark
- UI component creation & layout setup: 2 marks
- Two anonymous inner class listeners with correct logic: 3 marks
- `main` method with EDT invocation: 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** What is the Event Delegation Model? How is it implemented in `JButton`? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to design a simple calculator UI (only the interface, no computation logic) using `JButton`s for digits 0–9, operators +, −, *, /, and a `JTextField` for display. Use **lambda expressions** for at least one event handler. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Event Delegation Model:**

The Event Delegation Model is a Java AWT/Swing architecture where:
1. **Event Source** — The component that generates an event (e.g., `JButton`).
2. **Event Object** — Encapsulates information about the event (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener** — An object that implements a listener interface and handles the event.

In this model, the source does **not** process the event itself. Instead, it maintains a list of registered listeners and **delegates** the event to them.

**Implementation in `JButton`:**

- `JButton` (the source) internally holds an `EventListener` list for `ActionEvent`.
- When the user clicks, the button calls `fireActionPerformed()` on its `ButtonModel`.
- This method iterates over the list of `ActionListener` objects and calls `actionPerformed(e)` on each.
- The programmer registers a listener via `addActionListener()` and implements the `actionPerformed()` method with the desired response.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Definition & three components of model: 3 marks
- Source–Event–Listener flow in JButton: 2 marks
- Specific method names (`addActionListener`, `fireActionPerformed`, `actionPerformed`): 2 marks

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Calculator UI Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class CalculatorUI extends JFrame {
    private JTextField display;
    private JButton[] digitButtons;
    private JButton btnAdd, btnSub, btnMul, btnDiv, btnEquals, btnClear;

    public CalculatorUI() {
        setTitle("Simple Calculator");
        setSize(300, 400);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));

        // Display
        display = new JTextField();
        display.setEditable(false);
        display.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.RIGHT);
        add(display, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // Center panel for buttons
        JPanel buttonPanel = new JPanel();
        buttonPanel.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));

        // Digit buttons 0-9
        digitButtons = new JButton[10];
        for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
            digitButtons[i] = new JButton(String.valueOf(i));
            buttonPanel.add(digitButtons[i]);
        }

        // Operator buttons
        btnAdd    = new JButton("+");
        btnSub    = new JButton("-");
        btnMul    = new JButton("*");
        btnDiv    = new JButton("/");
        btnEquals = new JButton("=");
        btnClear  = new JButton("C");

        // Lambda expression for the Clear button
        btnClear.addActionListener(e -> display.setText(""));

        // Lambda for Equals (placeholder logic)
        btnEquals.addActionListener(e -> display.setText(display.getText() + " = ?"));

        buttonPanel.add(btnAdd);
        buttonPanel.add(btnSub);
        buttonPanel.add(btnMul);
        buttonPanel.add(btnDiv);
        buttonPanel.add(btnEquals);
        buttonPanel.add(btnClear);

        add(buttonPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new CalculatorUI());
    }
}
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Correct frame, layout, and display field: 2 marks
- Digit buttons (0–9) created and added: 2 marks
- Operator buttons created: 1 mark
- At least one lambda-based listener: 1 mark
- `main` method & EDT: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `getContentPane().setBackground()`** — Setting background directly on a `JFrame` (top-level container) often fails; the content pane is the correct target. **Lose 1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Calling `addActionListener` on the wrong component** — Students sometimes register a listener on a `JLabel`. **Lose 2 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Not overriding `actionPerformed` correctly** — Forgetting the `@Override` annotation or mismatching the signature (`public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`). **Lose 2 marks** for signature errors.
> 4. **Thread-safety neglect** — Not using `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` in `main`. **Lose 1 mark** if the question explicitly mentions "best practice."
> 5. **Confusing `JButton` with `Button`** — AWT's `Button` does not support icons, mnemonics, or Look & Feel. **Lose 2 marks** for mixing these up in theory.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Package:** `javax.swing` ✓
- **Direct superclass:** `javax.swing.AbstractButton` ✓
- **Constructor count:** **4 official constructors** (no-arg, text, icon, text+icon) ✓
- **Event type generated:** `java.awt.event.ActionEvent` ✓
- **Listener interface:** `java.awt.event.ActionListener` (functional interface — 1 method: `actionPerformed`) ✓
- **Registration method:** `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` ✓
- **Mnemonic setter:** `setMnemonic(int)` or `setMnemonic(char)` — activates via **Alt + key** ✓
- **Manual click trigger:** `doClick()` ✓
- **Inheritance chain:** `Object → Component → Container → JComponent → AbstractButton → JButton` ✓
- **Default button concept:** A `JButton` can be the default button of its root pane (activated by **Enter** in a dialog) — use `getRootPane().setDefaultButton(b)` ✓
- **Three listener strategies:** (1) `implements` in same class, (2) anonymous inner class, (3) lambda ✓
- **Lambda eligibility:** Works because `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** ✓
- **EDT rule:** Always construct Swing GUIs on the **Event Dispatch Thread** using `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` ✓
- **Lightweight vs Heavyweight:** `JButton` is **lightweight** (pure Java, no OS peer) — unlike AWT's `Button` ✓
- **Pluggable Look & Feel:** `JButton` supports `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` for cross-platform theming ✓
- **Accessibility:** Implements `Accessible` for screen readers and assistive technologies ✓
- **Drag-off behavior:** If the user clicks then drags off the button before release, **no `ActionEvent`** is generated ✓
- **Companion interfaces inherited:** `SwingConstants`, `ImageObserver`, `MenuContainer`, `Serializable` ✓

> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always include a **diagram** (inheritance hierarchy or event flow), a **complete compilable program**, and **inline comments**. KTU examiners reward well-structured, commented code with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JTextField. Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Architecture
Creating and Executing Queries - Create Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating and Executing Queries - Create Table

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Structured Query Language (SQL)**, the `CREATE TABLE` statement is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** command used to define a new table within a relational database management system (RDBMS). It establishes the **schema** of a table by specifying:

1. The **table name** (a unique identifier within a schema/database).
2. A list of **columns**, each with a data type and optional constraints.
3. **Table-level constraints** that govern relationships and integrity rules across columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEET419 - Module 4):**
> *"The `CREATE TABLE` statement is a DDL command that defines the structure (schema) of a relation by assigning a name, a set of attributes (columns), data types, and integrity constraints. Once executed, it allocates physical storage metadata within the database catalog."*

### Syntax (ANSI SQL Standard)

$$ \text{CREATE TABLE } \langle \textit{TableName} \rangle \text{ (} \langle \textit{ColDefs} \rangle \text{, } \langle \textit{TableConstraints} \rangle \text{);} $$

Where:
- $\langle \textit{TableName} \rangle$ → Name of the new table.
- $\langle \textit{ColDefs} \rangle$ → Comma-separated list of column definitions.
- $\langle \textit{TableConstraints} \rangle$ → Optional constraints like `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `CHECK`, etc.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architectural Blueprint"

Imagine you are an **architect designing a new building**:

| Real-World Concept | SQL Equivalent | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Building Plan / Blueprint** | `CREATE TABLE` statement | The master design document. |
| **Building Name** (e.g., "Lakshmi Towers") | Table Name (`Employees`) | Unique identifier in the city. |
| **Rooms** (Bedroom, Kitchen, Hall) | Columns (`emp_id`, `name`, `salary`) | Each serves a unique purpose. |
| **Room Dimensions** (10ft x 12ft) | Data Type (`VARCHAR(50)`, `INT`) | Defines what kind of value fits. |
| **Building Rules** ("No smoking", "Max 10 floors") | Constraints (`NOT NULL`, `CHECK`) | Enforces the data integrity rules. |
| **Municipal Approval** | DBMS Engine Commit | Final acceptance and storage. |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a blueprint must be approved *before* construction begins, a table must be successfully defined *before* any data (`INSERT`) can be stored in it. If the `CREATE TABLE` command fails, no data can be added.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

- **DDL (Data Definition Language):** The subset of SQL used to define, alter, and drop database objects. Commands include `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`.
- **Schema:** The structural blueprint of the entire database (all tables, views, indexes).
- **Data Type:** A classification that specifies what type of value a column can hold.
- **Constraint:** A rule enforced on data columns to maintain **ACID** properties.
- **Catalog / Data Dictionary:** The internal system tables that store metadata about all defined objects.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the `CREATE TABLE` Statement

The command is logically divided into **three operational blocks**:

### Block A — The Header
$$\text{CREATE TABLE [IF NOT EXISTS] } \langle \textit{TableName} \rangle$$
- `IF NOT EXISTS` (MySQL/PostgreSQL extension): Prevents an error if the table already exists. **Highly recommended for KTU practical exams.**

### Block B — Column Definitions
Each column follows the pattern:
$$ \langle \textit{column\_name} \rangle \; \langle \textit{data\_type} \rangle \; [\langle \textit{column\_constraint} \rangle \ldots] $$

### Block C — Table-Level Constraints
Applied after column definitions, useful for **composite keys** (keys formed by combining two or more columns) and **foreign keys** that reference multiple columns.
$$ \text{PRIMARY KEY (col1, col2), FOREIGN KEY (col1) REFERENCES Parent(col1)} $$

## 2.2 Core SQL Data Types (Standard & MySQL Variant)

| Category | ANSI / MySQL Type | Memory | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integer** | `INT`, `SMALLINT`, `BIGINT` | 4 / 2 / 8 bytes | IDs, counts, age |
| **Decimal** | `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `FLOAT`, `DOUBLE` | Variable | Money, scientific values |
| **Character** | `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)` | Fixed / Variable | Names, codes, emails |
| **Date/Time** | `DATE`, `TIME`, `DATETIME`, `TIMESTAMP` | 3–8 bytes | DOB, log timestamps |
| **Boolean** | `BOOLEAN` (alias of `TINYINT(1)`) | 1 byte | Flags, status |
| **Binary Large Object (BLOB)** | `BLOB`, `LONGBLOB` | Up to 4 GB | Images, PDFs |
| **Text** | `TEXT`, `LONGTEXT` | Up to 4 GB | Articles, descriptions |

## 2.3 The Six Integrity Constraints (Most Important for KTU)

| Constraint | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **`NOT NULL`** | Column cannot be empty | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |
| **`UNIQUE`** | No duplicate values allowed | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| **`PRIMARY KEY`** | Unique + Not Null; identifies each row | `emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| **`FOREIGN KEY`** | Links to another table's PK | `dept_id INT REFERENCES Dept(id)` |
| **`CHECK`** | Enforces a value condition | `salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK(salary > 0)` |
| **`DEFAULT`** | Assigns a default value | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` |

## 2.4 `CREATE TABLE` Execution Contexts in KTU Syllabus

The OOP course treats this topic from a **Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)** perspective. Students must know how to execute DDL from Java.

### The 5-Step JDBC Architecture

| Step | Class / Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Load Driver** | `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver")` | Registers the JDBC driver class |
| **2. Establish Connection** | `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd)` | Opens a session with the DB |
| **3. Create Statement** | `con.createStatement()` | Returns a `Statement` object |
| **4. Execute Query** | `stmt.executeUpdate(query)` | Runs DDL/DML; returns row count |
| **5. Close Connection** | `con.close()` | Releases DB resources (mandatory) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `executeUpdate()` for `CREATE TABLE`?**
> The `executeUpdate()` method is used for **DDL** (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`) and **DML** (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) statements. It returns an `int` representing the number of rows affected. For DDL like `CREATE TABLE`, it returns **0**.
> In contrast, `executeQuery()` is used only for `SELECT` and returns a `ResultSet`.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

`CREATE TABLE` is the **foundational operation** in:
- **Banking Systems** (Account, Transaction, Customer tables).
- **E-Commerce** (Product, Order, Cart, User).
- **IoT & Sensor Data Logging** (Device, Reading, Timestamp).
- **Library Management** (Book, Member, Issue, Author).

Without a properly constrained table, downstream operations (`INSERT`, `JOIN`, `GROUP BY`) will suffer from **data anomalies** (insertion, update, deletion anomalies — common in unnormalized databases).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code Construction

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Model KTU Practical)

> *"Design a database schema for a University Management System. Create a table named `STUDENT` to store the following information: Registration Number, Full Name, Date of Birth, Branch, CGPA, and Email. The RegNo must be the primary key. The Email must be unique. The CGPA must be between 0 and 10. The Branch should default to 'CSE'."*

## 3.2 Step-by-Step SQL Construction

### Step 1 — Identify the Columns and Data Types

| Logical Attribute | SQL Column | Data Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Registration Number | `regno` | `INT` | Numeric identifier |
| Full Name | `name` | `VARCHAR(100)` | Variable length text |
| Date of Birth | `dob` | `DATE` | Standard date format |
| Branch | `branch` | `VARCHAR(20)` | Short string |
| CGPA | `cgpa` | `DECIMAL(4,2)` | 2 decimal places (e.g., 9.85) |
| Email | `email` | `VARCHAR(100)` | Variable text |

### Step 2 — Identify the Constraints

| Constraint Type | Target Column(s) | Rule |
|---|---|---|
| `PRIMARY KEY` | `regno` | Uniquely identifies a student |
| `UNIQUE` | `email` | Prevents duplicate emails |
| `NOT NULL` | `name`, `dob` | These fields are mandatory |
| `CHECK` | `cgpa` | Must be $\geq 0$ AND $\leq 10$ |
| `DEFAULT` | `branch` | Defaults to `'CSE'` |

### Step 3 — Compose the Final DDL Statement

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS STUDENT (
    regno      INT             NOT NULL,
    name       VARCHAR(100)    NOT NULL,
    dob        DATE            NOT NULL,
    branch     VARCHAR(20)     DEFAULT 'CSE',
    cgpa       DECIMAL(4,2)    CHECK (cgpa >= 0 AND cgpa <= 10),
    email      VARCHAR(100)    UNIQUE,
    CONSTRAINT pk_student PRIMARY KEY (regno)
);
```

**Explanation of every line (Board-Exam Style):**

- **`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS STUDENT`** → Initiates DDL; checks existence to avoid runtime error.
- **`regno INT NOT NULL`** → Integer column; cannot be empty.
- **`name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL`** → Up to 100 characters; mandatory.
- **`dob DATE NOT NULL`** → Stores date in `YYYY-MM-DD` format.
- **`branch VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'CSE'`** → If branch is not provided, it becomes `'CSE'`.
- **`cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa >= 0 AND cgpa <= 10)`** → Total 4 digits with 2 decimals (e.g., 9.99). The `CHECK` enforces the 0–10 range.
- **`email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE`** → Ensures no two students share the same email.
- **`CONSTRAINT pk_student PRIMARY KEY (regno)`** → Named table-level constraint. Defines `regno` as the **Primary Key** (entity integrity).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step JDBC Execution (Java Code)

The OOP syllabus requires executing this `CREATE TABLE` from a **Java program** using JDBC.

### Step 1 — Add the MySQL Connector to the Project
The MySQL Connector/J is the official JDBC driver that enables Java to communicate with MySQL databases.
> *In NetBeans: Right-click Libraries → Add JAR/Folder → `mysql-connector-j-8.x.x.jar`*

### Step 2 — The Complete Java Program

```java
import java.sql.Connection;
import java.sql.DriverManager;
import java.sql.Statement;

public class CreateStudentTable {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // ---- STEP 1: Define connection parameters ----
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/universityDB";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "password123";

        // ---- STEP 2: Define the DDL query ----
        String query = 
            "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS STUDENT (" +
            " regno  INT          NOT NULL, " +
            " name   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, " +
            " dob    DATE         NOT NULL, " +
            " branch VARCHAR(20)  DEFAULT 'CSE', " +
            " cgpa   DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa >= 0 AND cgpa <= 10), " +
            " email  VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, " +
            " CONSTRAINT pk_student PRIMARY KEY (regno))";

        // ---- STEP 3: Use try-with-resources for auto-close ----
        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
             Statement stmt = con.createStatement()) {

            // ---- STEP 4: Execute the DDL command ----
            int result = stmt.executeUpdate(query);
            System.out.println("CREATE TABLE executed. Rows affected: " + result);

        } catch (Exception e) {
            // ---- STEP 5: Handle SQL exceptions ----
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

### Line-by-Line Analytical Breakdown

| Line | Explanation |
|---|---|
| `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)` | Establishes a session to the local MySQL server on port **3306**, database `universityDB`. |
| `con.createStatement()` | Creates a `Statement` object to send static SQL to the DB. |
| `stmt.executeUpdate(query)` | Executes the `CREATE TABLE`. Returns `0` (DDL produces no row count). |
| `try (...)` | Auto-closes `Connection` and `Statement` after the block. Prevents resource leaks. |
| `catch (Exception e)` | Catches `ClassNotFoundException` (driver) and `SQLException` (DB errors). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students confuse `executeUpdate()` and `executeQuery()`. For `CREATE TABLE`, **only `executeUpdate()` is correct**. Using `executeQuery()` will throw an `SQLException` because DDL does not return a `ResultSet`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Flow: From SQL DDL to Physical Table

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User writes CREATE TABLE SQL] --> B[DBMS Parser]
    B --> C{Syntax Valid?}
    C -->|No| D[Returns SQL Syntax Error]
    C -->|Yes| E[Check System Catalog]
    E --> F{Table Exists?}
    F -->|Yes and IF NOT EXISTS used| G[Skip Execution]
    F -->|Yes without IF NOT EXISTS| H[Returns Table Already Exists Error]
    F -->|No| I[Allocate Storage Metadata in Data Dictionary]
    I --> J[Create Empty .ibd File or Tablespace]
    J --> K[Register Columns, Types, Constraints]
    K --> L[Commit to System Catalog]
    L --> M[Empty Table Ready for INSERT Operations]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual ER Mapping of the `STUDENT` Table

```mermaid
erDiagram
    STUDENT {
        INT regno PK
        VARCHAR name
        DATE dob
        VARCHAR branch
        DECIMAL cgpa
        VARCHAR email
    }
    DEPARTMENT {
        INT dept_id PK
        VARCHAR dept_name
    }
    STUDENT ||--o{ DEPARTMENT : "belongs to"
```

> [!NOTE]
> This ER diagram visually confirms that `STUDENT` is the entity and `regno` (Primary Key) is its unique identifier. The `branch` attribute would ideally be replaced with a `dept_id FOREIGN KEY` referencing the `DEPARTMENT` table in a normalized design.

## 4.3 JDBC Execution Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Java Application Main Method] --> B[Class.forName Driver Load]
    B --> C[DriverManager.getConnection]
    C --> D[Connection Object]
    D --> E[Connection.createStatement]
    E --> F[Statement Object]
    F --> G[Statement.executeUpdate CREATE TABLE Query]
    G --> H[MySQL Server Executes DDL]
    H --> I[Returns int 0 to Java]
    I --> J[Connection.close]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the `CREATE TABLE` command in SQL. List any four common constraints that can be applied to a table during its creation.**

**Model Answer:**

The `CREATE TABLE` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** statement in SQL used to define a new table and its structure within a relational database. It specifies the table's name, the list of columns with their data types, and the constraints that govern data integrity.

**Four common constraints:**

1. **`PRIMARY KEY`** – Uniquely identifies each row in the table (NOT NULL + UNIQUE).
2. **`FOREIGN KEY`** – Establishes a referential link to the Primary Key of another table.
3. **`NOT NULL`** – Ensures the column cannot contain NULL (empty) values.
4. **`CHECK`** – Enforces a specific condition on the values in a column (e.g., `salary > 0`).
5. *(Alternative: `UNIQUE`, `DEFAULT`)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between `executeQuery()` and `executeUpdate()` methods in JDBC? When is each used?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `executeQuery()` | `executeUpdate()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Return Type** | `ResultSet` | `int` (row count) |
| **Used For** | `SELECT` statements (reads data) | DDL (`CREATE`, `DROP`, `ALTER`) and DML (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) |
| **Output** | Tabular data | Number of rows affected |
| **Example** | `ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Student");` | `int n = stmt.executeUpdate("CREATE TABLE ...");` |

For executing a `CREATE TABLE` query, `executeUpdate()` must be used.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the various SQL data types and constraints with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a Java program using JDBC to create a table `EMPLOYEE` with columns: `emp_id` (INT, Primary Key), `ename` (VARCHAR, NOT NULL), `salary` (DECIMAL, must be ≥ 15000), and `joining_date` (DATE, default '2024-01-01'). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Data Types and Constraints:**

**Data Types:** SQL supports several categories of data types:
- **Numeric:** `INT`, `BIGINT`, `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `FLOAT` — used for numbers.
- **Character:** `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)` — used for text strings.
- **Date/Time:** `DATE`, `DATETIME`, `TIMESTAMP` — used for dates and times.
- **Boolean:** `BOOLEAN` — used for true/false flags.
- **BLOB:** `BLOB` — used for binary large objects like images.

**Example:** `salary DECIMAL(10,2)` allows values up to 99,999,999.99.

**Constraints:**
- `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE`, `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `CHECK`, `DEFAULT`.

**Example:**
```sql
emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
ename  VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary >= 15000)
```

**(b) Java JDBC Program:**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class CreateEmployeeTable {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/companyDB";
        String user = "root";
        String pwd  = "root";

        String ddl = "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS EMPLOYEE ("
                   + "emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, "
                   + "ename VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, "
                   + "salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary >= 15000), "
                   + "joining_date DATE DEFAULT '2024-01-01')";

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd);
             Statement stmt = con.createStatement()) {
            stmt.executeUpdate(ddl);
            System.out.println("EMPLOYEE table created successfully.");
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Differentiate between DDL, DML, and DCL commands in SQL with examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design and create a `LIBRARY` table with the following specifications: `book_id` (INT, PK), `title` (VARCHAR, NOT NULL), `author` (VARCHAR), `price` (DECIMAL, must be > 0), and `available` (BOOLEAN, default TRUE). Write the JDBC code. **(7 Marks)**
