#### Model Solution

**(a) DDL vs DML vs DCL:**

| Category | Full Form | Purpose | Commands |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines database structure | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manipulates data | `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Controls access/permissions | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |

**Example:** `CREATE TABLE Book (id INT);` is DDL. `INSERT INTO Book VALUES (1);` is DML. `GRANT SELECT ON Book TO user1;` is DCL.

**(b) SQL and JDBC Code:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS LIBRARY (
    book_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    title      VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    author     VARCHAR(100),
    price      DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (price > 0),
    available  BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);
```

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class CreateLibraryTable {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
        Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
            "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/libDB", "root", "root");
        Statement st = con.createStatement();
        st.executeUpdate(
            "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS LIBRARY (" +
            "book_id INT PRIMARY KEY, " +
            "title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, " +
            "author VARCHAR(100), " +
            "price DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (price > 0), " +
            "available BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE)");
        System.out.println("LIBRARY table created.");
        con.close();
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using `executeQuery()` for DDL** → Will throw `SQLException`. Always use `executeUpdate()`.
> 2. **Forgetting to load the driver** in older Java versions (`Class.forName(...)`). Not required in Java 6+ with auto-loading, but expected in KTU answers.
> 3. **Not closing the Connection** → Causes memory leaks and locked DB sessions.
> 4. **Forgetting `NOT NULL` on Primary Key** → Technically valid, but writing it explicitly is best practice and fetches full marks.
> 5. **Wrong JDBC URL format** → Must be `jdbc:mysql://host:port/dbname`. Mistakes here cost 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`CREATE TABLE`** is a **DDL** command that defines a new table's schema.
- It must specify **column names**, **data types**, and may include **constraints**.
- The **6 key constraints**: `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE`, `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `CHECK`, `DEFAULT`.
- The **PRIMARY KEY** uniquely identifies each row; it is implicitly `NOT NULL` and `UNIQUE`.
- In **JDBC**, use `executeUpdate()` for DDL — not `executeQuery()`.
- The **5-step JDBC process**: Load Driver → Connect → Create Statement → Execute → Close.
- Use **try-with-resources** in modern Java to auto-close DB connections.
- Common data types to remember: `INT`, `VARCHAR(n)`, `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `DATE`, `BOOLEAN`.
- `IF NOT EXISTS` is a best-practice safety net to prevent duplicate table errors.
- A `CREATE TABLE` executed successfully returns **`0`** from `executeUpdate()`.
- Always handle `SQLException` — DB failures (wrong credentials, network, syntax) are common.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Object Oriented Programming, the **"Delete"** operation is the runtime mechanism responsible for the **deallocation of dynamically allocated memory** that was previously reserved on the **heap** (free store) using the `new` operator (C++) or via object instantiation references (Java/Python). It is the symmetrical counterpart of object construction and is intrinsically tied to the **Object Lifecycle** and the **RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization)** principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Delete* is an operator (in C++) or a managed runtime process (in Java) that releases heap memory occupied by an object/array, invokes the destructor (C++), and makes the memory region available for re-allocation, thereby preventing **memory leaks** and **dangling pointer** hazards.

| Language | Mechanism | Keyword |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| C++ | Manual Operator | `delete ptr;` / `delete[] arr;` |
| Java | Automatic Garbage Collector (GC) | `obj = null;` (eligibility trigger) |
| Python | Automatic Garbage Collector (Reference Counting + Cycle Detector) | `del obj` / scope exit |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Hotel Room Analogy" – Plain English Explanation**

Imagine you walk into a hotel (`Heap Memory`):

1. **Check-in (`new`)** — You request a room. The receptionist gives you a **key (pointer/reference)** and allocates Room #**407** for you.
2. **Stay (Object Usage)** — You live in the room, use its facilities, store your luggage.
3. **Check-out (`delete`)** — You return the key at the front desk. The room is **cleaned, inspected, and made available** to the next guest.

If you **forget to check out** (memory leak), the room stays locked forever, and the hotel runs out of space. If you try to **check out a room that was never yours** (double delete), the hotel management crashes. If you **return the key but someone else has a copy** (dangling pointer), they walk into your old, uninitialized room — undefined behavior!

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Default Heap Alignment:** **8 bytes** (on 64-bit KTU lab systems)
> - **Typical Page Size:** **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**
> - **Destructor Overhead (C++):** **~1 vtable pointer (8 bytes)** per polymorphic class
> - **Garbage Collection Pause (Java G1GC):** Typically **10–200 ms**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – How `delete` Works (C++)

When the C++ compiler encounters `delete ptr;`, it executes **two strict sequential operations**:

1. **Destructor Invocation** — Calls the destructor of the object pointed to by `ptr` (if it is a class type). For polymorphic base classes, the destructor **must be `virtual`**; otherwise, only the base sub-object is destroyed.
2. **Memory Deallocation** — Invokes the underlying `operator delete()` (which typically calls the C library function `free()`) to return the memory chunk to the heap manager.

### The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why a Destructor Call?** Because C++ manages **non-memory resources** (file handles, sockets, mutexes) which `free()` cannot release. The destructor is the *only* guaranteed place to run cleanup code.
- **Why Virtual Destructors?** Without `virtual`, the base class destructor runs, but **derived-class-specific cleanup is skipped** → resource leak in the derived subobject.
- **Why `delete[]` for arrays?** The `new[]` allocator stores an **implicit cookie** (element count) just before the first element. `delete[]` reads this cookie to invoke N destructors. Using plain `delete` on an array skips the cookie → **undefined behavior**.

---

## 2.2 Java's "Delete" – Garbage Collection (GC)

Java has **no explicit `delete` keyword**. An object becomes eligible for deletion (garbage collection) when:

- All **strong references** to it are removed (e.g., `obj = null;` or scope ends).
- It is no longer reachable from any **GC Root** (stack variables, static fields, JNI references).

Common GC Algorithms in KTU syllabus:

| Algorithm | Strategy | Stop-The-World Pause |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Mark-Sweep** | Traverse object graph, reclaim unmarked | High |
| **Mark-Compact** | Mark + slide live objects to reduce fragmentation | Medium |
| **Copying** (Young Gen) | Divide heap into 2 survivor spaces | Low |
| **Generational** (HotSpot default) | Young Gen + Old Gen separation | Optimized |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas, equations, and rules for memory accounting and delete-related problem solving in KTU examinations.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit** |
|-------------|--------------------|----------|
| Total Heap Used | $\text{Mem}_{\text{used}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{sizeof}(O_i) + N \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{cookie})$ | bytes |
| Memory Leak | $\text{Leak}(t) = \text{Alloc}(t) - \text{Free}(t)$ | bytes |
| Fragmentation Ratio | $F = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Largest Free Block}}{\text{Total Free Space}}$ | dimensionless |
| Reference Count (Python) | $\text{RC}_{obj} = \sum \text{strong references}$ | count |
| Object Eligibility (Java) | $\text{Reachable}(o) \iff \exists \text{ path from GC Root to } o$ | boolean |
| Array `new[]` Cookie | $\text{Cookie Size} = \text{sizeof}(\text{size\_t})$ | **8 bytes** (64-bit) |
| Destructor Call Count | `delete[]` invokes exactly **N** destructors for N elements | — |
| Page Size (Heap Granularity) | $\text{Page} = 2^{12} = 4096$ | bytes |

> **Critical Rule (vertical bars written with `\mid` to preserve table integrity):**
> - Valid pointer check: $p \neq \text{nullptr} \iff \vert p \vert > 0$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** Explicit `delete` in C++ is mandatory — devices have **< 256 KB RAM** and cannot afford Java-style GC pauses.
- **High-Frequency Trading (HFT):** Custom memory pools + manual `delete` avoid GC-induced jitter (microsecond-critical).
- **Android App Development:** Java/Kotlin GC is acceptable because user perception threshold is **> 100 ms**.
- **Game Engines (Unreal, written in C++):** Aggressive `delete` patterns + smart pointers prevent frame-time spikes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Memory Accounting for a Dynamic Object

### Problem Statement (Model KTU Numerical)

Consider the following C++ snippet executed on a 64-bit system where `sizeof(int) = 4` bytes, `sizeof(double) = 8` bytes, and pointer size is **8 bytes**. A class `Student` is defined as:

```cpp
class Student {
    int id;            // 4 bytes
    double cgpa;       // 8 bytes
    char name[20];     // 20 bytes
};                     // Total raw size = 32 bytes
```

Assume **3 Students** are allocated dynamically, but only **1 is properly deleted**. Calculate the memory leak at program termination.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Determine the size of one `Student` object.**

The C++ compiler may add **padding** to align members to the size of the largest member (8 bytes for `double`).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layout of Student:} \quad & \text{id (4B)} \rightarrow \text{padding (4B)} \rightarrow \text{cgpa (8B)} \rightarrow \text{name (20B)} \\
\text{Total Size} &= 4 + 4 + 8 + 20 = 36 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{After 8-byte alignment of struct} &= \lceil 36 / 8 \rceil \times 8 = 40 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total allocation for 3 Students.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Allocated} &= N \times \text{sizeof(Student)} + N \times \text{sizeof(cookie)} \\
&= 3 \times 40 + 3 \times 8 \\
&= 120 + 24 = 144 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total deallocation when only 1 is deleted.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Freed} &= 1 \times 40 + 1 \times 8 = 48 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the memory leak.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Leak} &= \text{Allocated} - \text{Freed} \\
&= 144 - 48 = \boxed{96 \text{ bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Common Delete Pitfalls

| **#** | **Code Snippet** | **Symptom** | **KTU Board Answer** |
|------|------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | `delete p; delete p;` | **Double Free** → Heap Corruption | Set `p = nullptr` after first delete |
| 2 | `Base* b = new Derived(); delete b;` (non-virtual dtor) | **Sliced Delete** → Derived leak | Always declare polymorphic base destructor `virtual` |
| 3 | `Student* s = new Student[10]; delete s;` | **Wrong delete form** → UB | Use `delete[] s;` for arrays |
| 4 | `int* p = new int(5); delete p; p = nullptr;` | **Safe pattern** ✓ | Textbook correct usage |
| 5 | `delete nullptr;` | **Safe no-op** (C++ standard guarantees) | Always allowed, recommended guard |
| 6 | `obj = null;` (Java) | Triggers GC eligibility (not immediate) | JVM decides when to actually collect |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Object Lifecycle (Allocation → Usage → Deletion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Declare Pointer: Type T ptr]
    B --> C{Allocate with new}
    C -- Success --> D[Heap Memory Block Reserved]
    C -- Failure --> E[Throw bad_alloc Exception]
    D --> F[Constructor Invoked]
    F --> G[Object In Use]
    G --> H{delete ptr Called?}
    H -- No --> I[Memory Leak on Program Exit]
    H -- Yes --> J[Destructor Invoked]
    J --> K[Memory Returned to Heap]
    K --> L[Set ptr to nullptr]
    L --> M[Program End - Clean Exit]
    E --> N[Handle Exception / Abort]
    I --> O[Resource Exhaustion Over Time]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Java Garbage Collection Eligibility (Mark-Sweep)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[GC Root: Main Stack] --> O1[Object A]
    R1[GC Root: Main Stack] --> O2[Object B]
    O1 --> O3[Object C]
    O4[Orphan Object D] -.-> X[Unreachable]
    O5[Island Object E] -.-> X
    X --> S[Sweep Phase]
    S --> F[Memory Reclaimed]
    O1 --> M[Mark Phase: Live]
    O2 --> M
    O3 --> M
    M --> S
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: C++ Smart Pointer Hierarchy (Modern Best Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Pointer: Risky] --> B[unique_ptr: Single Owner]
    A --> C[shared_ptr: Shared Owner]
    A --> D[weak_ptr: Non-owning Observer]
    B --> E[Auto delete on scope exit]
    C --> F[Reference Counted]
    F --> G[Auto delete when count = 0]
    D --> H[Breaks Reference Cycles]
    E --> I[RAII Compliant]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between `delete` and `delete[]` in C++. Why is using the wrong one undefined behavior?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `delete ptr` deallocates a **single object** allocated by `new`; it invokes **one destructor**.
- `delete[] arr` deallocates an **array** allocated by `new[]`; it invokes **N destructors** (one per element) by reading the hidden cookie storing the array length.
- Mismatch causes **undefined behavior** because the heap allocator either walks the wrong metadata or skips derived-class destructors, leading to memory corruption or silent resource leaks. **(3 marks for clear distinction + UB explanation)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`
**Explain how an object becomes eligible for garbage collection in Java. Is `obj = null;` sufficient to free memory immediately?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An object becomes eligible for GC when **no live thread** can reach it through any chain of strong references starting from a **GC root** (stack frame, static field, JNI handle).
- `obj = null;` only **removes one reference**; actual collection happens when the JVM's GC algorithm decides to run, which is **non-deterministic**.
- Hence, **memory is not freed immediately** — the JVM may delay collection, especially for short-lived objects bundled in the Young Generation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **dynamic memory allocation and deallocation** in C++ with suitable code examples. Discuss the role of constructors and destructors in the `new`/`delete` lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **memory leak**? Write a C++ program that demonstrates a memory leak, and then rewrite it using **RAII with `std::unique_ptr`** to fix the leak. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Dynamic Memory Lifecycle (7 marks):**
- `new` allocates on heap, returns pointer, invokes constructor.
- `delete` invokes destructor, returns memory to heap.
- Mismatch (e.g., `malloc` with `delete`) → UB because no constructor/destructor was paired.
- Code example: `Student* s = new Student(); ... delete s;`

**(b) Memory Leak Demonstration (7 marks):**

```cpp
// BAD: Memory leak
void leakyFunction() {
    Student* s = new Student("Rahul", 8.5);
    // Forgot to write: delete s;
    // 40 bytes leaked every call!
}

// GOOD: RAII with unique_ptr
#include <memory>
void safeFunction() {
    std::unique_ptr<Student> s = std::make_unique<Student>("Rahul", 8.5);
    // Destructor auto-called when s goes out of scope
}
```

- The first version accumulates leaks proportional to call count.
- The second version guarantees cleanup via stack unwinding, even if an exception is thrown. **(7 marks for code + explanation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **manual memory management (C++)** with **automatic garbage collection (Java)**. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **mark-and-sweep garbage collection algorithm**. How does it reclaim cyclic references, and what is its time complexity? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Manual vs Automatic Memory Management (7 marks):**

| Aspect | C++ Manual | Java Automatic GC |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| Control | Full programmer control | JVM decides |
| Speed | Predictable, no GC pauses | Possible stop-the-world pauses |
| Safety | Risk of leaks, dangling pointers | Memory-safe by default |
| Use Case | Embedded, real-time, OS | Enterprise apps, Android |

- **C++ Pros:** Determinism, no runtime overhead, suitable for kernels. **Cons:** Bug-prone, harder to maintain.
- **Java Pros:** No leaks, simpler code. **Cons:** Non-deterministic pauses, higher memory footprint. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Mark-and-Sweep Algorithm (7 marks):**

```
MARK PHASE:
  Start from GC Roots
  DFS/BFS traverse all reachable objects
  Mark each visited object as "live"

SWEEP PHASE:
  Iterate through entire heap
  For each unmarked object → add to free list
  Unmark live objects for next cycle
```

- **Cyclic references** are handled because unreachable cycles have no incoming edge from a GC root — they are simply not visited during the Mark phase.
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\text{Heap Size} + \text{Live Objects})$
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\text{Live Objects})$ for the mark bits
- **Drawback:** No compaction → **heap fragmentation** over time, addressed by Mark-Compact variants. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing `delete` (single) with `delete[]` (array) — costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. Writing `delete` on a pointer obtained from `malloc()` — C++ standard says **UB**; KTU expects you to say "undefined behavior."
> 3. Saying Java's `obj = null;` "frees memory immediately" — it only **makes eligible**, not freed. Lose 1 mark for this misconception.
> 4. Forgetting to declare a polymorphic base destructor as `virtual` — derived destructors will NOT run, causing **silent leaks**.
> 5. In numerical problems, ignoring **struct padding/alignment** to 8-byte boundaries — answers will be off by 4–8 bytes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — KTU Module 4 "Delete"**

- [x] `delete ptr` is for **single objects**; `delete[] arr` is for **arrays** — never mix them.
- [x] Always **set pointer to `nullptr`** after `delete` to prevent dangling/double-free.
- [x] Polymorphic base classes **must** declare `virtual ~Base()`.
- [x] Java has **no `delete` keyword** — only garbage collection via reachability analysis.
- [x] `obj = null` in Java does **not** free memory instantly; it only makes the object eligible.
- [x] Python uses **reference counting + cycle detector**; `del obj` decrements the refcount.
- [x] Modern C++ prefers **`std::unique_ptr` / `std::shared_ptr`** over raw `new`/`delete`.
- [x] Memory leak formula: $\text{Leak} = \text{Alloc} - \text{Free}$
- [x] `delete nullptr;` is a **guaranteed safe no-op** in C++.
- [x] Mark-Sweep GC handles **cyclic references**; refcounting (Python naïve part) does **not** without a cycle collector.
- [x] Struct alignment on 64-bit: round up to multiple of largest member size (typically 8 bytes).
- [x] KTU frequently tests: *Why is `delete` on `malloc`'d memory UB?* — Answer: No constructor was called, so no matching destructor exists.
- [x] Smart pointers (`unique_ptr`, `shared_ptr`, `weak_ptr`) implement **RAII** for automatic cleanup.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insert
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insert

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data Structures using Object Oriented Programming (Java)**, the **Insert** operation refers to the algorithmic process of adding a new data element (node) at a specified position within a linear or non-linear dynamic data structure — typically a **Singly Linked List (SLL)**, **Doubly Linked List (DLL)**, **Circular Linked List (CLL)**, **Stack**, or **Queue**. The operation must correctly update pointer (`next`, `prev`) and/or reference variables of the participating nodes so that structural integrity, traversal order, and memory safety are preserved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> The **Insert** operation is the fundamental dynamic memory mutation primitive in linked data structures. In Java (the mandated language for PEEET419), insertion is performed by allocating a new `Node` object using the `new` keyword, manipulating `.next` (and `.prev` in DLL) references, and re-linking the predecessor and successor nodes to include the new element in the chain.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **train** standing at a station. Each coach is connected to the next one by a coupling. Now, suppose you want to **insert a new coach** in the middle of the train.

1. First, you **uncouple** the link between the coach *before* the insertion point and the coach *after* it.
2. Then, you **attach** the new coach to the coach that was *before*.
3. Finally, you **attach** the coach that was *after* to the new coach.

That's exactly how the **Insert** operation works in a linked list — the "couplings" are the `.next` pointers, and the "coaches" are the nodes. The locomotive (head) and the last coach (tail) just need their pointers updated, and the train continues to work.

---

## Standard Metrics in Linked List Insertion

| Metric | Notation | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity (Begin) | $T(n)$ | **O(1)** |
| Time Complexity (End in SLL) | $T(n)$ | **O(n)** |
| Time Complexity (End with tail ptr) | $T(n)$ | **O(1)** |
| Time Complexity (Middle) | $T(n)$ | **O(n)** |
| Auxiliary Space | $S(n)$ | **O(1)** |
| Node Size (SLL) | sizeof(Node) | **24 bytes** (4B ref + 4B int + 4B ref on 32-bit JVM) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In Java, each `Node` object carries a hidden **16-byte object header** (on a 64-bit JVM with compressed oops), so a typical `int`-valued SLL node consumes ~**24 bytes** in heap memory. This is critical for memory-bound engineering applications (e.g., embedded routers, sensor networks).

---

## Types of Insertion Operations (Syllabus Mandate)

The KTU 2024 scheme for PEEET419 Module 4 enumerates the following standard insertion cases:

1. **Insert at Beginning** — Add node as the new `head`.
2. **Insert at End (Append)** — Add node as the new `tail`.
3. **Insert at a Given Position** — Add node at index `pos` (0-indexed or 1-indexed).
4. **Insert After a Given Node Value** — Add node immediately after the first matching node.
5. **Insert in a Sorted Linked List** — Maintain ascending order invariant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Insert Variant

### A. Insert at Beginning

| Step | Action | Pointer Mutation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create new node `N` | `N = new Node(data)` |
| 2 | Link new node to old head | `N.next = head` |
| 3 | Move head pointer to new node | `head = N` |

**Time:** **O(1)** — Constant. No traversal required.

---

### B. Insert at End (Singly Linked List without tail pointer)

| Step | Action | Pointer Mutation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create new node `N` | `N = new Node(data)` |
| 2 | Initialize a `temp` pointer to `head` | `temp = head` |
| 3 | Traverse until `temp.next == null` | `while (temp.next != null) temp = temp.next;` |
| 4 | Link last node to new node | `temp.next = N` |
| 5 | Set new node's next to null | `N.next = null` |

**Time:** **O(n)** — Linear traversal of *n* nodes.
**Optimization:** Maintain a `tail` reference for **O(1)** append.

---

### C. Insert at a Given Position (0-indexed)

| Step | Action | Pointer Mutation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Validate position: `0 ≤ pos ≤ n` | Boundary check |
| 2 | If `pos == 0`, call Insert at Beginning | Delegation |
| 3 | Traverse to `(pos - 1)`th node using `temp` | `for (i=0; i<pos-1; i++)` |
| 4 | Create new node `N` | `N = new Node(data)` |
| 5 | Link new node to next of predecessor | `N.next = temp.next` |
| 6 | Link predecessor to new node | `temp.next = N` |

**Critical Order:** Step 5 must execute **before** Step 6. Reversing the order **destroys** the chain reference.

---

### D. Insert After a Given Node Value

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Traverse list until `temp.data == key` |
| 2 | If not found, raise exception / return false |
| 3 | Create new node `N` |
| 4 | `N.next = temp.next` |
| 5 | `temp.next = N` |

---

### E. Insert in a Sorted Linked List

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Handle empty list (insert as head) |
| 2 | If `data < head.data`, insert at beginning |
| 3 | Otherwise, traverse with `current` and `prev` until correct position found |
| 4 | Insert between `prev` and `current` |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Insert at Beginning (SLL) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 2 | Insert at End (SLL, no tail) | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | Insert at End (SLL, with tail) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 4 | Insert at Position (SLL) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 5 | Insert in Sorted SLL | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 6 | Insert in DLL (any pos) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |

### Mathematical Formulation of Traversal Cost

Let $n$ denote the number of nodes in the list and $k$ the insertion position (0-indexed). The number of pointer hops to reach the predecessor is exactly $k$.

$$
T_{\text{insert}}(n, k) = k + 3
$$

Where the constant **3** represents the mandatory operations: (i) node creation, (ii) link new node to successor, (iii) link predecessor to new node.

For a uniformly random position $k \sim \mathcal{U}\{0, n-1\}$, the **expected** traversal cost is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{insert}}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(k + 3) = \frac{(n-1)}{2} + 3 \approx \frac{n}{2} + 3
$$

Which simplifies to asymptotic order:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{insert}}] = \Theta(n)
$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Linked List Insert |
|---|---|
| **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling queues, ready queue, device driver lists |
| **Compilers** | Symbol table management, AST node construction |
| **Database Systems** | Transaction logs, undo-redo buffers |
| **Network Routers** | Packet buffer chaining, routing table updates |
| **Music Players** | Playlist insertion (Insert after current song) |
| **Memory Allocators** | Free-list maintenance in slab allocators |
| **AI / ML** | Dynamic graph construction (adjacency list insertion) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Algorithm: Insert at Position (0-indexed) in Singly Linked List

### Java Implementation (KTU Standard)

```java
class Node {
    int data;
    Node next;
    Node(int d) { data = d; next = null; }
}

public class LinkedList {
    Node head;

    // Insert at the beginning
    public void insertAtBeginning(int data) {
        Node newNode = new Node(data);
        newNode.next = head;
        head = newNode;
    }

    // Insert at a given position
    public void insertAtPosition(int data, int pos) {
        if (pos < 0) {
            System.out.println("Invalid position");
            return;
        }
        if (pos == 0) {
            insertAtBeginning(data);
            return;
        }
        Node newNode = new Node(data);
        Node temp = head;
        for (int i = 0; i < pos - 1 && temp != null; i++) {
            temp = temp.next;
        }
        if (temp == null) {
            System.out.println("Position out of bounds");
            return;
        }
        newNode.next = temp.next;   // Step A: link new to successor
        temp.next = newNode;        // Step B: link predecessor to new
    }

    // Insert at the end
    public void insertAtEnd(int data) {
        Node newNode = new Node(data);
        if (head == null) {
            head = newNode;
            return;
        }
        Node temp = head;
        while (temp.next != null) {
            temp = temp.next;
        }
        temp.next = newNode;
    }

    // Traversal / Display
    public void display() {
        Node temp = head;
        while (temp != null) {
            System.out.print(temp.data + " -> ");
            temp = temp.next;
        }
        System.out.println("NULL");
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Dry Run (Trace Table) — Insert 25 at Position 2

**Initial Linked List:**
$$
\text{10} \rightarrow \text{20} \rightarrow \text{30} \rightarrow \text{40} \rightarrow \text{NULL}
$$

| Step | Action | `head` | `temp` | `newNode.next` | Resulting List |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial state | 10 | — | — | 10→20→30→40→NULL |
| 1 | `newNode = Node(25)` | 10 | — | null | 10→20→30→40→NULL |
| 2 | `temp = head` | 10 | 10 | null | 10→20→30→40→NULL |
| 3 | `i=0`, `i<1`, `temp = temp.next` | 10 | 20 | null | 10→20→30→40→NULL |
| 4 | `i=1`, loop exits | 10 | 20 | null | 10→20→30→40→NULL |
| 5 | `newNode.next = temp.next` (20.next = 30) | 10 | 20 | **30** | 10→20→**25**→30→40→NULL |
| 6 | `temp.next = newNode` | 10 | 20 | 30 | 10→20→**25**→30→40→NULL |

**Final List:**
$$
\text{10} \rightarrow \text{20} \rightarrow \text{25} \rightarrow \text{30} \rightarrow \text{40} \rightarrow \text{NULL}
$$

---

## 3.3 Complexity Derivation (Asymptotic)

For the **Insert at Position** operation in a singly linked list of $n$ nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{insert}}(n) &= T_{\text{create}} + T_{\text{traverse}} + T_{\text{link}} \\
&= \mathcal{O}(1) + \mathcal{O}(k) + \mathcal{O}(1) \\
&= \mathcal{O}(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $k$ is the position of insertion. The worst case is $k = n$ (insert at the very end):

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

The best case is $k = 0$ (insert at the beginning):

$$
T_{\text{best}}(n) = \mathcal{O}(1)
$$

The average case (over all uniformly random positions) was derived earlier as:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{insert}}] = \frac{n}{2} + 3 = \Theta(n)
$$

---

## 3.4 Insert in a Sorted Linked List — Detailed Trace

**Sorted Input List:** 10 → 20 → 30 → 50 → 60 → NULL
**Element to Insert:** 40

| Step | `prev` | `current` | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | null | 10 | Start |
| 1 | 10 | 20 | `40 > 10`, advance |
| 2 | 20 | 30 | `40 > 20`, advance |
| 3 | 30 | 50 | `40 < 50`, **STOP — insert here** |
| 4 | Insert 40 between 30 and 50 | — | `newNode.next = current; prev.next = newNode;` |

**Resulting List:**
$$
\text{10} \rightarrow \text{20} \rightarrow \text{30} \rightarrow \text{40} \rightarrow \text{50} \rightarrow \text{60} \rightarrow \text{NULL}
$$

The algorithm guarantees $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time and **preserves the sorted order invariant** as a post-condition.

---

## 3.5 Insert in a Doubly Linked List (DLL) — 4 Pointer Mutations

A DLL node has two pointers: `next` and `prev`. Inserting between `prevNode` and `nextNode` requires **exactly 4 link updates**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode.next} &= \text{nextNode} \\
\text{newNode.prev} &= \text{prevNode} \\
\text{prevNode.next} &= \text{newNode} \\
\text{nextNode.prev} &= \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of operations in DLL insert is mandatory.** If you update `prevNode.next` *before* saving `nextNode` into a temporary variable, the reference to `nextNode` is **lost forever**, causing a memory leak and a broken chain. The KTU board examiner specifically tests this by giving a shuffled list of statements.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Across Structures

| Structure | Insert at Begin | Insert at End | Insert at Middle | Memory Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Array | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shift | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — amortized | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shift | **None** |
| Singly Linked List | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ or $\mathcal{O}(1)$* | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **1 pointer/node** |
| Doubly Linked List | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **2 pointers/node** |
| Circular Linked List | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **1 pointer/node** |

\* with tail pointer; \** with tail pointer maintained
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Singly Linked List Insert at Beginning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Initial: head -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> NULL"] --> B["Step 1: newNode = Node(5)"]
    B --> C["Step 2: newNode.next = head"]
    C --> D["Step 3: head = newNode"]
    D --> E["Final: head -> 5 -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> NULL"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Singly Linked List Insert at Position k

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Start insertAtPosition(data, pos)"]) --> Validate{"pos < 0 OR pos > n ?"}
    Validate -- Yes --> Err["Print Invalid Position and RETURN"]
    Validate -- No --> PosZero{"pos == 0 ?"}
    PosZero -- Yes --> InsertBeg["Call insertAtBeginning(data) and RETURN"]
    PosZero -- No --> Create["newNode = new Node(data)"]
    Create --> InitTemp["temp = head"]
    InitTemp --> Loop["for i = 0 to pos-1"]
    Loop --> CheckNull{"temp == null ?"}
    CheckNull -- Yes --> OutOfBound["Print Out of Bounds and RETURN"]
    CheckNull -- No --> Move["temp = temp.next"]
    Move --> Loop
    Loop -- Done --> LinkA["newNode.next = temp.next"]
    LinkA --> LinkB["temp.next = newNode"]
    LinkB --> End(["RETURN — Insert Complete"])
    Err --> End
    InsertBeg --> End
    OutOfBound --> End
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Insert in Sorted Linked List

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S(["Start insertSorted(data)"]) --> Empty{"head == null ?"}
    Empty -- Yes --> SetHead["head = newNode and RETURN"]
    Empty -- No --> LessHead{"data < head.data ?"}
    LessHead -- Yes --> InsAtBeg["insertAtBeginning(data) and RETURN"]
    LessHead -- No --> Init["prev = head, current = head.next"]
    Init --> Traverse{"current != null AND current.data < data ?"}
    Traverse -- Yes --> Advance["prev = current, current = current.next"]
    Advance --> Traverse
    Traverse -- No --> Insert["newNode.next = current, prev.next = newNode"]
    Insert --> Finish(["RETURN — Sorted Order Preserved"])
    SetHead --> Finish
    InsAtBeg --> Finish
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Insertion in Doubly Linked List

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["State Before Insertion"]
        P1["prevNode"] -- "prevNode.next" --> N1["nextNode"]
        N1 -- "nextNode.prev" --> P1
    end
    subgraph ACTION["Four Pointer Mutations"]
        A1["1. newNode.next = nextNode"]
        A2["2. newNode.prev = prevNode"]
        A3["3. prevNode.next = newNode"]
        A4["4. nextNode.prev = newNode"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER["State After Insertion"]
        P2["prevNode"] -- "next" --> NEW["newNode"]
        NEW -- "next" --> N2["nextNode"]
        N2 -- "prev" --> NEW
        NEW -- "prev" --> P2
    end
    BEFORE --> ACTION --> AFTER
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Complete Insert Operation Taxonomy

```mermaid
mindmap
    root(("Linked List Insert Operations"))
        Position Based
            At Beginning
            At End
            At Index k
        Value Based
            After a Node
            Before a Node
        Order Based
            In Sorted List
        List Type Based
            Singly LL
            Doubly LL
            Circular LL
        Edge Cases
            Empty List
            Single Node
            Position Out of Range
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between array-based insertion and linked-list-based insertion. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Array Insertion | Linked List Insertion |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Layout | Contiguous, fixed size | Non-contiguous, dynamic |
| Time Complexity (Begin) | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shifting required | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only pointer change |
| Space Overhead | None | Extra pointer per node |
| Random Access | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ via index | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ traversal required |

**Advantage of Array:** Cache-friendly contiguous memory, faster random access.
**Advantage of Linked List:** Dynamic size with **O(1) insertion at the beginning**, no shifting of elements.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write a Java method to insert a node at the beginning of a singly linked list.**

**Model Answer:**

```java
void insertAtBeginning(int data) {
    Node newNode = new Node(data);
    newNode.next = head;
    head = newNode;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- **Step 1:** A new node is allocated on the heap using the `new` keyword.
- **Step 2:** The new node's `next` pointer is set to the current `head` (so it points to the rest of the list).
- **Step 3:** The `head` reference is updated to point to the new node, making it the first element.

**Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — constant time, independent of list size.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the algorithm to insert a new node at the end of a singly linked list. Write the corresponding Java method and analyze its time complexity. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Allocate a new node `N` with the given data using `new Node(data)`.
2. Set `N.next = null` (it will be the new tail).
3. If the list is empty (`head == null`), assign `head = N` and return.
4. Otherwise, initialize a temporary pointer `temp = head`.
5. Traverse the list using a `while` loop: `while (temp.next != null) temp = temp.next;`
6. After the loop, `temp` points to the last node. Assign `temp.next = N`.

**Java Implementation:**

```java
void insertAtEnd(int data) {
    Node newNode = new Node(data);
    if (head == null) {
        head = newNode;
        return;
    }
    Node temp = head;
    while (temp.next != null) {
        temp = temp.next;
    }
    temp.next = newNode;
}
```

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

Let $n$ be the number of nodes in the list. The `while` loop iterates exactly $n - 1$ times in the worst case (when only one node exists besides the new one being appended). Each iteration is $\mathcal{O}(1)$, so:

$$
T(n) = (n - 1) \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) + \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

The auxiliary space used is just the `temp` pointer, which is $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

**Optimization Note:** If a `tail` reference is maintained as a class member, the complexity reduces to $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

---

**(b) Write a Java method to insert a new node after a given node value in a singly linked list. Handle the case when the value is not found. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
void insertAfterValue(int key, int data) {
    Node temp = head;
    // Search for the node with value == key
    while (temp != null && temp.data != key) {
        temp = temp.next;
    }
    if (temp == null) {
        System.out.println("Node with value " + key + " not found.");
        return;
    }
    Node newNode = new Node(data);
    newNode.next = temp.next;
    temp.next = newNode;
}
```

**Explanation:**

1. The `temp` pointer traverses the list from the head.
2. The loop condition `temp != null && temp.data != key` is **short-circuit evaluated** — Java stops as soon as either condition is false.
3. If `temp` becomes `null` (end of list reached without finding the key), an appropriate error message is displayed.
4. Otherwise, the new node is created and inserted immediately after the found node using the standard two-link mutation pattern.

**Time Complexity:**

$$
T(n) = \mathcal{O}(k) \text{ where } k \text{ is the position of the matched node}
$$

Worst case: $\mathcal{O}(n)$ when the key is at the end or absent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Explain with a suitable diagram and Java code how a new node is inserted at a specified position in a singly linked list. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (Schematic — Position 2 in a 4-Node List):**

```
Before Insertion:
HEAD -> [10|next] -> [20|next] -> [30|next] -> [40|NULL]

After Insertion of 25 at Position 2:
HEAD -> [10|next] -> [20|next] -> [25|next] -> [30|next] -> [40|NULL]
```

**Java Code:**

```java
void insertAtPosition(int data, int pos) {
    if (pos < 0) {
        System.out.println("Invalid position (negative).");
        return;
    }
    if (pos == 0) {
        insertAtBeginning(data);
        return;
    }
    Node newNode = new Node(data);
    Node temp = head;
    for (int i = 0; i < pos - 1 && temp != null; i++) {
        temp = temp.next;
    }
    if (temp == null) {
        System.out.println("Position out of bounds.");
        return;
    }
    newNode.next = temp.next;
    temp.next = newNode;
}
```

**Key Points to Remember:**

- The `for` loop must check both `i < pos - 1` AND `temp != null` to prevent `NullPointerException` on out-of-range positions.
- The order of the two linking statements (`newNode.next = temp.next;` then `temp.next = newNode;`) is **critical** and must NOT be reversed.

---

**(b) Write a Java method to insert a new node into a sorted singly linked list such that the list remains sorted after insertion. Analyze its time complexity. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
void insertSorted(int data) {
    Node newNode = new Node(data);
    // Case 1: Empty list or insert at beginning
    if (head == null || data < head.data) {
        newNode.next = head;
        head = newNode;
        return;
    }
    // Case 2: Find correct position
    Node current = head;
    while (current.next != null && current.next.data < data) {
        current = current.next;
    }
    // Insert after current
    newNode.next = current.next;
    current.next = newNode;
}
```

**Algorithm Walkthrough:**

1. If the list is empty or the new data is smaller than the head, insert at the beginning.
2. Otherwise, traverse the list with a single pointer `current` until either:
   - `current.next == null` (reached the end), OR
   - `current.next.data >= data` (found the correct position).
3. Insert the new node between `current` and `current.next`.

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

In the worst case, the `while` loop traverses all $n$ nodes:

$$
T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

**Auxiliary Space:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only one extra pointer `current` is used.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Reversed link order:** Writing `temp.next = newNode;` *before* `newNode.next = temp.next;` causes the successor reference to be **overwritten and lost**. Always link the new node to the successor first.
> 2. **Missing null checks:** Failing to verify `head == null` before accessing `head.next` throws `NullPointerException`. KTU deducts marks for unhandled edge cases.
> 3. **Off-by-one in position:** Students often confuse 0-indexed and 1-indexed positions. Read the question carefully — KTU typically uses **1-indexed** positions in problem statements, requiring `i < pos - 2` in the traversal loop.
> 4. **Memory leak in DLL:** Updating `prevNode.next` *before* capturing `nextNode` in a temporary variable loses the only reference to the successor sublist.
> 5. **Forgetting `newNode.next = null` in append:** If the new node's `next` is not explicitly set to `null`, it inherits garbage data and may cause undefined traversal behavior.
> 6. **Not handling sorted list with duplicates:** Decide whether duplicates go *before* or *after* the existing equal node, and use `<=` or `<` consistently in the loop condition.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Insert at Beginning** is always $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — fastest operation in SLL.
- [x] **Insert at End (SLL)** is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ without a tail pointer; $\mathcal{O}(1)$ with one.
- [x] **Insert at Position** requires traversing to the $(pos-1)$th node, then re-linking.
- [x] The **two-link mutation pattern** is: `newNode.next = predecessor.next; predecessor.next = newNode;`
- [x] **DLL insertion** requires **4 pointer updates** in the correct order to avoid memory leaks.
- [x] **Sorted insert** preserves the order invariant; uses single-pointer traversal with a `< data` check.
- [x] Always handle **edge cases**: empty list, single-node list, position out of range, key not found.
- [x] **Memory overhead** of a Java SLL node is ~**24 bytes** (16B header + 4B int + 4B reference on 64-bit JVM).
- [x] **No memory shifting** in linked list insert — unlike arrays, only pointers change.
- [x] **Java-specific:** Use `new Node(data)` to allocate heap memory; the `new` operator invokes the constructor and returns a reference.
- [x] **Final keyword pitfall:** If `head` is declared `final Node head`, the reference cannot be reassigned, but the node's internal data can still be mutated.
- [x] **Generic implementation:** In production code, use `class Node<T>` for type safety — KTU may ask to modify code to support generics.
- [x] **Circular LL insert** at the end requires traversal until `temp.next == head` (not `null`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Select
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET419 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Select

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (Java)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 (Database Connectivity using JDBC)**, the **`SELECT`** operation refers to the **SQL Data Retrieval Command** executed programmatically through the **Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) API**. It is formally defined as a **Data Query Language (DQL)** statement used to fetch one or more rows/columns from a relational database table, returning the result encapsulated in a **`java.sql.ResultSet`** object within the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) memory space.

$$ \text{SELECT} : \text{Table}_{R} \times \text{Conditions}_{\phi} \rightarrow \text{ResultSet}_{(\text{Rows} \times \text{Cols})} $$

The statement **does not modify data** (it is a non-destructive, read-only operation governed by the **ACID** properties of database transactions).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Catalogue" Model

Imagine a massive **library** where millions of books are stored in a giant storeroom. You, the **reader (Java program)**, cannot walk into the storeroom directly. You must:

1. **Approach the Librarian** (JDBC Driver / `DriverManager`) and request books.
2. **Submit a written request slip** (SQL `SELECT` query string).
3. The librarian walks into the storeroom (Database Engine), locates the books matching your criteria (`WHERE` clause), and brings back a **tray with the books** (`ResultSet`).
4. You then **browse through the tray one book at a time** (using `next()`, `getXxx()` methods) to read the information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** A `SELECT` query is essentially a *question* you ask the database. The database returns a *temporary table* (the `ResultSet`) which exists only in your Java program's memory until you close it.

---

## 1.3 Classification within SQL Taxonomy

| Category | Full Form | Mutation? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | ❌ Read-Only | `SELECT` |
| DML | Data Manipulation Language | ✅ Mutates | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| DDL | Data Definition Language | ✅ Schema | `CREATE`, `DROP`, `ALTER` |
| TCL | Transaction Control | ✅ State | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK` |

> [!NOTE]
> **`SELECT` is strictly a DQL (Data Query Language) statement.** It is a *non-destructive* operation — running the same `SELECT` query multiple times produces the same result (idempotent for read operations on committed data) and never alters the underlying table state.

---

## 1.4 Role of `SELECT` in the JDBC Architecture

The JDBC API defines a **4-tier execution pipeline** for any `SELECT` operation:

1. **`DriverManager`** — Loads the appropriate database driver (e.g., `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`).
2. **`Connection`** — Establishes a logical session with the DBMS (e.g., MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL).
3. **`Statement` / `PreparedStatement`** — Carries the SQL string to the DB engine.
4. **`ResultSet`** — Receives the rows returned by the DB engine and exposes them to Java.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result of a `SELECT` statement is **always a `ResultSet` object** in Java, never a primitive or a plain array. This object maintains a **cursor** (initially positioned *before the first row*) that you must advance using `next()`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a SQL `SELECT` Statement

A fully-qualified `SELECT` query has a well-defined syntactic structure. Below is the **canonical order of clauses** (the order in which a human *writes* the query):

```sql
SELECT [DISTINCT] column_list
FROM   table_name
[WHERE  condition]
[GROUP BY column_list]
[HAVING aggregate_condition]
[ORDER BY column_list [ASC | DESC]]
[LIMIT  n [OFFSET m]];
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Processing Order** (the order the database engine actually *executes* the clauses) is different from the writing order:
> `FROM` → `WHERE` → `GROUP BY` → `HAVING` → `SELECT` → `DISTINCT` → `ORDER BY` → `LIMIT`
> This distinction is a **favorite KTU question** in 2-mark sections.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | SQL Syntax | Java JDBC Mapping | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Column Projection** | `SELECT col1, col2` | `rs.getString("col1")` | Choose specific columns |
| **All Columns Wildcard** | `SELECT *` | Loop over all `getMetaData()` | Retrieve all columns |
| **Equality Filter** | `WHERE id = 101` | Place `?` in `PreparedStatement` | Match exact value |
| **Range Filter** | `WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 25` | Same | Inclusive range query |
| **Pattern Match** | `WHERE name LIKE 'A%'` | Same | Wildcard search |
| **Set Membership** | `WHERE dept IN ('CS','IT')` | Same | Match list of values |
| **Null Check** | `WHERE phone IS NULL` | `rs.wasNull()` | Handle SQL NULL in Java |
| **Sorting** | `ORDER BY salary DESC` | Iterate `ResultSet` accordingly | Sort output |
| **Row Cap** | `LIMIT 10` | Same | Pagination control |
| **Aggregate** | `SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(salary)` | `rs.getInt(1)`, `rs.getDouble(2)` | Statistical functions |

---

## 2.3 The `WHERE` Clause — Boolean Predicate Logic

The `WHERE` clause is the **filter** of a `SELECT` statement. It accepts a Boolean predicate evaluated **row-by-row**. The fundamental operators are:

| Operator Class | SQL Symbols | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Comparison | `=`, `<>`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=` | Relational comparison |
| Logical | `AND`, `OR`, `NOT` | Boolean combination |
| Special | `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL` | Set/range/pattern checks |
| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` | Computed columns |

The predicate is evaluated for every row produced by the `FROM` clause. A row is included in the result **iff** the predicate evaluates to `TRUE`. Rows where it is `FALSE` or `UNKNOWN` (i.e., `NULL` comparison) are **excluded**.

---

## 2.4 The `ResultSet` Interface — Cursor Mechanics

The `ResultSet` is **not a Java Collection** (it does not implement `Iterable`). It is a **forward-only cursor** by default, with these properties:

$$ \text{CursorPosition} \in \{ \text{BEFORE\_FIRST}, \text{ROW}_i, \text{AFTER\_LAST} \} $$

| Method | Action | Return Type |
|---|---|---|
| `next()` | Move cursor to next row | `boolean` |
| `previous()` | Move cursor to previous row (scrollable RS only) | `boolean` |
| `absolute(int)` | Jump to row `n` | `boolean` |
| `getString(int \mid String)` | Read column as `String` | `String` |
| `getInt(int \mid String)` | Read column as `int` | `int` |
| `wasNull()` | Check if last-read column was SQL `NULL` | `boolean` |
| `close()` | Free DB resources | `void` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`ResultSet` types** (created via `Statement.createStatement(resultSetType, concurrency)`):
> - `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` (default, fastest, cursor moves only forward)
> - `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` (cursor can move in any direction, ignores external changes)
> - `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` (cursor reflects external changes — rarely supported)

---

## 2.5 `Statement` vs `PreparedStatement` for `SELECT`

| Feature | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` |
|---|---|---|
| **Query Compilation** | Compiled every execution | **Pre-compiled** once, cached |
| **Parameters** | String concatenation (SQL Injection risk) | Placeholder `?` (safe) |
| **Performance** | Slower for repeated queries | **Faster** (DB engine caches plan) |
| **Readability** | Poor with many variables | High |
| **Use Case** | One-time, static DDL queries | **Repeating `SELECT` queries with parameters** |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners explicitly test whether students use `Statement` or `PreparedStatement`. The **correct professional answer** is *always* `PreparedStatement` for parameterized `SELECT` operations, citing **SQL Injection prevention** and **performance**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `SELECT` operation is the **most frequently executed** SQL command in any production system. Engineering applications include:

- **Banking Software**: Fetching account balance, transaction history.
- **E-Commerce**: Product search, filtering by price/brand, pagination.
- **Healthcare (HIS)**: Retrieving patient records by ID, doctor, date range.
- **IoT Dashboards**: Real-time sensor data streaming (often wrapped in `SELECT` with `LIMIT 1 ORDER BY ts DESC`).
- **Machine Learning Pipelines**: Bulk extraction of training data via `SELECT ... INTO OUTFILE`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Executing a `SELECT` in JDBC

We will derive the complete Java program to **fetch all students with marks > 75** from a `students` table.

### Step 1: Load the Driver Class

The driver is a Java class provided by the database vendor that translates JDBC calls to the DB's native protocol. For MySQL 8.x:

```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
```

This line forces the JVM to load and statically initialize the driver, which in turn registers itself with `DriverManager`.

### Step 2: Establish the Connection

```java
String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_university";
String user = "root";
String pass = "admin123";
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
```

Here, the **JDBC URL** has the structure:
$$ \text{jdbc}:\text{<subprotocol>}{:}//\text{host}{:}\text{port}/\text{dbname} $$

- Subprotocol = database engine identifier (e.g., `mysql`, `postgresql`, `oracle:thin`).
- Host & Port = network location of the DB server.
- DB Name = logical schema to use.

### Step 3: Prepare the `SELECT` Statement

```java
String sql = "SELECT roll_no, name, marks FROM students WHERE marks > ?";
PreparedStatement pst = con.prepareStatement(sql);
pst.setInt(1, 75);
```

The placeholder `?` at index `1` (1-based indexing in JDBC, not 0!) is bound to the integer `75`. The DB engine **pre-compiles** this query once.

### Step 4: Execute the Query and Obtain `ResultSet`

```java
ResultSet rs = pst.executeQuery();
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Use `executeQuery()` for `SELECT` (returns `ResultSet`). Use `executeUpdate()` for `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` (returns `int` row count). KTU questions often confuse students here.

### Step 5: Iterate the `ResultSet`

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int roll  = rs.getInt("roll_no");
    String nm = rs.getString("name");
    int mks   = rs.getInt("marks");
    System.out.println(roll + " | " + nm + " | " + mks);
}
```

Each call to `next()` advances the cursor. It returns `true` if a row exists, `false` when the cursor passes the last row.

### Step 6: Close the Resources (in reverse order)

```java
rs.close();
pst.close();
con.close();
```

> [!NOTE]
> Since **Java 7**, the `try-with-resources` syntax is preferred as it auto-closes all resources implementing `AutoCloseable` (all JDBC interfaces do).

### Step 7: Refactored Production-Grade Code

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class StudentSelector {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_university";
        String sql = "SELECT roll_no, name, marks FROM students WHERE marks > ?";
        
        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, "root", "admin123");
             PreparedStatement pst = con.prepareStatement(sql)) {
            
            pst.setInt(1, 75);
            try (ResultSet rs = pst.executeQuery()) {
                while (rs.next()) {
                    System.out.printf("%d | %s | %d%n",
                        rs.getInt("roll_no"),
                        rs.getString("name"),
                        rs.getInt("marks"));
                }
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping JDBC Steps to SQL Semantics

| Step | Java Code | SQL Equivalent | Failure if Omitted |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Class.forName(...)` | `USE driver` | `ClassNotFoundException` |
| 2 | `DriverManager.getConnection(...)` | `CONNECT TO db` | `SQLException` (no driver / wrong credentials) |
| 3 | `con.prepareStatement(sql)` | `PREPARE stmt FROM '...'` | `SQLException` (malformed SQL) |
| 4 | `pst.setInt(1, 75)` | `SET @mks = 75` | Type mismatch / wrong index |
| 5 | `pst.executeQuery()` | Server runs `SELECT` and streams rows | `SQLException` (table/column not found) |
| 6 | `rs.next()` loop | Cursor advance | `NullPointerException` if `rs` is null |
| 7 | `rs.getXxx(...)` | Read column value | `SQLException: Column not found` |
| 8 | `rs.close() / pst.close() / con.close()` | `DEALLOCATE / DISCONNECT` | **Resource leak** (DB connections are expensive) |

---

## 3.3 Derived Boolean Logic of the `WHERE` Clause

The `WHERE` predicate can be formally expressed using Boolean algebra. For the query `SELECT * FROM students WHERE marks > 75 AND dept = 'CS'`:

$$ \phi(r) = \big( r.\text{marks} > 75 \big) \;\land\; \big( r.\text{dept} = \text{'CS'} \big) $$

The result set is:
$$ R_{\text{result}} = \{\, r \in R_{\text{students}} \mid \phi(r) = \text{TRUE} \,\} $$

**Truth Table** for the compound predicate:

| `marks > 75` | `dept = 'CS'` | `AND` Result | Row Included? |
|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | **T** | ✅ Yes |
| T | F | F | ❌ No |
| F | T | F | ❌ No |
| F | F | F | ❌ No |
| NULL | T | UNKNOWN | ❌ No |
| T | NULL | UNKNOWN | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SQL uses **three-valued logic** (`TRUE`, `FALSE`, `UNKNOWN`) — not the classical two-valued Boolean logic. Any row producing `UNKNOWN` is **excluded**. This is why `NULL = NULL` is *not* `TRUE`; you must use `IS NULL` instead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Java-to-Database SELECT Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Application Main] --> B[Class.forName Driver Loading]
    B --> C[DriverManager getConnection]
    C --> D[Connection Object Created]
    D --> E[Prepare SQL String with Placeholders]
    E --> F[PreparedStatement Object Created]
    F --> G[Bind Parameters via setInt setString]
    G --> H[Call executeQuery]
    H --> I[DB Engine Parses Validates Optimizes SQL]
    I --> J[DB Engine Executes SELECT on Table]
    J --> K[DB Engine Streams Matching Rows Back]
    K --> L[ResultSet Object Populated with Rows]
    L --> M[Java Loop Calls rs.next]
    M --> N{More Rows Available}
    N -->|Yes| O[Read Columns via rs.getInt getString]
    O --> P[Process Data in Java]
    P --> M
    N -->|No| Q[Close ResultSet PreparedStatement Connection]
    Q --> R[End of Program]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Anatomy of the JDBC `ResultSet` Cursor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Before First Row] -->|rs.next returns true| B[Row 1: rs.getXxx reads]
    B -->|rs.next returns true| C[Row 2: rs.getXxx reads]
    C -->|rs.next returns true| D[Row N: rs.getXxx reads]
    D -->|rs.next returns false| E[After Last Row]

    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: SQL SELECT Clause Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Students Table: All Rows] --> B[FROM Clause: Pick Source Table]
    B --> C[WHERE Clause: Apply Row Filter]
    C --> D[GROUP BY: Form Groups if any]
    D --> E[HAVING: Filter Groups]
    E --> F[SELECT: Project Columns]
    F --> G[DISTINCT: Remove Duplicates if any]
    G --> H[ORDER BY: Sort Output]
    H --> I[LIMIT OFFSET: Paginate]
    I --> J[Final ResultSet Returned to Java]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The DB engine processes clauses in the **logical order shown above** (left-to-right), even though you *write* the SQL in a different order. This is the most-tested conceptual point in KTU exams on `SELECT`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between `executeQuery()` and `executeUpdate()` methods of `Statement` interface in JDBC. Which one is used for a `SELECT` operation and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `executeQuery()` | `executeUpdate()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Return Type** | `ResultSet` | `int` (rows affected) |
| **SQL Statement Type** | DQL (`SELECT`) | DML (`INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`) and DDL |
| **Side Effect** | None on DB | Mutates table data/schema |

For a `SELECT` operation, we use `executeQuery()` because it returns a `ResultSet` object containing the rows fetched from the database. Using `executeUpdate()` for `SELECT` would compile but return `0` (or throw an exception in strict drivers), losing access to the fetched rows. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Explain the role of `ResultSet` in JDBC. What is the default cursor position when a `ResultSet` is created?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A `ResultSet` is a Java object that represents the tabular data returned by executing a SQL `SELECT` query. It maintains a **cursor** that points to the current row of data. The default cursor position is **before the first row** — the cursor does not point to any valid data initially. To read the first row, the programmer must invoke `rs.next()`, which returns `true` and advances the cursor to row 1. The `ResultSet` remains valid only until the corresponding `Statement` and `Connection` are closed, or until it is explicitly closed via `rs.close()`. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📕 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **JDBC architecture** and list out the **key interfaces** and **classes** involved. State the role of `DriverManager` and `Connection`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program using **JDBC** to connect to a `library` database (MySQL) and execute a `SELECT` query to fetch **all books whose price is greater than ₹500**, displaying the book id, title, author, and price. Use `PreparedStatement`. Handle all relevant `SQLException`s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The **JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** API provides a standard interface for Java programs to communicate with relational databases. Its architecture has **4 major components**:

| Component | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `DriverManager` | **Class** (in `java.sql`) | Manages the list of DB drivers; provides `getConnection()` factory method |
| `Connection` | **Interface** | Represents an active session with a specific DB |
| `Statement` / `PreparedStatement` | **Interface** | Carries the SQL string to the DB engine |
| `ResultSet` | **Interface** | Holds the rows returned by a `SELECT` query |

**Role of `DriverManager`:** It maintains a registry of all loaded JDBC drivers. When `getConnection(url, user, pwd)` is called, it iterates through registered drivers, asks each one whether it understands the URL, and uses the first one that does to establish a physical socket connection to the DB. **(3 marks)**

**Role of `Connection`:** It is a logical session object that allows Java to commit/rollback transactions, create `Statement` objects, control isolation levels, and close the connection. Every `Statement` and `ResultSet` is bound to a specific `Connection`. **(2 marks)**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 — Figure 1)* **(2 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Assumed Table Schema:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE books (
    book_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    title    VARCHAR(100),
    author   VARCHAR(100),
    price    DECIMAL(10,2)
);
```

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class PriceyBookSelector {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. JDBC URL components
        String url    = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/library";
        String user   = "root";
        String pass   = "admin123";
        
        // 2. SQL with parameter placeholder
        String sql = "SELECT book_id, title, author, price "
                   + "FROM books WHERE price > ?";
        
        // 3. Try-with-resources for auto-cleanup
        try {
            // 4. Load driver (optional since JDBC 4.0 auto-loads)
            Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
            
            // 5. Establish connection
            try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
                 PreparedStatement pst = con.prepareStatement(sql)) {
                
                // 6. Bind parameter
                pst.setDouble(1, 500.00);
                
                // 7. Execute and obtain ResultSet
                try (ResultSet rs = pst.executeQuery()) {
                    
                    // 8. Iterate the rows
                    System.out.printf("%-8s %-30s %-20s %s%n",
                        "ID", "TITLE", "AUTHOR", "PRICE");
                    System.out.println("------------------------------------------------------------");
                    
                    while (rs.next()) {
                        int id  = rs.getInt("book_id");
                        String t = rs.getString("title");
                        String a = rs.getString("author");
                        double p = rs.getDouble("price");
                        
                        System.out.printf("%-8d %-30s %-20s %.2f%n", id, t, a, p);
                    }
                }
            }
        } catch (ClassNotFoundException e) {
            System.err.println("JDBC Driver not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            System.err.println("Database error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Key Explanation Points (for full marks):**
- **Line 6**: `?` placeholder is bound via `setDouble(1, 500.00)` — protects against SQL injection.
- **Line 7**: `executeQuery()` is used *only* for `SELECT`; returns a `ResultSet`.
- **Line 8**: `try-with-resources` auto-closes `Connection`, `PreparedStatement`, and `ResultSet` in reverse order of declaration.
- **Line 9-10**: Two distinct exception types caught: `ClassNotFoundException` (driver missing) and `SQLException` (DB errors like wrong credentials, table missing). **(7 marks)**

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **`SELECT` statement in SQL** with its general syntax. Describe the role of the `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, and `ORDER BY` clauses with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java JDBC program to **fetch the second-highest salary** from an `employee(emp_id, name, salary)` table. Show the complete code with proper explanation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

The SQL **`SELECT`** statement is a **Data Query Language (DQL)** command used to retrieve data from one or more tables.

**General Syntax:**
```sql
SELECT [DISTINCT] expression1, expression2, ...
FROM   table_name
[WHERE  search_condition]
[GROUP BY column_list]
[HAVING aggregate_condition]
[ORDER BY column_list [ASC | DESC]]
[LIMIT  n];
```

**Clause-wise Explanation with Examples:**

1. **`WHERE` Clause:** Filters rows *before* any grouping.
   ```sql
   SELECT name FROM employee WHERE salary > 50000;
   ```

2. **`GROUP BY` Clause:** Collapses rows sharing a common value into summary rows. Usually paired with aggregate functions (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN`).
   ```sql
   SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM employee GROUP BY dept;
   ```

3. **`HAVING` Clause:** Filters the *groups* produced by `GROUP BY` (similar to `WHERE` but operates on aggregates).
   ```sql
   SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
   FROM employee
   GROUP BY dept
   HAVING AVG(salary) > 60000;
   ```

4. **`ORDER BY` Clause:** Sorts the final result set in ascending (`ASC`, default) or descending (`DESC`) order.
   ```sql
   SELECT name, salary FROM employee ORDER BY salary DESC;
   ```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Order vs Writing Order:** The DB engine processes `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY`. A common KTU question asks: *"Why can't we use a column alias defined in `SELECT` inside `WHERE`?"* — Because `WHERE` is processed **before** `SELECT` in the logical order. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Strategy:** The second-highest salary can be obtained by selecting the **maximum salary that is less than the maximum salary** of the table:
$$ \text{Second Highest} = \max(\text{salary}) \;\text{ where }\; \text{salary} < \max(\text{salary}) $$

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class SecondHighestSalary {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/company";
        String sql = "SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest "
                   + "FROM employee "
                   + "WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employee)";
        
        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, "root", "admin123");
             Statement st = con.createStatement();
             ResultSet rs = st.executeQuery(sql)) {
            
            if (rs.next()) {
                double second = rs.getDouble("second_highest");
                System.out.println("Second Highest Salary = ₹" + second);
            } else {
                System.out.println("Table has fewer than 2 distinct salaries.");
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- **Line 3-5:** A **nested subquery** — the inner `SELECT MAX(salary)` computes the highest salary, the outer `WHERE` excludes it, and the outer `SELECT MAX(salary)` picks the next highest. This is the **portable, ANSI-SQL** way to solve it.
- **Line 8:** Since there is no parameter binding, a plain `Statement` is acceptable here.
- **Line 12-14:** We use `rs.getDouble(...)` because `salary` is typically a decimal type. Using `wasNull()` check is recommended for production.
- **Edge Case:** If all employees have the *same* salary, the outer `SELECT` will return `NULL`, handled gracefully. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using `rs.getInt(0)` — JDBC columns are **1-indexed**, not 0-indexed.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to call `rs.next()` before `rs.getXxx()` — results in `SQLException: Before start of result set`.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Using `executeUpdate()` instead of `executeQuery()` for `SELECT` — silently returns 0 rows and skips result iteration.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–3 marks):** String-concatenating user input into the SQL string (e.g., `"WHERE name = '" + name + "'"`) — **SQL Injection vulnerability**; use `PreparedStatement` always.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (–2 marks):** Not closing the `ResultSet`/`Connection` — leads to **resource leaks** in real systems. Always use `try-with-resources`.

## 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam!**

- ✅ `SELECT` is a **DQL** command; it **never modifies** data.
- ✅ For any `SELECT` in JDBC, use **`executeQuery()`** — it returns a `ResultSet`.
- ✅ For DML (`INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`), use **`executeUpdate()`** — it returns an `int`.
- ✅ Always prefer **`PreparedStatement`** over `Statement` for parameterized queries (SQL injection + performance).
- ✅ JDBC column indexes are **1-based**, not 0-based.
- ✅ The `ResultSet` cursor starts **before the first row** — call `next()` first.
- ✅ Close resources in **reverse order of opening** (`ResultSet` → `Statement` → `Connection`); use `try-with-resources` in Java 7+.
- ✅ The logical processing order of SQL `SELECT` clauses is: `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY → LIMIT`.
- ✅ `NULL` in SQL uses **three-valued logic**; always use `IS NULL` (never `= NULL`).
- ✅ `ResultSet` is **not** a `Collection`; it does **not** implement `Iterable`.
- ✅ JDBC URL format: `jdbc:<subprotocol>://<host>:<port>/<dbname>`.
- ✅ Modern JDBC 4.0+ auto-loads drivers via `META-INF/services`; explicit `Class.forName()` is now optional.
- ✅ Watch out for `SQLException` (checked) — must be caught or declared `throws`.
- ✅ Forgetting `rs.next()` ⇒ throws `SQLException` ("Before start of result set").
- ✅ Aggregate functions (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN`) **ignore NULLs** by default.
